question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she intends to introduce a new, modernised energy efficiency programme to succeed the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme, beyond 2027.
answer_text: The Executive’s Energy Strategy commits to substantially increasing funding and support for retrofitting buildings. In this context, my officials are developing a new Domestic Energy Efficiency and Low Carbon Heat Programme that will replace NISEP with the intent to consult on the design and related funding options in the coming months.  	In the interim, the NISEP, delivered by the Utility Regulator, will be extended for one year from 1 April 2027 to ensure no gaps in provision of support. My Department continues to work with the Utility Regulator and partners across government to assess options for further scaling of support as this new programme is being developed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any independent evaluation of the Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Programme has been carried out in the last five years.
answer_text: The NI Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP) is administered by the Energy Saving Trust on behalf of the Utility Regulator. My officials requested a response from the Utility Regulator in relation to your question and have been advised that no independent evaluation of NISEP has been carried out in the last five years.  The last independent review of the NISEP was carried out between 2018 – 2019.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of court cases, in each of the last five years, which were adjourned due to the unavailability of accredited interpreters.
answer_text: Details of adjournments due to the unavailability of accredited interpreters are not recorded on the Integrated Court Operations System.  Although it is not routinely collected, some information on the unavailability of accredited interpreters may be held in court records and management information.  However, to extract the information would require a manual trawl, the extracted information would then need to be matched to information on court adjournments.  This may not accurately capture all instances of an adjournment due to the unavailability of accredited interpreters, and is likely to incur a disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 31759/22-27, whether Ards and North Down Borough Council has made any requests for an abandonment order for Conway Square, Newtownards, since her answer was provided.
answer_text: I can confirm that Ards and North Down Borough Council have not requested an abandonment order for Conway Square, Newtownards in the past twelve months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail on the current stocks of (i) trout; (ii) pollan; (iii) bream; (iv) perch; and (v) pike, in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: There is no routine populationwide stock assessment providing current estimates for trout, pollan, bream, perch or pike in Lough Neagh. Fisheries monitoring and research undertaken by the AgriFood and Biosciences Institute is targeted and speciesspecific and does not generate comprehensive “current stock” figures. Available information from surveys and studies provides partial insight into population status but does not constitute a definitive assessment of total stock size for each species.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail on the age structure of the current stock of (i) trout; (ii) pollan; (iii) bream; (iv) perch; and (v) pike, in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: Age information for trout, pollan, bream, perch and pike in Lough Neagh is not derived from a routine, populationwide agestructure assessment. Where age data exist, they arise from limited sampling or speciesspecific research undertaken for defined management or scientific purposes. These data indicate a range of age classes within sampled fish, but do not provide a comprehensive or current agestructure profile for the overall stock of each species in the lough.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the age structure of brown eels in Lough Neagh, broken down by (i) juveniles; (ii) adults; and (iii) mature, ages.
answer_text: Sampling undertaken by the AgriFood and Biosciences Institute shows that brown eels in Lough Neagh comprise a wide range of age classes, reflecting slow individual growth rates and variable maturation times. Age information is derived from samples of brown eels taken annually and analysed to estimate age in years. This information is reported through international reporting mechanisms, including the ICES Working Group on Eels (WGEEL).The data available is based on sampled eels only and does not represent a full population census, nor are brown eels classified into juvenile, adult or mature categories within routine monitoring programmes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any equality impact assessment or rural needs assessment carried out in relation to changes to funding advances and payment processes, notified by way of his Department's memo of 25 March 2026 to community and voluntary groups.
answer_text: The Department issued correspondence in March 2026 to provide clarity to Voluntary and Community funded organisations in advance of the 2026/27 financial year and to support the use of the Department’s new Digital Grant Management System (DGMS) while awaiting final budget allocation arrangements.As the correspondence reflects established governance arrangements it is therefore not considered necessary to conduct an equality impact assessment or rural needs assessment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rationale for the decision that advance payments under Small Pockets of Deprivation funding in 2026-2027 will be limited to salary costs only, and that programme and running costs will now require submission and approval of forms.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my response to AQW 43366/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any assessment was undertaken of the cash flow implications of changes to funding advances and payment processes for Small Pockets of Deprivation funded organisations, particularly smaller organisations with limited reserves.
answer_text: There is no change to the operation of the funding arrangements to Small Pockets of Deprivation (SPODs).SPODs funding is administered by the NIHE and will continue to be paid in advance on a quarterly basis, consistent with arrangements in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any reason notification of changes to funding advances and payment processes was issued on 25 March 2026 to community and voluntary groups, only days in advance of 2026-2027 financial year.
answer_text: The Department issued correspondence in March 2026 to provide clarity to Neighbourhood Renewal funded organisations in advance of the 2026/27 financial year and to support the use of the Department’s new Digital Grant Management System (DGMS) while awaiting final budget allocations arrangements.The changes reflect established governance practice, in line with grant funding guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any data on the stock of brown eel in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: My Department does not hold data on the current stock of brown eels in Lough Neagh, as monitoring of brown eel stock abundance is not part of the statutory monitoring requirements. However, recruitment, escapement, fat content and eel health are monitored. This information is reported in the International Council for the Exploration of the Sea (ICES) Working Group on Eels (WGEEL).  ICES report is open source on ICES website (WGEEL Country reports - UK).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of buildings, within the ownership or control of (i) their Department; and (ii) their Department’s arm’s-length bodies, that are unoccupied.
answer_text: The Department owns one unoccupied building at the former Regional Government Headquarters site in Ballymena. The Maze Long Kesh Development Corporation owns thirteen unoccupied buildings, eleven of which are associated with the former prison complex.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail when it is expected that a proposal will come forward to address the outstanding PSNI holiday pay due to officers, following the Supreme Court’s decision on this matter.
answer_text: My officials are currently considering the PSNI Holiday Pay business case which must satisfy the requisite approvals process, including approval by the Department of Finance. While I cannot give a definitive timeframe at this stage, I want to reassure you that my officials are working closely and constructively with the PSNI to progress the Holiday Pay business case as quickly as possible.  Holiday Pay is an exceptional pressure that is wholly unaffordable under any circumstances within existing or future PSNI and DoJ baseline budget. Furthermore, Holiday Pay is an issue that has an impact across all departments and, therefore, a collective solution at Block level will be required.Further engagement will be required with the Executive and the Department of Finance to explore potential funding options when the liability crystallises.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the (i) number; (ii) scale; and (iii) geographic distribution, of solar farm developments currently in operation across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department does not hold a centrally maintained dataset on solar farm developments currently in operation across the North. Information on connected electricity generation assets, including solar farm developments, is primarily held by the electricity system operators.SONI, as the Transmission System Operator, and NIEN, as the Distribution Network Operator, may hold relevant information within their respective system and connection datasets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of buildings, within the ownership or control of (i) her Department; or (ii) her Department’s arm’s-length bodies, that are unoccupied.
answer_text: There are five unoccupied buildings across the six Further Education college campuses and one unoccupied building at Stranmillis University College.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of buildings, within the ownership or control of (i) his Department; or (ii) his Department’s arm’s-length bodies, that are unoccupied.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the strategic management of the NICS Office Estate. The Department of Finance has seven buildings within its ownership or control that are unoccupied, as well as five smaller vacant non-office properties that are domestic and/or are listed. There are no unoccupied buildings within the ownership or control of the Department’s arm’s-length bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment she has made of the contribution of solar farms to Northern Ireland’s renewable electricity targets.
answer_text: The Electricity Consumption and Renewable Generation Statistics, published by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency, indicate that in 2025 solar photovoltaic generation contributed 6% of total renewable electricity generation in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) number; (ii) scale; and (iii) geographic distribution, of solar farm developments currently approved but not in operation across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Information relating to renewable energy applications is published for applications between April 2002 and September 2025:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/renewable-energy-planning-applications-list-april-2002-september-2025. My Department does not hold information on whether solar farm developments currently approved are in operation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of seasonal agricultural workers employed in Northern Ireland, in each of the last five years, broken down by country of origin.
answer_text: The Home Office publishes data on grants of entry clearance visas for seasonal workers (previously Tier 5) by nationality at a UK level. These statistics do not include a Northern Ireland specific breakdown.The most recent UK figures from the Home Office show that a total of 38,676 seasonal worker visas were granted in the year ended December 2025.  The Defra Seasonal Workers Survey in 2024 (the most recent available) found that 2.4% of those who responded indicated that they primarily worked in Northern Ireland.A breakdown of visas granted under this route by nationality is only readily available for the year ending December 2025. The largest nationality groups were Kyrgyzstan (12,606 granted), Uzbekistan (6,293), Kazakhstan (5,740), Tajikistan (5,702) and Kenya (2,541).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of GP practices that have closed or handed back contracts, in each of the last five years; and (ii) any contingency arrangements were put in place.
answer_text: Despite the pressures on General Practice and the number of practices that have handed back their contracts in each of the last five years, my Department has been able to secure arrangements/new contractors to ensure continuity of General Medical Services.   Only one of these contract hand-backs has resulted in a practice closure.  In this case the patient list was dispersed to ensure continuity of General Medical Services. The table below presents the number of GP practices that have handed back their contract, in each of the last five years: Financial Year Number of Contract handbacks2021/202222022/2023132023/2024112024/20255*2025/20261*Includes one practice closure where the patient list was dispersed to ensure continuity of General Medical ServicesWith regards to the contract handback in 2025/2026 my officials in the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) are actively working through the process to seek a new contractor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Belfast Central Library project.
answer_text: Redevelopment of Belfast Central Library remains a key strategic priority for Libraries NI. My officials continue to engage with Libraries NI as work progresses on assessing the short and medium terms needs of the asset.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting times for mental health assessments and treatments for Child and Adolescent Mental Health Services, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any capital investment that has been made to the library estate, in the last ten years, broken down by (i) location; and (ii) level of investment.
answer_text: In the last ten years (2016 to 2026) my Department, working in conjunction with Libraries NI, has supported delivery of a wide range of capital investment projects across the public library estate, totalling approximately £15.70 million. 		Due to the volume of detail required to provide an answer to your question, I have arranged for this to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) when work will commence to have the Queen of Ulster recertified; and (ii) an estimate of when the vessel will be operational.
answer_text: Work to recertify the Queen of Ulster has already commenced. It is anticipated that certification will be achieved by the end of summer 2026, after which operational duties will recommence.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) number; and (ii) location, of fixed speed cameras that are currently inactive or not operational, including the reasons why this is the case.
answer_text: The policy related to speed detection cameras is the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership, and I suggest that you contact the Partnership directly for this information.Their contact details are:Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership42 Montgomery RoadBelfast BT6 9LD

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 41496/22-27, whether his Department was in breach of it’s statutory duty under the Fisheries Act 2020 by (i) not deploying the fisheries protection vessel in 2025; and (ii) reducing its activities in previous years.
answer_text: There is no provision within the Fisheries Act 2020 that creates a statutory duty requiring any UK Fisheries administration, including DAERA, to deploy a Fisheries Protection Vessel in any given year, or to maintain any specific level of enforcement of at sea inspections or activity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 41496/22-27, to detail the number of days that the Queen of Ulster spent in (i) Strangford Lough; (ii) the scallop fishery; and (iii) the Mourne Herring Shore, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of days that Fisheries Protection Vessel (FPV) Queen of Ulster spent patrolling on each of the five years for the area and fisheries specified is: 2021 – 25 days Scallop Fishery, 14 days Strangford Lough, and 0 days Mourne Shore patrols.2022 – 16 days Scallop Fishery, 3 days Strangford Lough, and 0 days Mourne Shore patrols.2023 – 17 days Scallop Fishery, 17 days Strangford Lough, and 3 days Mourne Shore patrols.2024 – 7 days Scallop Fishery, 7 days Strangford Lough, and 0 days Mourne Shore patrols.The vessel was not deployed to sea in 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of vacant positions in the Marine and Fisheries Division, broken down by the roles; and (ii) the number of agency staff employed, including any cost associated with their employment.
answer_text: Vacancies in all parts of the Department remain under close review to ensure that they remain affordable in the current public expenditure context, but I understand that there are currently 68 registered vacancies within the Department’s Marine and Fisheries Directorate.  This figure excludes student placement positions offered across the Department each year.The table below provides a breakdown by role type:RoleNo of registered vacanciesSenior leadership roles (Grades 6/7)Professional roles(includes veterinary, fisheries and scientific specialisms)7Other roles3Middle management roles (Deputy Principal/Staff Officer)Professional roles (fisheries and scientific specialisms)14Other roles 25Other management roles (Executive Officer level)Professional roles (fisheries and scientific specialisms)19Other roles 0The number of agency staff employed in DAERA’s Marine and Fisheries Directorate is 24 at the end of February 2026. The cost for this current employment is £559,671.70.The agency posts and vacant posts referenced above are split across functions for which I carry ministerial responsibility and functions that rest under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Defra and her ministerial team.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when recruitment for a skipper and crew for the Queen of Ulster will commence.
answer_text: A recruitment competition is in the early planning stages and is expected to commence in Summer 2026.		The Queen of Ulster and other departmental enforcement vessels will be crewed by existing fisheries staff and successful appointees from the forthcoming competition.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) when the Queen of Ulster was last surveyed; (ii) the cost of the survey; (iii) the estimated cost of bringing the vessel up to Maritime and Coastguard Agency standard; and (iv) the cost of recertification.
answer_text: The last examination for Queen of Ulster certification was completed in March 2024 and cost £1,625.83.A more extensive ongoing programme of work to recertify the Queen of Ulster, also includes maintenance, enhancement of navigation and communication and is expected to cost approximately £50,000.  The cost of examination and certification itself is expected to be similar to that of March 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Queen of Ulster has been checked for Diesel Bug.
answer_text: The Queen of Ulster has been inspected for diesel bug.  It was detected in one of the vessel’s fuel tanks in April 2025.  All required remediation work was carried out and completed within one week of detection.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) when the Maritime and Coastguard Agency certification for the Queen of Ulster lapsed; and (ii) the rationale as to why this was permitted to happen.
answer_text: The Fisheries Protection Vessel (FPV) Queen of Ulster certification lapsed in February 2025 due to issues including the new Maritime and Coastguard Agency requirements, under the new Workboat Code 3, which have come into force.  These new requirements, in relation to vessel construction, equipment, and operational standards, mean that a programme of work is needed to bring the FPV back into certification.A significant amount of progress has been made to date, and it is anticipated that certification will be achieved by the end of summer 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of days his Department has chartered outside agencies to police fishing in Northern Ireland territorial waters in each of the last two years, including the cost of doing so.
answer_text: DAERA Marine and Fisheries Group has not chartered any outside agencies to police fishing within Northern Ireland territorial waters in each of the last two years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of speeding offences detected by each fixed speed camera, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer to AQW 42613 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any support schemes are available through his Department to assist beekeepers experiencing colony losses.
answer_text: Whilst my Department does not presently operate a support scheme specifically to assist beekeepers experiencing colony losses my officials provide guidance and advice on disease prevention and control which is available here: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bee-health  In addition, my Department provides for a testing and diagnostic service via the Agrifood and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) assisting in minimising colony losses from disease outbreaks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has made any assessment of the impact of the 2025-26 wet and mild winter on bee populations.
answer_text: My Department does not make specific assessments on the impact of weather patterns on the bee population. However, the Agrifood and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) conduct an annual survey which assesses overall colony losses during winter in Northern Ireland. The survey for 2025-26 has not yet concluded, therefore the figures are not available at this time. Results from previous surveys can be found here: https://www.afbini.gov.uk/article/bee-health.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of fixed speed cameras currently installed on roads, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The policy related to speed detection cameras is the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership, and I suggest that you contact the Partnership directly for this information.Their contact details are:Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership42 Montgomery RoadBelfast BT6 9LD

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total revenue generated from fixed speed camera detections, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer to AQW 42613.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has made of the reliability and uptime of public electric vehicle charge points, across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The provision and operation of EV charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity in the North. Responsibility for the maintenance, development and utilisation of charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any funding that has been allocated by her Department for electric vehicle infrastructure, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The provision of EV infrastructure is a commercially led enterprise in the North. As part of a funding initiative to assist local councils that made an application for funding under the 2021-22 Office for Zero Emission Vehicles’ On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme, my Department provided £449,450 to a consortium of nine councils, led by Derry City and Strabane District Council and £21,235 to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council in support of the installation of charge points in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any engagement her Department has had with the UK Government, regarding access to UK-wide funding schemes for electric vehicle charging infrastructure, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My officials maintain regular and ongoing engagement with the British Department for Transport and the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV) on all matters relating to the transition to electric vehicles. This engagement has included discussions on access to their EV charging schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the next steps in proposals for the redevelopment of Newtownards Library.
answer_text: Following my Department’s approval of the Outline Business Case for the redevelopment of Newtownards Library, Libraries NI will now reengage with Ards and North Down Borough Council to complete the design development phase, in parallel with the Council’s plans for the adjoining community facilities.Once design development is complete and statutory planning approvals are secured, the project will move to tender stage to appoint a contractor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether Libraries NI business case in respect of Newtownards Library has been approved by his Department.
answer_text: The Outline Business Case for the redevelopment of Newtownards Library was approved by my department on 6 March.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the proportion of rural bus shelters installed prior to 2005 that remain non-compliant with current accessibility standards.
answer_text: Translink currently manages 1,460 bus shelters on behalf of my Department. This figure relates solely to shelters under Translink’s responsibility and does not include any bus shelters owned or maintained by local councils. Of the 1,460 bus shelters managed by Translink, 729 were installed before 2005. While all shelters have the necessary statutory consents, many of the pre 2005 installations do not meet current accessibility standards. In addition, a substantial number of bus shelters across the North fall outside the responsibility of my Department and Translink as they are owned and maintained by local councils.  Translink have submitted a business case to replace and upgrade 221 bus shelters across the network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of pre-1970 reinforced concrete pedestrian footbridges.
answer_text: My Department has identified five reinforced concrete pedestrian footbridges constructed prior to 1970. These structures are:Bridge No. 10432 – Ballykeel Footbridge, Mid & East AntrimBridge No. 40971 – Craigavon Lakes Footbridge, Armagh City, Banbridge & CraigavonBridge No. 40972 – Cherry Footpath Footbridge (Drumgor West Lakes), Armagh City, Banbridge & CraigavonBridge No. 40983 – Navan Street Footbridge, Armagh, Armagh City, Banbridge & CraigavonBridge No. 62009 – Mill Race Footbridge, Derry & StrabaneAll five structures are subject to a routine inspection every 2 years in accordance with the inspection Standard CS 450 “Inspection of Highway Structures”.The most recent inspections of these footbridges were carried out between March and September 2024. These inspections confirmed that all five structures were in good overall condition, with only minor defects identified, and no evidence of deterioration or other concerns requiring further investigation or assessment.My Department will continue to monitor these structures through the established inspection programme and will undertake further investigation or assessment should it be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has made of the structural integrity of pre-1970s reinforced concrete pedestrian footbridges.
answer_text: My Department has identified five reinforced concrete pedestrian footbridges constructed prior to 1970. These structures are:Bridge No. 10432 – Ballykeel Footbridge, Mid & East AntrimBridge No. 40971 – Craigavon Lakes Footbridge, Armagh City, Banbridge & CraigavonBridge No. 40972 – Cherry Footpath Footbridge (Drumgor West Lakes), Armagh City, Banbridge & CraigavonBridge No. 40983 – Navan Street Footbridge, Armagh, Armagh City, Banbridge & CraigavonBridge No. 62009 – Mill Race Footbridge, Derry & StrabaneAll five structures are subject to a routine inspection every 2 years in accordance with the inspection Standard CS 450 “Inspection of Highway Structures”.The most recent inspections of these footbridges were carried out between March and September 2024. These inspections confirmed that all five structures were in good overall condition, with only minor defects identified, and no evidence of deterioration or other concerns requiring further investigation or assessment.My Department will continue to monitor these structures through the established inspection programme and will undertake further investigation or assessment should it be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when Veoza will be available on prescription as an alternative to hormone replacement therapy.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.On 11 March 2026, NICE published its final draft guidance recommending the use of fezolinetant (Veoza®), as an option to treat moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms caused by menopause when hormone replacement therapy (HRT) is unsuitable. A date for final guidance is to be confirmed.When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the proportion of children that left custody with an agreed resettlement plan in place, in the last five years.
answer_text: Children are admitted to custody in one of three ways; Police and Criminal Evidence Act (PACE), remanded or committed/sentenced.  Under PACE, Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre (JJC) can act as a place of safety where children are held for one or two nights until the next available court. These children would not usually have a resettlement plan.  Where children are remanded (particularly for more than five days), all will have an agreed resettlement plan. All children who are sentenced/committed will also have an agreed resettlement plan in place. This has been consistent practice over the last five years.On admission, each child remanded or sentenced / committed is allocated a Case Management Team within the JJC who ensure that links to the community are maintained and regular Case Management Reviews are held with outside partners to ensure an effective transition back into the community. Most children who are admitted to the JJC are already known to the Youth Justice Agency (YJA) via the community delivery team, Youth Justice Services (YJS). This supportive relationship continues throughout their time in JJC. YJS is significantly involved in both agreeing and delivering the resettlement plan when the child returns home.Internal educational and health services within JJC also contribute to community transition by liaising with external partners to ensure continuity of these services on release.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment that has made of the impact of rising fuel prices, arising from the conflict in Iran, on his Departmental budget.
answer_text: My Department anticipates that the rising oil prices will increase our costs, particularly in areas like transport, healthcare logistics and heating, which will put further pressure on the Health budget which is already facing significant challenges.While we expect the increase in oil prices can be managed within our 2025-26 budget, there is the potential for this to have significant adverse impact on our 2026-27 budget depending on how long the conflict continues. It’s too soon to fully assess the impact, but the Department will continue to monitor this position as we develop our 2026-27 financial plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment that has made of the impact of rising fuel prices, arising from the conflict in Iran, on her Departmental budget.
answer_text: Whilst future fuel prices and their impact on energy costs remain uncertain there is some hedging in place within my Department and the ALBs which will help mitigate the impact in the short term. Officials will continue to assess the situation and any resulting impacts on budgets should the current volatility continue in the longer term.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made of the Kinship Zones pilot scheme being delivered in England.
answer_text: The Kinship Zones pilot is being established initially in 7 local authorities in England, for a period of up to three and a half years, and aims to provide consistent financial support to allow more children to grow up within their existing family network.  The pilot programme is targeted at supporting informal kinship care arrangements where there is in place a Special Guardianship Order (SGO) or a ‘lives with’ Child Arrangement Order (CAO).  These orders are for children who would otherwise be in local authority care, and as part of the pilot the carers will receive a financial allowance equivalent to the National Minimum Allowance provided to kinship foster carers.As the pilot programme was only launched in England on 26 February 2026, no assessment has yet been undertaken.  However, my officials will monitor its progress and consider learning for Northern Ireland over the period of the pilot.SGOs are not currently available in Northern Ireland. The Department is aiming to introduce this new private law order, together with special guardianship support services (including financial support), during 2027/28, as part of the phased implementation of the Adoption and Children Act (NI) 2022, subject to funding being secured. The expectation is that, once SGOs have been introduced, some of those who may apply for such an Order will include kinship carers. A CAO is the English equivalent to our Article 8 Orders that are set out in the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995.  Article 8 of that Order sets out the basis for the provision of Residence Orders through family proceedings (private family law cases), which specify the living arrangements for the child. In Northern Ireland, the current framework for kinship care arrangements is set out in the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, and the Foster Placement (Children) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1996.  A kinship care placement occurs when an HSC Trust places a looked after child with a relative, friend, or other connected person who assumes full-time care.  All kinship carers who are caring for a looked after child are considered to be foster carers, and as such are eligible for the full range of financial allowance and support as other foster carers.As part of the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme, there is a wider programme of reform underway to address a range of challenges across children’s social care, which includes provision of foster care.  As part of this work, a Foster Care Strategic Plan is currently being drafted, which aims to outline how HSC Trusts will recruit and retain non-kinship foster carers, and how they will strengthen the support available to all foster carers to assist them in their important role.  This plan will inform the future direction of services, and it is currently aimed to be completed in mid 2026. A review of the financial arrangements for foster care is also underway.  When complete the review will lead to the development of a sing

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any assessment that has made of the impact of rising fuel prices, arising from the conflict in Iran, on their Departmental budget.
answer_text: Whilst future fuel prices and their impact on energy costs remain uncertain, the Department has access to collaborative procurement contracts for oil, gas and electricity. As a result, no significant impact on the Department’s 2025/26 budget is anticipated but is being kept under review.As part of ongoing budgetary management, risks of increases in fuel prices and travel costs will be monitored and budget impacts assessed accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any assessment that has made of the impact of rising fuel prices, arising from the conflict in Iran, on his Departmental budget.
answer_text: Rising fuel prices and their impact on energy costs will have a knock-on effect on the cost of delivering public services and therefore have a detrimental impact on an already constrained budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment that has made of the impact of rising fuel prices, arising from the conflict in Iran, on her Departmental budget.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the potential impact this issue will have across all utilities and fuel requirements, and business areas/energy managers are currently reviewing the position.Whilst our previous analysis for the draft multi-year budgets will have included some forecast cost increases around pay, price and inflation, these increases will not have been able to account for these latest impacts.  Given that energy costs continue to be extremely volatile, and whilst costs may increase further if tensions continue to rise across the Middle East, it is unclear at this point for how long both this volatility and the associated price increases will be sustained. On that basis, at this point, we are unable to quantify the impact rising fuel prices will have on my Department’s budget.  However, my officials remain engaged with colleagues both within and across the Department and its arm’s length bodies, as well as with colleagues across the wider NICS, in order to seek to understand the impact of these increases on my Departmental budget and that of the wider NICS position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any assessment that has made of the impact of rising fuel prices, arising from the conflict in Iran, on his Departmental budget.
answer_text: Given the emerging and volatile nature of the situation, a detailed assessment has not yet been undertaken of the impact of fuel price increases linked to the conflict in Iran on my Department’s budget. Any sustained increase in fuel and energy costs has the potential to add further pressure across the education sector and to an Education budget that is already under significant strain. The position will be kept under review as the situation develops and my priority remains protecting education provision and essential services for children, young people and families.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment that has made of the impact of rising fuel prices, arising from the conflict in Iran, on his Departmental budget.
answer_text: Fuel prices are being monitored and no significant impact on my Department’s budget is anticipated for 2025-26.  However, if fuel prices remain high, this is likely to place additional budget pressures on my Department in 2026-27 and this will be reviewed as part of the Monitoring Round exercises in the next financial year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment that has made of the impact of rising fuel prices, arising from the conflict in Iran, on her Departmental budget.
answer_text: Sustained increases in fuel and energy costs have the potential to place additional pressures on my Departments budget. Any impacts will be considered as part of the department’s ongoing budget monitoring processes and future budget planning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment that has made of the impact of rising fuel prices, arising from the conflict in Iran, on his Departmental budget.
answer_text: Lead responsibility for oil and gas prices falls to the Department for the Economy.  At this stage, my Department has not identified any direct or immediate impact on its budget.  This will be kept under review as the budget-setting process for 2026/27 progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of incidents of self-harm, involving children in custody, up to 18 years of age, recorded in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The factors associated with self-harm are complex and all incidents of self-harm in Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre (JJC) are recorded. A suite of Standard Operating Procedures are in place to prevent and minimise self-harm but some instances do occur. Few children do self-harm; however, sadly, those that do tend to do so often. Number of incidents in the last five years:2020/2021 - 542021/2022 - 802022/2023 - 172023/2024 - 452024/2025 - 46

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the proportion of children currently in custody, up to 18 years of age, who have identified special educational needs; and (ii) any specialist support that is provided to these children.
answer_text: Given the low number of children currently in custody, it is not possible to answer this question without disclosing personal information that could identify individuals.  Education in Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre (JJC) is funded by the Department of Justice and delivered by the Education Authority (EA) under the auspices of Education Other Than At School (EOTAS).  While in the JJC, children with Special Educational Needs (SEN) have an addendum made to the SEN Personal Learning Plan (PLP) held by their community education setting which details the specialist support they will receive while in the JJC. If the child no longer attends a community education placement, then teachers in Woodlands will create a PLP. Typical provisions made include:1 to 1 or small group teachingPersonal tutoringIndividualised timetableTargeted interventions e.g. literacy support Input from the Mental Health teamOccupational Therapy input

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the educational provision currently available to young people in custody at Hydebank Wood College; and (ii) the average number of hours of education delivered per week.
answer_text: In conjunction with Belfast Met, the Northern Ireland Prison Service provides a varied and progressive curriculum to prisoners that takes account of the unique needs of both the young male and young female population at Hydebank Wood College.The curriculum offers accredited educational, vocational and employability skills training, from Entry Level to Level 3, in subjects ranging from Essential Skills in Numeracy, Literacy and Information Computer Technology, Art, Barbering, Horticulture, and Light Vehicle Maintenance to Hair and Beauty, Hospitality and Catering and Joinery.Individuals can also undertake higher education through distance learning with the Open University. Over the course of a week, education staff typically deliver 62 days of learning, which equates to 310 hours per week.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of children up to 18 years of age detained in sentenced custody, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre (the JJC) is the sole custodial facility for children (aged 10 to 17) in Northern Ireland.  A breakdown of children sentenced in the JJC over the last five financial years is available in the Youth Justice Agency Annual Workload Statistics 2024-25 publication.  This is available on the department website at the following address YJA Annual Workload Statistics. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of children diverted from custody, up to 18 years of age, through youth conferencing, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Youth Justice Services directorate, within the Youth Justice Agency, provides a range of services for children, their families, communities and victims across Northern Ireland.  One of the key services provided are Youth Conferences.  When a child admits guilt at court, they can receive a court ordered Youth Conference, which supports diversion from custody by requiring the child to meet with victims, acknowledge their offending behaviour, and comply with an agreed plan aimed both at reducing reoffending and reintegration back into their community.  Figures relating to court ordered youth conferences are available in the Youth Justice Agency Annual Workload Statistics 2024-25 publication.  This is available on the department website at the following address YJA Annual Workload Statistics.  The published figures are provided in the following table.Court Ordered Youth Conferences, 2020/21 to 2024/25Financial YearCourt Ordered Youth Conference2020/212912021/223662022/233672023/243672024/25338

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there has been any evidence of the bluetongue virus in aborted cattle foetuses, since the start of the recent bluetongue outbreak.
answer_text: Bluetongue Virus Strain type 3 (BTV3) is a Notifiable Disease in Northern Ireland, and all reports of suspicion or clinical signs must be reported to the Department. On receipt of aborted foetuses, tests have been conducted to detect presence of Bluetongue Virus as part of surveillance for the disease.		There has been no detection of BTV-3 Virus in aborted foetuses presented to the official laboratory (the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute) since the start of the recent bluetongue outbreak in November 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider allowing the movement of young claves from TB-restricted herds to dedicated beef finishing units.
answer_text: The TB Partnership Steering Group’s (TBPSG) Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication, which I endorsed and published in April 2025, included a commitment to review the criteria for the establishment and approval of Alternative Control Herds (ACH), essentially beef finishing units which could help alleviate the pressure of over-stocking in herds suffering a bovine TB breakdown.  While criteria for such herds have been in place since 2013, and occasional expressions of interest were received regarding establishing an ACH, no such herd has yet been set up in Northern Ireland.Since the Blueprint was published, officials have been engaging with TBPSG members and wider industry to discuss how barriers to uptake might be overcome while also taking into account statutory requirements under the Animal Health Law and the department’s requirement to compensate for cattle slaughtered for disease control.  I understand that this work is nearing completion and I expect to be briefed by officials on this matter in the coming weeks on prospective next steps.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider allowing the movement of young calves from TB-restricted herds to dedicated calf rearing units.
answer_text: The TB Partnership Steering Group’s (TBPSG) Bovine TB in Northern Ireland: Blueprint for Eradication, which I endorsed and published in April 2025, included a commitment to review the criteria for the establishment and approval of Alternative Control Herds (ACH) as a potential means to help alleviate the pressure of over-stocking in herds suffering a bovine TB breakdown, while maintaining robust biosecurity.Since the Blueprint was published, officials have been engaging with TBPSG members and wider industry to discuss how criteria for dedicated biosecure calf rearing units might be developed while also considering statutory requirements under the Animal Health Law and the department’s requirement to compensate for cattle slaughtered for disease control.  I understand that this work is nearing completion, and I expect to be briefed by officials on this matter in the coming weeks on prospective next steps.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what the presence of the bluetongue virus in aborted cattle foetuses would indicate.
answer_text: The presence of Bluetongue virus (BTV) in aborted foetuses would indicate infection of the dam during pregnancy resulting in trans-placental infection of the foetus. Infection during the gestation period can result in the birth of a normal calf or alternatively result in either silent or visible evidence of abortion. Abortions, stillbirths and congenital abnormalities are clinical signs of BTV infection. Other infectious and non-infectious causes of abortion are common in Northern Ireland, however the presence of the BTV virus in an aborted foetus would be strongly indicative that BTV was the most probable cause of the abortion.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions his Department has had with the Isle of Man Government regarding research being conducted by Bangor University on the Western Irish Sea nephrops grounds, between 3 March 2026 and 8 March 2026.
answer_text: My department has had no discussion with the Isle of Man Government in respect of this research. However, I understand this research is being undertaken by the RSS Discovery, including a team from Bangor University, working on a Natural Environment Research Council programme. This is investigating seabed carbon, and it has received the necessary permissions to sample in Northern Ireland waters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has undertaken any analysis of the number of approved wind turbine projects that are currently unable to connect to the grid, due to capacity limitations.
answer_text: Connection applications and capacity assessments for wind turbine projects are operational matters for NIE Networks.My department is working closely with the system operator, SONI, NIE Networks and the Utility Regulator to address dispatch down, including capacity-related constraints, through ongoing monitoring, engagement and policy development.SONI manages dispatch down levels and is delivering the Dispatch Down Action Plan, with the support of my Department and the Utility Regulator. I established the Grid Development Monitoring Group with senior representatives from SONI, NIE Networks and the Utility Regulator to help accelerate delivery of essential network upgrades to support power system decarbonisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures her Department is taking to reduce the curtailment of wind energy on the electricity system.
answer_text: My Department is taking forward a programme of policy, governance and infrastructure measures to reduce the curtailment of wind energy, working closely with SONI, NIE Networks and the Utility Regulator.The Department is supporting the delivery of SONI’s Dispatch Down Action Plan, which focuses on safely reducing curtailment while maintaining system security. On the delivery of grid infrastructure, I established the Grid Development Monitoring Group to drive progress on strategic transmission and distribution projects which support the delivery of the Executive’s Energy Strategy.The Department is also progressing policy work on energy storage and interconnection, and the North South Interconnector remains a strategic priority as it is essential to reducing curtailment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has been made of the economic cost associated with wind generation curtailment, over the last five years.
answer_text: Information on the volume of wind generation curtailed is published annually by the system operator, SONI. While this data provides transparency on the scale of curtailment, it does not translate directly into a single monetary value, as the economic impacts depend on system conditions, market prices, generation mix and network constraints at the time curtailment occurs.My Department’s focus is on addressing the underlying causes of curtailment. This includes engagement with SONI, NIE Networks and the Utility Regulator on grid investment, system operability, interconnection, energy storage and demand-side flexibility, all of which are intended to reduce curtailment and improve overall system efficiency.Reducing curtailment remains important to ensure the electricity system makes the best use of low-cost renewable generation, delivering lower costs for consumers and supporting sustainable economic growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any modelling her Department has carried out, regarding the total number of additional wind turbines required to meet the 2030 renewable electricity target.
answer_text: Modelling efforts have focused on the additional renewable capacity required to meet the target from a mix of renewable technologies, not a single technology.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount of wind generation curtailed in Northern Ireland, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: SONI publishes annual data on wind generation dispatch-down in its Annual Renewable Energy Constraint and Curtailment Reports. Based on this published data, the amount of wind generation dispatched down here over the five years from 2020 to 2024 was 461GWh in 2020, 185GWh in 2021, 291GWh in 2022, 539GWh in 2023 and 915GWh in 2024.The increase in 2023 and 2024 reflects a combination of factors rather than any single cause. These included the closure of the coal plant at Kilroot, which significantly reduced costs and emissions for consumers but also led to more wind curtailment due to higher minimum generation levels at remaining thermal plant. Another significant factor was increased Moyle Interconnector imports due to lower electricity costs in Scotland. This helps lower costs for electricity consumers here at the expense of higher dispatch down of local renewable wind energy. My Department is addressing this with a coordinated programme of action, including overseeing delivery key projects in the £2.23 billion of network investment under NIE Networks’ RP7 programme, through the Grid Development Monitoring Group, supporting the delivery of SONI’s Dispatch Down Action Plan, and progressing policy work on energy storage, interconnection and demand-side flexibility. Official dispatch down figures are not yet available for 2025 but are expected to be lower than in 2024, showing a positive impact from this work to date. This was reflected in year to end of 2025 percentage of renewable electricity rising to 47% from 44%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any educational provision available to children in custody at Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre, including the average number of hours of education, delivered per week, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Education in Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre is funded by the Department of Justice and delivered by the Education Authority (EA) under the auspices of Education Other Than At School (EOTAS) with vocational subjects delivered via a contract with People 1st. EA provide 5 full time teachers and People 1st provide 4 part time vocational instructors. There is a fully functioning school onsite.The school day runs from 9.15am to 3.20pm. 30 hours of education and vocational subjects are delivered to each young person per week. This provision has been the same across the last 5 years.Attendance at school within Woodland’s Juvenile Justice Centre across the last 5 years has ranged from 86% to 98%.By way of a snapshot in 2024/2025 there were 5126 lessons delivered by EA teachers and 1742 lessons delivered by People 1st.

answer_text: Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre (the JJC) is the sole custodial facility for children (aged 10 to 17) in Northern Ireland.  A breakdown of children held on remand and sentenced in the JJC over the last five financial years is available in the Youth Justice Agency Annual Workload Statistics 2024-25 publication. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for kinship carers to receive (i) financial; (ii) therapeutic; and (iii) practical support, following the placement of a child, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Through the Statutory Functions (SF) report received annually from each HSC Trust, the information provided below details the number of children residing in unapproved kinship placements, approved kinship placements, and the number of children in unregulated kinship placements on 30th September 2025, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust. Children in unapproved kinship placements Children in approved kinship placementsChildren in unregulated kinship placementsTotalBHSCT82443165690NHSCT39332141512SEHSCT33174159366SHSCT2324733303WHSCT3133713381TOTAL20815335112252A kinship placement is defined as unapproved when the child has been in placement for less than 12 weeks and a kinship assessment is in process. The allowances paid to foster carers including kinship foster carers in Northern Ireland (NI) are for costs associated with day to day care; and are outlined within the annual published Fostering Model Scheme. The current allowances rates are published on the Department of Health website and are available to view at Foster care allowances Foster care allowances | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk).Finally, a review of the financial arrangements for foster care is underway.  When complete the review will lead to the development of a single framework for the provision of financial support to foster carers.  The framework will also be published, providing greater clarity to foster carers, including kinship carers, on the financial support which is available to them.The Department does not collate data or information from the HSC Trusts in relation to the average waiting times to access support for kinship carers. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any policy framework governing kinship care, including any plans for reform.
answer_text: Kinship care is a particular type of foster care, where a looked after child is cared for by a family member or close family friend.  It plays a vital role in our children’s social care sector, with over half of children in care now in a kinship care placement. The current framework for kinship care arrangements is set out in the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, and the Foster Placement (Children) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1996.  My Department has also produced Minimum Kinship Care Standards, which recognise the unique role played by family and friends carers in the lives of children separated from parents.As part of the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme, established in 2023 in response to the issues which led to the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services, there is a wider programme of reform underway to address a range of challenges across children’s social care, which includes provision of foster care.  As part of this work, a Foster Care Strategic Plan is currently being drafted, which aims to outline how HSC Trusts will recruit and retain non-kinship foster carers, and how they will strengthen the support available to all foster carers to assist them in their important role.  This plan will inform the future direction of services, and it is currently aimed to be completed in mid 2026. Work is also continuing on the development of draft Minimum Standards for Fostering Services in Northern Ireland. When complete, the new standards will incorporate and replace the existing Minimum Kinship Care Standards.  It is anticipated that the new standards will provide greater clarity and transparency on the arrangements for foster care services, and provide the minimum level of support which all foster carers can expect to receive, irrespective of any previous relationship with the child. The draft standards were subject to consultation in 2025, and my officials are currently analysing the consultation responses and identifying any arising issues submitted in the responses for further consideration by the Department. I have already approved the making of the new foster regulations, to be brought into operation during 2026/27, which also introduce a requirement for independent fostering agencies to be registered with, and inspected by, RQIA.  The regulations will also allow independent agencies to carry out assessments of foster carers (including kinship carers where appropriate) on behalf of Health and Social Care Trusts, streamlining the current processes.Finally, a review of the financial arrangements for foster care is underway.  When complete the review will lead to the development of a single framework for the provision of financial support to foster carers.  The framework will also be published, providing greater clarity to foster carers, including kinship carers, on the financial support which is available to them.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has undertaken any consultation with kinship carers or representative organisations regarding policy development, in the last two years.
answer_text: During the development of draft Minimum Standards for Fostering Services in Northern Ireland, a working group was established in early 2024 to work collaboratively to develop the standards, prior to public consultation.  Membership of this group included representative organisations (The Fostering Network and foster carer unions) and a kinship carer.In addition, The Fostering Network carried out engagement on behalf of the Department in relation to the introduction of fostering standards and care planning, with both kinship and non-kinship carers.  As part of this engagement, officials attended an in-person event in October 2024 and spoke directly to kinship and non-kinship carers about their experiences.  This engagement informed the development of the standards at an early stage.The draft Minimum Standards for Fostering Services in Northern Ireland were subject to public consultation in 2025.  The Fostering Network undertook a consultation with their members and their response to the consultation included representations from kinship carers (166 people submitted a response to the Fostering Network of which 19% were kinship carers).  My officials are currently analysing the consultation responses, identifying any arising issues submitted in the responses for further consideration by the Department. The Fostering Network is also a member of the Fostering Workstream of the Children’s Social Care Services Strategic Reform Programme, established in response to the issues which led to the Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services.  The workstream is developing a Foster Care Strategic Plan, which will set out actions to support the recruitment, retention and support of all foster carers, both kinship and non-kinship, over the coming years.  It is anticipated that work on the plan will be completed by mid 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of prisoners released early under any early release or temporary release scheme, including details of any criteria applied. in each of the last five years,
answer_text: The figures below show the number of prisoners released early under the Conditional Early Release Scheme, Compassionate Early Release, Covid-19 Temporary Release Scheme, Early Removal Scheme, and the Transition Leave Scheme in each of the last five years.YearConditional Early ReleaseCompassionate Early ReleaseCovid-19 Temporary ReleaseEarly Removal SchemeTransition Leave20213805836020224510670202349005992024391011110202546001497     				     The Conditional Early Release Scheme provides for the release of certain categories of determinate custodial sentence prisoners on a Probation Board supervised licence up to 135 days before their custody expiry date.  Applicants must satisfy a range of criteria including a number of statutory and non-statutory offence related exclusions, the latter designed to maximise public confidence in the scheme.  Successful candidates must also demonstrate that they pose a low likelihood of re-offending; that they have been of good behaviour whilst in custody; that they have approved, stable and supportive accommodation in the community; and that they have complied fully with all conditions imposed during any periods of temporary release.Prisoners may be released on licence on compassionate grounds in exceptional circumstances.  One of the following two criteria must be met before such a release may be considered:the prisoner is suffering from a terminal illness, and death is likely to occur soon; orthe prisoner is permanently incapacitated, and they require a level of care that cannot be provided in a prison environment. The Covid-19 Temporary Release Scheme, which ran from April 2020 to the end of March 2021, allowed for the release of certain categories of determinate custodial sentence prisoners up to three months before their custody expiry date.  Applicants had to satisfy a range of criteria including a number of exclusions designed to maximise public confidence in the scheme. Successful candidates had to have approved, stable and supportive accommodation in the community. The Early Removal Scheme is voluntary.  All determinate sentenced foreign national prisoners who are in prison in Northern Ireland and who are liable for deportation from the United Kingdom on the expiry of the custodial element of their sentences may be considered for removal up to 135 days early. Those prisoners serving an extended custodial sentence, an indeterminate sentence or a life sentence cannot be removed under the scheme.  Prisoners must be serving a sentence of at least six months imprisonment to be considered.The Transition Leave Scheme provides for the temporary release of some prisoners just before the end of the custodial part of their sentences.  It is designed to promote their resettlement and transition back into society and to reduce their risk of reoffending by giving them additional time to re-engage with a range of support services in the community before their formal release.  Prisoners applying for release under the sche

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients waiting longer than five years for elective treatment, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) any specific interventions that have been implemented to address these waits.
answer_text: Waiting list statistics, broken down by Trust, are published each quarter on the Department of Health’s webpage. Please see the link below for the official statistics, where information on elective surgery waits can be found:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/inpatient-waiting-times#toc-0 The Elective Care Framework sets out the key actions to drive down our waiting lists including the expansion of core surgical capacity across a range of specialties including general surgery, ENT, gynaecology and urology. The expansion of recurrent capacity has been supported by the introduction of the new waiting list reimbursement scheme and the continued use of both in-house and independent sector waiting list initiatives.		Despite the current financial constraints, my Department has made significant progress in relation to the number of patients who are projected to be waiting four years by the end of March 2026. Between March 2025–January 2026, there has been a reduction of 47% for outpatients, 61% for inpatient and day-case, and 79% for the named procedures in the Elective Care Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of electronic monitoring tags currently in use.
answer_text: As of 3rd March 2026, 443 electronic monitoring tags were in use across Northern Ireland providing curfew monitoring for individuals on court bail, or those supervised by the Probation Board for Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the cost per electronic monitoring tag; and (ii) the running costs per tag.
answer_text: The cost per tag information is commercially sensitive. The Department of Justice believes that releasing information on device costs would prejudice, or likely prejudice Buddi Ltd’s (the provider of the monitoring equipment) commercial interests.AQW 41661/22-27 provides the costs of Electronic Monitoring contracts for the last five financial years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount that has been spent on electronic monitoring contracts, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The amount spent on electronic monitoring contracts in each of the last five financial years is set out in the table below:Time periodSpend2021 – 2022£1,472,9792022 – 2023£1,678,1702023 – 2024£1,503,7682024 – 2025£1,807,0682025 – February 26£1,998,222Total£8,460,207

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the percentage of the overall health budget that is allocated to mental health services; and (ii) how this compares with commitments set out in the Mental Health Strategy 2021–2031.
answer_text: In 2025/26 a total of £428.7m has been allocated to mental health Programme of Care which is approximately 7% of the overall budget allocated to programmes of care.The Mental Health Strategy 2021–2031 was supported by a Funding Plan estimating a total additional investment need of £1.2 billion over ten years. Based on current health budget levels, this equates to approximately 2% of the overall health budget over the decade. However, as at 2024/25, a total of £12.3m has been allocated, representing 16% of what was envisaged by this point in the lifetime of the Strategy.  Figures for 2025/26 are currently being validated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any guidance that exists for schools seeking to develop income-generating activities; and (ii) whether any additional flexibilities are being considered.
answer_text: (i) Guidance for schools on income-generating activities is set out in the  Governor Guide:Chapter 8 (School Finances), paragraph 8.2 states that schools are free to raise extra funds through sponsorship schemes, voluntary contributions from parents and fund-raising activities.  Chapter 18 (School Fees), paragraphs 18.35 - 18.36 outline the legal requirements governing requests for voluntary contributions by a school’s Board of Governors (BoG). Chapter 20 (School Premises), paragraphs 20.14 - 20.20 provide guidance on the use of school premises by third parties, including the BoG responsibilities in setting charges and compliance with the relevant policies and procedures.(ii) I am content that the existing flexibilities for schools to generate additional income are sufficient and there are no plans to introduce further changes at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of jobs currently supported in the low-carbon and renewable energy sector; and (ii) any anticipated growth over the next 10 years.
answer_text: (i)  The ONS survey ‘Low Carbon and Renewable Energy Economy, UK: 2024’ estimates employment of 7,100 (FTE) in the low carbon and renewable energy sector in the north.(ii) My department has not modelled anticipated growth in this sector over the next 10 years; however, it is worth noting that, over the 10-year period from 2015 to 2024, the north recorded the largest percentage increase in employment (from 4,400 to 7,100 FTEs) – compared to Scotland, England and Wales. My Low Carbon / Net Zero Sector Action Plan sets out the range of key activities my department and its delivery partners are taking forward to grow the sector, building on its innovative potential.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the total expenditure in research and development as a percentage of GDP in Northern Ireland, in each of the last five years; and (ii) how this compares to other regions in the UK.
answer_text: According to the most recent estimates from the Office for National Statistics (ONS), total expenditure on research and development (R&D) as a percentage of GDP in the North was 2.2% in 2023 (latest year available). It should be noted that R&D intensities for years before 2022 are not directly comparable with those after due to differing methods used to estimate R&D spending by businesses.
