question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on the School Enhancement Project application for Killyleagh Primary school.
answer_text: On 8 May 2018, Killyleagh Integrated Primary School was announced to advance in design under SEP.  The project is being managed by the Education Authority (EA) and will deliver an extension to the existing school site to address the deficiencies identified in the schedule of accommodation.  This will include reconfiguration of existing spaces in the school building, to align as far practically possible with the DE Handbook recommendations.My Department and the EA are currently reviewing the costs for the project to allow it to move forward to RIBA Stage 3 (Developed Design).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will develop a strategy to address rural litter and fly-tipping and the associated risks to wildlife and livestock.
answer_text: DAERA leads a number of initiatives to reduce litter and fly-tipping, including: the draft Plastic Pollution Plan to support the elimination of plastic pollution; the Deposit Return Scheme (to be launched in October 2027) which will provide a financial incentive for consumers to return empty drinks containers for recycling; and the Fly tipping Protocol which clarifies the responsibilities of local councils and the NI Environment Agency, to ensure fly-tipping incidents are addressed quickly, minimising environmental harm and community impact.  My department is also committed to developing Northern Ireland’s first Litter Strategy as resources permit.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of days at sea the fisheries protection vessel, the Queen of Ulster, has been deployed, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The DAERA Fisheries Protection Vessel, the Queen of Ulster, has been deployed at sea in each of the last 5 years as follows: 2021 – 71 deployments, 2022 – 74 deployments, 2023 – 57 deployments, 2024 – 33 deployments and in 2025 – 0 deployments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether a register is kept of schools who own their own minibus.
answer_text: Neither my department nor the Education Authority maintain a register of schools who own a minibus.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration that has been given to developing an agricultural apprenticeship programme.
answer_text: My Department, through the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) already delivers Agricultural Apprenticeship programmes at Level 2 and Level 3.  These are funded through the Department for Economy’s Apprenticeship scheme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount expended by each DFI Roads Section Office for the cleaning of road signs, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular inspections of all public roads and footways to ensure that essential response maintenance is identified and completed as necessary. During these inspections all defects are noted, including signs requiring cleaning to improve their visibility.Defective, damaged or missing signs, as well as those needing to be cleaned, are categorised as urgent or non-urgent work with priority given to mandatory and warning signs. Cleaning of other signs is carried out as permitted by availability of resources.My Department currently has a number of Environmental Contracts in place which include for sign cleaning as an element within the contract.  To provide the specific costs relating to sign cleaning only would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the Department, and this would likely involve disproportionate costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of environmental enforcement cases referred for prosecution, in each of the last five years, broken down by whether the perpetrator was (i) a public body; or (ii) a private individual or entity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total amount spent on agency and locum staff, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: HSC Trust expenditure on agency staff is incurred to ensure that safe and effective services are sustained.  The use of agency staff in HSC, therefore, contributes importantly to maintaining service provision to patients and clients. A Regional Agency Reduction Implementation and Planning Group has been established to co-ordinate the improvement of staff utilisation, with the aim of reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend. The main function of the Group is to develop and implement a plan, with local and regional commitment and coordination, to safely facilitate, and manage the service impact of, reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend.Significant progress has already been made since the implementation of the new nursing & midwifery framework with expenditure of ‘off-framework’ agency for nurses & midwives reducing by £120m against the base year 2022/23. A new framework aimed at reducing medical & dental locum costs is due to come into effect at the start of March 2026. Based on current expenditure, the costs for both nursing & midwifery and medical & dental locums in 2025/26 is expected to be lower than 2024/25.  This is a positive reversal of the previous trend.Reducing agency spend is a key priority and not just because of the costs involved; HSC staff have been clear that excessive agency use is unfair and impacts on morale and more importantly, having consistent clinical teams which also enhances patient safetyAlongside this work the Department’s strategic approach to workforce development, supported by sustained increased investment in pre-registration education and training, has facilitated a 18.7% (+10,630) increase in whole time equivalent staff in post across the HSC in Northern Ireland between March 2018 (56,803) and September 2025 (67,432).   This includes a 31.7% (+1,331 wte) increase in medical and dental staff, a 19.0% (+2,875 wte) increase in registered nursing and midwifery staff and a 25.9% (+2,104 wte) increase in professional and technical staff, 1,017 (+23.9%) more wte allied health professionals and 971 (+25.8%) more wte social workers, directly employed by the HSC.The total amount spent on agency and locum staff, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust is detailed below.TRUST2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25BHSCT£95,312,025£103,397,070£132,983,733£128,266,501£135,223,452NHSCT£50,691,410£64,405,384£80,139,926£76,703,615£72,026,403SEHSCT£36,124,468£39,389,422£43,775,974£51,798,418£52,760,418SHSCT£50,648,488£57,989,599£68,092,049£56,642,732£58,035,908WHSCT£44,319,740£50,542,932£64,271,905£61,419,346£62,008,073NIAS£5,152,313£4,688,649£4,290,200£2,096,210£1,923,806TOTAL£282,248,445£320,413,056£393,553,787£376,926,822£381,978,060Source HSC Trusts

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the anticipated timeframe for the construction of the section of the Eastern Distributor Road in Newtownards, between the Bowtown Road and the Portaferry Road.
answer_text: I understand that two sections of the developerled Eastern Distributor Road scheme in Newtownards, between the Bangor Road and Movilla Road, have commenced, while a further section between Movilla Road and Bowtown Road has yet to be progressed by the relevant developer.The Department’s proposed section of the route, between Bowtown Road and the Portaferry Road, is intended to complement the wider Eastern Distributor Road and is planned to be taken forward once there is sufficient progress on the adjoining developerled sections. As a result, it is not currently possible to provide a definitive timetable for construction of this section.I can confirm that the scheme remains within my Department’s forward planning programme and has progressed through the preliminary options stage. Any further development towards construction will depend on the pace of progress by developers, the Department’s wider scheme priorities and the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any role they have undertaken, in the development of a cross-departmental strategy, for maximising non-tax income generation.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any assessment that has been undertaken of the scope for additional income generation across Departments, through (i) enhanced cost recovery; (ii) commercial activity; or (iii) improved asset management.
answer_text: It is important that all Ministers and their departments routinely examine their income generation measures to ensure that income generation is appropriate, adheres to relevant public expenditure guidance including MPMNI, and that relevant services are operating at full cost recovery where appropriate.My officials are currently undertaking a Strategic Income Review which provides a comprehensive overview of income generation opportunities available to the Executive, and includes information gathered from all departments to identify and examine both existing and additional income generation measures. The Review also refers to the work being undertaken Strategic Asset Management Unit on the new Strategic Asset Management Planning Framework, which is introducing a consistent, evidence-based approach to estate planning across departments. This includes improved use of the Government Land and Property Register (GLPR), stronger governance, long term planning for disposals and investment and opportunities for collaboration and better utilisation of the public sector estate.The Review is not a policy document recommending specific changes, but rather a strategic overview to inform Executive and Ministerial decision-making.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any consideration has been given to offering incentive payments to GPs to prescribe weight-loss medication or to refer patients on to weight-loss programmes, similar to those announced in England.
answer_text: In May last year, I announced plans for the establishment of a Regional Obesity Management Service (ROMS) in Northern Ireland to provide support to individuals who are struggling with obesity or being overweight and to provide a clinically approved pathway for weight management in Northern Ireland.As part of commissioned service delivery within the ROMS, adult patients will have access to weight loss medication, if clinically appropriate, as well as lifestyle support to ensure maximum effectiveness.Given the size and complexity of the patient cohort who could potentially access weight loss medication, and to ensure the required level of support to effectively tackle the root causes of obesity, the initial roll out of weight loss medication has to be carefully managed as part of a specialist obesity service. In addition, there is a need to ensure the health care professionals delivering the service have the specialist skills and knowledge to support this initial patient cohort.Therefore, in considering the commissioning and financial implications, as well as workforce capacity, weight loss medication will initially be commissioned within a specialist obesity management service delivered within ROMS. Patient numbers will be limited in phase one before increasing capacity as the service becomes fully embedded. Full implementation of this service will require consideration of financial and service requirements, including clinical capacity, specialist support and the development of a coordinated and sustainable service model and care pathways. My Department is actively working on the commissioning of this specialist service, in partnership with Health and Social Care Trusts, to put the necessary arrangements in place for delivery of phase one in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any cross-departmental work being undertaken to address long-term unemployment and barriers to labour market participation.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) leads on employability and economic inactivity, however, addressing this issue requires collaboration across multiple departments and agencies due to its complex causes. My Department is already contributing significantly to tackling these issues through a coordinated, crossdepartmental approach focused on skills, employability, inclusion and progression at a policy level and through delivery via partners in the skills and vocational education infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on any work his Department has undertaken with Northern Ireland Water to address wastewater infrastructure deficiencies contributing to nutrient pollution, in the last five years.
answer_text: I remain very concerned about the impact that inadequate wastewater infrastructure continues to have on water quality across Northern Ireland. Responsibility for investing in and upgrading this infrastructure lies with the Department for Infrastructure (DfI), while my department regulates and enforces Northern Ireland Water’s (NIW) discharges under the Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999. We assess NIW’s annual compliance with discharge consent conditions and take enforcement action where required.My department has a key statutory role in regulating discharges, setting environmental conditions, and responding to water quality pressures. The Third Cycle River Basin Management Plan strengthens controls on urban nutrient discharges, while the Environmental Improvement Plan seeks to address the continuing impact of nutrient pressures and longterm underinvestment in wastewater infrastructure.To meet our statutory duties, DAERA must ensure lawful, consistent regulation and enforcement across all sectors. A draft Executive paper proposing withdrawal from the Statement of Regulatory Principles and Intent with NIW is under consideration. The timing of DAERA’s withdrawal from SORPI is critical due to interlinked and time sensitive pressures, including the identification of the Inner Belfast Lough Shellfish Water Protected Area as a Sensitive Area as required by the Urban Wastewater Treatment Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2007, which subject to Executive approval, would require enhanced wastewater treatment to meet regulatory standards.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of cases of brain injuries in childbirth that have been reported to the Health and Social Care Trusts, in the last ten years.
answer_text: My officials have advised that this level of detailed information is not available centrally and could only be obtained at individual Trust level. There are numerous variables involved, and providing an accurate response would require an extensive review of records across multiple services. This would take a considerable amount of time and resources. Accordingly, undertaking this work would incur a disproportionate cost relative to the information sought.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has made any assessment of the financial impact on his Department following the Supreme Court judgement on CCC v Sheffield Teaching Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust on 18 February 2026.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the Supreme Court judgment in CCC v Sheffield Teaching Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust, delivered on 18 February 2026. Officials are considering the ruling and its potential implications for health and social care services.  At this stage, no final assessment of the financial impact has been concluded. However, the Department is reviewing the judgment in consultation with legal and professional advisers to determine any current operational and future budgetary consequences as the final assessment of the financial impact of the ruling becomes known.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of people (a) currently banned from libraries; and (b) who have had their borrowing privileges suspended; and (ii) the reason for these sanctions.
answer_text: (i)(a)	the number of people currently banned from libraries is two.and(i)(b) the number of people who have had their borrowing privileges suspended is 109.(ii) The reason for these sanctions is as follows:Two people have been banned for breaches of library byelaws due to unacceptable behaviour.109 people have had their borrowing privileges suspended for having items overdue by 28 days or more.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of borrowed books currently overdue for return to libraries, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The total number of borrowed books currently overdue for return to static libraries across Northern Ireland is 130,079. Mobile Library or Homecall (home delivery) data is not included as this service covers more than one constituency area and cannot be broken down by individual constituency.The table below details the number of borrowed books currently overdue, broken down by Parliamentary Constituency, as of 20 February 2026:ConstituencyNumber of overdue booksBelfast East5,537Belfast North8,500Belfast South9,738Belfast West 6,256East Antrim5,841East Derry6,712Fermanagh and South Tyrone8,328Foyle7,797Lagan Valley7,027Mid Ulster9,273Newry and Armagh7,903North Antrim6,961North Down5,735South Antrim5,091South Down8,299Strangford5,181Upper Bann6,815West Tyrone9,085

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his Department's assessment of the impact that Heraeus Medical two-month shutdown of surgical cement production at its main site will have on waiting lists.
answer_text: There are emerging national supply issues in relation to orthopaedic bone cement and work is ongoing to explore all available mitigating actions. My Department is working with the Business Services Organisation (BSO) and suppliers to better understand the wider supply outlook. This includes exploring the potential use of alternative clinically appropriate products and engaging with independent sector organisations to explore whether any support may be available during this period.There is clear, clinical consensus that available bone cement must be prioritised for urgent trauma procedures, particularly neck of femur (NoF) fractures. In order to maintain safe trauma services, Trusts may therefore need to temporarily postpone some elective orthopaedic procedures that require the use of cement. Any decisions will be taken on a Trust-by-Trust basis, depending on available stock levels and the situation will continue to be closely monitored with regular meetings scheduled with all Trusts, the BSO and the Public Health Agency.Further updates have been provided following a regional meeting between the Trusts, the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG), and the BSO. The SPPG have advised that the overall regional position has improved following receipt of additional deliveries of cement across a number of Trusts, who are working together on a regional basis to actively manage the impact of the supply constraint.Patient safety remains my overriding priority, with Belfast Health and Social Care Trust taking precautionary action to defer a small number of elective procedures in order to protect trauma services. Those patients are now being rescheduled, and no further cancellations are anticipated at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration that has been given to the requirement for the labelling of halal meat.
answer_text: There are no mandatory legal requirements for meat slaughtered according to halal requirements to be labelled as such, in either Northern Ireland, Great Britain or the European Union. In Northern Ireland, all animals are pre-stunned prior to slaughter for human consumption.A change in labelling for method of slaughter would require the passage of legislation, in light of various resource constraints and pressures on the legislative timetable, I do not consider it a pressing need to introduce new legislation in Northern Ireland to require mandatory labelling of meat as being slaughtered to halal requirements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of road maintenance backlogs outstanding for more than three years; and (ii) the estimated cost of clearing that backlog in full.
answer_text: The ‘Review of the Structural Maintenance Funding Requirements for the Department for Infrastructure (Roads)’ highlighted the need for increased spending on Capital Structural Maintenance, however the impact on the integrity and safety of the road network is as a result of the longer-term underfunding, rather than the impact of a single year. This report valued the backlog at £1.2 billion in 2018.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any obligations that rest with driving examiners, where there are dual controls in a vehicle, to use them to avoid accidents.
answer_text: There is no obligation requiring a driving examiner to use dual controls during a practical driving test.  A driving examiner is not legally in charge of the vehicle during a practical driving test and, under Regulation 12(7) of The Motor Vehicles (Driving Licences) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1996, the normal supervision requirements for provisional licence holders do not apply.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment that his Department has made of the number of animals slaughtered without being stunned for the production of meat, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: All animals slaughtered in Northern Ireland for the production of meat within the past 5 years have been stunned preslaughter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail a timeline on the lifting of the bluetongue Temporary Control Zone.
answer_text: DAERA’s Bluetongue Disease Control Framework indicates that lifting of zones will be considered based on the available epidemiological and scientific evidence.  Economic implications for the sector must also be carefully considered.  My officials have discussed these matters with key industry representatives and sought their views on what the impacts would be on the Northern Ireland agri-food sector.  These discussions remain ongoing, including a meeting officials had with industry representatives on Monday 16 February 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of community restorative justice schemes which are currently operating without direct departmental funding.
answer_text: My department provides funding to two accredited restorative justice schemes, Community Restorative Justice Ireland and Northern Ireland Alternatives. The Department does not hold a record of community restorative justice schemes that are not accredited by the Department of Justice. A new accreditation process is underway that will see more schemes and independent practitioners become accredited. These accredited practitioners will become part of a new Restorative Hub and take forward criminal cases as part of a new adult restorative justice pilot. Each practitioner within the hub will receive funding from the Department for any work undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether an audit has been undertaken of unregistered historic burial grounds, that are not currently maintained by local councils.
answer_text: My department has not conducted an audit of unregistered historic burial grounds and is unaware of such an audit existing.   As part of my department’s Historic Environment Record of Northern Ireland, burial grounds or graveyards that are considered to be of historic/archaeological interest are recorded and descriptions published online through the Sites and Monuments Record. However, this is not a specific or comprehensive audit of all burial grounds, and many burial grounds which relate to a more recent era are not included.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any measures she plans to introduce to expand alternatives to custody, including any safeguards to ensure public confidence in those measures.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring a range of viable alternatives to remand are available for the judiciary to utilise.  On this basis, a justice-wide Working Group on Remand has been exploring options, including the development of a bail support pilot for women and the potential expansion of electronic monitoring technologies.Once implemented, it is envisaged the bail support pilot will support women to remain in the community while awaiting the outcome of their case, providing support to live independently, whilst helping to address any offending behaviours.    Additionally, my Sentencing Bill will contain provisions to strengthen suspended sentences and allow the court, in suitable cases, to impose community requirements such as unpaid work or requirements relating to the offender’s residence, activities or treatment, along with the suspended sentence.These provisions draw on well-documented evidence that offenders who are subject to community sentences tend to have a lower reoffending rate than those receiving short custodial sentences.Where a community requirement is imposed, the sentence will also include a supervision period, during which the offender will be under the supervision of a responsible officer.  Offenders may also be subject to curfew or Electronic Monitoring.Through the Public Sector Transformation Programme, my Department secured additional resource to support the transformation of the current Electronic Monitoring services available to criminal justice agencies, in particular through the exploration and potential use of GPS/location monitoring.This enhanced monitoring could be used to enhance risk management and offer the Judiciary additional options when considering whether to grant bail or remand a person to prison custody.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department is considering any additional specialist courts models.
answer_text: My Department has no current plans to introduce any additional specialist court models.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her Department's assessment of how effective the current suite of rehabilitation programmes have been in reducing reoffending rates; and (ii) to detail any baseline this being measured against.
answer_text: Since 2016, the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) has delivered rehabilitation through a personcentred Prisoner Development Model (PDM). This assesses an individual’s risks, needs and strengths across nine resettlement pathways. A Personal Development Plan (PDP) is then created to identify barriers to desistance and the support required. The PDP guides a person’s progression in custody and helps prepare them for release with the aim of reducing reoffending.Whilst there is also no single baseline against which programme effectiveness can be measured, it is important to note that recidivism alone is not an indicator of prison performance. The support a person receives on release - particularly in relation to accommodation, healthcare and employment - is a key factor in enabling desistance.Under the Programme for Government, the Executive has committed to developing a new crossdepartmental strategy to reduce offending and reoffending. Work is underway on a Strategic Framework for 2026–27, which will align existing Executive activity and act as a bridge to the future strategy. A key element of this work is the development of a robust outcomes’ measurement framework, including a clear baseline for assessing rehabilitation effectiveness. The Department continues to engage with partners to develop Data Sharing Agreements to improve the quality, consistency and accessibility of relevant data.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will bring forward proposals that balance environmental protection obligations with the operational needs faced by the agricultural sector, during extreme weather events.
answer_text: The Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) aims to improve water quality and protect the environment by reducing nutrient pollution from agricultural sources.To support the fourth review of the Programme, I have established a NAP Stakeholder Task and Finish Group.  This Group is comprised of representatives from farming, agri-food, environmental groups and the Department and has been asked to propose measures and actions that will meet legal requirements, are evidence based and workable at farm level within realistic timeframes in a way that balances environmental protection and agricultural sector needs, for my consideration. The NAP Regulations have included provisions relating to slurry spreading and adverse weather conditions since 2007 and are based on the Code of Good Agricultural Practice and are underpinned by science, including agronomy.Increasingly frequent and intense rainfall highlights the need to ensure that the NAP remains effective and to support resilience within the agricultural sector.The Sustainable Agriculture Programme is a strong example of climate adaptation action being progressed by my department, as we work with and support our agricultural sector to be more resilient to the more frequent and severe impacts brought by climate change.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the reason why the proposed uncontrolled crossing, adjacent to Kirkistown Primary School, Cloughey, has not yet been installed, despite appearing on previous forward works programmes; and (ii) when this work will now be completed.
answer_text: Road safety continues to be a priority for my Department to ensure that all road users can travel safely and with confidence. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, especially near our schools. I can advise that whilst the new uncontrolled crossing had been programmed for this financial year, due to other priority work within the Department, its delivery was delayed and is now targeted for completion later in the year, as far as resources permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been made of the impact of prolonged periods of heavy rainfall on the ability of farmers to comply with slurry spreading closed seasons.
answer_text: Compliance with the closed period for slurry spreading is dependent on adequate slurry storage.  The Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) Regulations require farms to have at least 22 weeks of slurry storage capacity to allow slurry to be held until soil and weather conditions are suitable for spreading after the 16 week closed period.  Heavy rainfall can reduce available storage by increasing the volume of rainwater collected from farmyards and entering slurry tanks and uncovered slurry stores.Northern Ireland experienced the wettest January in 149 years and continued exceptionally wet conditions have left many farms with only the minimum 22 weeks of storage capacity under pressure.I understand the challenges farmers face as climate change contributes to more extreme winter rainfall. The Sustainable Agriculture Programme is a strong example of climate adaptation action being progressed by my department, supporting the agricultural sector to become more resilient to increasingly frequent and severe weather impacts.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timescale for the completion of the installation of a controlled crossing, adjacent to Andrews Memorial Primary School, Comber.
answer_text: Regrettably,  the proposal for Andrews Memorial Primary School did not score highly enough for inclusion in the 2025/26 works programme. The scheme will, however, remain on our list for future consideration should funding become available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider a derogation from the slurry spreading closed season for this year, given the prolonged rainfall period in the past month.
answer_text: There is no provision in the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) Regulations for a derogation or to waive the closed period dates set in the legislation. The closed period for slurry spreading runs from 15 October until 31 January, however, the NAP Regulations require farms to have a minimum of 22 weeks of slurry storage capacity. This is to ensure that farms have the flexibility to retain slurry in store following the 16-week closed period until soil and weather conditions are suitable for spreading in the spring. However, given the prolonged exceptionally wet weather this year, many farms with the minimum 22 weeks of slurry storage will now be coming under pressure for storage capacity. I appreciate these difficulties and the challenge here is changing weather patterns, more extreme rainfall and building resilience to deal with this in the future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount of compensation her Department has paid arising from unlawful detention, in the last five years.
answer_text: Details of the amount of compensation paid by my Department and its Agencies, arising from unlawful detention, during the last five full financial years, are outlined in the table below: Core Department and AgenciesFinancial Year£k2020-21222021-22202022-23892023-24252024-2546Any expenditure incurred by the Police Service of Northern Ireland arising from unlawful detention is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, for which he is accountable to the Policing Board.

answer_text: The total budget allocated to the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise Horticulture Pilot Scheme is £7,709,980.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the projects supported by Invest NI that did not meet job creation targets, including any clawback provisions activated, over the past five years.
answer_text: When a business brings forward a project to create additional jobs it is usually over a five-year period.Over the last five years, Invest NI has issued approximately 1,500 employment related offers to businesses with the potential to create nearly 17,000 new jobs.  Many of these projects are still in their delivery period with the associated jobs still being created. It is therefore too early to indicate which, if any, have not met their target.  However, our historical data suggests that, on average, businesses create approximately 77% of the jobs they intend to create. Of these projects, approximately 65% of grant was paid out against these jobs. Invest NI support is paid in arrears. In the case of job projects this is when the jobs have been created.  This means Invest NI employment support effectively generates a greater return on its investment.Clawback is only initiated if a business does not maintain the supported level of employment within an acceptable threshold. As employment grant is only ever paid in arrears, it is rare for projects where the overall employment target is not achieved to be the subject of clawback action.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of payments under the Troubles Permanent Disablement Scheme which remain unresolved beyond target timescales; and (ii) the reasons for delay, in each category of case.
answer_text: To date, the Victims’ Payments Board (VPB) has paid over £126m to eligible applicants through the Scheme.Due to the complexity of the Scheme, it is not possible to work to fixed target timescales.  Each case is unique and can vary significantly depending on its specific circumstances and requirements. The progress of each application is dependent on the provision of supporting information from external organisations – something outside of the control of the VPB.  The historical nature of the subject matter and the number of applications which include multiple incidents, adds further complexity to the process. Application processing includes, but is not limited to, verification checks, evidence gathering, and a medical assessment. Delays also occur where an application form is incomplete, supporting evidence is missing or there are gaps in incident disclosure etc.  The VPB work diligently to avoid unnecessary delays and to bring applications to a conclusion as efficiently as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether consideration is being given to stocking from hatcheries as a remediation measure, given the low level of Atlantic salmon numbers recorded in the Rivers Finn, Roe, Faughan and Mourne.
answer_text: The rivers referenced in this question are all within the remit of Loughs Agency. The Agency has advised that its position on the stocking of Atlantic salmon follows the NASCO Stocking Guidelines which states “where integrity (i.e. evolutionary and ecological naturalness) of the wild stock is a management priority, stocking should not be considered as a remediation measure”. The Agency’s evidence-base shows that none of the stocks in the Foyle and Carlingford catchments are at immediate risk of extirpation (in that spawning activity and the presence of juveniles still exist) and as such the integrity (i.e. evolutionary and ecological naturalness) of the wild stock is the management priority. Therefore, it does not currently consider that stocking from hatcheries should be used as a remediation measure.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has any plans to have an urgent meeting with the Isle of Man Government and the Home Office to discuss the change to visa rules by the Isle of Man Government requiring an Isle of Man work visa to fish in Manx waters, including the impact this will have on the Northern Ireland fishing industry.
answer_text: I share the Member’s concerns over this apparent development. I am seeking a meeting with the Isle of Man authorities to secure further clarity on this issue and to ensure that the potential impacts it has for the Northern Ireland fleet are fully understood.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department was made aware in advance of the change to visa rules by the Isle of Man Government requiring an Isle of Man work visa to fish in Manx waters.
answer_text: My department has not been informed of any change in visa rules by the Isle of Man government in relation to fishing in Isle of Man territory.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any rules that will be introduced by the Loughs Agency to protect Atlantic salmon numbers in the Rivers Finn, Roe, Faughan and Mourne.
answer_text: On 13 February 2026 Loughs Agency introduced measures within the Foyle and Carlingford catchment areas to ease pressure on already vulnerable salmon populations.The Agency has announced a reduction in salmon tag allocations for the 2026 season following new fish count data showing exceptionally low salmon returns.No blue (spring) tags will be issued, meaning all rivers in Loughs Agency's area will operate on a catch-and-release basis until 1 June 2026. From 1 June to 31 October 2026, anglers will be issued with one black tag per licence, a reduction from two.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment that has been made of the impact on the Northern Ireland fishing fleet of the change to visa rules by the Isle of Man Government requiring an Isle of Man work visa to fish in Manx waters.
answer_text: I share your concerns over the impact this apparent change may have for the Northern Ireland fleet who rely on access to the Isle of Man Waters to catch part of their allocated fish quotas. From initial analysis there are some 38 vessels over the last 4 years that have accessed Isle of Man waters, mainly involved in fisheries for herring, Nephrops or scallops. Total landings were worth approximately £9 million in total. Foreign crews on Transit or Skilled Worker Visa are heavily relied upon in the Nephrops and scallop fisheries. However, my department does not maintain information on the visa status of workers on each fishing vessel. At this stage I cannot say with certainty how many of these vessels will be impacted going forward or the total potential loss faced.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of apprenticeships available through public sector bodies, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: As Public Sector Apprenticeships are not funded by my Department, information is not held on the number of apprentices supported by individual public sector organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she plans to bring forward proposals to enable public sector bodies to access Apprenticeship Levy funding.
answer_text: Whilst eligible private and public sector organisations here are liable to pay the Apprenticeship Levy, apprenticeship policy and spending matters are devolved.Due to ongoing financial constraints my Department does not currently fund Public Sector Apprenticeships however we have committed to exploring what is possible in the Apprenticeship Action Plan, building on a small number of existing successful public sector apprenticeship programmes. I will continue to make the case for increased funding for skills and apprenticeships here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount of funding that has been (i) made available; and (ii) been allocated, as a result of the Apprenticeship Levy for schemes in Northern Ireland, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: While apprenticeship policy and spending are devolved, the collection of the Apprenticeship Levy is a reserved matter and my Department has no remit over either the collection of the levy.  As Barnett consequentials provided at a Spending Review are identified at Whitehall department level, rather than individual programme level, we cannot identify all consequentials that relate to spending on apprenticeships.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the rationale for public sector bodies in Northern Ireland being excluded from accessing funds raised through the UK-wide Apprenticeship Levy.
answer_text: Although eligible private and public sector organisations here are liable to pay the Apprenticeship Levy, apprenticeship policy and spending matters are devolved. As skills policy is a devolved matter, the funds are instead returned to the Executive as part of the block grant. These funds are not ring-fenced specifically for apprenticeships and it is for the Executive to decide how to use this funding within its devolved budget.My Department invests almost £50 million per year in apprenticeships.  Given current budget constraints, it is not possible to fund both private and public sector apprenticeships, though I continue to press for increased resources for skills and apprenticeships.While my Department does not currently fund Public Sector Apprenticeships (PSA), the Apprenticeship Action Plan commits us to exploring what may be possible, building on a number of successful PSA programmes. Officials remain willing to engage with public sector employers to maximise apprenticeship opportunities within existing resources and to inform future policy development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will bring forward a support package for potato and vegetable farmers impacted by current flooding and extreme rainfall.
answer_text: Whilst, at this stage, a support package for potato and vegetable farmers impacted by current flooding and rainfall is not planned, I am continuing to monitor the situation.The SAP’s Farm Sustainability Payment Scheme is designed to improve the resilience of farm businesses and to provide a balance between providing a safety net which will help farm businesses withstand ‘shocks’ that are beyond their ability to manage effectively and encouraging them to become more environmentally sustainable, efficient and resilient.A Crisis Framework is being developed as part of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) This framework will provide a pre-defined approach for government intervention in the event of a significant crisis impacting the agricultural sector in Northern IrelandThe SAP is a strong example of climate adaptation action being progressed by my Department, as we work with and support our agricultural sector to be more resilient to the more frequent and severe impacts brought by climate change. As such, delivery of the SAP is a key action in the draft Northern Ireland’s third Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP3), which my Department has developed, with input from all Departments. As a cross-cutting Programme with more than 280 actions to build climate resilience, the NICCAP3 requires Executive agreement. I am currently engaging with my Executive colleagues to seek their agreement to finalise NICCAP3 and lay it in this Assembly as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the percentage of the total received in domestic rates, paid through benefits as (i) housing benefit; or (ii) rate rebate, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer for AQW 40247/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has been made of how a third phase of the Comber bypass could impact congestion, journey times and road safety, in the context of current and projected traffic volumes through Comber.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.Comber Bypass is not a current scheme listed on the Major Works Programme. Any decision on an additional phase of the Comber Bypass will be informed through the development of the Eastern Transport Plan (ETP). As the ETP is being integrated with the Local Development Plans of five councils, it will be published in iterations with the Ards and North Down iteration developed to tie in with the publication of the council’s draft Local Policies Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any next steps her Department plans to take in relation to a third phase of the Comber bypass.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.Comber Bypass is not a current scheme listed on the Major Works Programme. Any decision on an additional phase of the Comber Bypass will be informed through the development of the Eastern Transport Plan (ETP). As the ETP is being integrated with the Local Development Plans of five councils, it will be published in iterations with the Ards and North Down iteration developed to tie in with the publication of the council’s draft Local Policies Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure following the receipt of the results of the Welsh trial on the use of recycled material from nappies in road resurfacing schemes, in order to assist his Department in informing its approach on the collection and recycling of nappies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration her Department has given to the implementation of a one-way system on Mill Street and Castle Street, Comber.
answer_text: I recognise the benefits of one-way systems in our towns and cities which can improve traffic progression and reduce congestion. Mill Street and Castle Street currently have waiting restrictions in the form of double yellow lines to aid traffic progression and where parking is provided, this acts as a traffic calming measure in both directions by keeping speeds low. I have asked officials to consider the introduction of a one-way system Mill Street and Castle Street, Comber.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any impact his decision to suspend NI Reval2026 has had on the penny product calculation previously sent to each local council, for the calculation of their rates.
answer_text: My decision to stop Reval 2026 means that councils will set their 2026/27 district rates on the basis of the current valuation list.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any costs incurred, to date, in relation to NI Reval2026.
answer_text: As the Member will be aware, I announced on 29 January that I was stopping the Reval 2026 process. I would direct the Member to my oral statement to the Assembly:Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of FinanceAny costs will primarily relate to the staffing resource required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to resurface Mill Street and Castle Street, Comber.

To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department publishes statistics regarding answering Assembly Questions for Written Answer on time, as required by paragraph 3.3 of the Guidance for Ministers in the Exercise of their Official Responsibilities.
answer_text: The Department does not currently publish statistics on the timeliness of responses to Assembly Questions for Written Answer (AQWs). While internal monitoring processes are in place to support the effective management of AQW workflows, this information is not collated or released in a statistical format. The Department remains committed to meeting the expectations set out in paragraph 3.3 of the Guidance for Ministers in the Exercise of their Official Responsibilities and continues to work to ensure that responses are issued as promptly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of Assembly Questions for Written Answer that remain unanswered, in each year during the 2022-27 Assembly mandate.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any (i) impact, to date; and (ii) projected impact, of the withdrawal of services by criminal barristers on criminal case waiting lists, for each week, since the withdrawal of those services.
answer_text: The impact of the withdrawal will not be understood for some time, but it is exacerbating the delays we had already been working hard with partners to address. Management data shows that, as of Friday 13 February 2026, 654 cases, that is 119 trials; 289 arraignments; and 246 plea and sentencing hearings have been affected since the escalated withdrawal began on 5 January. I have detailed the weekly breakdown below. However, the numbers do not tell the real story, of the victims and witnesses who are suffering stress and uncertainty and real and continuing trauma. They are my focus and I am working with the organisations who are directly supporting those affected to deploy extra resources so additional support can be provided. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the review of old mineral permissions.
answer_text: The Review of Old Mineral Permissions (ROMPs) are provisions of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 that have not to date been commenced.  I am aware that there are issues involved in commencing these provisions in their current form and for the provisions to be implemented effectively and with legal certainty, legislative changes will be required to both primary and subordinate legislation.I have instructed officials to begin a strategic scoping exercise to consider the future direction of the planning system, including regional and strategic planning policy, which will include considerations with respect to ROMPs in the context of a potential future Planning Bill. In the interim, I am reassured that councils have powers to address environmental impacts of mineral operations through existing mechanisms such as the Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (NI) 2011, planning enforcement, and other environmental legislation, which provides a degree of regulatory oversight.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the PSNI's Injury on Duty and Re-Injury on Duty award bandings, from Band 1 to Band 4, broken down by year, from 2018 to the present day.
answer_text: The Department does not hold this information.The Northern Ireland Policing Board who, under the Police Service of Northern Ireland and Police Service of Northern Ireland Reserve (Injury Benefit) Regulations 2006 administers this scheme, is better placed to answer your question.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department will take to address the significant subsidence, evident along stretches of the B5 Ballywalter Road, Greyabbey.
answer_text: My Department is aware of an area of subsidence on the B5 Ballywalter Road, Greyabbey. I can confirm that repairs have been instructed though our external contractor and will include some localised drainage works, minor carriageway modifications and the relaying of surfacing.  Recent storm events have directed resources to higher priority safety critical works but my Department is committed to completing works at this location as soon as resources permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will develop a long-term coastal resilience strategy for the Ards Peninsula, based on robust climate modelling to mitigate against future tidal and storm risk.
answer_text: My Department has published flood maps for all of the North.  These maps identify areas which may be vulnerable to both present day and projected climate change impacts associated with river, surface and coastal flooding. The maps are available to view at: Flood Maps NI | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk).In addition, a regional Flood Risk Management Plan (FRMP) for 2021 – 2027 has been developed by my Department. The development of this plan has, utilised industry accepted flood modelling techniques, and identified areas of existing and potential flood risk, including areas subject to coastal flood risk. The FRMP is readily available on the Department’s website at: 2nd Cycle - Flood Risk Management Plan 2021-2027 | Department for Infrastructure (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk).The Ards peninsula has not been identified, through that work, as an area of potentially significant flood risk and there are presently no plans to carry out any additional works in that area.  The Coastal Forum, which I jointly chair with Minister Muir, prepared a Work Programme in 2019. The current Work Programme is available on the Coastal Observatory site at:https://experience.arcgis.com/experience/b8454f3d62164518817e4c581b5555c8/page/Coastal-Forum-Information.At the November 2025 meeting of the Coastal Forum, it was agreed that the Coastal Forum’s Working Group should make recommendations for a 2026-2031 Work Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any discussions he has had with the UK Government to strengthen medicine security and supply, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: There are over 14,000 medicines licensed in the UK, and the majority are in good supply and are available in sufficient quantities to meet patient needs. However, medicines are manufactured as part of a highly regulated, complex and global industry; therefore, medicine supply disruptions are not uncommon and can occur for a variety of reasons such as manufacturing issues or increases in demand.The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) leads on the maintenance of medicine supply chains to the UK and works closely with the devolved administrations, including my Department, the UK medicines regulator and other operators within the supply chain. There are well established tools and processes that can be applied both locally and nationally to ensure patients can have access to the treatments they need if a supply disruption does occur.Medicine supply issues have increased in complexity over recent years, and I recognise the impact these supply disruptions can have on patients and on Health and Social Care providers in Northern Ireland. Consequently, I had previously written to Wes Streeting MP, Secretary of State for Health and Social Care, and Karin Smyth MP, Minister of Health (Secondary Care), highlighting my support for wider collaboration to improve long-term supply resilience across the UK. DHSC published a document in August 2025, entitled ‘Managing a robust and resilient supply of medicines’ which aims to reduce the frequency of shortages and minimise the impact on patients when they do occur. The document provides examples of collaboration between my Department, the UK Government and others to resolve some recent complex medicine supply disruptions. I welcome the aims of this publication and my Department is committed to working with our partners across the UK to find strategies to improve the security and supply of medicines to the UK. Additionally, there are commitments within the document to provide the public with more information on medicine shortages. Through shared learning with colleagues from across the UK, my Department has recently published information for the public on steps they can take if they are experiencing medicine supply disruptions. This information can be found via https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/medicine-shortages. Finally, my Department has worked with DHSC to develop proposals for amendments to the Human Medicines Regulations 2012 to enable greater flexibilities for pharmacists when dispensing medicines, with a 12-week public consultation period closing on 11th December 2025. These proposals aim to improve patient access to medicines, reduce the burden on healthcare systems and improve person-centred care. While this measure is not expected to resolve all the issues surrounding medicine supply difficulties, it is anticipated that the proposals could provide another tool for pharmacists to improve patient access to their medicines. My Department will work with DHSC to carefully consider next steps.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to provide (i) an update on the current status of the works associated with the scaffolding to the rear of Parliament Buildings; and (ii) a revised timeline when the works will be completed and the scaffolding removed.
answer_text: The remedial works to the roof of Parliament Buildings has been split into two phases.  Phase 1 will address the defects at the rear of the building in addition to Courtyards 1, 2 and 3.  Phase 2 will address the remaining areas.  A tender exercise for Phase 1 was undertaken by the Department of Finance’s Property Services Division in January 2026 and the submitted tenders are currently being evaluated. Following completion of this process, a mandatory standstill period will apply prior to the formal appointment of the successful contractor.  Once appointed, the works will be carried out on a phased basis, with Roof 1 and Courtyard 3 prioritised, followed by Courtyards 1 and 2. Scaffolding will be removed as works in each area are completed.A full and detailed timeline, including completion dates, will not be available until a contractor has been formally appointed and an initial commencement meeting has taken place, at which point the programme can be confirmed though it is hoped that the work will be completed over the next six months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the current statutory guidance for Selected Medical Practitioners and Independent Medical Referees, engaged by the Northern Ireland Policing Board, in respect of ill health retirement and injury on duty awards.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland and Police Service of Northern Ireland Reserve (Injury Benefit) (Amendment) (No.2) Regulations 2024 added a regulation to the Police Service of Northern Ireland and Police Service of Northern Ireland Reserve (Injury Benefit) Regulations 2006.This new regulation provides that both Selected Medical Practitioners and Independent Medical Referees must have regard to the provisions of the Guidance to Medical Practitioners on Injury on Duty Awards. The current Guidance document is Version 2 published in 2016.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission (i) to provide a breakdown of the total cost to date associated with the (a) erection; (b) hire; and (c) maintenance of the scaffolding currently to the rear of Parliament Buildings; and (ii) whether the prolonged presence of the scaffolding has resulted in additional costs beyond those originally projected.
answer_text: Scaffolding was erected at the rear of Parliament Buildings and in Courtyards 1, 2 and 3 to carry out investigatory works necessary to inform the full scope of the roof repair programme and to address health and safety concerns within the areas that require access.  Costs incurred for scaffolding up to 31 January 2026 amount to £244,695.  This figure includes the costs associated with erection (£74,625) and ongoing hire including weekly statutory inspections to ensure the scaffolding remains safe for use (£170,070). No costs have been incurred in respect of scaffolding maintenance as the hire cost is inclusive of maintenance.  The remedial works for these areas have now completed a tender process and will be awarded in February 2026.Preliminary costings for the complete project have not been exceeded.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the date on which the scaffolding to the rear of Parliament Buildings was first erected.
answer_text: The remedial works to the roof of Parliament Buildings has been split into two phases.  Phase 1 will address the defects at the rear of the building in addition to Courtyards 1, 2 and 3.  Phase 2 will address the remaining areas. The project was split into these phases to prioritise works to the areas that required ongoing access including the slope entrance, boiler room and kitchen.  In order to enable the Integrated Consultancy Team (ICT) to carry out investigatory works necessary to inform the full scope of the roof repair programme and to mitigate the health and safety risks in these areas including the risk of falling debris, the scaffolding was erected in August 2024.  This ensured that these areas remained accessible for operational reasons including the need to carry out essential maintenance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans her Department has in place to (i) review; or (ii) reform (a) student finance; or (b) other support mechanisms, to better support lifelong learning.
answer_text: Access to further education as part of lifelong learning is essential to ensure individuals, particularly those facing disadvantage, can continue developing the skills, confidence and opportunities they need to participate fully in society and the economy.My Department is currently undertaking a review of student support measures to ensure they continue to meet the needs of the most vulnerable in society to enable them to continue their education and training within further education.Engagement is also continuing on the development of draft Terms of Reference for a wider Review of Higher Education Funding. It is proposed that the review will include consideration of the financial support available to students, including whether current arrangements remain appropriate and whether support should be extended beyond its existing scope. The overall timeframe for the review will be determined once the Terms of Reference are finalised and subject to the availability of resources.Officials from my Department have also met regularly with the Alliance for Lifelong Learning to discuss opportunities for partnership.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions her Department is taking to support lifelong learning.
answer_text: My officials have been working with the Alliance for Lifelong Learning to co-design approaches to support a culture of lifelong learning. Through the Skills Fund, we are pursuing several initiatives to enhance awareness of adult learning opportunities and promote the benefits of participation in adult learning. We launched ‘Measuring the Impact of Adult Education in NI: Current Practice and Ways Forward’ a report developed in partnership with the Alliance.Other initiatives include research into impact measurement of adult learning, especially non-accredited adult learning. Capturing previous participation in learning via the Voluntary Community and Social Enterprise sector at the point of FE enrolment, and the learner voice.The Alliance has also proposed an initial framework to set up an Adult Learners’ Forum, discussions are ongoing on how to progress this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how current lifelong learning policy aligns with her Department’s Economic Strategy, particularly in relation to (i) skills shortages; (ii) productivity; and (iii) economic inactivity.
answer_text: In line with my Economic Vision, my Department published its Skills Action Plan in October 2025 which includes a series of actions to address skills shortages, productivity and economic inactivity.The Action Plan includes a commitment to work with stakeholders to increase participation in lifelong learning. This will take the shape of continual assessment of current offering, increasing awareness of existing provision and building on our partnership with the Alliance for Lifelong Learning.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how the levels of financial support for adult learners undertaking full-time distance learning courses in Northern Ireland, compare with those available in England, Scotland and Wales, including, but not limited to (i) tuition fee support; (ii) maintenance support; and (iii) loan availability.
answer_text: Higher education is a devolved matter and support packages available to students vary across jurisdictions.		In relation to tuition fees, the financial support currently provided by my Department to eligible students from here undertaking a full-time distance learning course comprises a means-tested tuition fee grant of up to £1,230 as well as a fee loan of up to £3,641.25 (which is reduced by any tuition fee grant received). Students can also apply for a means-tested course grant of up to £265. Additional assistance is available in the form of: the Disabled Students’ Allowance which provides support of up to £25,000 per year plus additional eligible travel costs; and the Student Support Funds, managed by the five local Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) on the Department’s behalf, for students facing financial hardship. Maintenance support is not generally available for students domiciled here undertaking distance learning courses.	For those domiciled in England, eligible full-time distance learning students can access a tuition fee loan of up to £9,535. Disabled Students’ Allowance support of up to £27,783 plus additional eligible travel costs is also available. Maintenance support is not generally available for English domiciled students on distance learning courses. Eligible full-time distance learning students from Wales can access the same support as full-time in-attendance students, namely a tuition fee loan of up to £9,535 and maintenance support of up to £10,480, subject to means testing, for students undertaking their distance learning studies at home. Disabled Students’ Allowance support of up to £34,000 a year plus additional eligible travel costs is available. A range of means-tested dependents’ grants are available for Welsh domiciled distance learning students. Eligible distance learning students from Scotland can access a part-time fee grant of up to £1,820. Disabled Students’ Allowance support is available up to a maximum of £22,245 per year for consumable items and non-medical personal help, with additional funds of up to £5,160 available for equipment, software and accessories over the duration of a course, and support for additional eligible travel costs. Maintenance support is not generally available for Scottish domiciled students on distance learning courses.In addition to Disabled Students’ Allowance, distance learning students in each administration can apply for maintenance support if they cannot attend their course in person due to a disability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of deaths recorded as a result of house fires, in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Official statistics on death registration numbers, including causes of death, are produced by NISRA using death registration data collected by the General Register Office (GRO).As part of the statistical process, the cause of death information recorded by the certifying medical professional on the Medical Certificate of Cause of Death (MCCD) is converted to codes based on the International Classification of Diseases (ICD-10) which are used for monitoring and comparison purposes. Each death is assigned an underlying cause but may also have multiple contributing causes. Deaths arising from house fires were identified:where the ICD-10 code was X00 (Exposure to uncontrolled fire in building or structure);  additionally, by searching for deaths which included the term ‘house’ somewhere on the Medical Certificate of Cause of Death (MCCD). The resulting cases were then reviewed to identify cases where house fire was listed but did not include the ICD-10 code of X00.The data presented shows the constituencies where deaths due to house fires were registered and covers the years 2020 to 2024 - the most recent year for which this data is publicly available. Table 1 – Deaths where cause of death was due to a house fire by constituency, 2020 to 2024Constituency Label20202021202220232024Belfast East10000Belfast North02000Belfast South01000Belfast West10013East Antrim00000East Londonderry00000Fermanagh and South Tyrone21001Foyle01111Lagan Valley00100Mid Ulster00010Newry and Armagh01000North Antrim00100North Down01010South Antrim00002South Down00010Strangford00000Upper Bann00001West Tyrone00010Total47368 Please note that geographical information is based on the usual address of the deceased, or in the absence of this it is based on location of death. It is possible that address and place of death fall into different areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consultations or meetings his Department has had with animal welfare organisations, pest control professionals and other stakeholders on the use of glue traps, in the last five years.
answer_text: During the development of my Animal Welfare Pathway, I met with representatives from animal welfare organisations and they were asked to highlight areas where they felt reform was most needed.  None of these organisations highlighted a pressing requirement to prohibit the use of glue traps.  I do fully empathise with the concern over the use of glue-type traps.  Whether it was to introduce a restriction of sale or use through a licensing regime, or to introduce a complete ban, it would require a piece of standalone primary legislation to be taken through the Assembly. Unfortunately, due to this shortened Assembly mandate, there is insufficient time left to legislate to restrict their sale and use in Northern Ireland.  During a meeting between the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) and the British Pest Control Association in 2022, the issue of glue traps was raised. NIEA officials were asked whether there were plans for Northern Ireland to introduce similar legislation to other parts of the United Kingdom and Ireland, in relation to glue traps. It was confirmed that there were no plans. No further discussions or meetings have taken place.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration he has given to introducing a licensing or restriction regime for glue trap use similar to that under (i) the Glue Trap (Offences) Act 2022, in England; (ii) the Wildlife Management and Muirburn (Scotland) Act 2024, in Scotland; or (iii) the Wildlife Act 1976, in the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: During the development of my Animal Welfare Pathway, I met with representatives from animal welfare organisations and they were asked to highlight areas where they felt reform was most needed. None of these organisations highlighted a pressing requirement to prohibit the use of glue traps.I do fully empathise with the concern over the use of glue-type traps.  Whether it was to introduce a restriction of sale or use through a licensing regime, or to introduce a complete ban, it would require a piece of standalone primary legislation to be taken through the Assembly. Unfortunately, due to this shortened Assembly mandate, there is insufficient time left to legislate to restrict their sale and use in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any contact his Department has had with the periwinkle gatherers regarding the prohibition on gathering contained in the Shellfish Gathering (Conservation) Regulations) 2026.
answer_text: The Department does not hold personal details for individuals who gather periwinkles or other shellfish in the intertidal area, as this remains an unregulated fishery with no requirement for registration or reporting. Engagement with gatherers has therefore taken place through public consultation and direct field engagement, with the consultation published on the DAERA website, distributed electronically to stakeholders, and supported by DAERA staff raising awareness with harvesters and the public where possible. The consultation received 37 responses, including from the shellfish gatherer group. The Fisheries Management Plan working group, through which these regulations were developed, also includes representation from a periwinkle gatherer, ensuring local knowledge informed policy development. As gathered periwinkles are typically sold to processors, the prohibitions in the Shellfish Gathering (Conservation) Regulations 2026 were communicated to processors as a practical means of cascading information to those involved in gathering. The Department remains committed to developing evidencebased proposals for sustainable fisheries and encourages gatherers to engage and share local knowledge to inform future management of periwinkles and other intertidal species.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how teacher absence trends compare with those in other jurisdictions.
answer_text: Included below are tables detailing teacher absence published in other jurisdictions. However, it is not possible to make direct comparisons of trends between jurisdictions as each collects teacher absence information using different methodologies and reporting periods and each had different approaches to recording absence related to COVID-19.With regard to reporting periods, Northern Ireland reports data using financial years (April to March). Scotland reports data on a quarterly basis, while England and Wales report data using academic years.Methodologies vary across the jurisdictions.  For example, in England only absence periods that end during the academic year are counted. This means the total may include absence days carried over from the previous academic year but will exclude days at the end of the year if the absence period is still ongoing. In Northern Ireland, any days lost during the financial year are counted, regardless of whether the period of absence began in a previous reporting year or continues into the next.In England, the number of days of absence that can be recorded for a parttime teacher is limited to the days they are contracted to teach. In Northern Ireland, parttime teachers are treated in the same way as fulltime teachers, meaning that all working days—including days they are not contracted to work—are counted as days lost.In Wales, figures relate to the number of teachers ‘taking an absence’, meaning only those who have been absent are counted. This differs from Northern Ireland, England, and Scotland, where absence figures are based on all teachers.Republic of Ireland do not publish data on teacher absences.
