question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any scientific evidence and stock assessments being used, or that will be used, to inform the development of the proposed inter-tidal hand-gathering fisheries management plan.
answer_text: Currently, the hand gathering of shellfish in intertidal areas in Northern Ireland is unregulated. As a result, there is a limited availability of scientific data and stock assessments for certain species included in the intertidal hand-gathering fisheries management plan (FMP), specifically cockles, blue mussels, oysters, and razor clams. The department will reference the AFBI Strangford Lough Periwinkle Study and incorporate scientific findings from this report to guide recommendations for sustainable periwinkle harvesting.A working group has been established to ensure that the proposed Intertidal Hand-Gathering Fisheries Management Plan is guided by the most reliable scientific evidence available regarding cockles, blue mussels, oysters, and razor clams. The plan will also incorporate policy objectives that emphasise the long-term sustainability of these species.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 23630/22-27, for an update on when the Movilla Stones, which were removed from Movilla Abbey, and are currently in the possession of Historic Environment Division, will be returned.
answer_text: The Movilla stones have now been conserved by a stone conservation specialist and are safely stored by the Historic Environment Division (HED). HED has also commissioned an options appraisal to consider the potential approaches for returning the stones to Movilla Abbey whilst ensuring their long-term conservation. Once this appraisal has been received, HED will engage further with Ards and North Down Borough Council to explore the options for returning the stones to Movilla Abbey with appropriate protective measures in place to safeguard their long-term preservation for current and future generations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement his Department has undertaken, or plans to undertake, with local hand-gatherers, fishing communities and other stakeholders in relation to closed seasons and wider management measures under his inter-tidal hand-gathering fisheries management plan.
answer_text: My department has engaged with local handgatherers, fishing communities and other stakeholders through public consultation, targeted engagement and ongoing dialogue regarding closed seasons and wider management measures under the intertidal handgathering fisheries management plan. Officials wrote to local processors in September 2025 to inform them of plans to legislate.Initial preconsultation and formal consultation activity included presentations by officials to stakeholder groups and active encouragement to respond to the consultation. Stakeholder forums included the Inshore Fisheries Partnership, Strangford Lough, Carlingford Lough and Murlough Bay MPA stakeholder groups, and the UK Marine and Fisheries Working Group. Post-consultation, officials engaged processors, local councils, the Food Standards Agency and relevant interest groups. A dedicated Fisheries Management Plan working group has been established, and development of the plan will be informed by scientific evidence and industry expertise. The Department will continue to engage with stakeholders, including individual handgatherers, as this work progresses.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assistance that is being given to local fishing businesses operating in and around Strangford Lough.
answer_text: The Marine Environment and Fisheries Fund (MEFF) provides grant assistance to all eligible marine and fisheries businesses across Northern Ireland, including those based in and around the Strangford Lough area. The MEFF for 2025/26 closed for new applications on 31st December 2025. However, I hope to be in a position to announce a new MEFF early in the 2026/27 financial year, subject to confirmation of budget allocations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any criteria that would be used to determine the timing and duration of any closed seasons under his inter-tidal hand-gathering fisheries management plan, including (i) consideration of spawning periods; (ii) recruitment cycles; (iii) environmental conditions; and (iv) economic considerations.
answer_text: The timing and duration of any closed season would be determined on a speciesspecific, evidencebased basis. Key considerations include protecting stocks during spawning periods and other biologically sensitive stages, as well as recruitment cycles, where restrictions may improve juvenile survival and longterm stock resilience. Environmental conditions, including wider ecosystem pressures and climaterelated factors, are also taken into account where these increase stock vulnerability. Where evidence is limited, a precautionary approach may be applied. Decisions also consider economic impacts, particularly on smallscale and inshore fishers and coastal communities, with closures designed to be proportionate, timelimited and clearly linked to stock protection, recognising that healthy stocks underpin the longterm economic sustainability of the fishing industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department will review the impact of NI Reval26 on the hospitality sector and consider adjustments to valuation approaches, given the risks to the sustainability of the sector as a result of increased business rates due to the outworkings of NI Reval26.
answer_text: As set out in my statement to the Assembly on 2 February 2026, I have stopped Reval2026 in response to the concerns raised by businesses, including those in the hospitality sector, about the implications of proceeding with the draft valuations at this time. My full statement is available at:Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any steps his Department took to ensure that NI Reval2026 valuations accurately reflected the ability of hospitality businesses to pay, in light of rising salary, energy and food costs.
answer_text: As set out in my statement to the Assembly on 2 February 2026, I have stopped Reval2026 in response to the concerns raised by businesses, including those in the hospitality sector, about the implications of proceeding with the draft valuations at this time. My full statement is available at:Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any criteria used by Land and Property Services in carrying out NI Reval2026, including any valuation methodology applied in relation to hospitality premises.
answer_text: As set out in my statement to the Assembly on 2 February 2026, I have stopped Reval2026 in response to the concerns raised by businesses, including those in the hospitality sector, about the implications of proceeding with the draft valuations at this time. My full statement is available at:Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of FinanceIn addition, I have deposited a paper in the Assembly Library which sets out the Receipts and Expenditure valuation method used for valuing licensed premises for rates purposes. Members can access it through the Assembly’s Deposited Papers under Number 2034 at:Deposited Papers

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the reason why her Department’s portal for making damage claims has been repeatedly showing error messages; and (ii) any steps being taken to ensure the portal is functioning appropriately.
answer_text: My officials are aware of the recent errors relating to the online claim form process and are working with the IT contractor to address this problem urgently.I appreciate the frustration that this may cause to road users trying to process their claims and would ask for your patience as we work to resolve the issues in a timely manner.In the interim, claim forms are available for download from the NI Direct website, or can be posted to citizens if they contact my Department's Roads Claims Unit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions she has had with representatives of the insurance industry in relation to reducing the insurance premium costs for new drivers under the Graduated Drivers Licence scheme.
answer_text: I have engaged directly with members of the insurance industry to discuss the potential implications of Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL). This included a meeting held in June last year with the Association of British Insurers (ABI), where GDL was considered along with wider factors influencing motor insurance costs. Engagement also continued through correspondence with the Motor Insurance Taskforce, of which the ABI is a member. That correspondence reaffirmed the issues I raised during a meeting with the Minister of Finance and the former British Government’s Minister for the Future of Roads, Lilian Greenwood MP. Items discussed included local concerns about the affordability of insurance. I also reiterated that my Department’s priority remains the delivery of measures within its remit aimed at reducing the underlying risks factored into insurance pricing, including bringing forward GDL.The core purpose of GDL is to enhance road safety for young and novice drivers. Evidence from other jurisdictions such as New Zealand, Canada and various Australian states shows that GDL systems can achieve substantial and lasting reductions in collisions involving new drivers. If similar benefits are realised here, a reduction in insurance claims may follow.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures her Department is taking to improve public transport connectivity on the Ards Peninsula.
answer_text: My Department is currently preparing a new suite of multimodal Transport Plans which will set the framework for transport policy and investment decisions up to 2035. For Ards Peninsula, the relevant plan is the Eastern Transport Plan 2035 (ETP). This Transport Plan is being developed in an integrated manner with the Council’s Local Development Plan. The ETP will focus on the main urban settlements, while also considering transport needs across the wider council area. While the plan is primarily a capital investment plan, it will recommend areas where, subject to funding, improved provision of local bus services would have a significant impact. In parallel, my Department is also developing the Regional Strategic Transport Plan, which supported by data and evidence will identify the transport priorities and opportunities for the strategic transport network to 2035.Both plans are currently in development; and public and stakeholder engagement will be carried out at key stages as the plans progress.Translink continues delivering the Public Service Agreement network and timetable across the region. For the Ards peninsula there are no significant changes other than preparatory work for how services may operate once the Newtownards Park and Ride scheme is implemented. The local network has evolved to cater for school travel needs as well as reflecting the rural and inter-urban demand levels. At this stage planning approval for the Park and Ride has been granted and Translink is finalising the business case to submit to the Department to allow the development to commence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps being considered to address traffic congestion on the A21 Newtownards to Comber road.
answer_text: Congestion is an issue that affects many of our urban centres, including the A21 Newtownards to Comber Road, particularly during the morning and evening peak periods. However, as road safety remains a high priority for my Department, the limited resources available to make our roads safer, must be focused in areas that have a demonstrable evidence of collisions and where improvements will deliver most road safety benefit. I can advise there are no plans for a road improvement scheme to reduce congestion at this time.  I have asked my officials to continue to monitor the area and consider any opportunities to assist in reducing the traffic congestion, particularly with regard to further development along the route.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current waiting times for mental health services in Strangford; and (ii) any plans to reduce these.
answer_text: South Eastern Health and Social Trust can confirm that the current waiting time is within the 9-week expected target for accessing community mental health services in the Strangford area.  The waiting times are in keeping with the expected target.Current waiting times for children and adult mental health services are lengthy and growing across Northern Ireland.  This situation has arisen due to several factors, including higher prevalence rates in NI linked to deprivation and legacy issues, COVID, increased levels of acuity and funding that falls short of objectively assessed need.  I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been undertaken of school place capacity in Newtownards and Comber in (i) primary; and (ii) secondary, settings, in light of increasing housing development.
answer_text: Operational responsibility for the planning of education provision lies with the two statutory planning authorities, the Education Authority (EA) and the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS).  These bodies conduct area planning activity in consultation with a number of sectoral support bodies. In the development and delivery of its Strategic Area Plan for area planning, the EA monitors growth across all areas in Northern Ireland on an annual basis.   It assesses provision to identify areas of growth, and, conversely, areas of decrease and to scope actions for progression.  As part of that process, it assesses Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency (NISRA) live birth statistics, current and projected housing and any connectivity between schools.However, housing development will not, of itself, necessarily translate into reliable evidence of the need for school places, as matters such as age demographics of those who may move into a development and expressions of parental preference for particular types of education provision will have bearing on demand.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support available to schools in the Strangford constituency to meet rising demand for special educational needs provision.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:A list of schools providing specialist support for pupils with Special Educational Needs in the Member's constituency is available on the SEND Plan website at: Educational Settings | SEND Plan  Information on support available to schools with existing specialist provision, or those considering establishing such provision, can be found at: https://send.eani.org.uk/statutory-assessment/information-schools/establishing-specialist-provisions-mainstream-schoolsDespite significant financial pressures, an enhanced support package has been developed in collaboration with colleagues from the Department of Education (DE) to complement the Department's committed capital investments in this area.The EA continues to work closely with the DE on the next steps.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the media outlets invited to attend (i) Ministerial; and (ii) Departmental, press conferences, in the last two years.
answer_text: All mainstream media outlets are invited to planned press conferences.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the order in which media outlets are invited to ask questions at Ministerial press conferences; and (ii) how this order is determined.
answer_text: The order of questions at Ministerial press conferences is decided on a case-by-case basis. Ministers will endeavour to respond to all journalists in attendance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of drug offences that have (i) been detected; (ii) resulted in a caution or other diversionary disposal; and (iii) resulted in prosecution, in the Strangford constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on drugs offences detected by police, as requested in part (i) of the question.  The recording of information on drugs offences reported to or detected by police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to drugs offences in the Strangford constituency reported to or detected by police, as detailed in part (i) of your question, to the PSNI.  The Police Service of Northern Ireland publish statistics on drugs offences reported to them as part of their recorded crime bulletin.  However, this bulletin does not include figures based on Assembly constituency. In relation to parts (ii) and (iii) of the question, the Department holds figures on drugs offences dealt with at courts and by out of court diversionary disposal, for persons who had an address in the Strangford constituency.  These have been provided in the following table.Convictions at courts and out of court Diversionary disposals for drugs offences for Strangford constituency, 2020 – 2024YearDiversionary disposalsConvictions202047702021361012022308320235210620243799Source: Department of Justice.Note:1. Figures for convictions relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases where there was an out of court diversionary disposal or a conviction at court for at least one drugs offence.  Out of court diversionary disposals include cautions, informed warnings and Youth conference Plans.3. Figures relate to defendants who had an address in the Strangford constituency at the time their case was dealt with by the justice system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when works will be undertaken to address repeated flooding at Lower Crescent, Comber.
answer_text: Road drainage systems are designed to manage average daily rainfall on the carriageway, not short-duration, high-intensity rainfall events that are becoming more frequent. During such events, systems can become temporarily overwhelmed and in many instances the road drainage system connects to third party systems, and its performance dictates overall capacity.Lower Crescent is on a programme for investigation. Once complete the Department will be in a better position to determine if any action can be taken to relieve the flooding at this location. We hope to complete these investigations by end March 2026, in the meantime, routine cleaning will continue to be carried out maximise performance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when works will be undertaken to address gulley issues on the Cherryvalley Line, Comber, near St Mary’s Primary School.
answer_text: My Department places high importance on the need to adequately undertake day-to-day essential maintenance activities such as gully emptying, to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. Allocations of funding to maintain our roads have been less than required for many years and as a result, my Department has only been able to deliver a limited service across a range of functions including gully emptying. This year, however, I have been able to identify additional funding for the systems of pipeline and manholes which, along with the gullies, make up the drainage network.Work has been programmed to clean gullies and remove tree roots from the pipeline at this location. It is hoped that this work will be completed before the end of March 2026.The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made so my Department can deliver important aspects of road maintenance, such as gully cleaning, ensuring public safety and an efficient transport network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how far in advance media outlets are required to submit questions to the Minister, for answer at pre-arranged interviews; and (ii) whether this is consistently applied to all media outlets.
answer_text: Several media outlets have dedicated reporters and correspondents who cover health and social care issues. They are well known to those in the DoH Press Office, as are the general news journalists who might be assigned to cover health-related issues. It is not the practice to formally request questions in advance beyond the general subject area and any specific media interest is normally gauged through informal conversations on the day of the event/facility.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when works will be carried out to address persistent ponding at Bridge Street Link, Comber.
answer_text: Road drainage systems are designed to manage average daily rainfall on the carriageway, not short-duration, high-intensity rainfall events that are becoming more frequent. During such events, systems can become temporarily overwhelmed and in many instances the road drainage system connects to third party systems, and its performance dictates overall capacity.Gully cleaning operations are currently underway in the Comber area and include the gullies on Bridge Street Link. We expect these gullies to be cleaned by end March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when works will be undertaken to address repeated flooding at the Square, Comber.
answer_text: Gully cleaning operations are currently ongoing in the Comber area and will be completed by end March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of inspections, related to (i) licensed; and (ii) unlicensed asbestos works, that have been carried out by the Health and Safety Executive Northern Ireland, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Inspections relating to licensed asbestos works undertaken by HSENI in the calendar years 2021 – 2025 are (Total 152); 2021 (26); 2022 (25); 2023 (26); 2024 (35); 2025 (40).  In addition to these inspections, HSENI completed asbestos relicensing assessment of contractors for calendar years 2021-2025 (Total- 28); 2021 (5); 2022 (6); 2023 (6); 2024 (4); 2025 (7).	HSENI does not maintain a record of non-licensed inspections.  A contractor undertaking non-licensed asbestos works is not required to provide notification of those works to HSENI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the order in which media outlets are invited to ask questions at Ministerial press conferences; and (ii) how this order is determined.
answer_text: There is no formal or fixed order for inviting media outlets to ask questions at Ministerial press conferences.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline her Department's priorities for tourism growth in the Strangford constituency over the next five years.
answer_text: My Department’s priorities for tourism across the north are set out in the 10 Year Tourism Vision and Action Plan, including growing high-quality experiences and ensuring that there is an adequate mix and scale of tourist accommodation to meet visitor expectations. Tourism NI is exploring the rollout of a Destination Stewardship approach to delivering tourism and to achieve Regional Balance.  The Strangford area will be considered with other destinations across the north as part of this process. Tourism NI continue to work closely with several tourism businesses in the Strangford area as part of the Embrace a Giant Spirit portfolio. These include Castle Espie near Comber, Strangford Lough Activity Centre and Tracey's Farmhouse Kitchen, near Killinchy.  Also, Tourism NI’s five-year Food and Drink Experience Development Plan has supported distilleries in or near the Strangford area such as Echlinville and Copeland who have benefited from island-wide learning journeys, one-to-one business mentoring and promotional activity through the NI Spirits Trail and Distillery Passport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of prosecutions that have been brought for breaches of asbestos regulations, in the last five years, and (ii) the outcomes of those prosecutions.
answer_text: In the past five years four prosecutions for breaches of asbestos regulations have been brought by the Public Prosecution Service Northern Ireland (PPSNI) following an investigation by the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI). These prosecutions resulted in the sentencing of four dutyholders, with fines passed totalling £41,500.00.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assurances that can be given to local meat producers that imports under the EU-Mercosur trade agreement will be held to the same regulatory standard as our producers.
answer_text: I recognise the concerns among local meat producers in relation to the standards of meat imports under an EU-Mercosur trade agreement. It is my understanding that all meat imports into the EU from Mercosur countries (or any country) will continue to be subject to the same Sanitary and Phytosanitary (SPS) requirements as those applied to domestic EU producers. I expect the EU to have the necessary checks and controls in place to ensure that this is the case.I recognise that concerns have been expressed about standards in areas outside of SPS regulations such as the environment.  Trade agreements in general do not involve legislative alignment.  The requirements of the EU – Mercosur trade agreement in these areas and its overall content is a matter for the parties to the agreement.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any media outlets invited to attend (i) Ministerial; and (ii) Departmental, press conferences, in each of the last two years.
answer_text: As my Department holds press conferences frequently, often at short notice, it does not maintain a record of invitations to media outlets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how far in advance media outlets are required to submit questions to the Minister, for answer at pre-arranged interviews; and (ii) whether this is consistently applied to all media outlets.
answer_text: The Department does not require journalists to submit questions to the Minister for answer at pre-arranged interviews and this is applied consistently to all outlets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact of the EU-Mercosur trade agreement on the local meat industry.
answer_text: The terms of the EU and Mercosur free trade agreement and the consideration of any potential benefits and impacts are matters for those parties involved in its negotiation. I have noted that the EU have stated that the agreement will allow for 99,000 tonnes of beef from Mercosur to enter the EU market with a 7.5% duty of which 55% of this quota will consist of fresh or chilled meat and 45% of lower-value frozen meat. The volume represents 1.5% of total European beef production.  For poultry, the agreement will allow tariff free access for 180,000 tonnes with this volume being phased in over 5 years.  This represents 1.3% of EU production.   I understand the concerns raised by farmers and others about the potential impact of this agreement. An increase in imports into the EU can impact on the NI meat sector via increased competition for the domestic consumer market and by displacement of NI beef or poultry exports from the EU market.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been made regarding the quality of meat imports into the local food market should the EU-Mercosur trade agreement be ratified, given the differing regulations in the South American countries which are signatories to that trade agreement.
answer_text: I am aware of concerns within the agri-food sector around the quality of meat products entering the local food market given the differing regulations in certain South American countries. It is my understanding that all existing meat imports from Mercosur countries, and any additional imports that may occur under the EU – Mercosur trade agreement, should it come into force, must comply with EU Sanitary and Phytosanitary (SPS) regulations.  I expect the EU to have the necessary checks and controls in place to ensure that this is the case.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any planned or proposed sea defence works her Department intends to undertake, in the next five years, including the (i) location; (ii) estimated cost; and (iii) proposed date for the commencement of works.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate is currently progressing a number of studies to assess the viability of tidal flood alleviation proposals. These studies include a tidal Flood Alleviation Scheme in the Derry area. The options under consideration include measures to reduce the impact of coastal flooding within Derry City. In addition, a feasibility study is currently underway to confirm the extent of tidal flood risk in Newry as part of a wider flood alleviation programme for that area. This will include work to determine whether a viable scheme can be identified to reduce the tidal flood risk.Further feasibility studies are also ongoing for Warrenpoint, Newtownards, Larne, Newtownabbey and Carrickfergus. These studies aim to establish the extent of tidal, as well as fluvial (river), flood risk and to identify if there are any viable practical measures that could be developed to reduce the risks. At this stage the total estimated cost to progress these studies is in the region of £2.5M and whilst we do not yet have any estimated construction costs, as this will be determined through the feasibility study process, we envisage that the total construction costs could be in excess of £40million.In addition, my Department’s Rivers Directorate Operations, through routine inspections, anticipate carrying out concrete revetment repair works over the next five years on the four Lough Foyle Sea Defences. The cost of these works will be approximately £80k. Further locations may be identified for works following routine inspections or after significant weather events.My Department’s Roads Division advise that sea defences immediately adjacent to a carriageway are often maintained as part of the road network and are frequently an integral part of the road structure. The works associated with this type of sea defences are principally reactive in nature with funding allocated on the basis of need. My Roads Division do not hold a five-year programme for such works.I can also advise the following planned investments on sea defence works by Translink. Future investment is subject to the availability of funding and visibility of budgets to permit strategic planning.Council Area2026/27£2027/28£2028/29£2029/30£2030/31£Total £Derry City & Strabane787k56k57k56k57k1,013kCauseway Coast & Glens804k22k22k22k22k892kMid & East Antrim162k198k4,781k4,820k453k10,414kAntrim & Newtownabbey17k17k17k17k17k85kArds & North Down4k2k4k2k4k16kTotal Annual Spend1,774k295k4,881k4,917k553k

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the media outlets invited to attend (i) Ministerial; and (ii) Departmental, press conferences.
answer_text: My Department does not maintain a record of media outlets invited to press conferences. As Minister, I speak to a wide range of news outlets in order to communicate to the public about the work of the department and respond to relevant issues of the day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 8645/22-27, whether there is any update on measures to be introduced regarding the regulation of cosmetic treatments such as botox.
answer_text: In the short time that remains of this mandate, my Department has no current plans to introduce mandatory regulation for non-surgical cosmetic procedures. However, I can assure you that this remains an important issue for me and my Department. The risk to public safety posed by the unlawful distribution and misuse of unauthorised medicinal products within the non-surgical cosmetic sector in Northern Ireland is a serious issue. Such breaches relating to Northern Ireland can be referred to the Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group for investigation under the Human Medicines Regulations 2012. The Department continues to take decisive action to combat illegal promotion, supply or misuse of medicines and to alerting the public about the dangers of using unauthorised medicines outside of the regulated supply chain. We remain vigilant and continue to work with key partner agencies to monitor the illicit marketplace and will continue to take effective action where necessary to protect public safety in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail all (i) Ministerial press conferences; and (ii) Departmental press conferences, that have been held in the last two years.
answer_text: My Department does not keep a running tally of press conferences or media interviews in which I engage as Minister.  As Minister, I speak regularly to media outlets in order to communicate to the public about the work of the department and respond to relevant issues of the day.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) how far in advance media outlets are required to submit questions to her for answer for pre-arranged interviews; and (ii) whether this is consistently applied to all media outlets.
answer_text: There is no requirement for media outlets to submit questions to the Department of Justice in advance of pre-arranged interviews, though on occasion some may elect to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the order in which media outlets are invited to ask questions at Ministerial press conferences; and (ii) how this order is determined.
answer_text: The order in which media outlets ask questions at Ministerial press conferences is determined on a case-by-case basis in conjunction with the attending media, taking into account issues such as deadlines of the attending media.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how far in advance media outlets are required to submit questions to the Minister for answer for pre-arranged interviews; and (ii) whether this is consistently applied to all media outlets.
answer_text: There is no formal requirement for media outlets to submit questions in advance of pre-arranged interviews. It would be normal practice that media outlets would indicate the broad context within which an interview was being sought rather than merely indicate they wanted an interview. While some broadcasters or publications may provide indicative topics beforehand, it is not mandatory. The Minister and the Department respond to questions during interviews without requiring prior submission.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail all (i) Ministerial press conferences; and (ii) Departmental press conferences, that have been held in the last 2 years.
answer_text: The Department does not routinely keep an official record of media engagements it holds involving the Minister or Departmental officials including press conferences.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the order in which media outlets are invited to ask questions at Ministerial press conferences; and (ii) how this order is determined.
answer_text: There is no formal or fixed order for inviting media outlets to ask questions at Ministerial press conferences. The approach is informal and designed to ensure fairness and balance. Typically, the Minister or a Press Office facilitator will invite questions in turn and will ensure each outlet gets an opportunity to ask a question.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the media outlets invited to attend (i) Ministerial; and (ii) Departmental, press conferences.
answer_text: The Department does not operate a fixed list of media outlets for press conferences. Decisions on invitations to press conferences are at the discretion of the Department’s press office and the Minister and are based on a number of factors – for example, the subject matter’s relevance to specific news audiences or the timing of the press conference. Invitations are typically extended to the main Northern Ireland-based media organisations and relevant sector-specific publications.  This approach ensures broad and balanced coverage and helps make information accessible to the public.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the results of laboratory tests showed that bluetongue positive results were because of prior vaccination or by infection.
answer_text: I can confirm that all bluetongue serotype 3 (BTV-3) positive laboratory results were due to virus infection.  None of the animals which tested positive for BTV-3, from the four infected premises, had been vaccinated for bluetongue, nor had any livestock on these premises been vaccinated for bluetongue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) coastal locations which have been identified by her Department as being at risk of erosion, over the next (a) ten ; (b) 25; and (c) 50, years.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate maintains designated sea defences that offer protection from sea flooding and is responsible for 26km of these sea defences comprising of 43 individual structures. The defences are located at the Foyle estuary, Belfast, Strangford and Carlingford Loughs.  In January 2021, inspections identified that five of our sea defence assets (21%) across the network required specific works on small sections to bring them up to a satisfactory condition. This resulted in 5.5km of Rivers sea defence assets being entirely upgraded to a good condition.  In Ballykelly, for example, revetment restoration works included replacing sections of the sea facing surface with concrete which may assist in preventing erosion.  These works, carried out over a 4 month period, ensure that we continue to protect communities from coastal flooding.  Expenditure associated with the routine inspection and maintenance of sea defences carried out by my Department’s Rivers Directorate are absorbed within existing line-of-business budgets. Ringfencing budgets specifically for sea defences is not necessary.During this time my Department has also completed the construction of the £33m Belfast Tidal Flood Alleviation Scheme which involved the design and construction of tidal flood defences along approximately 8.5km of the tidal River Lagan.DfI Roads allocations for Coastal Defence spend, impacted by coastal erosion or storms, is absorbed within existing Structural Stability funding, which is part of the overall Capital Structural Maintenance budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether a Social Services registration is a pre-requisite for a pre-school, before it is added to the Education Authority’s portal for admissions.
answer_text: Social Services registration is a pre-requisite for a setting’s participation in the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP).  In some circumstances the EA may support a new setting joining the PSEP by facilitating its inclusion in the admissions portal while the necessary paperwork for registration is in progress with the Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT).  Settings are, however, not permitted to provide pre-school education provision through the PSEP without Social Services registration.The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) are responsible for securing sufficient pre-school provision in local areas to meet demand for funded pre-school education places.  If a setting does not meet the requirements for participation in the PSEP, including Social Services registration, the EA will make alternative arrangements to ensure that sufficient pre-school places are available in the affected area.  Any setting that considers it may have difficulty meeting the HSCT requirements for registration should contact the EA in the first instance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any measurable progress there has been as a result of the Speeding Up Justice programme.
answer_text: ANSWERAt present, the Department publishes case processing times (based on median days), as an official statistic and is published annually. The Criminal Justice Board also has access to more regular management information, which it uses to monitor delay across the system. Although there is more to be done, progress has been made with a 7.8% reduction in case processing times across 2023/24 and 0.5% across 2024/25. The withdrawal of services by the CBA is likely to contribute to further delay.  The Speeding Up Justice Programme is a five-year programme of work.  Some of the key milestones include: The first phase of Committal Reform was implemented in October 2022, seeing the abolition of the option of calling victims and witnesses to provide oral evidence during committal proceedings in the magistrates’ court. The launch of the second phase of Committal Reform - direct committal is due to be introduced in November 2026. The introduction of an expansion to the range of out of court disposals available to the PSNI. A 12-week consultation closed on 28 September 2025 and my department is currently analysing responses and next steps.   A No File Decision pilot was commenced with formal evaluation currently underway. Preliminary findings are positive and, subject to final evaluation, the plan would be to baseline this approach as business as usual. The exploration of opportunities for early engagement between police and prosecution and prosecution and defence, to speed up case progression and maximise efficiencies. This workstream is also exploring options in relation to ‘Better Case Management’, with a pilot scheduled to launch early this year.  The Department has also progressed work on Remit of the Magistrates Court project and developed options around giving District Judges in magistrates’ courts greater sentencing powers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any support in place for (i) parents; and (ii) other pre-school settings, in the event of a pre-school not being successful in obtaining Social Services registration.
answer_text: Social Services registration is a pre-requisite for a setting’s participation in the Pre-School Education Programme (PSEP).  In some circumstances the EA may support a new setting joining the PSEP by facilitating its inclusion in the admissions portal while the necessary paperwork for registration is in progress with the Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT).  Settings are, however, not permitted to provide pre-school education provision through the PSEP without Social Services registration.The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) are responsible for securing sufficient pre-school provision in local areas to meet demand for funded pre-school education places.  If a setting does not meet the requirements for participation in the PSEP, including Social Services registration, the EA will make alternative arrangements to ensure that sufficient pre-school places are available in the affected area.  Any setting that considers it may have difficulty meeting the HSCT requirements for registration should contact the EA in the first instance.

answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate maintains designated sea defences that offer protection from sea flooding and is responsible for 26km of these sea defences comprising of 43 individual structures. The defences are located at the Foyle estuary, Belfast, Strangford and Carlingford Loughs.  In January 2021, inspections identified that five of our sea defence assets (21%) across the network required specific works on small sections to bring them up to a satisfactory condition. This resulted in 5.5km of Rivers sea defence assets being entirely upgraded to a good condition.  In Ballykelly, for example, revetment restoration works included replacing sections of the sea facing surface with concrete which may assist in preventing erosion.  These works, carried out over a 4 month period, ensure that we continue to protect communities from coastal flooding.  Expenditure associated with the routine inspection and maintenance of sea defences carried out by my Department’s Rivers Directorate are absorbed within existing line-of-business budgets. Ringfencing budgets specifically for sea defences is not necessary.During this time my Department has also completed the construction of the £33m Belfast Tidal Flood Alleviation Scheme which involved the design and construction of tidal flood defences along approximately 8.5km of the tidal River Lagan.DfI Roads allocations for Coastal Defence spend, impacted by coastal erosion or storms, is absorbed within existing Structural Stability funding, which is part of the overall Capital Structural Maintenance budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Norwegian barnehage model for early years child care has been considered or assessed by his Department.
answer_text: My officials have considered a range of international early years and childcare models, including the barnehage model, during the development of the draft Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) Strategy. However, early years systems in other jurisdictions operate within specific legislative, social and funding contexts.My Department is focused on developing a bespoke Early Learning and Childcare Strategy that is tailored to the needs of children and families in Northern Ireland, drawing on relevant local and international evidence where appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to replace the sea wall on the Portaferry Road, near Mount Stewart.
answer_text: Following Storm Bram on 9 December 2025, my Department has carried out detailed surveys along the A20 Portaferry Road and A2 identifying damage to sea defences that fall under the Department’s responsibility. Two sections near the Mount Stewart Estate have been identified as requiring repair, one at Mount Stewart Road and another at ‘The Swan Hole’ in Greyabbey.Temporary emergency repairs were completed immediately after the storm to ensure the safety of the route. We have now engaged with DAERA to secure the necessary permissions for permanent repair works. At present, both the A20 Portaferry Road and A2 remain safe for all road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration her Department has given to ringfencing maintenance budgets for sea defences and coastal protection works, for relevant local council areas.
answer_text: My Department’s Rivers Directorate is responsible for the inspection and maintenance of over 26 kilometres of sea defences to provide protection against coastal flooding. These assets are managed through a programme of risk-based inspections, which identify defects, assign Condition Grades and generate repair lists to inform maintenance planning. Completed maintenance works are typically funded out of the Rivers Operations Annual Maintenance budget. Sea defences immediately adjacent to a carriageway are often maintained as part of the road network and are frequently an integral part of the road structure. Therefore, funding for repairs and maintenance of sea defences associated with roads is funded out of the Structural Maintenance budget on the basis of need.Sea defences which Translink are responsible for are progressed as business as usual within their capital programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the date when works will commence to repair the estate wall on the Mountstewart Road.
answer_text: Harvesting and clearance work is nearing completion.  Consideration of residual work associated with storm damage is ongoing between Forest Service and National Trust.  To date no work has been commissioned either jointly or individually by either party regarding wall repairs. This issue is an ongoing matter between Forest Service and National Trust, with consideration being given to lease arrangements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact the change in the proposed inheritance tax thresholds from £1 million to £2.5 million will have on farms.
answer_text: I welcome the decision taken by the UK Government to reconsider its planned changes to Inheritance Tax and increase the combined Agricultural Property Relief (APR) and Business Property Relief (BPR) allowance threshold for 100% relief from £1 million to £2.5 million. I also welcome the decision announced in the 2025 Budget to make the threshold transferable between spouses. Analysis undertaken by my Department in December 2024 revealed that approximately 50% of farms in Northern Ireland, accounting for 80% of total land farmed, would have been impacted by an inheritance tax threshold of £1 million. In light of the changes, additional analysis indicates that farms of a size greater that 48 hectares, are likely to have agricultural and business assets valued at over the £2.5 million and are therefore still potentially impacted by the inheritance tax policy. 17.5% of total NI farms, which account for around half of total area farmed, exceed 48 hectares of owned land. In cases where the allowance transfers from a spouse, a threshold of £5 million is available. At this threshold, analysis indicates that the number of farms impacted would be around 5% of total NI farms, equating to 23% of total area farmed. However, not all farmers (i.e. those without a spouse) will be able to avail of this combined £5 million allowance.Despite the substantial increase in the threshold, I recognise that some farms will still be impacted by the changes. Moreover, I acknowledge the risk that, if land prices in Northern Ireland continue to rise, an increasing number of farms may become subject to inheritance tax liabilities over time.	While the increase in the APR/BPR threshold is a welcome step forward, I continue to urge the UK Government to explore additional measures to safeguard family farms still affected by the reforms, particularly those owned by elderly or terminally ill farmers who cannot plan for such changes. I would also urge that the APR/BPR thresholds be kept under review given rising land prices, especially in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has planned any upgrades to the drainage infrastructure in Comber, to address recurring incidents of localised flooding.
answer_text: Road drainage systems are designed to manage average daily rainfall on the carriageway, not short-duration, high-intensity rainfall events that are becoming more frequent. During such events, systems can become temporarily overwhelmed and in many instances the road drainage system connects to third party systems, and its performance dictates overall capacity.During the Autumn months flooding occurs when intense rainfall combines with seasonal debris, restricting access to drainage gullies. Once the rain subsides, surface water begins to recede, indicative of the primary cause being short-term weather conditions rather than a recurring issue. This could be further reduced through targeted and timely sweeping operations by local Councils.Currently we have no plans to upgrade the road or river drainage infrastructure in Comber, however a number of gullies and pipelines are scheduled to be jetted and cleaned with any necessary follow up action being undertaken subject to available resources. We will continue to carry out routine inspections to maintain performance. My Department also undertook significant maintenance work during the summer months of 2025 on the section of the Comber (Enler) River extending from Enler Gardens to the Killinchy Road Bridge. We will continue to inspect and maintain designated watercourses and their associated infrastructure within the Comber catchment, in line with our maintenance standards. If an inspection determines that there is a blockage significantly impeding the free flow of water within a watercourse or that drainage infrastructure is in an unsatisfactory condition, then works will be programmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the welfare standards that apply to deer culling; and (ii) how compliance with those standards is monitored and enforced.
answer_text: The Wildlife Order (NI) 1985 (as amended) sets out certain measures that broadly protect deer welfare including a ‘closed season’ when they cannot be killed and specifying the weapons and ammunition that may be used to kill them humanely.There is no routine monitoring programme and enforcement is carried out by PSNI, with a focus on poaching.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any advice was provided to his Department prior to or during the original High Court hearing, which led to the quashing of the planning permission for the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme, that would have allowed the issues now being raised under appeal by his Department to be brought forward at that stage.
answer_text: My Department was not a party to the original High Court proceedings. DAERA was accepted as an intervenor in the Appeal proceedings on 11 November 2025. My Department has provided an affidavit to the Court of Appeal explaining the extent of its awareness of the proceedings in the High Court and why it only intervened on appeal to the Court of Appeal. As these matters are subject to ongoing legal proceedings, it is not appropriate for me to comment further.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the rational as to why matters now being advanced by his Department in the Department for Infrastructure appeal against the quashing of planning for the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme were not raised during the original High Court proceedings.
answer_text: My Department was not a party to the original High Court proceedings. Pursuant to the High Court judgment, my Department then applied to join the Appeal as an intervening party. DAERA was accepted as an intervenor in the Appeal proceedings on 11 November 2025. My Department has provided an affidavit to the Court of Appeal explaining the extent of its awareness of the proceedings in the High Court and why it only intervened on appeal to the Court of Appeal. As these matters are subject to ongoing legal proceedings, it is not appropriate for me to comment further.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any actions he intends to take to restore proper financial oversight and accountability at the Department for the Economy, and its arm’s-length bodies, following the disclaimed opinion given by the Northern Ireland Audit Office, for the second year in a row.
answer_text: My officials have reviewed the Comptroller & Auditor General findings from the 2024-25 Department for the Economy (DfE) Annual Report and Accounts and are pleased to note that DfE has made improvements on certain issues identified in the previous Audit reports. I welcome the commitment DfE has made to continue implementing a series of targeted improvements to strengthen the 2025–26 Group Accounts process. This is a highly complex group consolidation, due to differing accounting frameworks, systems, reporting requirements, and different year ends. I acknowledge the progress that has been made to date and it is important to see continued progress. Due to the use of comparatives in annual report and accounts, you will appreciate that the disclaimed opinion will take several years to unwind and my officials will continue to support and assist DfE colleagues to achieve this goal as soon as possible. It is important to note that the issues highlighted by the NI Audit Office do not call into question the approach of the Department or its Arm’s Length Bodies to value for money, propriety, or the management of its budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will undertake a new traffic impact study, to reflect the population growth and housing developments in the Comber area.
answer_text: The Eastern Transport Plan (ETP) will set the framework for making transport policy and investment decisions up until 2035 for a five council area including Ards and North Down. It will therefore consider transport improvements for Comber as it aims to ensure that the transport network meets the needs of the people and businesses living, working and visiting the ETP area, both now and in the future.The ETP is being integrated with the Local Development Plan of the five applicable councils to ensure population growth and locations are appropriately considered within an integrated transport and land use planning process. Due to the five local council areas being at different stages in their Local Development Plans, the ETP will be published in iterations. The Ards and North Down iteration will be published in line with the timetable for publication of the council’s Local Policies Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how much resource remains from the Barnett consequentials arising from Grenfell Remediation monies.
answer_text: The Executive received Barnett consequentials linked to building remediation in financial years 2018-19, 2019-20 and 2020-21.However, as Barnett consequentials provided at a Spending Reviews are identified at Whitehall department level rather than individual programme level, it is not possible to identify the full amount of consequentials received by the Executive as a result of Whitehall spending on building remediation.  Barnett consequentials are unhypothicated and must be utilised in the year they are received, with only limited scope for carry-over under the Budget Exchange Scheme. This means that no resource arising from building remediation consequentials remain—as they have already been allocated and utilised in line with Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when they received the results of the advanced testing from laboratories in Pirbright and Germany in relation to the suspected bluetongue outbreak in December 2025.
answer_text: Bluetongue virus, serotype 3 (BTV-3) was confirmed at four separate premises in Northern Ireland in December 2025. 	The first infected premises close to Bangor, County Down received confirmatory laboratory results for BTV-3 from the UK National Reference Laboratory (NRL) Pirbright on 04 December 2025 and from the EU Reference Laboratory the Friedrich-Loeffler-Institut (FLI) Germany on 5 December 2025.The second infected premises near Greyabbey, County Down received confirmatory laboratory results for BTV-3 from the UK NRL Pirbright on 15 December 2025 and from the EU Reference Laboratory FLI Germany on 17 December 2025.The third infected premises near Bangor, County Down also received confirmatory laboratory results for BTV-3 from the UK NRL Pirbright on 15 December 2025 and from the EU Reference Laboratory FLI Germany on 17 December 2025. The fourth infected premises near Kircubbin, County Down received confirmatory laboratory results for BTV-3 from the UK NRL Pirbright on 18 December 2025 and from the EU Reference Laboratory FLI Germany on 19 December 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of cattle aborted as a consequence of bluetongue, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department is not aware of any cattle which have aborted as a consequence of bluetongue, in the last 12 months in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of cattle which have died as a consequence of bluetongue, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department is not aware of any cattle which have died as a consequence of bluetongue in the last 12 months in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether bluetongue was confirmed by either of the laboratories in Pirbright or Germany, following the suspected bluetongue outbreak in Co Down in December 2025.
answer_text: Bluetongue Virus Serotype 3 (BTV-3) was confirmed by both laboratories.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any assessment that has been made of the impact that the current shortfall facing his Department for the 2025/26 financial year will have on core education services.
answer_text: Our Education system continues to face unavoidable cost pressures and rising service demands associated with delivering statutory and policy obligations that the current budget does not fully address.My Department’s allocation for 2025-26 post-December monitoring remains significantly less than what is required and leaves a considerable shortfall across the Education sector. The Education Authority currently forecasts a Resource DEL overspend of circa £253m for 2025-26 in respect of inescapable and pre-committed pressures only.Only the most limited funding is available for SEN, schools, transport, school meals and schools’ maintenance.  This has had an extremely damaging impact on the education system, with our children with Special Education Needs facing the most significant impact.My Department, in conjunction with the Education Authority continues to work through what options are available and what difficult decisions can be taken forward to constrain Education’s current year spend in areas that are not deemed essential or unavoidable, to the fullest extent possible. As we look ahead, my Department will continue to seek to identify areas that could potentially deliver a significant reduction in the education cost base, whilst aiming to protect the overall quality of education delivery. However, to be enacted, these measures will require support of the Executive.  I have worked hard to protect areas such as Sure Start and Youth Services, but my Department’s budget is so limited that continuing to fund vital services such as these is set to become impossible.  In respect of my Department’s forecast overcommitment for 2025-26, under current budgeting requirements, any net overspend at Executive level will reduce the amount of funding available in future years.  Clearly, this should be a cause for concern and I am engaged with the Finance Minister on this matter.In closing, I recognise the inherent value of education and the key role it plays in the future economic stability of our society.  We cannot afford to continue this neglect of education which we now see through years of underfunding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the alternatives to the revocation of staff parking permits at the Royal Victoria Hospital that have been, or are being, explored or costed, such as staff-only zones and shuttle services from designated secure car parks.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he will commit to extend the decision to revoke staff parking permits at the Royal Victoria Hospital until a fully consultative and risk-assessed long-term plan is agreed with staff representatives.
answer_text: The aim of the Department of Health’s policy on car parking is to ensure the effective provision of car parking for patients, visitors and staff. Decisions on how the Department’s policy is applied is for each Health and Social Care Trust to determine to enable them to take local issues into account. The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) have confirmed that the new criteria-based parking permit application process for the Royal Victoria Hospital remains paused and all staff who have previously held a car parking permit have retained their permit. The BHSCT Executive Team is considering the feasibility of increasing the number of available permits, the potential to provide alternative transport solutions, and the ability to reduce demand at the site. In line with the approach adopted when developing the initial application process, the Department of Health expects BHSCT to engage with staff representatives when considering the options to amend the criteria or reduce the level of unmet demand for staff parking permits.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been undertaken of nappy recycling schemes in other jurisdictions which could be applied in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: An Absorbent Hygiene Products study is currently being undertaken by the Waste & Resources Action Programme (WRAP) on behalf of my Department and will include information on AHP waste including nappies and nappy recycling in other jurisdictions. 		My officials would be happy to meet with you to discuss further.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will publish any risk assessments carried out in relation to staff safety when travelling to and from the Royal Victoria Hospital site for late-night or early morning shifts without secure on-site parking.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) are not responsible for staff safety when travelling to and from the Royal Victoria Hospital (RVH). If staff encounter any security incidents / concerns when travelling to and from the RVH site for late-night or early morning shifts these should be reported to the Police Service of Northern Ireland. The safety and security of their staff is a key priority for the Trust and in response, staff are offered training, awareness and support around how they can protect their own personal safety.The BHSCT also considers both organisational and staff needs when assessing on site staff parking permit allocations by including the following criteria: Carry out shift working as a requirement of the post and a set rota (scheduled rota to regularly start before 7:30am or finish after 7:30pm) and A public transport journey that involves one or more changes and exceeds 1 hour of a commute.To assist in deterring crime and making staff and visitors feel safer, the onsite car parking at the RVH site is supported by CCTV, lighting, access control, and night security regularly transiting the site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the paused decision on revoking staff parking permits at the Royal Victoria Hospital, to detail any engagement his Department has had with Translink to determine whether public transport can realistically accommodate shift patterns in critical care areas late at night and during emergencies.
answer_text: Departmental and Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) officials engage regularly with Translink representatives at both a regional and local level.Ongoing issues including the funding of public transport services, the lack of public transport in the early hours and late evenings as well as options for covering the final mile journey between Grand Central Station and the Royal Victoria Hospital (RVH) site are discussed. Translink also share their plans in relation to future service provision and development of the Glider service.  Improvements to the bus services to the RVH have taken place over recent years with enhanced service provision and monthly roadshows with representatives from Sustrans, Translink and the Trust’s Travel Plan Co-ordinator offering advice to staff on alternative sustainable commute options. The new criteria-based parking permit application process for the RVH remains paused and the BHSCT Executive Team is considering the feasibility of increasing the number of available permits, the potential to provide alternative transport solutions, and the ability to reduce demand at the site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the dates of any meetings held with local councils by his officials where a potential nappy recycling scheme was discussed, since taking office.
answer_text: My officials are involved with ongoing collaboration with local councils, through the Government Waste Working Group and the Council Waste Forum over matters relating to waste management and recycling.  However, there has not been specific engagement solely relating to a potential nappy recycling scheme at this stage. Consideration was given to absorbent hygiene products in the recycling consultation, which helped inform a Departmental Response. I hope to publish this shortly following Executive approval.  Publication will be supported by guidance to councils, including provisions for families and householders who are using nappies or absorbent hygiene products.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of people employed by his Department, or arm's-length bodies, who are involved in deer management.
answer_text: Forest Service undertakes deer management, on behalf of DAERA, for the protection and sustainable management of the forests on its estate.   Forest Service delivers this through the employment of two wildlife rangers who carry out wildlife screening and planned deer culling where necessary.  The wildlife rangers are supported and managed by two foresters as part of their overall forest estate management role.  DAERA does not have responsibility for or undertake wider deer management outside its forest protection role.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
