question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the date when movements will be permitted out of the current Temporary Control Zones; (ii) any tests that will be required; and (iii) whether the cost of the tests will be covered by his Department.
answer_text: Current movement restrictions in place as a result of the Temporary Control Zones are under active consideration.  Updates will be communicated directly to all stakeholders where appropriate and will be available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider providing funding or support for a pilot project to test the viability of nappy recycling, in partnership with local councils.
answer_text: Consideration was given to absorbent hygiene products in the recycling consultation, which helped inform a Departmental Response which I hope to publish shortly following Executive approval.  Publication will be supported by guidance to councils, including provisions for families and householders who are using nappies or absorbent hygiene products, 	While work is ongoing in the Department to assess the feasibility of separate collections of nappies and other Absorbent Hygiene Products (AHPs), future decisions will be taken in the context of the recycling reforms proposed in the Departmental Response.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how he will address disposable nappy waste as part of his Department’s circular economy strategy.
answer_text: The Circular Economy Strategy is the responsibility of my colleague Dr Caoimhe Archibald, the Minister for the Department for the Economy.The collection of nappies and other AHP waste as a separate waste stream is the subject of a study currently being undertaken by the Waste & Resources Action Programme (WRAP) on behalf of DAERAConsideration was given to absorbent hygiene products in the recycling consultation, which helped inform a Departmental Response. I hope to publish this shortly following Executive approval.  Publication will be supported by guidance to councils, including provisions for families and householders who are using nappies or absorbent hygiene products.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has undertaken a cost-benefit analysis of a nappy recycling scheme, compared to continued landfill disposal.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, we have made great progress in reducing the amount of waste we send to landfill in recent years. NI Local Authority Collected Municipal Waste Management Statistics released on 11 December 2025 show a landfill rate of 14% for 2024/2025, compared to 18.4% for 2023/2024. This is the lowest rate ever recorded.The collection of nappies and other AHP waste as a separate waste stream is the subject of a study currently being undertaken by the Waste & Resources Action Programme (WRAP) on behalf of DAERA.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement his Department has had with (i) manufacturers; and (ii) recycling companies, regarding nappy recycling, since taking office.
answer_text: My Department engages with manufacturers in relation to packaging waste under extended producer responsibility (EPR). Nappies and other AHP waste do not currently come under EPR.  While recycling companies are key DAERA stakeholders and there is regular engagement with them, there has been no specific engagement in respect of nappies and other AHP waste.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the extent to which deer contribute to the spread of bovine TB and other livestock diseases.
answer_text: Bovine tuberculosis (bTB) continues to be a major animal health issue in Northern Ireland, with its spread influenced by cattle-to-cattle transmission, the survival of its causative agent, Mycobacterium bovis, in the environment, and through disease reservoirs among wildlife.Current evidence points to deer being considered spill-over hosts rather than primary maintenance hosts. This means infection in deer usually occurs where bTB is already present in cattle or other wildlife, and the disease does not typically persist in deer populations without such external sources. In the Republic of Ireland one hot spot of high bTB prevalence has been observed in deer in Co. Wicklow.  However, this may simply reflect the higher density of the deer population in that area and does not provide conclusive evidence that bTB is self-sustaining in the local deer population or how much infected deer actually contribute to the spread of bTB in local cattle.Beyond bTB, deer can also act as reservoirs for other livestock diseases, such as liver fluke and certain gastrointestinal parasites.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the extent to which deer can contribute to the spread of bluetongue.
answer_text: Bluetongue is an insect-borne viral disease to which all species of ruminants are susceptible (including deer), although sheep are most severely affected. It does not affect humans.  Cattle and goats which often appear healthy can carry high levels of the virus and provide a source of further infection.The disease is caused by a virus which is transmitted by certain species of biting midges. Animal to animal transmission is not reported except through the transplacental route. The Culicoides species of midge which carries the infection is found in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of animals in Northern Ireland vaccinated against bluetongue to date.
answer_text: As of 12 December 2025, my department is aware that 662 animals have been vaccinated against bluetongue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any contact that he and his officials have had with the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine in relation to the movement of live animals for slaughter to the Republic of Ireland; (ii) his assessment of the impact of the refusal of factories in the Republic of Ireland to accept stock from Northern Ireland on the sector; and (iii) any discussions he has had with factories in Northern Ireland regarding capacity and prices, since the introduction of the bluetongue Temporary Control Zone.
answer_text: My officials have been in regular contact with counterparts in the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine in Ireland regarding the movement of live animals to slaughter in Ireland. Immediately following the confirmation of Bluetongue in NI, DAERA officials contacted DAFM to confirm that the trade in live cattle and sheep to direct slaughter in Ireland could continue without disruption. I have been made aware of reports that Irish abattoirs have refused to accept some Northern Irish lambs for slaughter after the confirmation of Bluetongue in Northern Ireland. I have been assured that while some abattoirs may have reduced their intake of Northern Irish lambs, this was as a result of other market factors and not related to the outbreak of Bluetongue in Northern Ireland. My officials and I will monitor the situation going forward.I have had no discussion with factories in Northern Ireland in relation to their capacity and prices since the introduction of the Bluetongue Temporary Control Zone.  Decisions on processing capacity and the prices paid to farmers are commercial matters for individual factories, and it would not be appropriate for me to intervene in those arrangements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether alternative non-lethal methods of deer management are required to be considered before authorising culling programmes.
answer_text: Article 21 (2) of the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985 permits the Department to issue licences to cull deer during the close season for the following purposes -Preserving public health and safetyConserving the natural heritagePreventing serious damage to propertyBefore granting a licence for any of these purposes, article 21 (3) states that the Department must be satisfied that there is no satisfactory alternative to taking and killing the deer of the species and description to which the application relates during the close season prescribed by Schedule 10’.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of deer culled under licence, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The number of deer culled under licence each year:YearNumber of deer culled20150201602017020180201912020122021020224202352024020250
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail on what basis a licence for deer culling measures can be grounded.
answer_text: Under the terms of article 21 of the Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985, licences can be issued to cull deer for the following –(1) Scientific or educational purposes, (2) Removing deer from one area to another, (3) Preserving public health or public safety, (4) Conserving the natural heritage, and (5) Preventing serious damage to property.Article 21 (3) specifies the detail that the Department must be satisfied with before granting a licence for these purposes. This includes confirmation that there is no satisfactory alternative to culling deer during the close season and that the applicant has a right of entry to the land for the purpose of killing or taking deer. For licences 3 and 4, DAERA must also be satisfied there is serious risk of the deer of the species and description to which the application relates either (3) putting public health or public safety at risk, or (4) causing deterioration of the natural heritage.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any financial support he will provide to Saintfield Livestock Market, given it is currently closed as it lies within the 20km Temporary Control Zone.
answer_text: There is no financial assistance planned at this time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any additional biosecurity or environmental control measures his Department will consider recommending or mandating to reduce the risk of further outbreaks in and around bluetongue affected zones from either biting midges or from animal-to-animal.
answer_text: My Department has no plans at this stage to mandate for any additional biosecurity or environmental control measures to reduce the risk of spread from either biting midges or from animal-to-animal in the bluetongue control zones. Bluetongue is primarily transmitted through biting midges, the bluetongue virus is not usually transmitted directly between animals however in certain circumstances, it can be passed onto the unborn foetus from its mother.Further information is available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongueAny updates to this developing situation will be made available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the current estimate of the number of (i) farms; and (ii) animals, located within the two 20km Temporary Control Zones.
answer_text: There are approximately 1100 cattle herds and approximately 980 sheep flocks registered in the Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) (TCZ relates to the entire area in County Down currently under restriction because of Bluetongue).  With respect to individual animal numbers, there are approximately 113,000 cattle and approximately 153,000 sheep.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any scientific assurances he has received regarding the effectiveness of the bluetongue vaccine in relation to the current strains of the disease.
answer_text: In July 2025, I permitted the voluntary use, under specific licence conditions, of three vaccines for Bluetongue serotype 3. Veterinary pharmaceutical products, including vaccines are authorised by the relevant Competent Authority: Veterinary Medicines Directorate (VMD) in the United Kingdom, and in Europe by the European Medicines Agency. I accept the assurances provided in these processes.Scientific appraisal of these vaccines is published by the VMD, including data sheet specifications and the Assessment Report. The selection of the most appropriate and effective product to use in any specific scenario, is one of professional determination by the attending private veterinarian.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail whether (i) there are any regulatory or legislative barriers preventing the (a) import of; or (b) use of, bluetongue vaccines in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the expiration of the grace period on veterinary medicines on 31 December 2025 will have an impact on these vaccines.
answer_text: I authorised the voluntary use of three bluetongue serotype 3 (BTV-3) vaccines in Northern Ireland, subject to licensing, from July 2025. The supply of BTV-3 vaccines to Northern Ireland and the supply of veterinary medicines more widely are matters under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the availability of the bluetongue vaccine; and (ii) whether there are sufficient supplies to meet the needs of farmers.
answer_text: I authorised the voluntary use of three bluetongue serotype 3 (BTV-3) vaccines in Northern Ireland, subject to licensing, from July 2025. These vaccines are intended to reduce the severity of clinical signs and mortality in affected animals, though they do not prevent infection or transmission. A decision to vaccinate animals is one for farmers to make in close consultation with their private veterinary practitioners.The supply of BTV-3 vaccines falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs. I am not aware of any issues regarding the supply of BTV-3 vaccines to Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any contingency process in place should demand outstrip supply of the bluetongue vaccine.
answer_text: The supply of BTV-3 vaccines to Northern Ireland and the supply of veterinary medicines more widely are matters under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether animals being vaccinated against bluetongue has any impact on trade and movement controls.
answer_text: With surveillance testing having now ended, and based on a further veterinary risk assessment, from Friday 19 December, all animals from farms that have not had a Bluetongue virus (BTV) case are free to move within and out of the Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) unless they are in certain high-risk categories that require proof of a negative pregnancy test, or pre-movement testing or proof of previous Bluetongue vaccination.  Further information on this development can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/news/changes-bluetongue-movement-restrictions Guidance for herd and flock keepers on moving animals within and from the TCZ can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/bluetongue-licences Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2020/688 provides for the movements of live animals within the EU. The currently available BTV3 vaccines do not meet the standards required to facilitate trade for Breeding and Production between Member States. The rules, however, allow for derogations whereby the Member State of destination accepts animals in compliance with certain animal health conditions. Derogations from 21 member states are published on the Commission website at https://food.ec.europa.eu/animals/animal-diseases/surveillance-eradication-programmes-and-disease-free-status/bluetongue_en. These are complex, technical and the requirements vary from country to country. Where derogations are available, they apply to the whole of NI, not just animals in a disease control zone. Such derogations may allow the use of the currently available BTV3 vaccines, but this is for individual Member States to decide. Moves from NI to GB for Breeding and Production from farms outside the TCZ are unaffected by vaccinal status. The conditions for movement from the TCZ to GB, including the requirements for vaccination, mirror those required for domestic moves.Vaccination status does not affect export moves for direct slaughter to either GB or the EU.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of licences for deer culling issued, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The number of licences for deer culling each year:Year Number of Licences Issued 2015020160201702018020191202012021020221202322024020250
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any increased testing for bluetongue is taking place outside of the 20km control zone.
answer_text: Following the detection of Bluetongue Virus (BTV) at a farm near Bangor, County Down, my department quickly implemented a 20km Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) around the holding.  This zone includes restrictions on movements of susceptible animals within and out of the area to mitigate the risk of onward spread of the virus. DAERA vets are now carrying out representative sampling and testing on large cattle herds in the TCZ to ascertain if there is further infection.Tracings are being conducted of any animals which moved onto or off the affected premises near Bangor, in the risk window from 1 September 2025.  Any holdings identified as receiving animals from the affected farm will be subject to surveillance and testing if required. A similar approach is now being taken with respect to the TCZ established on 6 December 2025 around a premises near Greyabbey.An enhanced active surveillance testing protocol was also introduced at all Northern Ireland abattoirs in June 2024, and this is ongoing.  You will be aware that it is as a result of this rigorous surveillance programme that my Department operates that Bluetongue was originally detected in cattle from the premises outside Bangor.In addition, any reports to DAERA by livestock keepers or private veterinarians throughout Northern Ireland of the suspicion of epizootic diseases such as Bluetongue will be thoroughly investigated by my veterinary officials, with the relevant surveillance conducted including sampling and testing at the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) in order to detect or rule out disease. Further consideration will be given to next steps once the epidemiological picture becomes clearer and we will remain in close contact with affected farmers, and wider industry, on next steps.  Updates on this developing situation will be made available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there were any movements of animals in the last six weeks into and out of the Clandeboye Estate.
answer_text: According to the Northern Ireland Food Animal Information System (NIFAIS) there were no moves in.  There were a number of moves out within the established risk window from 1 September 2025.  Tracings on these moves out have been completed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement he has had with the research team at Queen’s University, Belfast, in relation to the badger survey being carried out.
answer_text: My officials have met with Professor Dick from Queens University Belfast, both online and in person, to discuss his ongoing research into badger surveys using heat imaging technology and drones.Professor Dick has also been invited to meet with the TB Partnership Steering Group (TBPSG) to discuss his research and to provide insight into future usage of such technology.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how wild deer populations are monitored; and (ii) how often population assessments are carried out.
answer_text: Wild Deer populations can be monitored by direct observation counting or indirect counting methods. On open ground, direct counts can be undertaken on foot or by using helicopters or drones, while in woodlands, it may be possible to use thermal imagery or dung counts.  Deer frequently move between habitats which can present challenges when assessing populations.   There are currently no scientifically robust population estimates of wild deer population in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the animals which have tested positive for bluetongue in a farm outside Bangor, to detail (i) the dates on which the animals were tested; (ii) the dates on which the samples were sent for testing; (iii) the dates on which the samples were tested; and (iv) the dates on which the results of the testing were made known to his Department.
answer_text: On Friday 28 November 2025, the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute reported that Bluetongue virus was detected in two out of three cattle samples collected at routine slaughter as part of DAERA’s Bluetongue surveillance programme.  These had been submitted to the laboratory on 18 November and were part of the large number of samples submitted for surveillance and tested at regular intervals in batches. These samples were collected on 18 November and were tested on 28 November with my Department notified of serology results.  Further testing was completed on Saturday 29 November which included PCR testing and were also positive for the virus.  A 20km temporary control zone was therefore immediately implemented.  Sampling of all susceptible stock, commenced at the farm of origin, on Monday 1 December, with the preliminary results being submitted to my Department on Tuesday 2 December.  Further Positives were reported.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why an area of low risk level of bovine TB was chosen for the bovine TB regionalisation project.
answer_text: The area chosen for the bovine TB regionalisation project was selected based on several key factors, including local disease prevalence; geographical boundaries; patterns of cattle movement (specifically a net outward movement of cattle); and alignment with the corresponding regionalisation area in Donegal within the Republic of Ireland. This coordinated approach is intended to ensure consistency across jurisdictions and support effective implementation.In 2024, the annual bovine TB herd incidence in the selected area was 11.3% (rising to 23.4% in dairy herds), slightly above the overall figure of 10.7% across Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any work being carried out to assist non-land-based farmers that cannot avail of the Farm Sustainability Payment, in particular the mushroom industry.
answer_text: For those farm businesses, including those within the mushroom sector, that cannot avail of FSP there are other schemes within my Department’s Sustainable Agriculture Programme that are relevant. The Horticulture Pilot Scheme, which has recently launched includes a bespoke range of support measures, tailored for the specific needs of this sector. It will provide focused knowledge transfer, innovation, supply chain and capital support to the production horticulture industry through three schemes: Sustainable Sector Growth Groups, Innovation Drivers and Support, and a Growers’ Training and Support Pilot Scheme.The Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme will provide financial support to help primary producers with a Category 1 Farm Business ID, including mushroom growers, to improve business efficiency and environmental performance. It is planned that the scheme will be available from early 2026.Supply Chain schemes are being developed for NI agriculture and horticulture sectors, including the mushroom industry. The aim of these schemes is to encourage and facilitate improved collaboration between producers and growers within the supply chain, encouraging them to innovate, problem-solve and value-add beyond what they can achieve in isolation. In March 2025, I also confirmed that the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme will continue in Northern Ireland until a suitable successor, co-designed with the horticulture sector is in place. Under the scheme, my Department will provide match-funding for recognised NI Producer Organisations undertaking an approved 3-year operational programme, including those operating in the mushroom industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 25596/22-27, to detail any progress made on the Crisis Framework to assess potential impacts, such as weather or market failure, on farm businesses to be developed as part of the draft Sustainable Agriculture Programme.
answer_text: Work on the development of a Crisis Framework as part of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme has commenced and I plan to bring forward plans for this framework in the coming months.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement he has had with the Home Office on the UK Government’s immigration white paper regarding support for Northern Ireland’s specific needs for migrant workers, particularly in the agri-food sector.
answer_text: Since the publication of the Immigration White Paper, I have met with the then Minister for Migration and Citizenship Seema Malhotra MP and exchanged letters with her successor Mike Tapp MP to emphasise the impact of the reforms on NI agri-food. Minister Tapp affirmed that using the domestic workforce to fill vacancies within the sector remains a top priority. He also encouraged DAERA to work closely with Defra and the Labour Market Evidence Group. Many of the UK Government’s Immigration White Paper proposals have now been implemented, including an increase to the skill level of the Skilled Worker Visa, from RQF 3 (A-level equivalent) to RQF level 6 (degree-level). This has resulted in most agri-food occupations, which are classed as medium-skilled, being restricted from accessing the Skilled Worker Visa. Reforms are also being introduced to increase salary thresholds and the English Language requirements for Skilled Worker visa holders. The Migration Advisory Committee were commissioned by the then Home Secretary to undertake a review of the Temporary Shortage List in July 2025. The MAC reported on Stage 1 of the review in October 2025, which confirmed that agri-food occupations are also excluded from accessing the Temporary Shortage List. I strongly oppose the MAC’s and Home Office’s rationale that Food is not crucial to the delivery of critical infrastructure, and I emphasised this to Professor Brian Bell, Chair of the MAC, when I met him in September.In September 2025, I wrote to the Defra Secretary of State Emma Reynolds MP, highlighting concerns about the impact of UK immigration changes on Northern Ireland’s agri-food businesses, which rely heavily on seasonal and skilled labour. I stressed the operational challenges these changes create and called for urgent attention to ensure labour availability for sectors such as fishing and food processing. I also raised these issues when I met the Defra Minister of State Dame Eagle DBE MP, last month.Whilst the UK Immigration White Paper did not directly bring effect to changes to the Seasonal Worker’s Visa, I have continued to communicate the issues being faced by the NI mushroom sector when engaging with UK Government Ministers. A Statement of Changes to the Immigration Rules on 14th October 2025 contained an update to the time requirement between visas for seasonal workers. DAERA officials are liaising with NI mushroom stakeholders, as well as Defra and Home Office officials to clarify the implementation of the rule changes.  I remain committed to raising these issues with the UK Government and the Labour Market Evidence Group to put forward the case for the affected sectors and occupations while working with Defra to explore existing efforts on domestic recruitment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how his Department is communicating with farmers, vets and industry bodies in relation to the detected bluetongue cases and resultant restrictions.
answer_text: The oral statement I made in the Assembly on Monday 01 December 2025, of which you will have received a copy, details the actions my department have taken with respect to responding to this suspect case of Bluetongue.  Furthermore, it provides detail on next steps that are being progressed.Updates to this developing situation, including information on the most recent Temporary Control Zone announced on Saturday 6 December, are available on the DAERA website at: https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland   DAERA social media channels are highlighting key messaging, and this is in addition to direct stakeholder engagement and discussions with wider industry.  Herd and Flock keepers within the first TCZ 20km zone or who have land within the zone have been directly contacted by my Department via text message, email and by post. Officials will now take forward the same steps to contact farmers in the most recent TCZ established near Greyabbey, County Down. My officials have already met with industry stakeholders including direct meetings with the Ulster Farmers Union and NIAPA, with wider stakeholder meetings held on a number of occasions.  In addition, my officials are also in regular discussion with their counterparts both in the Republic of Ireland and across the rest of the United Kingdom.I wish to ensure you that as this situation develops, my department will ensure that regular communication is taken forward at appropriate intervals with all stakeholders and affected farmers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement that has taken place with (i) the Department of Environment, Food and Rural Affairs; (ii) the Scottish and Welsh Governments; and (iii) the Irish Government, in relation to cross-border disease monitoring.
answer_text: My officials are engaged in regular discussions with their counterparts in the Republic of Ireland (ROI) and across the United Kingdom (UK) on a range of animal health and welfare issues, including cross-border disease monitoring, wider disease surveillance, preparedness and response to epizootic disease incursions.I also speak regularly with my Ministerial counterparts in both in the ROI and across the UK.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the strain of bluetongue detected in a farm outside Bangor has been determined as being (i) midge-borne; or (ii) linked to animal movements.
answer_text: Veterinary epidemiological investigations are still ongoing therefore it is still too early to definitively determine the source of disease.  My officials have analysed midge plume data available during the established risk window and recent surveillance by DAERA has identified active midges in the locality.  However, it should be noted that my veterinary officials do not believe this to be imported infection. 		Bluetongue is primarily transmitted through biting midges and are most active from April to November.  The potential for spread depends on climatic conditions and wind patterns with lower temperatures significantly reducing the risk.Updates to this developing situation will be made available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there are plans to introduce whole herd testing on nearby farms following the detection of bluetongue near Bangor.
answer_text: DAERA vets are now carrying out representative sampling and testing on 20 large cattle herds in the 20km Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) to ascertain if there is further infection within the TCZ.Tracings are being conducted of any animals which moved onto or off the affected premises near Bangor, in the risk window from 1 September 2025.  Any holdings identified as receiving animals from the affected farm will be subject to surveillance and testing if required. Further consideration will be given to next steps once the epidemiological picture becomes clearer and we will remain in close contact with affected farmers, and wider industry, on next steps.  Updates to this developing situation will be made available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of premises currently under movement restrictions related to the detection of bluetongue outside Bangor; and (ii) any criteria that will be used to lift these restrictions.
answer_text: There are currently approximately 1040 cattle herds and 730 sheep flocks that are within the Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) and which are subject to the restrictions of the TCZ.  The TCZ declaration which details the movement restrictions on susceptible animals within the TCZ is available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/declaration-temporary-control-zone-tcz This situation is continually monitored and subject to change based on ongoing surveillance and disease investigations. The criteria for easing restrictions are based on several factors including the completion of disease investigations, surveillance results and veterinary advice.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the economic impact on the farming industry following the identification of two cases of bluetongue in a farm near Bangor.
answer_text: At this stage it is too early to assess the economic impact of bluetongue on the farming industry as this will depend on our effectiveness to curtail the disease and the actions of everyone involved.  It will also depend on the level of trade restrictions applied on livestock sold by the industry outside Northern Ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of reported incidents of livestock smuggling from Great Britain, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 the movement of livestock from Great Britain into Northern Ireland, including arrangements for detecting and preventing the illegal entry of livestock, falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any steps he is taking to curtail the spread of the bluetongue virus; and (ii) whether he has considered providing funding support for (a) a vaccine rollout; and (b) increased testing availability.
answer_text: The oral statement I made in the Assembly on Monday 01 December 2025, of which you will have received a copy, details the actions my department have taken with respect to responding to this suspect case of Bluetongue.  Furthermore, it provides detail on next steps that are being progressed.Updates to this developing situation will be made available on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland The primary focus at this stage of the investigation is the implementation of disease control measures to ensure that where possible the further risk of disease spread is mitigated.My Department has no plans at this stage to provide funding support for a vaccine rollout or increased testing availability in Northern Ireland. My officials do, however, remain in close contact with the Agri-food and Biosciences Institute regarding both capacity for, and ongoing results from, samples submitted by the Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department is taking to stop illegal livestock and other meat products entering Northern Ireland, circumventing disease control measures.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 the movement of livestock and meat products into Northern Ireland, including arrangements for detecting and preventing their illegal entry, falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the strain of bluetongue detected in the cattle outside Bangor has been identified.
answer_text: Results from the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) laboratory detected that the strain of bluetongue suspected in the cattle outside Bangor to be bluetongue virus serotype 3 (BTV-3). This has now been confirmed by the Pirbright Institute, the UK National Reference Laboratory.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the percentage of those with a disability, aged between 16 and 64, who are currently employed; and (ii) how this compares to other regions in the UK.
answer_text: The percentage of those with a disability who are employed is sourced from the Labour Force Survey (LFS).Figure 1 below outlines the percentages for each of the regions in 2024 with the percentage of those here with a disability, aged between 16 and 64 who were employed being the lowest at 44.4%. Table 1RegionPercentage of those with a disability who were employedNorth East47.6%North West51.4%Yorkshire & Humberside53.5%East Midlands57.3%West Midlands53.1%Eastern59.1%London57.9%South East61.1%South West60.0%Wales51.6%Scotland51.4%Northern Ireland44.4%

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of assaults reported by Northern Ireland Ambulance Service staff, in each of the last five years; and (ii) his assessment of claims that the number of assaults is under-reported.
answer_text: The number of physical and verbal assaults against Northern Ireland Ambulance Service staff, in each of the last five years is shown in the table below*.Financial YearReported Incidents of Physical AbuseReported Incidents of Verbal AbuseTotal Reported Incidents2020-213652466112021-224412536942022-234172386552023-245052177222024-25408191599*Please note: The information in the table above was requested from and provided by NIAS for the purpose of this response.Survey evidence suggests that healthcare staff tend to underreport the amount of violence and aggression they face when at work. For example, the latest NHS England Staff Survey found that just under 25% of staff did not report the most recent instance of physical violence they were subjected to. This highlights the importance of ensuring that the necessary structures are in place to ensure staff feel supported in the workplace and encourage reporting of any incidents. On 12 December 2023 the Department launched a new Management of Violence and Aggression in the Workplace HSC Framework. This framework outlines the HSC’s commitment, developed in partnership with staff representatives, to ensure the prevention, reduction and management of violence and aggression towards staff in the workplace.  It also ensures that the associated structures, policies and support systems are in place to enable staff to work safely. The Department recognises the challenges healthcare staff face and is committed to working with employers and trade unions to ensure robust protections are in place. Violence and aggression towards staff is never acceptable, and we will continue to take steps to address this issue and ensure staff feel safe, valued and supported.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the gross fixed capital formation, spent in Northern Ireland, as a percentage of GDP, in the last five years; and (ii) how this compares to other regions in the UK.
answer_text: According to NISRA’s Economic Accounts, Gross Fixed Capital Formation (GFCF) as a percentage of GDP increased from 13.3% in 2017 to 15.1% in 2021 in the North. The only other region in Britain for which comparable figures for this time period are available is Scotland where GFCF as a proportion of GDP increased from 17.8%in 2017 to 18.4% in 2021.				Table 1:  Gross Fixed Capital Formation Expenditure as a % of GDP (2017 – 2021) (NISRA National Economic Accounts)			2017	2018	2019	2020	2021	North of Ireland	13.3%	13.7%	14.7%	13.8%	15.1%	Scotland	17.8%	17.9%	18.2%	17.9%	18.4%			The Office for National Statistics (ONS) also produces experimental estimates of GFCF that allow for more detailed comparisons by region and over time. These estimates are currently produced for GFCF in certain types of assets (buildings and structures) meaning they are not comprehensive of all investment flows in the economy. 		On this measure, GFCF (in buildings and structures) as a share of GDP increased from 6.2% in 2018 to 6.3% in 2022 in the North. As per the table below, most regions in Britain saw modest improvements on this measure over the same time period. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any impact the removal of the two-child Universal Credit benefit cap will have in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: At August 2025 there were 17,600 children that were not eligible for the child element of Universal Credit because of the limits imposed by the two-child limit. My officials are undertaking further analytical work to determine the wider impact of this change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to support those with a disability into work.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that everyone in society can access good jobs, no matter what their barriers.  As such, my officials have worked closely with officials from the Department for Communities on the draft Disability and Work Strategy which is currently out for consultation. The strategy includes a wide range of commitments for my department centred around provision of specialist careers advice, and improved access to skills, education and training provision for individuals with disabilities and health conditions.In terms of Higher Education, the Department, through the Disabled Students' Allowance (DSA), provides support to students in higher education with disabilities, assisting them in obtaining the qualifications they need to pursue their chosen career path.  Work has also been progressing on a refreshed approach to Widening Participation with proposals co-designed with stakeholders.  A public consultation is now in the final stages of development.Disability support is also available within our Apprenticeship pathways and the Apprenticeship Inclusion Challenge Fund specifically promotes uptake from underrepresented groups, with two projects focusing on promoting apprenticeships for disabled people.I have also outlined my proposals for a programme of reforms for school leavers with Special Educational Needs. Subject to appropriate investment, the changes will enhance the pathways and supports for school leavers who want to access my Departments education, skills and training provision.  These proposals have the potential to make a significant difference to school leavers with Special Educational Needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the joint letter to the Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State at the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, from vets and representatives from agriculture and the wider animal health and welfare industry in Northern Ireland in their to call for clarity from the UK Government; and (ii) to detail any further steps he is taking to ensure that clarity is given.
answer_text: In line with the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 the supply of veterinary medicinal products into Northern Ireland falls under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs. The Veterinary Medicines Directorate (an Executive Agency of the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs [Defra]) is the Competent Authority for veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.   	I remain committed to ensuring the continued supply of veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland, safeguarding animal health and welfare, while supporting vets, farmers, and the agri-food industry.I welcome the UK Government’s Protecting Animal Health policy (June 2025), and its two new support schemes designed to address supply challenges, including unforeseen issues.I sit on the UK Government’s Veterinary Medicines Working Group, co-chaired by Baroness Hayman of Ullock, Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Defra with responsibility for veterinary medicines. The group includes veterinary experts, industry representatives, and political stakeholders. This forum enables me to advise on measures to prevent disruption to supply and to advocate for clear, comprehensive and timely communications to ensure that vets, farmers, and the agri-food industry are fully informed about the two schemes now and beyond their implementation on 1 January 2026.In the months ahead, I will continue working with the UK Government and the Veterinary Medicines Working Group to ensure that clarity is provided on these schemes and that they deliver their intended outcomes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the amount the Chancellor of the Exchequer’s Autumn Budget 2025 announcement falls short of what is needed to support the delivery of frontline services.
answer_text: Despite the Chancellor’s Autumn Budget resulting in increased taxes, by amounts rising to £26 billion in 2029-30, the Executive only received an additional £370 million from this announcement.Considering that this funding covers a five-year period, what initially appears to be a substantial allocation is, in reality, much less impactful. With only £18.8 million provided in the current financial year. This needs to be set against the considerable pressures on our public services with the Departments of Health and Education forecasting an overspend of some £387 million this year, despite those departments together accounting for some 70% of the total departmental Resource DEL.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in light of the Chancellor of the Exchequer's Autumn Budget 2025 announcement, and his expressed view that it falls far short of what is needed to support the delivery of frontline services, to detail any savings he has identified that can be made to ensure that frontline services will be prioritised.
answer_text: Each Minister and their Permanent Secretary are responsible for the management of their Departmental budget and should be always seeking the efficient use of public funds in the delivery of public services. These savings can in turn be used in the delivery of frontline public services. As part of the Budget Sustainability work commissioned by my department, departments are currently developing longer term financial plans and have been asked to identify efficiencies as part of that process.  Departments will be able to use that information to help inform their delivery of frontline services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of (a) universities; and (b) colleges, that Queen’s University Belfast has partnership agreements with; and (ii) in what countries.
answer_text: Queen’s University Belfast has educational partnership agreements with 120 universities and 19 colleges. Theses institutions are based in the following countries:Australia        BrazilCanadaChileChinaCzech RepublicFranceGermanyIndiaIndonesiaJordanKazakhstanMalaysiaMexicoNigeriaPakistanSerbiaSingaporeSouth AfricaThailandUkraineUnited KingdomUSAVietnam

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the reasons why antenatal services have been suspended in Portaferry.
answer_text: Community Midwifery services like other services routinely check their service demands to ensure efficient use of resources. Recently this highlighted that on average only two patients per week were attending the Portaferry Clinic to meet the Midwife who was travelling there to deliver the Midwifery led clinic. The clinic had originally been weekly but moved to fortnightly on 2 September 2025 owing to the low volume of women needing to attend.It was considered that subject to targeted engagement there was an opportunity to streamline and re-provide this clinic in an alternative location.  Those women attending agreed to the suggested approach to attend Ards Maternity Hub.In addition, the team carried out early engagement with the local GP Practice Manager who was also in agreement. It is not a permanent change to service; rather is specific to the low level of demand currently being experienced.This remains a flexible arrangement with the Portaferry GP Surgery and the Trust may consider reinstating the Antenatal Clinic there, if the demand for this service increases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the publication of a Gambling Code of Practice.
answer_text: I am currently considering proposals for a final Gambling Code of Practice following detailed consideration of responses to the  consultation. It remains my intention to publish Northern Ireland’s first Gambling Code of Practice as soon as practicably possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether consideration will be given to reintroducing a GP presence to Portavogie Health Clinic.
answer_text: General Medical Services to the patient population in Portavogie is served by three well established GP Practices (Loughview Medical Centre, Kircubbin, Ballywalter Surgery, Ballywalter, and Portaferry Surgery, Portaferry) who provide comprehensive and reliable Primary Care coverage to the local population.  This arrangement ensures access to GP led services, supports continuity of care and ensures patients can avail of General Medical Services.  Previously there was a GP presence provided in Portavogie Health Clinic as a Branch Surgery arrangement from both Loughview Medical Centre and Portaferry Surgery.  This ceased in 2016 due to sustainability issues.     At this time, there are no plans to introduce a GP presence to Portavogie Health Clinic.  Decisions regarding General Medical Services provision must be informed by population need, sustainability considerations, General Practice workforce and alignment to Primary Care policy. The current provision ensures General Medical Services to the patient population in the local area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether antenatal services will be re-established in Portaferry at the earliest opportunity.
answer_text: The South Eastern Trust has relocated antenatal care for women in the Portaferry area to the Ards Maternity Hub at Ards Hospital.  The Portaferry clinic had a very low attendance rate, with an average of just 2 women attending each week. Women from North Down & Ards already attend the Ards Hub for their initial booking appointments. Many expectant mothers from the Peninsula have asked to continue their care in the Ards Hub, as they can choose appointment times that fit around work and childcare.The Ards Maternity Hub provides access to additional services, including a Breastfeeding Support Group, Antenatal Classes and Postnatal Clinics, all of which are available to women from the Peninsula.This remains a flexible arrangement with the Portaferry GP Surgery and the Trust may consider reinstating the Antenatal Clinic there, if the demand for it increases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the circumstances when the PSNI use ink and paper to take fingerprints, instead of electronic scanning.
answer_text: The taking of fingerprints is an operational matter for the PSNI who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. You may therefore wish to raise this matter directly with the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the length of time the Hightown Incinerator planning application has been under consideration by her Department.
answer_text: A previous decision on the application for the Hightown Incinerator (planning reference: T/2014/0114/F), was quashed by the court in May 2023 and the application was remitted back to DfI for determination. Since then, my Department has been processing the application, including undertaking necessary statutory consultation with interested bodies and public authorities and considering further and updated environmental information. Once all statutory processes are satisfactorily completed, a recommendation will be brought forward by my officials.A number of comments and issues were raised within the most recent statutory consultee responses and it is understood that further information is currently being prepared by the applicant to address any outstanding issues. Once this information is received, further re-consultation and re-advertisement will be undertaken as part of the statutory process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of waste that has been deposited as a result of fly-tipping, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council.
answer_text: I recognise the significant negative impact that fly-tipping can have on the natural environment.  		Fly-tipping incidents occurring on public land are primarily managed by District Councils. The NIEA continues to work in close partnership with local councils to address fly tipping through a working protocol.		Under the protocol the NIEA leads on incidents of fly-tipped waste exceeding 20m³ or when it includes materials such as hazardous waste that Councils cannot process. 		Both the NIEA and District Councils have access to various provisions under the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, enabling them to undertake enforcement action where appropriate.		From the data held by NIEA, Table 1 below, the amount of waste that has been deposited as a result of fly-tipping, in each of the last five years, broken down by local council. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 27957/22-27, whether any further assessment has been made of (i) the Welsh Government’s use of recycled nappies in road resurfacing schemes; and (ii) the potential for this in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Officials have received the results of the Welsh trial and have contacted their counterparts about its outcome.  An assessment as to its feasibility on our road network is currently ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to the Hightown Incinerator, to detail (i) the statutory consultee responses outstanding, including which consultee or consultees; (ii) the date of the last correspondence sent by her Department to the consultee or consultees; and (iii) the date of the last correspondence received by her Department from the consultee or consultees.
answer_text: An Addendum to the Environmental Statement was received from the Applicant on 16 April 2025 and further consultation with relevant bodies was carried out on 08 May 2025. Consultation responses have been received from DfI Rivers and Roads; Public Health Agency, HSE NI, NIEA, HED, NI Water, Antrim and Newtownabbey Council Environmental Health Department and Chief Executive of Antrim and Newtownabbey Borough Council. There are currently no outstanding consultation responses.However, a number of comments and issues were raised within the most recent responses received and it is understood that further information is currently being prepared by the applicant to address any outstanding issues. Once this information is received, further re-consultation and re-advertisement will be undertaken as part of the statutory process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of (i) the findings of the Buglife November 2025 survey Bugs Matter, which notes a 56 per cent five-year decline in flying insects in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the impact of measures introduced to address this decline to date.
answer_text: (i) Since the Bugs Matter survey started in 2021 it has recorded an average 8% annual decrease in insect ‘splats’ on Northern Ireland licence plates. A 56% five-year decline in Northern Ireland was comparable to Wales (55%) but less steep than in England (60%) and Scotland (59%). Encouragingly, the rate of decline slowed between 2023 and 2024. The report suggests it may even flatten or reverse in 2025. 	While these declines are concerning, it should be noted that this citizen science survey explores insect activity along a portion of road networks. Results may not be representative of how species populations are faring across the wider landscape. Insect populations and activity fluctuate from year to year in response to pressures such as weather conditions and climate change, available food resources, use of pesticides, predation and disease.		(ii)The Department supports various strands of work to address insect decline, including the Northern Ireland Priority Species List Review, protected sites and conservation management, environmental measures through the Environmental Farming Scheme and the forthcoming Farming With Nature scheme, grant aid, the Northern Ireland Species Recovery Partnership, and the UK Pollinator Monitoring Scheme.  The successful All-Ireland Pollinator Plan will be expanded for the period 2026-2030, with funding through the Shared Island Initiative and contributions from DAERA and ROI counterparts.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail who is responsible for the maintenance of (i) the sports hall floor; and (ii) the boiler, at St Columba’s College, Portaferry.
answer_text: The sports centre at St Columba’s College, Portaferry, is owned by the school trustees and is part of the St Columba’s College estate. Ards and North Down Borough Council manages the facility outside of school hours, for use by the local community and sports clubs. A management agreement between the trustees and the Council sets out the arrangements for the dual use of the facility, including arrangements for maintenance.	Discussions are ongoing between key stakeholders in relation to the prioritisation of works required to the floor of the sports centre.		Given competing priorities in the delivery of inescapable maintenance works across the schools’ estate, agreement has been reached that the Council will replace the boiler in the sports centre, with costs apportioned between the school and the Council in line with the management agreement. It is anticipated that the new boiler will be installed early in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 19072/22-27, to detail any update on the preparation and publication of Northern Ireland specific guidelines for those undertaking licensed archaeological excavations to implement selection, retention and dispersal.
answer_text: Guidelines for the implementation in Northern Ireland of this UK industry-wide process are in draft. In January 2026 this draft will be circulated to the cross-sectoral Archaeological Archives Working Group (convened by Historic Environment Division under Archaeology 2030), for comment and input, with publication then anticipated by early autumn 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps being taken to repair the boiler at St Columba's College, which also serves Portaferry Sports Centre, which has been broken since September 2025.
answer_text: Given competing maintenance pressures across the schools’ estate, agreement has been reached that Ards and North Down Borough Council will replace the boiler serving the sports centre at St Columba’s College, Portaferry. It is anticipated that the new boiler will be installed early in 2026. In line with the management agreement between the school trustees and the Council, the costs for the boiler replacement will be apportioned between the school and the Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps being taken to repair the floor of the sports hall at St Columba’s College, Portaferry, which has been out of action for three and half years.
answer_text: The sports centre at St Columba’s College, Portaferry, is owned by the school trustees and is part of the St Columba’s College estate. Ards and North Down Borough Council manages the facility outside of school hours, for use by the local community and sports clubs. The sports centre is used by the school and the Council for a number of activities and events. 	Discussions are ongoing between key stakeholders, including the Council and the Education Authority, in relation to the prioritisation of works required to the floor of the sports centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the rationale for the provision of Hardship Fund payments to local councils.
answer_text: My Department’s letter to Council Chief Executives dated 28 March 2023 advised that £4m of hardship funding would be available in 2022/23 to allow councils to provide financial support in 2023/24 to the most vulnerable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any discussions she has had with the Chief Constable, regarding measures to be taken to address the increasing numbers of PSNI retirements, through ill health.
answer_text: Responsibility for PSNI retirements on grounds of ill-health rests with the Northern Ireland Policing Board in its capacity as the Police Pension Authority.  In line with my commitment to maintaining the operational independence of the Chief Constable and respecting the statutory role of the Policing Board, it would be inappropriate for me to comment on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any further Hardship Fund payments will be made to local councils during this financial year.
answer_text: My Department’s letter to Council Chief Executives dated 28 March 2023 advised that £4m of hardship funding would be available in 2022/23 to allow councils to provide financial support in 2023/24 to the most vulnerable.  Chief Executives were also advised that the funding was non-recurrent; it would not be available in future years; and should be considered additional to any hardship support funding already offered by the Council. There will therefore be no Hardship Fund payments made to councils during this financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of why PSNI retirements, through ill health, have increased by 700 per cent, in the last six years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when updated guidance will be issued, following the Supreme Court Ruling in the Supreme Court judgement decision, in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant).

answer_text: In response to part (i), the first row in Table 1 details legal aid expenditure in each financial year since April 2020 for cases where the Primary Matter is recorded as Immigration and Asylum Chamber of the First Tier Tribunal.  Similarly, for part (ii), the second row in Table 1 provides the equivalent legal aid expenditure where the Primary Matter is recorded as Immigration and Asylum Chamber of the Upper Tribunal.  Table 1: Legal aid authorised expenditure for representation before the Immigration and Asylum Chamber Tribunal (First Tier and Upper), 2020/21 to 2024/25.2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25Immigration and Asylum Chamber of the First Tier Tribunal£119,860£160,098£151,055£212,399£214,506Immigration and Asylum Chamber of the Upper Tribunal£15,699£18,143£13,190£6,670£34,974Source: Legal Services Agency (Official Statistics)Notes:Authorised expenditure figures set out in Table 1 incorporate a mix of legal fees along with disbursement remuneration that includes interpreter costs as well as expert witness payments e.g. Asylum and Immigration expert, GP Report.  Where appropriate, VAT is also included.As a legal aid case can span multiple years, expenditure authorised in one financial year will not necessarily reflect legal aid cases granted, or work undertaken, in that same year.  Annual expenditure figures for cases at the Upper Tribunal should be considered independent from those relating to the First Tier Tribunal. This is because the financial year in which payment was made in relation to an Upper Tribunal case may differ from the year in which payment was made in respect of the original application to the First Tier Tribunal. Consequently, these two sets of figures should not be directly compared to avoid drawing unwarranted conclusions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department’s next steps, following the Supreme Court judgement decision, In the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant).
answer_text: The Supreme Court’s decision in the matter of JR87 has significant implications for our schools. I will consider the judgment in full before determining any essential remedies that both respect the law and safeguard the role of religion in education.  The judgment focuses on both religious education taught as part of the school curriculum and collective worship held in schools, mainly through assemblies and services. The Court held that the arrangements for religious education and collective worship in this case breached European Convention rights. It further determined that the core syllabus for Religious Education does not provide teaching in an objective, critical, and pluralist manner and that collective worship is similarly not conveyed in such a way.However, it is vital to underline that the Court did not strike down the existing legislation.  Both Religious Education and collective worship can continue in schools.  Indeed, there is a legal obligation that they must continue. Article 21(2) Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 makes this clear: controlled schools must provide undenominational Religious Education based on the Holy Scriptures and collective worship that is not distinctive of any denomination. These statutory duties remain unchanged.The case is not about whether Christianity should be the main or primary faith that pupils learn about in schools in Northern Ireland.  Historically and today, Christianity is the main religion in Northern Ireland. Furthermore, the Court has explained that “it is within the Department’s margin of appreciation in planning and setting the curriculum for the greater part of religious education to focus on knowledge of Christianity”.It is clear that this is a complex judgment that cannot be ignored, but it is important to fully understand the issues before acting.  I have requested further legal advice to understand the full implications of the judgment. I have written to schools on this matter and advised that guidance will be provided in coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of what the Supreme Court judgement decision, in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant), will mean for those schools with a Christian ethos.
answer_text: The Supreme Court’s decision in the matter of JR87 has significant implications for our schools. I will consider the judgment in full before determining any essential remedies that both respect the law and safeguard the role of religion in education.  The judgment focuses on both religious education taught as part of the school curriculum and collective worship held in schools, mainly through assemblies and services. The Court held that the arrangements for religious education and collective worship in this case breached European Convention rights. It further determined that the core syllabus for Religious Education does not provide teaching in an objective, critical, and pluralist manner and that collective worship is similarly not conveyed in such a way.However, it is vital to underline that the Court did not strike down the existing legislation.  Both Religious Education and collective worship can continue in schools.  Indeed, there is a legal obligation that they must continue. Article 21(2) Education and Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986 makes this clear: controlled schools must provide undenominational Religious Education based on the Holy Scriptures and collective worship that is not distinctive of any denomination. These statutory duties remain unchanged.The case is not about whether Christianity should be the main or primary faith that pupils learn about in schools in Northern Ireland.  Historically and today, Christianity is the main religion in Northern Ireland. Furthermore, the Court has explained that “it is within the Department’s margin of appreciation in planning and setting the curriculum for the greater part of religious education to focus on knowledge of Christianity”.It is clear that this is a complex judgment that cannot be ignored, but it is important to fully understand the issues before acting.  I have requested further legal advice to understand the full implications of the judgment. I have written to schools on this matter and advised that guidance will be provided in coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any additional measures taken to control sheep scab, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Sheep scab is a notifiable disease in NI. The Sheep Scab Order (NI) 1970 places a legal obligation on any person who has reason to believe that a sheep in their possession or care has sheep scab to notify their local Divisional Veterinary Office (DVO) as soon as possible. This legislation gives my Department the necessary powers to restrict any potentially infectious flocks and to enforce any treatment required.DAERA continues to use its social media channels to raise awareness around sheep scab, and the DAERA website provides detailed information on the disease and key advice to flock keepers to prevent disease spread.  DAERA staff continue to report cases of sheep scab encountered as part of their official duties and those staff involved in market and abattoir inspections remain vigilant for the signs of the disease. Earlier this year I asked officials to explore support to industry through a communications drive/awareness campaign and knowledge transfer programmes to support control and eradication.In November, CAFRE, in conjunction with Sheep Industry stakeholders hosted a series of sheep scab awareness events.  The events titled “Sheep Scab: Breaking the Cycle” focused on raising the awareness of sheep scab symptoms and treatments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether a wildlife intervention will form part of the cross-border regional cooperation programme on tackling bovine TB, as announced by the Irish Government.
answer_text: I was pleased to recently announce, alongside Martin Heydon TD, Minister of Agriculture, Food and the Marine in the Republic of Ireland, an innovative, cross-border regionalisation pilot project which will aim to assist both jurisdictions in tackling the growing impact of bTB. While engagement is continuing with the TB Partnership Steering Group and colleagues in the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine (DAFM) on the specific wildlife, cattle and people measures to be included in the pilot, it is envisaged that a test, vaccinate or remove approach to wildlife intervention will be included.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how the €6.4m investment for a cross-border pilot regional cooperation programme on tackling bovine TB, as announced by the Irish Government, will be spent in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The €6.4 million in Shared Island funding will enable the development and delivery of a regionalised pilot project in partnership with the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine (DAFM) in the Republic of Ireland. 		The money is anticipated to be spent on a range of proposals within the pilot, including wildlife intervention implementation and preparatory work; people measures including on farm biosecurity advice, and additional cattle measures.  	Further engagement with DAFM, the TB Partnership Steering Group and farmers in the proposed area is ongoing.  Final decisions on the proposals for inclusion in the pilot are anticipated to be made early in the New Year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the issue of bins being left on public footways after their collection date has been factored into his Department’s proposals on Rethinking Our Resources.
answer_text: The Department’s proposals on Rethinking Our Resources focus on waste and recycling reforms for NI, aiming to assist in meeting legislative targets for recycling, landfill and reduction in greenhouse gas emissions.The consultation did not seek views on the specific issue of bins being left on public footways after collection.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any communication his Department has had with local councils around the need for enforcement powers to address the problem with bins being left on public footways after their collection date.
answer_text: My Department received correspondence from the Chief Executive of Ards and North Down Borough Council in June 2024 requesting that powers be introduced to deal with the problem with bins being left on public footways after collection. My Department’s response was that an amendment to primary legislation would require evidence and support from across the district councils that such powers are necessary and all other potential options have been given due consideration. There has been no further correspondence received from any of the local councils regarding this matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 35666/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has carried out any assessment of the need for enforcement powers to address the problem of bins being left on public footways after their collection date.
answer_text: The relevant legislation is The Waste and Contaminated Land Order 1997 (Articles 20 and 21), which contains no provisions as to when bins should be placed or removed.To amend this primary legislation, my officials would require evidence that this is a significant concern for a significant number of councils.  In the absence of such evidence, my officials have not carried out any such assessment.
