question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of funding provided by his Department for the establishment and running of Shared Environmental Services, in each year since 2015.
answer_text: Shared Environmental Services (SES) was established in 2015 as part of the Review of Public Administration and is a joint initiative by NI’s 11 councils to provide environmental expertise for planning applications, particularly for habitat regulation assessments.  This is a role that was previously held by the Department of the Environment.  DAERA has not provided funding for (i) the establishment or (ii) the operation of Shared Environmental Services since 2015.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether any funding has been provided by his Department for the (i) establishment; and (ii) operation, of Shared Environmental Services, since 2015.
answer_text: My Department has not provided funding for (i) the establishment or (ii) the operation of Shared Environmental Services since 2015.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions he has had with his counterparts in (a) England; (b) Scotland; (c) Wales; and (d) the Irish Republic, with regard to the threat to the bee population from the tropilaelaps mite and how to protect colonies in Northern Ireland, since taking office in February 2024.
answer_text: My officials maintain regular communication with their Great Britain and DAFM counterparts in respect of Tropilaelaps mite, ensuring the timely exchange of information on surveillance, monitoring, preparedness and best approaches.DAFM officials input to DAERA’s Contingency Plan for Exotic Pests and Diseases of Honeybees for Northern Ireland. The contingency plan is currently under review, and a joint exercise by DAERA, DAFM and AFBI was held in February 2024 to test arrangements for a simulated finding of Tropilaelaps mite in Northern Ireland.Whilst I have not had specific discussions specifically relating to Tropilaelaps mite with my counterparts in Great Britain or the Irish Republic North South Ministerial Council Agricultural Sector meetings do provide a platform for DAERA and Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine (DAFM) to discuss threats to animal and plant health, including pests and disease threats to bees.The British-Irish Council’s Ministerial Environment meetings also offer opportunities to consider threats from invasive species relating to bees such as the Asian Hornet.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of funding her Department has provided for the establishment and running of Shared Environmental Services, since 2015.
answer_text: My Department has not provided any funding for the establishment and running costs of the Shared Environmental Services (SES). My department does avail of SES services and pays for these as and when required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the benefits of early diagnosis of dementia, to outline any steps he is taking to address waiting lists for dementia diagnosis across Northern Ireland.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of trained and dedicated bee inspectors within his Department; and (ii) any plans to recruit bee inspectors.
answer_text: My Department does not employ dedicated bee inspectors, however, it recruits Agriculture Inspectors and provides training in bee inspections to meet its legislative obligations. At present there are three inspectors who perform bee inspections as part of their overall duties. Recruitment competitions are underway to increase the overall inspector capacity to ensure that the Department’s legislative obligations continue to be met, including its response to confirmed bee disease outbreaks. The Department continues to monitor changes to risk profiles, including from bee diseases and undertakes inspections accordingly. Contingencies are in place to deal with significant outbreaks, including the temporary use of staff from across the Department and from other organisations if required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the importance of having an adequate number of trained and dedicated bee inspectors within his Department to identify and contain outbreaks of diseases affecting bee colonies.
answer_text: I fully recognise the important role of honeybees in horticulture and agriculture and whilst DAERA does not recruit dedicated bee inspectors, appropriately trained Agriculture Inspectors perform bee inspections as part of their overall inspection duties to meet legislative obligations.My officials are continuing to deliver legislative requirements regarding bee inspections, and support apiary owners and associations in promoting bee health through published guidance and engagement at events.DAERA supports local beekeepers through  scientific and laboratory testing provided by Agri-food & Biosciences Institute(AFBI).I would encourage beekeepers to remain vigilant and regularly monitor their apiaries to ensure disease levels within Northern Ireland remain at a low level.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the management of the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute farm at Hillsborough.
answer_text: Management of the farm at AFBI Hillsborough is an operational matter for the AFBI management team, with appropriate oversight by the AFBI Board.The farm comprises a dairy herd and followers, a beef herd, sheep flock and pig herd with poultry being on site as required.  This platform supports the delivery of a research programme on sustainable livestock and grassland sciences.AFBI has confirmed that the farm is inspected and certified under the Red Tractor and Northern Ireland Food Chain Certification “NIFCC” quality assurance standards, ensuring compliance with industry requirements for dairy, beef, sheep, and pig production.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps being taken to tackle homelessness during the winter period.
answer_text: A cross departmental Homelessness Winter Preparedness forum has been established to explore and coordinate actions and responses.Alongside this, the Housing Executive will continue to implement the Severe Weather Emergency Protocol (SWEP).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department plan to take to clear gullies, to mitigate against localised flooding.
answer_text: My Department places high importance on the need to adequately maintain the road network through day-to-day essential maintenance activities such as gully emptying, to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. However, allocations of funding to maintain our roads have been less than required for many years and as a result, my Department has only been able to deliver a limited service across a range of functions including gully emptying.My Department’s current Limited-Service Policy aims to inspect and clean, where necessary, all gullies once annually.Further ad-hoc cleaning may also be undertaken to address any specific localised issues as required, within available budgets, and in advance of and in response to severe weather events. However, in recognition of the importance of this area of work, I have allocated further funding to deliver additional works in the form of cleaning and maintenance of drainage lines and manholes.My Department is the Lead Government Department for the strategic coordination of the emergency response to storm impacts on roads as well as for significant flooding. As part of this role, we have worked closely with partner organisations to develop a multi-agency Severe Weather Framework, which outlines preplanned arrangements for a coordinated multi agency response to severe weather warnings, including flooding. This work is embedded within the North’s wider Resilience Strategy 2025-2030. The strategy is delivered through a structured governance model, including Emergency Preparedness Groups (EPGs) across the region, supported by the local resilience team and overseen by the Civil Contingencies Group (NI). These arrangements ensure that emergency planning and response are coordinated, robust and continuously improved. Moreover, my Department has well established protocols in place for the response to severe weather, including flooding. These emergency plans and processes are continually evaluated and developed throughout the year. The unpredictable nature of weather events means heavy rainfall can occur at any time and in preparation the Department operates an on-call Duty and Flooding Officer Rota system 24 hours a day, 365 days a year. At times, my Department’s response may involve working alongside our multi-agency partners, with both internal and external staff being deployed as necessary. Often, my staff have to deal with multi-weather events which may involve wind, rain and melting snow, resulting in fallen trees and flooding issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail a timeline for the development of a new rural policy.
answer_text: My Department is developing its new rural policy using a co-design process involving a wide range of stakeholders. The three Rural Policy Co-design Working Groups established to assist with the policy development work submitted their final reports to the Department in September 2025.  My officials have now considered these reports and are working to produce a cohesive set of outline policy proposals.  Work on developing the new rural policy will continue over the coming months and it is my intention that public consultation on draft policy proposals will commence early next year. During the policy development phase, the Department will continue to engage with other NI Government Departments, SOLACE, Equality Promotion Groups and a broad range of rural stakeholders.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: My Department places high importance on the need to adequately maintain the road network through day-to-day essential maintenance activities such as gully emptying, to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. However, allocations of funding to maintain our roads have been less than required for many years and as a result, my Department has only been able to deliver a limited service across a range of functions including gully emptying.My Department’s current Limited-Service Policy aims to inspect and clean, where necessary, all gullies once annually.Further ad-hoc cleaning may also be undertaken to address any specific localised issues as required, within available budgets, and in advance of and in response to severe weather events. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the delivery of her Department’s 10x Economy An Economic Vision.
answer_text: Since my predecessor, Conor Murphy, announced a new Economic Vision for the North in February 2024, my Department’s delivery focus has centred around the four key priorities of: Good Jobs, Productivity, Regional Balance, and Decarbonisation.Significant progress has been made against these priorities during the mandate so far. For example, we have:taken significant strides in the development of an Employment Rights Bill to majorly update our employment law framework and raise working standards;set out a clear roadmap to expand university provision in Ulster University’s Magee campus, honouring a long-standing commitment;established Local Economic Partnerships in all Local Government Districts to help put local economic decision-making in the hands of local people;put in place Sectoral Action Plans to stimulate some of our highest-growth and highest-productivity potential sectors; andput in place a new Skills Action Plan and Skills Fund to better the skills needs of our economy. At the outset of this year my Department published a year one progress report on the delivery of my Economic Vision: Delivering the Economic Vision - Year one progress report | Department for the Economy. Further information on my Department’s priorities this financial year is available in our published 2025-26 Business Plan: DfE Business Plan 2025-26 | Department for the Economy. My Department will publish further updates on delivery as the mandate progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the Police Ombudsman’s report on an investigation of a serial child sex abuser, whether she has plans to properly resource the PSNI, including its Cybercrime Centre.
answer_text: I want to acknowledge the serious findings of the Police Ombudsman’s investigation into the PSNI’s handling of the offending activities of Alexander McCartney. His actions were abhorrent, and my thoughts and deepest sympathies remain with the victims and their families, who have endured unimaginable trauma.Recognising the critical importance of investing in our police workforce, the Executive agreed in June that my Department would have first call on up to £7 million funding in future monitoring rounds this year for PSNI recruitment. This commitment has been reaffirmed at subsequent Executive meetings.Alongside this, the Finance Minster’s recommendation that the remainder of the Recovery Plan is fully funded in the pending three-year Executive Budget, should ensure that the PSNI gets the additional officer and staff numbers it needs in full and on time.	Rebuilding police capacity is not optional – it is essential.  And so too is sustained investment in the PSNI, so they have the people, skills and tools required to keep people - especially our children - safe. To do so, the Department of Justice must be properly resourced, given that almost 65% of all funding to my Department goes to the PSNI, with only 35% available to fund all other justice services.In terms of the Police Ombudsman report in question, it is important to highlight a number of details that have often been missing from the public debate to date. Firstly, the Ombudsman found that reduced capacity in the PSNI’s Child Internet Protection Team in 2016 was due to absences and other factors; this was not a simple issue of wider police headcount.Secondly, the report found that the PSNI’s Cybercrime Centre used a prioritisation process in which terrorism and serious crime offences received priority and this impacted on the examination of devices submitted by the Child Internet Protection Team. It is not for my Department to make judgements on the operational prioritisation of resources within the PSNI; however, I believe this conclusion is pertinent as it illustrates, again, that the challenges in this case extended far beyond overall police numbers.The allocation of resources within the PSNI is, ultimately, an operational matter for the Chief Constable, for which he is accountable to the Policing Board

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 12560/22-27,  for an update on the provision of accommodation and facilities in prisons, to address the needs of neurodivergent prisoners.
answer_text: Inclusive design principles play an important role in the design of accommodation provision across the NIPS estate for all people, regardless of their age, gender, mobility, ethnicity or circumstances.  A range of design factors, including layout, fixtures and fittings, materials including sound reduction, contrast, lighting, simple wayfinding and quieter spaces shape the provision of accommodation to meet the needs of a diverse prison population. Responsibility for health and social care of prisoners in Northern Ireland sits with the Department of Health who have commissioned South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust (the Trust) to provide primary, mental health and addictions services within prisons. Many people arrive in prison with complex needs.  Support is available for people who have additional needs, including those who have been confirmed by the Trust as having a neurodivergent condition.  NIPS staff are provided with a range of training interventions to equip them to do their job.  This includes training in neurodiverse conditions and mental health.  In addition, the Trust support NIPS staff by providing advice on how best to communicate with individuals who have specific needs. Together with environmental changes, access to garden spaces and therapeutic activity at each prison site, Prisoner Safety and Support Teams provide support to meet the needs of neurodivergent prisoners.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of vehicles (i) his Department; and (ii) its arm's-length bodies, own or lease which are (a) diesel; (b) petrol; (c) hybrid; (d) hydrogen; and (e) electric.
answer_text: The number of diesel, petrol, hybrid, hydrogen and electric vehicles in my department and its arm’s-length body are:Type NumberDiesel9Petrol0Hybrid1Hydrogen0Electric0In addition, the Department owns two off road electric vehicles for use on the Stormont Estate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the (i) Queens Hall; and (ii) Newtownards Library, capital projects, following the consideration of the business case submitted by Libraries NI.
answer_text: An updated business case is under development and my officials continue to engage with both Libraries NI and Ards and North Down Borough Council to address several complex issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 26718/22-27, whether her proposed review will consider (i) remuneration; (ii) warm meals; and (iii) other steps, to support those undertaking jury service.
answer_text: Whilst juror expenses is one of the issues identified for inclusion within my Department’s planned policy review of jury service, there are no plans to consider remuneration.The decision whether or not to supply a warm meal to jurors is an operational matter managed by the Northern Ireland Courts & Tribunals Service, and not for the planned policy review.My planned review will consider a range of issues relating to jury service, including the jury-related recommendations made by Sir John Gillen in his report into the law and procedures in serious sexual offences in Northern Ireland, along with some recommendations made by Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland on the need for jury directions in relation to particular types of offences.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 11478/22-27, for an update on the Executive Disability Strategy.
answer_text: The draft Executive Disability Strategy is now out for public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on progress made on the implementation of recommendations made in the Northern Ireland Law Commission's 2010 report on Land Law.
answer_text: Initial work was commenced in relation to land law reform by the Department some years ago following the publication of the Law Commission report. Following discussions both inside the Department and with the Office of Legislative Counsel it was concluded that implementation of the detailed and complex recommendations of the Law Commission’s report would require a fully funded project team involving both subject-matter and legislative process experts. At that time, as now, that expertise and resource was not available in the Department.While there are aspects of the report that may be desirable in terms of legislative reform, further consideration of the Law Commission recommendations will be dependent on future resource and competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 21910/22-27, (i) whether her officials have commenced a feasibility study on the Northern Ireland Law Commission; and (ii) to detail the timescale for completion.
answer_text: I can confirm that my officials have commenced a feasibility study which considers options to re-establish the Northern Ireland Law Commission on a fully operational basis. This involves looking at different structures, funding arrangements, and lessons learned from other jurisdictions.As indicated in my response to AQW 10331/22-27, I expect this work to be completed before the end of the current mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 9522/22-27, whether he plans to consult on the mandatory microchipping of cats.
answer_text: As set out in my Animal Welfare Pathway 2025-27, I have committed to gathering an evidence base about mandatory microchipping of cats and it is my intention to consult on the matter early in 2026.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps she is taking to ensure that planning fees are adequate to pay for the processing of planning applications.
answer_text: The financial sustainability of the planning system remains a key priority for my Department and is embedded as a key theme of the planning improvement programme. My officials continue to work with local government to scope and review the financial resilience of the planning system with the aim of identifying opportunities to increase levels of income for statutory and non-statutory functions and services.  In October 2025, and a public consultation was launched to seek views on introducing charges for planning services that are not currently subject to fees, such as Non-Material Changes and the Discharge of Conditions. The consultation will also seek views on broader planning fee structures, exploring potential new fee categories, adjustments to existing charges, and the overall appropriateness of the planning fees charged in the current system.  My Department has also introduced three inflationary uplifts to planning fees, with a 12.3% increase in 2023, 4% increase in 2024 and 2.1% increase in 2025 with plans to introduce a further uplift of the planning fees in April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment carried out on the cost of reinstating automatic passporting of health assistance for those in receipt of Universal Credit.
answer_text: The underlying threshold eligibility criteria for the Help with Health Costs have not changed, rather, some individuals who may have previously accessed support through the Low-Income Scheme will now be automatically passported. As a result, it is not possible to accurately estimate the number of individuals who will benefit from the reinstatement of automatic passporting, or to provide a reliable assessment of the overall impact on cost or service delivery. However, there is no evidence to suggest that reinstating the scheme will create capacity pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether there will be a support payment alongside compensation for turkey farmers affected by bird flu.
answer_text: Compensation is payable for birds culled at the Department’s direction for disease control purposes.  There are no plans for the introduction of additional financial support.		While I acknowledge the impact of an incursion of highly pathogenic avian influenza on farmers, I have to act within the current legal and budgetary framework. I am therefore grateful to industry for the ongoing efforts to protect flocks from avian influenza. 	I would reiterate the need for all bird keepers to practice the highest levels of biosecurity and adhere to the requirements of the Avian Influenza Prevention Zone which includes the housing of all birds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will consider reconvening the High Street Task Force to (i) review the progress of the implementation of recommendations contained within their 2022 report; and to (ii) consider further recommendations, or amendments, to reflect the changing economic environment.
answer_text: The High Street Task Force concluded its work when it presented its Report to Junior Ministers in March 2022. Since then, a number of the recommendations have been taken forward by Ministerial colleagues at the Departments for Communities, Economy, Finance, and Infrastructure. Further detail can be sought from those departments.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any measures in place to determine the success, or otherwise, of the funding allocated for social and racial cohesion grants provided to (i) councils; and (ii) other bodies, following the unrest in Ballymena, in June 2025.
answer_text: The outcomes of additional funding provided for social and racial cohesion purposes are assessed using participant questionnaires, designed to measure the impact of the activities they have undertaken, and qualitative feedback reports.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the amount paid for social and racial cohesion following unrest in Ballymena in June 2025; (ii) any criteria used to allocate funding; (iii) whether specific councils were invited to apply for these allocations; and (iv) the projects on which allocated funding was spent, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Councils were allocated a total of £520k following the unrest in Ballymena in June. This comprised of an initial allocation of £220k confirmed with councils on 9 July 2025, and a subsequent allocation of £300k confirmed with councils on 30 Oct 2025. Councils were asked to submit applications for the extra funding, outlining emerging or existing needs in the council area relating to issues around social and racial cohesion, and to detail how they planned to address these issues.All 11 councils were invited to apply for the additional funding.The following table outlines projects on which allocated funding has been spent to date:Council Programmes/ProjectsABCGood Relations Family Fun Day, 6x Community Support Hubs, New Residents Guide, Challenging Behaviours ProgrammeANAANA Education Programme, ANA Engagement Programme, ANA Enrichment Programme, ANA Early Action ProgrammeANDGood Relations International Community Event, 2x Multicultural School WorkshopsBCCNo Room for HateCCGDiversity Education Cultural Workshops, Changemaker ProgrammeDCSCWaterside Neighbourhood Partnership, Greater Shantallow Area Partnership, TriaxFAOCoolnagard Community Development Association, Hospital Community Development Association, Strathroy Community Development Association, Lisanelly Community Development AssociationLCCCSeymour Hill ASB Project, Glenavy Youth Project, Inclusion Football, Derriaghy/Milltown Hate Crime Project, LCCC Young Asylum Seeker ProjectMEAMEA Common Good Programme, MEA Integrate ProgrammeMUDiversity through Creative Arts, New and Diverse Communities, BME InclusionNMDNewry Water Festival, Cultural Day Downpatrick, Newry Mela, Small Worlds Sessions, Chinese Celebration Event, Romanian Family Day, Newry DiwaliTo note: councils are at various stages of delivery, having opted for differing approaches based on local knowledge and expertise. This ensures adaptability to changing environments and maximum impact within local council areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what (i) correspondence; (ii) telephone calls; (iii) meetings; and (iii) requests for meetings, that have taken place between him or his officials and the Minister for Infrastructure or her officials to discuss sewage pollution by Northern Ireland Water, since the restoration of the Executive in February 2024.
answer_text: There have been eight items of correspondence between myself and the DfI Minister.  My officials correspond with staff from DFI regularly in respect to the contribution of NI Water Infrastructure to water quality. This has included the oversight of NI Water delivery of their business plan, the delivery of the Living with Water Belfast Plan and the Urban Wastewater Treatment Regulations (NI) 2007 Sensitive Area Review (SAR) designation.Telephone calls are not recorded.There have been five meetings (July, December 2024 and June, September 2025 and most recently November 17th 2025) between myself, my officials and the Minister of the Department for Infrastructure (DfI) and her officials. I met with my counterpart in DfI on 3 of these occasions, on the others, I met with officials. My officials are also engaged with DfI officials on a range of forums focused on improving water quality and oversight of NI Water. These include the Lough Neagh Stakeholder Forums; the Living with Water engagement; the Price Control 21/Price Control 27/Price Control 28 Environmental Quality Working Group; and the Output Review Group.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of arrests made for non-crime hate incidents, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the number of arrests for non-crime hate incidents reported to police.  The recording of information on arrests for offences perceived to be motivated by hate crime is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to arrests for non-crime hate incidents reported to police, as detailed in your question, to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of complaints for non-crime hate incidents reported, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the number of complaints for non-crime hate incidents reported to police.  The recording of information on incidents perceived to be motivated by hate crime that are reported to police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to non-crime hate incidents reported to police, as detailed in your question, to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has considered additional restrictions on the times and locations for the use of fireworks.
answer_text: A fireworks licence includes conditions on use, including restrictions on; times, locations and types of fireworks that can be used depending on the display, and will only be issued if it is deemed safe to do so.  Further conditions can be added to any licence as necessary. Unlike other parts of the UK, members of the public are only permitted a licence for use of fireworks specified on their licence, the majority of which are ‘F2’ category, commonly referred to as ‘garden fireworks’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the effectiveness of the enforcement of current existing laws on the sale and licensing of fireworks; and (ii) whether she is considering any further measures.
answer_text: I have no current plans the review the existing fireworks licensing arrangements.  The existing regulations provide a robust framework that seeks to ensure fireworks are sold and bought responsibly for use by the public. They are significantly more robust that the legislative arrangements in other neighbouring jurisdictions.The licensing system aids partners, including PSNI, to identify unlicensed and illegal fireworks use.  Offences committed under the firework regulations may result in a fine of up to £5,000, or a prison sentence, or both.Any concerns about the misuse of fireworks should be reported to the PSNI.In recognition of the public concern around the misuse of fireworks, I have instructed my officials to examine penalties and enforcement concerning the illegal use or sale or fireworks in the pending review of sentencing issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of defects, recorded in the last year due to substandard repairs, following utility works, in the Ards and North Down local council area.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers and requires the undertaker to comply with the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads.My Department applies the procedures for inspection of reinstatements and for dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the NI Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections.  Where a utility company fails to carry out reinstatement to an acceptable standard, the utility company is duly notified to carry out remedial works to bring the reinstatement to the required standard.Location1 April 24 – 31 March 251 April 25 – 21 October 25North Down & Ards11088My Department does not hold information on a constituency basis, however the numbers of failed utility reinstatements on roads and footways, in North Down and Ards area, are provided in the table below:

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to legislate for the use of silent or low-noise fireworks for private and community use.
answer_text: The determination of the maximum noise levels of fireworks is a product safety issue and a reserved matter which rests with the UK Government.Information on firework safety and the availability of low noise fireworks is available all year round on the government services webpage nidirect.I have advised as to the benefits of low-noise fireworks in my communications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures she is taking to (i) support; and (ii) develop, the public transport network.
answer_text: My department continues to provide substantial funding to Translink to maintain essential bus and rail services, ensuring accessibility and affordability for passengers. This includes support for concessionary fares, investment in zero-emission buses, and maintaining service levels in rural and urban areas.(ii)  In terms of development, my Department is progressing several key strategic projects, including the delivery of Belfast Grand Central Station and the development of integrated ticketing systems. We are also working to improve active travel infrastructure and support modal shift through better integration and planning of public transport services.Officials are currently developing a new suite of Transport Plans that will guide transport policy and investment decisions through to 2035. These plans will provide the strategic framework for future improvements to the public transport network.The All-Island Strategic Rail Review, published in July 2024, is an ambitious and collaborative piece of work with the potential to transform the rail network across the island. The final report includes 32 recommendations aimed at enhancing rail services through new and reopened routes, higher speeds, increased frequencies, and additional capacity, connecting more communities to the network.While the Review provides an evidence-based framework for future investment, further work is required to assess the feasibility and affordability of its recommendations and to secure the necessary funding. My officials are working closely with counterparts in the Department of Transport, Translink, Irish Rail, and the National Transport Authority to consider the Review’s findings. This includes the development of a Project Prioritisation Strategy, which is due to be published later this year. In parallel, Translink has undertaken several feasibility studies linked to the Review’s recommendations. I am currently considering these studies and intend to publish a summary report in the coming months.Delivering the Review’s recommendations will be challenging, given the scale of the proposed interventions and the transformative change required. However, my Department remains committed to exploring all available funding opportunities, recognising the importance of this work in driving economic growth, enhancing connectivity, and addressing long-standing regional imbalances.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of vehicles (i) their Department; and (ii) its arm's-length bodies, own or lease which are (a) diesel; (b) petrol; (c) hybrid; (d) hydrogen; and (e) electric.
answer_text: The Department owns four Ministerial cars, all of which are petrol/hybrid and are provided for the Junior First Minister, the Junior deputy First Minister, senior Departmental Officials and one is on loan to the Department for Infrastructure. None are owned by the Department’s arm’s length bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) who requested inspections of the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) research farm at Hillsborough by (a) the NIEA; (b) the Royal Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals; and (c) Red Tractor; (ii) when these requests were made; (iii) when each of the inspections were carried out; (iv) what were the results of these inspections; (iv) when was AFBI notified of the results of the inspections; and (v) the reasons why he refused to speak to the media on 23 October 2025 in relation to conditions at the fa
answer_text: My Permanent Secretary requested that an inspection be undertaken by Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) Water Quality Inspectors on 27 October 2025 following notification to the Department of allegations relating to water quality.   Neither AFBI nor my Department is aware of any inspection being carried out by the RSPCA.  I understand that on 28 October AFBI made a request to Red Tractor to undertake an audit. The inspection by NIEA water quality inspectors was carried out on 28 October 2025. At the time of writing AFBI awaits the completion of the audit by Red Tractor.NIEA inspectors found no evidence of any breach of the NAP Regulations in respect of the Closed Period Spreading. Inspectors did however identify evidence of low severity pollution in a waterway on the farm, and a request was made for action to immediately minimise pollution impact. NIEA is continuing to follow up on this issue.  AFBI was advised of the findings following the inspection.Prior to considering a request to speak to the media on these allegations, my Department requested evidence and details of the allegations being made. To date neither has been provided.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any measures being proposed with regard to the sites selected for his Department’s pilot programme of site specific actions to reduce ammonia emissions around designated sites; and (ii) whether these have been agreed with the relevant landowners.
answer_text: A prioritisation exercise is currently being undertaken to identify potential sites to take forward a pilot programme of site-specific actions to reduce ammonia emissions around Special Areas of Conservation (SACs). Engagement with landowners and other stakeholders will be crucial in the development of the pilot programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the sites identified for its pilot programme of site specific actions to reduce ammonia emissions around designated sites; and (ii) the reasons those sites have been selected.
answer_text: A prioritisation exercise is currently being developed to identify potential protected sites to take forward a pilot programme of site-specific actions to reduce ammonia emissions around Special Areas of Conservation (SACs).  Site selection criteria are yet to be agreed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any policy on declarations of interest, in relation to actual, or perceived, conflicts of interests both pecuniary, and non-pecuniary, as it relates to civil servants of grade 3 or above.
answer_text: DAO (DoF) 07/21, as revised July 2023, sets out Guidance on Conflicts of Interest, including a number of points specifically related to senior officials. This is available at Dear Accounting Officer letters (DAOs) 2021 | Department of Finance.There are a number of related documents; the NICS Staff Handbook and the NICS Code of Ethics set out the standards of conduct for all civil servants, with all required to act with integrity, impartiality, and honesty, avoiding any actual or perceived conflicts of interest. The Standards of Conduct policy reminds civil servants of the requirement to report conflicts of interests; ensure their conduct does not foster the suspicion of a conflict of interest; and not to take part in activity that conflicts with the interest of their department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of lighting columns removed permanently, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: When street lighting is deemed to have reached the end of its life and needs to be replaced, the area is assessed and new lighting is provided only in those areas that meet my Department’s policy for the provision of lighting, RSPPG E072 which can be accessed via the link below.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/street-lighting-rsppg-e072-2019.pdfThe policy aims to ensure replacement lighting is installed where it provides the greatest public benefit, particularly where road or infrastructure safety is concerned, as well as reducing future maintenance and energy costs and environmental impacts.Whilst my Department keeps detailed records of the number of street lights across the network, details of lights which have been removed and not replaced are not recorded separately and would not be easily attained.  To attempt to provide details of these removals would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the budget provided to each section office in the Southern Division of DfI Roads, for the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: My Department does not allocate budget by section office, however, I can advise that budgets are allocated to the four Roads Divisions. In 2025-26, DfI Roads Southern Division will benefit from Resource and Capital budget allocations of £11.8m and £32.4m respectively. The resource budget delivers a wide range of essential maintenance activities such as patching, street lighting and environmental works whilst the capital budget will fund structural maintenance such as resurfacing and structural drainage; and contribute to various capital works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount recovered from landowners, who have not complied with tree and hedge cutting notices, in the Strangford constituency, in each of the last 10 years.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail all the traffic calming projects, completed in the Ards and North Down local council area, in the last 10 years, together with the scores and costs associated with these projects.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.Details of all completed traffic calming schemes are included in the Department’s annual reports to Ards and North Down Borough Council which are published on my Department’s website. My department does not hold the costs and scores of completed projects in an easily accessible format and any attempt to provide the information requested would require an extensive exercise to be conducted which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of tree and hedge cutting notices, issued to landowners in the Strangford constituency, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: There are only a limited number of trees in my Department’s ownership.  The ownership and primary maintenance responsibility for almost all roadside trees lies with the adjacent landowner.During regular routine maintenance inspections, officials will note trees and hedges, within or immediately adjacent to the road boundaries, which are causing an obstruction; obscuring road signs; or have the potential to create danger for the road user. In those instances, landowners will be contacted and asked to address the issue.  If the landowner fails to respond, the Department can instigate legal action to have the danger addressed, although this action is not usually required. The Department also places notices in the local press and in farming journals reminding landowners of their responsibility and the need to carry out maintenance during the early part of the year, avoiding the bird nesting season from 1 March to 31 August. My Department has issued numerous written notifications to landowners, urging them to carry out the necessary remedial work. Alongside this, officials have also held direct discussions with landowners to clarify their responsibilities. Since 2021, which represents the available data held by my department, 759 notices have been issued to landowners within the Ards & North Down Borough Council area. The table below provides a breakdown for the available years. Financial YearNotices issued in the Ards and North Down Area2024/252352023/242022022/231892021/22133There was no requirement to initiate the formal enforcement process as landowners complied with all issued notices.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail which statutory consultees have submitted call in requests to her Department in respect of local planning authority decisions, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Section 29(1) of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 allows my Department to direct that an individual application or applications be referred to my Department instead of being dealt with by a council.  A similar ‘call in’ power exists for conservation area, listed building and hazardous substance consent applications. Should a statutory consultee wish to request that a planning application is ‘called in’, separately from the notification process set out above, this  will be fully considered by my Department before a decision is made. Whilst my Department holds certain data in relation to notifications and ‘called in’ planning applications, it does not hold information in the format requested by the member. I can advise, however, that there have been instances where a statutory consultee, such as the Housing Executive , NI Water  and also my Department (DfI Roads), have requested the ‘call in’ of a planning application. It is important to note that the ‘call in’ function provides a necessary safeguard where there are applications that raise issues of such importance that their impact is considered to extend to a sub-regional or regional level. Whilst it is a discretionary power, it is generally only used in exceptional circumstances as it is recognised that councils are best placed to deal with the vast majority of planning applications within their own council areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of pollution incidents which have occurred on the Dibney River, Killyleagh, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department substantiated five water pollution incidents, in the Dibney River catchment, in the last 5 years: two in 2021, and one each in 2022, 2023 and 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any water quality tests including their results that have been carried out at Strangford Lough at Killyleagh, in the last five years.
answer_text: Killyleagh is situated in the Strangford Lough South water body. The sites monitored within that water body are Ardmillan Bay, Long Sheelah, Marlfield Bay, Green Island, and Ballyhenry Island.  Water quality monitoring under the Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017 at these sites includes parameters such as temperature, salinity, and dissolved oxygen. During the winter months (November – February) these sites are also monitored for nutrients and chlorophyll. Monthly samples are also collected from Ballyhenry Island for various pollutants.  These data contribute toward the assessment of the ecological and chemical status of Strangford Lough South water body.The most recent (2024) ecological and chemical status assessment of Strangford Lough South has indicated that dissolved oxygen, dissolved inorganic nitrogen, phytoplankton, and seaweeds (macroalgae) are at high status; this is based on data over the period 2019-2024.  Overall ecological status of this water body was assessed as ‘moderate’ due to the presence of specific pollutants, including the insecticide Permethrin. The site also failed to meet chemical status due to the presence of the pesticide hexachlorocyclohexane (HCH).  The Environmental Quality Standards for these pesticides are extremely tight and failures due to the presence of these chemicals are ubiquitous.The Infaunal Quality Index (IQI) is an index that indicates the ecological health of marine benthic invertebrate communities under the Water Environment (Water Framework Directive) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2017. The IQI for Strangford Lough South scored either ‘good’ or ‘high’ on the index for the most recent years of available data (2021-2023).
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any work being carried out to improve water quality at Strangford Lough at Killyleagh.
answer_text: My department’s 3rd Cycle River Basin Management Plan, outlines measures to protect and improve water quality across Northern Ireland, including Strangford Lough. Further information can be found at: www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/third-river-basin-management-plan-northern-ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the number of social enterprises, established in the Strangford constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy does not have a breakdown of the Social Enterprises established in the Strangford Constituency over the each of the past five years. However, the Department for Economy commissioned the 2025 Northern Ireland Social Enterprise Sector Report earlier this year. Of the social enterprises surveyed for this report, 22% were based in County Down. This report also indicates that of all the Social Enterprises surveyed: 4% were created in the last year; 14% were created one, two or three years ago; and 13% were created four or five years ago.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether cabotegravir injections will be made available in Northern Ireland following approval in England and Wales.
answer_text: In line with the publication of final National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidance my Department is reviewing the recommendation to make cabotegravir available as an option for pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) to reduce the risk of sexually acquired Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) infection in adults and young people at high risk of getting HIV who are unable to take a regimen of oral daily PrEP.Where found to be applicable, this guidance will be endorsed for implementation within Northern Ireland HSC organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of requests for local authority planning decisions, that have been (i) made; and (ii) accepted by her Department, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Section 29(1) of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 allows my Department to direct that an individual application or applications be referred to my Department instead of being dealt with by a council.  A similar ‘call in’ power exists for conservation area, listed building and hazardous substance consent applications. The ‘call in’ function provides a necessary safeguard where there are applications that raise issues of such importance that their impact is considered to extend to a sub-regional or regional level. A planning application can be ‘called in’ by my Department at any time up until a council has issued a decision. Whilst it is a discretionary power, it is generally only used in exceptional circumstances as it is recognised that councils are best placed to deal with the vast majority of planning applications within their own council areas. To become aware of applications that may be of interest to my Department, councils must notify my Department in certain circumstances. In addition to this, a third party can also request that a planning application is ‘called in’ and all such requests are fully considered by my Department before a decision is made whether or not the application warrants referral. Whilst my Department holds certain data in relation to ‘called in’ planning applications, it does not hold information in the format requested. Table 1, however, provides details on the number of planning applications ‘called in’ by my Department since April 2015. It is important to note that those applications called in prior to 8 May 2016 would have been called in by the former Department of the Environment. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how a local planning authority decision can be validly called in by her Department.
answer_text: Section 29(1) of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 allows my Department to direct that an individual application or applications be referred to my Department instead of being dealt with by a council.  A similar referral power exists in the 2011 Act in relation to applications for listed building consent (section 89), conservation area consent (section 88(1) by virtue of section 105(6)) and hazardous substance consent (section 114). Once a ‘call in’ direction has been issued to a council, jurisdiction for the determination of the application passes to my Department.An application can be referred to my Department at any time during a council’s consideration of that application, up to the date at which the council has issued a decision. Where a planning application has already been decided, my Department cannot use the referral power to intervene.Whilst the power to ‘call in’ a planning application is discretionary, it is generally only used in exceptional circumstances as it is recognised that councils are best placed to deal with the vast majority of planning applications within their own council area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what steps are being taken to mitigate the risk of the spread of Asian hornets.
answer_text: NIEA officials have traced and removed the first recorded Asian hornet nest in Northern Ireland in Dundonald and are continuing their monitoring and trapping programme in that area to ensure that there are no further nests. Learning points from this experience will be used to review and refine the published DAERA Asian Hornet Contingency Plan which focuses on response to reported hornets. Given this species ability to hide in imported goods and on transport, it is extremely difficult to implement effective proactive measures to keep them out of Northern Ireland, and therefore maintaining government and public vigilance is key.NIEA is working in collaboration with the Irish National Parks and Wildlife Service and the National Biodiversity Data Centre (Ireland) through the Shared Island Biosecurity and Invasive Species Initiative, to establish a more joined up approach to responding to the Asian hornet across the island of Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) their assessment of low numbers of Protestant woman coming forward to The Inquiry (Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses) and Redress Scheme Bill; and (ii) any steps being taken to address this.
answer_text: We are committed to supporting all victims and survivors to come forward to the Truth Recovery Programme including to the forthcoming public inquiry.  The Public Inquiry and Redress Scheme Bill was introduced in the Assembly on the 16 June 2025, and we hope will become an Act next year.  However, the non-statutory investigation by the Truth Recovery Independent Panel has collected over 230 oral testimonies since its establishment in April 2023. This includes oral testimonies from institutions linked to the Protestant tradition, for example Thorndale House in Belfast.The Department funded a leaflet drop to every household (over 800,000 homes) in early 2025, inviting people to come forward to provide oral testimony. The Independent Panel has taken additional steps to appeal to victims and survivors from a Protestant background using articles, community networks and engagement with Churches. For example, on 16 October 2024, Leanne McCormick (co-chair of the Independent Panel) published an article in the Newsletter which aimed to address the common misperception that ‘This was just a Catholic issue’. We will continue to take steps to encourage all those affected to participate in the process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) what support is provided by his Department for (a) businesses; and (b) individuals, in removing invasive non-native plant species; and (ii) whether any biocontrol research has been carried out on his Department's behalf into the use of living organisms as a cost and time-effective way of managing invasive non-native plant species.
answer_text: (i) With regards to (a) businesses and (b) individual property owners, when it comes to invasive non-native species, in both national and EU legislation, DAERA’s policy context is to protect the ecology of an area as opposed to safeguarding property or other commercial assets. From this perspective, DAERA offers a variety of grants and funding to support local farming, fisheries, rural business, communities and environmental NGOs through, for example, the Environment Fund and Environmental Farming Schemes, DAERA continues to encourage and fund, many community groups, non-government organisations, farmers and landowners to carry out management and removal of invasive plant species from their land, prioritising designated and high value biodiversity sites. DAERA also provides a wide range of advice and guidance on how to manage invasive plants on its website, https://www.invasivespeciesni.co.uk.  Removing invasive non–native plant species must be part of any land or homeowners’ general maintenance responsibilities.(ii) Officials from the NIEA Invasive Non-Native Species (INNS) Team are working closely with Cabi, an organisation dedicated to improving lives worldwide by providing information and applying expertise to solve agricultural and environmental problems, to set up a trial site for the biological control of aquatic weeds.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the criteria for the designation of (a) ancient woodland; and (b) long-established woodland; and (ii) the protections afforded to each designation.
answer_text: The Ancient Woodland Inventory undertaken by the Woodland Trust during 2003-7 identified ancient woodland and long-established woodland in Northern Ireland. This project categorised ancient woodland based on the first accurate, comprehensive mapping by the Ordnance Survey in 1830. Woodlands present on the 1830s map were accessed and further classified as ancient based on other evidence that supported its continued woodland status since the 1600s. The long established woodland category was used for woodlands identified in the 1830 maps, that could not be verified in continuous woodland use.Woodlands including ancient and long established woodlands are protected under the Forestry Act (Northern Ireland) 2010, and the Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) (Forestry) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2006.Additionally, where woodlands are situated in Areas of Special Scientific Interest or Special Areas of Conservation protection is afforded under the Environment Order (Northern Ireland) 2022 and the Conservation (Natural Habitats, etc.) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1995.Furthermore, local councils have powers for the protection of individual trees and woodlands through the issuing and enforcement of Tree Preservation Orders. Also, councils have responsibility for planning decisions regarding proposed tree removals for development purposes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what additional assistance does his Department provide to help restore plantations on ancient woodland sites.
answer_text: The Department’s competitive Environment Fund provides support to the not-for-profit voluntary sector and district council partners to deliver key environmental outcomes. This can include measures to improve and manage important habitats including ancient woodland sites.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the progress made with the commencement of Part 5 of the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, covering Enduring Power of Attorney and health and welfare decisions.
answer_text: The Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (MCA), a piece of legislation brought forward by the Department of Health and the Department of Justice (DoJ), provides a framework for people who lack capacity to make a decision for themselves and for those who now have capacity but wish to make preparations for a time in the future when they may lack capacity. The MCA has been implemented in a phased approach, with provisions relating to deprivation of liberty, research and money and valuables commenced to date. Although there are currently no indicative timings for full commencement of the MCA, my Department is currently working collaboratively with DoJ officials to develop an updated implementation plan to better understand the resource need to enable full MCA implementation, which will help to guide future Departmental decision making, including that of these provisions. Any decision regarding provisions related to lasting powers of attorney would need to be considered in collaboration with DoJ.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 9403/22-27, for an update on the development and publication of the Nature Recovery (Biodiversity) Strategy.
answer_text: The draft Nature Recovery Strategy is being finalised for consultation later this year. Timescales for the publication of a final agreed strategy are dependent on the outcome of the public consultation exercise.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of motoring offence cases brought before Newtownards Magistrates, in the last year, broken down as (i) a number; and (ii) a proportion of the total number of cases in that court.
answer_text: There were 3,100 cases dealt with at Newtownards adult magistrates’ courts in 2024, the most recent year for which information is available.  Of these, a motoring offence was the main offence in 2,198 (70.9%) cases.Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures for motoring offence cases relate to cases where the main offence at disposal was a motoring offence.3. Figures relate to cases dealt with at adult magistrates’ courts.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of social housing applicants with a disability, in need of accessible housing in the Strangford constituency, in each of the last five years.
