question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of people on remand, either (a) in custody; or (b) on bail, whilst awaiting trial; and ii) the length of time spent awaiting trial.
answer_text: (1a) On 1st October 2025 there were 867 prisoners in custody being held on remand.(1b) Whilst NICTS does record remand on bail and custody orders within the Integrated Courts Operating System (ICOS) it is not stored in a format that can track each defendant’s status over time. It is estimated that to locate, retrieve and extract the information would involve a manual trawl of individual court records and this would incur a disproportionate cost.(2) Information from NIPS is not available on the length of time the prisoners on remand have spent awaiting trial. However, the following table provides details on the length of time spent on remand by prisoners in custody on 1st October 2025.Time on RemandNumber of Prisoners3 months or less502Over 3 months to 6 months187Over 6 months to 1 year118Over 1 year to 2 years44Over 2 years to 3 years10Over 3 years6Total Remand Prisoners867Source: PRISM – Management InformationNote: Information is snapshot data, correct as at midnight on 1st October 2025.Individuals classified as ‘Remand’ includes those who are ‘Remand’ or ‘Awaiting Trial’ which depends on what Court Tier (Magistrates or Crown) the holding charges are being heard in. An individual serving a custodial sentence, may also have had separate holding charges active in either a Magistrates and/or Crown Court. They are not classified on PRISM as ‘Awaiting Trail’ or ‘Remand’ due to serving a custodial sentence.Time spent on remand includes time spent so far during the current period on remand and excludes any other separate periods of time on remand within the time in custody. If a period of ‘Awaiting Trial’ directly follows a period of ‘Remand’ then these are combined and counted as one period of remand.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the rationale for the lack of provision of a student maintenance grant for full-time students attending the Open University.
answer_text: The reasoning behind giving students on full-time distance learning courses the same financial support as part-time students is that distance learning courses are by their nature flexible and can generally be fitted around work and other commitments, making their support needs much more like those of part-time students.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has carried out any assessment into the Section 75 implications of the lack of provision of student maintenance grants for full-time students attending the Open University, given the disproportionate impact on mature students.
answer_text: When maintenance grants for full-time students undertaking distance learning courses (including study at the Open University) were first introduced, it was concluded that the policy was likely to have no adverse impact on equality of opportunity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of cases closed by the Child Maintenance Service as a result of a failure to trace the paying parent, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Due to IT system limitations the information requested is not available for the full period. However, an IT system change was implemented on 25 August 2024 which allows the recording of case closures due to a failure to trace the paying parent. Therefore, the information requested can be provided from this date.  From 25 August 2024 to 30 September 2025, the Child Maintenance Service has closed 213 cases due to the failure to trace the Paying Parent.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has carried out any assessment of the prevalence of directors of insolvent companies engaging in phoenixing.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out any assessment of the prevalence of directors of insolvent companies engaging in phoenixing.  However, officials from my Department’s Insolvency Service liaise closely with the Insolvency Service of England and Wales who are actively looking at this issue with other enforcement agencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department is considering any additional measures to prevent directors of insolvent companies engaging in phoenixing.
answer_text: My Department’s Insolvency Service will seek the disqualification of company directors who are found to have been guilty of misconduct.  This will include those cases where a company has entered insolvency and the directors have started up a new company with the same business model allowing the new company to continue trading while leaving the debts of the old company unpaid (known as phoenixing). The new company then also enters insolvency. My Department has no current plans to introduce additional measures to prevent this type of abuse occurring.  However, officials from my Department’s Insolvency Service liaise closely with the Insolvency Service of England and Wales who are actively looking at this issue with other enforcement agencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he is taking to encourage the recycling and reuse of textiles to reduce the amount of waste from the clothing and fashion industry.
answer_text: In accordance with the waste hierarchy, DAERA aim to prevent waste generation and promote reuse and recycling to keep products in circulation for as long as possible. 		To support this goal, my Department has funded multiple projects focused on enhancing resource efficiency and minimising waste, including textile waste. When reduction, reuse or repair is no longer feasible, my Department encourages and supports the recycling of unwanted clothing and textiles, through behaviour change campaigns, support to councils and by working with the third sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any assessment has been carried out of the effectiveness of short term let control areas, to manage high concentrations of secondary lettings.
answer_text: Short term let ‘control areas' and 'secondary lettings' are not currently defined in the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) or in planning legislation. No local development plan document has designated the type of short-term letting control zones referred to and therefore no assessment of the effectiveness of such measures has been made. The Department for Infrastructure is responsible for planning legislation and policy and will continue to engage with my Department on this issue through the cross departmental working group involving DfC, DfI and the Department for the Economy. As part of a coordinated Executive response, Departments may consider whether policy and/or legislation might better regulate this form of development, informed by the findings of the DfC commissioned research on the nature, scale and potential impacts of short-term holiday lets on local housing markets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will produce an allergy strategy.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail what percentage of the total prescriptions of Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis are being provided to women.
answer_text: Across Northern Ireland the percentage of the total prescriptions of Pre-Exposure Prophylaxis (PrEP) which are being provided to women is as follows: Cis-females = 0.9% Trans-females = 0.5%.   This data represents the PrEP prescribing position for the period 1 July 2025 to 30 September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what percentage of women account for new diagnoses of HIV.
answer_text: Based on provisional 2023 data, Public Health Agency HIV Surveillance indicates that the percentage of newly diagnosed women with HIV in Northern Ireland in 2023 is 33.7%.  This includes women who reside in Northern Ireland who were previously diagnosed outside of the UK.   The percentage of women who were first diagnosed in Northern Ireland in 2023 is 19.1%.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of young people who have presented as homeless, broken down by the reason for homelessness, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin October 2024 – March 2025, published on 12 June 2025, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-october-2024-march-2025 	Information on homeless presenters is available in Section 1 of the accompanying tables; however, I am unable to provide a breakdown in the format you have requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of young people who have presented as homeless, broken down by the type of accommodation they were residing in at the time of presentation, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer this question, I refer the Member to the information placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of young people who have presented as homeless, broken down by Housing Executive district, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin October 2024 – March 2025, published on 12 June 2025, contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-october-2024-march-2025 	Information on homeless presenters is available in Section 1 of the accompanying tables; however, I am unable to provide a breakdown in the format you have requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support, provided by his Department, available to young people leaving care.
answer_text: My Department provides employability support and information on claiming benefits to young care leavers through my Department’s Jobs and Benefits office network. My officials are working in partnership with the Health Trusts to ensure these services are as accessible as possible.  The Northern Ireland Housing Executive, an Arm’s Length Body sponsored by my Department supports young people leaving care via delivery of shared responsibilities with Health and Social Care Trusts towards meeting the accommodation needs of 16 and 17-year-olds.  The Housing Executive has published a Youth Homelessness Action Plan which can be accessed here: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/getattachment/05fb0d2e-603f-4067-a381-04b2919b0ccd/Youth-Homelessness-Action-Plan-Accessible.pdf This action plan contains a commitment to review the Regional Good Practice Guidance to ensure that young people leaving care are supported and work on the delivery of this action is ongoing.The Housing Executive is also working with the Department for Communities and the Departments of Health and Justice to deliver the New Foundations Pilot Programme. This programme aims to prioritise support for young people leaving care and help to prevent this group from the risk of homelessness.Additionally, the Supporting People Programme, provides grant funding towards the provision of housing related support services for young people (including those leaving care) across Northern Ireland, with provision in each of the five Health and Social Care Trust Areas. Housing related support is tailored to meet the needs of young people through accommodation-based provision for those in housing need, and Floating Support for those starting a new tenancy and setting up a home. This support can also be provided in times of need to help prevent young people becoming homeless. Support can include guidance and advocacy on housing options, assistance with finance and budgeting, developing practical skills to maintain a home, and assistance with education, employment and mental wellbeing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of incidents of anti-social behaviour recorded on the Glider service, in each month of the last 12 years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Firstly, Translink would like to advise that the Glider rapid transit system first came into service in September 2018 so it is not possible to supply the full extent of the information you have requested nor would information be retained for that duration in any event.  They can however supply details of reported anti-social behaviour over the last 5 years - see table below.Whilst anti-social behaviour is a wider societal issue for many organisations, Translink’s top priority is safety and it take all incidents extremely seriously.  Thankfully, such incidents are relatively infrequent, and with over half a million passenger journeys every week on Belfast Metro and Glider services, the vast majority of passengers experience safe and comfortable journeys on these services. Translink takes a multi-agency approach to mitigate anti-social behaviour, working with the PSNI Safe Transport Team, Community Representatives, and other agencies including youth groups and schools, with the aim of deterring incidences of anti-social behaviour.  Translink staff are trained to deal with anti-social behaviour and the use of body-worn cameras, security personnel and CCTV cameras in place all play an important role in this regard. Furthermore, Translink collaborates with trade unions and industry colleagues to find new ways to combat anti-social behaviour; it also offers a reward scheme of up to £1,000 for those who give evidence which leads to a conviction. 202020212022202320242025January016200February315212March745571April011502May132184June241224July1514116August022367September023534October117414November45102December30312Yearly totals323534314630For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has had an input in the feasibility study into a squirrelpox vaccine being undertaken by the Welsh Government.
answer_text: My Department has not had any input in the Welsh Government’s vaccine feasibility study to protect vulnerable red squirrel populations from squirrel pox.With invasive American grey squirrels having a higher resistance to the disease, currently the best method to protect red squirrels from the virus is to prevent contact between populations of red and grey squirrels, but the outcome of the Welsh feasibility study is awaited with interest.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to develop a built heritage strategy.
answer_text: I launched the Heritage, Culture & Creativity Programme in July 2024 to deliver four new policies for public libraries, museums, the arts and the historic environment.  Significant progress has been made since I announced the programme, with a comprehensive round of engagement across local government and executive departments having been completed.The focus has now moved onto policy drafting and planning for public consultation. Allowing for the full public consultation processes, it is anticipated that all the policies in this first phase of the Programme will be in place by spring 2026. Subject to public consultation and publication, an outcome of the Historic Environment Policy strand of the HCC Programme will be to consider and implement specific historic environment related policy actions within DfC.  These may include, for example, strategies such as ‘Heritage at Risk’ and ‘Stimulating Investment in Northern Ireland’s Historic Environment’, which are currently under development by officials in my Department, to support specific anticipated Historic Environment Policy priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to support owners of heritage buildings, in order to assist them with the maintenance of those buildings.
answer_text: I have already doubled the support available for this year to listed building owners for repairs and maintenance from £240k to £500k via the Department’s Historic Environment Fund.  I am keen to do more, and to this end, my officials are currently developing a business case, building upon Departmental research published earlier this year, looking at how best to stimulate investment in the historic environment. This will seek to improve support for homeowners and help them meet the costs they face, given the wide benefit of these buildings to our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any impact the legal challenge to the Northern Ireland Football Fund decision will have the ongoing process.
answer_text: I am not yet in a position to confirm whether any legal challenge in relation to The Northern Ireland Football Fund will have an impact on the ongoing process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any support provided by his Department to young people leaving care.
answer_text: The Department of Health (DoH) is responsible for developing and maintaining the legislative framework governing leaving care services, including the implementation of the Children (Leaving Care) Act (Northern Ireland) 2002 and associated regulations.Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts deliver services funded by the Department of Health. The Department maintains a performance oversight role to ensure Trusts comply with statutory duties and minimum standards for care leavers.Key services provided to care leavers through HSC Trusts include:Personal adviser supportPathway planningEducation, training, and employment supportAccommodation supportFinancial assistanceHealth and wellbeing servicesIndependent living skillsExtended support beyond age 21Departmental officials lead regional implementation projects aimed at standardising service delivery across all HSC Trusts. This includes promoting inter-agency collaboration to integrate housing, education, and employment into leaving care support.Recent strategic developments aim to further enhance provision for care leavers. They include the utilisation of the Children’s Services Co-operation Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 to strengthen cross-departmental engagement strengthening the wider Corporate Family Role within service delivery.Children’s Services Co-operation Act underpins the development of the New Foundations Action Plan, aligned with Programme for Government (PfG) 2024–2027 Priority 6, and the Review of Jointly Commissioned Accommodation. Together, these initiatives aim to improve outcomes for care-experienced young people.The New Foundations Action Plan promotes collaboration between the Department of Health, the Department for Communities, and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive to prevent homelessness and improve transition planning through tailored support.As part of the New Foundations Action Plan, the Department of Health is leading efforts on behalf of HSC Trusts to develop and publicise a local Offer for care leavers in Northern Ireland.  This Local Offer will clearly outline the full range of statutory and discretionary services available to care-experienced young people, ensuring transparency, consistency, and accessibility across all Trust areas.  It reflects a commitment to the corporate parenting principles and aims to empower young people by making support pathways more visible and navigable.The Review of Jointly Commissioned Accommodation highlights the need for trauma-informed, flexible housing models that reflect the complex needs of care leavers. It recommends improved governance and planning partnerships to ensure safe, supportive living environments. The action plan is in its 2nd year of a 4-year plan and is being co-led by Department of Health officials and Supporting People (NIHE).A review of Leaving and Aftercare Services, currently in draft form, represents a significant step toward enhancing the quality and consistency of support across Northern Ireland. Led by Departmen

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the prevalence of hypermobility.
answer_text: The Western Health & Social Care Trust has now taken the decision to close the current Public Consultation on Emergency General Surgery and plan to work with all relevant stakeholders to develop a clear vision for health and social care provision in Fermanagh and West Tyrone, including SWAH.The Western Trust are committed to the long-term sustainability and development of SWAH as a vital and successful hospital within the HSC regional network of hospitals. My officials are engaging with Trust colleagues to determine a timeline for this important work to be undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any consideration is being given to a hypermobility pathway for healthcare, similar to that in operation in Wales.
answer_text: I am aware of the recent developments in Wales to develop and implement a nationally co-produced clinical pathway for hypermobility disorders, including hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (hEDS) and hypermobility spectrum disorder (HSD), and delivered through the HealthPathways system.  My Department will monitor the progress of the Welsh initiative and consider any emerging evidence or best practice that may inform future service development in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his plans to (i) reduce; and (ii) restrict, the amount of refuse-derived fuel exported from Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My aim is to move waste as far up the waste hierarchy as possible, but not all waste can be fully recycled, so energy recovery is the next best option and in most cases is preferable to landfilling.Refuse-derived fuel (RDF) is a waste with a calorific value that can be used as energy from waste. Northern Ireland does not currently have the capacity or infrastructure to use all the refuse-derived fuel that is produced from recycling operations. It is therefore exported to countries that have the facilities and capacity to use it and get the benefit from this energy.It is important to note that responsibility for planning rests with the Department for Infrastructure and / or Local Councils. As such, many elements of contingency planning sit outside the remit of DAERA and are largely market-led, however, my officials continue to collaborate and play their part in supporting the decision-making process.  In addition, my Department has commissioned the Strategic Investment Board to undertake an analysis of NI waste arisings and infrastructure through to 2050. The findings of this review should inform future capacity decisions including consideration of the feasibility of alternatives to waste exports.Consequently, at present, I have no plans to (i) reduce; and (ii) restrict, the amount of refuse-derived fuel exported from Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the rationale behind the Education Authority’s Review of the Teaching Appointment Scheme for Controlled Schools.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any assurances she can give to allay concerns over energy security in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Security of supply is a key priority for my Department.My officials continue to engage with SONI and the Utility Regulator who each have statutory responsibilities in respect of Security of Supply.  SONI has responsibility for the real time operation of the power system and is continually monitoring the situation and providing regular updates to my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has any plans to (i) ban; and (ii) restrict, the sale of high-strength nicotine pouches.
answer_text: In the UK there are currently no age of sale or advertising restrictions for nicotine pouches and, unlike vapes, there are no set nicotine limits, and nicotine strengths vary.  Tobacco-free nicotine pouches can legally be sold to under 18s in Northern Ireland whereas oral tobacco products, such as ‘snus’, have been banned in the UK since 1992 as part of EU-wide legislation. The Tobacco and Vapes Bill, which is currently progressing through  the House of Lords, will make it an offence to sell a nicotine product to a person who is under the age of 18 and will also make it an offence for a person aged 18 or over to buy, or attempt to buy, a nicotine product (including nicotine pouches) on behalf of a person who is under 18. The Bill will also ban advertising and sponsorship of nicotine products and introduce regulation making powers to set limits for nicotine content. A UK-wide Call for Evidence (CfE) was published on the 8th October 2025 to gather information on the nicotine content, strength and absorption rates of nicotine products, such as pouches, and any associated health risks. The CfE also seeks information on the presence of heavy metals in these products and potential risks to oral health caused by flavours, ingredients or emissions. The results of the CfE will support the development of policy proposals, which will be further informed by public consultation,The Bill will also extend the existing NI Tobacco Retail Register so that all retailers that sell vaping and nicotine products will need to register with their local Council. In the longer term it is my intention to consult on the introduction of a licensing scheme for all retailers that sell tobacco products, herbal smoking products, cigarette papers, vaping and nicotine products.  Licensing will strengthen enforcement, support legitimate businesses and act as a deterrent to rogue retailers, which in turn will support the Department’s strategic aim of improving public health.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what age restrictions are in place regarding the sale of nicotine pouches.
answer_text: In the UK there are currently no age of sale or advertising restrictions for nicotine pouches and, unlike vapes, there are no set nicotine limits, and nicotine strengths vary. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the implementation of the Adult Restorative Justice Strategy Action Plan.
answer_text: A five-year adult restorative justice strategy was published in 2022. Annual progress reports, for years one and two, can be found at https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/justice/adult%20rj%20strategy%20-%20annual%20progress%20report%20year%201%202022-23.pdfand https://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/justice/adult-restorative-justice-second-annual-progress-report%202023-24.pdf   The third annual progress report (2024/25) will be published shortly.  Significant progress has been made during this year, including the development of a new Practice Standards and Accreditation Framework, launched on 17 October 2025 along with the opening of a new accreditation application process.The Adult Restorative Justice Strategy commits to delivering restorative justice at every stage of the criminal justice system and this will directly contribute to that commitment.  Much of the focus this year has been on the planning for an Adult Restorative Justice scheme that will focus on diversionary cases, in the first instance, that can be more appropriately dealt with as an out-of-court disposal.  A multi-agency Restorative Justice Working Group, involving both statutory and voluntary sector partners continues to be heavily involved in the progression of the Year 4 Action Plan and the new Framework, while also progressing the phased expansion of adult restorative justice services across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will introduce Jess’s Rule for patients who present three times with the same symptoms or concerns.
answer_text: My Department expects medical professionals to act in line with the General Medical Council’s “Good Medical Practice” guidance, which requires medical professionals to adequately assess a patient’s condition(s), taking account of their history, carry out a physical examination where necessary, and promptly provide (or arrange) suitable advice, investigation or treatment where necessary.The GMC’s guidance in regard to assessment, investigation and treatment is available at https://www.gmc-uk.org/professional-standards/the-professional-standards/good-medical-practiceThis should be the case whether a patient consultation takes place in person, online or via telephone. As part of the Cancer Strategy, within Rapid Diagnosis Centres (RDC) my Department has established a Vague Symptom Pathway which offers GPs a way to refer patients with non-specific but worrying symptoms that might indicate cancer but don't fit existing "red flag" pathways. Patients receive a coordinated, one-stop assessment to investigate their symptoms, and results are reported quickly, enabling earlier diagnosis and onward referral Two Rapid Diagnosis Centres are currently in operation, located at South Tyrone Hospital in the Southern HSC Trust and Whiteabbey Hospital in the Northern HSC Trust. The Vague Symptom Pathway became a fully regional service in July 2024 with patients across all five Trusts in Northern Ireland now able to be referred into either of the two RDC sites directly by their GP, regardless of their location.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount of funding her Department has allocated to promote restorative justice, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department has provided the following amounts to the two currently accredited restorative justice organisations, Community Restorative Justice Ireland (CRJI) and Northern Ireland Alternatives (NIA), each year:CRJINIA2015-16£54,984£54,9902016-17£54,984£54,9902017-18£69,984£69,9892018-19£107,000£107,0002019-20£106,892£107,0002020-21£106,919£107,0002021-22£99,000£99,0002022-23£69,000£69,0002023-24£69,000£68,9652024-25£69,000£69,0002025-26£69,690£69,690£876,453£876,624The work that is undertaken by both organisations will include the promotion of restorative justice services and the benefits that this can provide.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the recent discussions she has had with relevant stakeholders to promote restorative justice.
answer_text: In preparation for the expansion of adult restorative justice measures, there has been considerable engagement between my Department and relevant stakeholders to design the process and promote the use of restorative justice more generally.  A multi-agency Task and Finish Group has been established which includes representatives from our criminal justice partners to inform operational planning and implementation.  The Department’s Restorative Justice Working Group also meets regularly, and comprises membership drawn from criminal justice organisations, and wider voluntary and community sector partners to ensure their views are considered in the work taken forward by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of households affected by the UK Government's two-child benefit cap.
answer_text: At May 2025, there were 13,810 households in Northern Ireland impacted by the two-child policy. Notes:Data Source: Universal Full Credit System (UCFS) at May 2025. Due to retrospection of UCFS data these figures are subject to change.Due to instances of missing data around children declarations and dates of birth please treat these figures as estimates.All figures are rounded to the nearest ten.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the consideration her Department has given to the introduction of the agent of change principle as a planning consideration, similar to that in operation in the rest of UK.
answer_text: Impacts, including for example, noise from pre-existing businesses on nearby development proposals, are material considerations for planning authorities in the development management process, along with the local development plan, regional strategic planning policy, local circumstances and characteristics, and the views of relevant statutory consultees such as environmental health departments. Whilst I am aware of the ‘Agent of Change Principle’ which exists in other jurisdictions, there are no plans to formally introduce the principle in regional strategic planning policy here in the North at this time. However, I will keep the matter under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her department has (i) undertaken any assessment on the impact that AI will have on the employment market in the next ten years and (ii) considered strategies to meet AI challenges and opportunities.
answer_text: In September 2025, Matrix published research on AI and the Future of Work in Northern Ireland - Matrix-AI-and-the-future-of-work-in-NI_Sept25.pdf. This research provides a comprehensive assessment of how AI will transform work in the North through to 2030.   The research suggests that AI’s impact on the workforce will be profound, touching every sector and every community, bringing with it many risks and opportunities. It is estimated that AI could add between £213 million and £7.8 billion in incremental GVA to the North’s economy by 2030 (mid-range estimate: £1.2 billion), whilst 12–23% of employment tasks are expected to be reconfigured, not eliminated, depending on various factors. The Artificial Intelligence Collaboration Centre (AICC) also recently published the first-ever comprehensive baseline of the AI ecosystem in the North, setting the foundation for growth and innovation across the region Artificial Intelligence Capability Census - AICC.  The report finds that the North’s AI-related revenue reached £188 million in 2024, generating £82 million in GVA.  The mid-growth scenario outlined in the report shows that AI-related GVA could reach £200 million by 2028, supporting over 2,000 AI professionals.My Department is taking forward a range of activity to ensure we are well positioned to make the most of the opportunities of AI, aligned with my economic priorities. Through Invest NI, my department is investing £16.3 million in the AICC, led by Ulster University in partnership with Queen’s University Belfast. The Centre is promoting AI awareness and responsible adoption among businesses and provides hands-on support and skills development for SMEs, helping them to harness the potential of AI to improve their processes.Building on the work of the AICC, City Deal-funded projects such as Momentum One Zero in Belfast and the Cognitive Analytics & Digital Robotics Innovation Centre (CADRIC) in Derry are driving digital innovation and AI adoption to the next stage. We are also developing the skills pipeline, working closely with universities, colleges, and businesses to ensure people can gain the skills needed for new and evolving roles. This includes delivery of the Digital Skills Action Plan, the Software and Cyber Sectoral Action Plan, the funding of 390 postgraduate scholarships in AI-related courses through the AICC and a range of skills initiatives such as Skill Up, Assured Skills Academies, and Higher Level Apprenticeships supporting both new entrants and the existing workforce to take advantage of AI-driven opportunities.In light of the publication of two important AI-themed reports in recent months,  I am at the early stages of establishing a working group to take forward the recommendations and to identify how we can collaborate with industry, academia and wider society to manage, and capture fully the opportunities from, the transition to AI technologies. I am working closely with the Executive’s Chief Science and Technology Adviser i

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps taken to mitigate the potential impact of the recent Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute data breach.
answer_text: The Agri- Food and Bioscience Institute’s (AFBI) Chief Executive has advised that AFBI are discharging its obligations in response to the data breach.  The incident has been reported to the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO) and AFBI has established an internal Data Breach Incident Team to investigate the circumstances of the breach and respond to it.AFBI has written to those whose data has been identified as released and has established dedicated call handling facilities to respond to queries from affected parties. The original recipient of the data has also been contacted and asked to delete the information that has been released in error.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of retailers and individual producers affected by the recent Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute data breach.
answer_text: Information relating to up to 290 farmers and producers was inadvertently released in response to a Freedom of Information request. In addition, the breach also includes up to 70 commercial or public entities, which includes retailers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any assessment he has made of the impact of the expansion of Artificial Intelligence and the use of locally-based data centres on climate targets.
answer_text: Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions projections, published as part of the recent consultation on the draft Climate Action Plan, are based on a median energy demand estimate, using the latest figures from the System Operator for Northern Ireland (SONI) which includes assumptions for data centres and other new technology. The impact of the draft Climate Action Plan on future GHG emissions was assessed to be sufficient to meet Northern Ireland’s first carbon budget for the 2023 to 2027 period. Energy policy matters fall under the responsibilities of the Minister for the Economy. The Department for the Economy has advised that it recognises the important role that data centres play in modern digital infrastructure, in facilitating increased digitalisation of the economy and in supporting technology services and related activities such as cloud computing and artificial intelligence.However, it also highlighted that there are key considerations in relation to their energy consumption which must be addressed in moving forwards to a just transition.DfE has indicated it is working with SONI and the Utility Regulator to develop a policy in relation to large electricity users in Northern Ireland, and data centres will be a part of that.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the consideration her Department has been given to a Northern Ireland-wide register of short-term lets.
answer_text: Tourism NI maintains a register of certified accommodation for visitors in line with its statutory functions set out in The Tourism (Northern Ireland) Order 1992. This register holds property information for the eight existing accommodation categories required to be certified by Tourism NI, with data able to be provided by Council area.My Department’s and Tourism NI’s roles in this area are limited to accommodation for visitors. Accommodation let on a short-term basis spans a broad range of provision, e.g. residential tenancies of up to 90 days. Therefore, a proportion of short-term lets are not required to be certified by Tourism NI.Local Councils hold information on properties that have applied for planning permission or change of use to operate as a short-term let. Tourism NI is currently working to put data sharing agreements in place with all 11 Councils. These agreements will assist each party in carrying out its functions and responsibilities. My Department and Tourism NI are also examining options for the future regulation of visitor accommodation across the North. Any changes will require new or revised primary legislation. In the meantime, a public consultation will shortly commence on proposed amendments to the Statutory Regulation of Tourist Accommodation. The proposals are a first step in modernising Tourism NI’s Certification Scheme for visitor accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities pursuant to AQW 27361/22-27 to detail an update on (i) whether the Access and Inclusion Programme will open for applications for 2025-26; and (ii) the budget that will be allocated to it.
answer_text: The capital budget position within my Department continues to be extremely challenging this financial year. The capital budget for 2025/26 has been utilised to ensure that all inescapable capital and competing high priority requirements are met in the first instance. I am therefore unable to confirm at this time if the Access and Inclusion Programme will open for applications this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any guidance issued on diversity network events taking place during working hours.
answer_text: No guidance has issued on diversity network events taking place during work hours. Staff network members’ behaviours and activities, including at events during and outside of work hours, must adhere to the Civil Service Standards of Conduct Policy and Code of Ethics.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether manure which has been granted a licence by the Department of Agriculture, Food and the Marine in the Republic of Ireland is a valid route for manure from Northern Ireland livestock farms where this is subject to annual returns to the NIEA and veterinary approval.
answer_text: Under the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the movement of animal by-products, including manure, to and from Northern Ireland is a matter under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 29290/22-27, for an update on her response.
answer_text: The A5WTC High Court judgment was delivered on 23 June 2025. The court ruled that the Orders including the Vesting Order (VO) would be quashed. The Court Order was filed on 27 June 2025, and ownership of vested lands reverted to the original landowners with effect from that date. Following serving of the notice of appeal officials have now written to all landowners identifying the proposed next steps in relation to the treatment of their lands that have been the subject of vesting. My Department is offering a payment for landowners to enter into a licence agreement to hold the land, in its current state. Landowners will also receive payment for Use and Occupation of their lands between making of orders on 25 November 2024 and 27 June 2025 when ownership of vested lands reverted to them.  Officials are actively engaging with landowners and their agents to arrange on-site meetings to discuss and agree the landowner’s preference in relation to the options available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline for what purposes his Department has used Artificial Intelligence, in the last twelve months.
answer_text: The availability of and interest in Artificial Intelligence has increased in last twelve months, and how it may be used to support departmental business is under consideration. Purposes being explored include to analyse responses to departmental consultations, to review reports for key points, to research policy across jurisdictions and legislative interpretations, to assist the preparation of minutes of meetings, to review and revise departmental policies, procedures and guidelines, to produce presentations, to prepare correspondence templates, to consider its use in training/learning opportunities. The use of AI in my department, is subject to staff oversight and follows the guidance in the NICS AI Policy – Securing Maximising the Benefits of Artificial Intelligence.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline for what purposes their Department has used Artificial Intelligence, in the last twelve months.
answer_text: The Executive Office (TEO) has made some use of Artificial Intelligence in the last twelve months, with an increasing focus since January 2025 on utilising Microsoft Copilot Chat and M365 Copilot to assist with: • document drafting; • summarisation of documents; • information retrieval for internal workflows; and • enhancement of document quality. To date the AI tools within TEO have been used to support staff in enhancing personal productivity and streamlining administrative tasks. The Office of AI and Digital, in collaboration with the Government AI Network, is developing an AI strategy suitable for the public sector. This strategy will provide a robust ethical framework to support departments in adopting AI responsibly and effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline for what purposes his Department has used Artificial Intelligence, in the last twelve months.
answer_text: Over the past twelve months, the Department for the Economy has used Artificial Intelligence through a structured 12-week trial of Microsoft Copilot, involving 56 staff across 31 use cases. The trial focused on practical applications of AI within Microsoft 365—such as document summarisation, drafting, data analysis, and workflow automation—with most participants reporting improved productivity and time saving and recommending wider departmental rollout.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14605/22-27, whether any further consideration has been given to developing a new Community Asset Transfer Delivery Framework, based on the recommendations contained in the Independent Report to advance Community Wealth Building in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I acknowledge the recommendations made by the Independent Panel relating to Community Asset Transfer (CAT). To support an informed Executive consideration of the Panel’s CAT proposals, I have asked officials to commence an evaluation of the impact and effectiveness of CAT. The evaluation will also examine the barriers to effective delivery, the roles and responsibilities of Executive departments in addressing these barriers, and the governance arrangements required to support future implementation. Following the completion of this exercise, I intend to bring forward a paper for Executive consideration with the aim of agreeing the next steps in relation to Community Asset Transfer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline for what purposes his Department has used Artificial Intelligence, in the last twelve months.
answer_text: ANSWERApproved generative Artificial Intelligence use in my Department in the last 12 months has included drafting meeting notes, analysis of IT scripts, the design of promotional materials and general administrative tasks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline for what purposes her Department has used artificial intelligence, in the last twelve months.
answer_text: The Department of Infrastructure has made available Microsoft Copilot Chat, an enterprise level secure AI assistant, to all staff and a more advance separately licensed Microsoft Copilot for 365 AI assistant to around 60 staff within the department. The Department has also commenced several proof of concepts exercises using AI Chatbot technology using departmental data and information to train the AI Chatbots to assist staff in responding to queries or providing advice. Evaluation of these exercises are ongoing. The Department is also working closely with other areas of the NICS to develop NICS wide uses for AI to deliver economies of scale and shared learning in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline for what purposes his Department has used artificial intelligence, in the last twelve months.
answer_text: Over the past year, the Department of Health has actively employed artificial intelligence (AI) to improve both the productivity of the Health and Social Care (HSC) workforce and the quality of care delivered. AI has been applied across a range of areas, including:Diagnostics and Clinical Decision SupportAI-enabled tools have supported the analysis of medical imaging, facilitating earlier and more accurate diagnosis of health conditions. An HSC AI Working Group is currently reviewing available AI solutions and identifying necessary mitigations to support broader deployment. Notably, the NIPACS project already integrates AI tools for medical image analysis in specialties such as trauma and orthopaedics.AI-Driven Smart Search and Evidence RetrievalAI has played a key role in enhancing systematic review processes. By streamlining the development of search strategies, these tools help researchers and analysts locate relevant studies and data sources more efficiently, improving both the speed and quality of evidence gathering.Collaborative Development and Engagement The Department has collaborated with academic institutions, industry partners, and other government bodies to advance AI research and innovation. Key focus areas include personalised medicine, data analytics, and cybersecurity.In response to growing interest across Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) departments, the Department convened an internal AI Working Group on 23 January 2025. This group is exploring how AI might be used to free up capacity currently devoted to routine administrative tasksThese initiatives demonstrate the Department’s commitment to responsibly harnessing AI to transform health and social care services, with a continued emphasis on safety, ethics, and public benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline for what purposes his Department has used artificial intelligence, in the last twelve months.
answer_text: My department has engaged with other NI and UK government departments over the last 12 months to gather information, learn lessons and understand opportunities for Artificial Intelligence (AI) use within DfC. We are advancing a two-stage approach to AI adoption. Stage 1, now complete, delivered a Copilot Chat usage policy aligned with UK Government guidelines. This policy gives DfC staff safe and responsible access to Microsoft Copilot Chat, helping them complete tasks faster by summarising documents, drafting responses, analysing data, and preparing for meetings.Stage 2 aims to establish a comprehensive departmental AI policy. This phase will focus on identifying key use cases and launching proof-of-value pilot projects using Microsoft 365 Copilot.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline for what purposes his Department has used artificial intelligence, in the last twelve months.
answer_text: My Department has used artificial intelligence for the following purposes in the last twelve months:Using Gen AI tools to enhance staff productivity such as drafting emails or meeting notes and summarising reports;AI powered coding assistant for IT developers;Process automation of documentary checks;Environmental monitoring purposes using remote sensing analysis on satellite, orthophotography and LiDAR imagery;Predictive analysis and modelling for facilities development.To ensure responsible use of AI, all potential use cases must have a risk assessment carried out, and approval from the DAERA Senior Information Risk Owner.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline for what purposes her Department has used artificial intelligence, in the last twelve months.
answer_text: As the understanding of AI technology develops, the Department of Justice welcomes the wide range of opportunities available to consider how it might be used. Although not a Department of Justice Programme, The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) is hosted from within my Department.  EPPOC has been exploring the use of AI to enhance efficiency and support strategic delivery.  For example, a Proof of Concept previously demonstrated how AI can automate the extraction of management information from project returns for a management dashboard. Any activity is undertaken in a secure Microsoft 365 environment managed by IT Assist and in line with NICS guidance on use of GenAI.  The Department of Justice recognises that new technology brings new challenges, and it is important we take the time to fully understand these and to make sure that appropriate safeguards are in place. Already the Department has adopted a DoJ policy on the use of AI which follows an internal approval process involving technical experts and the Data Protection Officer as well as oversight from the Department’s Senior Information Risk Owner. This ensures a full assessment of the risk is carried out before any business areas proceeds with a consideration to use AI for a task in the Department. My officials have started to evaluate using Microsoft Co-Pilot to aid with tasks such as background research, summarising complex documents, preparation of document templates and prioritisation of emails.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what level of cleaning is permitted on beaches without the need for a licence.
answer_text: Local councils in Northern Ireland are permitted to use mechanical equipment to clean public beaches under the powers granted by the Litter (Northern Ireland) Order 1994, which allows for the removal of litter and debris.However, under the requirements of the Environment Order (2002), beaches with protected status, such as those designated as Areas of Special Scientific Interest (ASSIs), require Councils to apply to my department for Assent before undertaking activities, including beach cleaning.  This ensures that such activities are conducted in a way that does not negatively impact the designated site selection features.Additionally, under the Marine Licensing (Exempted Activities) Order (Northern Ireland) 2011, the use of vehicles for the removal of litter, seaweed, or dead animals from beaches does not require a marine licence, provided the activity is carried out by or on behalf of a local authority. This exemption is designed to support routine beach maintenance while ensuring that more significant or potentially harmful activities remain subject to regulatory oversight. However, prior notification to the Department is required for the removal of dead animals.Local authorities are encouraged to consult DAERA’s Marine Licensing Team if there is any uncertainty about whether a proposed activity falls within the scope of the exemption or requires formal licensing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of local councils that have permission to use mechanical cleaning equipment on their beaches.
answer_text: All local councils in Northern Ireland have the authority to use mechanical equipment on public beaches for cleaning purposes.  This is outlined under The Litter (Northern Ireland) Order 1994 for the removal of litter and debris. However, under the requirements of the Environment Order (2002), beaches with protected status, such as those designated as Areas of Special Scientific Interest (ASSIs), require Councils to apply to my department for Assent before undertaking activities, including beach cleaning.  This ensures that such activities are conducted in a way that does not negatively impact the designated site selection features.Additionally, under the Marine Licensing (Exempted Activities) Order (Northern Ireland) 2011, the use of vehicles for the removal of litter, seaweed, or dead animals from beaches does not require a marine licence, provided the activity is carried out by or on behalf of a local authority. This exemption is designed to support routine beach maintenance while ensuring that more significant or potentially harmful activities remain subject to regulatory oversight. However, prior notification to the Department is required for the removal of dead animals.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when Ards and North Down Borough Council last applied for a licence to clean the its amenity beaches.
answer_text: My Department has no record of any licence applications, assent applications or exemption notifications from Ards and North Down Borough Council relating to cleaning amenity beaches within the past five years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what steps have been taken to resolve outstanding issues which are preventing full payment of monies to Ards and North Down Borough Council for the lands vested for the new Park and Ride facility in Newtownards.
answer_text: In 2021 my Department vested a large area of land (formerly the old Ards Leisure Centre Willam Street Newtownards) from Ards & North Down Borough Council to construct a Park & Ride facility. Prior to the Vesting Order, in December 2020, a provisional only Valuation was sought by my Department, for budgeting purposes, from the District Valuer, Land & Property Service (LPS) This Valuation does not constitute an agreement on compensation. As is the process in vesting compensation claims, it is incumbent on the Claimant to establish their claim to compensation by production of their title to the land.  Due to the complex nature of the title, it has taken time for Ards and North Down Borough Council to produce this title documentation. All title and statutory declarations have been investigated promptly upon receipt and a formal report on title has recently been furnished to LPS.  It is envisaged that the formal valuation exercise will soon be completed which will facilitate consideration of agreement with Ards & North Down Borough Council and payment of compensation for the land.The amount of interest owed can only be detailed when LPS completes their valuation and the compensation figure is agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the budget allocated for works to connect the Comber to Bangor Greenway through both locations.
answer_text: I’m aware that the Bangor to Newtownards greenway is on programme to complete early next year, and my officials are positively engaged with the Council to progress delivery of the Comber to Newtownards greenway. My Department has provided approximately £6.5m in capital funding to support both projects.  I am committed to working with our council delivery partners to provide the highest quality active travel infrastructure to encourage more people to walk, wheel and cycle. The significant investment from the department is a demonstration of this commitment.Future active travel routes within our towns and cities will be prioritised primarily in line with the emerging Active Travel Delivery Plan. Public consultation on the draft Plan closed on 28 February 2025 and my officials are now considering the responses received with a view to presenting a final plan to me for consideration shortly and I hope to be in a position to publish the final plan by the end of the year.The plan will include network maps for our larger towns and cities, including Bangor, Newtownards and Comber.When complete, the Active Travel Delivery Plan will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan and the Strategic Plan for Greenways to provide a firm basis for prioritising delivery of improved active travel infrastructure for the next ten years and beyond.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 15135/22-27, to provide an update on any steps he is taking regarding long-term substitute teachers' accrual of employment rights such as holiday pay and sickness pay.
answer_text: Principals, Boards of Governors and employing authorities have been advised of the appropriate use of the Northern Ireland Substitute Teachers’ Register (NISTR).NISTR should only be used to provide immediate, unplanned, short-term cover for a period not (normally) in excess of 6 months. In exceptional circumstances, where a short term appointment through NISTR may require extension beyond a 6 month period, schools are required to seek advice and, if necessary, approval before an extension to a contract through NISTR is effected.If it is known from the outset that a teaching vacancy is permanent then the post should be advertised accordingly and the teacher appointed on a permanent basis.Employers have been asked to keep under review the use of supply teachers and if a substitute teacher's appointment goes beyond one year they should be moved from the temporary payroll to the permanent payroll and thereby accrue employment rights the same as a comparable permanent teacher.NISTR is managed by the Education Authority (EA) on behalf of all education sectors and EA is working to provide employers with a list of substitute teachers with long term service to ascertain which teachers can be confirmed as permanent and which posts should be advertised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 16038/22-27 and AQW 16640/22-27, (i) for an update on testing for Maedi Visna; and (ii) whether this will cease from January 2026.
answer_text: Currently, five flocks are subject to restrictions and further testing for Maedi Visna (MV). Since the initial outbreak of MV in November 2021, seven confirmed flocks have had their restrictions lifted following rigorous testing and tracing protocols.All potential future control measures for MV are still under review. Officials are actively consulting with industry representatives and the Scottish Rural College (SRUC) regarding possible approaches to MV control in Northern Ireland. No final recommendations have been made at this time, and consequently, no decisions have been reached.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to alleviate the increasing congestion at the Castlebawn roundabout, Newtownards.
answer_text: Congestion is an issue that affects many of our urban centres, including Castlebawn Roundabout, particularly during the morning and evening peak periods. However, as road safety remains a high priority for my Department, the limited resources available must be focused in areas that have a demonstrable evidence of collisions and where improvements will deliver most road safety benefit. I can advise there are no plans for a road improvement scheme such as the provision of traffic signals at Castlebawn Roundabout at this time.  I have asked my officials to continue to monitor the area and consider any opportunities which may lead to a reduction in traffic congestion in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the 2025-26 budget associated with the screening of Maedi Visna.
answer_text: Approximate costs incurred by the department on Maedi Visna (MV) controls for the first six months of this current financial year 2025-26 are outlined below: £60,830 on laboratory testing costs to the Agri-food and Biosciences Institute.Veterinary Service Animal Health Group staff costs are estimated at £100,000.£965 paid as compensation to affected flock owners, for sheep culled at the department’s direction for disease control purposes.The total costs incurred by the Department to date since the first incursion of MV in November 2021 is estimated at £1,645,000.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that Northern Ireland is free from Maedi Visna.
answer_text: Northern Ireland (NI) has previously claimed Maedi Visna (MV) / Caprine Arthritis Encephalitis (CAE) free status based on the historical disease profile, a limited amount of surveillance and post-import testing. This claim supports access for pedigree breeders to MV-accredited sales and shows in Great Britain (GB).  Although historically rare and mainly linked to imports, MV outbreaks have increased since 2021. Despite ongoing stringent measures by the department and in close collaboration with stakeholders to stamp out these incursions and uphold NI’s national flock status for continued access to GB MV- accredited sales and shows, cases of MV reported in 2024 and 2025 have prompted concerns among certain GB stakeholders, including the Scottish Rural College (SRUC).  The SRUC delivers the flock level MV accreditation scheme that GB flock keepers rely on for access to MV-accredited sales and show. The situation remains under constant review with officials continuing to engage with industry stakeholders, the SRUC and colleagues in the Republic of Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on plans for the O’Neill Memorial Primary School building, Crossnacreevy.
answer_text: Further to previous correspondence, the Education Authority continues to explore whether or not the site and buildings could be redeveloped for future educational use and is currently considering its suitability for development for Specialist Provision accommodation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14519/22-27, for an update on the review of regional strategic planning policy for renewable and low carbon energy.
answer_text: Having recently secured Executive agreement, I intend to publish an update to the Strategic Planning Policy Statement, to include revised policy on the topic of renewable and low carbon energy, in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14601/22-27, for an update on the review of the strategic planning policy on oil and gas development, which commenced in September 2021.
answer_text: The answer to AQW 14601/22-27 advised you that the Department was awaiting the outcome of the Department for Economy’s (DfE’s) separate review of its petroleum (and minerals) licensing policies.The DfE’s preferred policy option is to introduce a moratorium and eventual legislative ban on the exploration and production of all forms of onshore oil and gas (petroleum), a position which was agreed by the Executive at its meeting on 5 December 2024. I understand that a moratorium on the acceptance and processing of petroleum licence applications is therefore now in place. As the legislation to enact a full ban is brought forward, the Department will take account of the implications of it for the review of regional strategic planning policy on oil and gas development.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on his Department’s consideration of the 26 recommendations contained in the Report of the Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing System in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Work on my Department’s response to the Report of the Independent Review of the Liquor Licensing System in Northern Ireland is progressing. The response will be published in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education pursuant to AQW 15135/22-27, to provide an update on long-term substitute teachers' accrual of employment rights, such as holiday pay and sickness pay.
answer_text: Principals, Boards of Governors and employing authorities have been advised of the appropriate use of the Northern Ireland Substitute Teachers’ Register (NISTR).NISTR should only be used to provide immediate, unplanned, short-term cover for a period not (normally) in excess of 6 months. In exceptional circumstances, where a short term appointment through NISTR may require extension beyond a 6 month period, schools are required to seek advice and, if necessary, approval before an extension to a contract through NISTR is effected.If it is known from the outset that a teaching vacancy is permanent then the post should be advertised accordingly and the teacher appointed on a permanent basis.Employers have been asked to keep under review the use of supply teachers and if a substitute teacher's appointment goes beyond one year they should be moved from the temporary payroll to the permanent payroll and thereby accrue employment rights the same as a comparable permanent teacher.NISTR is managed by the Education Authority (EA) on behalf of all education sectors and EA is working to provide employers with a list of substitute teachers with long term service to ascertain which teachers can be confirmed as permanent and which posts should be advertised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the policy with regards to wound treatment by GPs following privately funded operations where the GP has referred the patient for the privately funded operation.
answer_text: In instances when GPs write referral letters to support a patient’s decision to pay for private treatment, they are doing this as a courtesy and in the interests of good clinical care to ensure that the private consultant is fully aware of the clinical history.  It does not mean that the GP is then accepting a transfer of responsibility for immediate follow up care, for example wound treatment including suture removal, and it is still the patient’s decision to procure private care as they are the payer. Individuals choosing to procure healthcare privately are effectively stepping outside of the health and social care (HSC) system.  In these circumstances, there is no obligation on GPs to provide pre and/or post treatment support.  Therefore, it remains important for individuals wishing to avail of private healthcare to ensure that all pre and post treatment support costs are included in the overall package of care procured from the private healthcare provider.  In instances where any ongoing treatment and medication is recommended, but is not provided, by the private provider as part of the private service then individuals should be prepared to meet any associated costs.   Where a GP is made aware of an individual’s plans to procure private healthcare in a private capacity, it is recommended that: the individual is encouraged to ensure all pre and post treatment costs are included in the overall package.the individual is informed that they may have to pay for any ongoing treatment or medications which are recommended.GPs remain responsible for clinical decisions regarding the care of individual patients and as such they should exercise their clinical judgement prior to providing any pre and/or post treatment support for individuals availing of private healthcare.  Whilst there is no obligation on GPs to provide such support, in instances where a GP considers that the support being requested is clinically appropriate, falls within their area of competence, does not breach the terms of their GMS contract or indemnity cover, and is in keeping with the General Medical Council (GMC) standards on prescribing and Good Medical Practice then they may choose to provide this support or continue to provide this support.In line with normal practice, individuals procuring healthcare privately will continue to be able to access emergency care where necessary.In circumstances where a patient is receiving care in the Independent Sector as an HSC patient, pre and post treatment care will be provided by the HSC as normal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of requests for non-emergency patient transport which would have otherwise been eligible were unable to be fulfilled by the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) does not maintain a dataset capturing unfulfilled eligible requests, including those never formally registered or those potentially met through alternative arrangements by Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts. As such, no comprehensive data exists to quantify this unmet demand.Table 1 shows the number of requests that have been turned down by NIAS for non-emergency patient transport, in each of the last five years, broken down by HSC Trusts. Table 1YearTrustTotalBelfastNorthernSouth EasternSouthernWestern2021 7849172836176093,2102022 2,8982,6421,1781,4111,5269,6552023 3,6203,8811,5732,4542,18213,7102024 2,6143,3511,5112,1591,32710,9622025*1,0129894644795343,478Total10,92811,7805,0097,1206,17841,015* 2025 is a partial data returnTable 2 shows the number of non-emergency patient transport bookings provided by NIAS from GPs (bookings are recorded by clinic names) in each of the last five years.Table 2YearCount of Bookings20216,850202219,804202329,373202430,7252025*21,987Total108,739* 2025 is an incomplete year I understand that several patients have had to cancel multiple appointments due to the unavailability of ambulance transport. This is deeply regrettable and I have asked officials within my department and NIAS to review the situation urgently.This issue has arisen due to the recent withdrawal of non-contractual activities by GPs as part of a collective action aimed at highlighting pressures on primary care and demands for increased funding.  This includes booking non-emergency ambulance transport for first outpatient appointments. While regional guidance places this responsibility with a medical practitioner, it may be delegated to an appropriate healthcare professional. Historically, GPs have fulfilled this role, but as part of their current collective action, they have stepped back from this function.In response, NIAS has developed draft guidance to enable nursing home staff to book transport directly, on the condition that the individual has sufficient clinical knowledge of the patient’s needs. This guidance is currently under review and will be issued to nursing homes once agreed.In the interim, NIAS has advised that care homes should contact the relevant hospital clinic or department to request that they initiate the booking process. While this approach has had mixed success, it remains the recommended route until the new guidance is in place.I acknowledge that this does not yet resolve the issue for patients living independently in the community, and further work is underway to ensure that NIAS receives the necessary clinical information to arrange transport safely and appropriately.I trust this response provides assurance that my department is actively working to resolve these challenges and improve access to care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the policy regarding the request and provision of ambulance transport for non-emergency patients by the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) provides ambulance transport for patients whose clinical needs mean they cannot reasonably make their own way to or from hospital or other health and social care facilities. The framework for this provision is set out my Department’s Transport Strategy for Health and Social Care Services in Northern Ireland.Non-emergency ambulance transport is provided on the basis of medical need, assessed against eligibility criteria which take account of the patient’s mobility, clinical condition and a potential necessity for professional assistance during the journey. Where a patient is assessed as eligible, transport is arranged by NIAS through co-ordination with Health and Social Care Trusts. Patients who do not meet the criteria are expected to make their own way to their appointments, although they may be able to make use of other transport schemes funded by my Department and other Departments, including those on low incomes, who may may be eligible to reclaim hospital transport costs through the Hospital Travel Costs scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 25671/22-27, to detail what plans are being pursued to address the ongoing problems with parking in Conway Square, Newtownards.
answer_text: I am advised that my officials are continuing to engage with their counterparts in Ards and North Down Borough Council to discuss ownership, boundary rectification and maintenance responsibilities relating to Conway Square, Newtownards.When these issues are agreed my officials can consider appropriate measures to address any problems with parking on land owned by my Department in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Ards and North Down Borough Council has made any requests for an abandonment order for Conway Square, Newtownards, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: I can confirm that Ards and North Down Borough Council have not requested an abandonment order for Conway Square, Newtownards in the past twelve months.My officials continue to engage with their counterparts in the Council to discuss ownership, boundary rectification and maintenance responsibilities relating to this location.
