question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will ensure that her Department will take forward the work previously started by the Newtownards Town Centre Sustainable Transport group, given that it last met in June 2022.
answer_text: The Newtownards Town Centre Sustainable Transport group last met in June 2022.  The objective of the Group was to take forward projects to contribute to the improvement of transport and active travel in and around Newtownards. I can confirm that my Department and the Council, are working collaboratively to deliver improvements for Newtownards.My Department has developed a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan which includes a draft network map of the proposals for Newtownards, and approval has been granted to deliver a 450 space park and ride facility in Newtownards. The Council is also progressing work on Greenway networks which connect to the town.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 15803/22-27, for the current update on (i) regulations for graduated driver licences; and (ii) a timeline for implementation.
answer_text: Work is ongoing on the operational planning and subordinate legislation changes that are required before GDL can be implemented. That preparatory work is advancing well, and I expect to be able to announce the anticipated date that GDL will be introduced shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of the courses provided by Queen’s University Belfast, where her Department contributes towards maintaining the tuition fee cap.
answer_text: All full-time undergraduate courses provided by any publicly funded university here, such as Queen’s University Belfast, are subject to the tuition fee cap for home students.The Department does not maintain a list of courses at Queen’s subject to the cap.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the courses provided by Ulster University, where her Department contributes towards maintaining the tuition fee cap.
answer_text: All full-time undergraduate courses provided by any publicly funded university here, such as Ulster University, are subject to the tuition fee cap for home students.The Department does not maintain a list of courses at Ulster University subject to the cap.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding provided to projects in the Strangford constituency from the Mental Health Support Fund, in each year since the beginning of the Fund.
answer_text: The Mental Health Support Fund (MHSF) was established in April 2021, for a period of three years, using one-off, non-recurrent funding to help equip and enable our local charity sector to continue to provide vital mental health services in our communities. Some projects have received an extension beyond March 2024 to allow completion, however this is using their existing funding and not additional monies.Four projects within the Strangford constituency received funding from the Mental Health Support Fund, as outlined in the table below:Project Funding provided in 2021/22 Funding provided in 2022/23 Funding provided in 2023/24 Funding provided in 2024/25 Funding provided in 2025/26 Action Mental Health, Newtownards £123,727.75 £106,052.34 £216,104.68 £57,526.23 Beyond the Battlefield, Newtownards £25,000 £10,714.28 £53,571.40 £19,214.32 Home Start Down District, Downpatrick   £24,000   £9,000  NI Rare Disease Partnership, Newtownards     £16,000 £4,000 with further £4,000 to be released pending spend verification One charity, the NI Rare Disease Partnership is due to receive another £4,000 of the grant. Following receipt of their claim and once verified, it will be released by Community Foundation NI. All other charities’ projects have been completed and no further monies have been provided under the Fund. Due to the ongoing financial challenges which the Department continues to face, there is no budget to extend or replace this Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how the requirement to allocate a minimum of 10 per cent of overall transport budgets spend on active travel, under Section 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, would achieve climate targets, rather than investment in public transport.
answer_text: My Department has adopted a three-pronged approach to reducing the carbon impact of transport: switching fuels, shifting modes and reducing journeys.  The commitment to set a minimum spend on active travel from the overall transport budgets of 10 per cent and the associated increased uptake of active travel will support the shifting modes policy.Active travel is a virtually carbon free, low-cost mode of transport.  Research shows that over one third of journeys are less than two miles long and two thirds are less than 5 miles long offering a tremendous opportunity for active travel to become a practical and attractive option for many people.  Public transport is also an essential element of a sustainable transport system for longer journeys and for those unable to walk, wheel or cycle and continued investment in both is required to deliver on climate change targets.  By prioritising walking, wheeling, and cycling for shorter journeys, and combining these modes with public transport for longer trips, we can cut greenhouse gas emissions and improve air quality. Less reliance on private car travel can have significant impact on reducing congestion within our urban areas - supporting clean, green, healthy and vibrant city and town centres.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount the 10 per cent of overall transport budgets, as referred to in Section 22 of the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, equates to in the 2025-26 budget.
answer_text: Work is ongoing on calculating the transport element of the overall 2025-26 budget, however the average over the last three financial years was in the region of £0.9 billion. The member will appreciate that transport budgets may change during the year and officials plan to report on the overall position and percentage spend on active travel at the end of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the current limit for student places at Northern Ireland universities; and (ii) the rationale for maintaining that limit at its current level.
answer_text: The MaSN for 2025/26 has not yet been finalised however it is expected to be 26,002. This is up from 25,584 in 2024/25.Due to the significant level of public funding provided to the universities, the Department has to control spending to ensure it stays within its budget allocation. The Maximum Student Number (MaSN) limits the Department’s financial exposure and ensures that funding per place is maintained at a level appropriate to sustain quality. The MaSN, therefore, plays a crucial role in balancing the growth of educational institutions with the available public funding.However, recognizing the need for educational institutions to grow and thrive, my Department maintains a dialogue with all of the higher education institutions on the full range of issues affecting their sustainability and growth. My Department has increased the number of funded undergraduate student places at local universities in 2024/25 and again in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline (i) when a sectoral transport plan will be published, as required under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022; and (ii) whether any measures to allocate a minimum of 10 per cent of the overall transport budget are being implemented,  in advance of the publication of the sectoral transport plan.
answer_text: The Transport Sectoral Plan, required under the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 (the Act), is currently under development. No date has been set for its publication. However, significant work has already being progressed to decarbonise the transport sector in line with my Department's responsibilities under the Act. In respect of active travel, my Department is working on the basis of ramping up overall investment in active travel to meet the requirement of Section 22 of the Act by 2030. Since the introduction of the Act, my Department has been developing plans to support increased investment in the active travel necessary to meet its requirements. This work has included the development of a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan.  Officials are currently reviewing responses to the public consultation on the draft Plan and are aiming to present a final plan to me for consideration in the autumn, with a view to publishing the final plan by the end of this year.  When complete, this plan will complement the Belfast Cycling Network Delivery Plan and Strategic Plan for Greenways to provide a firm basis for the prioritisation and delivery of improved active travel infrastructure for the next ten years and beyond.My Department has also taken a number of important steps to increase capacity to support the delivery of active travel projects, including filling key posts within the Active Travel team and increasing supply chain capacity to facilitate an increased delivery pipeline. I have also increased funding allocation for dedicated active travel in the current financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the amount spent by her Department on maintaining the tuition fee cap in Northern Ireland universities, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Local Higher Education Institutions receive annual funding in the form of block grant from my department and tuition fees from students.Tuition fees are raised every year in line with projected inflation.My department provides block grant funding to support core teaching and research activity within local universities.  The level of block grant funding, which includes targeted allocations, provided over the last five years is shown below.Academic YearQueen’s University BelfastUlster UniversityOpen University2020/21£115.9m£87.0m£7.0m2021/22£106.5m£83.1m£7.1m2022/23£109.9m£86.3m£7.2m2023/24£109.4m£86.5m£7.2m2024/25£107.2m£88.3m£7.2m

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any restrictions in place that prevents local councils from cleaning amenity beaches.
answer_text: Local councils are empowered to remove litter from amenity beaches under their control through the Litter (Northern Ireland) Act 1994.		For beaches not under control of councils the responsibility to clean these beaches is with the owner operator or another recognised statutory body, such as my department in the case of Crawfordsburn.	Under the requirements of the Environment Order (2002), beaches with protected status, such as those designated as Areas of Special Scientific Interest (ASSIs), require Councils to apply to my department for Assent before undertaking activities, including beach cleaning.  This ensures that such activities are conducted in a way that does not negatively impact the designated site selection features.The removal of litter, seaweed, or dead animals from a beach using a vehicle does not require a marine licence, provided it is carried out by or on behalf of a local council. This is an exempted activity under Article 21 of the Marine and Coastal Access Act 2009. However, prior notice to the department is required for the removal of dead animals.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of former employees that did not return work devices such as (a) mobiles, (b) laptops; or (c) tablets after leaving the employment of the Department and arm’s length bodies, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the value of the unreturned items.
answer_text: ANSWERMy Department provides a range of shared services, one of which is IT Assist who facilitate the provision of IT equipment to all departments. It is the responsibility of each department to manage their assets and line managers are responsible for arranging the return of devices to IT Assist as outlined in both the NICS Mobile Device Security policy and the NICS Employee Leaving Checklist V11.0. Five laptops have been reported lost or stolen in the Department of Finance over the past five years. This equates to £3,717 based on the original purchase price, but the laptops will have reduced in cost due to their age. Business areas within the Department manage mobile devices (Mobile Phones & Tablets) separately and this information is not centrally collated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of former employees that did not return work devices such as (a) mobiles, (b) laptops; or (c) tablets after leaving the employment of the Department and arm’s length bodies, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the value of the unreturned items.
answer_text: The provision of IT equipment and mobile devices to Departmental staff is provided through a shared service with IT Assist.  Information on staff leaving the service comes from HR Connect.My department has measures in place with information from HR Connect on staff leaving the service.Appropriate action is taken to retrieve their IT equipment, if the staff member has not already done so.  During the last 5 years my department has not recorded any instances of work devices not being returned when former employees left the department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of former employees that did not return work devices such as (a) mobiles, (b) laptops; or (c) tablets, after leaving the employment of the Department and arm’s length bodies, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the value of the unreturned items.
answer_text: My Department and the ALB’s have procedures in place to ensure the return of devices from all employees who leave employment. Occasionally, there are exceptional circumstances where an employee has not returned their devices; this has happened only three times in the past five years. Security arrangements mean that any device not seen on the network for over three months will have its permissions removed.FY20/21 - NILFY21/22 - NILFY22/23 – one employeeFY23/24 - NILFY24/25 – two employees(ii)The estimated value of the devices not returned is:FY20/21 - NILFY21/22 - NILFY22/23 – £430FY23/24 - NILFY24/25 – £630

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of former employees that did not return work devices such as (a) mobiles, (b) laptops; or (c) tablets, after leaving the employment of the Department and arm’s length bodies, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the value of the unreturned items.
answer_text: DAERA complies with the NICS Mobile Device Security policy and has robust procedures in place that require all work devices from leavers to be returned to the Department of Finance’s ICT Shared Service Organisation which manages ICT assets for DAERA staff. Line managers are responsible for ensuring the return of mobile and tablet devices to IT Assist and for providing assurance that this has been done. While DoF has no specific record of devices not being returned by former employees of DAERA, the message about the importance of ensuring compliance with requirements continues to be stressed to staff and line managers and the Leavers’ Checklist includes a specific provision regarding the return of IT equipment that managers must sign off as complete.All DAERA ALBs have confirmed they do not have any record of unreturned devices by former employees.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the policy on the return of work devices in the event of an employee leaving (a) the Civil Service; or (b) an arm’s-length body; and (ii) the steps taken when these devices are not returned.
answer_text: ANSWERMy Department provides a range of shared services, one of which is IT Assist which facilitate the provision of IT equipment to all departments.It is the responsibility of each department to manage their assets and line managers are responsible for arranging the return of devices as outlined in both the NICS Mobile Device Security policy and the NICS Employee Leaving Checklist V11.0. All devices must be returned to IT Assist after use for reallocation or secure disposal. Annually IT Assist provides departments with a list of assets for verification. If a device is lost or stolen, the asset list is updated accordingly.These policies also apply to those arm’s length bodies availing of the IT Assist service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of former employees that did not return work devices such as (a) mobiles, (b) laptops; or (c) tablets after leaving the employment of the Department and arm’s length bodies, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the value of the unreturned items.
answer_text: The number of former employees that did not return work devices such as (a) mobiles, (b) laptops; or (c) tablets after leaving the employment of the Department and Arm’s Length Bodies, in each of the last five years; along with the value of the unreturned items is as follows:Number of former employees	that did not return work devices:	6Value of unreturned items:		£2,013

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of former employees that did not return work devices such as (a) mobiles, (b) laptops; or (c) tablets after leaving the employment of the Department and arm’s length bodies, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the value of the unreturned items.
answer_text: During the last 5 years The Executive Office has not recorded any instances of work devices not being returned when former employees left the department. ALBs have reported the non-return of two laptops, with an overall remaining value of £999.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of former employees that did not return work devices such as (a) mobiles, (b) laptops; or (c) tablets after leaving the employment of the Department and arm’s length bodies, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the value of the unreturned items.
answer_text: My Department and its arm’s length bodies have no recorded instances of work devices (including mobiles, laptops, or tablets) not being returned by former employees in any of the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of former employees that did not return work devices such as (a) mobiles, (b) laptops; or (c) tablets after leaving the employment of the Department and arm’s length bodies, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the value of the unreturned items.
answer_text: In common with other Civil Service core departments, the Department for Infrastructure takes IT services from the Department of Finance managed IT Shared Service Organisation. As part of this service, DoF manage the IT assets for my department and records the full life cycle of the device from deployment to secure disposal. For members of staff leaving the service, the NICS HR Employee Leavers Checklist is completed, section 4 of which, instructs line managers to contact the DoF IT Shared Service Organisation to arrange return of all IT equipment. Devices that are not returned are to be formally notified to DoF as either lost or stolen. This information is recorded in the central DoF register. DfI does not hold this information separately and relies on the information held by DoF. DoF has no record of any devices being recorded as lost or stolen by former employees of DfI over the last five years. Translink and NI Water, which fall under the governance of DfI manage their own IT Service provision and have also reported no cases where devices have not been returned.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of former employees that did not return work devices such as (a) mobiles, (b) laptops; or (c) tablets after leaving the employment of the Department and arm’s length bodies, in each of the last five years; and (ii) the value of the unreturned items.
answer_text: My Department has no recorded instances of work devices such as (a) mobiles, (b) laptops; or (c) tablets not being returned by former employees in any of the last five years.Information regarding this Department’s Arm’s Length Bodies can only be provided at disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the planned maintenance by DfI Roads in the Strangford constituency, in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: The Department does not collate or manage information on a constituency basis, rather this is done on a Council area basis. The Strangford Constituency forms part of the Ards and North Down Borough Council; Newry, Mourne and Down District Council; and Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council areas.The 2025-26 work programmes are currently being finalised and will be included in my Department’s Annual Roads Reports to Councils. Following the presentations to Councils, reports will be published on the Department’s website, issued to MLAs and MPs in each respective Council area, and placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the herd level incidence of bovine viral diarrhea in the last 12 months; and (ii) for an update on the eradication programme.
answer_text: Bovine Viral Diarrhoea (BVD) herd incidence in the 12 months to the end of August 2025 was 2.36%, a slight drop from 2.41% in the 12 months to the end of August 2024. The BVD eradication programme entered a more stringent phase at the start of February 2025, when legislation came into force enabling my department to apply herd restrictions in specific circumstances to control BVD. Restrictions will be applied on a progressive basis up to June 2026 and will be monitored closely to evaluate the effectiveness and determine next steps.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 27685/22-27, whether there has been any progress on the disposal of the old Castle Gardens Primary School site.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised it is continuing to work actively to progress the disposal of the former site of Castle Gardens Primary School.Solicitors are undertaking legal due diligence to resolve complex title issues and ensure the property has a marketable title.In parallel, EA officers are liaising with Land and Property Services to establish the updated value of the site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 16400/22-27, (i) to detail the number of times the Coastal Forum has met since he took office; and (ii) to detail all planned meetings of the Forum.
answer_text: The Coastal Forum Working Group is meeting on 13th October 2025, and the inaugural Coastal Forum since restoration of devolved government on 3rd February 2024 is meeting on 19th November 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on an additional roll-out of the part-time 20mph speed limit scheme outside schools, within the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North and are listed on my Department’s website, link; 20mph speed limit at schools - Tranche 3 | Department for Infrastructure.  This third tranche focuses on rural schools that are located on roads where the national speed limit applies while considering the safety implications of introducing lower speed limits.  This will bring the total delivered across the North since 2020/21 to 273 schools.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.My Department is also currently carrying out a review on a number of speed related matters including 20mph speed limits in general and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review.  Further consideration on the use of part time speed limits at schools will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15728/22-27, for an update on the creation of a statutory duty for public bodies to plan for coastal change.
answer_text: As detailed in my response in September 2024, the creation of new statutory duties for public authorities is for the Executive to consider. I can update that the recommendations from the Climate Change Committee for the Executive to bring forward legislation on managing coastal change and to designate departmental responsibility were incorporated into the public consultation undertaken by my Department on the Third Northern Ireland Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP3). The public consultation closed on 4th August and the responses are being reviewed by the relevant Departments. The Coastal Forum will consider a potential timetable for the development of policy options and introducing legislation to manage coastal change when it meets on 19th November 2025. This will inform the Executive’s consideration of the matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any consideration is being given to the installation of part-time traffic lights to manage peak congestion at the Castlebawn roundabout in Newtownards.
answer_text: Congestion is an issue that affects many of our urban centres, including Castlebawn Roundabout, particularly during the morning and evening peak periods. However, as road safety remains a high priority for my Department, the limited resources available to make our roads safer, must be focused in areas that have a demonstrable evidence of collisions and where improvements will deliver most road safety benefit. I can advise there are no plans for a road improvement scheme such as the provision of traffic signals at this time.  I have asked my officials to continue to monitor the area and consider any opportunities to assist in reducing the traffic congestion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 16400/22-27, to detail the work of the Coastal Forum.
answer_text: A Coastal Forum work programme, developed by the Coastal Forum and the associated working group, was agreed by DAERA and DfI Ministers in 2021.  The work programme details the actions required by the Forum to advance sustainable coastal management in Northern Ireland.There are 9 objectives of the work programme detailed as follows:Objective 1 – Progress coastal management issues through the Coastal Forum;Objective 2 - To improve coastal management baseline;Objective 3 – Establish a coastal management co-ordinated monitoring programme;Objective 4 – To develop a position paper to facilitate coastal planning decisions;Objective 5 – Identify funding to progress coastal management objectives/projects;Objective 6 – Infrastructure inspection and maintenance;Objective 7 – Progress coastal management pilot projects;Objective 8 – Develop coastal management policy options; andObjective 9 – Education/awareness on coastal management.The Coastal Forum Working Group is preparing a progress report which will be reviewed when the Coastal Forum meets on 19 November 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has carried out, regarding the cost of moving or creating an additional train halt, serving George Best Belfast City Airport.
answer_text: Translink submitted the Belfast City Airport Connectivity report earlier this month. This is currently under consideration in line with the established procedures for all capital investment projects. The next step will be for Translink to develop a Strategic Outline Case setting out the rationale for any future investment in this proposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15646/22-27, to provide a timeline for the finalisation and implementation of the Clean Air Strategy.
answer_text: Work is ongoing in the development of this Strategy with considerable cross-departmental engagement to finalise actions having taken place throughout the summer. I intend to publish this Strategy for consultation by the end of 2025. This may again be impacted by competing resource pressures in relation to other key departmental priorities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the Farming for the Generations pilot scheme, including the number of farm businesses who have applied to participate on the Planning for Succession element of the scheme.
answer_text: The Farming for the Generations pilot scheme is currently being delivered by Rural Support and is due to conclude in March 2026.  There are 69 farm businesses participating on the Planning for Succession element of the scheme. These businesses have nominated 55 successors to participate in the Preparing the Successor workshops.  Twelve people have completed the Recently Qualified training programme with recruitment for an additional tranche currently underway. Participants of both the Preparing the Successor and Recently Qualified programmes will have the opportunity to participate in a study tour to Great Britain in November 2025 as part of their ongoing personal development.  My Department will also provide funding for a further five people from this cohort to participate in the Study Abroad element of the scheme. The application process for this is now open and completed applications must be received by 27 October 2025. Travel will take place between 1 December 2025 and 28 February 2026. It is encouraging to see the older generation also engage with the scheme and 81 people have participated in Leading Succession.Seven applications and 26 expressions of interest have been received to Farming in Partnership.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any powers the National Association of Councillors Northern Ireland Region has to call a strike or other form of industrial action by councillors.

To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a breakdown of (i) applications made; and (ii) applications approved, under the Affordable Warmth Scheme, for the past two years, broken down by Housing Executive district.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 30984/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the budget allocated to the Affordable Warmth Scheme for this financial year; and (ii) how does this budget compare to the last two years.
answer_text: I allocated a budget of £7.5m to the Affordable Warmth Scheme this financial year. In 2024/25 I allocated an opening budget for the Affordable Warmth Scheme of £7.175m, which increased to a closing budget of £7.944m. In 2023/24, there was a budget of £14m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding allocated to support community pharmacies in the Strangford constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In general, my Department commissions community pharmacy services on a Northern Ireland-wide basis and contracts those services with individual community pharmacy contractors under the general pharmaceutical services (GPS).  In order to comply with information governance advice, my Department cannot release information on the payments made to individual contractors. Information on the total payments made to community pharmacy contractors in Northern Ireland in each of the 5 years between and 2019/20 and  2023/24 is shown in the table below.  Data for 2024/25 is not included as figures are still to be finalised.Payments made for Community Pharmacy Services in Northern Ireland by Financial YearHSCNI Payments for Community Pharmacy Services (£ millions)*2019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24£119£152£152£144£163* All figures rounded to the nearest £100,000,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of new approvals expected to be granted under the Affordable Warmth Scheme, given the budget allocation this year; and (ii) how does this compare to the number of approvals in each of the last two years.
answer_text: The issues at (i) and (ii) are operational matters for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“£7.5m in funding was allocated (to the Affordable Warmth Scheme for 2025/26).  As there was circa £5m of outstanding financial commitments from approvals issued in the previous financial year, £2.5m of the current year budget allocation is available to meet expenditure for new approvals. It is estimated that this will fully fund circa 570 new AW approvals in 2025/26.”“Financial YearBudget (£m) *Approvals (No.)2023/24£14.0m2,675 **2024/25£7.9m2,118 ***2025/26£7.5m570 *****Closing budget position is provided for 2023/24 and 2024/25. The current budget allocation is reflected for 2025/26.** Includes approvals related to building a carried forward financial commitment into the next financial year (circa £3.5m), which was subsequently funded from 2024/25 budget allocation.*** Includes approvals related to building a carried forward financial commitment into the next financial year (circa £5m), which was subsequently funded from 2025/26 budget allocation. **** This may further increase.”I will continue to press for additional funding for this important scheme through the monitoring round process. The figure for 2025/26 approvals may therefore increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of sites deemed suitable for wind turbines on the Forest Service estate.
answer_text: The Forest Service Wind Energy Market Engagement exercise closed in March 2025. The exercise sought to gather information from the wind energy sector, on the forest areas where there is a market interest for their development for renewable electricity generation.  The forests identified by the sector through the market engagement returns are in the process of consideration by the Department in line with its statutory forestry obligations, following which their overall suitability will be determined via a Business Case.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of passengers that used the Strangford ferry service, each week, over the last year.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what barriers there are to renewable energy development on the Forest Service estate.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, places a requirement on Department for Economy to ensure that at least 80% of electricity consumption is from renewable sources by 2030. I recognise the potential for the use of the Department’s forests to contribute to this requirement and Forest Service is already engaged with the wind energy sector in taking this forward. Considerable interest in developing wind farms on the forest estate was demonstrated through a recent market engagement exercise.The Forestry Act (Northern Ireland) 2010 provides the powers for the Department to use or develop the forest estate for purposes other than forestry provided it has due regard to its general duty of promoting afforestation and sustainable forestry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of funding he requested from the Minister of Finance for the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture.
answer_text: The Executive agreed to allocate £12.3m Capital DEL for the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture in the Final Budget exercise for 2025-26.My Department has recently submitted Capital DEL bids of £47.8m / £77.3m / £106.0m / £121.6m over the four years from 2026-27 to 2029-30 in relation to the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture as part of the ongoing multi-year Budget exercise commissioned by the Department of Finance following the outcome of the 2025 Spending Review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the compensation scheme available for passengers when their train has been delayed.
answer_text: There are two compensation schemes available for rail passengers when their train is delayed. Details of the compensation scheme available for delays on Enterprise services can be accessed at: https://www.irishrail.ie/IrishRail/media/Imported/enterprise_charter.pdf. Details of the compensation scheme available for delays on all other rail services can be accessed at: https://www.translink.co.uk/delay-repay-scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether consideration is being given to a transition pathway to future environmental farming support for those farms involved in the Environmental Farming Scheme who are currently excluded from the Farming with Nature programme.
answer_text: I am encouraged by the uptake within the new Farming with Nature Transition Scheme providing support for farmers to carry out environmental actions that create habitats and new green infrastructure and protect water quality.		My officials are working at pace to develop further phases of the Farming with Nature (FwN) Package to realise my ambition to scale up nature friendly farming across Northern Ireland to protect and restore our natural environment.	In terms of replacement for EFS Higher, other elements of the FwN Package are being progressed, such as focused support for Designated Areas and Priority Habitats. These are currently being developed with the aim of opening for application in 2026.  Prioritisation of those with EFS Higher Agreements due to expire at the end of this year is being considered.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail why (i) Agrisearch; and (ii) the Northern Ireland Food and Drink Association, have not been included within the membership of the Nutrients Action Plan stakeholder forum.
answer_text: Following an introductory meeting of the NAP Stakeholder Task and Finish Group, I reviewed its membership. I have extended this to include the Northern Ireland Food and Drink Association and the Rivers Trust. However, I understand that the group may need to draw on additional expertise and for that reason, I have asked my Chief Scientific Advisor’s Office to establish a Science Sub-group which will include representation from AFBI, AgriSearch and other relevant organisations. This Sub-group will assist the work of the Task and Finish Group ensuring that the final NAP proposals are scientifically robust and evidence-based.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of occupational health house inspections that took place in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust held Occupational Therapy (OT) information systems do not hold details that capture the number of people referred specifically for housing assessments. The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trusts Occupational Therapy information systems do not hold details that capture the number of people referred specifically for housing assessments and are unable to detail average wait times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the development of a Farming with Nature Higher Level Scheme.
answer_text: As Minister I have been clear that roll out of the new Farming with Nature Scheme (FwN) must be a priority within the Sustainable Agriculture Programme, supporting the scaling up of nature friendly farming and delivering environmental public goods, alongside food production. It is my ambition that agri-environment support should become an enterprise on farms, as a key source of income for farmers.My officials are working at pace to develop further phases of the FwN Package to realise my ambition to scale up nature friendly farming across Northern Ireland to protect and restore our natural environment. I am encouraged by the uptake within the new Farming with Nature Transition Scheme providing support for farmers to carry out environmental actions that create habitats and new green infrastructure and protect water quality.In terms of replacement for EFS Higher, other elements of the FwN Package are being progressed, such as focused support for Designated Areas and Priority Habitats. These are currently being developed with the aim of opening for application in 2026.  Prioritisation of those with EFS Agreements due to expire at the end of this year is being considered.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for house inspections by occupational health in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust held Occupational Therapy (OT) information systems do not hold details that capture the number of people referred specifically for housing assessments. The South Eastern Health and Social Care Trusts Occupational Therapy information systems do not hold details that capture the number of people referred specifically for housing assessments and are unable to detail average wait times.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of sailings of the Strangford Lough ferry cancelled, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Strangford Lough Ferry Service aims to provide more than 97% of the 22,000 scheduled sailings per year. The table below shows the number of cancellations in each of the last five years separated into two categories: those that are within Departments control such as maintenance and those outside the Departments control such as adverse weather, a pandemic or industrial action.Financial YearWithin DfI controlOutside DfI control2020-21762212021-22611352022-2338302023-241606052024-2518233

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 27686/22-27, whether she has explored any sources of funding for a feasibility study for a permanent bridge between Portaferry and Strangford.
answer_text: As explained in my previous response to AQW 27686/22-27, my Department’s transport plans are the key driver for continued investment in our road network. The Eastern Transport Plan covers the local transport movements in Ards and North Down, the outworking of which will determine my Department’s priorities with regards to any new road infrastructure, including any permanent linkage between Strangford and Portaferry. Information relating to the development of the Plan can be viewed via my Department’s website at; Eastern Transport Plan (ETP) 2035 | Department for InfrastructureConsideration of the need for sources of funding to take forward a feasibility study for a permanent bridge between Portaferry and Strangford will be informed by the outcome of the Eastern Transport Plan.The Strangford Lough Ferry Service operates every day of the year except Christmas day, from early morning to late - night and serves both the local community and local tourism. It provides a reliable service and is currently the most economical method of providing an effective transportation link between Strangford and Portaferry that complements the existing road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a potential permanent bridge between Portaferry and Strangford will be considered for inclusion in the Transport Strategy 2035 as an alternative to the Strangford Ferry.
answer_text: The Transport Strategy 2035 is a strategic document and does not provide commentary on individual transport schemes. However as explained in my previous responses on this subject (ref: AQW 30608/22-27 & AQW 30610/22-27) my Department’s suite of transport plans will be the place for scheme detail.  The Eastern Transport Plan covers the local transport movements in Ards and North Down, the outworking of which will determine my Department’s priorities with regards to any new road infrastructure, including any permanent linkage between Strangford and Portaferry. Information relating to the development of the Plan can be viewed via my Department’s website at Eastern Transport Plan (ETP) 2035.The Strangford Lough Ferry Service operates every day of the year except Christmas day, from early morning to late - night and serves both the local community and local tourism. It provides a reliable service and is currently the most economical method of providing an effective transportation link between Strangford and Portaferry that complements the existing road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any assessment has been made of the benefits of a permanent bridge between Portaferry and Strangford.
answer_text: As explained in my previous response to AQW 27686/22-27, my Department’s transport plans are the key driver for continued investment in our road network.  The Eastern Transport Plan covers the local transport movements in Ards and North Down, the outworking of which will determine my Department’s priorities with regards to any new road infrastructure, including any permanent linkage between Strangford and Portaferry.  Information relating to the development of the Plan can be viewed via my Department’s website at; Eastern Transport Plan (ETP) 2035 | Department for InfrastructureConsideration of the need for a detailed assessment of the bridge between Portaferry and Strangford will be informed by the outcome of the Eastern Transport Plan.The Strangford Lough Ferry Service operates every day of the year except Christmas day from early morning to late at night and serves both the local community and local tourism. It provides a reliable service and is currently the most economical method of providing an effective transportation link between Strangford and Portaferry that complements the existing road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount (i) spent on; and (ii) of income generated by, the Strangford ferry, over the last ten years.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive and she has responded as follows.“The specified application/transfer reason is not a reportable category in the Housing Executive’s Housing Management System (HMS).”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether any Health and Social Care Trusts use Trinity Biotech machines to diagnose diabetes; and (ii) whether this is being reviewed in light of errors detected in NHS England.
answer_text: A Device Safety Information notice in relation to Trinity Biotech Premier Hb9210™ HbA1c Analyser was issued by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency on 22 July 2025.The notification provided information for action by healthcare professionals and was published on the Department of Health (NI) Central Alert System, following notification by the Northern Ireland Adverse Incident Centre.Health and Social Care Trusts across Northern Ireland use a different Point of Care Testing analyser and suppliers for HbA1c testing and therefore no patients in Northern Ireland are impacted by this notification.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reasons for delays of more than 15 minutes, to the scheduled Enterprise train service, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink can only provide information for those trains arriving into Belfast which was answered in your previous question, AQW 30493/22-27. Once the trains enter the Irish Rail network all delays are recorded through its signalling system which Translink does not have access to.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) number; and (ii) length of delays, of more than 15 minutes, in the scheduled Enterprise train arriving in Belfast, in each of the last 12 months.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Year Month Delays Minutes 2024 September 20 498 2024 October 38 1355 2024 November 60 1797 2024 December 34 1319 2025 January 32 734 2025 February 21 601 2025 March 20 615 2025 April 11 260 2025 May 19 554 2025 June 19 597 2025 July 23 806 2025 August 12 275 For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether motorcycling will form part of an integrated transport strategy.
answer_text: We recognise the important role motorcyclists play in the north’s transport landscape and are committed to ensuring that all road users, including vulnerable groups such as motorcyclists, are considered in the development and implementation of the final Transport Strategy 2035.Motorcycles and Mopeds are referenced as an important mode of transport in the Supports Connected and Inclusive Communities chapter of the draft Transport Strategy. However, your feedback is welcomed and will be reviewed alongside all other consultation responses before the Transport Strategy is finalised. We are committed to reflecting the full diversity of transport users in the final plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on a potential scheme on the collection and recycling of nappy and absorbent hygiene waste.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 15033/22-27, to detail the amount of rates arrears that were written off in 2024-25.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the response provided for AQW 30220/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14519/22-27, to outline when the review of regional strategic planning policy for renewable and low carbon energy, which commenced in January 2021, will be completed and published.
answer_text: I refer the member to part (ii) of my answer to AQW 27057/22-27, which was answered on 29 May 2025. The final Policy will be published as soon as possible following Executive Committee agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14444/22-27, for an update on the work undertaken to review the interpretation of the implementation of Article 15 of The Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1992.
answer_text: Following the completion of a review of Article 15 of The Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1992, my officials are currently finalising a guidance document for use by Registered Housing Associations. Officials expect to publish the guidance document shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total amount of rates currently in arrears.
answer_text: As at the 31 August 2025, unpaid rates carried forward from previous years is £145.4 million, this is a 20% reduction from the position as at 31 March 2025.Land & Property Services (LPS) continues to collect all rates due and encourages ratepayers unable to pay in full to make contact to discuss options available. Ratepayers who refuse to pay or fail to constructively engage with LPS will face legal action.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) an update on potential capital grants being delivered by Sport NI; and (ii) a timeframe for when these may be launched.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline any steps he is taking to reduce waiting times for those with neurological conditions.
answer_text: I acknowledge that the current waiting times for neurology are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.To address current pressures regarding neurology waiting lists, there are a number of recommendations proposed in the final report of my Department’s Regional Review of Neurology Services. This includes growth in the workforce to provide additional capacity as well as an improved skill mix. This, in turn, will support new ways of working to ensure that access to outpatient appointments is prioritised for people who need to see a Consultant Neurologist while also offering alternatives to outpatient appointments for those whose needs can be met in a different way.   A public consultation on the final report closed on 6th August 2025. My officials are currently considering the responses to that consultation which will inform the development of a final action plan. I expect this work to be completed in the coming months. In the interim, my officials continue to actively consider ways to address lengthy waiting lists and to understand how current systems could be effectively used to enhance patient access through new, and innovative ways of working and collaborative approaches. Tackling our lengthy waiting times is one of my priorities and is indeed an Executive priority.  It is, however, undoubtedly a long-term issue that requires sustainable and recurrent funding, workforce development and system wide transformation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will introduce traffic courts in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 8 - AQO 2363/22-27		  ANSWER The listing and scheduling of court business, including traffic offences, is a matter for the independent judiciary.  Along with my Criminal Justice Board colleagues, including the Chief Constable and the Director of Public Prosecutions, I am working with the Lady Chief Justice to explore more proportionate and effective ways for the criminal justice system to handle less serious offences, including some motoring offences. Exploring options for the handling of such offences is a significant element of the Criminal Justice Board’s Speeding up Justice Programme, which seeks to minimise delay in the criminal justice system.    This is part of my Department’s Programme for Government commitments to reform the way in which criminal cases are handled. This includes getting the most serious cases to court more quickly, looking at the jurisdiction of the courts, enhanced digital capabilities to deliver efficiencies and how we can remove demand from the court system.  Consideration as to how motoring offences might be more effectively and efficiently dealt with forms part of the current consultation on the expansion of Out of Court Disposals, which is due to close on 28 September.  That consultation seeks views as to how less serious offences could be diverted away from courts and the formal criminal justice system, whilst ensuring that more resources are available to deal with the most serious cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of whole time equivalent neurological consultants there are in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The number of whole time equivalent (WTE) neurology consultants in each Health and Social Care is outlined below.Belfast HSC Trust has 13.8 WTE neurology consultants. An historical arrangement was set up whereby the Belfast HSC Trust also provides outreach to other Trusts equating to 1 WTE.Northern HSC Trust do not currently have any directly employed neurology consultants in post.South Eastern HSC Trust has 4.8 WTE neurology consultants.Southern HSC Trust has 5 WTE neurology consultants.Western HSC Trust has 2.8 WTE neurology consultants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the number of people who suffer from epilepsy in Northern Ireland; and (ii) how does this compare to the prevalence of the condition in other parts of the UK.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland approximately 1 in 83 people have epilepsy.Epilepsy prevalence in Northern Ireland is the highest among the UK nations and compares to 1 in every 107 people in the UK overall. It is also an increase from 2011 when the prevalence in Northern Ireland was 1 in 90 people. The number of new cases in Northern Ireland is just over 45 in every 100,000 people a year. This is higher than the UK overall, which is just over 42 new cases in 100,000 people a year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 29291/22-27, whether her Department has established the amount spent on defending recent judicial review proceedings, in relation to the A5 Western Transport Corridor scheme.
answer_text: My Department has not received all external legal costs in relation to the most recent A5 Western Transport Corridor Statutory Appeal proceedings, therefore we are not in a position to release the amount spent at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how many buildings being considered for designation as listed have been destroyed, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: There were four buildings under consideration for listing that have been destroyed in the last five years. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting times for a first outpatient appointment for those suffering from neurological conditions, broken down by health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Information on the average waiting times by Health and Social Care Trust for neurology across Northern Ireland is available on My Waiting Times NI. This website provides an option to select which Trust you wish to view average waiting times for. The average waiting times for first consultant-led neurology appointment will be displayed by clinical priority i.e. red flag, routine and urgent. This information is updated by the first Thursday of each month. The most recent information is available at; My Waiting Times NI - DOH/HSCNI Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG)It should be noted that published Encompass data is subject to ongoing validation by each Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to set targets for early stage diagnosis of blood cancers.
answer_text: Cancer waiting time targets in Northern Ireland aim for at least 98% of patients to start treatment within 31 days of a decision to treat, and at least 95% to start within 62 days of an urgent GP referral. Additionally, all urgent breast cancer referrals should be seen within 14 days.Whilst actions 13 and 14 of the Cancer Strategy 2022-2032 refer to the introduction of a 28-day standard for suspected cancer and a review of current cancer targets respectively, there are no immediate plans to review cancer targets at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what steps are being taken to improve GP awareness of blood cancer symptoms.
answer_text: As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of topics including symptoms and signs of haematological disorders.GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in Revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.I have a number of initiatives which are designed to ensure that GPs and Practices can access training readily and at a time that is convenient.GPNI was established during the Covid-19 pandemic to ensure effective communication was maintained, with all those working within GP Primary Care. This service is commissioned from the Eastern GP Federation Support Unit and allocation for this service for 2025/26 is £51.8k. The GPNI website and webinars are now widely accepted as a key regional communications platform designed to improve dissemination of, and access to, information for Primary Care practitioners.Practice Based Learning (PBL) is a programme of learning sessions for GPs and the wider General Practice staff.  The aim of these PBL is to allow dedicated time to be set aside for educational sessions to contribute to the continued professional development of GP’s and their Practice Staff. Provision of PBL was previously developed by the then HSCB for all GP Practices, however from March 2020 SPPG commission the provision of PBL from the Federations for their member Practices.  PBL is facilitated by local GP Federation Support Units and £178k was allocated for PBL for the 24/25 year.  They have provided a cost-effective programme of ongoing learning for their GPs, nurses and wider Practice staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline how many listed buildings have been (i) damaged; or (ii) destroyed by fire, in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: DfC is aware of twenty-seven fires which occurred at listed buildings over the past five years (twenty-five if 2020 is not included). There is no requirement for such incidents to be reported to the Department, so this information may not be fully accurate.202020212022202320242025Fires at listed buildings 254538		None of the buildings were destroyed but sixteen were noted to be extensively damaged.  In 2023 two fires occurred in curtilage structures, and these are counted as they are considered as protected under the listing of the main building.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current timeline for delivery of the Newtownards park and ride facility, following recent planning permission approval.
answer_text: Translink is currently completing the required Outline Business Case for a new Park and Ride facility in Newtownards. This is expected to be submitted to my Department by Spring 2026. Any decision on the proposed Park and Ride will be subject to the assessment of the business case and the availability of funding at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of her departmental budget allocated to each local council area in the Southern Division for roads maintenance in 2025-26.
answer_text: The Department does not hold or analysis budget information on a Council area basis.The budget for road maintenance is not distributed solely on a geographical basis but rather is allocated to each of the four Roads Divisions on the basis of need using a range of weighted indicators tailored to each maintenance activity (e.g., resurfacing, patching). Divisions broadly use these indicators when apportioning across council areas and this ensures, as far as practicable, an equitable distribution of funds across the whole of the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many operations have been (i) cancelled; and (ii) rearranged as a result of a lack of ambulance transport in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Trusts have advised that the majority of patients cancelled due to transport issues are cancelled when the ambulance is being requested, which is normally before the day of surgery. The cancellation reason is only recorded for patients cancelled on the day of surgery. There is currently no specific reason on the system for ‘lack of ambulance transport’. This information however may be recorded within a free text field on the system, but this is not mandatory and therefore the data may not be complete. Likewise, Trusts do not hold any additional information relating to operations that have been rearranged as a result of lack of ambulance transport. Following the move to the new Encompass system, work is underway to improve the recording and reporting of cancellation reasons. This will include being able to identify cancellations due to lack of ambulance transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any discussions with Translink regarding the alignment of school transport to the start of the new school year.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of school transport and maintains regular contact with Translink regarding the alignment of school transport for the start of the new school year.All home to school transport services, including those operated by Translink, commence operation on 1st September each year. Individual schools are responsible for determining their own calendar, however the EA writes to each school in February/March to inform them of the non-operational dates for the following academic year and to remind them that home to school transport services do not commence until 1st September.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the policy on the reuse of returned walking aids, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Walking aids is a broad term and can include many items such as walking sticks, crutches, canes, rollators, walking frames some with wheels and brakes, and trolleys.  The particular aid prescribed will depend on the individual’s particular needs and ability.There is currently no policy on the reuse of returned walking aids, however there is agreed best practice which Trusts are encouraged to adhere to.  BSO have agreements in place with four out of five HSC Trusts for the central recycling of particular items, for example, rollators and 3 wheeled walking frames.  Local arrangements vary from Trust to Trust depending on the item of equipment.  The Medical Device Regulations (UK MDR 2002), as enforced by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) provide guidance on the return, decontamination and re-use of medical devices in the UK.  This is taken into consideration by BSO when procuring medical devices including walking aids.The HSC Trusts have responded on local variations, which will include single use equipment such canes and walking sticks.  The following responses were provided:HSC TrustPolicy of the reuse of returned walking aidsBelfastRe-usable devices must be designed by the manufacturer for re-use - Returned devices must go through a controlled process, including inspection, cleaning and decontamination, functionality checks and repacking or labelling (if needed) – Mobility equipment is often marked as single user item and, the cost of decontamination and cleaning in line with infection control guidelines is not cost effective. It is worth noting that mobility equipment will also have a typical life expectancy defined within the manufacturing instructions which clinicians need to be mindful of regarding returns and re-issue.Manufacturers must provide validated reprocessing instructions, including cleaning, disinfection and sterilisation if applicable.  Devices labelled as single use must not be re-used under any circumstances.  Re-use would breach MHRA regulations and risk patient safety”.NorthernAll walking aid equipment on loan is returned via the Community Equipment Service.  Mobility aids are inspected and assessed for reuse.  Equipment that is unfit for reuse and not cost effective to reissue due to infection control standards, being damaged beyond economical repair or being aged or obsolete are disposed of correctly following Trust Waste Disposal Policy.  Whilst all efforts are made to economically recycle mobility aid equipment there will be occasions when equipment is not suitable to reuse. Zimmer frames, crutches and walking sticks are classed as not cost effective to reuse as the cost to refurbish is higher than the cost to procure new.  The Trust has several schemes ongoing with external contractors to recycle materials and to make provision for the safe disposal of equipment. South EasternThe SEHSCT continues to re-use a range of mobility/walking aids as part of the Trust’s recycling programme.  For the pe

answer_text: Details of Structural Maintenance expenditure by Roads divisions analysed between Resource and Capital is detailed in the Table below for each of the last five financial years. £’mStructural Maintenance  20-21 21-2222-2323-2424-25Northern DivisionCapital20.326.426.322.725.7Resource4.13.63.55.05.7Total24.430.029.827.731.4Southern DivisionCapital24.023.529.231.632.7Resource5.15.25.17.17.4Total29.128.734.338.740.1Eastern DivisionCapital10.413.914.914.119.8Resource2.62.72.83.75.6Total13.016.617.717.825.4Western DivisionCapital27.524.840.629.128.9Resource6.44.54.96.99.8Total33.929.345.536.038.7Capital Structural Maintenance includes resurfacing and reconstruction, asphalt patching, surface dressing, capital investment in both drainage infrastructure and in the structural stability of embankments.
