question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures she will introduce to effect central collation of information on Environmental Impact Assessment applications and permissions to assist Environmental Impact Assessment system monitoring.
answer_text: My Department has been at the forefront of delivering comprehensive Environmental Impact Assessment training and guidance to enhance capacity and consistency across the system.  I am content that all information related to Environment Impact Assessment applications and permissions is already publicly accessible and I do not consider it necessary to introduce additional measures for central collation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to (i) legislate for; and (ii) incentivise, increased separation of recyclable waste.
answer_text: Proposals to improve the quality and quantity of our recycling and to help meet our legislative targets were included in a consultation document, Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI, published in 2024. Analysis of responses to this consultation has helped inform a draft Departmental response, which I am currently considering.It is my intention to bring this to my Executive colleagues in the coming weeks to seek their support in moving forward together to not only meet our legal targets for recycling, but to also help drive green growth and develop our circular economy in Northern Ireland.Subject to the approval of Executive colleagues, it is my intention to publish the Departmental Response. I will then consider what legislative interventions may be required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what regeneration schemes are planned for the Strangford constituency, in the next five years.
answer_text: My officials work closely with colleagues in Councils to develop and prioritise regeneration projects for delivery when budgets are allocated.  The Department has issued a Contract for Funding for up to £500k to Ards and North Down Borough Council for a Borough Wide Shop Front project in Bangor, Newtownards, Donaghadee, Comber and Holywood.  As the budget has not been set beyond 25/26, future projects are not yet approved for delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of street light faults reported; and (ii) the number repaired, in the Ards and North Down area in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table provides details of the number of street light outages reported and repaired in the Ards and North Down area in each of the last five years.YearOutages ReportedOutages Repaired2020/21428241692021/22385536512022/23420541542023/24384834862024//2538113634My Department is providing a full street lighting maintenance service, with the majority of outages generally repaired within the established target of 5 working days.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what system monitoring measures her Department undertakes to ensure the Environmental Impact Assessment process is effective and robust.
answer_text: It is the responsibility of the relevant determining authority to ensure that, where applicable, the requirements of the Planning (Environmental Impact Assessment) Regulations (N I) 2017 (the Regulations) are properly satisfied.  For the vast majority of planning applications under the two-tier system this responsibility rests with the relevant district council in its role as local planning authority when making decisions.  My Department fulfils its responsibilities as set out by the Regulations in relation to the small number of applications for which it is the determining authority.  Consequently, my Department does not maintain a monitoring system of decisions relating to environmental impact assessment made by the separate decision-making bodies within local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail: (i) the number of hours each library is open per week; and (ii) a breakdown of which libraries offer an out of hours service.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Libraries NI. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive.Due to the volume of detail required to answer your question, the information attached at Annex A has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the provision of the mobile library services in the Ards and North Down area; and (ii) what steps which are being considered to broaden this provision.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Libraries NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘In addition to the six libraries in the Ards and North Down area, the mobile library service visits primary schools and various public stops. The mobile library timetable is available on the Libraries NI website. While there are no plans at present to broaden the provision, the mobile library service is kept under review.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any plans he has to review current approaches to assessing pupils to ensure they support effective learning.
To ask the Minister of Health what support is available to the parents of children diagnosed with Sensory Processing Disorder.
answer_text: Sensory processing disorder is not a recognised diagnosis in the UK or Ireland. This classification does not appear in the DSM 5, Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders 5 (DSMV) or within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems 11th Revision. (ICD 11). The Royal College of Occupational Therapist’s position paper (2021) states ‘It should also be noted that ‘Sensory Processing Disorder’ is not recognised as an independent diagnosis in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders 5’ (American Psychiatric Association 2013). If indicated, Occupational Therapists will assess a child’s sensory processing difficulties as they impact on function in their everyday lives and provide interventions to address these functional difficulties as appropriate. There is a wide range of accessible support available to families across a range of services. This includes online universal resources and more targeted resource support for parent such as; online workshops and videos and training and small groups within school settings. More individualised or specialist support is provided for children and young people with the most complex needs and circumstances.Occupational Therapists adopt a strengths-based approach, helping individuals and families develop the skills and confidence to understand and manage their occupational needs throughout their lives. The primary focus of Occupational Therapy is on functional difficulties and their impact on every day functioning. Within this context, the child is assessed from a holistic perspective looking at the range of difficulties they experience across a range of domains. Sensory processing challenges may be identified within this assessment and intervention process.  As Sensory Processing Disorder is not a recognised diagnosis there are no staff within Social Care Trusts trained to diagnose and/or treat Sensory Processing Disorder.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 26719/22-27, when the next assessment of the status of horse mussels in Strangford Lough will be carried out.
answer_text: The next survey to assess the status of horse mussel (Modiolus modiolus) reef in Strangford Lough is scheduled for summer 2025.  The condition assessment will be reported on as part of the 2020-2025 condition assessment for Strangford Lough SAC once the data gathered during the survey has been analysed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs why he has decided not to proceed with his Department’s research work plan on Lough Neagh eels.
answer_text: There is an ongoing programme of research supported by my Department in relation to Lough Neagh. This has not changed. I remain fully committed to providing as much support as possible to this important and historic fishery. There has, however, been an operational change to the sampling programme. As a result of concerns from the Lough Neagh Fishermen’s Co-operative Society (LNFCS), AFBI has been able to deliver an amendment to the sampling procedure. This change will reduce the number of samples that are required and simplify working procedures by avoiding the need to anaesthetise fish on the LNFCS premises. It does not represent a reduction of research or support for the fishery.This simplification is only possible due to the extensive historical information already collected by AFBI and the LNFCS. This approach will ensure there is no loss of data for annual assessments or in the calculation of escape figures. There is no substantive change to the direction or outcomes of the agreed Annual Work Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) what the research work plan on Lough Neagh eels planned to achieve; and (ii) how not progressing this work plan will affect the eel fishing industry on Lough Neagh.
answer_text: (i) The Agri-Food Biosciences Institute (AFBI) undertake a programme of research, sampling and data collection on behalf of my Department, which includes the sampling and collection of data in respect of eels.  This data collection programme has several uses. Firstly, it fulfils our commitments under the UK Data Collection Work Plan where there is a standing legislative requirement to collect information on commercial fisheries for catadromous species.The programme supports the UK commitments to provide data for annual stock assessments undertaken by the International Council for Exploration of the Seas, as well as being used to gauge compliance with the requirements of the Eel Management Plan for the Neagh / Bann River Basin.  The same data also supports the maintenance of a Non Detrimental Finding to allow trade, in this critically endangered species, to continue to Great Britian in compliance with CITES (Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of wild Fauna and Flora) regulations. (ii) The work plan continues to be applied. The recent change to the sampling programme, undertaken to address the concerns of the Lough Neagh Fishermen’s Co-operative Society, provides for simplification. It does not jeopardise the delivery or outcomes of the existing data sampling programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps he is taking to support the Lough Neagh eel industry.
answer_text: I am very concerned about the developments within the eel fishery this season and recently met with the Lough Neagh Fishermen’s Co-operative Society to discuss the situation and what assistance my Department may be able to provide. My officials will be continuing this liaison in the weeks ahead.A regulation is being prepared to protect eel stocks and support compliance with the Eel Management Plan. This regulated is intended to protect the future of the eel fishery and is essential for maintaining the Non Detriment Finding that enables trade to Great Britian in this endangered species.I remain fully committed to addressing the environmental issues around Lough Neagh, which are of fundamental importance to protecting this fishery in the long term and these are being taken forward within the Lough Neagh Action Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider the implementation of an emergency financial support package for eel fishermen due to the truncated fishing season this year.
answer_text: The Lough Neagh Fishermen’s Co-operative Society has now suspended eel fishing pending a further review in mid-July.  I am very concerned over this development; this further suspension represents a significant loss of fishing opportunity for this year. This decision was made following the Co-operative’s assessment of the quality of their catch.  This is primarily a decision for them, to gauge if the quality meets the market standard, however my department’s services stand ready to undertake any routine fat analysis as part of the annual eel sampling programme.I remain committed to providing whatever support is practical and affordable to the eel fishers, including through existing grant schemes.  Any case for direct financial support in respect of the truncated fishing season would need to be robustly supported with scientific and financial evidence for the scheme and the impact and the scale of the losses.  I have instructed my officials to engage further with the Co-operative in the development of any request.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider developing a criteria for gritting greenways, which is separate from the roads network.
answer_text: I have confirmed my commitment to connecting communities and encouraging more active travel and have also indicated that developing a safer, cleaner travel culture is central to my key priorities. I want to carefully consider how best to deliver on my active and sustainable travel ambitions and part of that consideration should involve how we improve conditions for walking, cycling and using public transport in the winter months. Greenways are real asset and I am delighted that its popularity with walkers, wheelers and cyclists continues to grow.  However, coming up with a solution that would allow it and other greenways and footways to be treated in icy weather does present some significant logistical, financial and resource challenges which, with so many other competing pressures, my Department is unable to take forward at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of Assembly Written Questions submitted to his Department that currently remain unanswered in each of the last five years.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the budget allocated for the provision of bus shelters in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: I have not yet taken decisions on my Department’s 2025-26 budget outcome.  In doing so I will, of course, consider the responses received on the Equality Impact Consultation on my Department’s Draft 2025-26 Budget which closed on 5th June 2025.This is likely to require some difficult decisions and bearing down on costs will be essential to protect essential public service delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the amount of funding being allocated to the (a) Comber; and (b) Newtownards, park and ride projects; and (ii) for an update on progress for both sites.
answer_text: The preferred site in Comber is located along the A22 Belfast Road corridor immediately adjacent to the Comber Greenway with the potential to provide parking for 410 vehicles in the first phase with scope for further extension.  Appraisal and design works are ongoing to enable the submission of a planning application.  The Newtownards Park and Ride site is being led by Translink on behalf of my Department.  In October 2023, a planning application was lodged for a new 450 space Park and Ride on the site of the former leisure centre at William Street and Translink are currently working through planning matters with the local council.  It is expected that an outline business case will be completed and submitted to my Department for approval in Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of businesses from the Strangford constituency which have (i) filed for bankruptcy; (ii) been subject to a creditor's petition for bankruptcy; or (iii) entered into a voluntary arrangement with their creditors, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of sole trader businesses (bankruptcies) and winding up orders for limited company businesses where the individual or director has petitioned for the bankruptcy/winding up, the number subject to a creditors petition and the number of Individual Voluntary Arrangements (IVAs) in the Strangford constituency for the previous four financial years is detailed in the table below. 2020-212021-222022-232023-242024-2025 Sole TraderWinding-Up OrdersSole TraderWinding-Up OrdersSole TraderWinding-Up OrdersSole TraderWinding-Up OrdersSole TraderWinding-Up OrdersBusiness Own Petitions0000000000Creditors Petitions0001010529Individual Voluntary Arrangements (IVAs)5876735988		Individual Voluntary Arrangments (IVAs) are administered by private sector insolvency practicitoners. My Department is only responsible for maintaining a register of IVAs in the North of Ireland and it cannot be determined, from the statutory information held, how much of the debt, if any, is attributable to business debt.	The directors of limited companies may also enter into arrangements with the companies creditors, Company Voluntary Arrangements (CVAs), however, my Department has no role in this process which is administered solely by private sector insolvency practitioners therefore I am unable to provide numbers in relation to these.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of his Department's budget that has been allocated to tree planting in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: My Department has budgeted £2.11m for planting new woodlands in the 2025/26 financial year.Additionally, £1.9m has been budgeted for spend in 2025/26, to meet continuing grant commitments for woodlands planted in previous years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Movilla Road, Newtownards, will be resurfaced.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the park and ride sites which will be developed in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: My Department has identified a list of priority bus and rail-based Park and Ride sites and further potential sites will be considered within the new Transport Strategy and Transport Plans continuing to be developed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the budget allocation to the Department of Infrastructure Roads Southern Division for the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: I have not taken decisions on the 2025-26 Budget. I will be considering budget allocations in line with my priorities, the wider Executive PfG commitments and equality considerations, following closure of my Department’s Equality Impact Assessment consultation on 5 June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 12009/22-27, to detail the number of pre-application discussion meetings the Department of Infrastructure Southern Division has attended with Ards and North Down Borough Council, in the year 2024-25.
answer_text: Whilst Pre-Application Discussions (PADs) are non-statutory, they are a positive and proactive part of the planning process and can be a useful collaboration between an applicant and the Council or the Department in relation to draft development proposals.  The type of discussions can vary depending on the nature, scale and benefits of each planning application; however, they very rarely involve face to face meetings with Statutory Consultees and are normally carried out through a remote consultation process and then placed on the Planning Portal.During the year 2024-25 my officials from DfI Southern Division have attended 1 pre-application discussion meeting with Ards and North Down Borough Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 12085/22-27, whether any further assessment has been made of (i) the Welsh Government’s use of recycled nappies in road resurfacing schemes; and (ii) the potential for this in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the work in Wales to trial the use of fibres recovered from used nappies in the production of road resurfacing materials. I am advised that the findings from the trial are not yet published, and my Officials will be updated on this through their routine engagements with their counterparts in the Welsh Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether any instances of Reinforced Autoclaved Aerated Concrete, being used in court service buildings, have been identified.

No evidence of the material was discovered during the inspection.

answer_text: This matter is currently under consideration by His Majesty’s Treasury.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the impact reducing nitrogen application rates will have on (i) grass growth; and (ii) financial performance, as proposed in the consultation for the Nutrient Action Programme for 2026 – 2029.
answer_text: (i) The proposal in the consultation on the whole-farm limit for nitrogen on silage ground of 310 kg Nitrogen/hectare/year is based on the best available scientific evidence.  This proposed rate supports an agronomic optimum requirement for grass growth in Northern Ireland. The proposed rates for grazed fields are as specified in the UK Nutrient Management Guide RB209 2023 edition and are considered to be the agronomic optimum requirements. The upper application limit of 270 kg Nitrogen/hectare/year for a grass Dry Matter (DM) yield of 12-15 tonne/hectare takes account of nitrogen recycled from livestock at grazing. It therefore represents the same total nitrogen application rate of 310kg Nitrogen/hectare/year as silage ground.(ii) The proposed rates are considered to be the agronomic optimum requirements for grass growth in Northern Ireland and therefore will not be detrimental to grass growth or economic performance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail all the options considered but not included in the consultation for the proposed Nutrient Action Programme for 2026 – 2029.
answer_text: The approach that has been taken to the proposed Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) 2026 -2029 consultation, is to only include proposals for additional measures which have been informed by robust scientific evidence and changes to existing measures which will improve implementation.Section 6 of the Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) provides consideration of alternatives to the proposals included in the NAP consultation. The SEA can be found at www.daera-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-04/DAERA%20NAP%20-%20Strategic%20Environmental%20Assessment.PDF
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the methodology used to assess the impact on livestock numbers; and (ii) the results of such an exercise, in relation to the proposed Nutrient Action Programme for 2026 – 2029.
answer_text: An initial regulatory impact assessment has been prepared to assess the impact of implementing the proposed measures in the revised Nutrients Action Programme which aims to improve water quality and nutrient use efficiency in agricultural systems.  The initial regulatory impact assessment can be accessed on the DAERA website https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-04/Initial%20Regulatory%20Impact%20Assessment%20%28RIA%29.PDF.Following completion of the consultation my officials will undertake a full economic impact assessment of the final measures. That will ensure that any final decisions are informed by a full and complete understanding of their potential impact.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a breakdown by sector, of the estimated 3,500 farm businesses which will be subject to the proposed Nutrient Action Programme for 2026 – 2029.
answer_text: The consultation on the Nutrient Action Programme for 2026-2029 proposes that more intensively stocked farms with annual livestock manure nitrogen production at and above 150kg Nitrogen per hectare per year will be subject to specific measures. These measures are use of Low Emission Slurry Spreading Equipment (LESSE), a Farm Phosphorus Balance and a Liming Programme for mineral soils on grassland farms. The estimated breakdown of these farm enterprises by sector is as follows:Farm Enterprise sectorEstimatednumber of farmsDairy1808Other Cattle 1379Poultry371Pigs135Total3693It should be noted that the numbers are estimated because there is some overlap between these enterprise categories, as farms can have multiple categories of livestock.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the methodology used to evaluate the economic impact; and (ii) the economic impact, on each sector relating to the proposed Nutrient Action Programme for 2026 – 2029.
answer_text: In line with statutory process, an initial Regulatory Impact Assessment has been published with the Nutrients Action Programme consultation. A final Regulatory Impact Assessment will be completed, informed by the consultation. The initial Regulatory Impact Assessment considered the impact of the proposed measures included in the consultation.  It identified that the main affected group will be farms with annual livestock manure nitrogen production at and above 150kg Nitrogen/hectare per year. The requirements on Low Emission Slurry Spreading Equipment (LESSE) are estimated to have the greatest capital cost implications of all the proposed measures as a proportion of farms will invest in their own equipment. The impact of specific measures on three dairy farm systems at individual farm level is also assessed and regulatory resource costs for the Department are estimated.The final Regulatory Impact Assessment will be complemented by a full economic impact assessment to ensure any decisions on the draft proposals are informed by a full understanding of the potential impact.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any land that the Education Authority has deemed as surplus to requirements, in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s definition of surplus property is those properties that are actively being disposed of having been declared surplus prior to the disposal process commencing.One property appears in that list that is within the Strangford Constituency when cross checked with the Asset register, it is:Former Castle Gardens Primary School site, Portaferry Road, Newtownards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any land that NI Water has deemed as surplus to requirements in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: Land Disposal is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought an update from NI Water and been advised that the following land has been deemed as surplus to requirements in the Strangford constituency:Craigantlet Low Service Reservoir, Whitespots Service Reservoir,Moneyreagh South Service Reservoir, andMoneyreagh South Water Tower.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has made any assessment of the feasibility of a bridge across the Narrows, Strangford Lough.
answer_text: My Department’s transport plans are the key driver for continued investment in our road network. A new Transport Strategy is being developed along with a suite of transport plans that will guide the development of our transport system through to 2035.  It is my intention to publish a draft Strategy for public consultation in the coming weeks that will subsequently be followed by the Regional Strategic Transport Network Transport Plan (RSTNTP) next year that covers the Trunk Road Network. I would encourage you to respond to these consultations. Also, the Eastern Transport Plan covers the local transport movements for five council areas including Ards and North Down where Portaferry is located. Information relating to the development of the Eastern Transport Plan is more relevant to any new permanent linkage between Strangford and Portaferry and can be viewed at via the Department’s website at; Eastern Transport Plan (ETP) 2035 | Department for InfrastructureAt this time my Department would not have the economic justification to begin to explore a permanent bridge connection between Strangford and Portaferry. Taking forward a feasibility study is not considered a good use of public funding particularly during a time when the Department is facing significant funding and resource challenges due to the austerity of the British government. The Strangford Lough Ferry Service operates every day of the year except Christmas day from early morning to late at night and serves both the local community and local tourism. It provides a reliable service and is currently the most economical method of providing an effective transportation link between Strangford and Portaferry that complements the existing road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to mitigate the impact on staff of the withdrawal of current parking passes and the implementation of a new policy where they will not receive a new pass, given poor public transport connections and lack of parking surrounding the Ulster Hospital site.
answer_text: To ensure that those staff with the greatest need are provided with a free on-site parking permit, the Department has requested that all HSC Trusts implement an assessment of demonstrable “need” for designated staff car parking spaces / permits. The criteria which will be used on the Ulster Hospital site takes into consideration individual circumstances, operational requirements, and the individual’s ability to access alternative and sustainable forms of transport.The application process is specifically designed to prioritise on-site parking for those most in need of access to a vehicle during working hours.  When the new policy is implemented in April 2026, the number of staff permitted to park on the Ulster Hospital site is anticipated to remain broadly unchanged. However, with the introduction of automatic number plate recognition (ANPR) technology in May 2026, the Trust believes that the car parking data obtained will provide an opportunity to improve resource utilisation and increase the number of staff permit holders on site by the later part of 2026 into 2027.  Existing staff parking permit holders, who do not meet the new criteria for a parking permit, can avail of the free park and ride service, from Dundonald Omniplex to the Ulster Hospital site, which has been operated by the Trust since 2019. The number of registered staff using this service has been increasing year on year.In addition, the Trust has a long history of engagement with the local community on traffic issues in the Dundonald area. Since 2016, the South Eastern Trust has convened and facilitated bi-annual Multi Agency meetings for Dundonald, Ards and Lagan Valley Hospital areas to discuss the impact of their activity on the local community with regards to travel and traffic issues with elected representatives, PSNI, Translink, and the Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any land that the Northern Ireland Housing Executive has deemed as surplus to requirements in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘The Housing Executive can advise that we have no lands declared surplus to requirements in the Strangford constituency for sale on the open market.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail on what grounds a parking permit will be granted to staff at the Ulster Hospital under the new staff parking policy.
answer_text: Staff at the Ulster Hospital who wish to apply for a staff parking permit are first required to complete an online application which is used to identify staff who meet the automatic qualification for an on-site pass, for example a “Blue Badge” holder. Following this initial screening process, which provides a baseline score for the application, applicants are invited to submit details of any extenuating circumstances that an independent panel can anonymously assess and indicate whether a permit should be granted or not.The assessment criteria used by the South Eastern Trust was originally developed in 2016-17 after extensive research and engagement with stakeholders, staff and Trade Unions. These stakeholders remain satisfied that the criteria and online application process, with a built-in independent appeals process, is fair and equitable to South Eastern Trust staff.The criteria can be broadly categorised into four areas:Automatic eligibility, for example ‘blue badge’ holders, volunteers, or those persons on the Trust major incident response plan;Extenuating Circumstances such as any relevant section 75 considerations, for example those with dependants;Operational Requirements of the post holder to use a private vehicle to deliver healthcare or during a major incident; andEnvironmental considerations for example the individual’s ability to access public, active or other sustainable forms of transport.The South Eastern Trust parking criteria is available for all staff to view on the Trust intranet site. A link to this resource was provided to all Ulster Hospital staff in May 2025, embedded in the first communication to notify staff of the upcoming changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the reasons for the decision by the South East Health and Social Care Trust to require all staff at the Ulster Hospital, currently in possession of a car parking pass, to reapply for parking passes.
answer_text: To ensure that those staff with the greatest need are provided with an on-site parking permit, the Department has requested that all HSC Trusts implement an assessment of demonstrable “need” for designated staff car parking spaces / permits. The South Eastern Trust has used a staff parking permit assessment process, based on “need” for many years at the Lagan Valley and Ards Community Hospital sites. The criteria used were developed in 2016-17 after extensive research and engagement with stakeholders, staff and Trade Unions. These stakeholders remain satisfied that the criteria and online application process, with a built-in independent appeals process, is fair and equitable to South Eastern Trust staff.In August 2024, the South Eastern Trust Executive Management Team approved the extension of this assessment of demonstratable need to the Ulster Hospital site.To be fair to all staff, and to ensure that those staff with the greatest need are provided with an on-site parking permit, the Trust decided that both existing staff parking permit holders and new applicants would need to progress through the parking permit application process and be assessed, based on the newly applied criteria.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider a delay in progressing a new statutory regulation for Farm Sustainability Standards and a Revised Penalty Matrix until after concerns in respect of the Nutrients Action Programme 2026-2029 consultation proposals have been addressed.
answer_text: The draft Farm Sustainability Standards which will replace Cross-Compliance Verifiable Standards with effect from 2026 are derived from the existing Nutrient Action Programme Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2019 (as amended).  The current draft standards will not include any underpinning requirements from the proposed Nutrients Action Programme Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2025 until such times as the consultation and policy development process are complete.  There is therefore no need to delay the introduction of the new Farm Sustainability Standards and the associated penalty matrix.  I acknowledge concerns about the need for more time to consider and respond to the detailed proposals within the Nutrients Action Programme 2026-2029 consultation document, and have extended the consultation period by 4 weeks in light of that. My department has arranged for a number of in-person and online information sessions and I will also be meeting industry representatives during the consultation period to present supporting data and answer any questions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment was carried out on the impact the withdrawal of current parking passes and the implementation of the new policy might have on staff, given the lack of parking in the area surrounding the Ulster Hospital site and poor public transport connections in rural areas.
answer_text: The award criteria which will be used on the Ulster Hospital site for free on-site parking permits takes into consideration individual circumstances, operational requirements, and the individual’s ability to access alternative and sustainable forms of transport and is subject to ongoing Equality Assessment to ensure a positive impact for staff.Engagement with staff and Trade Unions has been taking place since 2023, to assess the impact of the upcoming staff parking permit changes on staff. During these discussions there was unanimity amongst all stakeholders that a move away from a ‘first come first serve’ model of allocating on-site parking permits to one based on merit and “an assessment of demonstrable need” was both necessary and desirable to provide a positive impact for staff.Additionally, to treat all staff fairly and ensure that those staff with the greatest need are provided with a parking permit, it was also unanimously agreed that all staff, including existing parking permit holders, would need to apply for a new parking permit.To mitigate against any potential negative impact on staff, the South Eastern Trust will continue to promote the free staff park and ride service from Dundonald Omniplex to the Ulster Hospital site and engage on traffic and travel matters with elected representatives, PSNI, Translink, and the Department for Infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any staff or union consultation took place (i) on the new staff parking policy; and (ii) before the decision was taken to require all staff at the Ulster Hospital, currently in possession of a car parking pass, to reapply for parking passes.
answer_text: The criteria, that will be applied to grant staff parking permits at the Ulster Hospital site, were originally developed in 2016-17 after extensive research online, engagement with the NHS England Car Parking & Sustainable Travel Network and consultation with stakeholders, staff and Trade Union Representatives. In 2023, the South Eastern Trust consulted with Trade Unions and the Chair of staff side, initially to remove the staff parking permit waiting list at the Ulster Hospital. This was agreed unanimously and completed by May 2024. During the second phase of this engagement, there was unanimity amongst all stakeholders that a move away from a ‘first come first serve’ model of allocating on-site parking permits to one based on merit and “an assessment of demonstrable need” was both necessary and desirable.  All parties remained satisfied that the existing criteria and online application process, with a built-in independent appeals process, was fair and equitable to South Eastern Trust staff. To ensure that those staff with the greatest need are provided with a parking permit, it was also unanimously agreed that all staff, including existing parking permit holders, would need to apply for a new parking permit, with the understanding that this potentially contentious process must be managed sensitively. In August 2024, the South Eastern Trust’s Executive Management Team approved the extension of the criteria-based application process to the Ulster Hospital site, at which time further engagement took place with Trade Unions to agree a suitable implementation timeline. It was agreed that the new application process would open for all staff in July 2025 and implemented in April 2026, in advance of the May 2026 Hospital Parking Charges Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline why the Lough Neagh Fishermen’s Co-operative Society was not consulted directly as a stakeholder by his Department in the development of the Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan.
answer_text: A joint Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs / Department for Infrastructure Water Quality Steering Group led on the development of the Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan containing science led actions to tackle the blue-green algae blooms in Lough Neagh and secure longer-term improvements in water quality across Northern Ireland.  Prior to Executive agreement of the Report and Action Plan, I engaged with a range of stakeholders which included Lough Neagh Partnership, of which Lough Neagh Fishermen’s Co-operative Society Ltd is a member. The Lough Neagh Fishermen’s Co-operative Society Ltd are also members of the Lough Neagh Stakeholder Forum which I chair, meets quarterly.  The Forum is a platform to set the foundations for collaboration to deliver the solutions needed for Lough Neagh.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any plans she has to improve the pace at which local development plans are developed and adopted.
answer_text: Good progress is being made in the Local Development Plan (LDP) programme, with four Councils having adopted Plan Strategies, and Departmental directions issued on a further two which are due to be adopted in the coming months. A further  Council is due to publish their draft Plan Strategy in June, and the remaining Councils later this year.  A number of Councils are also preparing their Local Policies Plans, and will be consulting upon those towards the end of this year into next. My officials are committed to ensuring that the regional planning system operates in an efficient and effective way, building on the existing work of the Planning Improvement Programme. These include the delivery of legislation to enable councils to produce local validation checklists which will improve the quality of applications and in turn performance; three planning fee inflationary uplifts, a review of my Department’s internal notification/call-in process, a review of the Development Management Regulations, extensive work within the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum regarding performance and training and a review of the LDP process. Focus has now turned to the next phase of the Planning Improvement Programme.The collaborative work and actions to improve the LDP process remains a key priority in this phase.  My officials are working closely with councils to improve the speed and efficiency of plan making, with a focus on streamlining processes and providing greater clarity in respect of the roles and responsibilities of key stakeholders. We will reflect on experience to date and consider short term and longer term improvements while being mindful of the ongoing process.  A review of the legislative provisions for LDPs is underway to explore options for streamlining processes and procedures, including updates to guidance, and changes to the Independent Examination process. The experience of other jurisdictions is also important and may highlight aspects of the LDP process elsewhere which could assist in the quicker delivery of development plans here. For example, officials have had useful conversations with the Office of the Planning Regulator in the South regarding their LDP process.Collaboration between central and local government as well as other key stakeholders in the planning system is key. At the start of April, my Department organised and hosted the ‘Plan to Improve - Pathway to Progress’ Round Table Event which was an important engagement event with a wide range of stakeholders in attendance.  This work is also a key action in DfI’s ‘Foundations for a Better Future’ programme.At the event in April, I welcomed the open and collaborative dialogue between a range of key stakeholders in the planning system including the discussion on Plan-making in a plan led system.The views, ideas and key messages from this event are being considered by my officials and will help inform and prioritise the actions within the next phase of the planning improvement agenda.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9842/22-27, to detail what communication her Department had with local councils to (i) advise them of the failure by the former Department for Environment's Planning Service to confirm the Tree Preservation Orders which it had made; and (ii) assist them in ensuring the Tree Preservation Orders would be enforceable.
answer_text: In the two-tier planning system, it is the responsibility of a council to interpret and ensure that it fully meets its obligations under relevant planning legislation, environmental directives and regulations. This includes consideration of the relevant sections of 121 to 128 of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 (“the 2011 Act”) which place statutory duties on councils to make adequate provision for the protection of trees, where appropriate, within the planning system.My department will however assist and provide advice where appropriate to a council on matters such as revocation of an existing tree preservation order (“TPO”) given council’s do not have the necessary statutory powers under section 122 of the 2011 Act to revoke a TPO, but these powers are available to my department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the Tree Preservation Order, for Kiltonga, Belfast Road, Newtownards, was (i) issued; and (ii) confirmed, by the former Department for Environment's Planning Service.
answer_text: Details of Tree Preservation Orders are held on a public register maintained by each local council. You may wish to contact Ards and North Down Council directly to request this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9842/22-27, for a breakdown of the number of Tree Preservation Orders made but not confirmed by the former Department for Environment's Planning Service, prior to the transfer of planning powers to local councils, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Details of Tree Preservation Orders are held on a public register maintained by each local council, you may wish to contact each directly to request this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of PSNI officers currently in post; (ii) the number of PSNI officers required to be in post under the PSNI Recovery Plan; (iii) the number of PSNI officers required to reflect the rising population; and (iv) what actions her Department is taking to assist the PSNI in being adequately resourced in terms of PSNI officer numbers.
answer_text: My Department neither collates nor holds this information.  The Member may wish to visit PSNI’s website (Strength of Police Service Statistics | PSNI) which provides a breakdown of the number of officers currently in post or contact the PSNI directly. The Chief Constable is seeking to recruit to recover workforce levels to 7,000 police officers and 2,572 police staff over the next three years.  The operational requirements, including the number of officers required by PSNI, to reflect the rising population, is a matter you may wish to address directly with the Chief Constable or with the Policing Board to which he is operationally accountable. On 7 May 2025, the Department of Finance approved the value for money aspect of the PSNI’s Workforce Recovery business case. However, affordability remains a significant challenge because without additional resource, it will not be possible to implement the recovery plan.   I am firmly committed to addressing the issue of affordability and will raise it with both the Minister of Finance and the Executive, recognising that this is not just my responsibility but a critical collective challenge for the entire Executive. Safer communities is a cross-cutting priority in the Programme for Government and the onus is on the entire Executive to help deliver that through providing the level of resource the PSNI needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9842/22-27, for a breakdown of the number of trees felled as a result of the failure by the former Department for Environment's Planning Service to confirm the Tree Preservation Orders which it had made, prior to the transfer of local planning powers to local councils, broken down by local council area
answer_text: Details of Tree Preservation Orders are held on a public register maintained by each local council, you may wish to contact each directly to request this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the Chief Executive of the Forest Service will next be meeting with the Woodland Trust.
answer_text: The next meeting is scheduled for Forest Service Chief Executive, John Joe O’Boyle to meet with the Woodland Trust on 20 June 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what measures are in place to protect the impartiality of the Northern Ireland Civil Service from organisations who provide training and resources where those organisations also lobby on political issues.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Civil Service Code of Ethics sets out the values and standards of behaviour expected of all civil servants, requiring them to carry out their role with dedication and a commitment to the Civil Service’s core values of: Integrity, Honesty, Objectivity and Impartiality. In living out the core value of ‘impartiality’, civil servants must carry out their responsibilities in a way that is fair, just and equitable and reflects the Civil Service’s commitment to equality, diversity and inclusion, including the obligations under the Section 75  of the NI Act.1998.If a civil servant believes they are  being required to breach the Code of Ethics, this can be raised as a concern.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the provision of training and resources to Northern Ireland Civil Service by Stonewall was subject to an open competition.
answer_text: Learning and development for the Civil Service is provided through various sources, including external groups identified as subject matter experts and corporate memberships aligned to Section 75 groups.  Corporate memberships are not subject to a procurement process, but relevant business cases and governance processes are adhered to.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of hectares of land issued with letters of offer for forestry grants; and (ii) the number of hectares of land planted after the award of forestry grants in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of hectares of land issued with letters of offer and planted under forestry grant schemes over the last five years are: Letters of offer (ha)Area planted (ha)2020/214622832021/223614222022/235024512023/245874332024/25259427Total2,1712,016In addition to new woodland created under grant aid, 91 hectares have been planted on the Department’s forest estate during this period.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department is on course to meet the target of 9,000 hectares of new woodland by 2030 as set out in the Forests for Our Future programme.
answer_text: The Forests for Our Future Programme is committed to planting 9,000 hectares of new woodland between 2020 and 2030, recognising that planting rates would need to increase annually from about 200 hectares when the programme is launched. To date, new woodland planting has reached about 500 hectares per year, achieving 2,300 hectares planted, under the programme to March 2025. My Department is working to address the challenge of further increasing the annual planting rates necessary to achieve 9,000 hectares over the decade.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of block de-registrations carried out by NHS dental practices in Strangford in each of the last five years, and (ii) the reasons for these block de-registrations.
answer_text: Current arrangements allow General Dental Practitioners to de-register patients individually or de-register a group of patients at the same time (a so-called block de-registration). The Business Services Organisation (BSO) does not hold information which identifies the process used to bring about de-registrations of patients by Health Service dental practices, nor the reasons for de-registration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the measures undertaken by her Department to improve mobile coverage in Strangford.
answer_text: Ofcom, the telecoms regulator, is responsible for reporting mobile coverage information in its annual Connected Nations and infrastructure reports, and also provides individual Nations reports which break down coverage at council level. Individual user’s experience may not lineup with the Ofcom data supplied; this can result in frustration from members of the public. I have raised this issue with Ofcom, who are progressing plans to improve the accuracy of the data they hold / publish. Citizens should be urged to use the postcode checker at View mobile availability - Ofcom Checker and report any issues to their service provider.Telecommunications policy is a reserved matter with the Department for Science, Innovation & Technology (DSIT) holding the policy responsibility on improvements to mobile connectivity. The Department for the Economy (DfE) maintains regular contact with DSIT on all telecommunications matters ensuring that local issues are given appropriate consideration for any policy decisions taken regarding the improvement of mobile connectivity here.Mobile infrastructure is currently provided on a wholly commercial basis by Mobile Network Operators (MNOs), however, my officials are involved in a number of initiatives which will help improve mobile connectivity here. These include the Mobile Action Plan and the Barrier Busting Taskforce which aims to identify the main issues that impact on the roll-out of mobile technologies and provide solutions to remove barriers that prevent the fast and efficient deployment of mobile connectivity here. In addition, my Department worked with the DSIT led Shared Rural Network (SRN) Programme on local delivery. SRN is a project DSIT developed with the four main MNOs. SRN aims to deliver 95% 4G coverage and enable rural businesses and communities to thrive. More information about the SRN can be found at www.srn.org.uk and Mobile coverage obligations - Ofcom. MNOs are willing to provide you with more specific information on request.My officials will continue to engage with and support relevant stakeholders with a view to improving mobile coverage here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail: (i) whether any individual or estate surveys have been carried out in relation to complaints of “cold wall” in the Bowtown estate, Newtownards in the last ten years; (ii) how many of these have taken place; and (iii) the results of these surveys.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘For the purposes of this query the Housing Executive has interpreted ‘cold walls’ to include complaints of mould, damp, condensation and/or where there has been mention of cavity walls. We have interpreted ‘complaints’ to be repair requests from tenants in this context. A search has been carried out of our housing management system for the period 1/4/2014-31/3/2025 for repair requests including these words.To inform the last external cyclical maintenance scheme in Bowtown estate (2020), surveys were carried out of all properties in 2019 to assess the elemental condition of roofs, external walls, windows, eaves, fascias, soffits, rainwater goods and chimneys. The survey findings informed the content of the scheme work which followed. These surveys did not find any significant issues with cold walls, insulation, damp, or structural concerns.Over the ten-year period, the Response Maintenance team has commissioned 44 independent surveys of individual properties to help inform responses to specific repair requests from tenants. 42 of these related to dampness and 2 to cavity walls. Recommendations from these surveys were actioned through the response maintenance service to address the problems. One formal Stage 1 complaint was lodged. Response Maintenance carried out an extraction of Cavity Wall Insulation and refill in 2020 and no further issues have been reported.’

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any interventions which have been undertaken in the last ten years to address complaints of “cold wall” in the Bowtown estate, Newtownards.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘For the purposes of this query the Housing Executive has interpreted ‘cold walls’ to include complaints of mould, damp, condensation and/or where there has been mention of cavity walls. We have interpreted ‘complaints’ to be repair requests from tenants in this context. A search has been carried out of our housing management system for the period 1/4/2014-31/3/2025 for repair requests including these words.Over the ten year period, 325 repair reports from tenants on mould, damp, condensation or cavity wall issues have been dealt with through response maintenance. This level of repairs activity is broadly in line with the NI average for these issues. As such, the Housing Executive has not identified a requirement to address issues of ‘cold walls’ in Bowtown estate.One formal Stage 1 complaint was lodged. Response Maintenance carried out an extraction of Cavity Wall Insulation and refill in 2020 and no further issues have been reported.’

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of  complaints of “cold wall”  that have been made in relation to properties in the Bowtown estate, Newtownards in the last ten years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘For the purposes of this query the Housing Executive has interpreted ‘cold walls’ to include complaints of mould, damp, condensation and/or where there has been mention of cavity walls. We have interpreted ‘complaints’ to be repair requests from tenants in this context. A search has been carried out of our housing management system for the period 1/4/2014-31/3/2025 for repair requests including these words.A total of 561 repair requests were received by the Housing Executive by tenants for mould, damp, condensation or cavity wall issues during the last 10 years. Of these, 236 were cancelled (158 due to failed access and 78 where no action was required by the Housing Executive), meaning 325 were actioned. These 325 actions relate to 147 distinct properties. One formal Stage 1 complaint was lodged. Response Maintenance carried out an extraction of Cavity Wall Insulation and refill in 2020 and no further issues have been reported.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will extend the period of consultation for the Nutrient Action Programme for 2026 – 2029.
answer_text: On 16 May I announced that I am extending the consultation period for the Nutrients Action Programme (NAP) 2026 – 2029 by 4 weeks.  I recognise that the proposals in the revised NAP have a level of complexity because of the detailed science and evidence underpinning them. I have listened to the concerns from stakeholders about the need for more time to consider the proposals and have extended the consultation period until 24 July.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider introducing legislation to give local planning authorities the power to remove estate agent signage, in breach of planning regulations, similar to the powers granted to DfI Roads.
answer_text: While there is currently no corresponding provision within The Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (the 2011 Act) or The Planning (Control of Advertisements) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 (the Advertisement Regulations) similar to the Roads (NI) Order 1993, there are other statutory regimes providing legislative provisions to enable councils to remove estate agent signage.Section 175 of The Planning Act (Northern Ireland) provides councils with powers to deal with the enforcement of advertisement control.  This allows those landowners, occupiers and businesses contravening advertising regulations to be prosecuted, and if convicted, they may be liable to a fine not exceeding Level 4 on the standard scale (currently £2,500) and potentially a daily fine for ongoing offences.  Councils can also continue to exercise their powers under regulation 7 of the Planning (Control of Advertisement) Regulations (NI) 2015 which enables them to remedy what appears to be a substantial injury to the amenity of the locality or a danger to members of the public, by serving a discontinuance notice.As such, I consider there to be adequate provisions within current planning legislation to address this issue and I have no current plans to introduce additional provisions similar to the powers in The Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 8340/22-27, whether any further consideration has been given to bringing forward proposals to supplement existing provisions in Section 59 of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, which would disallow the variation of a development proposal at appeal.
answer_text: I want to see a good performing planning system in the North and am committed to working with all stakeholders to bring that about. This will include actioning the various recommendations set out in the first report on the Implementation of the Planning Act 2011, including the commitment to review the process for planning appeals.  The latter will however require policy development, engagement with the Planning Appeals Commission, public consultation and thereafter, primary legislative change. My Department has been working closely with Local Government and other key stakeholders through the Planning Improvement Programme to improve performance in the system. The programme has already delivered positive changes including legislation to enable council planning authorities to introduce statutory validation checklists to frontload and improve the quality of planning applications entering the system, and in turn reduce the potential for delay through the application process including at the appeal stage.Ongoing work with statutory consultees has already resulted in performance improvements and this engagement will continue. My officials are also currently engaging with individual council planning authorities and other key stakeholders in the planning system with a specific focus on identifying challenges in current development management (planning application process) practice and procedures as well as individual performance related matters, to inform further opportunities for efficiencies and improvements to the system.I will continue to monitor progress with my officials on the improvement programme, and other proposals aimed at improving the planning system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 22564/22-27, when will the new model licence conditions under the Caravans Act be published.
answer_text: I intend to publish updated Model Licence Conditions for caravan sites in June 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the progress being made to designate 2026 as the Northern Ireland Year of Food.
answer_text: I have no plans to designate 2026 as the Northern Ireland Year of Food. I was delighted to recently launch on behalf of my Executive colleagues, the Northern Ireland Food Strategy Framework Action Plan which sets out the Executive’s collaborative approach to food over the next two years. This details the practical food-related actions we are prioritising that benefit our health, economy and environment. It is my intention rather than focussing on a single Year of Food and Drink in 2026, to direct resources to a two-year programme of engagement to promote and support the implementation of the Food Action Plan. Given the long-term nature of the Food Strategy Framework I believe a partnership approach to promote the Framework and Action Plan across a range of key stakeholders including industry, community groups and local councils would be very helpful in establishing the enduring collaborative approach that will be necessary for success.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether there are any current proposals for public art projects; (ii) where these projects are; and (iii) what budget is available for each of these projects.
answer_text: In Bangor, my Department funded Ards and North Down Borough Council in March 2024 for an art installation for Bangor's Waterfront area. The total project cost is £1.287m, with DfC grant funding totalling £1.05m across 3 financial years.Furthermore, in Londonderry £97K of the public realm scheme currently under construction, and £49K of the forthcoming public realm scheme in Strabane relates to public art.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has plans to bring forward a decommissioning scheme to assist in the modernisation of the fishing fleet.
answer_text: I have no plans to bring forward a decommissioning scheme for the Northern Ireland Fishing fleet at this time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils who are: (i) attending independent schools; and (ii) being home schooled.
answer_text: The most recent school census figures collected in October 2024 showed there were 732 pupils enrolled in 13 independent schools.  The Education Authority (EA) has provided information as of the 15 April 2025 showing that there are 1,104 children it was informed of who were being electively home educated (EHE).  These figures may not reflect the entirety of children in Northern Ireland who are electively home educated. Parents are responsible for ensuring their child receives an education, at school or otherwise. Parents may therefore choose to educate their children at home rather than register them at a school and they may choose to deregister their child from school with the intention of educating them at home. Whilst the EA is aware of children who have attended and subsequently been deregistered from a school, there is no duty on parents to inform the Department of Education or the EA if they have chosen to educate their children at home. Thus, there is no complete record of children being electively home educated in Northern Ireland. This is the case in other jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 22565/22-27, whether there have been any discussions between the Department, Libraries NI and Ards and North Down Borough Council around an indicative timeline for the delivery of a new library for Newtownards.
answer_text: Libraries NI continue to work collaboratively with Ards and North Down Borough Council on the redevelopment of Newtownards Library. A four year development/delivery programme is anticipated, subject to approval of the business case and securing the necessary capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7194/22-27, whether any progress has been made on the outstanding recommendations following the review of restraint and seclusion in educational settings.
answer_text: In terms of recommendation 1, I plan to consider the matter of repealing Article 4(1)(c) in the context of revised guidance.Recommendations 2, 3 and 5 relate to the publication of statutory guidance on the use of restrictive and supportive practices in educational settings. In late 2023, my department held a consultation on draft statutory guidance on the Reduction and Management of Restrictive Practices in Educational Settings. Following this, officials established a Task and Finish Group which includes practitioners, health professionals and child rights advocates to support the development of a revised version of the guidance. Progress has been challenging due to differing viewpoints provided through the consultation, within the Task and Finish Group and among wider stakeholders, and work on the revised draft has not progressed as quickly as initially expected as a result. To address some of the key issues, a workshop was held on 16th June with representatives from child rights organisations, health and education.  Work on the revised guidance is moving forward as officials reflect on the constructive and informative feedback shared during the workshop.On recommendation 4, my department is progressing an update to the ‘Supporting pupils with Medication Needs’ Guidance. In respect of guidance on the appropriate and safe use of recommended equipment to enable the child or young person to access the curriculum, a photographic quick reference guide has been developed by the Public Health Agency regionally for use, commencing within Occupational Therapy. This has been developed as an explicit photographic guide and will be available for classroom and educational staff, as a quick reference overview of the safe and appropriate use of specialist equipment, specifically within that setting. As this guidance depends upon a digital interface with HSC Trust, its implementation will be in line with the regional roll out of the Encompass programme.  Recommendation 6, which relates to the Department commissioning a periodic review and publishing a report on the use of restrictive practices in educational settings, can only be progressed once the statutory guidance is in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the New Decade, New Approach commitment to review arm's-length bodies.
answer_text: ANSWERDepartments will have been conducting their own reviews of their ALBs, in line with the New Decade, New Approach commitment.  The findings of those departmental reviews will inform next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 20199/22-27, (i) to outline the reasons for the delay in publishing the Strategic Planning Policy Statement (SPPS) on renewable and low carbon energy; and (ii) for an indicative date when this SPPS will be published.
answer_text: (i) A Draft Executive Paper was first circulated to Executive colleagues seeking their agreement to publish the final policy in September 2024. A Version 2 paper issued in December 2024; a Version 3 paper issued in January 2025; and a Version 4 paper issued in February 2025. On 2 April 2025 I wrote to my Executive Colleagues on the need for the paper to be permitted on to the Executive agenda so that decision can be taken on it. On 13 May  2025 I wrote to the First Minister and Deputy First Minister on the matter. I remain hopeful that it will make the agenda for the next Executive Committee meeting on the 29 May 2025.(ii) The final Policy will be published as soon as possible following Executive Committee agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 11482/22-27, to list (i) the (a) primary legislation; and (b) secondary legislation drafted by her Department that has not yet been introduced or made; and (ii) the year that each was drafted.
answer_text: The Disclosure Of Victims’ And Witnesses Information (Prescribed Bodies) (Amendment) Regulations were drafted in 2024 but have not been made to date as queries raised by the Information Commissioner’s Office are being addressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether consideration will be given to moving the 30mph speed limit on the Comber Road, Ballygowan, to include Olivet Court and the Platinum Jubilee Park.
answer_text: My Department’s policy for setting the appropriate speed limit for a road aims to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads taking into account factors such as: the characteristic of the road; the speed of existing traffic; the degree of frontage development; and road traffic collision data, which is continuously monitored using data provided by the PSNI.A review of injury collision data shows there has been no recorded injury collisions on this part of the Comber Road, Ballygowan within the last three years. The current speed limits on the Comber Road are in line with our policy document and considered to be appropriate.However, I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominantly residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.  I have also asked that consideration be also given to the national speed limits that apply on rural roads and HGV speed limits.To ensure that other’s views are considered in the setting of speed limits here, I plan to hold a public consultation exercise later this year.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline (i) when the decision was taken not to record the names of all visitors to Parliament Buildings; (ii) the rationale for this; and (iii) if this will be reviewed, given the potential security and health and safety risks of not knowing who or how many people are in the building at a given time.
answer_text: The decision to remove the requirement to record the names of visitors to Parliament Buildings was taken in July 2024 by management, following careful consideration and discussion and in parallel to the introduction of a Visitor Experience Team to carry out reception duties in the Great Hall.Whilst historically the names of visitors had been recorded, this was of very limited use or value from a security standpoint, as visitors not attending pre-planned events were not required to produce valid identification upon arrival at Parliament Buildings. The need to balance security against the retention of personal data was also a consideration in those circumstances, given that any visitor names recorded would remain on a database for up to 90 days.Health and safety, particularly as regards evacuation, was also considered however it was determined that recording visitor names did not produce a conclusive or entirely reliable list of building occupants for use by management or the emergency services at any given time, since not all occupants, particularly Members, were routinely recorded upon entry. On exiting Parliament Buildings in an evacuation, any visitors would also be free to disperse as they so wished, with emergency services subsequently carrying out their own checks of the building.As with all operational procedures, this will be kept under review.
