This funding is supplied to a single partner, currently Advice NI, to operate the NI Debt Advice service across Northern Ireland.  It is not possible to break down this allocation into council areas.  However, the full allocation for the last five years is outlined below. 2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25NI Debt Advice Service£1,402,906.00£1,619,227.22£1,458,600.00£1,534,284.00£1,715,071.00

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of his Department's budget that has been allocated to tree planting in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: My Department has budgeted £2.11m for planting new woodlands in the 2025/26 financial year.Additionally, £1.9m has been budgeted for spend in 2025/26, to meet continuing grant commitments for woodlands planted in previous years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how long the Virtual Officer Attendance pilot will last.
answer_text: The Virtual Officer Attendance pilot is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct your question to the PSNI for a response.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount being spent on the Virtual Officer Attendance pilot.
answer_text: The management of resources within the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), including expenditure on the launch of the Virtual Officer Attendance pilot, is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI for a response.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there will be a review of the tuition fee cap in the higher education sector.
answer_text: I recognise the financial challenges raised by the universities in their recent letter to the local party leaders. I also recognise the financial pressures that students are under given the challenges posed by the cost-of-living crisis.	I am committed to working closely with all stakeholders, including the universities and student representatives, to address the funding challenges that we face in a way that is both fair and sustainable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 13654/22-27, whether any discussions have been held with (i) her Department; or (ii) arm's-length bodies, around (a) funding; or (b) other support for the centenary of the Ards TT races in 2028.
answer_text: I understand that Ards & North Down Borough Council are working on plans for the centenary celebrations of the Ards TT Races in August 2028.Tourism NI is responsible for the administration of tourism events funding and support within my Department. The event funding mechanisms that will be in place for 2028 are not yet known, however Tourism NI can also provide a range of other support opportunities including marketing and promotion.Tourism NI representatives met with officers from Ards and North Down Borough Council in October 2024 for an initial discussion on the event and are happy to continue discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the land that DfI Roads has deemed as surplus to requirements in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: Detailed below is land/property within the Strangford area that the Department has declared surplus to requirements. These areas of land are currently in the process of being disposed of, in accordance with the Department of Finance’s ‘Disposal of Surplus Public Sector Property in Northern Ireland’ guidelines.LocationTownLand at 1 Newton Grange, Donaghadee RoadNewtownards20 Millstreet, BT23 4JENewtownards70 Killinchy Road, BT23 5LXComberLands adjacent to 1-8 Talbot StreetNewtownards

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) what objections DfI Roads has to the cycle path along the Comber to Newtownards carriageway being used as part of the extension of the Comber Greenway, after this was previously suggested by her Department; and (ii) what steps are being taken to resolve this issue.
answer_text: As part of the planning process, my officials raised safety concerns in relation to a section of the proposed Comber to Newtownards greenway that runs adjacent to the A21 dual carriageway. In discussion with Ards and North Down Borough Council the planning application was amended and planning permission was granted earlier this year to enable other sections of the project to progress while the project team develop a suitable design solution for the remaining section.My officials continue to positively engage with Ards and North Down Borough Council to find a suitable design solution for the A21 section that satisfies planning and road safety requirements. A capital funding letter of offer of up to £2.4m was issued to the Council and my Department remains committed to this funding to support project delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 20200/22-27, for an update on the Hightown Incinerator planning application.
answer_text: The applicant submitted further information to my department on 16 April 2025 for consideration. My officials are continuing to undertake the necessary administrative processes, including assessing consultation advice from stakeholders and public authorities. When all statutory processes are satisfactorily completed, a recommendation will be brought forward by my officials; however, at this time I cannot confirm when a recommendation will be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any plans in place to tackle black mould and damp in public and private rented houses.
To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of whether there is any conflict in the civil service and other public bodies funding lobby groups for training and resources.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans she has to increase the number of electric vehicle charge points in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: The development of publicly accessible Electric Vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity, the provision of which rests solely with Charge Point Operators (CPOs). Individual CPOs are best placed to advise on their plans to install further charge points within their wider networks. There has been significant investment from CPOs in EV infrastructure over the last two years. The commercial publicly accessible charge point network now has 672 chargers in place here, a growth in EV charging provision of 73% since April 2023. The Rapid/Ultra Rapid charging public network, which offers the fastest, most convenient method of charging has grown by over 400% here in the same period.   My Department has committed over £470,000 match funding to support applications from Councils to the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme. Individual Councils are best placed to advise on the number of additional charge points this will provide in their area.My officials continue to engage with officials from the British Government’s Office for Zero Emission Vehicles on grants and funding available for EV Infrastructure here. I am pleased to confirm that the north has now had over 1,000 workplace charge points installed through the Workplace Charging Scheme. These charge points provide additional options for charging to EV owners and further compliment the growing publicly accessible charge point network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what payments are outstanding for hospital treatment administered to EU nationals in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of complaints The Charity Commission for Northern Ireland has received in relation to the activities of charities, in each of the last five years; (ii) how many of these complaints have been investigated; and (iii) in how many cases wrongdoing was discovered.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Charity Commission Northern Ireland (CCNI). I have sought the requested information, and the Commission has provided the following breakdown:YearNo. of concerns about charities receivedConcerns closed with no further actionConcerns which led to a self- regulatory caseConcerns which led to a regulatory caseConcerns which led to a statutory inquiry case202413330220202316653101020221318420532021112662871202010583961The Commission does not categorise cases as ‘wrongdoing’.  The regulatory response to concerns spans a wide range of outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the resurfacing on (i) Ardmore Grange; and (ii) The Granary, Ballygowan, will commence.
answer_text: Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has any plans to conduct a review of jury service.
answer_text: The Department intends to conduct a review of jury policy and has commenced early work to scope and determine the full range of issues requiring review.  It is anticipated these will include the jury-related recommendations made by Sir John Gillen in his report into the law and procedures in serious sexual offences in Northern Ireland, along with some recommendations made by Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland on the need for jury directions in relation to particular types of offences, and the issue of juror expenses. These will be the subject of a public consultation exercise which I hope to commence before the end of the current mandate, once resource becomes available to take this work forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment on the status of the horse mussel population in Strangford Lough.
answer_text: The designated feature Horse mussel, Modiolus modiolus, reef in Strangford Lough was assessed as ‘Unfavourable – recovering’ in 2019 as part of the Strangford Lough Special Area of Conservation (SAC) condition assessment. This is an improvement from the previously reported “Unfavourable- declining” status.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether DfI Roads has any plans for further road safety measures on the A20 Newtownards to Portaferry road.
answer_text: I am advised that the current speed limit has been reviewed based on records of existing speeds along this stretch of road, which has determined that the extension of the existing 40mph speed at this site would not provide tangible road safety benefits at this time. However, I have asked officials to review this again should further development of the area adjacent to Teal Rocks be undertaken.I am pleased to advise that my Department has completed approximately 1km of resurfacing between Newtownards and Teal Rocks to improve surface quality.  These works also included a new bus layby and footway opposite Strangford Filling Station as well as a crossing point for pedestrians which provides a safer facility for vulnerable road users to access public transport. Furthermore, a minor works scheme involving footway widening between Old Shore Road and Teal Rocks was completed in February 2025 which provides a safer route for pedestrians.  We will also be installing a replacement enhanced bend warning sign for motorists travelling in a northerly direction approaching Teal Rocks as soon as resources allow.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans he has to introduce legislation for the licensing of (i) animal sanctuaries; (ii) rehoming charities; and (iii) dog pounds.
answer_text: A key component of my upcoming Animal Welfare Pathway will involve bringing forward legislation to mandate the inspection and licensing of any organisation providing a rescue and rehoming service. This is likely to include animal sanctuaries and charities which find homes for unwanted animals. The Dogs Order (Northern Ireland) 1983 contains powers for councils to establish and maintain dog pounds. Where a council chooses to utilise a boarding kennel provider as a dog pound, that kennelling provider must have been inspected and licenced by my Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the steps his Department is taking to introduce Lucy’s Law to regulate the third-party sale of puppies.
answer_text: My officials are working to finalise a consultation document which will seek views on the introduction of a version of Lucy’s Law in Northern Ireland. It is my intention to launch the public consultation by the end of June.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the grass verge cutting schedule within the Ards and North Down Borough Council area.
answer_text: It would be my intention for my Department to deliver a programme of grass cutting in the Ards and North Down Borough Council area in accordance with the current verge management policy. I can confirm that grass cutting operations in the Borough commenced on 6th May 2025 and should take around 8 weeks to complete, subject to weather conditions.In addition to the programmed work, additional cuts at sightlines on bends and at junctions will also be undertaken as required throughout the season to maintain visibility for road users and in the interests of road safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what measures are (i) in place; and (ii) proposed by his Department, to address illegal puppy breeding.
answer_text: Currently, the Welfare of Animals (Dog Breeding Establishments and Miscellaneous Amendments) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2013 mandates that dog breeding establishments must be licensed by the relevant local district council. Councils can prosecute unlicensed establishments or those violating the regulations.To identify whether there are any potential gaps in this legislation, my Department has recently launched a recruitment competition for an Expert Advisory Group. The Group, when established, will be tasked with conducting a thorough review of the regulations and their implementation in Northern Ireland. This Group will engage with key stakeholders, including local councils in their statutory enforcement capacity, to collect pertinent evidence that will inform its report and recommendations for my consideration.My Department also chairs the Paws for Thought campaign, a multi-agency initiative aimed at addressing the issues of breeding and trafficking low welfare puppies.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what plans his Department has to undertake maintenance or improvement works to (i) Scrabo Tower; and (ii) the site around the monument, in the 2025-26 financial year.
answer_text: I have allocated funding to this project which will extend across the 2025/26 and 2026/27 financial years. Historic Environment Division officials are currently working in conjunction with the Department of Finance Construction & Procurement Delivery engineers and architects on the design of a new rainwater management system and improvements to the windows at Scrabo Tower to address water ingress issues. Once this work is complete and statuary approvals obtained, conservation works to resolve the issues will be undertaken. These will require new windows and comprehensive repairs to the wall walk to enable the interior to be made safe for public access.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the weed spraying schedule for Ards and North Down Borough Council area.
answer_text: The Department has no statutory obligation to remove weeds, other than in relation to the control of noxious weeds, however there are policies and procedures in place in relation to weed control, aimed at ensuring the safety of road users and preventing the deterioration of the road pavement. The Department will also treat invasive species such as Japanese Knotweed found growing within areas for which it has responsibility.I can confirm that the weed control programme in the Ards and North Down area commenced 29th April 2025.   The weed control schedule consists of all footways and carriageways in the Borough which are maintained by the Department. As the operation is weather dependent, the schedule is constantly reviewed and updated to take account of associated impacts.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will introduce legislation to amend the Traffic Management (Northern Ireland) Order 2005 to permit local councils to implement their own parking strategies on council owned off-street car parks, without reference or action by her Department.
answer_text: I am aware of a legislative anomaly pertaining to the ability of Local Councils to set their own off-street parking tariffs under The Off-Street Parking Order (Northern Ireland) 2000. My officials are engaging with representatives from the Councils in order to progress a legislative solution, using powers in the Local Government Act 2014, that would disapply the provisions of the 2000 Order for Councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the development of the residents’ parking schemes in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: I can advise that officials from the Department are currently working to develop an application and assessment process for residents’ parking schemes to ensure that any requests can be appropriately considered. This process will take into account the findings and recommendations of the review of residents’ parking published on the Department’s website on 29 November 2024, and will provide a streamlined approach for considering, assessing and prioritising requests going forward. It is my intention to make an announcement as to how future schemes will be assessed by my Department over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there is any legal impediment to the disposal by local councils of off-street car parks transferred to local councils under the Off-street Parking (Functions of District Councils) Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: Ownership of Off-Street Car Parks and responsibility for provision of Off-Street Car Parking 	spaces transferred to the local councils on 1 April 2015, by virtue of the Local Government 	Act (NI) 2014 and Transfer Schemes set up for each Council pursuant to the 2014 Act. 		Off-Street Car Parks are therefore assets belonging to the local councils and my Department is not aware of any legal impediment preventing a council considering their disposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to  AQW 22562/22-27, to detail (i) when the next housing condition survey will be carried out; and (ii) the frequency housing conditions surveys are conducted.
answer_text: (i) The fieldwork for the next Northern Ireland House Condition Survey will be carried out in 2028.  (ii) The Northern Ireland House Condition Survey is generally carried out every five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the grass verge cutting schedule for the Ards and North Down Borough Council area.
answer_text: It would be my intention to deliver a programme of grass cutting in accordance with the new approach to verge management across the road network announced in October 2022.  The new approach increases focus on protecting wildlife and promoting biodiversity, without compromising on roads safety and will support the actions needed to comply with the Climate Change Act passed by the Assembly in 2022.I can confirm that grass cutting operations in the Ards and North Down Borough commenced on 6th May 2025 and should take around 8 weeks to complete.In addition to the programmed work, additional cuts at sightlines on bends and at junctions will also be undertaken as required throughout the season to enhance visibility for road users and therefore improve overall road safety.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail progress being made to establish a single agri-food marketing organisation for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I understand that in late 2019 to early 2020 consensus was not reached in relation to establishing a new industry-led agri-food marketing body in the North at that time.  However, should a new industry-led agri-food sustainability body be established to drive environmental sustainability in the North, as recommended as part of the Kendall Review, there is potential for industry to market our food on its world class sustainability credentials.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a timeline for the delivery of support to the sheep sector.
answer_text: The Future Agricultural Policy Decisions for Northern Ireland, announced by my predecessor in March 2022, did not include proposals for sheep support at that stage but indicated that work would be undertaken to explore options for support that would improve the overall performance and resilience of the sheep sector.     On 29 January 2025 I announced the timeline for the roll out of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme over the next two years (subject to necessary business case cover and legislation in place). While I wish to see a sheep support scheme added to the SAP’s remit, delivering on the published SAP timeline is now the key focus for the resources available, particularly in relation to the Farming with Nature Package, which is my immediate priority. As the Farming with Nature Package and other schemes included in the current timeline move towards delivery, I will ensure that resource is diverted to progress co-design work on sheep sector support including development and approval of policy, business case and any required legislation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: Information on the number of defendants dealt with at courts, where the there was a conviction for at least one offence under animal welfare legislation, has been provided for the period 2020 – 2024, the most recent five-year period for which information is available.Convictions at courts for animal welfare offences, 2020 - 2024YearConvictions202044202162202249202359202450Note:1. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of new social homes that have been built or acquired in the Strangford constituency in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 26428/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on legislation for mandatory court approval of minor settlements following the consultation carried out in 2021.
answer_text: I do not intend to bring forward legislation for mandatory court approval of compensation settlements for children reached before the issue of legal proceedings in the current Assembly mandate, but the department remains open to doing so in future. While there was strong support for court approval of all minor settlements in my Department’s 2021 consultation, and I am in favour of doing so, detailed policy consideration highlighted evidential and practical difficulties with legislating at this time.I have asked my officials to consider next steps, including commissioning further research as well as exploring non-legislative alternatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 17547/22-27, to detail the amount of financial support his Department has budgeted for to assist agricultural shows in 2025.
answer_text: Provision of funding for local shows is not a statutory obligation under the Agricultural Act 1949 (NI), and opportunities for financial support in the form of discretionary sponsorship (as per Section 12 of the Act) are extremely limited.Under the NI Regional Food Programme (NIRFP), which provides funding for the promotion of local food and drink at regional fairs and exhibitions, a total of £36,939.68 has recently been awarded to Antrim, Armagh, Ballymoney, Castlewellan and Clogher shows.  Any further potential support will be to cover costs associated with my department’s exhibits at agricultural shows during the 2025 show season.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: Information on the number of cases where a custodial disposal was handed down following a conviction for at least one offence under animal welfare legislation, for cases dealt with at courts in 2020 – 2024, has been provided in the following table. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of vacant domestic properties in the Strangford constituency in each of the last five years.
answer_text: ANSWERThe table below provides the number of vacant domestic properties in the Strangford Parliamentary Constituency at 31st March, in each of the last five rating years.Rating YearNo. of vacant Domestic properties2020/219742021/229372022/239382023/249612024/25998The member should note that the numbers presented may not accurately reflect all vacant domestic properties in the Strangford constituency. Since 2011, rates are payable at 100% of a property’s rateable value, and as such, ratepayers are not required to inform Land & Property Services when a property becomes vacant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when the last diabetes audit was carried out; and (ii) when the next audit is due to be carried out.
answer_text: Audits were undertaken at a regional, local Trust and general practice level in the development of the Diabetes Strategic Framework, published in 2016.  The Northern Ireland Audit Office completed the Type 2 diabetes prevention and care report in 2018.  Available data on diabetes treatment and care has been considered in the development of the Regional Integrated Pathway for Diabetes, which has been co-produced with stakeholders over the past three years.The Diabetes Strategic Framework (2016) included an action to participate in National Diabetes Audit, similar to the Diabetes Audit used in England and Wales. The Department of Health remains committed to working towards participation in the National Diabetes Audit.  However, there have been significant challenges in progressing this recommendation, as the required infrastructure is not currently in place to participate fully in the audit. The full implementation of Encompass, currently being rolled out across all Health and Social Care Trusts, will enable the provision of regional data that is both available and extractable.  This will support participation in the National Diabetes Audit in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when is it anticipated that the review and update of the Highway Code will be completed.
answer_text: I am aware that in Britain a substantive update to the Highway Code was made in 2022. My department considers any initiatives that can improve road safety however it is imperative that my limited resources are concentrated on areas with the greatest evidence of positive impact.  My department published the Road Safety Strategy on 16 September 2024 and an associated Action Plan.Evidence on the impact of the changes to the Highway Code in Britain is not yet available to demonstrate whether these have been effective. It is therefore not currently prioritised within the Road Safety Strategy Action Plan. This will be kept under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of vacant commercial properties in the Strangford constituency in each of the last five years.
answer_text: ANSWERThe table below provides the number of vacant non-domestic properties in the Strangford Parliamentary Constituency at 31st March, in each of the last five rating years.Rating YearNo. of vacant Non-Domestic properties2020/214952021/224692022/234252023/244412024/25450

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any support his Department has provided to foodbanks, churches and charitable organisations to deal with poverty in the Strangford constituency in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department has provided funding to each local council to commission Social Supermarket projects since 2021/22. This is done via the provision of wraparound services, such as debt and benefits advice, budgeting, healthy eating, cooking on a budget and training and volunteering opportunities to enhance future employability alongside access to food.In 2020/21, in response to the Covid Pandemic, funding was allocated to local councils to enable grassroots community organisations to respond to emerging needs of those struggling with access to food and essential items. For both funding programmes, councils hold responsibility for commissioning delivery organisations and my Department is unable to disaggregate the levels of support organisations or individuals received in the Strangford parliamentary constituency. My Department’s Jobs & Benefits Offices provide a range of support to local communities including the Strangford constituency. This includes attending and participating in community events to support citizens in accessing and understanding benefit claims, help with finding and maintaining employment and signposting to other services and programmes as appropriate.In 2022/23 The Housing Executive provided a Community Involvement Grant to Bowtown Community Development Group of £1,590 for Christmas Support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the proposed Revised Operational Protocol permits an incremental reduction in emissions from existing development in the context of proposals for replacement buildings, aligning with the recently adopted Environmental Improvement Plan which makes commitments to improve the condition of protected sites.
answer_text: The proposed Revised Operational Protocol is designed to facilitate development proposals which are in line with strategic, site-specific reduction requirements.  I have instructed my officials to investigate the appropriateness of implementing pollution reduction plans, based on the strategic approach adopted by Natural England through Site Nitrogen Action Plans (SNAPs), in particular for European sites such as Special Areas of Conservation (SACs). SNAPs are specific to individual protected sites and are designed to identify key emission sources around the site, develop a pathway towards sustainable emission levels which support ecological recovery, and promote abatement and mitigation measures.  This would help to define the relative contributions of existing facilities to achieving emission reduction goals.  This will be an important mechanism to meet the ASSI favourable condition target in the recently adopted Environmental Improvement Plan.Where an existing development impacts an ASSI only, my Department may support an incremental reduction approach that reduces pollution from the project to acceptable levels. NIEA is applying this approach to reviews of Pollution Prevention and Control (PPC) permits, where it is appropriate. In order for NIEA to agree to the improvement plan, NIEA must be satisfied that there is certainty of the plan’s implementation, and it must be sufficiently ambitious within a time frame that is both achievable and that reduces pollution as soon as is practically possible to support ecological recovery.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of individuals that have applied for a waiver from the Charity Commission NI to be permitted to act as a charity trustee in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Charity Commission Northern Ireland (CCNI). question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail why the public register of removed trustees is currently unavailable on the Charity Commission NI website.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Charity Commission Northern Ireland (CCNI). I have sought the requested information from CCNI, who have advised me as follows:The “register of removed trustees” details individuals who have been removed by CCNI under section 33(2) of the Charities Act (Northern Ireland) 2008. CCNI have not removed any charity trustees under this provision, so the register remains empty and has not been published on the website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of trustees that have been barred from acting as charity trustees in each of the last ten years; and (ii) the grounds for each sanction.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Charity Commission Northern Ireland (CCNI). I have sought the requested information from CCNI, who has advised me as follows:There have not been any trustees barred by CCNI from acting as a charity trustee in the last ten years.Some people are disqualified from acting as charity trustees by law under section 86 of the Charities Act (Northern Ireland) 2008. CCNI does not hold the names of individuals who are disqualified in this way.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether land subject to vesting for the A5, which is currently subject to judicial review proceedings, is eligible for consideration when applications are made for farm payments for this forthcoming year.
answer_text: To be eligible to apply for farm-based subsidies, the eligible land on which a farm business undertakes agricultural activity must be at their disposal on 15 May 2025 and remain in agricultural use throughout the full calendar year.DAERA deem land to be removed from agricultural use once a fence or other barrier has been erected to prevent it being accessed by livestock and/or the farm business no longer has permission to use it for agricultural activity.The Department for Infrastructure confirmed to DAERA that it has issued no licences giving permission to former landowners to use the land vested in the 2025 scheme year for agricultural use, therefore the land is not eligible for payment.  As notification was in 2024 that no access would be given in 2025, force majeure does not apply in these circumstances.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any local councils that have written to him expressing an interest in discussing a potential scheme on the collection and recycling of nappy and absorbent hygiene waste.
answer_text: Since December 2024, four local councils have written to me regarding a potential collection and recycling scheme for nappies and absorbent hygiene products (AHPs) in Northern Ireland. The four councils are Ards & North Down Borough Council, Antrim & Newtownabbey Borough Council, Fermanagh and Omagh District Council and Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any progress that has been made since the Disposable Nappies: Collection and Recycling Scheme motion was passed in the Assembly on 27 January 2025.
answer_text: My officials have recently commenced work to consider the feasibility of establishing a separate collection scheme for nappies and other Absorbent Hygiene Products (AHPs) in NI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will commence Part 12 of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 to allow for the correction of errors in decision notices.
answer_text: I currently do not have any plans to commence Part 12 of the Planning Act.  Commencement in its current form would prove problematic in that it would affect the date on a decision document for which planning permission was granted or refused.  This would in turn lead to a revised date on a new decision notice which could have the unintended consequences of extending the permission, and the period for making an appeal. This matter was considered in the First Report on the Implementation of the Planning Act where the Department gave a commitment to making a minor technical amendment at an appropriate legislative opportunity to address this anomaly and subsequently commence Part 12. In the interim, a council may correct an error to any decision document administratively, without the commencement of Part 12, providing there is a clear and recorded audit trail of that correction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she has considered the introduction of legislation to exempt funeral limousines as Public Service Vehicle classification, in line with other jurisdictions in the UK.
answer_text: Funeral limousines are included as part of the Taxis Act 2008 (the Act) because they are regarded as taxis within the meaning of the Act, as they are vehicles which carry passengers for hire or reward, in the course of their business. This is not new but rather a continuation of regulation under previous legislation (Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1981). This means that they must undergo PSV testing and that drivers of such vehicles must hold a taxi driving licence. The aim of the legislation in place was to introduce a proportionate licensing regime which would strike the right balance between the important safeguards that have been put in place to maintain road and passenger safety and the commercial interests of funeral businesses. You may be aware that I have recently instructed officials to undertake a phased review of taxi policy and legislation, commencing with a review of the Class C taxi licensing system, which includes funeral  limousines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether capivasertib will be made available for the treatment of advanced breast cancer.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.On 11 April 2025, NICE published its final draft guidance recommending the use of capivasertib (Truqap®) plus fulvestrant, as an option for treating hormone receptor (HR)-positive HER2-negative breast cancer that has 1 or more PIK3CA, AKT1 or PTEN gene alterations and has spread within the breast tissue (locally advanced) or to other parts of the body (metastatic). Final guidance is expected on 15 May 2025.When the final guidance for this treatment is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the usual process for NICE Technology Appraisals as per the guidance set out in circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 which is available at:https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-hsc-sqsd-12-22.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of import licenses issued for exotic animals in Northern Ireland in the last five years, broken down by (i) type of animal; and (ii) location.
answer_text: Following the coming into force of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the import of exotic animals into Northern Ireland no longer sits within my ministerial responsibilities and is under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he plans to bring forward (i) a wildfire action plan; and (ii) a training course for farmers and landowners on controlled burning.
answer_text: The Environmental Improvement Plan has a target to develop action plans to address specific pressures on nature including wildfires by 2026. Although the target does not refer to a specific date in 2026, I have asked officials to ensure this plan is co-designed through the NI Wildfire Stakeholder Forum this financial year.The Department has no plans to bring forward a training course for farmers and landowners on controlled burning. I understand that such training courses are already available and delivered by others. However, I anticipate there will be a recommendation included in the proposed Wildfires in Northern Ireland a Strategic Way Forward to undertake a review of the availability of wildfire management training courses for landowners within NI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Environment Farming Scheme Higher Level allows for the controlled burning of land.
answer_text: The Environmental Farming Scheme (EFS) is a voluntary agri-environment scheme that supports farmers and land managers to carry out environmentally beneficial farming practices on agricultural land.  EFS has agreements that last 5 years and it has three levels.  EFS Higher is designed specifically for designated areas and priority habitats / species, EFS Wider offered options outside those areas, and EFS Group level provides support for co-operative work.  Heather regeneration by burning is an option available within the EFS Higher and EFS Group schemes. EFS Higher agreements are based on information specific to each site and designed to bring the area under favourable management.  Heather regeneration by burning may only be approved in limited circumstances with applicants required to submit additional information including a specialist plan, a wildfire management plan, and evidence from the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service, to support their application. To date this option has not been approved as part of an EFS Agreement.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures his Department is taking to reduce fire load in designated sites, such as the Mournes, in light of the current restrictions which result in increased fire load.
answer_text: The most effective long-term sustainable solution for addressing wildfire risk in designated peatland sites such as in the Mournes, is to return these areas to fully functioning ecosystems and removing those factors that can cause degradation. Re-wetting and restoring will naturally remove the higher fuel load from degraded peatland vegetation and reduce wildfire risk.To help achieve this goal and reduce wildfire risk, my Department has developed a number of Conservation Management Plans and is providing support for the development, procurement and implementation of Wildfire Management Plans. Specific measures within these plans can include reducing fuel load in specific locations as currently takes place in the Eastern Mournes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when the last five meetings of the NI Wildfire Stakeholder Forum took place.
answer_text: The NI Wildfires Stakeholder Forum has met 3 times to date. It met on 14 January 2022, 07 March 2022 and again on 08 September 2022.These meetings were to brief the group and seek feedback on proposals regarding the development and content of the Wildfires in NI a Strategic Way Forward. The draft Strategic Way Forward was issued for public consultation in March 2023. The NI Wildfires Strategic Group then considered consultation responses received to inform any required revisions to the draft Strategic Way Forward. Input from the Forum was not required for this task. I intend to bring this document to the Executive for its consideration shortly. After the Strategic Way Forward is agreed, the NI Wildfires Stakeholder Forum will be reconvened and tasked with developing and initiating an Action Plan to deliver the recommended actions outlined in the Strategy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any representations he or his officials have made on behalf of the Northern Ireland organic egg sector in relation to the maintenance of the derogation on feed composition regulations to ensure Northern Ireland organic eggs are not at a competitive disadvantage in the UK market.
answer_text: Following the coming into force of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the regulations on organic production and the labelling of organic products no longer sit within my ministerial responsibilities and now come under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.I have received two letters on this matter, one which I have redirected to the Department of Environment, Food and Rural Affairs for response and the other one which will follow shortly.My Department is also not the competent authority for this matter. The policy area of Organic regulation has always been UK wide and the competent authority role falls to the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs.When this issue first arose in 2022, my Department facilitated meetings with the local poultry and feed industry and the Competent Authority.  All subsequent contact has been made directly between the local industry and the Competent Authority, and through the representative Organic control bodies.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures being taken by her Department to address the ongoing problems with parking in Conway Square, Newtownards.
answer_text: I am advised that the limited waiting and disabled parking bays at the south side of Conway Square are enforced 3 to 4 times a day, Monday to Saturday inclusive.  This continued enforcement ensures a high turnover of vehicles and increased footfall for nearby shops and businesses.Since the completion of the Environmental Improvement Scheme in 2016, my Department has been unable to enforce parking in the pedestrianised area of Conway Square as it has been unclear which areas are adopted into the local road network due to the new layout.My officials continue to engage with their counterparts in Ards and North Down Borough Council to discuss ownership, boundary rectification and maintenance responsibilities relating to Conway Square, Newtownards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any current plans for her Department to issue an abandonment order for Conway Square, Newtownards.
answer_text: My officials continue to engage with their counterparts in Ards and North Down Borough Council to discuss ownership, boundary rectification and maintenance responsibilities relating to Conway Square, Newtownards.At present there are no plans for my Department to issue an abandonment order for Conway Square, Newtownards.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 10955/22-27, to detail the progress being made in the diversion of some motoring offences and other low level offences from the formal court process to reduce demand on the system.
answer_text: My department is continuing to work with justice partners to expand the use of Out of Courts disposals to, where appropriate, divert more low-level offending behaviour away from courts to help reduce pressure on the wider criminal justice system.  This work, which is one of the five workstreams of the Speeding up Justice Programme, is led by Criminal Justice Board.  A number of measures have been introduced, such as, the introduction of a new careless driving penalty which includes a fixed penalty notice of £100 and three penalty points for careless driving offences;broadening the range of cases for which a Community Resolution Notice (CRN) can be issued; andchanges to the PSNI and Public Prosecution Service (PPS) process in relation to the handling of files where there is a PSNI recommendation not to prosecute, streamlining the process and freeing capacity.      Work to bring forward further changes is ongoing.  My officials are presently engaging with justice partners in relation to proposals to divert more low-level motoring offences from the court system.  This work will review further possible changes to Penalty Notices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when Part 5 of the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, pertaining to the creation of lasting powers of attorney which covers health and welfare decisions, will be commenced.
answer_text: The Mental Capacity Act (NI) 2016 (MCA), a piece of legislation brought forward by the Department of Health and the Department of Justice, provides a framework for people who lack capacity to make a decision for themselves and for those who now have capacity but wish to make preparations for a time in the future when they may lack capacity.The MCA has been implemented in a phased approach, with provisions relating to deprivation of liberty, research and money and valuables commenced to date. While there are currently no indicative timings for full commencement of the MCA, my Department is currently working collaboratively with Department of Justice officials to develop an updated implementation plan to better understand the resource need for full MCA implementation, which will help to guide future Departmental decision making, including that of these provisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to remove barriers and delays within the adoption process.
answer_text: The current framework for adoption in Northern Ireland is set out primarily within the Adoption (Northern Ireland) Order 1987 and associated regulations and guidance.  The Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 (the Act) will, when implemented, reform the legislative framework and procedural arrangements governing adoption in Northern Ireland and introduce a range of new measures aimed at improving the adoption process by making it as efficient and robust as possible, reducing unnecessary delay and uncertainty for children.The Act changes the process of adoption itself and places the needs and welfare of children at the centre of the adoption process.   The Act will introduce a new pre-adoption order, a “Placement Order”, which will replace the current system of Freeing Orders. Doing so will enable parents to retain parental responsibility until a later stage in the adoption process and should lead to fewer contested proceedings. The arrangements for contact will be strengthened with the introduction of new pre and post adoption contact orders and children will be empowered to make or influence decisions about contact.  It places a duty on adoption agencies to make arrangements for the provision of adoption support services, according to arrangements to be set out in regulations. Those involved in the adoption process will have a new right to request an assessment of their needs for adoption support services and authorities will be required to provide support services which have been assessed as needed to certain categories of people. These measures to improve adoption support are intended to encourage more people to come forward to adopt and to help adoptive placements to succeed. To build confidence in the adoption approval process, the Act includes powers enabling the Department to establish an independent mechanism to review adoption agency determinations relating to the suitability of individuals to adopt. The Act also contains powers to enable some adoption functions or services to be delivered on a regional basis which could promote greater efficiency, more equal access to services and consistency of service user experience.The Act also includes measures intended to tackle delays in the adoption process. It introduces a statutory principle that, in general, delay is likely to prejudice the child’s welfare and a requirement for courts to draw up timetables for resolving adoption cases without delay, and to give directions to ensure the timetable is adhered to. The aim is to reduce the drift and unnecessary delay that prolongs instability for children and families when adoption would be in a child’s best interests.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any financial support available for essential therapeutic services for adoptive families.
answer_text: The framework for financial support for adoptive families is set out in the Adoption Allowance Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1996 (‘the 1996 Regulations’).  These Regulations give any adoption agency a power to pay allowances to secure a suitable adoption placement for children for whom adoption is not practicable (i.e. possible or achievable) without the payment of any allowance.  They aim to ensure that no child who needs an adoptive home should be denied such a home for financial reasons. The circumstances in which the adoption allowance may be paid are set out in the 1996 Regulations; these include placement of sibling groups, placement of a child who has physical, emotional or behaviour needs and requires additional care, or the placement of a child who will need additional care in the future due to illness or disability. The Adoption Panel, which makes the best interest decision in respect of the child and recommends the placement with his/her prospective adopters, recommends whether or not an allowance should be paid.  The Adoption Agency Decision Maker considers all available documentation and ratifies (or otherwise) the recommendation of the Adoption Panel in respect of placements and the payment of the adoption allowance.  Each adoptive family who receives an allowance has the discretion to utilise the financial support as required to meet the needs of the child in their care, to include funding for therapeutic services. The five HSC Trusts report to the Department of Health on a quarterly basis, for monitoring and performance management purposes, on the number of children subject to an Adoption Order and in receipt of an Adoption Allowance.On 30 September 2024, 376 households in Northern Ireland received an Adoption Allowance which equated to 520 children.Quarter ending: 30 September 2024Adoption allowance by householdAdoption Allowance by childBelfast Trust86106Northern Trust76122Southeastern Trust7286Southern Trust6894Western Trust74112Total:376520The Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 will provide a new framework for the assessment of the need for adoption support services.  The Act contains a power to make regulations about the carrying out of assessments, and these regulations will underpin the delivery of the new framework for adoption support, including financial support. In Northern Ireland, HSC Trust Therapeutic Teams for Looked After and Adopted Children provide support to young people and their families who require specific therapeutic support as part of an adoptive family. Further work on the nature and quantum of support required to meet needs will be undertaken in the context of implementing the Adoption and Children Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, which is currently being implemented on a phased basis, over a number of years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) who will be given priority in the provision of the around 2000 parking permits at the Royal Victoria Hospital; and (ii) the basis on which this will be determined.
answer_text: (i) To ensure that those staff with the greatest need are provided with a free on-site parking permit, the Department has requested that all HSC Trusts implement an assessment of demonstrable “need” for designated staff car parking spaces / permits. The assessment criteria takes into consideration individual circumstances, operational requirements, and the individual’s ability to access alternative and sustainable forms of transport.(ii) The Belfast HSC Trust will apply the following assessment criteria, to prioritise organisational need, when assessing staff parking permit allocation:Organisational Need - Carry out shift working as a requirement of the post and a set rota (scheduled rota to regularly start before 7:30am or finish after 7:30pm).  OR Required to use private vehicle on a daily basis to directly deliver/or provide critical support* to patient or client care as part of role. *a time critical response required where a delay or failure to do so is likely to lead to an adverse effect on patient care. ORRequired to participate on a 24 hour on call rota (excludes out of hours only on call). Staff to indicate on call frequency. AND Personal Circumstances Commute - A public transport journey that involves one or more changes and exceeds 1 hour of a commute. Allocation of staff car parking permits against the criteria combination above, including the outcome of any successful appeals, will be exhausted before commencing allocation against the criteria below: Organisational Need Provide Services on more than 1 site. ORManage services on more than 1 site. ANDCaring Responsibilities Parent/Guardian/Carer that uses car to transport children or provide caring responsibilities on way to or from base site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on what measures are to be introduced as part of a wildlife intervention to address bovine TB.
answer_text: In October 2023, a judicial review quashed my predecessor’s decision to advance a non-selective cull of badgers in targeted intervention areas as part of the Northern Ireland bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Eradication Strategy.  Consequently, my Department currently does not have a wildlife intervention policy. The newly established TB Partnership Steering Group is actively developing a new plan for bTB Eradication in Northern Ireland based on their consideration of the recently published Chief Veterinary Officer review of bTB here.  I expect that this plan will be agreed shortly and will also include further detail on next steps for any wildlife intervention proposals in Northern Ireland.In parallel, my officials continue to work on the issues and gaps identified by the judicial review ruling, in order to provide me with detailed advice on potential wildlife intervention options.  This involves looking at the latest scientific evidence; the potential legislative options relating to an intervention; and updating figures relating to how much these interventions could cost.  I anticipate having advice detailing options on this matter before summer.  Any decision taken on wildlife intervention must be science-led and would be subject to a full public consultation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when works will be undertaken to strengthen and resurface Killinchy Road, Comber.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am aware that my Department is monitoring the condition of Killinchy Road in Comber for the potential of a resurfacing scheme but will be subject to the availability of funding.  Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council. My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 17443/22-27, whether the assessment for traffic signals on the Comber Road, near Blenheim Drive, Newtownards, has now been carried out.
answer_text: Road safety remains an extremely high priority for my Department and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users. I am advised that an assessment at this location will be taken forward in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to widen Messines Road, Newtownards, as a means to address congestion in the area.
answer_text: My Department is aware that traffic can build up on the Messines Road, Newtownards for short periods at peak times which is consistent with traffic patterns and conditions in many of towns and cities across the North.There are no plans to widen Messines Road, however I am advised that road markings were previously installed as part of a traffic management scheme on the approach to the Comber Road Roundabout to aid traffic flow and supplementary road signage was erected to aid driver discipline.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of data breaches recorded by his Department and its arm’s-length bodies in each of the last five years, broken down by type; and (ii) any action taken as a result of these data breaches.
answer_text: The Department of Education (DE) and its arm’s-length bodies are required to report personal data breaches to the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO) based on an assessment of the potential adverse consequences for individuals, including the severity and likelihood of these consequences. If a breach is likely to result in a high risk of adversely affecting individuals’ rights and freedoms, those individuals must be notified without undue delay. Data breaches under UK GDPR can generally be classified into three categories:• Confidentiality Breach: Unauthorised or accidental disclosure of, or access to, personal data.• Integrity Breach: Unauthorised or accidental alteration of personal data.• Availability Breach: Unauthorised or accidental loss of access to, or destruction of, personal data. The Department also took forward any recommendations from the ICO following their investigation.Education Authority (EA)In response to each data breach the EA’s Information Governance team liaised with the relevant EA service to investigate how the incident occurred, how it was dealt with and what follow up action could be taken to reduce the risk of future similar breaches. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of data breaches recorded by his Department and its arm’s-length bodies in each of the last five years, broken down by type; and (ii) any action taken as a result of these data breaches.
answer_text: ANSWERMy Department has robust procedures in place to manage data breaches and incidents. In the last 5 years, my Department has had one reportable data breach, as a result of a system technical issue in 2021. Following consideration of the technical and organisation measures taken to immediately contain and rectify the issue, there was no further action taken by the Information Commissioner’s Office as a result.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of malicious emails to Northern Ireland Civil Service accounts that have been successfully blocked or detected in each of the last five years, broken down by type.
answer_text: The cyber threat for government organisations remains high. My Department has a range of cyber security technical controls and measures in place to detect and block malicious emails before they reach the Civil Service shared IT infrastructure.The specific information you have requested, if available in the public domain, could be utilised by an attacker to build up a picture of the cyber resilience of the Civil Service and as such it is best not to publicly disclose this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of data breaches recorded by her Department and its arm’s-length bodies in each of the last five years, broken down by type; and (ii) any action taken as a result of these data breaches.
answer_text: For my department, this is a nil response. It is also a nil response for some of my partner bodies but for those bodies that did have data breaches, the numbers are as follows: 2020/21 – 72021/22 – 22022/23 – 6 2023/24 – 2 2024/25 – 3This represents twenty notifiable data breaches across nine of the fourteen (Executive Non-Departmental Public) bodies. The data breach type is: Confidentiality – 18; Integrity – 1; and Availability – 1.Actions taken internally as a result of these data breaches included: internal investigations; apologies issued; training / refresher training undertaken; staff reminders issued; lessons learned reports produced; processes / procedures updated; and sectoral sharing of lessons learned. No enforcement action was taken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the process for a staff network or forum to be recognised in the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: The establishment of Civil Service staff networks is approved by the Civil Service Diversity Champions Network, informed by workforce data, staff feedback and best practice benchmarking.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the active staff networks and forums that are recognised and operational in the Northern Ireland Civil Service.
answer_text: There are currently six recognised Civil Service staff networks: Cancer Support Network; Disability Staff Network; LGBTQ+ Network; Race and Ethnicity Network; Student Network; and the Women’s Network.  Departments may have other staff forums; however, these are specific to the department and are not managed centrally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the reasons why disused medication would be collected from dispensing pharmacists and destroyed.
answer_text: As soon as a medicine leaves a pharmacy it cannot be reused or recycled. Healthcare settings, including community pharmacies must ensure that pharmaceutical waste is disposed of or destroyed in a safe and secure manner. This should be in accordance with legislation, regulations, and organisational policies or procedures.The legislative framework is summarised in NHS England’s Management and disposal of healthcare waste (HTM 07-01). This HTM is applicable in Northern Ireland. The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) advises that medicines should not be reused because, once they have left the pharmacy, quality cannot be guaranteed.  When medicines have left a pharmacy, how they have been handled and stored is unknown and, because of that, the quality, safety and how effective they may be cannot be guaranteed. There are also concerns over possible counterfeit medicines and possible risks of malicious tampering.  The Pharmaceutical Society of Northern Ireland Code of Ethics, June 2009, (Professional Standards and Guidance for the Sale and Supply of medicines, 1.1) states: “The pharmacist must ensure that the segregation and appropriate disposal of medicines returned to the pharmacy from a patient’s home, a care home or a similar institution must not be supplied to another patient.”

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether any consideration has been given to trialling a social media monitoring programme to manage online threats against MLAs, similar to the service initiated by the Scottish Parliament.
answer_text: It is unacceptable that anyone is subjected to online abuse, and the Assembly Commission takes very seriously its responsibility for the provision of support to Members in dealing with such behaviour.  On 31 March 2025, the Speaker addressed the Assembly on these issues, encouraged Members to report threats and said: “While many of the tools to tackle these issues lie beyond the role of the Speaker and the Assembly Commission, I can inform Members that there has been good, proactive engagement with the PSNI and we are engaging with the Electoral Commission.”A programme of social media and online security training for Members has been ongoing since 2021 and is continuing. In the past 12 months, the Assembly Commission’s Parliamentary Excellence Programme has provided sessions to support Members and their staff, including:Awareness of Personal Security session with the PSNI was delivered in April 2024;Cyber Security Session delivered in June 2024 by the Assembly Commission’s IS Office and PSNI; andBuilding Personal Resilience has been delivered on 5 occasions to Members and their staff in May, September, January and February.A Security and Safety Awareness ‘drop in’ session with the PSNI is scheduled for 8 April.As much of the abuse Members encounter online goes unreported, the Clerking and Member Support Office, in consultation with RaISe, have developed a survey of all Members to gauge their experience of online abuse. It is anticipated that the survey will be conducted after Easter recess. Assembly Commission officials are also liaising with their counterparts in the Scottish Parliament regarding their approach to social media monitoring for Members – a service which has now been permanently established.The provision of a social media monitoring service in the Assembly has not been factored into the Assembly Commission’s budget for 2025-26 and has not been included within the Corporate Plan. Nonetheless, officials intend to bring a paper for the Assembly Commission’s consideration in advance of summer recess, exploring the merits of conducting a similar pilot at the Assembly including the potential resource implications.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the introduction of creche and childcare provision for Members and staff.
answer_text: At its meeting on 5 November 2019, the Assembly Commission was advised that the then Permanent Secretary of the Department of Finance, had liaised with the Assembly’s Clerk/Chief Executive, to explore the feasibility of opening a pre-school childcare facility on the Stormont Estate. The intention was that such a facility could be used by Members, Members’ staff, Assembly Commission staff and Civil Service staff working on the Estate and conceptual proposals were developed.The Department had advised that, at the time, the feasibility of a childcare facility on the Stormont Estate could have been considered as a potential action for inclusion in a draft Childcare Strategy which was being developed by the Department of Education.  In November 2022, the Department informed the Assembly Commission that work on the Childcare Strategy had been paused due to the pandemic, and that it was unknown if the Strategy would now include the provision of a childcare facility, due to the significant changes of the Civil Service workforce within the Estate, notably with regards to Hybrid working.  Subsequently, the Department confirmed in September 2023 that it had no immediate plans for a childcare facility on the Stormont Estate.  While the Assembly Commission remained open to exploring cooperation on such a project, the viability of such proposal is questionable without other partners and the Assembly Commission did not consider the matter further.In October 2024, the Head of the Civil Service approached the Assembly’s Clerk/Chief Executive to discuss the concept of a workplace nursery on the Stormont Estate and subsequently convened a meeting with other potential stakeholders to discuss the matter further.At that meeting, the Assembly’s Clerk/Chief Executive outlined the work that had been undertaken previously in this regard and those conceptual proposals are being revisited.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether relugolix combination therapy has been approved for use to treat endometriosis symptoms in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department of Health has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals and NICE NI Formulary recommendations are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.In Northern Ireland, relugolix-estradiol-norethisterone is accepted for use, within its marketing authorisation, as an option for treating moderate to severe symptoms of uterine fibroids in adults of reproductive age in accordance with NICE TA832.NICE also recently recommended relugolix-estradiol-norethisterone for treating symptoms of endometriosis in adults of reproductive age who have had medical or surgical treatment for endometriosis.  NICE published final draft guidance on 13 March 2025 with the final published guidance expected 16 April 2025. The Strategic Planning and Performance Group within my Department are currently considering the latest NICE guidance as per normal process and it is anticipated that the treatment will be included as part of the suite of treatments available via the Managed Entry of New Medicines Process in the next few weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the water and pumping station agreements and bonds in the Strangford constituency, including (i) the length of time these have been in place; and (ii) the (a) initial; and (b) current value of these bonds.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:The storm and foul sewers in a private streets development are approved for adoption by NI Water, under Article 161 of The Water and Sewerage Services (Northern Ireland) Order 2006.  NI Water has advised that there are 59 developments within the Strangford Constituency, that have an Article 161 adoption agreement in place, dating from March 2008 to March 2025. The total original bond value of these 59 sites was £3,293,029 and the remaining bond value is £2,627,828.Article 17 of the Water and Sewerage Services (Northern Ireland) Order 1973 was the predecessor of Article 161. NI Water has advised that there are 11 developments within the Strangford Constituency, that have an Article 17 adoption agreement in place, dating from August 1995 to 2007.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 17549/22-27, following the monitoring carried out by her officials, whether any further opportunities to assist in reducing the traffic congestion at Castlebawn roundabout, Newtownards, are now being considered.
answer_text: Congestion is an issue that affects many of our urban centres, including Castlebawn Roundabout, particularly during the morning and evening peak periods. However, as road safety remains a high priority for my Department, the limited resources available must be focused in areas that have a demonstrable evidence of collisions to make our roads safer.Officials are currently considering if any further proposals in the area could be introduced primarily to improve safety for all its users and aid traffic progression. If any additional engineering measures are proposed, they will be considered for inclusion in a future work programme, subject to the availability of funding.
