question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) how productivity is measured within (a) her Department; and (b) its arm’s-length bodies; and (ii) any measures in place to improve productivity.
answer_text: Productivity is one of my four economic priorities and it is a driver for delivering more good jobs, addressing regional economic imbalance and decarbonising our economy.  The headline measure for monitoring productivity is gross value-added (GVA) per hour worked, which is a widely recognised measure of labour productivity.  This is supported by the following indicators, which all contribute to a measure of broader productivity growth:Gross fixed capital formationTotal investment in research and development as a percentage of gross domestic product (GDP)Number of companies engaging in research and developmentProportion of innovation-active businesses Number of innovation recognitionsProportion of leavers from our higher education institutions with narrow STEM qualifications Business births and deaths as a percentage of active enterprises Management practices scoreExports as a percentage of GDPExternal sales as a percentage of GDPThese are economy-level indicators, and each individual arm’s-length body contributes to these overall measures in terms of the economic impact of their activities.  Our ALBs may develop specific measures appropriate to their activities.  For example, Invest NI is using a productivity measure based on GVA per full-time equivalent employee, as this data can be gathered more readily from their client companies than GVA per hour worked.  The work of the whole of my department is focussed on improving productivity, as skills and investment are the main drivers of productivity growth.  This is exemplified by the SME Productivity Booster, launched in November 2024.  This £350k initiative is designed to support over 70 SMEs across the north is equipping businesses with the skills and insights they need to enhance their productivity, improve their competitiveness, and secure their long-term growth prospects.  The SME Productivity Booster provides targeted upskilling to businesses operating in key economic areas such as fintech, healthcare and advanced manufacturing. My department is also investing over £3m in training directly related to productivity growth including leadership, project management, digital skills, and business improvement techniques through other skills interventions such as Skill Up, Skills Focus, ApprenticeshipsNI, and Higher Level Apprenticeships. To drive investments that support productivity growth, Invest NI has developed a productivity assessment tool to assess the impact of its support interventions.  There are a number of programmes currently offered by Invest NI that contribute to productivity growth, including Operational Excellence, Supply Chain Resilience and Development Solutions, Skills for Growth and Leadership. My department’s 2024/25 business plan sets out a range of other interventions that will support my productivity priority, along with delivery across all of my economic priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) how productivity is measured within (a) her Department; and (b) its arm’s-length bodies; and (ii) any measures in place to improve productivity.
answer_text: Each year the Department produces an Annual Business Plan which sets out actions it is aimed to achieve within that financial year.  Progress towards achieving the actions within the Business Plan is monitored by the Departmental Management Board and reported in the Annual Report and Accounts.Managing people performance is fundamental to achievement of Departmental objectives.  My Department manages the performance of its people against their individual objectives through the NICS Performance Management policy.The Department also continually seeks to deliver service improvements and was successful recently, for example, in securing transformation funding to speed up and transform the criminal justice system and to modernise electronic monitoring.The Department’s arm’s-length bodies prepare business plans and annual reports and accounts and we hold annual accountability meetings with them at Chief Executive/Chair and Ministerial level to review performance.  You may wish to contact them directly for further information on their business plans, annual reports and accounts.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) how productivity is measured within (a) their Department; and (b) its arm’s-length bodies; and (ii) any measures in place to improve productivity.
answer_text: The Department’s Business Plan sets out the key actions that each business area has in place to ensure delivery within defined timescales. Progress against this Plan is monitored quarterly, with appropriate actions implemented to improve outcomes where required. Performance against these objectives is also published in the Department’s Annual Report and Accounts.		At individual/team level, managers measure workload, outputs and performance and therefore productivity as part of their standard management responsibilities.Similar arrangements apply in ALBs. In addition to this, each ALB is monitored, where relevant, by its own Management Board, and output and performance is monitored by the relevant TEO Sponsor Division via regular accountability and liaison meetings. Reports are also provided quarterly to the TEO Departmental Board.       		The Department’s Business Improvement Plan also contains several specific actions to improve performance, efficiency and output.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how productivity is measured within (a) his Department; and (b) its arm’s-length bodies; and (ii) any measures in place to improve productivity.
answer_text: Departmental performance against key activities is monitored throughout the year via the Department's Business Plan and published at year end in the Annual Report and Accounts. My Department manages the performance of its people against their individual objectives through the Performance Management policy as set out in the Civil Service Handbook and also focuses on workforce development through training and capability-building initiatives such as the Evolve Leadership Programme, to ensure staff are equipped with the necessary skills to perform effectively.	My Department publishes statistics and performance reports and these can be found on the link below.  	 	https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/topics/statistics-and-research-communitiesMy Department and the ALB's sponsored by my Department work closely together to ensure close and ongoing alignment of strategic and business planning through regular review and formal assurance meetings held throughout the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) how productivity is measured within (a) his Department; and (b) its arm’s-length bodies; and (ii) any measures in place to improve productivity.
answer_text: Performance for my Department and our arm’s length body, SEUPB is measured through the monitoring and review of objectives set out in the respective Business Plans. This includes consideration of outputs over the business plan period.  Corresponding divisional plans contain operational targets pertaining to standards, efficiency and effectiveness. Managing people performance is fundamental to achievement of these corporate objectives. My Department manages the performance of its people against their individual objectives through the Performance Management policy as set out in the Civil Service Handbook.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how productivity is measured within (a) his Department; and (b) its arm’s-length bodies; and (ii) any measures in place to improve productivity.
answer_text: My Department’s Business Plan for 2024-25 details the key commitments that DAERA will deliver in 2024-25 and includes the monitoring and reporting arrangements in place in relation to these. It can be accessed online at:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/daera-business-plansThe NICS HR Performance Management (policy) provides an outline of the performance management processes for staff in how they meet their personal performance objectives, including productivity, in support of the business plan targets, and how these are monitored and reported on by line managers on a continuous basis throughout the year. It can be accessed online at: https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/10-performance-managementProgress against the department’s business plan targets are reported to the Departmental Board throughout the year. At the end of the 2024-25 financial year the DAERA 2024-25 Annual Reports and Accounts will be published and placed on the DAERA website. The annual report will include a performance section showing the extent to which the targets were achieved. The performances of the Department’s Arm’s Length Bodies (ALBs) are evaluated in line with agreed Framework Documents and a review of performance is published in each bodies Annual Report and Accounts. These published documents contain a performance section showing the extent to which targets have been achieved.The productivity of ALBs is measured against the Key Performance Indictors (KPIs) agreed each year.  Delivery against set KPIs is monitored through formal accountability meetings between the Department and the relevant ALB.  ALB Chief Executives/Accounting Officers, with oversight from their Boards, are responsible for ensuring value for money is ensured in the use of any public funds allocated.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how productivity is measured within (a) her Department; and (b) its arm’s-length bodies; and (ii) any measures in place to improve productivity.
answer_text: My Department measures productivity through a combination of methods. This includes: quarterly monitoring of performance against the targets identified within the Department’s Business Plan; the publication of the Department’s Annual Report and Accounts which provides a comprehensive overview of yearly performance and is audited by the Northern Ireland Audit Office (NIAO); and the regular dissemination of statistics and service delivery to the public. For arm’s-length bodies, productivity is similarly assessed by the above methods and through regular performance meetings with the Department as sponsor organisation.To improve productivity, my Department has implemented several measures . These include investing in modern technological solutions to enhance operational efficiency, adopting best management practices to streamline processes and conducting bi-annual performance reviews as part of the performance management arrangements. My Department also has a focus on workforce development through training and capability-building initiatives to ensure staff are equipped with the necessary skills to perform effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how productivity is measured within (a) his Department; and (b) its arm’s-length bodies; and (ii) any measures in place to improve productivity.
answer_text: Productivity in my Department and across its arm’s-length bodies (ALBs) is measured primarily through the Business Planning, monitoring and reporting process. Approved Business Plans set out an agreed programme of work to be undertaken across the Department and its ALBs for the year ahead, based on the available resource allocation.  Delivery is monitored and reported at key stages throughout the year using a variety of mechanisms, such as Business Plan progress reports, Governance Statements, Governance and Accountability Review meetings (for ALBs) and end-year Departmental and ALB Annual Report and Accounts. At workforce level, individual performance and productivity is monitored through the performance management system. Personal Performance Agreements and Personal Development Plans are agreed between each individual and their line manager. Performance is monitored throughout the year and formally reported at mid-year and end-year stages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the money allocated under the transformation fund will be used to re-establish the Cross-Border Healthcare Scheme.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any work he is undertaking to protect the supply of vital veterinary medicines beyond the end of 2025.
answer_text: The Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 came into force on 12 April 2024, conferring powers upon the Secretary of State in relation to Articles 5, 6, 7 and Annex 2 of the Windsor Framework, which includes Regulation (EU) 2019/6 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 11 December 2018 on veterinary medicinal products.  As the Secretary of State has not exercised discretion with respect to this regulation, it is now under the direction and control of the Secretary of State for the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).		Since becoming Minister I have discussed the veterinary medicines issue with various groups and have developed an understanding of the issues for our future supply of veterinary medicines. 		I am a member of the Veterinary Medicines Working Group which aims to help find and advise the UK Government on the solutions that agricultural and pharmaceutical industries, as well as private animal owners and keepers, will require to aid in ensuring the continued availability and supply of veterinary medicines into Northern Ireland.  In addition, I hold regular meetings with Baroness Hayman, Defra Minister for Biosecurity and Animal Welfare on a broad range of issues including the future supply of veterinary medicines.		I will continue to engage with the UK Government on this important matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the consultation on the Strategic Environmental Assessment Environmental Report for the Proposed Ammonia Strategy and Revised Operational Protocol enables stakeholders to comment on the Proposed Ammonia Strategy and Revised Operational Protocol as opposed to just the associated Environmental Report.
answer_text: The consultation on the Environmental Report for the Proposed Ammonia Strategy and Revised Operational Protocol, as part of the process of Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) will also enable responses to be provided on the associated documents including the updates to the Ammonia Strategy and the revised Operational Protocol. The DAERA website has been updated to clarify this position and to set out the revised dates for the closure of the consultation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of how the planning permission needed for farm improvements to meet the requirements for the Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme, which focuses on capital support for technology and equipment to help farm businesses reduce ammonia emissions, carbon emissions and nutrient losses, will be possible under the Proposed Ammonia Strategy and Revised Operational Protocol.
answer_text: I am bringing forward the new Sustainable Farming Investment scheme to help primary food producers in the agricultural and horticultural sectors improve their environmental performance and business efficiency.Initially the scheme will focus on support for technology and equipment to reduce ammonia emissions, carbon emission and nutrient loss, and will not itself create any requirements around planning permissions.Where technology or equipment adopted by an individual farm business provides mitigation to reduce potential environmental impacts of a development proposal, this will be considered in provision of advice by the Department to local authorities as part of their deliberations on planning applications.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on what basis was guidance on appropriate mitigation measures designed to reduce overall ammonia emissions and to encourage farmers to carry out improvements to existing operations to reduce ammonia emissions omitted from the Strategic Environmental Assessment Environmental Report for the Proposed Ammonia Strategy and Revised Operational Protocol.
answer_text: The purpose of Strategic Environmental Assessment is to identify, describe and evaluate the likely significant effects of specified plans and programmes on the environment. Providing guidance on mitigation measures is not part of this process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in the absence of sufficient guidance on appropriate mitigation measures designed to reduce overall ammonia emissions or the encouragement for farmers to carry out improvements to existing operations that result in an overall reduction in ammonia emissions at farm level in the Strategic Environmental Assessment Environmental Report for the Proposed Ammonia Strategy and Revised Operational Protocol, when his Department intends to produce that guidance.
answer_text: My Department publishes information on the DAERA website on measures farmers can take to reduce the amount of ammonia emitted by their farming system. Farm Case Studies examining the impact of implementing a combination of reduction strategies on emissions at individual farm level have been published in Annex B of the Draft Ammonia Strategy. The Call for Evidence on the Future Operational Protocol to Assess the Impacts of Air Pollution on the Natural Environment also sets out a series of Farm Mitigation Measures Case Studies. Information on ammonia Emission Factors for different livestock sectors are available on the DAERA website in the Publications section.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether representations received on the Strategic Environmental Assessment Environmental Report for the Proposed Ammonia Strategy and Revised Operational Protocol will be taken into account by him before any final decision is made on the adoption of these documents.
answer_text: All responses received on the Strategic Environmental Assessment Environmental Report for the Proposed Ammonia Strategy and Revised Operational Protocol and any feedback provided on the update to the Proposed Ammonia Strategy and the Revised Operational Protocol will be taken into consideration before taking final decisions on these documents.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of pothole reports that were assessed as meeting departmental criteria following inspection in the last 12 months, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and this has had a significant impact on its ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. Details on pothole enquires can be found by following the Open Data NI website link: https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/pothole-enquiries. The table below shows the total number of pothole enquiries received between 1st March 2024 to 11th March 2025 broken down by Council area.  The information also shows the number investigated by my officials, detailing where action or no action has been taken as a result.Action taken with regard to defects can involve the addition of the repair to a works programme or notification to a third party for them to take action.  Those marked as no action refer to defects which do not meet the intervention levels of my department’s current policy, are duplications in the system, or have already been repaired as part of follow up action from a routine safety inspection of the area.You might also be interested in the road surface defects data published on: https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/surface-defects, mainly recorded during routine safety inspections as well as ad-hoc site investigations.  This includes the surface defect category descriptions, e.g. pothole, rutted, depressed, cracked, crazed uneven, edge deterioration etc, and also shows the status and completion date of any repairs. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any further steps he is taking to deliver an extension to the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme; and (ii) when he will meet with his UK counterparts to advance this issue.
answer_text: UK Immigration Policy is an excepted matter and therefore I do not have the remit to deliver changes to the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme. My officials and I continue to liaise with NI agri-food stakeholders to fully understand the impact that UK Immigration Policy changes have had on their businesses ability to secure appropriate labour. Inadequate labour is causing serious operational and financial problems at both a business and sector level and threatening the viability of the mushroom, fishing and meat processing sectors. I have communicated these issues to Ministers at Defra and it is intended that the evidence gathered will inform my Department’s input to an Executive Paper on access to labour issues, with subsequent representations being made to the UK Government. As part of his speech at the NFU Conference on 25th February 2025, the Defra Secretary of State announced an extension of the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme until 2030 and noted that annual quota reviews will ensure the right balance is struck between supporting farms while gradually reducing visa numbers as alternative solutions are developed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there will be any review of the Use Classes regulations to assist with the streamlining of the planning system.
answer_text: I have no current plans to review the Planning (Use Classes) Order (Northern Ireland) 2015 at this time.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when a new International Relations Strategy will be published.
answer_text: This Strategy will intersect with broader Executive and departmental strategies and actions and will be important in setting out our long-term approach to international engagement.The Strategy requires Executive agreement; therefore, it will be subject to consultation and Executive colleagues’ consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department is taking to meet the Courtauld Commitment 2030 on food waste.
answer_text: Courtauld 2030, now known as the UK Food and Drink Pact, unites nearly 200 organisations across the food and drink supply chain to help provide solutions for a sustainable, circular food system. By 2030, the pact aims to:Reduce food waste by 50% per capitaHalve GHG emissions arising from the food and drink systemMy department has provided funding to the Waste and Resources Action Programme (WRAP) to deliver this pact since 2019. This funding has meant that interventions specific to Northern Ireland can be implemented and these largely focus on the prevention of food waste. For example, in 2024/25, work has been undertaken to identify and prioritise the changes that can have the biggest impact including changes in the retail environment such as better labelling and storage advice. As a contributor to the pact, my department has access to the research, data and lessons learnt. However, whilst my department is already working on this range of interventions to help prevent food waste, we know that the average household in NI still wastes around £1000 worth of food per year. To help reduce this, my department funds a range of other initiatives which aim to achieve goals of the UK Food and Drink Pact such as Food Waste Action Week and the Food Waste Reduction Roadmap. Both campaigns encourage the elimination of food waste from households and businesses.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the action her Department is taking to streamline the planning application process.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that the planning system in the North operates in an efficient and effective way. My Department has been working collectively with local government, who are responsible for over 99% of all local and major planning applications, and other stakeholders to improve the planning system through the Planning Improvement Programme.The programme has already delivered positive changes including legislation to enable council planning authorities to introduce statutory validation checklists to improve the quality of planning applications entering the system, and in turn reduce the potential for delay through the application process. My Department is also conducting a review of the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015 to  enable the planning application process to be further streamlined. Ongoing work with statutory consultees has already resulted in performance improvements and this engagement will continue.My officials are currently engaging with individual council planning authorities and other key stakeholders in the planning system with a specific focus on identifying challenges in current development management (planning application process) practice and procedures, as well as individual performance related matters, to inform further opportunities for efficiencies and improvements to the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the review of the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015, including a time frame of when the review will be completed.
answer_text: The review of the Planning (Development Management) Regulations (NI) 2015 is ongoing, following the public consultation undertaken in 2024.  The aim is to ensure that the Regulations are practical and appropriate to support current and future development trends, streamline the planning application process, and encourage greater participation in the planning process.  My officials are continuing to review and analyse the responses from the consultation and I look forward to considering their recommendations at the appropriate time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how the £3 million for her Department's Transforming Planning proposal, as announced by the Minister of Finance on 4 March 2025, will be spent; and (ii) what practical impact this will have on transforming planning.
answer_text: I am delighted that the Transforming Planning proposal has been successful in securing Executive agreement to fund the appointment of Appointed Persons (Inspectors) to deliver reports to the Department on the planning and environmental considerations relating to major ‘hear and report’ work. Broadly this is work where a report and recommendation is made to my Department on a planning application or a council development local development plan.  Currently the Planning Appeals Commission carries out this work.  The use of the Department’s existing powers under the Planning Act (NI) 2011 to appoint inpsectors to underatke this work will help address and prevent backlogs of such work. It also allows the opportunity to look at how such a service could be better provided to the benefit of all by improving the agility of the processes. This will help improve the reputation of the planning system by providing more certainty to all those impacted on by the planning system, and by facilitating quicker decision making there is also the potential for savings for councils, consultees and applicants.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to introduce legislation, similar to that in Section 124 of the Localism Act 2011 in relation to concealed development, to assist planning authorities in enforcement proceedings for unlawful development.
answer_text: I do not have any plans currently to introduce legislation similar to section 124 of  the Localism Act 2011 with regards to planning enforcement.

answer_text: The table below sets out the amount spent on prescriptions for vitamins in each of the last five years from 2020 to 2024.YearVitamin Gross CostVitamin Actual Cost2020£2,677,755.77£2,419,494.302021£2,664,905.21£2,408,986.462022£2,555,748.37£2,310,790.132023£2,537,702.78£2,289,446.662024£1,931,394.85£1,728,371.86The Gross Cost is the price listed in the Drug Tariff, concessionary price lists or in standard price lists and is not necessarily the price that has been paid. The Actual Cost is the estimated cost to the Health Service in Northern Ireland for ingredients and appliances and takes account of the discounts applied to relevant products. The figure does not include dispensing, coding or other fees paid to contractors.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to ensure that prescriptions are not being issued for over the counter medicines.
answer_text: Steps are being taken to reduce the inappropriate prescribing of over-the-counter (OTC) medicines for the treatment of self-limiting illnesses through a combination of targeted GP guidance, patient education, and awareness campaigns.  My Department has issued correspondence to GP practices, supported by quarterly ‘COMPASS’ prescribing reports and visits from Departmental Pharmacy Advisors, advising clinicians to encourage self-care where appropriate.Alongside this, ongoing social media campaigns and patient information leaflets promote the benefits of purchasing OTC medicines, such as paracetamol or ibuprofen, from pharmacies or supermarkets. These campaigns highlight the cost savings and wider benefits to the health service, reinforcing the message that minor ailments such as aches and pains, coughs, colds, upset stomachs and sore throats can often be managed without a prescription.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) when commercial logging was undertaken by the Forest Service at Mount Stewart; and (ii) the number of trees cut down as part of that logging process in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Forest Service has carried out timber harvesting operations at Mount Stewart on four occasions between Sept 2023 and May 2024. This involved both thinning and clear-felling harvesting operations.Further clearance of storm damaged trees is now required following storm Éowyn. Harvesting of these trees is planned to commence later this month, and due to the extent of damage and clearance required works will be ongoing over the next few months. Details of areas and estimated number of trees removed by the four harvesting operations completed in the past ten years, including areas that were thinned for silvicultural reasons, are detailed below. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the measures that are intended to be put in place to ensure that the Movilla Stones are protected from further deterioration upon their return to Movilla Abbey.
answer_text: As part of its plans to return the Movilla stones the Department intends to install a new protective shelter accessible to visitors, within the cemetery at Movilla, where the stones can be safely housed for their conservation, be more accessible for visitors and be better interpreted to enhance the experience for visitors to the State Care Monument. I should stress that, as with all planned expenditure, this is subject to budget allocations in what has been an extremely challenging budget situation for the Department for Communities in recent years. Returning the stones to Movilla remains a priority project for the Historic Environment Division.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on when the Movilla Stones, which were removed from Movilla Abbey and are currently in the possession of Historic Environment Division, will be returned.
answer_text: The Movilla stones are currently stored safely and appropriately at Historic Environment Division’s conservation depot at Moira, awaiting conservation work before their return to site. Their conservation remains a high priority for HED and our intention is to procure this specialist service in 25/26 financial year. Once the stones are conserved, HED will liaise with Ards and North Down Borough Council to return the grave slabs to Movilla Abbey.I should stress that, as with all planned expenditure, this is subject to budget allocations in what has been extremely challenging budget settlements for the Department for Communities in recent years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) when Forest Service will address the aftermath of Storm Éowyn within its forest in the Mount Stewart demesne; and (ii) the reasons for the delay.
answer_text: Plans for the interventions required to address the impacts of the storm at Mount Stewart are well advanced and have been developed in consultation with National Trust staff.  This includes identifying the priority areas for clearance works and sourcing specialist resource capacity and substitution of previously planned timber sales with timber harvested due to the storm. I am advised that clearance of the wind damaged areas is expected to commence in the next few weeks and continue over the next few months.   Storm Éowyn has caused catastrophic damage to the Forest Service woodlands across its estate, including the Mount Stewart demesne. Due to the scale of the damage, I recognise that commencement of clearance works was not possible at all sites in the immediate aftermath of the storm. However, with priority clearances to public roads, residence and business accesses, utility infrastructure now completed, the focus is now on harvesting and re-establishing the storm affected areas.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether there is a policy on performance-related pay and bonuses for staff at the Education Authority.
answer_text: Performance-related pay is in place in the Education Authority (EA) for senior staff at Assistant Director level and above. This is outlined in the EA Performance Management Framework, which allows for annual pay progression subject to satisfactory performance. Performance-related pay is not in place for other staff groups. However, the EA does recognise the importance of timely performance management and an effective relationship between line managers and staff.The EA does not offer bonuses as part of staff terms and conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the amount that has been incurred in the transfer of prisoners from outside the UK to serve their sentences in Northern Ireland since 2014; and (ii) whether this has been repaid.
answer_text: There have been four transfers to the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) from outside of the UK. Three of the transfers were direct to Northern Ireland from the Country of origin, two from the Republic of Ireland and one from the Netherlands. There were no costs to NIPS.The fourth transfer, from the United States of America was a repatriation, and was charged a conveyance cost of £1950.00. The monies were repaid by the prisoner’s family on arrival to Northern Ireland.The Repatriation Act does not require payment of expenses for the escort.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the implementation of the Regional Dementia Care Pathway.
answer_text: My Department acknowledges that Dementia is one of the biggest challenges to our health and social care system in Northern Ireland, with the region projected to have the largest increase in the number of people living with dementia across the UK by 2040. My Department established a Regional Dementia Project Board in May 2024 in order to bring a sharpened focus on improvement in demenita care and services across the region and they last met on 20th January 2025. The Dementia Project Board will aim to focus on the roll out of the Regional Dementia Care Pathway.The project board developed four key workstreams which focus on the improvement within dementia services in line with the Regional Dementia Care Pathway. The Data and Diagnosis workstream is focusing on a HSC regionally-agreed service delivery model which facilitates timely access to accurate diagnosis and treatment for patients with dementia. The System Readiness and Service Delivery workstream is reviewing the current system of Dementia Services across the region to identify where improvements can be made. This workstream will also ensure system readiness for the introduction of any new Disease Modifying Treatments for early Alzheimer’s Disease.The Education and Training workstream is addressing training requirements for staff, families and carers.The Service Users and Carers workstream ensures that key decisions and actions in regard to the provision of dementia services is informed by those with lived experience.It is recognised that whilst some improvements to the current dementia pathway can be made through some service redesign within existing services, that other aspects of improvement will require a funding allocation. I am committed to identifying additional funding for dementia.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether the PV panels on Parliament Buildings, which were disconnected in late 2020, have since been reconnected.
answer_text: Following system upgrades to meet new certification requirements, the PV panels were successfully reconnected on 31 October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the redevelopment of Belfast Central Library.
answer_text: I am advised that the redevelopment of Belfast Central Library remains a key strategic priority for Libraries NI. Although progress has been impacted by recent resourcing pressures, I understand that Libraries NI are continuing to work with my officials with a view to resolving issues and progressing the completion of an updated Outline Business Case (OBC) for the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 5966/22-27, whether the business case referred to in relation to the Newtownards park and ride facility has been approved.
answer_text: Translink are leading on the delivery of the Newtownards Park and Ride site.  The Business Case to purchase the land of the former Ards Leisure Centre from the previous landowner, Ards and North Down Borough Council, was progressed by my Department.  The planning application for the Newtownards Park and Ride was submitted by Translink in November 2023 and is not yet issued.  Responses have been received from relevant statutory bodies and Translink are currently liaising, as appropriate, to any requests for clarification.  In preparation for obtaining planning approval, Translink is preparing the Outline Business Case for the Proposed Park & Ride facility at Harford Link / William Street, Newtownards. It is expected that this work will be complete by June 2025 when it will be sent to my Department for consideration / approval.  Whilst my officials continue to advance Park & Ride sites, including Newtownards, progression of any works will depend on funding availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any progress made on the implementation of the recommendations from the High Street Task Force.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for recommendation 8 of the High Street Task Force Report: ‘Fair Work on Our High Streets’. Delivering on this recommendation aligns with my economic priority on Good Jobs. Ongoing work within my Department on the development of a new Employment Rights Bill, promotion of greater adoption of the Real Living Wage, and collaboration with the Labour Relations Agency to create a Good Employment Charter will serve to support implementation of the recommendation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the measures he will be adopting to ensure that local councils meet their waste recycling targets.
answer_text: My department is continuing to progress several initiatives to ensure Northern Ireland is best placed to meet the ambitious recycling targets set in legislation. Extensive stakeholder engagement, including with all eleven local councils, has been ongoing since the publication of the Future Recycling Discussion Document in 2020. This engagement provided useful feedback which was used to inform the development of proposals in the recent Rethinking Our Resources consultation on recycling. Analysis of consultation responses has helped inform a draft government response, which my officials are currently finalising. It is my intention to bring this to my Executive colleagues to seek their support in moving forward together to not only meet our legal targets for recycling, but to also help drive green growth and develop our circular economy in Northern Ireland.The Household Waste Recycling Collaborative Change Programme (HWRCCP) offers financial support to local councils to transform kerbside recycling and Household Waste Recycling Centre (HWRC) infrastructure and services. The programme is designed to support projects that further increase recycling rates, improve the quality of recycling and reduce reliance on landfill and offers 50% match-funding to successful applications.  To date, £8.3m has been made available for eleven projects. My department also provides funding to the Waste & Resources Action Programme (WRAP) to provide essential local authority support, including specialist technical guidance to support change. The programme includes collaborative efforts with local councils on key national and regional behaviour change campaigns through the National Communications Advisory Panel (NCAP), such as Recycle Week and Food Waste Action Week, designed to enhance the quality and quantity of recycling.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of pupils being electively home schooled in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has provided the information below in respect of the number of children it was informed of who were being electively home educated (EHE) in each of the last ten years. These figures do not reflect the entirety of children in Northern Ireland who are electively home educated. SCHOOL YEARTOTAL EHE (known to EA)2024-2025*10322023-202410602022-20239072021-20228082020-20217102019-20204722018-20194392017-20182872016-20172932015-2016266*This figure represents the number of EHE children known to the EA at 31 January 2025. Parents are responsible for ensuring their child receives an education, at school or otherwise. Parents may therefore choose to educate their children at home rather than register them at a school and they may choose to deregister their child from school with the intention of educating them at home. Whilst the EA is aware of children who have attended and subsequently been deregistered from a school, there is no duty on parents to inform the Department of Education or the EA if they have chosen to educate their children at home. Thus, there is no complete record of children being electively home educated in Northern Ireland. It is also the case in other UK jurisdictions that no complete record is held of EHE children who have never been registered at a school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how much a weekly 20mg oral dose of methotrexate over a six month period would cost the health service.
answer_text: Further to a MHRA (Medicine and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency) safety alert in 2006, methotrexate 2.5mg tablets is the preferred strength to be prescribed in primary care in the UK. Therefore, a dose of 20mg weekly is 8 (2.5mg) tabletsThe current price for 28 methotrexate 2.5mg tablets is £1.44 (February 2025 Northern Ireland Drug Tariff)6 months (26 weeks) treatment will therefore cost £10.70This cost does not include payment for GP practice time or dispensing via the community pharmacy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how much a support glove for the treatment of hand osteoarthritis costs; and (ii) what is its projected lifespan.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Drug Tariff, published by the Business Services Organisation (BSO), outlines the reimbursement rates for drugs and appliances. This is available at the following link: Drug TariffAs of February 2025, hand supports are not available to prescribe in Primary Care but can be purchased from community pharmacies and other providers over the counter. The Department does not hold information on the cost of these products or their lifespan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current options for treating hand osteoarthritis.
answer_text: Osteoarthritis (OA) is a long-term disorder of synovial joints which occurs when damage triggers repair processes leading to structural changes within a joint, with features of localized cartilage loss, remodelling of adjacent bone and formation of osteophytes, and mild synovitis.There's no cure for osteoarthritis, but the condition does not necessarily get any worse over time. There are a number of treatments to help relieve the symptoms.The main treatments for the symptoms of osteoarthritis include:lifestyle measures – such as maintaining a healthy weight and exercising regularlymedication – to relieve your painsupportive therapies – to help make everyday activities easierIn a few cases, where other treatments have not been helpful, surgery to repair, strengthen or replace damaged joints may also be considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact of the financial constraints on Northern Ireland Water on the delivery of the Housing Supply Strategy.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration his Department has given to prescribing methotrexate for hand osteoarthritis following research conducted by Monash University and Alfred Health published in the Lancet in 2023 showed it to be an effective treatment,
answer_text: All medicines authorised for use in Northern Ireland, are authorised by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA). Authorisations are granted by MHRA if they meet the MHRA’s high standards on safety, quality and efficacy. Methotrexate is not currently licensed by MHRA for osteoarthritis, including hand osteoarthritis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 22098/22-27 and AQW 22099/22-27, whether the Forest Service will carry out repairs to the boundary wall of the Mount Stewart estate on the Mount Stewart Road which partially collapsed several years ago.
answer_text: The lease to my Department for the forest area at Mount Stewart has 89 years remaining in the lease term.  The Department will meet its lessee responsibilities regarding repairs to the boundary wall.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 8975/17-22, to detail why the works, which NI Water deemed not required at that time, are now being carried out on the Springfield Road, Portavogie.
answer_text: Detail on the operational works taken forward by NI Water is a matter for NI Water; however, I have requested an update on this matter.NI Water has advised that the works that commenced on the Springfield Road Portavogie on Friday 14th February are necessary to provide a storm sewer to serve the Seaview Court development. This work was subject to necessary funding becoming available to complete required infrastructure for the housing development. It is anticipated to last four weeks.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will require NI Water to provide a full resurfacing of the area of Springfield Road, Portavogie, which is being dug up for works by NI Water, given that recent resurfacing by her Department was carried out following an assurance from NI Water that works were no longer required.
answer_text: Article 31 of the Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 requires Statutory Undertakers, including NI Water, to reinstate the excavated road in accordance with the relevant materials specifications and engineering standards of workmanship. Statutory Undertakers are responsible during a warranty period after the reinstatement is completed. As such, my Department is unable to instruct NI Water to fully resurface Springfield Road following their essential sewer upgrade work.However, my Department will continue to inspect and take forward road surface defects that meet the Limited Service intervention levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any further consideration has been given to a graduated reduction in speed on the A21 Ballygowan Road approaching Comber, given increased development along this road.
answer_text: When considering the appropriate speed limit for a road my Department’s policy and procedures guide entitled “Setting Local Speed Limits in Northern Ireland” is used to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads taking into account factors such as: the characteristic of the road; the speed of existing traffic; the degree of frontage development; and road traffic collision data, which is continuously monitored using data provided by the PSNI.I am advised that, based on data currently held by my Department, there has been no significant changes along A21 Ballygowan Road approaching Comber and as such, there are no current plans to introduce a graduated reduction in the speed limit approaching Comber.  I have asked my officials to continue to monitor the road and to review any data that may become available which may change this position.  One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as inconsiderate parking and/or speeding should be directed to the PSNI using the non-emergency 101.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps she is taking to address the skills gap in the installation and maintenance of green technologies in the construction sector.
answer_text: Decarbonisation is one of my four key objectives and the construction sector will play a significant role in its delivery. My Department has worked in collaboration with the Green Skills Delivery Group to develop an Action Plan to address the needs of the construction sector as well as wider green industries. This is a comprehensive framework to guide Government, trade unions, employers, education providers and individuals on what is needed to deliver the skills for the future and meet net zero targets. The Plan will be launched in the next couple of months. A sub-group of this Delivery Group, with representatives from business, academia and across government will advise us on future green skills needs including those for the construction sector.- Delivery has already commenced in some areas, with free courses through the Skill Up programme, including heat pump installation and maintenance, passive house, retrofitting and insulation and building treatments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact of Storm Éowyn on the commercial forests owned or managed by the Forest Service in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: Storm Éowyn has impacted to some degree all woodlands managed by Forest Service within the Strangford constituency.  I understand that Forest Service’s assessment and analysis of the extent of damage is ongoing. However, at this stage, I am aware that the storm damage has devastated the forest at Mount Stewart.  It has also caused substantial damage at Ballysallagh forest, with the commercial forest area, adjacent to the reservoir being completely blown over.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any areas in the Strangford constituency that have been closed to further connections by NI Water due to high polluting assets.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:Within the Strangford constituency, as in the rest of the North, there are areas where capacity is constrained, and new connections may not be possible.  This may be due to lack of capacity in the wastewater treatment works or due to problems in the sewerage network and each development application will be considered on a case-by-case basis depending on the location within the catchment.  The towns which may be affected within the Strangford constituency are Comber, Saintfield, Darragh Cross, Newtownards, Killinchy and Killyleagh.   However, it is important to note that capacity issues may not affect all areas within these towns.  NI Water publishes up to date capacity information, by council area, on its web site at Wastewater System Capacity Information .For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail Land and Property Services' policy on the use of credit cards to pay rates bills.
answer_text: Land and Property Services (LPS) offers a range of payment methods to facilitate the payment of rates, balancing fairness and convenience for ratepayers with the cost-of-service delivery. Payment by credit card incurs costs to the Department which are not passed on to ratepayers.Domestic property ratepayers can pay rates by credit card (Visa and Mastercard) up to the value of £9,999.99 through the Pay your Rates Online service.Non-Domestic property ratepayers can pay by credit card (Visa and Mastercard) through the Pay your Rates Online service provided the property for which payment relates has a Net Annual Value below £15,000 and where the ratepayer has received a Magistrates Court Process Notice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide details of the programme which will replace the Land Mobility Programme which closed on 31 August 2023, including when the replacement programme will commence.
answer_text: The Farming for the Generations pilot scheme is part of my Department’s Sustainable Agriculture Programme and aims to raise awareness of the need for succession planning on farms to help ensure the long-term sustainability of the farm business. This pilot scheme was launched in October 2024 and will bring together a range of interventions to support succession planning and encourage land mobility options.   	The ‘Farming in Partnerships’ element of the pilot scheme specifically considers land mobility options for farm businesses. This is a facilitation service which will link farmers without an identified successor to consider opportunities to partner with new entrants and other farm businesses to provide access to land and other resources, developing sustainable farm partnerships.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of eligible farmers that did not opt into the Beef Carbon Reduction Scheme for 2024; and (ii) any barriers that have been identified for those farmers not participating.
answer_text: i) A total of 672 eligible businesses (i.e. claimed and eligible for payment of BPS and kept eligible animals) chose not to opt in to the Beef Carbon Reduction Scheme in 2024. (ii) My Department has not identified any barriers for those farmers not participating in the Beef Carbon Reduction (BCR).The BCR is very simple to join. To increase awareness of the BCR scheme among NI farm businesses, an extensive communications plan was executed. Efforts included issuing of:Press releases and social media posts ahead of key dates, creation of BCR webpages, A BCR opening letter was issued approximately 24,500 registered farm businesses containing a link to the BCR guidance and opt in screens.SMS texts sent to businesses subscribing to DAERA communications containing links to press releases. A BCR opening letter to all Authorised Persons, with instructions on how to opt their clients in to the BCR scheme.To encourage uptake in the final months of the scheme year, eligible farm businesses that had yet to opt in to the BCR scheme were contacted, with reminders to complete their opt in before the end of the scheme year, via SMS text, email or telephone (only where no registered email or mobile number existed).Stakeholders in NI Livestock Marts and slaughterhouses were provided with leaflets and information on BCR to help target Beef Farmers. I recorded a short video message encouraging participation in the BCR scheme, this was circulated on the DAERA website and social media. The measures employed by my Department effectively removed barriers for farmers opting in to the BCR scheme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the last time he or his officials met the Young Farmers' Clubs of Ulster directly to discuss future support.
answer_text: My Department’s Agricultural Policy Stakeholder Group (APSG) was established in June 2021, to engage the major stakeholder organisations across farming, food and environmental interests on the co-design of the future programme of farm support. In setting up APSG, my Department invited key agriculture and environmental representative organisations to nominate members and the Young Farmers Clubs of Ulster (YFCU) is represented via Ulster Farmers Union (UFU).Also, in developing the Farming for the Generations pilot scheme, my Department set up a specific Generational Renewal Workstream Committee in which the YFCU engaged with. These meetings were held on Monday 22 October 2022, Tuesday 10 January 2023 and Tuesday 3 October 2023. A further update of the pilot scheme with the President of YFCU and Land Mobility was held on 19 June 2023.		A meeting of APSG took place with my officials on 18 February 2025 to discuss Farming for the Generations pilot scheme. Evaluation of the pilot scheme will input into a future Farming for the Generations scheme which is planned to commence in 2026. This will be in co-design with stakeholders, including the YFCU and will actively consider the provision of financial support. 		I was also delighted to address the UFU/YFCU Conference on 22 January 2025 to outline my vision for the future of farming in the years ahead.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of Land Registry requests processed (i) within 3 months; (ii) within 6 months; (iii) within 12 months; (iv) within 18 months; (v) within 24 months; (vi) within 36 months; and (vii) over 36 months, in the last five years.
answer_text: In years 2020 to 2024, Land Registry processed (i) 4,609,364 requests within 3 months, (ii) 61,383 requests within 6 months, (iii) 23,818 requests within 12 months, (iv) 5,191 requests within 18 months, (v) 1,568 requests within 24 months, (vi) 902 requests within 36 months and (vii) 189 requests over 36 months.Due to the complex and contested nature of some of the applications made to Land Registry, completion times depend significantly on the circumstances of the specific registration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Queens Hall and Newtownards Library capital project, following the consideration of the business case submitted by Libraries NI.
answer_text: Libraries NI submitted an updated business case supporting the proposed redevelopment of the library which is still being considered by my Department. There are a number of complex issues that continue to be worked through. Any decision to proceed with a new library facility will be dependent on prioritising of capital investment in a very difficult budgetary situation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of houses designated as unfit in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Unfitness is measured through the House Condition Survey, which is generally carried out every five years. The sample design does not permit constituency level figures. The most recently published House Condition Survey was in 2016, which found that 2% of dwellings (n16,400) in Northern Ireland were unfit. This level of unfitness (2%) is too small to provide meaningful results at a lower geographical level. In order to provide some indication of findings at a sub N. Ireland level, seven out of the eleven council areas have unfitness rates of between 1% and 2% with Fermanagh and Omagh and Mid Ulster having the highest rates of unfitness. A House Condition Survey is currently underway and a main report is planned for publication in 2026.”  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the wild birds protected by legislation outside of any designated Special Protection Area; and (ii) the extent of any protection.
answer_text: Article 4 of the Wildlife (NI) Order 1985 makes it an offence to kill, injure, take any wild bird, or destroy its nest while in use or destroy their eggs.There are several exceptions to this regulation. These exceptions include shooting of wildfowl and game birds during the open season depending on the species. For example, partridge and snipe from 1 September to 31 January, and grouse from 12 August to 30 November. Another exception relates to the Department’s responsibility to issue general licences to allow the shooting of pest bird species. This action is taken to assist prevent the spread of disease; promote public health and safety; prevent serious damage to livestock, crops, etc, or for conservation of other wild species. The species targeted for shooting include the hooded crow, jackdaw, magpie, rook, feral pigeon and woodpigeon.No wild bird species can be shot on a Sunday, Christmas Day or one hour after sunset and one hour before sunrise.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps taken to recover fines from drivers from the Republic of Ireland who have received fines for driving in bus lanes in Northern Ireland, once they have returned to their own jurisdiction.
answer_text: My Department has an MoU with the Department of Transport Driver and Vehicle Computer Services Division (DVCSD) in the South for the exchange of keeper details enabling my Department to send correspondence to these addresses. Registered keepers of vehicles detected driving in contravention of bus lanes will receive a Moving Traffic Offence Penalty Charge Notice in the post informing them of this contravention and next steps to pay.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider developing a scheme for the purchase and installation of floodlights for football clubs with grass pitches.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Football Fund will provide investment for football at all levels. There are three strands - the Performance Programme, the Grassroots Facilities Programme and the National Football Centre.The call for applications to the Performance Programme opened on 30 January 2025. This will begin a rolling programme of awards and project delivery. Items that are within each football ground’s boundary will be considered eligible for funding, this includes pitches and lighting as stated in the Performance Programme’s Application Guidance document.Work to finalise detailed policy proposals for the grassroots facilities programme is underway and I expect to be presented with recommendations on need, scope and delivery options for a programme of grassroots investment by Spring 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the preservation and erection of the McGavock house at the Ulster American Folk Park, a property which was brought back from Virginia and holds significant historical value as the venue for the signing of the Fincastle Resolution.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for National Museums NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The McGavock house was dismantled and relocated from Virginia to Ulster American Folk Park in the 1980s. It has been stored there ever since and, as a national museum, we remain committed to its care and preservation. Unfortunately, it has never been displayed due to the amount of conservation/restoration work required to reconstruct it. We have also been concerned about how the house would fare in our damp climate. However, we are now planning a redevelopment of the visitor centre and galleries as part of the Mid-South-West Regional Growth Deal and the McGavock House has been an integral part of our discussions. It is one of the oldest houses in our collection and we fully appreciate the importance of the emigrant story of the McGavocks and their role in the story of American independence. James McGavock was one of thirteen elected representatives to sign the Fincastle Resolutions on 20 January 1775, which promised resistance to the British crown to preserve political liberties. This was an important step on the road that led to the American War of Independence and the Declaration of Independence of 1776. We are looking at ways to incorporate it into our new development, potentially as an iconic centrepiece in an indoor setting, with the necessary environmental controls”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact National Museums NI directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has conducted any investigation into the scale of damp and black mould in the public and private rented sector.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out any investigation into the scale of damp and mould in the private rented sector. Environmental Health Officers in local councils have powers to deal with damp and associated mould issues that are prejudicial to health through enforcing the statutory minimum fitness standard for housing, as set out in Article 46 of the Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1981. This standard is applicable to all tenures. You may wish to contact each council area to see if they have any information relevant to your request.In relation to registered housing associations (RHAs), management of damp and mould is an operational matter for each association.  Since 2023/24, Housing Regulation Branch (HRB) has undertaken biannual surveys aimed at ensuring that RHAs are appropriately managing all cases of damp and mould within their stock, have policies and procedures in place to address damp and mould and provide regular reports to their respective boards.  Survey results have shown that, in the main, RHAs have policies in place and continue to take a proactive approach towards damp and mould. RHAs also carry out a range of exercises, education events and initiatives to help ensure that tenants are made aware of how to prevent damp and mould and how to raise any concerns with the association.In relation to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, management of damp and mould is an operational matter.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive formed a Task and Finish group in November 2022 to review how the Housing Executive deals with Damp and Mould on a cross-divisional basis given its multi-faceted nature.  The Housing Executive developed and is delivering an extensive Action Plan that will address the wide-ranging contributing issues of damp and mould within their properties on a short term, medium term and long-term basis.  The Housing Executive has set up a dedicated Project Team to deliver the Action Plan and they will review/develop policies and procedures for damp and mould cases through proactive and reactive measures and will continue to build data profiles through our Damp & Mould dashboard. They will also work with key internal and external stakeholders in all key areas and provide training to both Technical and Non-Technical staff and deliver a communication plan.  The anticipated result of the delivery of the Action Plan being a reduction in the risks posed by damp and mould to both customers, properties and organisation through effective communication, education, timely and effective response maintenance and a more targeted planned scheme works programme.”The NIHE also undertakes the House Condition Survey which assesses the condition and energy efficiency in properties across all tenures. The survey was last published in 2018. Further details on the current status of the HCS can be accessed at: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/working-with-us/research/house

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to introduce legislation to require damp and black mould to be addressed, similar to that proposed under Awaab’s Law in England.
answer_text: My Department is closely following the progress of Awaab’s law and officials are monitoring developments to consider the most appropriate approach here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the reasons why street lights in Comber town centre have not been repaired after many months; and (ii) when these repairs will be carried out.
answer_text: My Department is currently providing a full street lighting repair service, with outages generally being repaired within a 5 working day target.  Repairs that are more complex require more planning time, as in this case, and will therefore take longer to make the necessary arrangements for their repair.  I can confirm that the street lighting outages in Comber town were repaired on 9 February 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any work being carried out to develop an apprenticeship scheme to incentivise young people into the fishing industry.
answer_text: My officials continue to engage with industry representatives to identify proposals that may help in addressing the issue of crew shortages, including those aimed at attracting young people into the fishing industry. During a recent meeting on 7 February 2025 between industry representatives and my officials, the cost of securing the initial sea-going certificates for new entrants was discussed and a scheme of assistance is currently being considered for inclusion within the Department’s 2025/26 funding proposals.  My officials remain eager to learn of industry proposals regarding the shape and delivery of any apprenticeship scheme that would be effective in promoting and encouraging young people towards a career at sea within our fishing industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of company directors that have been disqualified in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The number of company directors that have been disqualified in each of the last ten years is detailed in the table below:	YEAR	Number of Undertakings	Number of Orders of the court	Total disqualifications	2015/2016	25	8	33	2016/2017	48	3	51	2017/2018	36	5	41	2018/2019	6	3	9	2019/2020	12	0	12	2020/2021	20	0	20	2021/2022	31	11	42	2022/2023	18	1	19	2023/2024	18	8	26	2024/2025 (year to date)	25	6	31	TOTAL 	239	45	284Where the High Court finds that there has been unfit conduct by a director, it must (under Article 9 of the Company Directors Disqualification (Northern Ireland) Order 2002) make a disqualification order for a period of between two and 15 years.The Company Directors Disqualification (Northern Ireland) Order 2002, which came into effect on 5 September 2003, allows directors, with the agreement of the Department, to avoid the need for a court hearing by offering an acceptable disqualification undertaking.  The undertaking has the same legal effect as a disqualification order made by the High Court and will also be for a period of between two and 15 years.  The consequences of breaching a disqualification undertaking are the same as those for breaching a disqualification order.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether any consideration has been given to publishing an online bankruptcy and insolvency register, similar to other jurisdictions.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the management and administration of online registers which contain details of individuals who are subject to Debt Relief Orders (DRO), Bankruptcy Restrictions Orders (BRO) and Individual Voluntary Arrangements.  The DRO and BRO register can be found at:DRO and BRO Register Search | Department for the Economy. The IVA register can be found at:Individual Voluntary Arrangement Register | Department for the Economy. The statutory responsibility for maintaining a register of individuals who are bankrupt, lies with the Department of Justice and is maintained by the Bankruptcy Office of the Chancery Division of the Royal Courts of Justice.  The register is publicly available online, however, a fee is charged.  The service can be accessed at:Online Services | Department of Justice. The register of corporate insolvency proceedings is maintained by Companies House and provides details of whether a company is active, in administration or in liquidation.  It is available at:Get information about a company - GOV.UK. In addition, all formal notices relating to personal and corporate insolvency matters are required to be published in the Gazette, which has an online search facility.  It is available at:The Gazette | Official Public Record
