question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail which parts of the external boundary wall of the Mount Stewart estate is the responsibility of Forest Service to maintain.
answer_text: My Department’s Forest Service has a lease agreement in place for woodland management for 97 hectares of forest area within the Mount Stewart estate boundary with the National Trust. The terms and conditions of the lease places responsibility on Forest Service for the repair and maintenance of the external demesne wall, where it bounds the leased area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the work carried out by Forest Service to address storm damage in its forest in the Mount Stewart estate since Storm Kathleen in April 2024.
answer_text: My Department’s Forest Service has a Memorandum of Understanding with the National Trust to work co-operatively to jointly develop and promote the use of forest areas for environmental, recreation and tourism objectives. Within the Mount Stewart estate, 97 hectares of forest area within the estate boundary are leased to Forest Service.Following Storm Kathleen, Forest Service officials met National Trust staff to agree a work programme to deal with the harvesting and removal of wind damaged trees. These operations resulted in over 590 tonnes of timber being salvaged following the storm.  Following the completion of the harvesting operations paths which were impacted by harvesting operations were re-instated to facilitate visitor access. Forest Service and National Trust have shared information on the impacts of recent storms and will continue to work together to discuss next steps in storm damage clearance and reinstatement of the woodlands.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the work that has been carried out to the external boundary wall of the Mount Stewart estate; and (ii) the cost of that work, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department’s Forest Service has a lease agreement in place with National Trust for 97 hectares of forest area within the Mount Stewart estate boundary, which includes lessee responsibility for the repair and maintenance of the external demesne wall, where it bounds the leased area.Forest Service has not commissioned any external boundary repair work in the period referenced, and therefore there are no associated costs. There is regular engagement between my officials and National Trust staff as part of ongoing development and promotion of forest areas within the estate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the policy for setback of forests from roads and utilities.
answer_text: My Department’s Forest Service does not have a policy detailing setback of forests from roads and utilities. Current sustainable forestry management practices in afforestation and re-forestation take account of the public road network.  Conifer planting is kept back 10-20 metres to allow for an edge of scrub species to develop adjacent to roadside.  Wayleave Agreements are in place across the Forest Service estate to enable utility providers to have access to a specific piece of land for purposes such as installation, repair, maintenance and operation, for example for electricity networks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the work undertaken to address ash dieback in its forest in the Mount Stewart estate in the last ten years; and (ii) any problems encountered in carrying out this work.
answer_text: Ash dieback is now established in the wider environment in Northern Ireland as in other European countries. This led to an EU legislative amendment to the Plant Health Regulations in 2019, which no longer considered ash dieback as a quarantine pest. On that basis, affected trees are not required to be removed as a disease control measure. It is important for landowners and managers to exercise their public safety responsibilities regarding the management of affected trees and woodlands. Forest Service has carried out planned tree safety inspections in Mount Stewart estate since 2015, these have been carried out on an annual basis since 2020, with the latest inspection having taken place in December 2024.  These inspections have resulted in a number of large ash trees being felled as they displayed signs of dieback and some smaller trees along the boundary were flailed. During these planned tree safety interventions, no major operational problems have arisen.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 10331/22-27, (i) whether his officials have commenced a feasibility study on the Northern Ireland Law Commission; and (ii) to detail the timescale for completion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, further to the reported case where a councillor states he was misled into providing a character reference, to detail any steps she is taking to change the law relating to the use of character references in criminal cases, particularly those where the charges relate to serious sexual offences.
answer_text: It is a matter for the judiciary to determine what is relevant to the sentencing process. The purpose of character references under common law is to provide the court with insight into any factors that could mitigate the defendant's sentence.This may be relevant when the defendant does not have a criminal history or a history of the same type of offence, but the courts have made it clear that personal circumstances should not normally rank highly in a serious case.I acknowledge that there are potential inequalities between complainants and defendants in serious sexual offence cases, as highlighted by the Gillen Review. This includes the fact that the complainant’s testimony is often treated with disbelief whilst the defendant can call on good character evidence and may seek to undermine the complainant’s character or actions. This is compounded by the fact that the complainant is not accorded the right to call character evidence or witnesses on their behalf. It is my preference to end or limit the use of good character references in certain categories of offending, particularly domestic and sexual offending.  My Department is, therefore, actively seeking to address the use of good character references, which will require legislation.  In the meantime, I would urge everyone to exercise personal responsibility when it comes to providing character references for use by a court.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, following the Report of the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime in Northern Ireland on Victim Personal Statements (VPS), to detail any plans she has to make it possible for victims of crime to read out their VPS, if they wish to, as is the case in England, Wales and the Republic of Ireland.
answer_text: Section 33 of The Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 sets out the provision for making a victim statement, including that the statement is made in writing and provided to the court. The opportunity to have the statement read out in court is not currently mandatory but may occur at the discretion of the court.I have welcomed the findings of the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime following her examination into the operation of victim personal statements, and indicated my support for the recommended changes, including that victims be permitted to read their statement in court, should they wish to do so.My officials are engaging with partners and stakeholders to agree and quickly progress the actions required to address the recommendations in the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the annual average daily number of vehicles that use the Kempe Stones Road, Newtownards, for each of the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department currently publishes an Annual Traffic and Travel Information Report. This presents traffic flow data for available census point locations across the network. Unfortunately, census data is not available for A20 Kempe Stones Road, however data obtained from surveys undertaken in October 2015 indicate an average daily volume of 22,058 vehicles.The latest published report is dated 2023, including the previous 4 years of information, can be found on the Department’s website; Traffic and travel information (incorporating annual traffic census and variations in traffic flow) | Department for Infrastructure. My officials are currently developing a bespoke growth forecasting tool for the north which will provide a standardised approach to forecasting travel demand for use in transport planning studies. The level of traffic growth predicted through the use of this forecasting tool will be location specific and generated from the forecasted changes in planning data (population, employment), car ownership rates and trip purposes. It is anticipated that the new tool will be available for use in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of deaths that have occurred in prison custody in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Since 2020, there have been 26 deaths in prison custody in Northern Ireland.  1 cause of death has been confirmed at inquest as by natural causes, with 14 potentially natural causes (58%); 9 deaths are potentially self-inflicted (35%); and 2 deaths are potentially substance related (7%).Year20202021202220232024No of deaths35459The appropriate authorities are notified of all deaths in custody.  The Prisoner Ombudsman takes forward investigations of all deaths in custody and if appropriate makes recommendations for both the Northern Ireland Prison Service and the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust following their investigation. Coroners’ inquests are also held following deaths in custody.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions that have taken place between his Department and Ards and North Down Borough Council in relation to the clearing of rotting seaweed and other debris from beaches in the borough.
answer_text: My officials have had no discussions with Ards and North Down Borough Council in relation to the clearing of seaweed from beaches during this winter season.  Requests of this nature are assessed for environmental impact by my Department and may require an assent by NIEA.  Although rotting seaweed can be unsightly post winter storms, it is a natural process that is part of the cycling of nutrients and forms an important habitat for coastal species.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether an announcement can be made throughout the Parliament Buildings to notify building users when business has concluded in the Assembly Chamber on sitting days.
answer_text: As this would be a change of procedure in relation to plenary sittings of the Assembly, it would be a matter for the Speaker rather than the Assembly Commission.                                                                                            On 28 January 2025, in a letter to Members on “Parliamentary Culture at the Assembly”, the Speaker reminded all Members that “it is the responsibility of Members to monitor how (Assembly) business is moving and ensure they are here (in the Chamber) when they are required to be.  That is one of the reasons Members are provided with televisions in their offices to follow proceedings as they happen”.In this context, Assembly officials have engaged with the Speaker who is not persuaded that a change is necessary at this point and is content that the responsibility remains on Members to monitor the progress of Assembly business.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any measures his Department is taking to promote and encourage the use of reusable nappies; and (ii) the organisations his Department is working with in relation to this matter.
answer_text: I am aware that several local councils have run nappy reuse schemes across NI, offering up to £30 reimbursement to residents who spend £50 or more on the purchase of reusable cloth nappies. In addition, some councils also work with ‘The Library Group’ to offer a two-week trial pack to residents which includes cloth nappies and reusable wipes.My department currently provide funding to Northern Ireland Resources Network (NIRN), a representative body which promotes re-use and repair throughout Northern Ireland.  All eleven local councils are members of NIRN and many local council initiatives, including reusable nappy schemes, are promoted via the network.My department will consider the inclusion of reusable nappies in the Waste Prevention Programme as part of a review that is due to be conducted later this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of instances of abuse towards staff that have been recorded in each of the last five years; and (ii) the action taken by management as a result.
answer_text: My Department places a high importance on the provision of quality services to our customers but do not condone any form of abuse or threatening behaviour towards our staff. The table below indicates the Departmental statistics over the last five years.YearTotal number of instances of abuse2020-2142021-2292022-23102023-2442024-25 to date13TOTAL38The Department has processes in place for staff when dealing with unacceptable customer behaviour. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the (i) number of cases; and (ii) sentences imposed for fuel laundering related offences in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: There is no specific offence of fuel laundering.  Rather, offences relating to illegal fuel may be prosecuted as evasion of duty type offences under the Customs and Excise Management Act 1979.  Information on the number of prosecutions, convictions and disposals imposed for offences under this legislation which relate to hydrocarbon oils is provided in the tables below. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of instances of abuse towards staff that have been recorded in each of the last five years; and (ii) the action taken by management as a result.
answer_text: The number of instances of physical and verbal abuse towards staff that have been recorded in the last 5 financial years are shown in the tables below;Instances of physical abuseYearBHSCTNHSCTNIASSEHSCTSHSCTWHSCTTotal2019/2044631388234143416671061102472020/2146731519340130414841416107362021/2243721582391152516251674111692022/2346731455381186517281542116442023/244322182645719912217138912202Total225037770180381198721708255998Instances of verbal abuseYearBHSCTNHSCTNIASSEHSCTSHSCTWHSCTTotal2019/2057939723227253432323372020/2169041727117036938222992021/2283847130321138531825262022/2387835627422747032825332023/248004682653776623552927Total37852109134512572420170612622Five year figures on attacks on staff were last published by the Department on 12 December 2023 as part of the announcement launching the Violence and Aggression in the Workplace Framework. They were figures which included physical attacks only.Each HSC Trust employs a comprehensive and systematic approach to the reporting, investigation, and management of incidents, including instances of abuse and workplace violence. Each Trust has a Violence and Aggression against staff Framework in place which has been disseminated to all relevant staff.  This approach ensures compliance with statutory requirements, fosters a culture of shared learning, and enables the implementation of targeted interventions to enhance workplace safety.Each HSC trust also has a strong emphasis on preventative strategies, training, and cultural change, which ensures that staff are equipped to handle challenging situations while fostering a safer working environment. These violence prevention strategies include;Workplace risk assessments focusing on high-risk areas such as emergency departments, mental health units, and social care settings.De-escalation training and Conflict Resolution Training (CRT) for frontline staff to manage aggressive behaviours effectively.Provision of access control to areas, CCTV, enhanced lighting, staff call alarms and security services.Personal safety measures, including access to personal alarms, body worn cameras and enhanced security protocols in identified high-risk areas.NIAS has introduced a range of measures to help protect staff physical safety and well-being.This includes the provision of body-worn cameras which are available to paramedics attending calls. These act as a potential deterrent and allow staff to capture video footage of instances of violence and aggression.NIAS has a dedicated Violence Reduction Officer in post whose role is to raise awareness about the risks and ensure that appropriate steps are taken following an incident, including that staff have access to psychological support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of instances of abuse towards staff that have been recorded in each of the last five years; and (ii) the action taken by management as a result.
answer_text: My Department has one recorded case of unacceptable behaviour (covers violent, aggressive, abusive or offensive behaviour) towards staff. A case can include a number of instances and span several years.An Unacceptable Behaviour (by customers) Policy sets out my Department’s approach to dealing with unacceptable behaviour when it occurs.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of instances of abuse towards staff that have been recorded in each of the last five years; and (ii) the action taken by management as a result.
answer_text: There have been no recorded instances of abuse towards staff within The Executive Office in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of instances of abuse towards staff that have been recorded in each of the last five years; and (ii) the action taken by management as a result.
answer_text: On occasions, abuse is directed towards staff within the Department from members of the public or individuals engaging with the Department.  Information on the number of instances of such abuse is not recorded routinely within the Department.  The Department has an Unacceptable Behaviour Policy which provides guidance to staff on how to respond to any unacceptable behaviour from any individual in contact with the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting time for an appointment with an addiction doctor in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: New patients are currently assessed across 3 geographical areas within the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust area –Ards, Lisburn and Downpatrick. The waiting times for a new patient assessment vary across the Trust due to population demand across the 3 geographical areas.As such the waiting times for a new patient assessment to the Community Addiction Service across each area is as follows;•	Ards – patient will be seen within 2 weeks•	Lisburn – patient will be seen within 4 weeks•	Downpatrick – patient will be seen immediately on referral

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to provide a breakdown of the (i) amount; and (ii) percentage of the total funding provided through Asset Recovery Community Scheme in each of the last ten years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Since it was first launched in 2012 the Asset Recovery Community Scheme (ARCS), has awarded approximately £9 million to projects aimed at tackling crime and the fear of crime.  It funds valuable initiatives, mainly in community settings, that actively help victims, communities or the environment in support of a culture of lawfulness.  A breakdown of the amount of funding in each of the last ten years is provided below;YearAllocations to ARCS2023/24£586,584.832022/23£342,611.672021/22£506,174.982020/21£423,945.812019/20£1,120,602.222018/19£718,549.372017/18£947,138.552016/17£836,012.142015/16£809,462.292014/15£1,594,719.41Whilst my Department does not hold a breakdown of funding by constituency area, there is information available on funding allocated by council area since the 2021/22 Scheme. Further detail on ARCS awards more generally is available athttps://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/publications/asset-recovery-community-schemesIt should be noted that whilst many projects will work in specific locations, many would impact on a Northern Ireland-wide basis, rather than within a single geographical area.

department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 20461/22-27, to clarify the contractor resources issues that delayed the repair of the boiler at Comber Primary School.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that the delay in repairing the boiler was due to the sourcing of replacement parts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of specialist addiction doctors assigned to the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust Addiction Team.
answer_text: There are currently 3 full time specialist Addiction Psychiatrists in the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust area. This equates to one full time Addiction Psychiatrist post which has been filled on a substantive permanent basis in the SEHSCT, and 2 full time posts which have been filled on a locum basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the amount of money obtained, following the seizure of criminal assets, in each of the last ten years; (ii) how much of this money was provided to agencies responsible for the recovery of the assets; and (iii) the basis on which the allocation of the amount provided to agencies responsible for the recovery of the assets is calculated.
answer_text: Since it was first launched in 2012 the Asset Recovery Community Scheme (ARCS), has awarded approximately £9 million to projects aimed at tackling crime and the fear of crime.  It funds valuable initiatives, mainly in community settings, that actively help victims, communities or the environment in support of a culture of lawfulness.  The amount of money obtained, following the seizure of criminal assets, in each of the last ten years ;Financial YearTotal Confiscation2023//24£1,173,169.612022/23£683,090.412021/22£1,012,349.952020/21£847,891.692019/20£2,241,204.432018/19£1,438,098.742017/18£1,894,277.102016/17£1,672,024.282015/16£1,618,924.572014/15£3,189,438.81Please note, due to rounding there may be slight fluctuations in the overall figures.Under the Assets Recovery Incentivisation Scheme (ARIS), law enforcement agencies receive back a portion of the proceeds from their asset recovery cases to further augment asset recovery performance and prevent crime. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14675/22-27 and AQW 16948/22-27, for an update on (i) progress of the proposed Newtownards park and ride facility; (ii) the outstanding title issues affecting the site; and (iii) whether the monies due to Ards and North Down Borough Council for the William Street and Hardford Link site have now been paid.
answer_text: The planning application for this proposed Park and Ride facility was submitted in November 2023. Responses have been received from relevant statutory bodies and we are currently liaising, as appropriate, to any requests for clarifications.My Department vested the subject site from Ards and North Down Borough Council on 19 May 2021. However, until such times as the Council can provide satisfactory proof of their former title to the vested lands, my Department is unable to assess the final compensation settlement to be paid.I would also advise that the final compensation package for this land will need to take into account the reconveyance of part of the site back to the Council to facilitate their plans for a new play park.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to introduce a new Living Over the Shops grant scheme.
answer_text: I refer the member to my previous answer on this topic in AQW 18197/22-27. I have no plans to introduce a new Living Over the Shops grant Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of staff that have left the Northern Ireland Prison Service in the last 12 months.
answer_text: Data from 1 April 2024 has not yet been published and the planned publication date is summer 2025.	The most recently published data from HR Connect and additional DoJ Databases is 1 April 2023 - 31 March 2024. This shows that 89 staff (both uniformed prison grades and others) left the Prison Service in that period. The figure includes staff that left for all reasons, including retirement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 19257/22-27, to detail the amount expended by his Department in (i) mandatory reconsideration of Personal Independence Payment applications; and (ii) defending Personal Independence Payment appeals during the five year period, in terms of (a) time; and (b) the monetary value of that time.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information regarding the actual time expended and the associated salary costs to complete mandatory reconsiderations in the format requested as staff work across a number of functional areas within the PIP Centre. My Department does not hold information regarding the actual time expended and associated salary costs to defend appeals in the format requested as staff work across a number of functional areas within the PIP Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 19257/22-27,  to detail (i) his assessment of the reason why nearly 65 per cent of appeals against his Department's decisions in relation to Personal Independence Payment applications have been successful; and (ii) any steps he intends to take to ensure the decision-making and mandatory consideration process improves.
answer_text: Around 4% of all PIP decisions result in an appeal. Personal Independence Payment decisions are typically overturned at appeal due to the provision of additional medical and oral evidence which was not available to the Department’s decision maker. In addition, the Tribunal may take a different view of the evidence available to the Department at the time the decision was made without finding the Department’s determination incorrect.   My Department is committed to ensuring that decisions made are right first time and will continue to take steps to improve decision making performance including: further improving customer awareness about the use of available medical evidence at the earliest opportunity to support PIP decision making; Identifying and implementing decision making learning from internal quality checks, from discussion at regular PIP quality forums, and analysis of overturned appeals; and  Utilising feedback from customer complaints and stakeholder engagement to identify areas for improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) whether interviews for posts in the Northern Ireland Civil Service or arm's-length bodies are conducted online; and (ii) whether there are specific circumstances where this medium is used.
answer_text: The method used for interviews is determined during the planning stage of each individual competition. Interviews in the civil service are conducted face to face and online via Webex or a pre-recorded platform. My Department does not manage recruitment for arm’s length.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how information gathered by Automatic Number Plate Recognition technology at Health and Social Care Trust sites will be stored; and (ii) how this will be compliant with GDPR.
answer_text: (i) A Traffic Management System contract to control parking, preserve blue light routes and protect designated spaces once charges are abolished, was awarded with a start date of 1 May 2024. For the purposes of data protection legislation, and in respect of Clause 24.1 of the Commercial Conditions of the Contract, the appointed contractor will exclusively install and operate the Automatic Number Plate Recognition technology and will collect, transfer, process and store associated data.(ii) Clause 24 of the Commercial Conditions of the Contract, states that the contractor must comply at all times with the GDPR, shall maintain complete and accurate records and information to demonstrate its compliance and shall allow for audits of its data processing activity by the client (HSCT) or the client’s designated auditor.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the cost of the intended installation of Automatic Number Plate Recognition technology, broken down by (i) Health and Social Care Trust; and (iI) hospital site.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) why Automatic Number Plate Recognition technology is needed to implement the Hospital Parking Charges Act (Northern Ireland) 2022; and (ii) whether its use will be complemented by parking enforcement patrols.
answer_text: (i) Advice from the Health and Social Care Trusts is that, once parking is made free, without a traffic control system in place, they will be unable to control parking, preserve blue light routes, protect designated spaces and maintain safe access to their sites for patients, clients, visitors and staff.The fact that parking on our hospital sites will be free will encourage more people to utilise the car parks, including staff, visitors, patients and those without any legitimate reason to be there.  Hospital appointments and visits will be delayed or missed completely as a result of the congestion, and we would witness a significant increase in the number of complaints and negative media coverage.Automatic Number Plate Recognition (ANPR) technology will ensure that effective parking enforcement can be applied in the absence of charging and is a tried and tested determent to inappropriate parking in other free tariff jurisdictions of the UK. By placing cameras at the entrance and exit points of car parks and at areas Trusts wish to control, for example set-down areas and delivery yards, they will be able to  determine if a vehicle has stayed in a car park beyond an allotted time frame or parked incorrectly; and to identify and facilitate parking of pre-registered public vehicles and registered staff permit holders. An unauthorised vehicle will automatically receive a Parking Charge Notice (PCN) if they remain in an area without valid reason, or park in an area without authorisation.  Trusts will work with the supplier and stakeholders to set specific parameters of use in individual car park zones to minimise this potential. ANPR management information will also enable Trusts to more closely monitor the capacity of car parks, enhance the efficiency of their resource and help to ensure the free flow of site traffic is maintained.(ii) Trusts may use parking enforcement patrols to issue Parking Charge Notices in areas or sites not suitable for ANPR technology.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the alternative measures considered by the Education Authority to ensure hot meals were provided at Comber Primary School between 28 November 2024 until 13 January 2025 when there was no running hot water as a result of a broken boiler; and (ii) why the alternative measures were disregarded.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that when it was discovered that the hot water facilities were unavailable at Comber Primary School, the EA’s Area Catering Manager contacted the Ards and North Down Borough Council’s Environmental Health Officers (EHO) for advice.  The advice provided by the EHO was that in light of the need to minimise the risk of staff handling food with no hand washing facilities available, food preparation should cease until hot running water is restored, as directed by food and safety regulations. During the period of 28 November 2024 to 13 January 2025, when hot meals were unavailable at Comber Primary School, the following options were considered by the EA:Meals brought to the school from another school production kitchen - Comber PS already receive lunches from Nendrum College. With no hot water for washing hands and equipment in Comber PS and on the advice of the EHO, a decision was taken by the EA to not provide hot meals onsite until hot water was restored. Pre-prepared meals provided by an external provider – this was not possible as the kitchen in Comber PS was not able to be used to pre-heat and serve the meals.Packed lunches for all students or to pupils entitled to free school meals – this was dependent on the number of packed lunches required and the capacity of a third party supplier.As there were no options available to the EA that would enable the safe provision of hot meals at Comber Primary School during the period without hot running water, the EA decided to offer a packed lunch to pupils entitled to free school meals during the period when the kitchen was unavailable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the advice obtained from Ards and North Down Borough Council by the Education Authority in relation to the provision of hot meals at Comber Primary School while there was no running hot water as a result of a broken boiler.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that when it was discovered that the hot water facilities were unavailable at Comber Primary School, the EA’s Area Catering Manager contacted the Ards and North Down Borough Council’s Environmental Health Officers (EHO) for advice.  The advice provided by the EHO was that in light of the need to minimise the risk of staff handling food with no hand washing facilities available, food preparation should cease until hot running water is restored, as directed by food and safety regulations. During the period of 28 November 2024 to 13 January 2025, when hot meals were unavailable at Comber Primary School, the following options were considered by the EA:Meals brought to the school from another school production kitchen - Comber PS already receive lunches from Nendrum College. With no hot water for washing hands and equipment in Comber PS and on the advice of the EHO, a decision was taken by the EA to not provide hot meals onsite until hot water was restored. Pre-prepared meals provided by an external provider – this was not possible as the kitchen in Comber PS was not able to be used to pre-heat and serve the meals.Packed lunches for all students or to pupils entitled to free school meals – this was dependent on the number of packed lunches required and the capacity of a third party supplier.As there were no options available to the EA that would enable the safe provision of hot meals at Comber Primary School during the period without hot running water, the EA decided to offer a packed lunch to pupils entitled to free school meals during the period when the kitchen was unavailable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the steps being taken to address the lack of hot meals being provided to pupils at Comber Primary School; (ii) the reason it has taken so long to address this issue; and (iii) when there is likely to be a resolution to this issue.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that a fault with the hot water boiler at Comber Primary School was reported to it on 28 November. The EA’s completion of the necessary repairs was delayed due to issues with the availability of the required parts. The relevant Council’s Environmental Health Officer did not permit the preparation and serving of hot meals in the absence of a supply of running hot water. As a result, the EA immediately put in place a packed lunch service at the school. The repairs have now been made and the EA’s provision of a hot meals service resumed on 14 January.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the amount of funding made available by his Department to help promote understanding of cultural traditions in Northern Ireland; and (ii) the amount of this funding that was allocated to single identity initiatives, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department provides a range of funding which could be considered to promote understanding of cultural traditions in Northern Ireland.  The Department is unable to disaggregate grant funding by single identity initiatives as this descriptor is not a criterion used in the allocation of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14673/22-27, for an update on when the recommendations of the review on Dial-a-Lift and Disability Actions Transports Schemes will be implemented.
answer_text: The initial Phase 1 evaluation of Dial-a-Lift and Disability Action Transport Schemes made a number of recommendations to be taken forward as part of a Phase 2 Review. I have approved the commencement of work on the Phase 2 Review which is due to report initial findings in the summer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of separate incidents of criminal damage caused by prisoners; (ii) the costs to repair the damage caused; and (iii) the number of prosecutions for these incidents, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: ANSWER There were a total of 446 recorded incidents of criminal damage by prisoners over the last five years;20202021202220232024Total74688811898The total cost to repair the damage over the last five years was;20202021202220232024Total£ 71,450£ 57,300£ 74,400£98,900£ 104,650Incidents of damage to prison property may result in adjudication against the prisoner(s) under Prison Rules. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of unclaimed social security benefits for the last five financial years, broken down by benefit category.
answer_text: My Department does not produce statistics on the amount of unclaimed social security benefits, other than Pension Credit, and therefore is unable to detail the amount unclaimed for the last five financial years by benefit category.For Pension Credit, the most recently published statistics were released on 01 September 2022 and provided estimates for the 2019/20 financial year. The analysis estimates that there was £1.1m per week in unclaimed Pension Credit in 2019/20. The Department is not in a position to update these statistics at present due to data constraints relating to State Pension data. The Department is working closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to resolve these issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 15880/22-27, to detail the (i) dates; and (ii) outcomes of the discussions he has had with the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs in relation to the replacement of (a) buildings; and (b) infrastructure, which will be lost by farmers as a result of the A5 development, in the context of the problems hampering agricultural development as a result of the current ammonia policy.
answer_text: My officials have established that just one agricultural unit will require demolition as a result of my recent part-authorisation for the progression of the A5 scheme. Nevertheless, I had a very useful and productive meeting with the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on 25 November 2024 when we discussed a number of issues, including current ammonia policy and its implications for landowners affected by the A5. There is a shared understanding of the issues faced around ammonia policy and I understand that the AERA Minister is in ongoing engagement with relevant stakeholders on the matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of emergency calls that did not have an ambulance attend due to unavailability in each day of December 2024, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) have a policy whereby all patients that require an ambulance response from NIAS will get a response. There is no circumstance where a patient will be denied an ambulance by NIAS. However, the trust recognises that the timeliness of the response to patients can often fall short of that which the Trust would strive for across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 16109/22-27, for a breakdown of the length of time DfI Roads took to respond to Ards and North Down Borough Council on each major application in the last five years.
answer_text: The information you have requested is not held in a readily accessible format and any attempt to do so would require an extensive exercise to be conducted across the department which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.I can advise that within Southern Division, who receive a higher number of applications that other divisions within the department, 80% of the 3,600 statutory consultations received during the 2023/24 year were responded to within the 21 day target, with a similar percentage attained in the 2022/23 year.  A total of 20,000 applications have been received in the division over the last five years

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average time for an ambulance to respond to a call in each day of December 2024, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 16109/22-27, to detail (i) the reason why DfI Roads' response times to Ards and North Down Borough Council on major planning applications in the last five years ranks the lowest at 26.8 per cent within the 21 day target; and (ii) the steps he will be taking to address this.
answer_text: My department has worked hard to maintain and improve the performance of the statutory consultation process over the last number of years. Major applications usually require significantly more analysis and the required response time of 21 days does not take into consideration this complexity.  I can advise that within Southern Division, who receive a higher number of applications that other divisions within the department, 80% of the 3,600 statutory consultations received during the 2023/24 year were responded to within the 21 day target, with a similar percentage attained in the 2022/23 year.In the 2024/25 year, the quarterly response rate within Southern Division is 81% for minor application responses and 64% for major applications.  This represents an increase from the 74% reported in the previous quarter and in the same period in 2023/24.Officials continue to work toward improving performance and I am advised that regular engagement with Ards and North Down planning officials has been put in place to enable discussion on consultation responses to maximise efficiency on complex cases.  An internal reorganisation has also been implemented to provide a dedicated team dealing solely with Ards and North Down applications.I am committed to ensuring that our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way and intend to review the progress that has been made to date through the Planning Improvement Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 15876/22-27, to detail any discussions he has now had with his counterpart in the Scottish Parliament to improve transport routes linking Northern Ireland and Scotland.
answer_text: My intention is still to have a Bilateral meeting with the Cabinet Secretary for Transport, Fiona Hyslop MSP to discuss transport routes between the North and Scotland, including the business case they are developing for the A75.  Unfortunately due to diary commitments this has not happened. However, my officials continue to engage on transport policy matters with their counterparts in Transport Scotland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 15803/22-27, whether there is any further update on the introduction of regulations for graduated driver licences.
answer_text: My responses both to AQW 15803 and other queries on graduated driver licensing (GDL) have confirmed that it requires substantial dedicated funding, detailed subordinate legislation and significant operational planning activity to introduce GDL.I have now agreed to provide the required funding and I have therefore asked my officials to resume work on operational planning and the supporting Regulations. I should emphasise that implementation of GDL is a major project, entailing very significant amendment of current driver training, testing and post-testing arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 15804/22-27, whether there is any further update on plans to consult on proposals to introduce a lower legal alcohol limit applicable to all drivers.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to review existing arrangements and options for amendments. Any proposed amendments will be subject to public consultation in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15393/22-27, to detail the measures being taken to address the challenges around the recruitment and retention of vets in his Department.
answer_text: My Department is adopting a multifaceted approach to addressing the challenges surrounding the recruitment and retention of veterinarians.  Recent measures deployed have included the launch of recruitment competitions in addition to securing additional veterinarian resource from Government agency frameworks alongside specialised agency contract providers.This is further supported through the initiation of a Renewal programme which has the objective of establishing the optimal structure, resources, working practices and systems necessary to transform Veterinary Services Animal Health Group (VSAHG) into the agile, cohesive and responsive organisation necessary to meet business requirements, and importantly, making it an attractive place to work.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 18087/22-27, whether their office has requested and will lead a delegation to meet with the Home Office, alongside the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and the Minister for the Economy, to try to resolve the issues in respect of visas for workers in the agri-food sector.
answer_text: The deputy First Minister and Junior Minister Reilly attended an Inter-Ministerial Group meeting on 21 May 2025 where they raised issues in respect of visas for workers in the agri-food sector. On 29 May the First Minister and deputy First Minister wrote to the Home Office Minister for Migration and Citizenship further detailing their concerns in this area and will continue to raise this important issue across all relevant sectors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14519/22-27, when the publication of the revised regional strategic planning policy for renewable and low carbon energy will be published.
answer_text: I circulated a draft Executive Paper to my Executive colleagues seeking their agreement to the final policy in September and December last year, and again on 13 January 2025. The revised policy will be published as soon as possible, following Executive Committee agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of buildings that were demolished or otherwise destroyed while under consideration for listed status by Historic Environment Division in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: There have been 13 buildings which were demolished or otherwise destroyed while under consideration for listed status, in the last 10 years.  This information has been gathered from Historic Environment Division’s Northern Ireland Buildings Database. I have arranged for the detail of these buildings to be placed in the Assembly Library due to the volume of information provided (Annex A).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 14523/22-27, to detail any steps that have been taken to address the 20 outstanding recommendations of the Independent Review of Charity Regulation which require primary legislation, including when the legislation will be tabled.
answer_text: Policy development for primary legislation to implement the relevant recommendations of the Independent Review of Charity Regulation is currently in progress. Two separate Bills are planned to provide for full implementation of the recommendations.  The first Bill is planned for introduction during this mandate, subject to Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 18288/22-27, whether a recommendation has now been made following the review of the recently completed traffic assessment in relation to the potential extension of the 40mph speed limit on Portaferry Road, Newtownards, to just beyond Teal Rocks.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department’s review into the potential extension of the 40mph speed limit on Portaferry Road, Newtownards, to just beyond Teal Rocks is now complete. This review, based on records of existing speeds along this stretch of road, has determined that the extension of the existing 40mph speed at this site would not provide a tangible benefit at this time. However, I have asked my officials to review this again should further development of the area adjacent to Teal Rocks be undertaken.I am pleased to advise that my Department has completed nearly 1km of resurfacing between Newtownards and Teal Rocks that will help with surface quality. We will soon be installing a replacement enhanced warning sign for motorists travelling in a northerly direction approaching Teal Rocks, and will undertake the widening of the footway from Teal Rocks towards Newtownards to aid safer pedestrian movements along this section of the A20 Portaferry Road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 14521/22-27, for an update on the Hightown Incinerator planning application.
answer_text: My officials are continuing to undertake the necessary administrative processes, including assessing consultation advice from the necessary interested bodies and public authorities. The applicant has also indicated that they intend to submit further information to the Department for consideration. When all statutory processes are satisfactorily completed, a recommendation will be brought forward by my officials; however, at this time I cannot confirm when a recommendation will be made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the turnaround time for ambulances in the last month, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Table 1 provides details of ambulance handover times for December 2024, by HSC Trust area.  Table 1 – Ambulance Handover Times December 2024 by HSC TrustTrust AreaTotal Ambulance HandoversTotal Handover Delays over15 minsTotal Handover Delays over 60 minsAverage Handover Time Belfast2,1572,0181,0311hr 33minsNorthern1,8371,7679732hrs 25minsSouth Eastern1,1161,0526243hrsSouthern1,6061,4916561hr 50minsWestern1,7941,7106791hr 5minsRegional8,5108,0383,9631hr 58 minsReducing ambulance handover delays outside emergency departments has been a key priority due to the impact this has on NIAS’s capacity to respond to calls in the community.  These risks were also articulated in the ‘Getting It Right First Time’ (GIRFT) Review of Emergency Medicine for Northern Ireland commissioned by the Department.Ambulance handover delays are monitored on a daily basis, including average delays and longest delays at each hospital.  This information is circulated widely within the HSC system including to the Trust Chief Executives.  NIAS has implemented a range of initiatives to improve ambulance response times. The introduction of an Integrated Clinical Hub has placed senior clinicians within NIAS’s control centre who are able to provide clinical triage to improve safety and refer patients to alternative pathways through the use of ‘Hear & Treat’ and ‘See & Treat’ pathways, effectively reducing unnecessary hospital conveyances. Additionally, the introduction of mental health practitioners within the control room has resulted in a reduction in ED attendances for patients presenting with mental health crises.  Collaboration with HSC Trusts, including the Hospital at Home initiative, have further supported community-based care by minimising hospital admissions and ensuring high-quality care delivery closer to patients' homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what cognizance did the Education Authority take of the previous and current experience of reapplying school governors.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that the EA Governor Application form provides opportunity for all those applying to serve as an EA appointed governor to indicate their skills, knowledge and experiences, relevant to the responsibilities associated with the role of school governor. Schools are also provided with the opportunity to indicate, through a questionnaire, particular skills knowledge and experience which would be of benefit to the school during the incoming term of office for their Board of Governors. All applications are considered fairly and consistently by EA Governor Selections Panels. Information was provided for applicants at each stage of the application process. In completing skills information on the application form, applicants were advised; - ‘The information provided in this section will be used by EA when taking account of any specific needs of the school(s). No specific skills or experience are required to become a governor, though applicants should provide as much information as possible in this section to assist with the selection process’.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools that have raised concerns with the Education Authority in relation to the board of governors reconstitution process.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised they have responded to a number of queries and requests for information from schools, governors and members of the public since reconstitution was initiated by DE in October 2023. In that time period, up to December 2024, a total of 14 schools have formally raised concerns with EA regarding the process (10 controlled and 4 maintained); 2 principal groups have also formally raised concerns regarding the reconstitution process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions he has had with the (i) Anglo-North Irish Fish Producers' Organisation; and (ii) Northern Ireland Fish Producers' Organisation in relation to the promotion of the fishing industry since taking up office.
answer_text: I have met with Anglo-North Irish Fish Producers Organisation and Northern Ireland Fish Producers Organisation to discuss a number of pressing issues for the fishing industry but not directly on the promotion of the fishing industry. Within the £3.5m funding package I launched on 25 July 2024, provision was included for the support of industry-led initiatives to promote the industry as a career and through the promotion and marketing of its landings. My officials have highlighted this funding opportunity to NIFPO and ANIFPO in recent meetings and further discussions are planned to develop such proposals.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of funding that (i) An Droichead; (ii) Cumann Cultúrtha Mhic Reachtain; (iii) Ionad Uíbh Eachach; (iii) Forbairt Feirste; (iv) Glór na Móna; and (v) Ionad na Fuiseoige received from (a) Foras na Gaelige; and (b) his Department in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of reapplying governors that were not appointed in the (i) Controlled sector; (ii) Integrated sector; (iii) Irish-medium sector; and (iv) Maintained sector.
answer_text: There are various appointing authorities for the appointment of school governors to school Board of Governors (BoGs), therefore my Department does not hold all the requested information in full.However, information has been provided by the Department (DE) and the Education Authority (EA) to provide answers in relation to its reappointments.As the reconstitution of all Controlled and Maintained school BoGs for the 2024-2028 term of office is ongoing, DE and EA are providing its latest figures.DE figures are based on governors reapplying for a further term of office as a DE governor at their current BoG. Five DE governors have not been selected for reappointment/renomination; two from the (i) controlled sector, two from the (iii) Irish Medium sector (both maintained schools) and one from (iv) Maintained sector.In respect of EA, at the start of December 2024, 1427 serving EA appointed governors had submitted applications to EA, of whom, 1236 had been appointed as EA governors for the new term of office.The number of serving EA appointed governors deemed unsuccessful in their application to serve a further term is less than 5.Of the 191 applicants not yet placed, 184 have expressed a particular preference in relation to where they would like EA to place them for the 2024-2028 term of office. The 184 have expressed the following first preference, in relation to sectors they would prefer to serve in:  122 – Controlled, 18 – Controlled Integrated, 40 – Catholic maintained and 4 – Other maintained (which includes Irish Medium). The remaining 7 applicants have stated no particular preference in relation to sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the appointment process for members of the selection panel in relation to the reconstitution process for school boards of governors, including (i) the qualifying criteria; and (ii) who made the final decision on appointment.
answer_text: There are various appointing authorities for the appointment of school governors to school Board of Governors (BoGs). The nomination/appointment of governors is a matter for each appointing authority and therefore different processes are utilised. As such, my Department does not hold all the requested information in full. However, information has been provided by the Department and the Education Authority (EA) to provide answers on its processes.	No selection panels are constituted for DE governor appointments. Under the respective legislation, the Minister of Education appoints DE governors to school BoGs.The Education Authority advised that all those wishing to serve as an EA appointed governor for the 2024-2028 term of office must submit an application form, including those who are currently serving. All serving EA governors were personally invited, through a letter from EA, to submit an application to serve for a further term and were provided with details of how and when to do so.The application form is available online along with full guidance information and FAQs. Details on how to request a form in an alternative format are also provided. Apply to become a School Governor | Education Authority Northern IrelandAll applications are considered by EA Governor Selection Panels comprising senior EA officers from School Governance Service, School Improvement Service, and Sectoral Support Service, together with officers nominated from CCMS and CSSC to attend as appropriate. These panels are recommending panels, and all recommendations are subject to scrutiny by EA senior managers, before being presented to the Education Authority’s Membership and Teaching Appointments Committee (MTAC). It is the EA MTAC which makes final decisions regarding the appointment of school governors by the EA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the overarching aims and objectives of the reconstitution process for school boards of governors.
answer_text: School Boards of Governors (BoGs) are usually reconstituted every four years when members complete their term of office, and they may be reappointed or replaced. The aim of the reconstitution is to achieve a good balance of individuals on the BoG, based on the skills, knowledge and experience that they bring to the role. The reconstitution involves retaining existing school governors and recruiting new governors.In addition, the process aims to ensure BoGs are reconstituted in accordance with statutory requirements and are equipped to fulfil their roles and responsibilities.A typical BoG should be representative of people from the local community.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the criteria used when appointing school governors during the current reconstitution process; and (ii) whether the criteria were weighted in reaching a decision.
answer_text: There are various appointing authorities for the appointment of school governors to school Board of Governors (BoGs). The nomination/appointment of governors is a matter for each appointing authority and therefore different processes are utilised. As such, my Department does not hold all the requested information in full. However, information has been provided by the Department and the Education Authority (EA) to provide answers on its processes.In respect of my Department, DE governor appointments to school BoGs are made by the Education Minister. Applicants’ school and location preferences, along with the skill requirements and/or views of the BoG/school are taken into consideration to rank candidate(s) for the Minister’s consideration. It is ultimately the Minister’s decision as to who is appointed.In respect of EA, the Education Authority Governor Selection Panels Guidance and Selection Criteria January 2024 has been deposited in the Assembly Library on 20 December 2024. The EA have advised that Selection Criteria are not weighted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of governor positions that were to be filled by the Education Authority during the recent reconstitution process in the (i) Controlled sector; (ii) Integrated sector; (iii) Irish-medium sector; and (iv) Maintained sector.
answer_text: The Education Authority has provided the following figures and information:Controlled schools			1242 EA appointed GovernorsControlled Integrated schools		136 EA appointed GovernorsCatholic maintained schools		882 EA appointed GovernorsOther maintained schools  			62 EA appointed Governors

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of reapplying governors who were (i) reappointed; and (ii) not reappointed in the recent reconstitution of school boards of governors.
answer_text: There are various appointing authorities for the appointment of school governors to school Board of Governors (BoGs), therefore my Department does not hold all the requested information in full.However, information has been provided by the Department (DE) and the Education Authority (EA) to provide answers in relation to its reappointments.As the reconstitution of all Controlled and Maintained school BoGs for the 2024-2028 term of office is ongoing, DE and EA are providing its latest figures.DE figures are based on governors reapplying for a further term of office as a DE governor at their current BoG. 385 DE governors have been selected for reappointment/renomination with five not being selected. The formal appointment/nomination process for the majority of those selected has been completed, with a minority remaining in progress, or have turned down the Department’s offer of reappointment/renomination.In respect of EA, at the start of December 2024, 1427 serving EA appointed governors had submitted applications to EA, of whom, 1236 had been appointed as EA governors for the new term of office.The number of serving EA appointed governors deemed unsuccessful in their application to serve a further term is less than 5.EA Governor selection panels continue to meet to consider applications and remaining vacancies. Accordingly, it is not possible at this time to indicate how many governor applicants may remain unplaced until the reconstitution programme is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the rationale for extending the appointment of only five of the eight Agri-Food and BioSciences Institute board members whose term of office was coming to an end in November 2024, leaving three spaces vacant on the board for the next six months.
answer_text: The relevant legislation specifies that the AFBI board must include no fewer than  eight ordinary members, and the current composition of the board meets that requirement. It is my intention to announce very shortly a new public appointments competition to appoint members to the AFBI Board.  To allow for the completion of this process, and to ensure continuity of governance in the interim, I have reappointed five members who have been chairs of board committees and who reached the end of their first term on 30 November for a further term of six months.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding allocated to the Youth Council for Northern Ireland to support the implementation of recommendation 18 of the Department of Education’s Review of Irish-Medium Education, in each year since 2009.
answer_text: Implementation funding for the Youth Council for Northern Ireland (YCNI) ceased in 2016 when its operational functions were transferred to the Education Authority.  As my Department does not hold any records of funding that fall outside the last 7 years, we are unable to provide details of any funding back to 2009.  Any funding previously provided to YCNI, however, would not have included a specified allocation for the Irish Medium Sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 18087/22-27, (i) whether they will lead a delegation to meet the Home Office to try to resolve issues in relation to visas for workers in the agri-food sector; and (ii) whether a meeting has been requested by the Executive Office.
answer_text: I have asked the UK Government to look again at finding a visa route that works for the mushroom sector as the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme is not suited to work that is not seasonal. In addition, the current situation reduces competitiveness with the Republic of Ireland which has much more generous support arrangements and a bespoke horticultural visa. It remains vital that labour solutions can be found for the Northern Ireland mushroom sector which is reliant on migrant labour, until such time that automation is more advanced and accessible. Evidence is emerging that labour shortages are impacting on the volume of fish caught and industry representatives have informed my department that the pressure the sector is facing with regards labour availability is reaching a critical point. Defra are in the process of developing an Agri-food Workforce Strategy, which is currently in the early scoping stages of development. My officials will be involved in this process and will share further information on it, including opportunities for engagement with stakeholders, when it becomes available. Access to labour challenges are being faced by sectors across the economy, hence the Department for the Economy leads on immigration policy for the NI Executive. My Department is working with the Department for the Economy to develop an immigration policy position for the Executive to consider. As I have previously indicated, I am content to attend any possible meeting with the Home Office alongside the First Minister and deputy First Minister plus the Economy Minister.  I will also continue to press access to labour issues concerning the agri-food sector with the UK Government.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how much rent is currently owed by social housing tenants.
answer_text: In terms of rent owed by Housing Executive tenants this is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“At the End of November 2024, the Current Tenant Domestic Stock Rent and Rates Arrears were;Gross Current Tenant Domestic Stock Rent and Rates Arrears: £17,993KNet Current Tenant Domestic Stock Rent and Rates Arrears: £14,097KNet Current Tenant Domestic Stock Rent and Rates Arrears exclude the technical arrears for tenants who are confirmed to be in receipt of Universal Credit, Rate Rebate, and Social Sector Size Criteria Mitigation payments. The technical arrears are created by the payment cycles of these housing costs benefits and account for the money due to the Housing Executive had they been paid up to date at the date Gross arrear figure is taken.”The Department does not hold information in relation to rent arrears owed to Registered Housing Associations.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive or Housing Associations directly. Making direct contact with will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of public petitions received by the Assembly and forwarded to his Department in each year since its formation in May 2016.
answer_text: Since the formation of the Department for Infrastructure in May 2016 the Assembly has received 8 petitions that they have forwarded to the department.  The details of the actions taken following each of these petitions are included below, in chronological order:A 40 mile per hour Speed Zone on the A48 for the Cotton Community 20/01/21:My officials undertook a speed limit review in 2022 which concluded that the current speed limit on the A48 Cotton Road was in line with the current policy and was considered to be appropriate.However, since this review the PSNI have updated the collision data for this area. Therefore, my officials intend to re-assess the speed limit at The Cotton, this should be completed over the next number of months.Ballycastle to Ballymoney Greenway 04/02/21:My predecessor wrote to P McGuigan MLA on 15/02/2021 setting out the Department’s engagement with Causeway Coast & Glens Council on their delivery of the proposed Ballycastle – Ballymoney Greenway. My officials had written to the Council seeking details  of businsess cases that had been developed and where construction could begin within a few years.The Council indicated the business case for the proposal was at an advanced stage. My officials confirmed that an approved business case is a key requirement for securing capital funding from the Blue/Green Infrastructure Fund and encouraged council to continue to advance the greenway proposal.Adoption of Ravensbrae Waste Water Treatment Plant, Garrison, Received 16/ 02/ 21:The public petition was presented with regard to the adoption of the wastewater treatment works in Ravensbrae, with the constructed private treatment works not having been built to a standard that NI Water could adopt.Ravens Brae is a private development, for which neither my Department  nor NI Water has any power or legal remit to maintain or, indeed, to intervene.However, a meeting took place in March 2021, to provide advice to the residents on the next steps to get this Wastewater Treatment Works adopted by NI Water and to assess the condition of the works.  NI Water were unable to access the site but believe its condition is poor.  Under the PEACE PLUS EU Funding Programme, NI Water aims to protect and improve water quality of transitional water bodies including an upgrade to the Garrison works. The project has been assessed through the SEUPB assessment process and it is expected that a decision on this application will be made soon.Strategy for Expanding the Electric Vehicle Charging Network, Received 07/12/21:An Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Task-Force has been created, comprised of key organisations with a role to play in this area. The aim of the Task-Force is to represent the views of drivers, consumers, energy providers, councils and government, to input into the development of an EV Infrastructure Action Plan which will be taken forward as part of a wider Transport Strategy.The Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Action Plan for Northern Ireland
