question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of applications for a judicial review commenced against his Department; (ii) the number of successful applications for judicial review against his Department; (iii) the total amount of legal costs to his Department in defending applications for judicial review, in each of the last ten years; and (iv) the number of current applications for a judicial review being dealt with.
answer_text: The Department for Economy was established in 2016 as a result of the Departments Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 and therefore no DfE records exist prior to this date.Information which provides the answer to questions (i), (ii) and (iii) for the period 2017 to 2022 has been published by the Northern Ireland Audit Office in their 4 July 2023 report on the Judicial Review Process in Northern Ireland. This report is available at:https://www.niauditoffice.gov.uk/publications/judicial-review-process-northern-irelandIn respect of (i) and (ii), I can confirm from records held within my Department that 9 applications for judicial review were submitted post 2022. All 9 were successful. The first was settled by agreement and, as the remaining 8 were all similar in nature, the court selected and heard a lead case and the ruling in that case was then applied to the other 7 cases.  In respect of (iii) the following table provides legal costs incurred post 2022:YearLegal costs 2023£128,0002024£342,000And finally, there are 2 judicial reviews currently being dealt with within my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of applications for a judicial review commenced against his Department; (ii) the number of successful applications for judicial review against his Department; (iii) the total amount of legal costs to his Department in defending applications for judicial review, in each of the last ten years; and (iv) the number of current applications for a judicial review being dealt with.
answer_text: Information which provides the answer to questions (i), (ii) and (iii) for the period 2017 to 2022 has been published by the Northern Ireland Audit Office in their 4 July 2023 report on the Judicial Review Process in Northern Ireland. This report is available at:https://www.niauditoffice.gov.uk/publications/judicial-review-process-northern-irelandIn 2022/23 and 2023/24 six applications for a judicial review were commenced against the Department of Education.In 2022/23 and 2023/24 no applications for judicial review against the Department of Education were successful. (iii) The Department of Education does not hold the information requested.(iv)   Four current applications for judicial review against the Department of Education, where papers have been served, are under consideration.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of applications for a judicial review commenced against their Department; (ii) the number of successful applications for judicial review against their Department; (iii) the total amount of legal costs to their Department in defending applications for judicial review, in each of the last ten years; and (iv) the number of current applications for a judicial review being dealt with.
answer_text: Information which provides the answers to questions (i), (ii) and (iii), for the period 2017 (following the establishment of The Executive Office) to 2022 has been published by the Northern Ireland Audit Office in their 4 July 2023 report on the Judicial Review Process in Northern Ireland. This report is available at:https://www.niauditoffice.gov.uk/publications/judicial-review-process-northern-irelandIn respect of (i) and (ii), post 2022, I can confirm that six judicial review applications were submitted. One of these is ongoing, and the others have either been discontinued, or TEO was identified as the incorrect respondent.The ongoing judicial review referred to above has been listed for full hearing in March 2025. To date, the Department has spent £2,676.68 on the associated legal fees, although this is likely to rise when the case is finally heard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of applications for a judicial review commenced against her Department; (ii) the number of successful applications for judicial review against her Department; (iii) the total amount of legal costs to her Department in defending applications for judicial review, in each of the last ten years; and (iv) the number of current applications for a judicial review being dealt with.
answer_text: Providing information prior to 2017 would involve a manual trawl of paper files which would therefore incur a disproportionate cost. Information which provides the answer to questions (i), (ii) and (iii) for the period 2017 to 2022 has been published by the Northern Ireland Audit Office in their 4 July 2023 report on the Judicial Review Process in Northern Ireland. This report is available at:https://www.niauditoffice.gov.uk/publications/judicial-review-process-northern-ireland.In respect of (i), (ii) and (iv), post 2022, I can confirm that one judicial review application was submitted, which is currently ongoing so the outcome is not yet known.It is not possible to provide a breakdown of spend on legal costs in defending applications for judicial reviews.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of applications for a judicial review commenced against his Department; (ii) the number of successful applications for judicial review against his Department; (iii) the total amount of legal costs to his Department in defending applications for judicial review, in each of the last ten years; and (iv) the number of current applications for a judicial review being dealt with.
answer_text: There are no centrally held records within my Department that retain details of Judicial Reviews, therefore details have been collated from across the Department.  Below is the collated response:  The number of applications for a judicial review commenced against my department and the number of successful applications for judicial review are detailed in the table below.2023-242022-232021-222020-212019-202018-192017-182016-172015-16Part (i)111061862001Part (ii)224122001In respect of part (iii) the following table provides legal costs incurred.  2023-24 £0002022-23 £0002021-22 £0002020-21 £0002019-20 £0002018-19 £000Legal Costs1931591052336108Due to time constraints and the need to manually review records my department has provided details of legal costs dating back to 2018-19.  During the review of our records only costs that were clearly marked as being as a result of a judicial review have been included. It is therefore possible that these totals are incomplete.  However, the totals provided are all legal costs associated with judicial reviews and therefore may also include plaintiff costs and settlement costs where such reviews were successful, thus overstating the amount that is in relation to the Department’s defence of these cases.  Finally, there are 13 judicial reviews currently being dealt with within my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of applications for a judicial review commenced against her Department; (ii) the number of successful applications for judicial review against her Department; (iii) the total amount of legal costs to her Department in defending applications for judicial review, in each of the last ten years; and (iv) the number of current applications for a judicial review being dealt with.
answer_text: Information for the answer to questions (i), (ii) and (iii) for the period 2017 to 2022 has been published by the Northern Ireland Audit Office in a report on the Judicial Review Process in Northern Ireland.  The report is available at:www.niauditoffice.gov.uk/publications/judicial-review-process-northern-irelandThe NIAO report noted, however, that the level of expenditure on judicial reviews by departments is not readily available as costs are recorded along with all other legal expenses.  The expenditure figures included within the NIAO report are therefore not complete and include a mixture of estimates and actuals.  The number of applications for a judicial review commenced against the DoJ and number of successful applications in 2023 and 2024 is as follows:20232024 (to end November)Number of applications for a judicial review commenced against the DoJ4837Number of successful applications for judicial review157There are currently 12 applications for judicial review being dealt with by the Department.  It is important to note that the figures do not include Pre-Action Protocols for a judicial review, or judicial reviews which relate to independent judicial decision making regarding the business of the courts or tribunals. For the reasons noted in the NIAO report, it is not possible to provide details of the total costs of defending applications for judicial review in 2023 and 2024.  Further detailed analysis would also be needed to identify the relevant information for judicial reviews prior to 2017 and that could only be obtained at disproportionate cost.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how many pieces of written advice the Attorney General has provided to each Department since her appointment, broken down by year.
answer_text: Legal advice given by the Attorney General is covered by the Law Officers’ Convention. This long-standing convention exists to ensure that Ministers are free to access advice from the Attorney General without adverse inferences being drawn from them having done so.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how many pieces of oral advice the Attorney General has provided to each Department since her appointment, broken down by year.
answer_text: Legal advice given by the Attorney General is covered by the Law Officers’ Convention. This long-standing convention exists to ensure that Ministers are free to access advice from the Attorney General without adverse inferences being drawn from them having done so.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how many times the Attorney General has (i) attended meetings of the Northern Ireland Executive; and (ii) held meetings with each Executive Minister, since her appointment.
answer_text: The Attorney General has attended 36 meetings of the Executive Committee since her appointment.  Meetings between the Attorney General and Executive Ministers are covered by the Law Officers’ Convention.  This long-standing convention exists to ensure that Ministers are free to access advice from the Attorney General without adverse inferences being drawn from them having done so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the performance of the Housing Possession Court Duty Scheme.
answer_text: ANSWER Since 2015 the Housing Rights Service has received a grant from the Legal Services Agency for the provision of advice and assistance at one court appearance per client in relation to mortgage repossession and ejectment proceedings. Feedback on the scheme, including an independent review in 2018, has proven positive, with stakeholders comparing the service positively to ordinary legal representation. While funding under the grant was reduced in recent years, this was reflective of lower than anticipated uptake as a result of reduced and remote hearings during the pandemic.Funding is kept under review, and I anticipate a modest increase in funding to reflect current demand. Scope to develop and expand the delivery model will be explored as part of the Enabling Access to Justice Reform Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of acts of assistance there have been at courts in Northern Ireland under the Housing Possession Court Duty Scheme in each year since its commencement in 2015.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of mobile vehicle licence enforcement cameras managed by the Driver and Vehicle Agency Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of human remains that have been found during archaeological excavations in each of the last ten years; and (ii) what happened to these remains.
answer_text: (i) It is not possible to provide a figure for the number of human remains found during archaeological excavations in the past 10 years as my department does not currently maintain a central record of finds from every archaeological excavation undertaken, and most are undertaken by commercial sector bodies. Information on individual excavations where human remains have been found can, however, be accessed in finalised excavation reports from each excavation, and these can be accessed online on the Historic Environment Map Viewer, or by contacting my department’s Historic Environment Record of Northern Ireland.(ii) As my department does not hold a central record of excavated human remains, it is not possible to provide a comprehensive response.  In general terms, human remains form part of the individual excavation archive on completion of the post-excavation analysis and finalised report.Excavation archives in Northern Ireland are generally retained by the archaeological body who excavated them, and they must maintain them in accordance with professional best practice standards. My department, however, holds archives for 12 excavations containing human remains from sites it has excavated itself, or which were excavated on behalf of other government departments, in the past 10 years.In a number of cases, my department has agreed the appropriate formal reburial of excavated human remains. An example, within the period in question, is the reburial of remains excavated on the former Downpatrick Workhouse site in 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether Historic Environment Division has a policy in place for the selection, retention and dispersal of collected material from archaeological digs.
answer_text: My Department has recently published a suite of three new guidance documents for archaeological practitioners in Northern Ireland, available at  https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/guidance-practitioners-archaeology-northern-ireland .In line with standard practice across the UK, these refer to the need for those undertaking licensed excavations to implement selection, retention and dispersal in preparation of the archive, which is the essential end product of all archaeological excavations. That guidance points to the Selection Toolkit, provided by the Chartered Institute for Archaeologists: https://www.archaeologists.net/selection-toolkit/toolkit-overview .My Department is, however, aware of the need to provide guidelines specific to Northern Ireland, which supports practitioners in making informed selection decisions relevant to the unique archaeological material which is uncovered here. This work is a current priority for the sector-wide Archaeological Archives working group, convened by Historic Environment Division. A working draft is already underway but requires input from a number of artefact specialists.  Subject to budget availability to procure this expert advice, my Department aims to produce this guidance in 2025-26.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the impact on service delivery as a result of the current level of vacancies within their Department.
answer_text: Delivery of high priority services has been maintained through redeployment of staff. However, undoubtedly Departmental work has been impacted.  We understand from the Department of Finance that recruitment competitions are going ahead to allow some vacancies to be filled in early in 2025.  The Department's approach will be to streamline service delivery where possible, then to seek the right skills match for vacancies which need to be filled and are affordable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) whether her Department pays performance bonuses to public servants; and (ii) the criteria under which these are awarded.
answer_text: The Department of Finance is responsible for pay of civil servants only, not the public sector as a whole. Within the Civil Service, there is currently one bonus scheme in operation. This is the Productivity Unit (PU) Scheme solely for Industrial Road Worker staff in the Department for Infrastructure. The PU Scheme pays bonus to Industrial 1 in addition to basic pay: the payment is directly related to an individual’s output based on Time and Motion studies of specific work activities.  It is calculated and paid on a weekly basis and the maximum payable is £2,700 per annum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the increase of traffic volume in Northern Ireland in each of the last ten years; and (ii) the projected traffic volume in five years' time.
answer_text: (i) My Department currently publishes an Annual Traffic and Travel Information Report. This presents traffic flow data for available census point locations across the network. The number of available census locations reported on in this document has decreased over the last 10 years due to resource limitations experienced across the Department. For this reason, the Department is currently unable to provide a year-on-year traffic volume comparison for the last 10 years. The latest published report is dated 2023 and can be found on the Department’s website - Traffic and travel information (incorporating annual traffic census and variations in traffic flow) | Department for Infrastructure(ii) My officials are currently developing an NI bespoke growth forecasting tool called TEMPRO-NI. This tool will provide a standardised approach to forecasting travel demand for use in transport planning studies across NI. The level of traffic growth predicted in this forecasting will be specific to each location and is generated from the forecasted changes in planning data (population, employment) car ownership rates and trip purposes. This TEMPRO-NI will be available for use in early 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact on service delivery as a result of the current level of vacancies within his Department.
answer_text: Staff vacancies are a challenge.  They can affect my department’s ability to deliver essential services and in turn create a ripple effect on employees, the quality of service and ultimately the experience of our customers.Staff vacancies create longer response times, delays in processing applications and difficulties in managing caseloads across a range of benefits. Currently my department is unable to maintain parity with GB in several areas, including keeping pace with Department of Work & Pensions plans to tackle fraud and error. Vacancies have also hampered our ability to provide equitable Work Coach support to claimants to help them progress towards finding work, remaining in work and to increase the hours they work. Disability and Carers Service has been in a new claim recovery position since April 2023 with respect to Disability Living Allowance, Attendance Allowance and Carer’s Allowance, meaning that it is taking longer to get financial support to children, carers and older people with disabilities.Staff vacancies have posed a barrier to effectively meet our statutory obligations to maintain, protect and provide access to NI’s Historic State Care Sites and Historic Environment. Development and revisions of several key policies and strategies have been delayed including the Tenant Participation Strategy, Alternative Supported Housing Models, Culture, Arts and Heritage Strategy, and policies for Museums and Libraries.Other general impacts include staff working longer hours and being unable to avail of leave, which not only impacts on employee morale but has a detrimental impact on employee well-being.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on his Department’s Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum.
answer_text: The Review of the Northern Ireland Curriculum formally commenced on 12 November.  The work is being undertaken by Lucy Crehan, an education consultant with experience of working with regional and national governments on curriculum policy.The overarching aim of the Review is to make a series of policy recommendations regarding the purpose, design, specification and implementation of the curriculum in Northern Ireland.The work is at an early stage, with desk-based research being carried out alongside a review of key documentation relevant to curriculum design.  Lucy recently visited Northern Ireland to meet with stakeholders and visit schools to observe the delivery of the curriculum first hand, speaking directly with school leaders, teachers and pupils.  There will be further engagement with stakeholders in the coming weeks and months to help inform the Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the impact on service delivery as a result of the current level of vacancies within his Department.
answer_text: There are some 3000 people on my Departmental headcount and as of Monday 25 November 2024, there are 498 declared vacancies.  My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This has had a significant impact on my department’s ability to deliver essential services, and to fill vacant posts. There has been some recent improvements in recruitment to the Department, however the attrition figure remains higher than the recruitment figure. The Department is simply not able to recruit at a fast enough pace to keep up with attrition rates.  NICS HR has responsibility for recruitment for the NICS.  Currently there is supply at most general service grades however we continue to experience significant gaps in supply at Professional & Technical (P&T) Specialist grades. DfI have currently registered circa 28 P&T competitions with NICS HR which are at various levels of development and progression. The People Development & Workforce Planning Directorate (PD&WPD) of DfI have been working closely with NICS HR to prioritise recruitment competitions for those grades which are having the most impact on the organisation.All measures that we have taken to date to recruit and retain professional people into our organisation are still heavily impacted by external challenges, the most significant issue being rates of pay. As many of our team are professionally qualified, we are competing with the highly competitive private industry to attract and retain professional colleagues. We simply are not able to compete with the salaries being offered by private industry at this time. NICS HR are responsible for the pay strategy and are currently leading and developing a pay strategy for NICS. Until the outworkings of this is known, we will continue to struggle with this issue.All three key groups within my Department continue to be challenged to deliver essential services whilst running with the current level of vacancies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact that proposed Post Office closures will have on the affected towns and city centres.
answer_text: Question 13 – AQO 1225/22-27The loss of any business is highly regrettable, with the potential to have a damaging effect on high streets and town centres.  I am disappointed to learn of these potential closures, on top of the recent shutdown of bank branches. I am committed to cooperating with my Executive colleagues to tackle the issues facing high streets and town centres, especially through the implementation of the recommendations of the High Street Taskforce.In support of this - I, along with the Minister for Finance, recently met with the Financial Conduct Authority to raise the issue of access to cash for customers.  The Consumer Council NI also works in close collaboration with the Financial Conduct Authority and the Post Office, to represent consumer interests.Under the Northern Ireland Act, postal services are a reserved matter and come under the responsibility of the Department for Business and Trade.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact on service delivery as a result of the current level of vacancies within his Department.
answer_text: The Department has recently restructured to deliver priorities within the Economic Plan and ensure our people are fully equipped and assigned to key business areas that will deliver our objectives.  Despite ongoing levels of vacancies, staff continue to perform at the highest level and Management are committed to communicating the current position and actions being taken.  The following areas of the Department have identified specific concerns:Skills & Education Group highlighted a risk to the delivery of careers guidance as Careers service is operating with an overall vacancy rate of 22%, and a 28% vacancy rate of specialist careers advisors. Recent specialist recruitment competitions have not yet been sufficient to fill all posts. Energy Group highlighted key policy areas that are impacted by ongoing vacancies including Departmental statutory obligations (80% electricity consumption from renewables by 2030, energy and fuel security), PfG commitments (Community Energy Pathfinder and 5 year review of the Energy Strategy) and the Executive’s Energy Strategy (a new low carbon heat and energy efficiency scheme, the Offshore Renewable Energy Action Plan, key primary legislation and a new scheme to funnel GB Energy funding).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the impact on service delivery as a result of the current level of vacancies within his Department.
answer_text: Despite the current level of vacancies, my Department continues to perform well with over 75% of actions in last year’s business plan (2023/24) being either achieved or substantially achieved. I am pleased to note that the latest NICS staff survey results indicated that my Department ranked among the highest Departments in respect of Employee Engagement Index and was 5 percentage points higher than the NICS average.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of vacancies within her Department that have been filled by an individual already employed in that position on (i) a temporary basis; and (ii) an agency basis, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: It is not possible to identify from central records held, the number of vacancies in the Civil Service in each of the last five years that have been filled by an individual already employed in that position on (i) a temporary basis; and (ii) an agency basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact on service delivery as a result of the current level of vacancies within her Department.
answer_text: Where vacancies exist across the Department, efforts are made to fill posts in line with the Department’s priorities.  On occasions, however, it has only been possible to fill some posts on a temporary basis while other posts have been vacant for an extended period of time.  That can often place additional pressures on existing staff.  It is not possible to provide a detailed breakdown of how vacancies have impacted on the wide range of workstreams across the Department.  The number of vacancies, however, has resulted in delays with the delivery of some workstreams and being able to support Executive and Assembly business in a timely way, alongside the need for the Department to prioritise resources to meet its statutory responsibilities.   The impact of available staff resources also remains a priority in the development and implementation of annual business plans

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of vacancies within her Department, including those currently filled by temporary or agency staff.
answer_text: NICSHR within the Department of Finance provides the Department of Justice and with operational resourcing services in collaboration with its service delivery partner, HRConnect.  The Department of Finance will provide a response on behalf of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of staff that have been recruited to fill posts in her Department in each of the last five years.
answer_text: NICSHR within the Department of Finance provides the Department of Justice and with operational resourcing services in collaboration with its service delivery partner, HRConnect.  The Department of Finance will provide a response on behalf of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of vacancies within her Department that have been filled by an individual already employed in that position on (i) a temporary basis; and (ii) an agency basis, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: NICSHR within the Department of Finance provides operational HR advice and services to the Department of Justice and other Executive departments.  The Department of Finance will be providing a response on behalf of the Department of Justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of vacancies within her Department, including those currently filled by temporary or agency staff.
answer_text: Detail of the total number of vacant posts in each Department is not held centrally by the Department of Finance. Each Civil Service department is responsible for managing its resources – both financial and staff. Each department determines its individual workforce needs and is responsible for ensuring that any additional staffing resources are affordable. When a Department identifies a vacant post it needs filled, the request is referred to the Department of Finance, NICSHR to initiate the process to fill the post. NICSHR liaises with all Departments on an ongoing basis to seek to prioritise recruitment activity within allocated financial and staff resources. As at the end of October 2024, NICSHR was managing a caseload of 4980 vacancies to be filled across the Civil Service. The breakdown by Department is shown in the table below.  NICSHR is unable to identify from central records held how many of the 4980 vacancies are currently filled by temporary or recruitment agency workers.DepartmentNumber of vacancies at the end of October 2024DAERA728DE76DfC2534DfE137DfI524DoF359DoH69DOJ450PPS55TEO48Grand Total4980			    Source: NICSHR

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any enforcement action that has been taken in relation to the Article 161 Agreement in the Strangford constituency in each of the last five years; and (ii) the outcome of any action taken.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and been advised that enforcement action in relation to Article 161 Agreements in the Strangford constituency has been taken six times over the last five years with the details shown:2021 - At three sites enforcement action was taken and completed.2022 - At one site enforcement action was taken and completed.2023 - At two sites enforcement action is underway where the required work has been completed and NI Water is awaiting information to allow it to progress to recoup the bond.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.  Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the number of concessionary travel passes that have not been cancelled following the death of the registered owner; and (ii) to detail the losses accrued as a result of this issue.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  My Department and Translink undertake a monthly exercise which seeks to match deceased records from the General Records Office with the concessionary fares database. Any records identified as deceased are updated accordingly and the concession passes are deactivated to prevent usage. In addition, Translink participates in the biennial National Fraud Initiative (NFI) on behalf of my Department for concession travel passes. As part of this exercise, on an agreed date, an extract of the concession fares database is securely provided to the National Fraud office for comparison with deceased records. The National Fraud office then provides a list of potential matches.   For the 2022/23 NFI, 454,024 records were provided to the NFI for comparison purposes Of the reported ‘potential’ matches provided by NFI, four concession passes were deemed to have pass usage after the reported date of death with a potential loss of £58.77. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) number; and (ii) location of unadopted roads rejected by his Department in each of the last five years, broken down by the grounds and basis on which they have been rejected.
answer_text: The number and location of developments that are 80% occupied but have not been adopted by my Department in the last 5 years due to the developer failing to provide the roads infrastructure to the appropriate standard is contained in the list of Priority 2 and 3 backlog developments which has been deposited in the Assembly Library in answer to AQW 18205/22-27.Unfortunately, the grounds for the development remaining unadopted is not easily obtainable from the Department’s database and any attempt to do so would require an extensive exercise to be conducted which might only be expected to produce data with a limited degree of exactitude, and this exercise itself would very likely involve disproportionate costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration his Department has given to the introduction of no overtaking zones, with associated signage, along the Portaferry Road, Shore Road and Rowreagh Road on the Ards Peninsula to improve road safety.
answer_text: Following the Adjournment Debate on 12 March 2024, I asked my officials to take forward a review of the A20 Portaferry Road from Newtownards to Teal Rocks to establish if engineering measures could be taken forward to enhance road safety.  I am pleased to advise that nearly 1km of resurfacing from Newtownards to just beyond Teal Rocks has been recently completed. This project included several key improvements including a new bus layby and footway near Strangford Filling Station, as well as an uncontrolled pedestrian crossing.  These improvements enhance public transport access and road safety for vulnerable users, while the resurfacing and refreshed markings improve driver visibility and aid lane discipline. This work is part of a 10-year resurfacing plan for the A20, Portaferry Road, subject to available funding.Prior to the recent resurfacing outlined above, this section was assessed for the introduction of a double white line to prevent overtaking manoeuvres, however, it was determined that sufficient road width did not allow for its introduction in the vicinity.My officials are also reviewing a recently completed traffic assessment regarding the potential extension of the 40mph speed limit from Newtownards to just beyond Teal Rocks. It is anticipated that this review will be finalised in the coming weeks.  Following the latest release of Road Traffic Collision data from the PSNI, my officials will be reviewing sites where there are clusters of collisions to identify if there are any common causation factors which would benefit from the introduction of effective road safety measures. This will include any sites on Portaferry Road, Rowreagh Road and Shore Road that meet the intervention criteria for assessment. Any proposed engineering measures will be considered for inclusion in a future work programme, subject to available funding.Concerns regarding driver behaviour such as speeding should be directed to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.  One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, further to the unadopted roads in the Strangford constituency for which a preliminary certificate of adoption was issued more than 12 months ago, to detail the steps that (i) have been taken; and (ii) will be taken to resolve any outstanding issues.
answer_text: The construction of private developments, including the provision of roads and sewerage infrastructure, is a developer led process with timescales for completion dependent on the developer and scale of the development.  The statutory basis for my Department to adopt privately constructed roads and streets is provided within the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980 (the Order) and the Private Streets (Amendment) Order 1992.Under the Order, my Department will only adopt those carriageways and footways, cycle tracks, footpaths, visibility splays and verges that are considered necessary for public access and passage, provided that they are laid out in accordance with policy and constructed to defined standards.Prior to construction, a developer is required to enter into an Agreement, to provide the roads, footways and sewers to the standards prescribed in The Private Streets (Construction) Regulations (NI) 1994.  This agreement is secured by a bond, that may be used by my Department to complete the road works should the developer default. It is an offence to commence building works without making prior provision for street works expenses.The information requested is not readily available from the Department’s database in the format requested. My officials could only obtain this information at a disproportionate cost as it would require an extensive trawl of records.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the developments in the Strangford constituency that have been issued with a preliminary adoption certificate by DfI Roads in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The construction of private developments, including the provision of roads and sewerage infrastructure, is a developer led process with timescales for completion dependent on the developer and scale of the development.  The statutory basis for my Department to adopt privately constructed roads and streets is provided within the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980 (the Order) and the Private Streets (Amendment) Order 1992.Under the Order, my Department will only adopt those carriageways and footways, cycle tracks, footpaths, visibility splays and verges that are considered necessary for public access and passage, provided that they are laid out in accordance with policy and constructed to defined standards.Prior to construction, a developer is required to enter into an Agreement, to provide the roads, footways and sewers to the standards prescribed in The Private Streets (Construction) Regulations (NI) 1994.  This agreement is secured by a bond, that may be used by my Department to complete the road works should the developer default. Once the developer joins the development to a public road and constructs it to an adoptable standard, my Department may issue a preliminary certificate. The developer is then responsible for a further one-year maintenance period, during which time they are responsible for the repair of any defects and retain full liability for the roads and footways. Following this period of 1 year, a final certificate can be issued and the development, in full or in part, may be adopted. However, the roads and footways, identified in the one year maintenance period, must be repaired by the developer prior to my department adopting the roads and footways. The responsibility is with the developer to repair the defects and then request a further pre-adoption inspection to ensure the infrastructure is suitable for adoption onto the public network.  The information requested is not readily available from the Department’s database in the format requested. My officials could only obtain this information at a disproportionate cost as it would require an extensive trawl of records.

answer_text: The construction of private developments, including the provision of roads and sewerage infrastructure, is a developer led process with timescales for completion dependent on the developer and scale of the development.  The statutory basis for my Department to adopt privately constructed roads and streets is provided within the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980 (the Order) and the Private Streets (Amendment) Order 1992.Under the Order, my Department will only adopt those carriageways and footways, cycle tracks, footpaths, visibility splays and verges that are considered necessary for public access and passage, provided that they are laid out in accordance with policy and constructed to defined standards.Prior to construction, a developer is required to enter into an Agreement, to provide the roads, footways and sewers to the standards prescribed in The Private Streets (Construction) Regulations (NI) 1994.  This agreement is secured by a bond, that may be used by my Department to complete the road works should the developer default. Once the developer joins the development to a public road and constructs it to an adoptable standard, my Department may issue a preliminary certificate. The developer is then responsible for a further one-year maintenance period, during which time they are responsible for the repair of any defects and retain full liability for the roads and footways. The information requested is not readily available from the Department’s database in the format requested. My officials could only obtain this information at a disproportionate cost as it would require an extensive trawl of records.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the policy on the building or purchase of commercial property by social landlords.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive hold no policy in relation to the building and/or purchase of Commercial properties.The Housing Executive (Development Programme Group) is responsible for the management of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) and can only advise on the building/purchase of commercial property by housing associations where this is included as part of a wider mixed-use development including the delivery of new social housing. In such cases, the commercial property element is separated out as a Non-Qualifying Cost, in line with the Housing Association Guide (www.communities-ni.gov.uk/articles/qualifying-and-non-qualifying-costs), and the housing association is not eligible to claim Housing Association Grant associated with the commercial element of the development.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.Article 15 of the Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1992 sets out the purposes and objects that a Housing Association may have, in order that it can be registered. This legislation includes allowable powers in relation to commercial premises.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider a policy for future social housing builds to be built to passive housing standards.
answer_text: My Department and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive have invited Registered Housing Associations to bring forward energy efficiency pilot schemes (including Passivhaus) through the Social Housing Development Programme. It is hoped that this will help inform future housing policy decisions on climate change, advance decarbonisation, lower future retrofitting costs, reduce fuel poverty, and support the sector in building a knowledge base.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of commercial properties that have been (i) built; and (ii) purchased by social landlords in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“We can confirm the Housing Executive have not built or purchased any commercial properties in the last 10 years.The Housing Executive (Development Programme Group) is responsible for the management of the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) and can only advise on the building/purchase of commercial property by housing associations where this is included as part of a wider mixed-use development including the delivery of new social housing. There have been limited examples of commercial property being included as part of mixed-use development (including new social housing) and the Housing Executive does not hold records of this information, as it is only required to be detailed as a Non-Qualifying Cost (i.e. not eligible for Housing Association Grant) by housing associations as part of their Application for Project Approval for new social housing schemes.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.My Department does not hold the information requested in relation to Housing Associations.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQT604/22-27 and its supplementary, for an update on a proposed cross-departmental delegation to meet with the Home Office to try to resolve the issues in respect of visas for workers in the agri-food sector.
answer_text: I am aware of the labour challenges faced by the mushroom, fishing and butchery sectors due to UK Immigration Policy rules. I am particularly concerned about the emerging situation in the mushroom and fishing sectors and the viability of these sectors is under threat. 			Our mushroom sector has reduced to a small number of albeit relatively large farms, and it needs to remain competitive with the Republic of Ireland which has much more generous support arrangements and a bespoke horticultural visa.  		Evidence is emerging that labour shortages are impacting on the volume of fish caught and industry representatives have informed my department that the pressure the sector is facing with regards labour availability is reaching a critical point.		I wrote to the previous Home Secretary, The Rt Hon James Cleverly MP, in March 2024 and raised my opposition to the changes to UK migration policy, specifically the increase of the minimum salary threshold for skilled workers. At that time, I sought a meeting with the Home Secretary to discuss these issues but did not receive a response to this proposal. 		I outlined the key issues facing the NI agri-food industry as a result of UK Immigration policy changes in a letter to the Defra Secretary of State in October 2024.  In the letter I provided suggestions of modest changes to UK immigration policy to provide a resolution to these issues, but I was very disappointed with the response, in which my suggestions were rejected. I and my officials continue to engage with Defra, the Home Office and the Migration Advisory Committee on these issues to seek resolutions.		Access to labour challenges are being faced by sectors across the economy, hence the Department for the Economy leads on immigration policy for the NI Executive. My Department is working with the Department for the Economy to develop an immigration policy position for the Executive to consider. Similarly, I am content to attend any possible meeting with the Home Office alongside the First Minister and deputy First Minister plus the Economy Minister.  I will also continue to press access to labour issues concerning the agri-food sector with the UK Government.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the policy under Dignity at Work in relation to the wearing of sports paraphernalia.
answer_text: References to the wearing of sporting paraphernalia are included in a number of Assembly Commission policies to take account of the different categories of users of Parliament Buildings. For staff of the Assembly Commission, the Staff Handbook contains a Dignity at Work policy which aims to make Assembly Commission staff aware of the types of behaviour that might cause offence.  It also highlights the sources of information and assistance which are available and the procedures for dealing with unwanted, unreasonable and offensive behaviours. The Dignity at Work Policy details some of the common forms of unwanted, unreasonable and offensive conduct dealt with under this policy and provides specific examples of such conduct. In relation to the wearing of sports paraphernalia, the policy states that:“the wearing of distinctive clothing or sportswear which may be deemed offensive by others, for example, football, gaelic, rugby tops”The Dignity at Work Policy covers all Assembly Commission staff, agency workers and inward secondees, however the Policy does not cover Members or Members’ support staff, other building passholders who are not Assembly Commission staff or visitors.   In relation to visitors, the Assembly Commission’s Conduct and Behaviour of Visitors in Parliament Buildings Policy states that:“Persons will not be permitted to enter Parliament Buildings if they are –wearing uniform or displaying regalia or emblems which could be construed as divisive, inappropriate or offensive, or which could be identified as sectarian or aligned to one particular section of the community;wearing any item of clothing with logos, illustrations or print displayed which may be deemed offensive, inappropriate or has the potential to cause disharmony, and especially where this might be linked to community conflict in Northern Ireland or to any matter being debated in the Chamber or in Committee.”In the specific context of sponsored events, the Assembly Commission’s Sponsored Events in Parliament Buildings Policy sets out the Commission’s policy on the wearing of sporting attire stating that the wearing of sporting attire (including blazers, tops, tracksuits) at sponsored events is:“Permissible with triple sponsorship and where those wearing are representing their sport in an official capacity.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any assessment that has been made in relation to the number of blue badges which may still be in use despite the holder having died; and (ii) any steps he is taking to address this issue.
answer_text: The Government Records Office for Northern Ireland provides my Department’s Blue Badge Unit with a monthly report on registered deaths. This information is used to update the Blue Badge system whereby badges belonging to deceased holders are cancelled.  Traffic Attendants are trained to detect Blue Badge fraud, and last year, over 100 cases were detected. Most of these cases were for use of a Blue Badge belonging to a deceased relative. Once fraud is detected, a report is sent to the PSNI, who will take appropriate action.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people estimated to be living in fuel poverty (i) currently; and (ii) in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the NIHE, who produce fuel poverty statistics as part of their research for the Northern Ireland House Condition Survey.  She has advised me as follows:The estimates of fuel poverty were modelled by BRE using data from the 2016 Northern Ireland House Condition Survey as a base and adjusting the three main components of fuel poverty (fuel prices, household incomes and energy efficiency) to each year of interest.  These modelled estimates of fuel poverty were produced for 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020 and 2021 (please see Table 1 below). It was not possible to produce further fuel poverty estimates due to the length of time from the base year data (2016).   Estimates of fuel poverty in Northern Ireland Table 1: Estimates of fuel poverty in Northern Ireland, 2016 to 2021. Fuel poor households*2016**2017***2018***2019***2020***2021***Number of households160,000128,000131,000179,000180,000179,000% of households221718242424* Full income definition** Base position from NIHCS*** Modeled estimatesSupporting NotesBase: all households, Northern Ireland 2016.Please note that estimates from 2017-2021 have been modelled from the 2016 position.

answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) does not have specific coding in the finance system to track expenditure in relation to expelled and suspended pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether the Access and Inclusion Programme will open for applications this year; and (ii) the budget that has been allocated to it.
answer_text: Since 2018, the Department has been running an Access and Inclusion Capital Programme which aims to improve access and increase participation for persons with a disability at arts, cultural and recreation venues. The 2024/25 reduced capital budget available to my Department has presented significant challenges. Therefore, the vast majority of capital budget allocated to arts, culture and sport has been utilised to meet contractual commitments, statutory, and health and safety projects. Given the constrained financial environment there has been no budget available to allocate to the Access and Inclusion Programme which unfortunately means there are no plans to launch in 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount his Department has paid to the private health care sector in each of the last five years, broken down by the reason for the spend.
answer_text: Expenditure reported by each Health and Social Care Trust on the private health care sector in each of the last five years is provided in the table below. This expenditure has been incurred in relation to waiting list initiatives and elective care reform. This expenditure is to address the current capacity and demand gap in elective to help reduce delays in patient access to care. question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the amount of (i) capital; and (ii) resource funding their Department or its arm’s-length bodies has provided to Glór Na Móna since 2007.
answer_text: Neither the Department nor any of its Arms’ Length Bodies (ALBs) have provided capital or resource funding to Glór Na Móna since 2007.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 17097/22-27, and further to the Department of Environment, Agriculture and Rural Affairs press release on 17 October 2024 in relation to sentencing for waste criminality, to detail the amount of legal costs and associated expenses expended by the (i) Public Prosecution Service of Northern Ireland; and (ii) PSNI for (a) extradition; and (b) prosecution.
answer_text: The Public Prosecution Service (PPS) is independent of my Department and does not fall within the scope of my responsibility. You may, therefore, wish to write directly to the Director of Public Prosecutions to request details of expenditure that PPS may have that is relevant to your question. My Department does not hold information on expenditure on legal costs and associated expenses relating to sentencing for waste criminality undertaken by the PSNI.  The recording of this information is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of (i) capital; and (ii) resource funding his Department or its arm’s-length bodies has provided to Glór Na Móna since 2007.
answer_text: The Department for Communities and its arm’s length bodies have provided £74,000 in capital funding and £617,729 in resource funding to Glór na Móna since the establishment of the Department in 2016.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount of (i) capital; and (ii) resource funding her Department or its arm’s-length bodies has provided to Glór Na Móna since 2007.
answer_text: No capital or resource funding has been provided to Glór na Móna by my Department since its formation in April 2010.My Department does not hold information in relation to funding provided to Glór na Móna by the following Non-Departmental Public Bodies of my Department: Police Service of Northern Ireland;Northern Ireland Policing Board;Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust;Office of the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland;RUC George Cross Foundation;Probation Board for Northern Ireland;Northern Ireland Police Fund; andCriminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland.You may, therefore, wish to contact them directly to request the information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to the RAISE programme, to detail (i) how the seven criteria were identified and used together; (ii) the weighting that was used when applying these criteria; (iii) how the income deprivation and health and disability deprivation criteria are measured and how these impact upon the selection methodology; and (iv) when the Department will publish the methodology used and results obtained from this to produce the list of schools and areas invited to participate.
answer_text: The criteria used to select RAISE localities reflects findings and recommendations from the work of the Expert Panel which published its report ‘A Fair Start’ in June 2021. It also reflects the discussions which took place with the Stakeholder Reference Group between October 2021 and September 2023. These discussions led to the development of seven indicators which incorporate educational disadvantage factors and those relating to socio-economic disadvantage including poverty, health and crime/anti-social behaviour. The indicators were used for two purposes:Shortlisting – to identify Super Output Areas (SOAs) that are disadvantaged. SOAs with four or more indicators in the top 30% most disadvantaged SOAs in Northern Ireland have been shortlisted; and Ranking – a ranking methodology has been applied to the shortlisted SOAs using GCSE achievement (5+ GCSEs (A*-C) including English and maths), i.e. lowest achievement ranks first.The highest-ranking SOA in each local government district was then chosen as a RAISE locality, with neighbouring shortlisted SOAs added to create a coherent area. No weighting was applied during the shortlisting process. The income deprivation affecting children and health deprivation and disability indicators use data published within the Northern Ireland Multiple Deprivation Measure 2017 by the Northern Ireland Statistics and Research Agency. More information on the NIMDM can be found here: NIMDM.  These indicators contributed to the shortlisting of SOAs.Work on programme design is ongoing and will continue to evolve as engagement with stakeholders continues.  The department will publish the methodology used to select programme localities and results obtained when it is practical to do so.  The localities that are to be included within the scope of the RAISE programme are subject to discussion at a locality level.  No school has availed of the RAISE programme to date as no funding has been allocated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of former employees of her Department who have, since leaving their positions, been re-employed on a part-time or consultancy basis by her Department; (ii) the applicable amounts of remuneration in each case; and (iii) the agency within her Department they were re-employed, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: ANSWERThere is record of one person engaged on a consultancy basis by the Department in the last three years who is known to be a former employee of the Department. The provision of any additional information on this engagement would potentially disclose the individual’s personal information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he plans to control and minimise the spread of sheep scab.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) keep the control of sheep scab under review in partnership with industry, stakeholders, affected flock keepers and through cooperation with our colleagues in Great Britain (GB) and Ireland.  The Department welcomes and continues to support the work of the NI Sheep Scab Control and Eradication Group and commends the group on the success of their pilot project ‘Stamp Out Scab’.  This project was co-funded by DAERA through the Biotechnology and Biological Sciences Research Council (BBSRC).The project has been successful in highlighting awareness around the disease. DAERA continues to use its social media channels to raise awareness around sheep scab, reminding flock owners to remain vigilant for the signs of sheep scab in their flock.The DAERA website provides detail on the disease including clinical signs, how the disease is spread and key advice to industry on preventing spread.  The Department also provides a range of practical support and advice to industry and individually affected flock keepers, in order to minimise spread.DAERA staff continue to report cases of sheep scab encountered as part of their official duties.  DAERA staff involved in market and abattoir inspections have been reminded to remain vigilant for the signs of sheep scab disease.I hope the above gives you confidence that DAERA are fully committed to supporting efforts aimed at detecting, further preventing and eradicating the disease.  DAERA recognise the significant opportunities that exist for stakeholders, particularly within the NI Sheep Scab Control and Eradication Group, to use their knowledge and experience to engage with flock keepers in seeking to develop new strategies and initiatives to improve flock health and welfare.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of civil servants currently paid from the public purse as full-time trade union officials.
answer_text: Civil servants may be seconded as trade union officers to recognised trade unions on a full-time basis subject to the agreement of the employing Department. Costs are met by the employing Department.  As of 1 November 2024, data held by my Department, indicates there are 27 civil servants acting as full-time trade union seconded officers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the marketing campaign in relation to the proposed Environmental Protection (Single-use Vapes) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024 will be a cross-departmental or Executive campaign.
answer_text: My officials are continuing to engage with UK and Devolved Governments to agree and prepare a joint communication plan and business guidance for the proposed ban on single use vapes. There are currently no plans for a cross departmental or an Executive marketing campaign.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how data pertaining to capturing the sale of veterinary medicines to farmers is collected.
answer_text: There is currently no mechanism to collect the data pertaining to the capture of sales of veterinary medicines to farmers specifically in either the UK or NI. However, the pharmaceutical companies have a statutory requirement to report the quantity of authorised veterinary antibiotics sold for use in all animal species throughout the UK to the Veterinary Medicines Directive since 1993.  This data is published publicly on an annual basis within the UK Veterinary Antibiotic Resistance and Sales Surveillance Report (UK- VARSS), but at present is not broken down by region.  Many antibiotics are authorised for use in more than one species, for example in both cattle and dogs. It is not possible to ascertain from sales data for which species the antibiotic was sold.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, regarding the Royal Ulster Winter Fair on 14 December 2024 at the Eikon Exhibition Centre, (i) whether his Department plans (a) to financially support it; and (b) to take exhibition space at it; and (ii) whether he plans to attend.
answer_text: Due to the challenging budgetary position, my department is not providing financial support to Winter Fair nor will it exhibit at this year’s event. However, a number of DAERA staff, including officials from CAFRE’s Knowledge Advisory Service, will be in attendance.  Appreciating the importance of the RUAS Winter Fair to our farmers, our dairy industry and agricultural sector workers and leaders, I am hopeful of attending the event on Thursday 12 December 2024, subject to diary commitments.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what consideration has been given to address the increasing traffic congestion problems around the Castlebawn roundabout, Newtownards.
answer_text: My Department is aware that a build up of traffic can occur at peak times in the vicinity of the Castlebawn roundabout, Newtownards. The recently completed resurfacing works on the A20 Portaferry Road Newtownards and the suspension of the Strangford Lough Ferry Service from Monday 14 October 2024 for five weeks has added to the traffic congestion in recent weeks however, traffic volumes should reduce now that the A20 Portaferry Road work is complete with a further reduction when the ferry service resumes.  My Department previously carried out a review of the nearby Comber Road roundabout and introduced improvements to help vehicular movements which also provided benefit at the Castlebawn roundabout.  I have asked my officials to continue to monitor the area and consider any further opportunities to assist in reducing the traffic congestion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail which agricultural shows he attended in 2024.
answer_text: I have been delighted to attend many of our local agricultural shows during 2024, both as Minister and in a personal capacity. These have included the Balmoral Show, Omagh Show and Castlewellan Show as well as Ballymoney Show, Lurgan Show and Saintfield Show. I also attended the National Ploughing Championships in Co Laois.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on his Department’s evaluation of the recommendations contained within the Independent Resilience Review of Agricultural Shows in Northern Ireland August 2022, published in June 2023.
answer_text: The Independent Resilience Review of Agricultural Shows brought forward recommendations for both industry and government aimed at maximising the sustainability of agricultural shows in the years ahead including future funding options.Due to the challenging budgetary situation opportunities for financial support from DAERA for agricultural shows, in the form of sponsorship, is extremely limited. I met with representatives of the Northern Ireland Shows Association (NISA) and Irish Shows Association (ISA) on 01 July 2024 and advised them that any future financial support would be within the context of rural development/future rural policy interventions. I also encouraged them to engage in strategic discussions with their local councils, drawing on the related findings of the Independent Resilience Review in support of their applications for funding.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether an assessment will be carried out in relation to the need for traffic lights at the Blenheim Drive junction with Comber Road, Newtownards, in light of the recent collision history.
answer_text: My Department is aware of a recent road traffic incident on Saturday 19 October 2024 which is currently being investigated by the PSNI.  On conclusion of the investigation, the PSNI will engage with my Department as appropriate.  Traffic management measures have been introduced on the Comber Road which include central hatching road markings with right turn pockets and pedestrian refuge islands to facilitate safe crossing for pedestrians.  These measures also enhance driver awareness and help to reduce speeds and improve overall road safety in the area.I have also asked my officials to undertake an assessment for traffic signals on the Comber Road, near Blenheim Drive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) why the footway resurfacing scheme in Castle Place, Carrowdore, did not take place in the 2023-24 year, despite appearing in that year’s DfI Southern Division’s Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council; and (ii) why it does not appear in the 2024-25 Annual Report.
answer_text: Whilst my department intended to deliver the footway resurfacing scheme in Castle Place in Carrowdore in the 2023/24 financial year as listed in the Annual Report to Ards and North Down Council, due to the tight timescales between the award of a new contract and the end of the financial year it was not possible to complete the programme as intended. The footway resurfacing of Castle Place in Carrowdore will be delivered as part of a larger scheme under consideration for inclusion in a future works programme, subject to available funding.

answer_text: When My Department identifies a bonded site where a developer has failed in their responsibility to construct a private street to the required standard, Article 11 of the Private Streets Order 1980 empowers the Department to issue a notice to force the Developer to bring a private street up to standard.However, enforcement action can often be a protracted process. The preferred approach to achieve completion of infrastructure works to an adoptable standard is through liaison with the developer. The Department does not collate or manage information on a constituency basis – instead it is collated on a Council area basis. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he intends to review the target processing times for planning authorities in relation to regionally significant, major and local planning applications, given the performance of the majority of statutory consultees in meeting their target times for a responses over the last seven years.
answer_text: There are multiple interrelated factors in the operation of the development management process, not least of which is adequacy of resources available to the key delivery bodies, which currently impact statutory consultee and planning application performance.The ongoing planning improvement work being led by my Department in collaboration with local government seeks to address these challenges across a number of fronts, in order to maximise improvement to the effectiveness and efficiency of the planning system. Whilst I am not considering a review of development management processing targets at this time, as the planning improvement actions and measures need time to take full effect and the impact and outcomes in relation to performance more fully understood, it may be appropriate to revisit this position at a later stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he intends to review the target times for statutory consultee responses to regionally significant, major and local planning applications, given the  performance of the majority of these consultees over the last seven years.
answer_text: There are multiple interrelated factors in the operation of the development management process, which currently impact statutory consultee performance. Not least of which is the adequacy of resources available, quality of applications entering the planning system and the numbers and levels of consultations received by statutory consultees. Whilst I am not presently considering a review of the 21-day statutory target for consultation responses, the ongoing planning improvement work being led by my Department in collaboration with local government coupled with the work of the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum will continue to seek to address challenges across a number of fronts, in order to maximise improvement to the effectiveness and efficiency of the planning system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether a policy (i) exists; and (ii) is in development in any Health and Social Care Trust in relation to having CCTV in areas of training for those with learning difficulties where they engage with the general public.
answer_text: The RQIA has produced guidance on the use of CCTV for the purpose of surveillance in regulated establishments, which can be found at Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority - RQIA.  This guidance relates only to the use of overt CCTV systems which may be deployed internally, externally or adjacent to regulated establishments.The Department will be bringing forward an Adult Protection Bill in the current mandate.  The Bill will set out enabling powers to make Regulations to regulate the installation and use of a CCTV system on the premises of certain establishments.  When the regulations are drafted it is intended that a full public consultation on their content will take place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, further to his Department’s press release on 17 October 2024 in relation to sentencing for waste criminality, to detail the amount of legal costs and associated expenses expended by his Department for the (i) extradition and (ii) prosecution.
answer_text: My department has not incurred legal costs or associated expenses for either the extradition or prosecution which led to the press release on 17 October 2024. The investigation and application for extradition were progressed by investigation officers from the Environmental Crime Unit (ECU) of the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA). The extradition and subsequent prosecution were undertaken by the Public Prosecution Service of Northern Ireland (PPSNI) at no additional cost to NIEA. NIEA and the PPSNI were supported during the extradition process by the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI).

This is due to an enhanced reporting mechanism whereby now non-acute Extra Contractual Referrals (ECR) are recorded separately to Private Residential Placements, since March 2024 and this is reflected within the reduction in placements recorded in the table above.  Regionally there were 12 ECR placements for Looked After Young People reported via Statutory Functions at March 2024.Number of Looked After Children placed with Independent Fostering Providers:YearNumber of Looked After Children20243622023345202229820212722020240This is a significant growth in the use of Independent Fostering provision which has been led by regional fostering placement sufficiency issues.
