question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 9403/22-27, for an update on when the Biodiversity Strategy will be published.
answer_text: A Nature Recovery (Biodiversity) Strategy is at present still under development, and it is hoped to launch a public consultation in the early part of 2025.  The timescale for publication of a final agreed strategy remains dependent on the outcome of the consultation exercise.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will introduce legislation to exempt funeral limousines as PSV classification, in line with other jurisdictions in the UK.
answer_text: Funeral cars are included as part of the Taxis Act 2008 NI (the Act). They are regarded as taxis within the meaning of the Act, as they are vehicles which carry passengers for hire or reward in the course of their business.  This is not new but rather a continuation of regulation under previous legislation (Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1981). The current regulatory arrangements help to ensure we have a funeral sector that has appropriate safety standards embedded throughout, in urban and rural settings. I acknowledge that there are differences between funeral cars and other types of taxis. However, the aim of the legislation was to introduce a proportionate licensing regime which would strike the right balance between the important safeguards that have been put in place to protect the public, and the commercial interests of operators.Currently there is no proposal to change the regulations set out under the Taxis Act NI. Any change to the current system would require a full review of the taxi regulatory framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15725/22-27, whether a shoreline management plan will be part of the policy framework as a means to identify and manage the risk of flooding and erosion along the coastline.
answer_text: The policies in the draft Marine Plan for Northern Ireland reflect existing Departmental responsibilities and require public authorities to have regard to Flood Risk Management Plans and associated flood risk and hazard maps. The draft Marine Plan requires public authorities to consider the potential impacts of proposals on coastal processes and coastal change, but as no department is responsible for coastal erosion risk management, shoreline management plans are not part of the policy framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he intends to introduce nature restoration legislation.
answer_text: The draft Nature Recovery Strategy contains objectives designed to halt the loss of biodiversity that may require new legislation. 	 	I have no current plans to bring forward a Nature Restoration Bill, as a result of a constrained mandate ending in May 2027.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) whether a bid was submitted for funding of the Biodiversity Strategy; (ii) whether the bid was successful; and (iii) the amount of funding awarded.
answer_text: The draft Nature Recovery (Biodiversity) Strategy (NRS) highlights how Northern Ireland intends to address the 23 targets of the UN Global Biodiversity Framework.  Many of the actions it includes are drawn from other policies and initiatives from across DAERA and beyond. Therefore the resource requirements have been, or will be, considered in these policies.  Any new policies agreed in the NRS will be subject to future bids, if required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department will pay the invoice issued by Ards and North Down Borough Council for the William Street and Hardford Link site for the proposed park and ride facility in Newtownards.
answer_text: My Department is unable to pay compensation for vested lands, simply upon receipt of an invoice.  My Department is awaiting confirmation of Ards and North Down Borough Council’s former title to the subject vested land to enable the final compensation settlement to be assessed and paid. It should also be noted that the compensation package for this land will also take into account the reconveyance of a significant part of the site back to the Council to facilitate their plans for a new play park.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reasons why his Department has not yet paid the invoice issued by Ards and North Down Borough Council for the William Street and Hardford Link site for the proposed park and ride facility in Newtownards.
answer_text: My Department is unable to pay compensation for vested lands, simply upon receipt of an invoice.  My Department is awaiting confirmation of Ards and North Down Borough Council’s former title to the subject vested land to enable the final compensation settlement to be assessed and paid. It should also be noted that the compensation package for this land will also take into account the reconveyance of a significant part of the site back to the Council to facilitate their plans for a new play park.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the inspection and monitoring processes that are in place for outside agencies who offer training and employment to adults with learning disabilities.
answer_text: The Department of Economy has policy remit for training and employment opportunities for people living in Northern Ireland. The Department for Communities also funds Labour Market Partnerships to help people find employment and training opportunities in their local area.The Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts commission community-based Day Opportunities to improve public participation and offer greater choice and control of day activities. These include training and supported employment opportunities for people with learning disabilities, which are typically delivered by the independent sectorAlthough these services are currently not regulated by the RQIA, Trusts have established checks and procedures as part of their normal commissioning processes to ensure the quality and safety of the support provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps he is taking to improve the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland response time to local planning authority consultations on local planning applications from the current 70 per cent within the 21 day response target.
answer_text: Of the 67 consultation requests of local significance received by the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI), 47 were delivered within target, 11 consultation responses were delivered beyond the 21 day response target and 9 were reported as outstanding.  Whilst there was a delay in responding to 9 specific outstanding cases, these cases were subsequently resolved.In respect of the responses that did not meet the 21 day response target or were outstanding, this is attributed to a number of factors including incorrect classification of consultations by the Local Planning Office (LPO), issues with document accessibility / document absence on the planning portal or delays in receiving accurate information from stakeholders.  In addition, when detailed analysis and modelling is required, a request for an extension to the 21 day period is submitted which pauses the response target.  However, there has been occasions when this request has not been acknowledged by the LPO and as a result the response has been recorded as late.  Statutory consultations are divided into 3 main categories of significance.  This includes regional, major and local.  Of the 81 statutory consultations received by the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI) during the 2023 – 2024 period, 1 was regionally significant, 13 were of major significance and 67 were of local significance.  Upon review of the above, it is not considered that immediate actions are necessary to improve the HSENI’s response time to local planning authority consultations on planning applications of local significance. However, HSENI seek to continually improve things within their control and will keep the matter under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the timelines for the DAERA-led plans in the Consultation on proposed amendments to Annex A of the Joint Fisheries Statement.
answer_text: Subject to the final UK Government response to the consultation on amendments to Annex A of the Joint Fisheries Statement, the timeline for the publication of the fisheries management plans for Irish Sea Pelagic, Irish Sea Demersal and the Northern Ireland Non-Quota Shellfish is by the end of 2026. The timeline for the publication of the fisheries management plan for periwinkles is by the end of 2027.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps he is taking to improve the Housing Executive’s response time to local planning authority consultations on local applications from the current 82 per cent within the 21 day response target.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“We welcome the Housing Executive’s role as a statutory consultee in the planning process, as this allows us to provide input into the planning polices and decisions that facilitate the provision of affordable housing.  As this is a key way to increase housing provision and to meet need, we understand it is important to act effectively within this process.   We actively aim to provide planning application responses within the 21 day statutory time frame that contain sufficient and accurate information which can enable planning authorities to take decisions based on a sound evidence base. In a few instances the timeframe to respond has been missed, due to the complexity of some cases and the need to prepare adequate information for planners. As Local Development Plans (LDP) have been adopted, we are required to respond to applications which trigger affordable housing policies.  This has meant that we are receiving an increased number of applications.  In addition, we have had to work through backlogs of applications within the planning system, requiring a statement on housing need, as new policies have been applied retrospectively. We are working to improve performance by:Engaging with applicants and encouraging early discussion with Housing Executive Place Shapers and/or Housing Associations;Developing templates to standardise responses to quicken preparation times;Engaging with Council Planners on individual applications and agreeing the format of standard responses;Reduce adopted LDP application backlogs. We have also been actively working with the Department for Infrastructure through a cross governmental Planning Forum, and we are a member of the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum. These Forums share ideas to improve consultee performance in responding to all planning applications.  However, these are particularly focused on improving consultee processes for major and economically important planning applications which are seen as a higher priority than local applications.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider providing core funding to support intergenerational practice infrastructure across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Farming for the Generations pilot scheme is part of my Department’s Farm Support and Development Programme and aims to raise awareness of the need for succession planning on farms to help ensure the long-term sustainability of the farm business. This pilot scheme will bring together a range of interventions to support succession planning and intergenerational practice including:support for all members of the farm family to develop a succession plan;the development of the successor;support for the lead generation;linking farmers without a family successor to new entrants to provide access to land and other resources.The pilot scheme will provide up to 20 hours mentoring support to the farm business and they may also avail of grant funding for consultation with a solicitor or accountant, and support for the farm business to either, create a Will, or set up business partnerships or limited company.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will consider providing core funding to support intergenerational practice infrastructure across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I recognise the role that intergenerational practice can play in reducing social isolation and loneliness, including building connections within communities that can also benefit community safety. I regret that, unfortunately, I would not be in a position to offer additional funding to support intergenerational practice infrastructure due to the significant funding pressures currently faced by my Department. Funding is currently available from my Department for some projects which have an intergenerational element, through The Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC), the Asset Recovery Community Scheme (ARCS) and Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will consider providing core funding to support intergenerational practice infrastructure across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department provides sectoral infrastructure support which funds groups to work with other voluntary & community organisations to support them in building capacity and capability.  My Department has no funding programmes specifically for intergenerational practice.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they will consider providing core funding to support intergenerational practice infrastructure across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: We welcome the innovative work that is evidently ongoing and would encourage all organisations within this sphere to apply for the various funding streams that are available across departments. There is not a dedicated scheme for this within the Executive Office, but intergenerational groups can apply for activities under the relevant criteria of the schemes such as supporting victims and survivors, good relations, race and other funding areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider providing core funding to support intergenerational practice infrastructure across Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will meet with representatives of the fishing industry in person to discuss ongoing issues with labour supply and the steps being taken by him to address this.
answer_text: I have already met with representatives of the fishing industry on a number of occasions where the ongoing issues with labour supply were discussed.  I visited Portavogie Harbour on 21 February, met with representatives of the Northern Ireland Fishermen’s Federation on 22 February, and visited Ardglass and Kilkeel harbours on 28 March. In a response to a similar request by the NI Fishermen’s Federation, I advised that my officials would be best placed to meet industry at this time given that it has been agreed that they will work in collaboration with Department of the Economy (DfE) officials to develop an immigration policy position for the NI Executive to consider. I can assure you that I remain fully aware of the issues experienced by the sector and I have written to the Defra Secretary of State on 10 October to stress the need for real and timely engagement on these matters. I have requested a meeting with him at his earliest convenience to consider the process to establish a resolution to these issues across the agri-food sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when his Department will be making the necessary changes to its Building Handbook to facilitate community use of schools to include (i) the installation of floodlights on synthetic playing pitches; and (ii) the construction of sports halls with safe run offs which can accommodate current sports such as netball and basketball.
answer_text: In the context of an extremely constrained capital budget it is vital to ensure that funding is prioritised to benefit as many pupils and schools as possible. This means that the School Building Handbook focuses on providing facilities to support delivery of the Northern Ireland curriculum rather than additional facilities such as floodlights or larger sports halls.  Funding for additional equipment and facilities to support wider community use of school premises is potentially available from other funding bodies such as Sport NI or local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has a policy of only meeting key stakeholders once on any particular issue.
answer_text: I do not have a policy of only meeting key stakeholders once on any particular issue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the Catholic maintained secondary schools that currently do not have a full complement of governors; and (ii) the number of spaces available, including the category of governor.
answer_text: My Department does not hold all the requested information in full. Where possible, information has been provided by the Department and the Education Authority (EA) to provide answers to the questions in full, or in part.The reconstitution of all Controlled and Maintained school Board of Governors (BoG) for the 2024-2028 term of office is ongoing. As a consequence, the number of positions filled changes on a daily basis. It is not possible to answer accurately the part of each question that asks whether BOGs have a full complement.To provide a more accurate and consistent approach to the information provided, EA has provided its input based on its ‘closing out’ position for the end of the 2018-24 term of office. Where applicable, the information provided by the Department is based on the number of its (DE) governor vacancies recorded at the date prescribed. Those governors that have reached their end of term and are awaiting reappointment or replacement through the reconstitution of all controlled and maintained schools, have not been included within the data provided by the Department.Controlled SchoolsFor the questions relating to controlled primary and secondary schools, this means all governor vacancies recorded on 30 September 2024 (as at the end of the 2018-24 term of office). The response details vacancies listed by school and governor designation and this information has been provided by the EA. The Department does not appoint governors to these schools.There are 142 controlled primary schools and 33 controlled secondary schools which do not have a full complement of governors. The number of spaces available, including the category of governor has been deposited in the Assembly Library on 29 October 2024.Catholic Maintained and Irish Medium schoolsThe Department nor the Education Authority hold the number of Catholic Maintained Primary and Secondary schools, or Irish Medium Primary schools that currently do not have a full complement of governors. To provide a partial answer to these questions, EA has provided its governor vacancies recorded on 31 August 2024 (as at the end of the 2018-24 term of office); the Department has provided its governor vacancies recorded on the same date; the Department do not appoint to all Irish Medium Primary schools. The number of spaces available in respect of DE and EA governor positions, including the category of governor has been deposited in the Assembly Library on 29 October 2024.Integrated SchoolsThe Department nor the Education Authority hold the number of Integrated Primary Schools that currently do not have a full complement of governors.To provide a partial answer to this question, EA have provided information on all governor vacancies recorded on 30 September 2024 (as at the end of the 2018-24 term of office) for Controlled Integrated Primary schools. The Department has provided information on its vacancies with Grant Maintained Integrated (GMI) Primary schools recorded on the same date. It is und

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 12430/22-27, to detail the (i) end locations; and (ii) amounts exported, broken down by (a) incineration; and (b) recycling.
answer_text: . Northern Ireland exports waste that falls into two main categories. Notifiable waste and green list waste. Notifiable waste generally consists of refuse derived fuel (RDF), mixed wastes and hazardous wastes. Green list waste generally consists of single stream wastes like metal, paper, plastics, tyres etc. a) Waste exported for incineration (data consists of notifiable waste incineration exports only).(i) End Locations.End locations for exports of RDF only for incineration are available on DAERA website below for the each of the last five years.https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/export-records-rdf-shipped-northern-irelandInformation on end locations for other waste exports for incineration in the form requested is not readily available.(ii) Amounts exported for incineration (as provided in AQW 12430/22-27).2019 – 122,799 Tonnes2020 – 147,936 Tonnes2021 – 188,194 Tonnes2022 – 213,925 Tonnes2023 – 281,660 TonnesThere may also be some green list waste movements sent for incineration, but NIEA do not record this data on their green list waste database. Only notifiable waste incineration figures provided above.(b) Waste exported for recycling (consists of notifiable waste and green list waste exports).(i) End locations.Information on end locations for waste exports for recycling in the form requested is not readily available.(ii) Amounts exported for recycling (as provided in AQW 12430/22-27).2019 – 549,691 Tonnes2020 – 563,170 Tonnes2021 – 783,582 Tonnes2022 – 710,311 Tonnes2023 – 697,636 Tonnes

It is not possible to answer accurately the part of each question that asks whether BOGs have a full complement.To provide a more accurate and consistent approach to the information provided, EA has provided its input based on its ‘closing out’ position for the end of the 2018-24 term of office. Where applicable, the information provided by the Department is based on the number of its (DE) governor vacancies recorded at the date prescribed. Those governors that have reached their end of term and are awaiting reappointment or replacement through the reconstitution of all controlled and maintained schools, have not been included within the data provided by the Department.Controlled SchoolsFor the questions relating to controlled primary and secondary schools, this means all governor vacancies recorded on 30 September 2024 (as at the end of the 2018-24 term of office). The response details vacancies listed by school and governor designation and this information has been provided by the EA. The Department does not appoint governors to these schools.There are 142 controlled primary schools and 33 controlled secondary schools which do not have a full complement of governors. The number of spaces available, including the category of governor has been deposited in the Assembly Library on 29 October 2024.Catholic Maintained and Irish Medium schoolsThe Department nor the Education Authority hold the number of Catholic Maintained Primary and Secondary schools, or Irish Medium Primary schools that currently do not have a full complement of governors. To provide a partial answer to these questions, EA has provided its governor vacancies recorded on 31 August 2024 (as at the end of the 2018-24 term of office); the Department has provided its governor vacancies recorded on the same date; the Department do not appoint to all Irish Medium Primary schools. The number of spaces available in respect of DE and EA governor positions, including the category of governor has been deposited in the Assembly Library on 29 October 2024.Integrated SchoolsThe Department nor the Education Authority hold the number of Integrated Primary Schools that currently do not have a full complement of governors.To provide a partial answer to this question, EA have provided information on all governor vacancies recorded on 30 September 2024 (as at the end of the 2018-24 term of office) for Controlled Integrated Primary schools. The Department has provided information on its vacancies with Grant Maintained Integrated (GMI) Primary schools recorded on the same date. It is und

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action his Department (i) has taken; and (ii) intends to take in response to the judgement in Hartlands (NI) Limited's Application [2021] NIQB 94 in relation to call in on regulatory and quasi-judicial decisions, including planning decisions and the development of planning committee protocols under the Local Government Act (Northern Ireland) 2014.
answer_text: My officials have been in contact with counterparts in the Department for Communities (DfC), Local Government and Housing Regulation Division to consider how this matter should be addressed however I am aware that DfC has confirmed that any solution would require primary legislation. My department’s view is that the issues raised are not confined to planning decision-making but also to the wider functions and protocols of the Councils.  Consequently given the wider implications of this issue for Councils, an option would be to address the issue through changes in local government legislation, where the opportunity becomes available. I understand that DfC has sought information from councils on the wider issue of the use of the call-in procedure including a request for council to quantify the number of internal call-ins and, at the request of my Department, also asking for further details on any planning cases. My officials will await the outcome of this exercise with a view to further engagement with DfC.The development of planning committee protocols is entirely a matter for councils and not within the remit of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of social houses that have been lost as a result of dereliction, demolition and the right to buy scheme in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 16637/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether funding will be made available for Home Repair Assistance Grants during the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: The Home Repair Assistance Grant is a discretionary grant administered by the Housing Executive. The Housing Executive receives an overall pot of home improvement grant funding (which also covers statutory grants). Due to constrained budgets, the Home Repair Assistance Grant is currently only awarded in instances where approval of same is specifically linked to the progression of a statutory Disabled Facilities Grant. The indicative budget allocation for the 2024-25 financial year is £14.751m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 16038/22-27, to detail the financial assessment which was provided to him which led to the decision that his Department would cease testing for Maedi Visna from January 2026.
answer_text: My officials are currently engaging with industry on possible options regarding our approach to testing for MV.  Final advice has not yet been provided and therefore no decision has yet been made on this matter.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action his Department (i) has taken; and (ii) intends to take in response to the judgement in Hartlands (NI) Limited's Application [2021] NIQB 94 in relation to call in on regulatory and quasi-judicial decisions, including planning decisions and the development of planning committee protocols under the Local Government Act (Northern Ireland) 2014.
answer_text: My Department has identified that the exclusion of any regulatory and quasi-judicial decisions from the call-in process would require primary legislation.  Departmental officials have also conducted a survey of councils to establish the extent to which the call-in procedure has been used by councils, including the number of planning decisions for which there has been a request for call-in.  My officials will continue to have discussions with colleagues in the Department for Infrastructure to establish how this matter could be addressed, either through changes to local government legislation or planning legislation, where the opportunity becomes available. My Department has no role in the development of planning committee protocols.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the recent International Investment Summit 2024.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the measures he is taking to address tenancy fraud in social housing.
answer_text: My Department is currently carrying out a review of Tenancy Fraud within the social housing sector, working closely with the NIHE, Registered Housing Associations and other stakeholders. This review aims to provide a clear, robust and joined-up approach to detecting, investigating, measuring and reporting the cost of tenancy fraud across the social housing sector.  Work is currently progressing to use the Financial Provisions Bill as the legislative vehicle to bring about proposed tenancy fraud provisions which will provide the NIHE with the power to undertake tenancy fraud investigations on behalf of Registered Housing Associations.  Further legislation and action will also be considered following the Review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the actions he plans to take following the Intertidal Hand Gathering of Shellfish in Northern Ireland consultation in June 2022.
answer_text: My Department published the Synopsis Report of responses to the consultation in July 2024. The information gathered in this consultation will inform the development of a fisheries management plan (FMP) for periwinkles. The FMP will set out policies designed to harvest periwinkles at sustainable levels. A periwinkles working group involving stakeholders has been set up to assist with the preparation of this FMP.The inclusion of the FMP for periwinkles is one of the proposed amendments to Annex A of the Joint Fisheries Statement which the UK fisheries policy authorities are currently consulting on.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to bring forward legislation to regulate the harvesting of periwinkles.
answer_text: Notwithstanding a prohibition on all shellfish gathering along the shores of Belfast Lough under the Belfast Corporation Act 1930, the intertidal hand gathering of shellfish, such as periwinkles, is currently unregulated in Northern Ireland. My Department is committed to preparing a fisheries management plan for periwinkles in Northern Ireland, and subject to that plan, I may consider bringing forward legislation to give effect to any recommended management measures which would regulate the harvesting of periwinkles.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Anti-Microbial Resistance Implementation Plan for 2024-2029, that his Department is working on collaboratively with the Department of Health, is linked to the National Anti-Microbial Resistance National Action Plan 2024-2029.
answer_text: I can confirm that the DAERA Business Plan 2024-2025 Reference 31 is related directly to the National Anti-Microbial Resistance National Action Plan 2024-2029 (NAP). In collaboration with Department of Health (DoH), we will bring forward a new Anti-Microbial Resistance (AMR) Implementation Plan which will provide NI specific deliverables aimed at achieving the targets for 2024-2029, underpinned by the 30 key commitments contained in the NAP.  Officials from my Department and that of the DoH collaborated with officials in UK Government, in the development on the second UK Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR NAP) “Confronting antimicrobial resistance 2024-2029”.I gave my approval to the UK AMR NAP  2024-2029 in parallel with the Minister of Health prior to its publication in May 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the terms of reference of the periwinkle working group; (ii) the membership of the group; and (iii) how often the group has met.
answer_text: The remit of the periwinkle working group is to define the aim and objectives for the periwinkle fisheries in the Northern Ireland zone and to develop proposals focusing on securing the sustainability of periwinkle stocks, their fisheries and the environment.The working group consists of representatives from my Department, Seafish, the Food Standards Agency, the Gangmasters and Labour Abuse Authority, The National Trust, Police Service of Northern Ireland, the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute, Northern Ireland Marine Task Force, Ards and North Down Borough Council, Newry, Mourne and Down District Council, Ulster Wildlife, and local shellfish processors. The group has met once with the next meeting scheduled for Monday 21st October 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what measures he is taking to improve planning application processing times.
answer_text: Local councils are responsible for processing over 99% of planning applications, this includes local and major planning applications.I am committed to ensuring that our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way.The Planning Improvement Programme is progressing, working closely with local government and partners, to improve current processes and the performance and delivery of the system.  This wide-ranging improvement programme has already delivered important changes, including fee uplifts; consultations on policy and procedures and the development of legislation, all of which will continue to improve the overall efficiency and effectiveness of our planning system.  I want to build on the positive work that has been taken forward to date and have asked my officials to arrange a series of meetings with Councils with a specific focus on improving performance; identifying challenges; recognising success and promoting key learning across the system.Within my Department, steps have already been taken to increase staffing, mitigate and address performance of consultees. I am pleased during 2023/24 the proportion of statutory consultations responded to on-time (within 21 calendar days or longer agreed timeframe) was 74%, this rate improved from the 72% recorded on-time in 2022/23. But there is more to do and I am delighted the ‘Transforming Planning’ bid to the Interim Public Sector Transformation Board has made it to Stage 2 of the process. This bid includes proposals to support and enhance the role of my Department’s Statutory Consultees and to appoint persons to undertake hear and report work that the PAC has been unable to progress. I am hopeful that this bid will ultimately be successful.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures he is taking to increase the number of whole time equivalent GPs.
answer_text: My Department is supporting a range of initiatives at various stages of the medical education and career pathway to increase the supply of GPs.Quality and positive experience of clinical placements in GP settings at undergraduate level can be formative in medical students subsequently choosing careers in general practice after graduation.  My Department has accordingly supported re-orientation of the Supplement for Medical and Dental Education, through which it provides funding to facilitate clinical placements, away from hospital into GP settings.  This has enabled the QUB under-graduate medical curriculum to be restructured to increase the time spent in primary care from a historic low of 7% to reach 25% by 2025.  Similarly, the new University’s Graduate Entry Medical Programme places a very significant emphasis on primary care, with a high concentration of clinical placements in rural settings in the west of Northern Ireland. Over recent years, despite the well-documented financial challenges, my Department has been able to secure very significantly increased investment in the training of future GPs.  The annual number of GP training places provided through the Northern Ireland Medical and Dental Training Agency (NIMDTA) has been increased from 65 in 2015/16 to the current all-time high level of 121 (an increase of 86%).  The local GP training programme is also being progressively restructured to provide trainees with much greater direct experience of working in practice settings.  My Department is funding a range of initiatives to support the recruitment and retention of GPs, including the “Attract, Recruit, Retain” Scheme; the GP Retainer Scheme; the GP Mentor Scheme; the GP Induction and Refresher Scheme; and the GP Fellowship Scheme.  Action has been taken to streamline the recruitment processes for GPs who qualified in the Republic of Ireland and we are currently updating the Northern Ireland Induction and Refresher schemes to align with the new General Medical Council (GMC) pathways for the recruitment of GPs from other countries joining the GMC GP Register.  My Department has also introduced a Streamlined Returner process with the aim of removing unnecessary barriers to those wishing to return to the GP workforce.   In terms of increasing the number of whole time equivalent (WTE) GPs specifically, information on the individual working hours of GPs is not collected or held by my Department.  GP Partners and sessional GPs (locums) are Independent Contractors rather than employees within the Health and Social Care System.  A GP practice may employ a salaried GP and in such cases the working hours are a matter for the employer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of GPs per 100,000 patients in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: At the end of June 2024, the latest available official statistics, there were 74.6 GPs (headcount) per 100,000 Registered Patients in practices located in the Strangford constituency.(Source: Family Practitioner Services within the HSC Business Services Organisation (BSO)).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of GPs per 100,000 patients in Northern Ireland; and (ii) how this compares to other jurisdictions in the UK.
answer_text: The Business Services Organisation publishes statistics on General Medical Services for Northern Ireland annually.  The most recent report, for 2023/24, was published on 6 June 2024 Publication of FPS General Medical Services for Northern Ireland, Annual Statistics 2023/24 | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk)The report and accompanying statistics tables can be found at the link below:General Medical Services Statistics - Business Services Organisation (BSO) Website (hscni.net)The report includes information on the number of GPs per 100,000 registered patients in Northern Ireland and in the other UK jurisdictions.   It notes that Scotland has the highest headcount number of GPs per 100,000 registered patients (74.6) followed by Northern Ireland (70.8). Wales had 61.9 GPs per 100,000 registered patients while England had the lowest ratio at 57.0.Whilst the report includes a number of UK comparisons, there can be important differences in how services are delivered between countries that can impact on the figures, for example, the figures take no account of any inter-country differences in working patterns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to ensure that existing experienced GPs remain in general practice.
answer_text: I recognise the ongoing challenges facing General Practice and the impact that increasing demand and workload pressures are having on GPs.  My Department has, and will continue, to take action to address these challenges and ensure the retention of existing experienced GPs in general practice.In 2023-24, in response to the recommendation in the report of the Review Body on Doctors’ and Dentists’ Remuneration (DDRB), a 6.0% uplift was applied for GP pay.  In addition, a 6% uplift for practice staff expenses and a 7.3% uplift for other expenses (3% recurrent) were also applied.    A key aim of the new 2024/25 General Medical Services (GMS) contract arrangements is to provide GPs with greater certainty over their income throughout the year, as well as reducing administrative burdens and associated costs to practices.  This includes the provision of £5m funding for indemnity costs.Against the background of a challenging financial climate, my Department has also supported the retention of experienced GPs through the GP Retainer Scheme, which is designed to assist in the retention of GPs in primary care in Northern Ireland.  It aims to attract those doctors who are approaching the end of their career and may be considering reducing their sessional commitment to general practice and /or to retirement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15726/22-27, to detail dates upon which the Coastal Forum has met in the last three years.
answer_text: The Coastal Forum met on 21 February 2022.The Coastal Forum Working Group met on 26 January 2021, 27 April 2021, 24 January 2022, 27 June 2022 and 26 June 2023.Both the Coastal Forum and Coastal Forum Working Group participated in policy workshops on 2 December 2021 and 7 December 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15563/22-27, to detail any assessment that has been made of the reasons for the increase in glass exportation in the last five years.
answer_text: I am aware of the increased volume of glass exported for recycling over the last five years. These figures incorporate glass collected from all sectors in Northern Ireland, including households and the commercial and industrial sector, making it difficult to identify a particular reason for the increase, however, changes to local collection systems and fluctuating materials prices are likely to have played a part. Officials from my department are working with key stakeholders to try to address this to retain as much of this valuable material in Northern Ireland for reprocessing.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps he is taking to improve DfI Rivers response time to local planning authority consultations on local planning applications from the current 61 per cent within the 21 day response target.
answer_text: I am very conscious of the implications of any delays in the planning system and I can assure you that my officials are doing all they can to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of the statutory consultee process and to assist Councils to improve planning performance. The current performance issues are due to a combination of factors but is primarily due to the level of vacancies in Rivers’ Planning Advisory Unit. Work to address this is ongoing but the marketplace for engineering staff is currently very competitive.However, within my Department’s Rivers Directorate, steps already taken to address planning consultation timeliness include some limited overtime working and exploring the potential for external consultant support. In addition, the ‘Transforming Planning’ bid to the Interim Public Sector Transformation Board has made it to Stage 2 of the process. This bid includes proposals to support and enhance the role of my Department’s Statutory Consultees.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 15563/22-27, and given the amount of glass exported, to detail any options that have been explored by his Department to increase processing in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I am aware that a significant volume of glass collected here for recycling is currently exported and my officials are working with local councils and other stakeholders to try to improve this.The recent Rethinking Our Resources consultation included a range of proposals designed to increase the quality and quantity of our recycling and ensure as much material as possible is kept locally for reprocessing. The document included proposals for household recycling, the introduction of mandatory recycling for businesses and the segregation of a core set of materials for recycling, including options for keeping glass separate from other waste streams to ensure the high quality of material is preserved. The consultation closed for responses on 27 June 2024 and those responses are being analysed. A Government response will issue in due course.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps he is taking to improve the Historic Environment Division response time to local planning authority consultations on local applications from the current 37 per cent within the 21 day response target.
answer_text: HED has engaged with DfI Planning and Council Planning Authorities and took actions in Quarter 4 of 2023/24 to streamline the number of all planning consultations HED receives, reducing unnecessary consultations where possible. This should increase its performance for 2024/25.HED has also reduced the backlog of overdue consultations from 396 in April 2024 to 74 to the end of last month. In addition, during reporting year 2023/24, some of HED’s responses were uploaded electronically but did not appear on the planning portal and were recorded as being late. To address this, HED is finalising its own internal system to process its planning consultation responses more effectively. This will permit both sections (Historic Monuments and Historic Buildings) to monitor workflow and upload their respective consultation assessments separately and is expected to be operable later this month.The Division also expects to appoint a substantive senior architect to this statutory role in January and has taken on several temporary agency staff to address other unfilled permanent positions.Lastly, HED is also actively contributing to the work of DfI’s Planning Forum to improve the overall performance of statutory consultees within the planning system. Validation checklists are being finalised to help ensure received planning applications are ‘fit for purpose’, which should also increase efficiencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of why only 37 per cent of statutory consultation responses to Ards and North Down Borough Council on major planning applications in 2023-24 were on time.
answer_text: My Department monitors the performance of planning statutory consultees (two of these Roads and Rivers are within my Department) through quarterly and annual publications. There are many reasons for delays in responding to consultations on major planning applications, for example, complex applications; poor quality applications; under resourcing of statutory consultees; and in some instances over-consultation by local planning authorities. The current regional ratio for consultations on major planning applications is 10:1 in comparison to 2:1 for local planning applications and there is a significant variation in consultation levels across councils.To improve processes, my Department works closely with statutory consultees and local planning authorities through the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum - a cross government group established to address issues impacting the effectiveness of the consultation process. My Department also works in collaboration with local government through the Planning Improvement Programme and has developed legislation to allow councils to introduce  statutory validation checklists which will improve the quality of applications entering the planning system. Furthermore, my Department is bidding for additional resources for its Rivers and Roads statutory consultees in order to improve the timeliness of consultation responses.I believe that planning is a collective issue and my Department will continue to work with all stakeholders to improve processes and ensure that the planning system operates in an efficient and effective manner for all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps he intends to take to replace the Business Development Groups Scheme in order to facilitate farming knowledge transfer.
answer_text: A suite of Knowledge Transfer and Innovation schemes are being developed as an enabling workstream within DAERA’s Farm Support and Development Programme.Knowledge transfer will be delivered through a range of interventions including peer learning groups.  The peer learning groups will be a direct replacement for the previously delivered Business Development Groups.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on capital funding support for Ards Football Club.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Football Fund will provide capital investment for football at all levels. It is a key priority for me, and I am committed to moving forward as quickly as possible whilst ensuring that funding is disbursed in a fair and transparent manner. The Northern Ireland Football Fund will prioritise performance clubs and I expect to open a call for applications in the New Year. I anticipate that Ards FC will be eligible to apply to the Fund at this stage if they wish. Separately, my Department's Football Ground Business Case Development Programme has awarded grants of up to £30,000 to selected performance clubs to develop a business case to inform future development plans.   Officials are currently preparing to release a further tranche of funding from this scheme, and Ards FC will be included within this round of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise advisers to farmers in place on (i) 7 October 2024; and (ii) 7 October 2019.
answer_text: On the 7 October 2024 there were 39 CAFRE Advisers delivering directly to farmers/growers.On the 7 October 2019 there were 50 CAFRE Advisers delivering directly to farmers/growers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of full-time lecturers employed at the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise on (i) 7 October 2024; and (ii) 7 October 2014.
answer_text: The number of full-time Lecturers employed at the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs (CAFRE) on 7 October 2024 was 61. Records for Lecturers employed at CAFRE on 7 October 2014 are no longer available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps he is taking to improve the Environment Marine and Fisheries, NIEA response times to local planning authority consultations on major planning applications from the current 33 per cent within the 21 day response target.
answer_text: Over the last few years, the Department has undertaken a range of measures to improve performance on major planning applications including:A review and update of guidance and internal processes and procedures.Analysis of consultations being receipted to inform targeted measures to reduce the level of re-consultation and improve the quality of information being submitted with a consultation.Engagement with a variety of stakeholders including specific sectoral engagements with agents to improve the quality of information submitted with a consultation; andTemporary redeployment of additional caseworkers.Additional processes have been introduced such as the cessation of updates to allow staff to focus on the processing of consultations. Major consultations can be very complex in nature and require detailed consideration by experienced staff who are limited in number.In addition, the Department is seeking to recruit additional permanent staff members for this work area and has been working in collaboration with the Department for Infrastructure cross-governmental Planning Statutory Consultee Forum which aims to drive forward continuous improvements in the planning system.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to improve drainage on Bog Road, Portavogie, to address flooding in the area.
answer_text: I am pleased to confirm that a drainage improvement scheme at Bog Road in Portavogie commenced in early October 2024 and is expected to be completed before the end of October, subject to favourable weather conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to resurface Ballyblack Road, Newtownards.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This has had a significant impact on my Department’s ability to deliver essential services such as road maintenance. As such there are many roads that would benefit from investment and work needs to be taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention. Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year in the Ards and North Down Council area are included in the Department’s Annual Report that was presented to Council on 8 October 2024 and is now published on my Department’s website; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-24-25.pdfThis link has also been issued to local MLAs and MPs in the council area and placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans his Department has to enhance the bus service between Carrowdore and (i) Bangor; (ii) Newtownards; and (iii) Belfast.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and can confirm that Translink have engaged with local stakeholders to enhance the bus service from Carrowdore to Bangor schools.  There are no current plans to enhance the bus service from Carrowdore to Newtownards and Belfast.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps he is taking to improve the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland response time to local planning authority consultations on major planning applications from the current 31 per cent within the 21 day response target.
answer_text: Of the 13 consultation requests of major significance received by the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI), 6 consultation responses were delivered beyond the 21 day response target and 3 were reported as outstanding.  Whilst there was a delay in responding to 3 specific outstanding cases, the cases were subsequently resolved.In respect of the responses that did not meet the 21 day response target or were outstanding, this is attributed to a number of factors including incorrect classification of consultations by the Local Planning Office (LPO), issues with document accessibility / document absence on the planning portal or delays in receiving accurate information from stakeholders.  In addition, when detailed analysis and modelling is required, a request for an extension to the 21 day period is submitted which pauses the response target.  However, there has been occasions when this request has not been acknowledged by the LPO and as a result the response has been recorded as late.  Statutory consultations are divided into 3 main categories of significance.  This includes regional, major and local.  Of the 81 statutory consultations received by the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI) during the 2023 – 2024 period, 1 was regionally significant, 13 were of major significance and 67 were of local significance.  Upon review of the above, it is not considered that immediate actions are necessary to improve the HSENI’s response time to local planning authority consultations on major planning applications.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any recent discussions that have taken place to establish a veterinary school in Northern Ireland to address the shortage in vets.
answer_text: At the request of former Minister Lyons, the Strategic Investment Board (SIB) was commissioned to undertake a situational analysis and baseline report to inform any future strategic business case for a veterinary school for Northern Ireland (NI).  SIB produced reports on both, and these have been published on the DAERA website. The steering group, established to provide governance, recognised that the situational analysis and baseline report was only a small element of the overall consideration of the need for a veterinary school and that further work to advance a business case now rests with the third level educational institutes.In May this year I met with Professor Paul Bartholomew of Ulster University to discuss their ambitions and intentions, including their plans for introducing veterinary related courses in NI.Of interest is the recent announcement of two new veterinary schools in Ireland by Patrick O’Donovan, Minister for Further and Higher Education, Research, Innovation and Science, and Charlie McConalogue, Minister for Agriculture, Food and the Marine. I have raised the issue of student mobility at the recent North South Ministerial Council so as to allow access to veterinary places offered in Ireland to students coming from NI.My Department will, where requested, continue to discuss with and support third level educational institutes should they seek to progress a business case for a veterinary school in NI.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the progress being made in producing an animal health and welfare strategy.
answer_text: DAERA consulted on a draft strategic framework for animal health and welfare policy in Northern Ireland in 2019, with a summary of responses published in 2021.  This was not intended to replace existing animal health and welfare strategies but rather to provide a framework where these could be put into a wider context.  Unfortunately, due to ongoing resource pressures, this work has not progressed.Our priorities and objectives with respect to animal health are, however, provided for in publications such as our Bovine Tuberculosis Eradication Strategy and within our various Disease Control Strategies pertaining to actions we would take in the event of an epizootic disease incursion.On animal welfare, I am intending to meet with various stakeholders in the time ahead to discuss priorities in this area until the end of the mandate. Further, my Department has also published its 2024/25 Business Plan which details key animal health and welfare objectives to be progressed through to March 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of (i) the Migration Advisory Committee review on the Seasonal Worker visa; and (ii) the impact this will have on labour availability and economic productivity in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The MAC’s recommendation to continue the Seasonal Worker Scheme is welcome. However, the retention of the same list of eligible roles on the visa route will invariably restrict certain stakeholders’ ability to use the route as they had anticipated, namely the fishing and mushroom sectors. These sectors use the route extensively to recruit workers to fill labour shortages.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any representations he has made to the Home Office in relation to the challenges facing the agri-food sector in accessing migrant workers.
answer_text: I have written to the Home Secretary regarding the most recent immigration rule changes not serving the needs of the economy here and it is my intention to continue to engage with British Ministers on a range immigration issues, including the level at which the minimum salary threshold and the levels that required skill are set to enable access to migrant workers in the agri-food sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the progress being made in producing an equine strategy.
answer_text: Following the commissioning of consultants by my Department in 2018 to review the equine industry in Northern Ireland (NI), a report was published in June 2019, which made a number of recommendations and set out themes for further consideration by government and wider industry.A cross-governmental steering group was set up in early 2020 to explore what action might be taken.  This included a focus on the development of an Equine Strategy for NI. The steering group produced a draft vision statement for a future Equine Strategy for consideration by returning ministers and was involved in progressing a number of actions linked to the proposed Strategy. Unfortunately, however, due to the current challenging resource environment within my Department, and indeed across the public sector as a whole, it is not possible to progress additional work on an Equine Strategy in the immediate future. The matter will remain under review to assess whether resources may permit further work in the time ahead.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps he is taking to improve DfI Rivers response time to local planning authority consultations on major planning applications from the current 57 per cent within the 21 day response target.
answer_text: I am very conscious of the implications of any delays in the planning system and I can assure you that my officials are doing all they can to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of the statutory consultee process and to assist Councils to improve planning performance. Current performance is due to a combination of factors but is primarily due to the level of vacancies in Rivers’ Planning Advisory Unit. Work to address this is ongoing but the marketplace for engineering staff is currently very competitive, leading to challenges recruiting suitable staff to fill technical vacancies.However, within my Department’s Rivers Directorate, steps already taken to mitigate and address performance, include some limited overtime working and exploring the potential for external consultant support. In addition, the ‘Transforming Planning’ bid to the Interim Public Sector Transformation Board has made it to Stage 2 of the process. This bid includes proposals to support and enhance the role of my Department’s Statutory Consultees.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps he is taking to improve DfI Roads response time to local planning authority consultations on major planning applications from the current 64 per cent within the 21 day response target.
answer_text: I am very conscious of the implications of any delays in the planning system and I can assure you that my officials are doing all they can to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of the statutory consultee process and to assist Councils to improve planning performance. Current performance is due to a combination of factors but is primarily due to the level of vacancies in DfI Roads Development Control teams. Work to address this is ongoing but the marketplace for engineering staff is currently very competitive, leading to challenges recruiting suitable staff to fill technical vacancies.However, within my Department’s Roads Asset Development Directorate, steps already taken to mitigate and address performance, include some limited overtime working and exploring the potential for external consultant support. In addition, the ‘Transforming Planning’ bid to the Interim Public Sector Transformation Board has made it to Stage 2 of the process. This bid includes proposals to support and enhance the role of my Department’s Statutory Consultees.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps he is taking to create further employment opportunities in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: Since 2021/22 my Department has supported employment opportunities created in the Strangford constituency area through its funding of the North Down and Ards Labour Market Partnership (LMP).  This funding is specifically designed to help the LMP improve employability outcomes and local labour market conditions by working with a wide range of partners, being flexible to meet local needs and helping to connect employers with employees.My staff also support the creation of employment opportunities by working directly with employers across different sectors of the economy to help match people and jobs.  They organise job fairs and bespoke employer recruitment events and our Work Coaches work directly with the people from Strangford, helping and supporting them to take up these employment opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps he is taking to improve Historic Environment Division response time to local planning authority consultations on major applications from the current 26 per cent within the 21 day response target.
answer_text: The current response times by HED are not acceptable, nor productive to the planning process. I have tasked officials with exploring options for improvement. HED has engaged with DfI Planning and Council Planning Authorities and took actions in Quarter 4 of 2023/24 to streamline the number of all planning consultations HED receives, reducing unnecessary consultations where possible. This should increase our performance for 2024/25.HED has also reduced the backlog of overdue consultations from 396 in April 2024 to 74 to the end of last month, and officials have been reminded to prioritise ‘Major’ consultations where possible. In addition, during reporting year 2023/24, some of HED’s responses were uploaded electronically but did not appear on the planning portal and were recorded as being late. To address this, HED is finalising its own internal system to process its planning consultation responses more effectively. This will permit both sections (Historic Monuments and Historic Buildings) to monitor workflow and upload their respective consultation assessments separately and is expected to be operable later this month.The Division has also placed additional temporary agency staff to address unfilled permanent positions.Lastly, HED is also actively contributing to the work of DfI’s Planning Forum to improve the overall performance of statutory consultees within the planning system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether an impact assessment has been carried out on the sheep sector if his Department stops testing for Maedi Visna.
answer_text: Maedi Visna (MV) is a notifiable disease under our domestic legislation. It is not, however, a notifiable disease under either European Union or World Organisation for Animal Health rules.  The disease is widespread among sheep in other countries and jurisdictions including throughout Great Britain (GB). Neither GB nor Ireland is considered to be MV free. Northern Ireland (NI) has, however, been considered by the Department to be MV free. While there is no legal requirement to undertake surveillance for MV, to maintain this status, my Department has carried out MV surveillance as part of the annual Sheep and Goat survey and takes immediate action to control and eradicate any new disease incursions.No impact assessment has been carried out on any potential cessation of departmental testing for MV, but we are aware the main impact would likely be on pedigree owners accessing pedigree shows and sales in other countries.The European Union (EU) does not require that NI is MV free for us to export sheep to Member States.  For pedigree sheep being exported to MV-free flocks, additional guarantees are required such as an attestation of MV-freedom at national or flock level.  In the absence of national freedom, membership of an MV-accreditation scheme can provide the necessary evidence of freedom at flock level.Given ongoing resource constraints and the need to focus our disease control efforts on priority areas such as bovine tuberculosis (bTB), bluetongue and avian influenza, we are engaging with industry to ensure that any cessation of testing by the Department causes minimal disruption to the sheep sector.  Our ongoing work includes facilitating continued engagement with Scotland’s Rural College (SRUC) and key stakeholders to explore the option of the establishment of an MV Free accredited scheme here to permit continued access to MV free shows and sales in GB and mainland Europe.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: The number of flocks that have tested positive for Maedi Visna/Caprine arthritis-encephalitis, excluding post-import positive cases, in each of the last ten years are:YearNumber of cases2014-20180201922020020210202282023120243

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the cost of testing for Maedi Visna in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The information requested is not retained in a format which is readily available. In order to provide a full and considered response my officials have engaged with the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute to collate the necessary information on the cost of testing for Maedi Visna in each of the last ten years.  When this is available, I will arrange for it to be provided to the Member and a copy placed within the Assembly Library.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans he has for maintaining and improving facilities at Movilla High School, Newtownards.
answer_text: Works to deliver one additional Specialist Provision in Mainstream School (SPiMS) classrooms is complete and a temporary rental unit for the second class has been handed over. The Education Authority (EA) will undertake a condition survey of the technology and design suite to identify any immediate remedial actions required.  The school should continue to raise any urgent maintenance issues with the EA Maintenance Helpdesk, while applications for unavoidable minor works can also be submitted to the Education Authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps his Department is taking to ensure that Northern Ireland is free from Maedi Visna.
answer_text: Maedi Visna (MV) is a notifiable disease under our domestic legislation. It is not, however, a notifiable disease under either European Union or World Organisation for Animal Health rules.  The disease is widespread among sheep in other countries and jurisdictions including throughout Great Britain (GB). Neither GB nor Ireland is considered to be MV free. Northern Ireland (NI) has, however, been considered by the Department to be MV free.For Northern Ireland to maintain its current MV free status, officials test any sheep and goats moved into NI for the presence of the disease. In addition, post-import MV testing from Ireland commenced in July 2022. Furthermore, DAERA also tests a limited but statistically valid proportion of the national flock for MV as part of the annual sheep and goat surveillance survey.Our approach to MV surveillance in Northern Ireland is currently under review and my officials are engaging with industry stakeholders as part of this review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on School Enhancement Programme works at Glastry College, Ballyhalbert.
answer_text: Glastry College was announced in January 2020 as part of the third tranche of projects within the second call for the School Enhancement Programme (SEP).  The project is being managed by the Education Authority (EA).An Integrated Consultancy Team has been appointed to take the project through the design and construction phases and they have completed a Technical Feasibility Study which has identified a number of options for enhancement works at the school.  The EA are now preparing a Business Case seeking financial approval of the project.I have made funding available to progress the project at Glastry College through the design stages to pre-tender.  A decision to release to tender will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.I will continue to make the case for additional capital funding for Education through the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the remit of the new rural policy unit in his Department.
answer_text: The delivery of successive EU Rural Development Programmes was a long-standing feature of DAERA’s rural policy.  The closure of the NI RDP 2014-20 then created the opportunity for DAERA to consider its future rural policy remit. I therefore established the Rural Policy Unit in April, tasking my officials with developing proposals for a new evidence-based rural policy for consultation next year.The new policy will reflect the changed rural landscape recognising that all departments have responsibility for exercising their functions in both urban and rural areas and my desire to enable and support, through collaborative working, thriving, sustainable and resilient rural communities.The remit of the Unit also includes overseeing DAERA’s responsibilities under the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016. The Rural Policy Unit will continue to provide support to public authorities to help ensure that the Act is implemented effectively and delivers better outcomes for our rural dwellers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether the duty to have due regard under the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 has meant that proposed policy changes which impact on rural communities have been changed.
answer_text: My Department’s obligation under Section 3(2) of the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 (the Act) is to publish an annual report containing a summary of each Rural Needs Impact Assessment completed by the public authorities listed in the Schedule to the Act. While the completion of a Rural Needs Impact Assessment does not require public authorities to undertake a certain course of action or demonstrate that they have met their due regard to rural needs, it requires them to consciously consider the needs of rural communities when considering developing, adopting, implementing, revising a policy, strategy or plan and designing or delivering a public service.  The overall number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments completed has increased significantly since commencement of the Act from 157 to 502 which demonstrates a significant degree of commitment by public authorities to give due regard to the impact of their policies on rural communities. I recognise that there is still scope for more to be done to help ensure that the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 delivers better outcomes for rural communities and I have asked my officials to consider this in the development of future rural policy proposals. I plan to develop a new evidence-based rural policy, which is reflective of the changed rural landscape and will be subject to public consultation next year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to produce a new Rural White Paper Action Plan.
answer_text: The Rural White Paper Action Plan (RWPAP) was an Executive initiative published in 2012 which included commitments from all departments on a wide range of rural issues across five themes - rural communities, the rural economy, urban-rural linkages, access to services and the countryside.My Department is reviewing previous rural policy initiatives, including the RWPAP, to inform the development of new rural policy proposals. I plan to develop a new evidence-based rural policy, which is reflective of the changed rural landscape and will be subject to public consultation next year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to conduct a review into the operation and effectiveness of the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
