answer_text: The Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 (the Act) came into operation seven years ago to ensure that public authorities listed in the Schedule to the Act have due regard to rural needs when developing, adopting, implementing or revising policies, strategies and plans and in the design and delivery of public services.DAERA previously undertook a review of the implementation of the Act in June 2019, the main purpose of which was to assess how the legislation was being implemented by public authorities and to identify any further measures that might be taken to help public authorities comply with the requirements of the legislation. The review, published in January 2020, set out 25 recommendations, all of which were accepted by DAERA and shared with the public authorities listed in the Schedule to the Act to enable them to take the necessary steps to implement those recommendations which were applicable to them. The outworkings of the implementation of these recommendations included the establishment of a rural needs network for public authorities, improved information on rural statistics and the development of revised guidance to the Act.I recognise that there is still scope for more to be done to help ensure that the Act delivers better outcomes for rural communities and I am keen that the Act is more widely reviewed now that it has been in operation for seven years. I have asked my officials to consider this in the development of new evidence-based rural policy proposals, which will be subject to public consultation next year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department is promoting safety on the road network for horse riders.
answer_text: My Department regularly issues a newsletter to our 30,000 subscribers on Share the Road to Zero about Road Safety for Horses - https://www.sharetheroadtozero.com/horse-sense-for-riders-and-motorists/A series of Horse Road Safety messages are issued on the Department’s road safety social media channels: Share the Road to Zero newsletter, Facebook and X accounts on a regular basis, over the last twelve years. A selection of Facebook posts can be seen below, these are also published on Twitter. https://www.facebook.com/photo/?fbid=793249012833146&set=a.616181590539890https://www.facebook.com/photo?fbid=797043879120326&set=a.616181590539890https://www.facebook.com/photo/?fbid=801301268694587&set=a.616181590539890Messaging on safety on rural roads which includes road safety around horses, was issued on Share the Road to Zero in April and July of this year, and is planned to be promoted again later this month. An example of the posts is belowhttps://www.facebook.com/photo/?fbid=798706328954081&set=a.616181590539890A Road Safety leaflet developed by my officials in partnership with the British Horse Society can be found at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/publications/horse-sense-riders-and-motorists-leafletInformation on Road Safety for Horses is available on nidirect: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/horse-safety-roadYou can follow Share The Road to Zero's social media pages https://www.facebook.com/ShareTheRoadToZero/ for Facebook and https://x.com /roadtozero on X.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the terms of reference for Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation, scheduled to complete in 2025.
answer_text: As the Tackling Rural Poverty & Social Isolation Framework has been in operation for 8 years, my Department commissioned the Strategic Investment Board (SIB) to conduct an independent review of the Framework.   The review will examine how effectively the framework has been implemented and engagement will take place with a range of stakeholders, including public authorities and rural community organisations.  The review is due to provide a written report in early 2025.   The objectives of the TRPSI Review Terms of Reference (ToR) have been laid out in the table below.Objectivesi.To assess the effectiveness of the TRPSI Framework as an initiative for DAERA to work in partnership with other public authorities to help tackle poverty and social isolation in rural areas.ii.To determine the extent to which the Objectives and Intended Outcomes set out in the Framework have been achieved.iii.To assess how the programme of measures delivered under the Framework has contributed to addressing the Priority Areas for Intervention set out in the Framework.iv.To determine the extent to which the Objectives and Intended Outcomes set out in the Framework have been achieved.v.To assess how the programme of measures delivered under the Framework has contributed to addressing the Priority Areas for Intervention set out in the Framework.vi.To determine the extent to which the Key Principles set out in the Framework have been adhered to.vii.To determine how effective the programme of measures delivered under the TRPSI Framework has been in targeting the Target Groups specified in the Framework.viii.To determine the extent to which the Implementation, Monitoring and Evaluation arrangements set out in the Framework have been implemented.ix.To identify the key benefits arising from the TRPSI Framework for:Rural CommunitiesThe Target Groups listed in the Framework DAERAOther DepartmentsOther Public AuthoritiesThe Voluntary and Community Sector x.To identify any barriers or potential barriers to the effective implementation of the TRPSI Framework.xi.To answer the following key questions:How does DAERA promote the opportunity to work in partnership under the TRPSI Framework to other public authorities?How is the need for a particular measure or grant scheme to be supported under the TRPSI Framework normally identified and to what extent is research and evidence considered?When the need for a particular grant scheme is identified which falls within the statutory remit of another public authority, what is the rationale for the scheme being taken forward or funded by DAERA rather than by the other public authority?What benefits does the flexibility of the TRPSI Framework have for DAERA and other public authorities?xii.To identify any lessons learned which should be considered in relation to the development or implementation of any successor to the TRPSI Framework.xiii.To make recommendations on:Any steps that should be taken to ensure the objectives of the TRPSI Framework a
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions he has had with his counterpart in the Scottish Parliament to improve transport routes linking Northern Ireland and Scotland.
answer_text: I was due to meet with the Cabinet Secretary for Transport, Fiona Hyslop MSP in June to discuss several issues including transport routes between the North and Scotland. Unfortunately, this was postponed due to the General Election and will be rescheduled in due course. However, my officials continue to engage on transport policy matters with their counterparts in Transport Scotland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs in relation to the replacement of (i) buildings; and (ii) infrastructure which will be lost by farmers as a result of the A5 development, in the context of the problems hampering agricultural development as a result of the current ammonia policy advice.
answer_text: I am committed to working with the DEARA Minister in regards these matters in line with my statutory duties.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he plans to complete the review of the Rural White Paper Action Plan, scheduled for 2017.
answer_text: The Rural White Paper Action Plan (RWPAP) was an Executive initiative published in 2012 which included commitments from all Departments on a wide range of rural issues across five themes - rural communities, the rural economy, urban-rural linkages, access to services and the countryside.The RWPAP contained a commitment by the Executive to review the Action Plan after a period of 5 years. The Executive did not sit between 2017-2020 and no review was undertaken at that time.My department is reviewing previous rural policy initiatives including the RWPAP to inform the development of rural policy proposals. I plan to develop a new evidence-based rural policy which is reflective of the changed rural landscape and will be subject to public consultation next year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what capital projects will be progressed by Libraries NI in the 2024-25 financial year.
To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to replicate the provisions of the Down Syndrome Act 2022 and apply them to Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There are no immediate plans to replicate the Down Syndrome Act 2022 in Northern Ireland. People with Down’s Syndrome will have some level of learning disability and are there considered as part of a wide continuum of Health and Social Care services. The Chronically Sick and Disabled Persons (NI) Act 1978 remains the primary legislative framework which guides the provision of social care for people with learning disabilities. As part of the ongoing exercise to finalise the Learning Disability Service Model, my officials will consider potential legislative changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 12084/22-27, for an update on (i) regulations for graduated driver licences; and (ii) a timeline for implementation.
answer_text: Full implementation of graduated driver licensing requires substantial dedicated funding, 3 sets of Regulations and significant operational planning activity. I have sought legal advice that will help me determine the best way forward, and I expect to receive that shortly. At this stage however, I am unable to provide a timeline for GDL implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 12084/22-27, for an update on regulations on lowering the blood alcohol level for specified and unspecified drivers under the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: As I set out in the Road Safety Action Plan 2024/25, I have asked officials to explore options and consult on proposals to introduce a lower legal alcohol limit applicable to all drivers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration his Department has given to a dedicated hydrogen innovation strategy.
answer_text: The Executive’s Energy Strategy, ‘The Path to Net Zero Energy’, states that hydrogen is a substantial opportunity for the north. My officials are developing a consultation on hydrogen policy and have established a Hydrogen Industry Working Group to inform this work. 		I recognise the economic opportunities from the hydrogen technology market and my department’s economic partner, Invest NI, has engaged with the British Hydrogen Innovation Initiative (HII) to promote the commercial opportunities for technology and supply chains in the hydrogen sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions (i) he has had; and (ii) he plans to have, with the UK Government to explore funding streams and incentives for small and medium-sized enterprises innovation in the hydrogen sector.
answer_text: My officials are engaged with the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) regarding hydrogen funding to ensure that the voice of the north’s small and medium-sized enterprises and larger companies are represented.I am aware of SMEs, such as Catagen and B9 Energy, being successful in accessing funding from the British government related to hydrogen innovation.I am also aware of larger companies, such as Mannok and Wrightbus, successfully applying to the Net Zero Hydrogen Fund. These larger companies can act as a catalyst for other local SMEs to become part of the hydrogen supply chain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the work of the Coastal Erosion Forum.
answer_text: The Coastal Forum developed a work programme to address the gaps that were identified in the report “A Baseline Study and Gap Analysis of Coastal Erosion Risk Management in Northern Ireland”.  The work programme was agreed in 2021 and good progress has been made in addressing the shortfall of scientifically robust baseline data on how the Northern Ireland coastline is changing.There have now been surveys of the complete coast and nearshore area, utilising methodologies such as LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging), high resolution orthophotography, satellite-derived bathymetry, and detailed analysis of Ordnance Survey maps, historical aerial photographs and geological datasets.  This evidence is published on the Northern Ireland Coastal Observatory website.The Coastal Forum held workshops on 2 December 2021 and 7 December 2022 to begin the process of exploring options for new coastal change policy for Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration has he given to the creation of a statutory duty for public bodies to plan for coastal change.
answer_text: I recognise the need for public authorities to take coastal change into consideration in their decision making and the draft Marine Plan for Northern Ireland includes a policy on this issue.  The draft Marine Plan policy is consistent with the Coastal Development policy in the Strategic Planning Policy Statement for Northern Ireland and reflects the existing policy and legislative framework. The creation of new statutory duties for public authorities is for the Executive to consider and I’m aware that the Climate Change Committee has recommended that the Executive considers bringing forward legislation on managing coastal change and designates responsibility to a Department to oversee coastal change risk management.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the co-operation on a (i) north-south; and (ii) east-west basis in relation to climate resilience and adaptation.
answer_text: The current and projected risks from climate change impact all of society and therefore cuts across the remit of all Departments. As such co-operation will fall across a number of Departments depending on the specific climate risk area.For those risk areas which sit within my Department’s areas of responsibility there is ongoing co-operation on a north-south and east-west basis across a number of areas which may be impacted by climate change including animal and plant health, invasive species, food production, research and nature as well as climate adaptation policy development.In relation to risks to plant and animal health from pathogens and diseases, some of which may be climate sensitive, we have ongoing engagement and co-operation with our counterparts in the South and across other parts of the UK to improve our preparedness through surveillance and best practice information exchange. As part of this co-operation DAERA officials recently took part in a visit by the UK Plant Health Coordination Group to Dublin where they visited Backweston at which climate change risks were discussed and evidence presented on novel pest introductions to Ireland which have been attributed to climate change. In managing risks of invasive species my Department has ongoing engagement and co-operation with the GB Non-Native Species Secretariat (GBNNSS) and with colleagues in Ireland including in the National Parks and Wildlife Service (NPWS) and National Biodiversity Data Centre (NBDC). This work includes a Shared Island Invasive Species and Biosecurity Initiative as well as horizon scanning for invasive species which takes account of climate sensitivity. In seeking to protect food supply, as part of ongoing co-operation, in June 2024 DAERA officials attended a two-day workshop which brought together climate, agriculture and food policy experts from across the UK to consider knowledge gaps and research priorities for effective climate adaptation and resilience in the UK food system.In working to protect and enhance our natural environment DAERA officials continue to engage and co-operate with Government Departments and Agencies in Ireland and across the UK to promote climate adaptation and to enhance resilience within our natural environment. This includes working co-operatively and exchanging knowledge, across themes such as evidence, monitoring, preparedness and adaptation measures relating to a wide range of issues such as peatland restoration and wildfires.  Through the British Irish Council (BIC) and North South Ministerial Council (NSMC) Environment Sectoral meetings we also have ongoing co-operation on a range of environmental issues including climate change. At present a number of Peace Plus applications are being assessed, which if successful, will also bring significant opportunities for North-South co-operation for nature and coastal and marine management under Theme 5 which recognises the important role coastal management, and healthy ecosystems can pr
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will develop a coastal policy framework.
answer_text: My Department is in the final stages of preparing a Marine Plan for Northern Ireland which will set the policy framework for sustainable development of the Northern Ireland marine area. The Marine Plan will sit alongside and interact with the terrestrial planning system, ensuring that coastal areas and activities taking place within them, are managed in line with the principles of Integrated Coastal Zone Management.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the targets in place for nature recovery; and (ii) the measures in place to deliver on these targets.
answer_text: As part of the UK, Northern Ireland is subject to the UN Convention on Biological Diversity and its internationally agreed Global Biodiversity Framework (GBF).  The GBF is comprised of 4 goals and 23 targets that cover the wide range of issues affecting biodiversity and, if adequately addressed, should go a long way to halt the loss of biodiversity or at least ensure many habitats and species are in a better ecological status.Target 3 of these is often viewed as the one that will have the biggest impact, if achieved.  This seeks to ensure that by 2030 at least 30% of all terrestrial, inland water, and coastal and marine areas, especially areas of particular importance for biodiversity and ecosystem functions and services, are effectively conserved and managed. This target is often referred to as the 30 x 30 target.My officials are developing a Nature Recovery Strategy, the aim of which is to identify key actions and objectives required to meet local and national commitments under the GBF, and it will also augment actions for promoting biodiversity in other major initiatives which my Department is leading on including the Environmental Improvement Plan and the Climate Action Plan.The strategy is cross cutting and will require Executive approval.  It is hoped, subject to Ministers’ views, to have the draft strategy ready for public consultation later in the year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has considered introducing legislation to permit NI Water to accept contributions directly from developers in order to address capacity issues which are preventing development.
answer_text: My officials are currently examining the legislative and regulatory frameworks to assess the feasibility of developers providing financial contributions to NI Water to improve wastewater infrastructure capacity and facilitate development.Policy development on this issue is still in the early stages and further stakeholder engagement and consultation will be required to progress this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a timeline for the finalisation and implementation of the Clean Air Strategy.
answer_text: I have now set the policy direction for this Strategy, which my officials are progressing. Some actions will be the responsibility of other Departments and I will work with my Ministerial colleagues to finalise this important cross-cutting Strategy.  It is my intention to publish this Strategy for consultation by 31 March 2025, however, this may be impacted by competing resource pressures in relation to other key departmental priorities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Farmers for Generation programme, including when it will be launched.
answer_text: A Farming for the Generations Scheme has been codesigned with stakeholders and the scheme will be rolled out as a pilot. This aims to raise awareness of the need for succession planning on farms, provide support to successors and those who currently lead the farm business.  It will also link farmers without a family successor to new entrants to provide access to land and other resources. Learning from this pilot will inform the development of a future scheme.  I plan to make an announcement on this pilot scheme soon.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide a timeline for the publication of a lung health strategy.
answer_text: Currently there is no decision to launch a lung health strategy for Northern Ireland. However, work has been underway since 2023 to modernise the current service and an update was given to the All-Party Group on 11th September 2024.The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (Department of Health) are leading jointly with the Public Health Agency to set up a Respiratory Forum.  A series of workshops took place with respiratory care stakeholders across NI in 2023 and the following priorities were approved:a)	Adult Asthma b)	Paediatric Respiratory Services c)	Interstitial Lung Diseased)	Primary Care/Diagnosticse)	Home Oxygen servicesf)	COPDg)	Non-invasive and long-term ventilationh)	Obstructive Sleep ApnoeaIt was agreed that the workstreams would be split into two tranches.  The first four workstreams (a – d) were convened and commenced in spring 2024 and are working to improve care pathways.  The second tranche (e – h) will be launched in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he plans to review and update the Regional Development Strategy 2035.
answer_text: The Regional Development Strategy 2035 continues to retain sound strategic and regional planning guidance.  Given existing committed priorities, wider budget and resource constraints, reviewing the RDS is not a priority for the Department at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how much glass has been collected for recycling in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My department does not hold information on all glass which may have been collected for recycling. However, it can provide the amount of Local Authority Collected Municipal Waste (LACMW) glass collected for recycling for the last 5 financial years. This is listed below:Year		Status		LACMW glass collected for recycling (T)2022-23	Final		31,1602021-22	Final		34,1012020-21	Final		35,6662019-20	Final		30,9482018-19	Final		31,699The data for the 2023-24 financial year will not be validated until December 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current waiting list for children awaiting a referral for an asthma diagnosis, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The current waiting list for children awaiting a referral for an asthma diagnosis, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust cannot be confirmed for the following reasons: GPs can diagnose asthma in children and adults across NI and in many occasions, they do carry out the diagnosis without the need for a child/adult to be referred to secondary care;GPs don’t normally refer a patient to their local Trust for ‘asthma’.  They will be referred for a range of conditions such as cough, wheeze, breathlessness, etc and it is for this reason that there are no waiting lists explicitly for children pending an asthma diagnosis;

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) an estimation on the amount of glass collected for recycling that was not recycled as glass products due to quality concerns in the collection process in each of the last five years; and (ii) the measures he proposes to address this.
answer_text: My department does not hold this information as it is not possible to determine the exact reason that a material is rejected for recycling.The recent consultation Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI set out several proposals to improve the quality and quantity of collections and ensure as much material as possible is kept locally for reprocessing. This includes options to separate materials further at the kerbside and the introduction of recycling for obligated businesses. The consultation received over 300 responses which are in the process of being analysed to provide me with the relevant information to enable evidence-based decision-making.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how much glass collected for recycling has been exported from Northern Ireland to be reprocessed in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Northern Ireland exports of waste outside of the UK fall into two main categories which are notifiable waste and green list waste. Notifiable waste generally consists of refuse derived fuel (RDF), mixed wastes and hazardous wastes. Green list waste generally consists of single stream wastes. Waste glass exports fall under green list controls. Waste glass exports for glass in the last 5 years are as follows:2019 - 9,222 Tonnes2020 - 5,243 Tonnes2021 - 12,875 Tonnes2022 - 18,129 Tonnes2023 - 25,914 Tonnes
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the proportion of glass collected in Northern Ireland that was reprocessed in Northern Ireland in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My department does not hold information on all glass which may have been collected for recycling. However it can provide the proportion of Local Authority Collected Municipal Waste (LACMW) glass which was reprocessed (including material attaining end-of-waste status) in Northern Ireland as a percentage of all LACMW glass waste sent for recycling for the last 5 financial years. This is listed below:Year		Status		LACMW glass sent for recycling in NIas a percentage of all LACMW glass sent for recycling2022-23	Final		59.7%2021-22	Final		65.8%2020-21	Final		73.3%2019-20	Final		61.7%2018-19	Final		67.9%The data for the 2023-24 financial year will not be validated until December 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding provided to projects in the Strangford constituency from the Mental Health Support Fund in each year since the start of the fund.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on an additional roll-out of the 20mph speed limit scheme outside schools in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and I recently discussed the draft review report with my officials.  I have asked for the report to be shared with the Committee for Infrastructure, following which it will be made publicly available.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people that have registered to opt out of organ donation since the Organ and Tissue Donation (Deemed Consent) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 became law.
answer_text: The latest information on the number of people that have opted out of organ donation in Northern Ireland is published on the NHS Blood and Transplant website at https://www.organdonation.nhs.uk/helping-you-to-decide/about-organ-donation/statistics-about-organ-donation/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of cases of mpox, previously known as monkeypox, in Northern Ireland in each of the last three years.
answer_text: Please see the table below with a breakdown of laboratory confirmed mpox cases reported to the Public Health Agency between 2022 and 2024 to date:Year of notification to PHANumber of laboratory confirmed cases2022342023320244The current Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC) declared by the World Health Organization on 14 August 2024 is in relation to the upsurge of clade I mpox in several African countries.  To date, no clade I mpox cases have been reported in the UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the engagement his Department has had with the Shared Rural Network in relation to improving mobile coverage in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: The Shared Rural Network (SRN) is a project developed by the four main Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) (EE, Vodafone, Virgin Media/O2 and Three) and the British Government. SRN aims to deliver 95% 4G coverage and enable rural businesses and communities to thrive.Although responsibility for delivery of SRN falls to the MNOs and British Government, many local stakeholders sit on the NI SRN working group to make representations and provide a local perspective. Progress of SRN is measured by Ofcom, who recently announced that the SRN programme has significantly reduced the scale of partial not spots (PNS) and consequently increased the areas where all MNOs provide coverage. Further detail is available in Ofcom’s Shared Rural Network Coverage Obligations document. More information about the SRN can be found at srn.org.uk and MNOs are willing to provide you with more specific information on request.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the herd level incidence of bovine viral diarrhea in the last 12 months; and (ii) for an update on the eradication programme.
answer_text: Bovine Viral Diarrhoea (BVD) herd incidence was 2.44% in the 12 months to 20 September 2024.  This represents a reduction of approximately 75% since BVD testing became compulsory, following the introduction of the BVD Eradication Scheme Order (NI) 2016. My officials continue to work closely with Animal Health and Welfare Northern Ireland to support the delivery of the industry led BVD eradication programme.  A public consultation was carried on in late 2022 on proposals to introduce herd restrictions, a measure deemed to be key in further reducing levels of BVD.  My officials are currently finalising the drafting of legislation required to introduce these measures and this is expected to be made before the end of this year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of vets employed by his Department; and (ii) the number of vacancies, broken down by business area.
answer_text: At the end of August 2024, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs had a headcount of 150 veterinary staff.The current number of Departmental veterinary staff vacancies registered with NICS Human Resources are detailed in the table below:GroupDivisionRegistered vacanciesDAERA, Veterinary Service and Animal Health GroupDAERA, Enzootic Division3DAERA, Epizootic Disease Control & Public Health Delivery Division16DAERA, Windsor Framework Implementation Directorate1Total20
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to the response to AQW 14856/22-27, (i) what has been the effect previously when a formal notification of action short of strike by social work staff in Child and Family Teams has been provided to the Northern Ireland Court Service and magistrates; and (ii) to detail the consequence of the failure by social work staff in Child and Family Teams to provide formal notification of their current Action Short of Strike to the Northern Ireland Court Service and magistrates.
answer_text: The Trade Union and Labour Relations (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 requires trade unions who wish to start industrial action to notify the relevant employers of those staff involved in the dispute.The Northern Ireland Courts & Tribunals Service (NICTS) does not employ social workers.  It was therefore not necessary for trade unions to provide NICTS with formal notification of the industrial action. The relevant employers for those social workers working in the Child and Family Teams, including those in the Court Children’s Service, are the Health & Social Care Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister of Finance in relation to a review of the salary scale, in order to attract and retain vets to roles in his Department.
answer_text: I can advise that I have had no discussions with the Minister of Finance in relation to the salary scale of vets. My officials have recently established a project to address the challenges around the recruitment and retention of vets within the Department.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of applicants; and (ii) the total amount of funding provided through the Help with Health Costs scheme to residents in the Strangford constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Help with Health Costs (HwHC) scheme provides individuals, primarily on low incomes, with financial help towards the cost of dental treatment, eyesight tests, glasses or contact lens, and travel costs for Health and Social Care (HSC) treatment on referral by a doctor or dentist. The Department does not provide a specific funding allocation for the HwHC scheme. The Business Services Organisation (BSO), usually through individual dental and optical practices, administer the HwHC scheme on behalf of the Department of Health for the purposes of dental treatment, eyesight tests and glasses or contact lens vouchers. The number of exempt patients (and thus provided help with their health care costs) residing in the Strangford Assembly Area, and the associated fees/costs covered by the health service, is shown in the tables below. Figures are provided for the last five financial years, including figures up to 2023-24, the latest publicly available annual Accredited Official Statistics. Please note: Financial year relates to when claim was paid, this is not necessarily the same year as when the activity took place.Table 1: Number of Exempt Dental Patients Seen and Total Item of Service (IoS) Fees of Exempt Patients residing in Strangford Assembly Area by Financial YearFinancial Year2019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24Patients Seen123,6469,76714,10816,93117,187Total IoS Fees2£1,658,000£730,000£1,089,000£1,307,000£1,219,000The total number of patients to have received treatment under any of the exemption categories listed as eligible for financial support. If a patient appears in multiple exemption categories or more than once in an exemption category they will only be counted once in this total. Based on paid claims and information supplied by dentist making the claim. Excludes all private work, and secondary care activity. Total fees are rounded to the nearest thousand pounds.The total IoS fees for patients represent a sum of the codes of all treatments that were undergone by patients claiming exemption. Excludes registration fees paid in relation to children and adults. For patients aged under 18, a number of minor treatments (e.g. examinations, x-rays, scale and polish) are included in the capitation fee – these patients will be included but only claimable treatments will be included in the IoS fees.  Table 2: Number of Exempt Ophthalmic Patients provided with a Sight Test and Total Sight Test Fees of Exempt Patients residing in Strangford Assembly Area by Financial YearFinancial Year2019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24Total Patients1,323,53616,31022,46222,47523,951Total Sight Test£565,000£367,000£533,000£547,000£615,000The total number of patients to have received treatment under any of the exemption categories listed as eligible for financial support. If a patient appears in multiple exemption categories or more than once in an exemption category they will only be counted once in this total. Based on paid claims and information supplied by optician

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the mitigation measures in place for (i) commuters; (ii) foot passengers; and (iii) schoolchildren during the works to the ferry slipways in Portaferry and Strangford.
answer_text: A procurement competition is ongoing for the provision of a suitable passenger only service during the works.My Officials are in consultation with the Education Authority and Translink around school transport, and mitigation measures will be put in place when the outcome of the procurement competition is known.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the health and safety issues with the ferry slipways in Portaferry and Strangford were first identified
answer_text: My Officials complete regular inspections of the slipways used by the ferry service an issue was first identified in May 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any consideration that was given to carrying out the works to the Portaferry and Strangford ferry slipways outside of school term time.
answer_text: My Department considered a number of options before deciding on this period, which coincides with school half term and when passenger numbers are 40% lower than July and August.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the level of TB incidence in the cattle population as a whole in Northern Ireland that would result in exports being restricted or stopped.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Bovine TB Programme is essential to providing the necessary assurance that facilitates the export of live cattle (from officially TB free herds) and to enable the export of our meat and dairy products.  While decisions relating to the import of our cattle and cattle products are matters for importing countries to make, our programme was approved by the European Commission in early 2022 and this is the basis on which we trade with EU Member States.  There is no arbitrary disease incidence figure within European law at which exports may be subject to restriction for our access to the EU market.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact of rising insurance costs on the availability of placements for apprentices and trainees.
answer_text: My Department’s Apprenticeship and Traineeship programmes, together with the Skills for Life and Work Programme, all require the involvement of employers and a network of training providers, both statutory and contracted.  Rising insurance costs have not been raised as a specific barrier to apprentice recruitment or trainee placement by employers or training providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding allocated to support community pharmacies in the Strangford constituency in each of the last five years.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of organisations in the Strangford constituency that have received Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme funding in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of organisations in the Strangford constituency that have received Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme (RMCGS) funding since 2019/2020 is as follows.  RMCGS Scheme yearNumber of Organisations fundedGrant received2019/202017£19,348.452020/202117£21,418.862021/202215£16,397.322022/202325£31,370.61Total£88,535.24For 2024/2025 27 voluntary and community organisations have been offered grants totalling £39,094.36 through Letters of Offer which issued on 31 August 2024. It is anticipated that the grants will be paid by the end of December 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any consideration they have given to the provision of a one-off support payment to those residents directly affected by the Second World War bomb evacuation and detonation in Newtownards.
answer_text: Any decision to make a one-off payment to householders affected by this incident would be a decision for the Executive.The Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance (SEFA) for non-flooding incidents was activated on 16 August 2024. This scheme provides financial support to Ards & North Down Borough Council for direct costs incurred by the council and services contracted out by the council. It does not provide any financial support to householders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of electric vehicle charge points in the Strangford constituency; and (ii) the number of these charge points that are currently out of order.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric (EV) vehicle charge points is a commercially led operation. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of those charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs). In this commercially led market, my officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to CPOs on operational matters. My Department does not hold records of the number of publicly available EV charge points. The British Government publishes periodic updates by both Local Council area, and Parliamentary constituency which you may find helpful. These can be found in the link below. https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/electric-vehicle-public-charging-infrastructure-statistics-july-2024Latest statistics show that there are 625 publicly accessible EV charge points in the North. This represents a growth of 60.5% in the total number of available EV charge points here since April 2023 and the number of rapid charge points, which provide significantly faster opportunity to charge, has grown by over 300% in that same period. Relevant CPOs are best placed to advise you of their plans for the installation of further charge points within your constituency and the position of their current installations.Local Councils have been able to avail of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emissions Vehicles (OZEV) ‘On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme’, which my Department has match funded. Derry City and Strabane District Council made an application under this scheme in 2021-22, on behalf of nine Local Councils to provide 127 charge points across those Council areas. Individual Councils are best placed to advise on the number of additional charge points this will provide in their area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health,  in light of the 45 per cent increase at the Ulster Hospital, whether there are plans to increase car parking fees in Health and Social Care Trust sites across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department’s policy on Car Parking Provision and Management in the Health and Social Care sector for Northern Ireland was published in 2012 and sets the direction for car park management.  The policy states that it is for each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) to determine the way that the car parking policy, including charging, is applied to each of their sites, to enable them to take into account local issues.The Northern HSCT approved the introduction of increased parking charges, from 1 August 2024. Increased parking charges were identified as part of the Trust’s Delivering Value / Savings Plan Proposals. You will be aware of the position that the wholly inadequate 2024/25 funding allocation, that was regrettably supported by a majority of MLAs,  placed our health and social care system in. Yet even with these often very difficult savings plans, our health service is still facing a funding shortfall.  Comparable pricing structure are outlined in the table below.Pre Increase Antrim and Causeway Hospitals Price Increases Antrim and Causeway Hospitals WEF 1 August 2024Free 15 minute drop off/pick up 1 hour             £1.30 1-2 hours        £1.90 2-3 hours        £2.30 3-4 hours        £2.50 4-5 hours        £3.00 5-6 hours        £3.50 6-8 hours        £4.008- 24 hours     £5.00Free 15 minute drop off/pick up 1 hour           £1.50 1-2 hours      £2.00 2-3 hours      £2.50 3-4 hours      £3.00 4-5 hours      £3.50 5-6 hours      £4.00 6-8 hours      £4.508- 24 hours   £6.00There have been no recent increases to parking charges at the Belfast, Western or Southern HSCTs however this will be kept under review by each trust as they consider any low or medium impact measures as part of the development of their Trust Financial Recovery Plans.In line with the Hospital Parking Charges Act 2024, trusts are working at pace to introduce ANPR technology to enable free car parking for all users by May 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on an additional roll-out of the 20mph speed limit scheme outside schools in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and I recently discussed the draft review report with my officials.  I have asked for the report to be shared with the Committee for Infrastructure, following which it will be made publicly available.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on planning maintenance by DfI Roads in the Strangford constituency in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: The Department does not collate or manage information on a constituency basis, rather this is done on a Council area basis. The Strangford Constituency forms part of the Ards and North Down Borough Council; Newry, Mourne and Down District Council; and Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council areas.The 2024-25 work programmes are currently being finalised and will be included in my Department’s Annual Roads Reports to Councils. Following the presentation to Council, reports will be published on the Department’s website, issued to MLAs and MPs in each respective Council area, and placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will provide additional support to the Warm Homes Scheme to help mitigate the impact of the cut in the winter fuel allowance.
answer_text: I have allocated an opening budget of £7.175m for the Affordable Warmth Scheme (AWS) to cover £5.2m of approvals carried forward and £1.975m for new approvals. The Equality Impact Assessment consultation on the Department’s budget closed on 3 September 2024.  My officials are reviewing the responses from the Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) on Budget 2024-25 and any areas identified as requiring mitigations will be considered.  Once all the responses have been analysed, a final decision report will be drafted for publication.  This will be provided to the Executive (via DoF), to support the Executive’s consideration of equality during the in-year monitoring rounds.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the steps he is taking to ensure that long-term substitute teachers accrue employment rights such as holiday pay and sickness pay.
answer_text: A teacher engaged and paid for each day worked at a rate of 1/195 of the annual salary of a full-time permanent teacher receives a higher daily rate to account for holiday pay. Since 2004 the following statement has been included on the pay slips of supply teachers: ‘Holiday pay is included within your daily rate of pay calculated as annual salary over 195 working days’. Teachers’ (including supply teachers’) terms & conditions are negotiated and agreed by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) which comprises representatives of teachers’ employers and sectoral bodies, the Department of Education and the 5 main teachers’ unions. Decisions are communicated to teachers and their employers via TNC Circulars.Through the TNC a review of the use of supply teachers has been carried out and the recommendations from this review are currently being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a timeline on the publication of the Housing Supply Strategy.
answer_text: I am in the process of finalising the Housing Supply Strategy and it is my intention to bring it to the Executive for its consideration shortly.  Publication will be dependent on any forthcoming approval by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will establish a sheep development programme to include a genetic improvement scheme and a health and welfare scheme.
answer_text: The Sheep Industry Taskforce produced its report to identify the needs of the sheep sector in Northern Ireland and a proposed package to encourage its development.My officials are currently working to better understand the strategic needs of this sector and I will consider deploying levers to meet those needs.  It is too early in the policy development process to give any indication of what might be included in any sheep scheme moving forward.  Officials recently met the Sheep Industry Taskforce to discuss the report and agree next steps.  I look forward to receiving an update on these discussions.  Any decision to introduce new policies or programmes will be in the context of budget availability within the ear-marked farm support funding envelope.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of rates arrears that were written off in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Rating debt written off by Land & Property Services in each of the last five rating years is detailed in the table below -Rating YearAmount written off (£ Million)2019/2014.52020/215.12021/2215.72022/2312.12023/2416.9

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she intends to continue with the Lone Pensioner Allowance.
answer_text: Yes. That specific policy was not the subject of the recent consultation pertaining to rating measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the total amount of rates currently in arrears.
answer_text: Figures on rate arrears are published at year end.On 31st March 2024, £180.5 million was still to be collected from previous rating years.Land and Property Services continues to reduce rating debt through the annual rate billing, collection and legal recovery cycle. As of 1st September 2024, rates from previous rating years has been reduced to £153 million.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she intends to continue with the early payment discount for rates bills.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer provided to AQW 14913/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps being taken to address waiting lists for rheumatology in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Unfortunately, the demand for rheumatology in all Trusts continues to exceed capacity. Regrettably patients are experiencing excessive waits for appointments and treatment which is not the service the Trusts would wish to provide. The Trusts are, however, working very hard to reduce the waiting lists and waiting times for their patients.Trust are continuing to seek alternative ways to provide outpatient consultations to help reduce waiting times, through initiatives such as virtual or telephone appointments that provide advice and guidance. They are also seeking to implement a ‘Patient initiated follow up’ (PIFU) service and enhanced triage clinics, to further reform the outpatient service. Each of the Trusts are working with the SPPG and PHA, along with the Voluntary sector, in a regional Rheumatology Group, to help introduce new pathways, to ensure that services are available that are appropriate to patient needs.Funding of £76m has been made available in 2024/25 to reduce waiting times for assessments and treatments across a range of specialties,.  This funding is being provided to address patients waiting with suspect cancer and time critical (life/limb) treatments only. Last year over 22,000 patients did not attend their allocated appointment. Without timely prior notice, these appointments cannot be offered to other patients on waiting lists.  Each of these missed appointments not only wastes a consultation slot but costs between £200 and £2,500.To address this matter, Trusts have implemented text reminders for all their Consultant led clinics to try and ensure these appointments are not wasted and waiting lists can be further reduced.Each of the Trusts, also face significant recruitment challenges in fulfilling medical vacancies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 11837/22-27, to detail (i) the recommendations of the review of hate crime legislation that are still outstanding; and (ii) the reasons why these have not yet been implemented.
answer_text: As Justice Minister, the implementation of the wide-ranging recommendations from Judge Marrinan’s Review of Hate Crime Legislation has been a key priority since the beginning of this mandate. Progressing work on the introduction of hate crime legislation has been a key focus for me since returning to office in February. The need for these provisions has gained greater urgency in the context of the recent anti-migrant violent disorder we have witnessed on our streets over recent months.There has been significant work undertaken to deliver both the legislative and non-legislative recommendations from Judge Marrinan’s Review. This includes: Policy development on the outcome of the phase one consultation to deliver foundational new hate crime legislative provisions which will be in place by the end of this mandate, including a statutory aggravation offence model for hate crime in the forthcoming Sentencing Bill which is planned to be introduced next year.The delivery of enhanced protection for victims of hate crimes including more specific support to victims and witnesses through provisions included in a Victims Bill later in this mandate.Appointment of the Commissioner Designate for Victims of Crime whose functions include a hate crime focus.  Establishment of a range of multi-agency fora to progress the implementation of Review recommendations including the establishment of the Hate Crime Delivery group and the Hate Crime Legislation Steering Group. Assisting PSNI in the public tender for provision of the Hate Crime Advocacy Service, to ensure a more secure footing for the service and enable to greater support to victims. Liaison with the UK Government on its development of the Online Safety Act (2023), to ensure alignment with Judge Marrinan’s recommendations in relation to online harms. Publication by the PPS on their prosecution guidelines for cases involving electronic communications.Ensuring hate crime is considered as part of the Adult Restorative Justice Strategy 2022—27 [published in March 2022] and in related Action Plans.  Work on policies to be included in the phase two public consultation, including further protected groups, is ongoing. Cross departmental engagement to consider and develop guidance on recommendations beyond a DOJ-only remit and the broader work on addressing the underlying factors enabling hate crime. Consideration of recommendations relating to stirring up hatred offences in Public Order legislation will be progressed in the next mandate.  Judge Marrinan is content with this approach. I have previously outlined the challenges the Department is facing, in terms of a reduced mandate and limited resources and how this has impacted on the time and resources to facilitate the implementation of Review recommendations as previously envisaged. The delivery of the proposed legislative provisions will complement and strengthen non-legislative work being taken forward to address hate crime by working collaboratively with partners

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 11837/22-27, to detail (i) the recommendations of the review of arrangements to deliver justice in serious sexual offences that are still outstanding; and (ii) the reasons why these have not yet been implemented.
answer_text: (i) The 253 recommendations set out in the Gillen Review are complex and interdependent. They are not equal in scale, weight or complexity and it would be misleading to assess progress simply in terms of the number of recommendations that are completed or outstanding. Nonetheless I remain committed to the full implementation of all accepted recommendations under the Gillen Review. Our focus has been on progressing those recommendations that will have the most beneficial impact on victims. The new measures, services and reforms that have been implemented to date are delivering real and meaningful improvements for victims of sexual crime.  This includes a number of recommendations which have been partially completed, which means that some provision is in place, but that further work or funding is required to establish longer-term, mainstreamed service provision.  These include:the establishment of the Sexual Offences Legal Adviser (SOLA) service which is empowering complainants by providing free, independent legal advice; the establishment of new Remote Evidence Centres (RECs) in Belfast and Craigavon to provide a safe and comfortable facility away from the courthouse from where complainants can give their best evidence;the operation of a judge-led voluntary protocol to expedite serious sexual offence cases involving child witnesses under 13 years of age that is delivering positive results. An evaluation found that there has been a 70% reduction in the average time taken between reporting the crime and court disposal in cases falling within this voluntary protocol. The principles of the protocol have been extended to children under 16 years;the introduction of new measures to protect the privacy of complainants, under the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, including the exclusion of the public from serious sexual offence trials;ongoing work to reduce delay, including the introduction of the first stage of the NI Criminal Justice (Committal Reform) Act; improvements to Achieving Best Evidence (ABE) and disclosure processes; and the delivery of trauma informed training for those who have direct contact with victims. Work is ongoing in a number of recommendations. Where this is the case preparatory work has been progressed, but it has not yet been possible to publish, introduce or operationalise the associated research, service or measure.  Examples include:research on attrition in serious sexual offences. The initial findings are due before the end of the current financial year;research on the use of the complainant’s previous sexual history as evidence in serious sexual trials;the establishment of a pilot to test pre-recorded cross-examination in serious sexual offence cases involving very young complainants. This project has complex interdependencies with other workstreams and its launch will be subject to resolving a number of outstanding issues and to securing funding; andestablishment of a SOLA scheme

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 11837/22-27, to detail (i) the recommendations of the review of anti-social behaviour legislation that are still outstanding; and (ii) the reasons why these have not yet been implemented.
answer_text: The multi-agency, cross-departmental review of Anti-Social Behaviour (ASB) legislation, which was led by my Department, recommended that further consideration be given to potentially amending four areas of legislation, namely Anti-Social Behaviour Orders (ASBOs), the legislative framework for tackling Drinking in Public, Injunctions Against Anti-Social Behaviour and Absolute Grounds for Possession. Policy responsibility for that legislation falls across both the Department of Justice (DoJ) and the Department for Communities (DfC) and on foot of the review, a joint consultation on potential amendments was launched, which closed on the 4 March 2024.Following the consultation my Department is working with DfC to address the issue of drinking in public, where there was support to amend the legislative framework to tackle this issue. Officials are currently engaged with operational partners and DfC colleagues to finalise proposals in this area. My Department is leading on consideration of any potential amendments to the ASBO, on which a number of issues were raised via the consultation. A Working Group comprising of statutory and operational partners and the community and voluntary sector has been established to consider these issues over a short timeframe and provide advice on how best to proceed. Following conclusion of the Working Group’s considerations and upon finalising those outstanding issues around drinking in public, it is intended to publish a full departmental response document, detailing the outcome of the consultation and a proposed way forward.It is important to note that although this was a joint public consultation with DfC, each Department will now progress any potential legislative amendments arising within their own area of responsibility, and any attendant steps, separately where it can.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 11837/22-27, to detail (i) the recommendations of the review of sentencing policy that are still outstanding; and (ii) the reasons why these have not yet been implemented.
answer_text: The Sentencing Policy Review considered ten discrete areas of sentencing policy: Principles and purposes of sentencing; Public perceptions of sentencing Sentencing guidanceTariff setting for murderUnduly lenient sentencesCommunity sentencingHate crimeAttacks on frontline public servicesCrimes against older and vulnerable peopleDriving offences causing death or serious injury.The 54 recommendations flowing from the review focussed primarily on the creation of new primary legislation and are being taken forward as part of my proposed Sentencing Bill. This is expected to be a large and complex Bill, given the variety of matters to be included. On present planning assumptions we anticipate this Bill being introduced later in 2025.  This will be dependent upon the progress of earlier Assembly business.  Work on public perceptions is being taken forward administratively including input to the Department’s ‘My Justice Journey’ initiative which will include information aimed at demystifying sentencing generally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when the formal notification of action short of strike action by social work staff was made to the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service.
answer_text: Social workers, including those working in the Court Children’s Service, are employed by the Health & Social Care Trusts. The Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service has not been formally notified that social work staff are taking action short of strike action.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the advice that has been provided by the Northern Ireland Court and Tribunals Service to private law parties affected by the action short of strike action being undertaken by social work staff in child and family teams.
answer_text: I understand that the Department of Health has established an Implementation Board with the aim of creating a more stable and sustainable social work workforce.  I have been advised that the shadow Family Justice Board is aware of the situation and has received an update from the Department of Health.  The provision of advice to private law parties affected by industrial action by social work staff is not a matter for the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on discussions between his Department and the Health and Social Care Trusts in relation to settling the industrial action taken by social work staff in child and family teams regarding staffing and pay.
answer_text: Officials from the Department continue to engage with Trusts and NIPSA representatives in relation to ongoing industrial action by social work staff in a number of children’s services regarding a range of issues in relation to workforce and workload pressures. Recent discussions have been productive. However, some issues remain unresolved at this point.The Department recognises the workforce and workload pressures in social work services and has an extensive programme of workforce and service reform underway to address these although this will take some time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of private law cases that have not been allocated a social work service during the course of the action short of strike action being undertaken by social work staff in child and family teams.
answer_text: The total number of private law cases that have not been allocated a social work service during the course of action short of strike is 30.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact of the action short of strike action being undertaken by social work staff in child and family teams on private law court lists.
answer_text: I understand that the Department of Health has established an Implementation Board with the aim of creating a more stable and sustainable social work workforce. I have been advised that the shadow Family Justice Board is aware of the situation and has received an update from the Department of Health.  Case management, including listing, is a matter for the independent judiciary and it would not be appropriate for me to comment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any engagement with representatives of the fishing industry who are concerned about the potential installation of offshore renewable energy wind farms in Irish Sea fishing grounds; and (ii) how these concerns are being addressed.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of engagement with marine stakeholders and I am delighted to confirm that my department has established the Offshore Renewable Energy Forum (OREF) to facilitate this engagement. The fishing industry is represented on the forum and have attended all sessions to date. All members of the forum have been given the opportunity to contribute towards and raise any concerns in relation to offshore renewable energy development in the north. My Department have appointed RPS Ireland to undertake an update to the 2012 Strategic Environmental Assessment and Habitats Regulation Assessment of Offshore Renewable Energy in the North of Irelands Marine Area. The SEA/HRA will enable us, in collaboration with key delivery partners, to identify suitable areas which maximise the potential of the seabed whilst causing the least disruption ecologically, as well as to other users of the marine area. A public consultation on potential areas for offshore renewable energy is due to be launched by my department and will also provide an additional opportunity for feedback.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the Migration Advisory Committee’s findings outlined in its Review of the Seasonal Worker Visa, published on 16 July 2024.
answer_text: On 23rd July 2024 I wrote to Professor Brian Bell following the publication of the Migration Advisory Committee’s (MAC’s) Review of the Seasonal Worker visa. I welcomed the recommendations in the review regarding the continuation of the visa route going forward, which supported announcements made in May 2024 by the previous UK Government that the Seasonal Agricultural Workers Scheme (SAWS) should continue until 2029. I agreed with the MAC’s observation that prompt confirmation from the new Government on whether they endorse this recommendation and detail on the yearly quotas for the scheme would provide certainty to users about the future of the scheme. In my letter I highlighted that while I understood the difficulties expressed in the Review regarding meeting differing stakeholder needs, it remains vital that a labour solution is found for our NI mushroom and fishing sectors, until such time that automation is more advanced and accessible in these industries. I expressed the view that our local stakeholders would be disappointed that the Review had not recommended the length of the visa should be extended beyond the 6 months currently in place.I also noted that the Review findings concluded that the eligible roles on the visa route should remain the same, which would continue to restrict the ability of the fishing sector to use the route.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 11838/22-27, when the review of the Inland Commercial Licensing and Permit Regime, commenced in July 2021, will be completed.
answer_text: The review of the Inland Commercial Licensing and Permit Regime has been incorporated into a broader review of the Fisheries Act (Northern Ireland) 1966 and the evidence that has been gathered is being used to inform the development of policy proposals for a new Fisheries Bill.  These policy proposals will be subject to public consultation in 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the outcome of any meetings that have taken place with the Home Office to discuss challenges facing the agriculture sector and the fishing sector in accessing migrant workers.
answer_text: I wrote to the then Home Secretary, Rt Hon James Cleverly MP on 8th March 2024 to request a meeting to discuss the labour challenges facing our agriculture and fishing sectors. Whilst I did receive a response from Tom Pursglove, the Minister of State for Legal Migration and the Border at that time, defending UK Government immigration policy, no meeting was offered. However, my officials continue to engage with Home Office and Defra officials on labour matters for the agri-food chain, including the Seasonal Workers visa route.On 9th July 2024, I wrote to the newly appointed Defra Secretary of State, Rt Hon Steve Reed MP, bringing to his attention a range of pressing issues for my department, including labour shortages and the importance of immigration policy to our agri-food industry. On 30th July 2024, the UK Government published a Ministerial Statement in respect of legal migration, confirming that changes to immigration policy and its associated visa routes introduced by the previous Government, would remain in place. It remains vital that labour solutions can be found for NI sectors currently reliant on migrant labour, until such time that automation is more advanced and accessible. Whilst immigration policy is not a devolved matter, I remain committed to working with local agri-food businesses, Executive colleagues, and the UK Government to find solutions for those occupations that are experiencing shortages.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the current excess prison capacity.
answer_text: At Maghaberry Prison, as a result of the rise in population, the Prison Service has re-opened two out of three square houses (Foyle and Erne).  In normal operation each of these houses can accommodate 130 prisoners.  On 12 September there were 63 prisoners in Erne House and 125 prisoners in Foyle House, meaning approximately 72 spaces remain.  Once Erne House and Foyle House reach capacity the only remaining accommodation at Maghaberry is in Lagan House which can also hold a further 130 prisoners.On 12 September the population of Magilligan Prison was 497.  The population at Magilligan is being carefully managed to sustain it as close to its capacity of 500 prisoners as possible, to alleviate the ongoing pressure on Maghaberry Prison.Hydebank Wood is the smallest prison establishment in Northern Ireland, housing both young male and female prisoners on a single site.  Whilst the young male population there has remained relatively stable, the female population has increased.  There are no immediate concerns about the capacity of Hydebank, however if the female population continues to increase it may be necessary to reconfigure the use of accommodation on that site.In respect of population management the Prison Service is also maximising the use of all available accommodation, including through the use of cell sharing, with 400 prisoners ‘doubling-up’ on a regular basis.  Dependent upon the configuration of the population there may be additional capacity through cell sharing if it is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the review of the policy on residents’ parking schemes, which commenced in December 2019, will be completed.
answer_text: The review of residents’ parking schemes is complete, and I have had the opportunity to consider the draft review report and I plan to issue the final review report soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 11838/22-27, when the review of the Inland Angling Licensing and Permit Regime, commenced in July 2021, will be completed.
answer_text: The review of the Inland Angling Licensing and Permit Regime has been incorporated into a broader review of the Fisheries Act (Northern Ireland) 1966 and the evidence that has been gathered is being used to inform the development of policy proposals for a new Fisheries Bill.  These policy proposals will be subject to public consultation in 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed park and ride facility for (i) Newtownards; and (ii) Comber.
answer_text: My Department has identified a list of priority Park and Ride sites, including suitable sites in Newtownards and Comber.  The preferred site at Newtownards is located at the former Ards Leisure Centre site with the potential for 450 vehicles.  The planning application for the Newtownards site was submitted in November 2023 and work is ongoing to address a number of remaining issues. The preferred site at Comber is located along the A22 Belfast Road corridor immediately adjacent to the Comber Greenway with the potential to provide parking provision for 410 vehicles in its first phase of development with scope to extend the site further.  Feasibility and appraisal works is ongoing to enable the submission of a planning application in the near future. Whilst I am supportive of expanding our Park and Ride schemes where needed, the member will be aware that my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to under funding and austerity by the British Government.  As the capital budget for my Department in 2024/25 is less than required, available funding must be prioritised accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) when the review of minerals licensing policy, commenced in 2019, will be completed; and (ii) to detail the reasons for the delay in its completion.
answer_text: The Department is reviewing the minerals licensing policy which is enshrined in the Minerals Development Act (Northern Ireland) 1969. As an initial step, to inform the scope of the review, independent research was commissioned in 2021 into the economic, social and environmental impact of mineral exploration and mining.  Research completed in 2022 and identified gaps in our knowledge.  There are now three key themes as part of the ongoing work of the review:Research on domestic supply opportunities both in terms of circular economy potential and building a better understanding of our ground resources;Stakeholder engagement on the role of minerals in the economy; and Updating the regulatory framework for mineral licensing.The review is also informed by developments in the EU with the adoption of the Critical Raw Materials Act in March 2024 and the UK Critical Minerals Strategy 2022 and refresh of 2023.  Both set out a range of critical raw materials/minerals which are essential to support the green transition and digitalisation of the economy. The review will consider our policy position in terms of both the development of a circular economy in minerals and the robust regulation of exploration and primary production.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the recommendations of the review on Dial-a-Lift and Disability Actions Transports Schemes will be implemented.
answer_text: I am still considering the next steps following the completion of the evaluation phase of the review of the Dial-a-Lift and Disability Action Transport Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the review of the strategic planning policy on oil and gas development, which commenced in September 2021, will be completed.
answer_text: The Department is awaiting the outcome of the Department for the Economy’s separate review of its petroleum (and minerals) licencing policies, and is unable to provide a date for completion of the review of regional strategic planning policy on oil and gas development at this time.
