question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any consideration has been given to amending the Stormont Regulation and Government Property Act (Northern Ireland) 1933 to allow public sector land to be disposed for the public good at less than market value.
answer_text: My officials are working collaboratively with colleagues in the Departments for Communities and the Economy to undertake a feasibility assessment of the recommendations in the Independent Panel Report on Community Wealth Building, that was commissioned by the Department for Communities (DfC). This includes consideration of a recommendation that DfC would prepare a new Community Asset Transfer Delivery Framework based on comprehensive legislation to ensure effective and efficient community asset transfer across public bodies here. Separately I have also asked my officials to identify the actions that my Department can take forward independently to make improvements in the Community Asset Transfer process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he intends to extend permitted development rights to allow for a larger number of homes through the re-purposing of existing buildings.
answer_text: I have no current plans to extend permitted development rights to allow for the re-purposing of existing buildings to residential use.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he intends to reduce the submission of a Pre-Application Notice period from 12 weeks to 21 days to bring it into line with other jurisdictions.
answer_text: The issue of reducing the 12-week period for Pre-Application Notice was raised and considered in the Report on the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 (RIPA), published in January 2022.  In its response, the Department concluded that any proposed reduction to the current 12-week period could negatively impact on pre-application community participation and may be viewed as an attempt to limit / curtail meaningful community engagement.  This position has not changed.I therefore consider the legislative planning provisions within section 27(3) of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 to be adequate and as such, I have no plans to amend the legislation at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has considered introducing legislative changes to enable statutory consultees to charge their own Pre-Application Discussion fees as a means to improve capacity and responsiveness.
answer_text: The issue of Pre-Application Discussions (PAD) was raised and considered in the Report on the Review of the Implementation of the Planning Act (NI) 2011 (RIPA), published in January 2022. Some respondents to the call for evidence suggested that PADs should be moved to a legislative footing, particularly for major and regionally significant proposals, with statutory consultees enabled to charge their own PAD fees with the income ring-fenced to improve capacity. Ultimately, and having considered available evidence, the Department concluded that it was more effective to retain the current discretion planning authorities have to undertake PADs on a proportionate basis.  This position has not changed.However, forthcoming legislative amendments to enable councils to prepare and publish planning application validation checklists is aimed at improving the quality of applications entering the system and should, when implemented, relieve pressure on consultees from the need for multiple re-consultations.  The targeted and proportionate use of PADs is an important element in effectively ‘frontloading’ the planning application process and this will continue to be considered by the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when it is anticipated that the Independent Review of Discretionary Support, commenced in June 2021, will be complete.
answer_text: The Independent Review Panel were appointed in May 2021 and their review of the Discretionary Support Scheme was completed in October 2021.  The Department published the Panel’s Report on 25 March 2022. The report is accessible here: https://communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/discretionary-support-independent-review

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9525/22-27, what is currently delaying a determination on the Hightown Incinerator planning application.
answer_text: My officials are continuing to undertake the necessary administrative processes, including assessing consultation advice from the necessary interested bodies and public authorities.When all statutory processes are satisfactorily completed, a recommendation will be brought forward by my officials; however, at this time I cannot confirm when a recommendation will be made. I am keen to bring a resolution to this long standing application, but if a sound decision is to be reached it is important the planning process is completed correctly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the rail safety review, commenced in March 2022, will be complete.
answer_text: The rail safety review has completed data collection and it is anticipated it will report by the end of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the review of regional strategic planning policy for renewable and low carbon energy, which commenced in January 2021, will be completed.
answer_text: The review of regional strategic planning policy for renewable and low carbon energy is at an advanced stage of completion. I expect to publish the revised policy later this year, following Executive Committee agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the interim measures under the Executive's Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: I made a Written Ministerial Statement on 23 May 2024 advising Members of a £25m package of measures for early learning and childcare in 2024/25.  The measures, which are being implemented in 2024/2025, are designed to; stabilise and support expansion of the early learning and childcare sector over time, offer all children 22.5 hours per week pre-school provision, reduce the costs of childcare for working parents and build the evidence base necessary to inform development of the longer-term strategy.  This Statement outlined the most significant enhancement of early years investment within Northern Ireland in decades.  I am pleased to report good progress in the implementation of the measures. My Department has introduced a new Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme, to help working parents with the affordability of their childcare.   I have been very encouraged by the interest in the scheme and that in a matter of weeks, approximately 12,000 children have been registered to receive the 15% subsidy.  The families of these children have already started to feel the financial benefits of the Scheme in their pockets as the first discounts were applied to bills for September 2024.I stated that making childcare more affordable was one of my top priorities, and I am pleased that this scheme is already delivering for hard-working families across Northern Ireland.  Another major transformation will be the standardisation of the Pre-school Educational Programme. My Department already provides over 40% target aged pre-school children with at least 22.5 hours pre-school education provision per week.  I have announced that I intend to increase this over time to become a universal offer.  I have secured £5 million to support approximately 100 pre-school settings to transition to offer at least 22.5 hours per week from September 2025. This will provide approximately 2,200 more children with a longer pre-school education session and access to a free school meal for those who are eligible to receive one.  I hope to begin announcing settings that will transform from September 2025 before the end of this calendar year.The interim measures have allowed for the stabilisation and support of our excellent targeted support schemes for children and young people facing challenges in circumstance, or with developmental delays. For example, I have been delighted to provide an uplift of £3.1m for Sure Start this year. We have also been able to allocate funding to other Departments for stabilisation measures related to early learning and childcare, such as funding for the Department of Health to support children with a disability and the Department for Communities to support the training of childminders. In regard to the Targeted Business Support Scheme, the Department for the Economy will be leading the scoping work to determine the need and appropriate model of intervention. This will require input from the sector, and the Department of Health, to better understand the i

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when the recommendations of the Independent Review of Charity Regulation will be implemented by his Department, following the Department’s response to those recommendations in November 2022.
answer_text: The Independent Panel for the Review of Charity Regulation made 93 linked and, in some cases, mutually dependent recommendations.  To date, 34 recommendations have been implemented.  Work on a further 19 recommendations will be undertaken by the Commission in this financial year.Work is ongoing on Departmental recommendations, 20 of these will require primary legislation, which will be brought before the Assembly in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the restructuring of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.
answer_text: Transformation of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, particularly focused on securing its access to borrowing, provides the opportunity to sustain public housing without acting as a drag on already stretched public services. I am actively pressing for a solution that will transform it onto a sustainable footing to increase routine & planned investment, to deal with maintenance backlogs and a program of retrofit to improve energy efficiency in homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration he has given to the provision of a one-off support payment to those residents directly affected by the Second World War bomb evacuation and detonation in Newtownards.
answer_text: Any decision to make a one-off payment to householders affected by this incident would be a decision for the Executive.The Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance (SEFA) for non-flooding incidents was activated on 16 August 2024. This scheme provides financial support to Ards & North Down Borough Council for direct costs incurred by the council and services contracted out by the council. It does not provide any financial support to householders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration he has given to amending Article 15 of The Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1992 to allow registered Housing Associations the ability to use Financial Transactions Capital to build homes for direct sale.
answer_text: My Department is not considering making any amendments to Article 15 of The Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1992, to allow registered Housing Associations (RHAs) the ability to use Financial Transactions Capital to build homes for direct sale.   However, my officials have undertaken a review of the interpretation of Article 15 to assess the scope of what activities may be permissible by RHAs such as the incidental disposal of properties, necessary as part of the delivery of a wider scheme.  Early work is underway to develop guidance for RHAs. This will not include any amendment to Article 15 of The Housing (Northern Ireland) Order 1992.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department’s policy in relation to the advance notification of road closures to affected constituents and elected representatives.
answer_text: My Department processes between 25,000 and 30,000 road or lane closure applications annually on a recently developed system. This system sends an email notification of each approved work permit in advance of planned roadworks to a council-based distribution list which includes the local council, blue-light services, coastal and transportation services.  Upcoming roadworks on the busiest routes are also advertised in the main press.  Individual approved roadwork applications are then manually added to the TrafficWatchNI website system for public viewing where resources permit.  Most of the closures prepared by my Department are on behalf of 3rd parties such as Utilities.  Given the volume of closures, and in line with the Limited Service principles being implemented by my Department because of funding pressures, communication with road users in relation to upcoming roadworks is focused on the busiest routes, primarily the 'A' Class network. Communications used include use of advanced notification signage or Variable Messaging Signs on the impacted route in advance of the works, as well as notification on TrafficWatchNI. Not all roadworks are displayed on the TrafficWatchNI website, however, priority is given to those roadworks that affect the public the most. This notification is extended to busier lower tier roads as resources permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update in relation to the issue of bluetongue in Great Britain, including the restriction of movement of animals in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Since 26 August 2024, bluetongue virus has been confirmed on 34 premises in England.  A brief timeline of latest situation updates can be accessed here.A Restricted Zone (RZ) has been established encompassing Norfolk, Suffolk and Essex.  A Temporary Control Zone (TCZ) has been established around East Yorkshire as this area is geographically distinct from the RZ.  An Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA) interactive bluetongue virus map which highlights the current RZ, TCZ and high risk counties in England can be accessed here.  You will be aware that due to confirmed bluetongue cases in Great Britain (GB), as of 10 November 2023 the direct movement of all live ruminants and camelids from GB to Northern Ireland (NI) is suspended.  There are currently no restrictions on the movement of animals within NI.  In respect of trade from NI to the EU (including the Republic of Ireland), there is no impact on moves.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he intends to introduce planning performance agreements into the planning system.
answer_text: A planning performance agreement (PPA) is a project management tool which local planning authorities and applicants may use to agree milestones, actions and resources for handling particular largescale and/or complex planning applications. It is a voluntary agreement made between applicants and local planning authorities prior to the application being submitted and can be a useful focus of pre-application discussions about the issues that will need to be addressed by a planning application.As PPAs are used as a management tool, and entered into voluntarily, there is nothing to prevent a planning authority seeking to utilise them where appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she intends to create a vacant sites register.
answer_text: My department holds records for two specific areas related to this:The rating system administered by Land & Property Services (LPS) identifies property within the taxbase that is capable of occupation for rates purposes, including such property that is vacant;The Government Land and Property Register (GLPR) was set up in 2019 in LPS to establish a central register of information on public sector assets here. The initial focus of the work has been to collate information on departmental owned or occupied land and property. When work on the register is complete it can be used to identify underused sites.A full register of vacant sites here including those in the private sector is not held by my department. This would be a cross-cutting matter for the whole Executive. Other departments will hold additional data sets and/or registers which may be relevant. While no part of government will hold a full picture, I am very willing to work with Ministerial and Executive colleagues to explore the benefit and feasibility of establishing such a register on a cross-departmental basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the compensation available to farmers who have an animal with bluetongue.
answer_text: Compensation is only payable if my Department considers it necessary to cull any animals for disease control measures.  If Bluetongue positive animals are culled, compensation will be paid at 50% of market value.Any imported animals that are found to be infected with Bluetongue during post import checks will be culled with no compensation payable.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the implications for the export trade of animals from Northern Ireland if a case of bluetongue is diagnosed locally.
answer_text: There could be significant implications for the export trade of live ruminants if we faced cases of Bluetongue Virus here.  That is why it is so important that famers source animals responsibly and maintain vigilance for signs of the disease and that they report any suspect cases immediately to the Department.		Those implications could include prohibitions on export of ruminants, particularly if Northern Ireland were to lose its Bluetongue Free status.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will consider reversing the decision to increase the minimum claim size for farms to access the new Farm Sustainability Transition Payment from three hectares to five hectares.
answer_text: Former Minister Poots outlined his position to the Assembly with regards to this matter on Thursday 24 March 2022, the last sitting day in the previous mandate, detailing that following consultation on increasing the threshold 10 ha he intended to set the minimum claim size at 5 hectares. Work is progressing to ensure that the secondary legislation requirements for the new payment are in place in advance of 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the impact on the farming community, and the wider Northern Ireland economy, of the decision to increase the claim size for farms to access the Farm Sustainability Transition Payment from three hectares to five hectares.
answer_text: Based on the 2023 Basic Payment Scheme application data around 1,400 farm businesses may be impacted by this decision, however these farm businesses will have the option to acquire additional eligible land and/or payment entitlements to ensure they remain eligible to claim the Farm Sustainability Transition Payment.  The removal of the farm businesses impacted by the increase in minimum claim size releases approximately £2 m annually which will be used to fund other schemes within the Farm Support and Development Programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has made, or will make, representations to his counterpart in DEFRA to highlight the effect that the Extended Producer Responsibility scheme pricing model based on weight rather than units will have on glass recycling as opposed to lighter less recyclable materials such as plastic.
answer_text: I acknowledge the concerns around indicative costs for glass producers and can assure you that these have been relayed to DEFRA.We have heard and understand the points raised by the glass industry in relation to the illustrative base fees. We are arranging a meeting with the glass industry to discuss the challenges and will continue to review the approach in response to industry feedback.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of DEFRA’s illustrative fees for the Extended Producer Responsibility scheme and its impact on glass recycling.
answer_text: I have heard the concerns raised by the glass industry in relation to the recently published illustrative base fees. I am keen to maximise recycling wherever possible and have set ambitious targets for recycling of packaging materials through the Extended Producer Responsibility scheme. A meeting has been arranged to discuss the challenges for the glass industry with officials from all UK nations. We will continue to review the approach in response to industry feedback.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the consultation that was undertaken prior to the 45 per cent increase in daily parking fees imposed at the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The Department’s policy on Car Parking Provision and Management in the Health and Social Care sector for Northern Ireland was published in 2012 and sets the direction for car park management.  The policy states that it is for each Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT) to determine the way that the car parking policy, including charging, is applied to each of their sites, to enable them to take into account local issues.Charging should be used for payback on investment and to help cover the cost of the provision and maintenance of car parking including the associated security costs.  In the event that there is a surplus, it should be reinvested in patient services. In 2015, the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust undertook a full public consultation to seek views on charging members of the public for parking.  Following extensive feedback, the Trust took the decision to extend charging for public parking across all major sites.  This included implementing exemptions for persons undergoing renal dialysis, chemotherapy, radiotherapy and for relatives of persons receiving care in ICU, HDU as well as relatives of long stay patients.   The Trust has not increased public car parking prices since 2015, however, during this decade, costs associated with car parking provision and maintenance have increased considerably, due to inflationary pressures.The Trust recently approved the introduction of an hourly pricing structure at the Ulster Hospital alongside introducing free parking for the first 30 minutes to facilitate short stay visits, drop off and collections. On-site staff car parking remains free for permitted staff, with alternative, free off-site parking options available for non-permitted staff.In line with the Hospital Parking Charges Act 2024, the Trust is also working at pace to introduce ANPR technology to enable free car parking for all users by May 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 13014/22-27, (i) whether his officials have completed their consideration of the Cass Review; and (ii) what actions his Department will take following the recommendations contained in the Cass Review final report.
answer_text: My officials arranged two workshops in June with members of the Gender Identity Review Group and Gender Identity Liaison Panel which included clinicians, representatives from the Belfast HSC Trust’s Knowing Our Identity Service for under 18s, and transgender representative organisations to discuss the Review’s recommendations and any implications for the service provided in Northern Ireland. At those workshops, the recommendations were discussed in detail, categorised, prioritised in terms of urgency, impact, and their cost implications were also considered. My officials have met with NHS England to discuss the Cass Review, its recommendations and implications for gender identity services across the UK and areas to work together going forward. The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC),England is also compiling a list of Gender Identity representative groups from across the UK, including Northern Ireland, with a view to carrying out a consultation on the implications of a permanent ban of puberty blockers for under 18s.  My officials are continuing to work with their DHSC colleagues in this process. In addition, my officials have also arranged a meeting with their Republic of Ireland colleagues later this month to get a fuller understanding of their position on the use of gender dysphoria medicines and gender healthcare in young people.  Following on from further engagement and work with NHS England, DHSC and their RoI colleagues, my officials will provide detailed advice for my consideration on any actions necessary in respect of the Cass Review recommendations.  Furthermore, an emergency 3-month prohibition order under Section 62 of the Medicines Act 1968 to prohibit the inappropriate use of gender dysphoria medicines in children in GB is currently in place.  This is due to expire on 3 September, and I am aware the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care in England intends to extend the order for a further three months until a permanent solution can be agreed. The making of an emergency order that will extend to Northern Ireland must be made on a sound evidential basis; that is, it is essential to make the Order with immediate effect to avoid serious danger to health.   I have therefore written to my Executive colleagues on 2 August with a detailed paper on the evidence base and recommending we agree firstly to align with GB and legislate on a UK-wide basis, by extending the emergency order to Northern Ireland, on the basis Executive members are satisfied the evidence is clear that it is essential to make it with immediate effect, to avoid serious danger to health; and secondly to agree we work towards a permanent solution which will require consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any lessons that have been learnt by his Department in the local development process; and (ii) any steps that will be taken to ensure that the problems and delays encountered will not be repeated in the next plan cycle.
answer_text: Following The Northern Ireland Audit Office report ‘Planning in Northern Ireland’ (Feb 2022) and Public Accounts Committee (March 2022) reports, the Department, working jointly with councils, agreed a total of 11 linked Planning Improvement Programme (PIP) actions aimed at improving the effectiveness and efficiency of the plan making system by further streamlining processes and providing greater clarity in respect of role/ responsibilities of all stakeholders.The identified actions are ambitious and range from a review of the Planning (Local Development Plan) Regulations 2015; “training” for statutory consultees to the LDP process; a memorandum of understanding to address mutually agreed roles and responsibilities of participants to the Independent Examination (IE) process; and the exploration of options for providing greater flexibility for necessary policy changes to be agreed and consulted upon within IE hearing sessions. The Department considers that the above measures will respond to the identified concerns and lessons learned and collectively contribute to the streamlining of the LDP process. Furthermore, my Department continues to liaise with the Department of Justice with regards to the resource and capacity issues faced by the PAC that has potential to impact future IEs of those forthcoming LDPs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children with epilepsy that are currently registered for services with the Scrabo Children’s Centre.
answer_text: There are 200 children with epilepsy that are currently registered for services based at Scrabo Children's Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 13020/22-27, to detail what proposals she has to amend in the Rehabilitation of Offenders (Northern Ireland) Order 1978 to reduce existing rehabilitation periods.
answer_text: The detail of the legislative reforms that I intend to bring forward to reduce existing rehabilitation periods and to allow more convictions to become spent during this Assembly mandate are as set out in the report on the consultation on Proposals to Reform Rehabilitation Periods in Northern Ireland, which is available to view on the Department of Justice website.  The report can be accessed here: Consultation Report - Proposals to Reform Rehabilitation Periods in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he is considering providing local authorities with the power to charge a community infrastructure levy to help address the growing impact on existing infrastructure such as water services.
answer_text: Developers may already be required to make contributions to mitigate the impact of a proposal in order to overcome a policy or legislative barrier to the grant of planning permission.  All such contributions must be reasonable and related to the development proposal in question.Following the transfer of planning functions to councils, Development Management Practice Note 21 was introduced as guidance for planning authorities in respect of planning agreements under section 76 of the Planning (NI) Act 2011. Planning agreements are however voluntary and, some councils may make more use of them than others

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the number of car parking spaces his Department and its agencies have in Belfast city centre; and (ii) whether there are plans to reduce this number.
answer_text: For the purpose of answering this question, I have included those car parks that fall within the Belfast City Centre BT1 and BT2 postcodes. I can confirm that my Department has no car parking spaces in Belfast City Centre.I have detailed in the table below, the number of spaces held in the city centre by my Department’s Arm Length Bodies. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) to detail the number of car parking spaces his Department and its agencies have in Belfast city centre; and (ii) whether there are plans to reduce this number.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department and its agencies have no car parking spaces in Belfast city centre.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail the number of car parking spaces his Department and its agencies have in Belfast city centre; and (ii) whether there are plans to reduce this number.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy has 17 parking spaces at its buildings in central Belfast. They are all located at Killymeal House, the Office of Industrial Tribunals and Fair Employment Tribunal (OITFET). answer_text: The information requested is shown in the tables below.(i) The number of car parking spaces the Department for Infrastructure has in Belfast city centre are listed in the table below:Numbers of car parking spacesLocation310Clarence Court, Belfast 2Departmental Headquarters James House, Belfast312TOTAL(ii) With the imminent closure of Clarence Court on the 30th August 2024 the number of car parking spaces in Belfast City Centre will be reduced to two spaces located in James House.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the average processing time in the last 12 months for dealing with requests to move animals under licence for herds that are restricted from moving animals as a result of a TB outbreak.
answer_text: In the 12 months from July 2023 to June 2024, the average processing time for moving animals under licence, for herds that are restricted from moving animals as a result of a TB outbreak:(i) for moves to meat plants was within the same working day.  (ii) for moves to other non-associated herds was an average of 14 calendar days, from the date the exceptional move was requested until an approval decision was given.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the percentage of animals that had TB detected during a TB test were animals less than 42 days old, in the last 12 months.
answer_text: During the 12-month period up to 31 May 2024, 15 calves under the age of 42 days were declared as TB reactors after skin testing, which is 0.08% of the total TB skin test reactors over that period.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the number of car parking spaces her Department and its agencies have in Belfast city centre; and (ii) whether there are plans to reduce this number.
answer_text: (i) 	As at June 2024, the number of car parking spaces held by my department and its agencies in Belfast city centre are as follows:The Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) has 117 car parking spaces at the secure car park at Laganside Courts and 55 car parking spaces at the secure car park at the Royal Courts of Justice.The Youth Justice Agency (YJA) has 22 car parking spaces at a car park in Tomb Street, Belfast.(ii)	Neither the NICTS nor the YJA have current plans to reduce the above number of spaces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of herds, that were restricted from moving animals as a result of a TB outbreak, requested to move animals to another holding under licence in the last 12 months.
answer_text: DAERA does not hold collated information on the total number of herds, that were restricted from moving animals as a result of a TB outbreak, which requested to move animals to another holding under licence in the last 12 months.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of herds, that were restricted from moving animals as a result of a TB outbreak, were approved to move animals under licence to another herd in the last 12 months.
answer_text: From the 12 months from July 2023 to June 2024, there were 31 distinct herds that, while being restricted from moving animals as a result of a TB outbreak, were approved to move animals under licence to another non-associated herd.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the number of car parking spaces his Department and its agencies have in Belfast city centre; and (ii) whether there are plans to reduce this number.
answer_text: The Department of Health is based at Castle Buildings, Stormont Estate and has no car parking space provision in Belfast city centre.The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) plans and oversees the delivery of health and social care services for the population of Northern Ireland. The Group is part of the Department of Health and have the following car parking spaces in Belfast City Centre:22 owned car parking spaces for staff and visitors in its car park at the rear of its Linenhall Street Office; and 20 rented car parking spaces under a regional HSC contract at Lanyon Place.SPPG reduced its rented car parking capacity in Belfast City Centre from 30 car parking spaces to 20 car parking spaces in 2023/24 and continues to review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) to detail the number of car parking spaces her Department and its agencies have in Belfast city centre; and (ii) whether there are plans to reduce this number.
answer_text: There are 468 car parking spaces located at 11 buildings owned or leased by my Department in Belfast City Centre. These spaces are used and managed by the departments and public bodies who occupy each building. Under my Department’s plans to reduce the size of the office estate, the number of car parking spaces in Belfast City Centre will reduce by 313 before the end of this financial year with the sale of two office buildings and exiting of one lease.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) to detail the number of car parking spaces their Department and its agencies have in Belfast city centre; and (ii) whether there are plans to reduce this number.
answer_text: The Department’s core Directorates have no parking spaces in Belfast city centre.  Its Arm’s-Length Bodies (ALBs) and independent bodies currently have 89 such spaces.The Department is instigating a review with each of its ALBs and independent bodies in light of sustainable travel policies and challenging budget pressures.You will wish to note that a reply to your question 1644/17-22 in 2020, also on the topic of car parking, indicated that the Department and its ALBs had a total of 67 staff car parking spaces in Belfast city centre, 16 of which were re-charged to staff.  In reviewing these statistics, it became apparent that this answer was incorrect and at the time there were 95 staff spaces, held by ALBs and independent bodies, of which 44 were re-charged to staff.  We will ensure a copy of this answer is placed in the Assembly Library and cross-referenced with AQW 1644/17-22 to reflect the accurate position for the record.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of TB reactor cattle that have been removed from farms and slaughtered due to disease since devolution commenced in 1998.
answer_text: I can advise that during the period from 01 January 1998 to 30 April 2024 a total of 314, 082 TB reactor cattle were removed from farms and slaughtered.Of this total, 302,630 were cattle who reacted to the Single Intradermal Comparative Cervical Tuberculin (SICCT) test or skin test.Following introduction of the Interferon Gamma test, a further 11,452 cattle were also removed from farms and slaughtered during the period 01 April 2011 to 30 April 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) how plant protection products are controlled and regulated; (ii) which body consults with stakeholders in relation to this; and (iii) who has the power to authorise the emergency use of these products.
answer_text: (i) DAERA is the Competent Authority responsible for implementing EU pesticide-related legislation and for pesticide policy in NI. This includes ensuring compliance with legislation on the placing on the market and sustainable use of pesticides and pesticide residues.DAERA is also the competent authority responsible for the organisation of official controls and of other official activities for the purposes of ensuring operators’ compliance with this legislation.The Chemicals Regulation Division of the Health and Safety Executive GB (HSEGB), delivers a number of Competent Authority functions on DAERA’s behalf under Agency Agreement.  These include the evaluation and authorisation of pesticides for sale and use in NI and setting the conditions of use of authorised pesticides.(ii) DAERA is responsible for stakeholder engagement on control and regulation of Plant Protection Products, in conjunction with HSEGB where applicable.(iii) Applications for Article 53 Emergency Use Authorisations must be submitted using the form CRD9 to HSEGB, who assess these on DAERA’s behalf.  They then make a recommendation on the decision and seek formal agreement from DAERA officials to exercise the decision-making function on the application on DAERA’s behalf in line with the Agency Agreement. Novel and contentious decisions on Emergency Use Authorisations may be referred to me, as Minister, either for decision or for information.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of businesses in the Strangford constituency which have (i) filed for bankruptcy; (ii) been subject to a creditor’s petition for bankruptcy; and (iii) entered into a voluntary arrangement with their creditors, in each of the last four years.
answer_text: The number of sole trader businesses (bankruptcies) and winding up orders for limited company businesses where the individual or director has petitioned for the bankruptcy/winding up, the number subject to a creditors petition and the number of Individual Voluntary Arrangements (IVAs) in the Strangford constituency for the previous four financial years is detailed in the table below.  2020-212021-222022-232023-24 Sole TraderWinding Up OrdersSole TraderWinding Up OrdersSole TraderWinding Up OrdersSole TraderWinding Up OrdersBusiness Own Petitions00000000Creditors Petitions00010105Individual Voluntary Arrangement (IVAs)58767359			Individual Voluntary Arrangments (IVAs) are administered by private sector insolvency practicitoners. My Department is only responsible for maintaining a register of IVAs in the North of Ireland and it cannot be determined, from the statutory information held, how much of the debt, if any, is attributable to business debt.		The directors of limited companies may also enter into arrangements with the companies creditors, Company Voluntary Arrangements (CVAs), however, my Department has no role in this process which are administered solely by private sector insolvency practitioners therefore I am unable to provide numbers in relation to these.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 10631/22-27; (ii) AQW 11653/22-27; and AQW (iii) 11921/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether Sport NI has developed a priority list of capital projects in the event of additional capital money becoming available.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI’s capital investment programmes over the last 4-5 years has delivered prioritised projects as and when exchequer funding became available. In some cases this was from a retained list of potential projects. New challenges now face the sector, impacting the relevance, deliverability and viability of projects retained on lists for 4-5 years.“Sport NI has been engaging with the sector which has highlighted demand for new capital funding opportunities to made available. In addition our engagement has shown that the sports sectors is facing new challenges since our previous funds were developed. Consequently, we are developing new capital funding opportunities to reflect this demand and the challenges.“Exchequer funding is in-year sensitive, therefore, early indication of budget within the financial year is critical for the successful delivery of capital project in year.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with Sport NI will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.You will be aware that there are budgetary pressures across all departments in the Northern Ireland Executive and this includes my Department for Communities. My Department has published an Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) on Budget 2024-25 at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/consultations/communities/dfc-budget-2024-25-eqia.pdf, which launched on 11 June 2024, and I encourage all interested parties to respond.At this time only limited resources are available for capital funding and it is unlikely that additional funding will be available to invest in a new funding programme for sports clubs.  However, I will continue to make the case for future investment across all sports.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will indicate the timescale for the launch of a new capital funding programme for sports clubs.
answer_text: You will be aware that there are budgetary pressures across all departments in the Northern Ireland Executive and this includes my Department. My Department has published an Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) on Budget 2024-25, which launched on 11 June 2024.[See link EQIA] and I encourage all interested parties to respond.At this time only limited resources are available for capital funding and it is unlikely that additional funding will be available to invest in a new funding programme for sports clubs. Having said that, I have continued to provide support for the Your School Your Club initiative that will be delivered though my Department’s arm’s length body, Sport NI. Your School Your Club Joint Working Group, which is led by my Department and includes representatives from Sport NI, Department of Education, Education Authority, local Councils and Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, will agree and commence the process to engage with schools to identify project proposals that can be delivered by March 2025. This programme encourages partnerships between schools and community groups/local sports clubs to open up or enhance existing  facilities on school sites for use outside of normal school hours.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the park and ride sites which will be developed in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: My Department has identified a list of priority bus and rail-based Park and Ride sites and further potential sites will be considered within the new Transport Strategy and transport plans currently being developed.I am supportive of expanding our Park and Ride schemes where needed.  Officials are currently considering the detailed programmes of work across the Department’s areas of responsibility, including plans for Park and Ride.  Once finalised, these programmes will be published in council reports.However, the member will be aware that my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to under funding and austerity by the British Government.  As the capital budget for my Department in 2024/25 is less than required, available funding must be prioritised accordingly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions he has had with the Grand Orange Lodge of lreland and the Royal Black lnstitution for the development of the (i) Twelfth of July; and (ii) Royal 13th to maximise the tourist potential.
answer_text: I have not had any discussions with Grand Orange Lodge of Ireland or the Royal Black Institution regarding these events.However, Tourism NI works with a range of organisations to connect and develop experiences. A Culture and Heritage Visitor Experience Development Plan was agreed in 2023 to provide a focus for work in this area. To date, the Culture and Heritage team at Tourism NI has not had any engagement with the Grand Orange Lodge of Ireland or the Royal Black Institution.Between 2016 and 2018, Tourism NI provided support of £41,000 to the Scarva and District Cultural Society for the Royal 13th event from the Tourism Events Sponsorship Scheme. Tourism NI events funding operates on an annual open-call basis. No applications or expressions of interest have been received from the Grand Lodge of Ireland or the Royal Black Institution for the Twelfth of July or the Royal 13th.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when he plans to make the Northern lreland appointments to the Board of the Ulster Scots Agency, including the position of Chairman.
answer_text: We intend that Executive nominees to vacancies on the Boards of the North South Bodies will be progressed over the forthcoming months and that appointments, including the positions of vacant Chair and Vice Chairpersons, will be made at a suitable NSMC meeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when Sport Nl's lnfrastructure Review will be published and the associated public consultation launched.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI is working with the sports sector and others to identify key trends, opportunities and deficiencies across space and places for sport and physical activity. We are taking this forward in a modular way, looking at particular aspects of sporting infrastructure, building a more complete picture over a period of time. In 2023-24 we have surveyed the sector on environmental sustainability, community use of schools and flooding. The findings of these surveys are being analysed and reviewed, and we will publish them over the course of the next few months to inform the sector.Drawn together, these findings and reports will inform our direction towards any significant infrastructure investment programme, and when those proposals are developed, they will be subject to sectoral engagement and public consultation, where they will be tested, challenged and further enhanced as a result.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has met with UEFA or the IFA to discuss the potential redevelopment of Windsor Park as an option for hosting 2028 UEFA European Football Championship matches.
answer_text: I met with the Irish FA in March 2024 and they advised that redeveloping the National Football Stadium at Windsor Park for hosting 2028 UEFA European Championship Football matches was not under consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has to provide adequate capital funding to upgrade facilities at Northern lreland's premier league cricket grounds.
answer_text: You will be aware that there are budgetary pressures across all departments in the Northern Ireland Executive and this includes my Department for Communities. My Department has published an Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) on Budget 2024-25, which launched on 11 June 2024 [See link EQIA] and I encourage all interested parties to respond.At this time only limited resources are available for capital funding and it is unlikely that additional funding will be available to invest in a new funding programme specifically for premier league cricket grounds in Northern Ireland. As this issue is also an operational matter for Sport NI, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI have no capital funding programmes currently open for applications.  However, we are currently developing several investment opportunities, aligned to the following themes: environmental sustainability, community use of schools, safety at sports grounds, the outdoors, and Sports facility improvement.  Sport NI will continue to develop these opportunities and will bring forward new programmes as and when appropriate budget becomes available.”  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any representations he has made to the UK government in relation to seasonal worker visas on behalf of the Northern Ireland fishing fleet.
answer_text: I wrote to the Home Secretary on 8 March 2024 to outline my concerns with the Home Offices proposed changes to immigration policy and the potential impact that those changes could have on the agri-food and fishing sectors. In this letter I asked to meet with the Home Secretary and highlighted the difficulties experienced by the fishing sector.  Furthermore, I asked the Home Secretary to consider abandoning the increase of the minimum salary threshold from £26,200 to £38,700. I expressed my disappointment regarding the outcome of the rapid review by the Migration Advisory Committee (MAC) of the Immigration Salary List (ISL), which was published on 23 February 2024. Mainly that the fishing occupations had not been recommended for inclusion on the ISL. I was pleased to hear from the response received that the Home Office did decide to retain fishing occupations on the ISL despite the MAC’s recommendation.In this letter I requested an update on the work of MAC regarding their self-commissioned inquiry of the Seasonal Worker visa route. I have since received confirmation that the MAC will publish their findings on 16th July 2024.My officials have been engaging with the MAC on the seasonal worker visa work and met with representatives on 6 March 2024 where they advocated for the fishing industry to have access to this visa route.Any further engagement on this matter has been deferred by the MAC and Home Office until after the general election has taken place.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on when legislation will be brought forward in relation to a replacement body for the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Since the decision to dissolve and replace the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland was announced, the Department has conducted a number of consultation and focussed engagement exercises with the teaching profession in an attempt to build a consensus on the preferred way forward.Officials are currently preparing an options paper for my consideration and I would hope to be in a position to review this paper before the end of September 2024 which should allow the required legislative changes to be made within the current Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will give consideration to the concerns of the local fishing industry in respect of the Irish Sea Demersal Fisheries Management Plan.
answer_text: Input from the fishing Industry has been sought throughout the development of the Fishery Management Plan process. A group of key stakeholders that includes industry representatives was established at the beginning of the process to co-design the plan. The primary function of these plans is to set out the specific actions and measures to restore fish stocks and maintain them at sustainable levels, but they can be extended to include wider fisheries management issues covering environmental social and economic concerns. 		I am keen to address the issues raised by the fishing industry, either within the context of the Fisheries Management Plan or through other approaches, and I will continue to work with the Industry to address their specific concerns as far as possible.		The plan is currently in draft form and there will be further opportunities to shape the specific actions and measures of the Irish Sea Demersal FMP during the formal public consultation period.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of his Department's budget that has been allocated to tree planting in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: The budget available to my Department’s Forest Service includes a provision of £4.7M for tree planting in 2024-25. This estimates the requirement of £3.5M to fund forestry grant schemes and £1.2M for re-establishment of forests following harvesting operations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: My Department has funded other landowners to plant trees over the past 5 years through the forestry grant schemes. Additionally it has funded tree planting on the public forest estate, through Forest Service managed budgets. This spend is mainly for the regeneration of forests following harvesting operations, with a minor spend element incurred for tree planting on land not previously woodland.The breakdown of spend in each of the last five years is set out as follows:		Year	Funding provided through Forestry Grant Schemes	£000’s	Funding for tree planting from Forest Service budgets	£000’s	Total		£000’s	2023/24	2,826	1,233	4,059	2022/23	3,052	1,453	4,505	2021/22	2,769	1,661	4,430	2020/21	1,779	1,528	3,307	2019/20	1,562	1,227	2,789

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the introduction of cultivated meat to achieve Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 targets.
answer_text: My Department has not made a formal assessment of the introduction of cultivated meat to achieve Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 targets.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any telephone calls, meetings or other communications he has had since taking office with (i) the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs; and (ii) the Minister of State for Food, Farming and Fisheries in relation to compensatory quota for the loss of catch due to the closure of fishing grounds to Northern Ireland fishermen by the Isle of Man government.
answer_text: Within the Memorandum of Understanding between the UK and the Isle of Man, it states that any participant seeking to introduce a new technical and conservation measure will: consult with each of the participants, following good consultation practice; and inform each of the participants in time for clarification to be sought prior to the change being made.The Isle of Man informed both DAERA and the Northern Ireland fishing industry prior to the Department of Environment Food and Agriculture (DEFA) consultation on proposed new spatial management measures in Isle of Man waters, which ran from 6th February to 18th March 2024.Fisheries Administrations may introduce management measures in their own territorial waters for the purposes of stock sustainability or environmental reasons, such as protecting the seabed.I and my officials were in contact with DEFA during and after the consultation.  The feedback from the Department and the Northern Ireland fishing industry were considered by DEFA, and the initial proposed closure area boundaries were subsequently adjusted to lessen the impact on the Northern Ireland fleet.It is likely that the closure areas will displace the Northern Ireland fleet to other part of the Irish Sea, in order that they may still catch their full quota which has been allocated.Fishing quota is allocated by the Defra Secretary of State at the beginning of the year from the Total Allowable Catch (TAC) that has been agreed at the coastal state negotiations between the UK and EU.  There is no surplus pool of fishing quota from which any compensatory quota could be provided.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any telephone calls, meetings or other communications he has had since taking office with (i) the Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs; (ii) the Minister of State for Food, Farming and Fisheries; and (iii) the Minister of Environment, Food and Agriculture in the Isle of Man in relation to the closure of fishing grounds to Northern Ireland fishermen by the Isle of Man government.
answer_text: The Department of Environment Food and Agriculture (DEFA), Isle of Man, launched a public consultation on their proposed spatial management measures in Isle of Man waters from 6th February to 18th March 2024. I wrote to Minister Clare Barber on this and requested a meeting to discuss further. Officials also wrote to the Isle of Man’s Department of Environment Food and Agriculture (DEFA) at this time to outline the impact that these closures would have on Northern Ireland’s fishing industry.The feedback from my department and the Northern Ireland fishing industry was considered by DEFA, and the initial proposed closure area boundaries were subsequently adjusted to lessen the impact on the Northern Ireland fleet. I am aware however that industry representatives remain concerned.I met with the Isle of Man Minister (Hon. Clare Barber MHK) on 17th June 2024 to raise the impact of the closures on the Northern Ireland demersal and pelagic fleet, and to discuss other issues of common interest in the Irish Sea such as science and data collection.It is worth noting that as a Crown Dependency, the management of the Isle of Man territorial waters does not fall under the jurisdiction of Defra or the UK.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps he is taking to assist local councils reduce (i) fly-tipping; and (ii) the impacts of fly-tipping on (a) natural environments; and (b) natural environments in rural areas.
answer_text: I recognise the significant negative impact that fly-tipping can have on the natural environment and the problems it can cause in rural areas. 	My Department works closely with local councils to support effective waste management across Northern Ireland. Responsibilities for illegal waste dumping are split between my Department and Councils. 	NIEA’s Environmental Crime Unit (ECU) focuses its resources on those cases which present the most significant risk to the environment and public health and utilises powers contained within the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 to pursue prosecutions. Such prosecutions are often coupled with confiscation proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 aimed at depriving those convicted of the proceeds generated from their crimes whilst asserting the ‘Polluter Pays Principle’.	On 2 January 2023 my Department commenced provisions of the Waste and Contaminated Land (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 affording Northern Ireland District Councils similar enforcement powers to those given to NIEA under the 1997 Act. 	ECU continues to work in close partnership with local councils to address fly tipping of waste across NI through a working protocol which has been agreed with the majority of councils.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any consideration has been given to an air quality grant to help improve air quality, similar to a scheme in England which promotes air quality and funds local community projects.
answer_text: Under The Environment (Northern Ireland) Order 2002 my Department, through the Local Air Quality Management grant, already financially supports district councils to carry out air quality reviews, assessments and to prepare and implement related action plans.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he will bring forward any regulatory measures in relation to wood burning stoves through the Clean Air Strategy.
answer_text: Regulatory measures for emissions within smoke control areas currently exist under the Clean Air (Northern Ireland) Order 1981 and are enforced by local district councils.  My officials continue to work closely with officials in the Department for the Economy, the department with policy responsibility for energy related products including stoves.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact of wood burning stoves on air quality.
answer_text: I recognise that air quality is a key issue in relation to wood burning in general and I am also acutely aware of the impact that domestic combustion from wood burning stoves has on public health.  The most recent figures published under the National Atmospheric Emissions Inventory has demonstrated that pollutant indicators for burning are increasing in Northern Ireland in part due to the rising trend for the use of wood as a fuel.  I accept that this is a worrying trend, and that is why my officials have worked closely with the Department for Economy on their commitment to ‘phase out certain solid fuels for home heating’ as outlined in their Energy Strategy, the Path to Net Zero (link enclosed): https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/economy/Energy-Strategy-for-Northern-Ireland-path-to-net-zero.pdf
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what prohibitions exist against those convicted of child sexual offences participating in or availing of surrogacy services.
answer_text: Surrogacy services are not commissioned, provided or regulated by my Department or by the Health and Social Care system in Northern Ireland. As surrogacy is a reserved matter, any regulations and prohibitions are the responsibility of the Westminster Government.

answer_text: The budget for the Innovation Lab for the period 2020/21 to 2023/24 is detailed below. question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance when the last evaluation of Northern Ireland Public Sector Innovation Lab was carried out.
answer_text: The Innovation Lab was last reviewed as part of a wider review of the services provided by both it and Business Consultancy Services (BCS) in the Department of Finance. This review concluded in February 2024 and led to the Innovation Lab merging with BCS to become Innovation and Consultancy Services (ICS).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration he has given to reopening a grant scheme for the removal of asbestos from residential properties.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Housing Executive operates a number of grant schemes on behalf of my Department. I have sought the requested information from NIHE’s Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“There is no specific grant-aid scheme available for the removal of asbestos. However, asbestos removal may be considered through Private Sector Grants, which NIHE administers on behalf of the Department of Communities.Home Improvement Grants - Mandatory GrantsShould a Statutory Notice be placed on a tenanted property by a local council because comprised asbestos is noted then grant aid could be considered under the mandatory Repair Grant. Under the Disabled Facility Grant, funding can be provided to remove the asbestos if this is likely to be disturbed to complete the permitted works. An assessment should be carried out by a ‘competent’ surveyor (approved by the British Institute of Occupational Hygiene) and grant aid can be considered for its removal by a specialist contractor who is licensed by the Health and Safety Executive.Discretionary Grants      If asbestos is discovered in a property, and it has been compromised (i.e. damaged or broken), asbestos removal could be considered for discretionary grant aid. An assessment should be carried out by a ‘competent’ surveyor (approved by the British Institute of Occupational Hygiene) and grant aid can be considered for its removal by a specialist contractor who is licensed by the Health and Safety Executive.Discretionary grants include the following: Renovation, Home Repair Assistance and Replacement, they are all subject to funding. Prior to considering an enquiry for discretionary grant aid, the case must meet the exceptional circumstances as listed below:1)	There is a need for a parallel discretionary grant to be processed to facilitate the completion of mandatory Disabled Facilities Grant (DFG) adaptations.  2)	Are the repairs identified presenting any imminent and significant health & safety risk that would impact upon the occupants? 3)	Is there any serious risk to the occupants under structural stability?Currently, due to budget restrictions there is currently no funding for discretionary grants, however, in exceptional circumstances, funding may be considered if deemed necessary for the completion of a DFG (point 1).  Energy Efficiency GrantsUnder the Affordable Warmth Scheme (AWS), funding can be provided to remove the asbestos if this is likely to be disturbed due to the installation of energy efficiency measures or prohibit the actual installation of the measure. An assessment should be carried out by a ‘competent’ surveyor (approved by the British Institute of Occupational Hygiene) and grant aid can be considered for its removal by a specialist contractor who is licensed by the Health and Safety Executive.  For the current year however, funding for new applications to the Affordable Warmth Scheme is restricted.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the key achievements of the Northern Ireland Public Sector Innovation Lab since its inception in 2016.
answer_text: The Innovation Lab was established in 2014 in recognition of the need to better enable innovation in the public sector. It has supported senior leaders to improve public services and policies by creating a safe space to research, co-create ideas, test prototypes and refine concepts with citizens, public servants and stakeholders. It has also promoted innovative methodologies, such as service design and behavioural science, with a view to widening their uptake and application across the service.The Innovation Lab has supported Departments and Public Bodies in many areas, including: health & wellbeing for young people in education; advanced care planning for people in later life; reducing expenditure on agency nursing; developing sustainable social housing; the optimum use of the Dormant Accounts Fund; and collecting additional rates revenues for LPS from rate payers with outstanding debt. It also supported the development of multiple strategy and policies across a vast range of areas, and provided advice and support to the Executive on their response to Covid-19.Examples of the Innovation Lab’s work were cited by the NI Audit Office as case studies in their 2023 Good Practice Guide on Innovation & Risk Management.The services provided by the Innovation Lab now form part of Innovation & Consultancy Services (ICS) within the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the steps she is taking to prevent financial cybercrime.
answer_text: Financial gain is one of the key motivators of cybercrime, primarily achieved by extorting money from victims directly or capitalising on the sale of stolen data.  Both individuals and companies can fall victim to cyber criminals.Policy and legislation to combat economic crime is often aligned across the UK jurisdictions.  Officials in my Department engage on a regular basis with their counterparts on economic crime, legislation to recover the proceeds of crime and the UK fraud strategy.  Most recently I have ensured that new provisions under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002, to enable the investigation and recovery of proceeds of crime facilitated by cryptoassets, were extended to Northern Ireland.   On work to prevent scams, my Department is also represented on the ScamwiseNI Partnership, in which the PSNI is a lead partner and which shares messaging with the public in Northern Ireland to raise awareness and highlight emerging trends, most commonly of cyber or internet-enabled crimes leading to a financial loss.  Based on reports of scams and UK-wide trends, this analysis enables preventative media campaigns to be conducted.  My Department is also represented on the Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF).  Information and key advice on preventing and reporting cyber crimes is set out in the OCTF Annual Report available at:  OCTF Annual Report and Threat Assessment 22-23 | Department of Justice (justice-ni.gov.uk).The Police Service of Northern Ireland commits specialist resources in this area and investigates under these strands: Cybercrime attacks against businesses and individuals for financial gain Financial crimes which are ‘Cyber or Internet Enabled’ Frauds and scams which are ‘Cyber or Internet Enabled’ Cyber sextortion for financial gain For further information on the work of the PSNI, you may wish to contact the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his Department is taking to address staff shortages in the Ards and North Down section office of DfI Roads.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This has had a significant impact on my Department’s ability to deliver essential services, and to fill vacant posts. Most posts in the Ards and North Down Section Office are funded from the Resource budget allocated to my Department and this is far from sufficient to meet the needs. I can however assure you that my Department is continuing to work to maximise the Resource budget available in order to fill affordable vacancies as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how much of the budget allocated to DfI Roads Southern Division during the 2024-25 financial year has been distributed to each local council area.
answer_text: My Department launched a Budget 2024-25 Equality Impact Assessment consultation on 10th June 24 and I will consider responses received within the first 4 weeks of consultation to help inform my final opening budget allocations. Once finalised, details of schemes will be included in the DfI Roads reports to be presented to the various Councils prior to general release.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the process through which section offices are allocated their share of funding for roads maintenance projects in DfI Roads Southern Division.
answer_text: Having decided on indicative budget allocations (subject to a consultation on the equality impacts) my officials can now consider the detailed programmes of work across the Department’s areas of responsibility, including plans for infrastructure projects across the DfI Section Offices. In distributing the resources available for road maintenance, allocations are made to the four DfI Roads Divisions based on need, using a range of weighted indicators tailored to each maintenance activity (i.e. resurfacing, patching, gully emptying, grass cutting etc). Divisions use these indicators when apportioning across Section Office areas to ensure, as far as possible, an equitable distribution of funds across the whole of the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions he has had with the Minister of Justice in relation to measures to address the backlog of cases and independent examinations of local development plans at the Planning Appeals Commission.
answer_text: I will be, engaging on this matter with the Minister of Justice, given Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) is responsible for providing resources and services to the Commission and is accountable for the use of these resources and associated expenditure. I am also exploring whether funding is available to allow the Department to consider exercising its power to appoint persons to undertake certain functions normally carried out by the Commission.
