question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of employers his Department has identified as being non-compliant with minimum wage legislation in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This is a reserved matter and does not fall under the remit of my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he plans to fully commence (i) Article 4(1); and (ii) Article 4(3) of the Tourism (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: Article 4(1) and Article 4(3) of The Tourism (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 relates to the transfer of responsibility for tourism accommodation capital support/financial assistance from Invest NI to Tourism NI. Over the years, there have been different assessments as to which body should provide this support, however, my Department has accepted Recommendation 6 of the Lyons Report which has recommended that Tourism NI should offer this support going forward.My officials have established a Working Group with colleagues in Tourism NI and Invest NI and are working to implement this Lyons Report recommendation. This work will involve undertaking an independent assessment of the local visitor accommodation sector, taking into account recent changes and trends, as well as the scale, location and mix of visitor accommodation which can be expected to be delivered by the private sector in the next decade. The findings of the assessment will be used to inform my Department’s policy and the scope of any schemes to be put in place by Tourism NI.Once this work has been completed, the Articles referred to above will be fully commenced to facilitate this transfer of functions. The target date for completion is October 2025. In the interim, Invest NI will continue to process applications for support from tourism businesses in the same way.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on investment plans for the South Eastern Regional College.
answer_text: In terms of capital investment plans, I am informed by South Eastern Regional College that it has identified one major capital project at its Lisburn campus for consideration within its ten year Estate Strategy.I expect the College to bring forward a formal proposal in due course, which will then be assessed by my officials in terms of need and in line with the availability of capital funding.My Department continues to provide annual capital investment to the College for minor works, which this year will be used to address curriculum equipment needs and replace Building Management Systems at the Bangor and Newtownards campuses to improve sustainability at both sites.           Annual funding is also provided by my Department to meet College operational costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the opportunities there are available to support plans by Ards and North Down Borough Council to celebrate the centenary of the Ards TT races in 2028.
answer_text: I recognise the heritage and importance of the Ards TT Races to the area and understand that Ards & North Down Borough Council are working on plans around centenary celebrations of the event for 2028.Tourism NI is responsible for the administration of tourism events funding within my Department. Events funding from Tourism NI operates on an annual open call basis, with agreed published criteria, and is dependent on budget availability. Tourism NI would be happy to discuss where they might be able to support from a tourism perspective.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of prosecutions that have been taken against employers who fail to engage with employees on redundancies in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Department has not proceeded with a prosecution in this area in the last five years.As per legislation, individual employees who have been made redundant and feel that their employer did not consult, can take a claim for unfair dismissal at tribunal.I would encourage individuals who have concerns about compliance with the redundancy consultation practices in their workplace, to consider contacting the Labour Relations Agency’s Workplace Information Service on 03300 555 300, which can provide information on employment rights matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether there are telecare devices being used that will no longer be fully operational after the Public Switched Telephone Network is switched off; and (ii) what steps his Department is taking to ensure the safety of telecare users during the switch to digital lines.
answer_text: Telecoms is a reserved matter and guidance on the transition has been published by UK Government at https://www.gov.uk/guidance/uk-transition-from-analogue-to-digital-landlines. OFCOM, the regulator for this area has also published information on the changeover at Upgrading landlines to digital technology – what you need to know - OfcomTelecommunications companies are replacing the technology they use to provide fixed telephone networks (landlines) with this work currently scheduled to complete by January 2027. It is their responsibility to ensure that those who depend on a landline are provided with a resilient solution. Currently 2,494 citizens receive telecare services through HSC contracts.  To date 1,429 (57%) have transitioned to a digital device and my officials have been working with the telecare service provider to complete this work by January 2027.My Chief Digital Information Officer is aware of the issues in this area and regular contact is maintained with colleagues in the other jurisdictions and with the Technology Enabled Care Services Association, to remain abreast of developments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what measures are in place to monitor trees planted by his Department (i) after a year; and (ii) after five years.
answer_text: Forest Service monitors tree health and survival rates over the first year post planting, to confirm that the Forestry Standards have been achieved.  The information from the field surveys is used to determine any follow up actions that may be required, including where necessary the replacement of failed plants. Further surveys are carried out in the following year to ensure that trees remain healthy and free from competing vegetation and other pests.  These inspections continue until the trees are recorded as being successfully established, which for the majority of sites is achieved within the first 5 years. All woodlands recently planted by Forest Service are subject to the monitoring requirements of the UK Woodland Assurance Standard (UKWAS) on annual basis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the consultation that has taken place with key stakeholders in relation to the new defective premises legislation.
answer_text: In my Joint Statement with Ministers Muir and Archibald of 17th March I undertook to introduce legislation at the earliest possible opportunity to address the disparity that existed in this area between Northern Ireland and England & Wales. Following this, I met with a number of parties, including the Departmental Solicitor and Attorney General and the Executive Committee. Subsequently the Assembly passed the motion for the use of accelerated passage on the 3rd of June.   Given the Executive desire to urgently bring forward this legislation at the earliest opportunity consultation was limited.However, my officials have engaged with the following groups:The Assembly Committee for CommunitiesThe Office for Product Safety and StandardsThe Department for Levelling Up, Housing and CommunitiesWelsh AssemblyScottish ExecutiveThe Building Safety Regulator (England)Royal Society of Ulster ArchitectsFinance UK; andThe Office of Legislative CounselAt earlier engagement sessions with residents, industry representatives, and building safety practitioners, the attendees were advised that the Department’s Residential Building Safety Division was working to ensure that Northern Ireland citizens were afforded at least the same level of protection as the other UK jurisdictions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether the Forests for our Future pledge to plant 18 million trees takes into account survival rates; and (ii) whether he will incorporate replacement planting to maintain the 18 million figure.
answer_text: My Department’s Forests for Our Future programme is to establish 9,000 hectares of new woodland, between 2020 and 2030, and the number of trees required to achieve the programme is estimated to be c.18 million trees. The UK Forestry Standard sets out the requirements against which individual project proposals are assessed and approved for planting, including the appropriate stocking densities for particular woodland types. Forestry grant schemes support the replacement of failed trees, in the first few years after planting, at a level considered acceptable due to natural losses. My Department inspects grant aided woodlands and verifies that they meet good forestry practice guidelines by year 5, in line with UK Forestry Standard requirements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures are in place to monitor trees planted by his Department (i) after a year; and (ii) after five years.
answer_text: My Department follows the Design Manual for Roads & Bridges to specify post-planting maintenance. For major works schemes, all trees shall be maintained, or defective trees replaced by the Contractor, for a period of normally 156 weeks or 3 full growing seasons after planting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the measures he is taking to ensure innocent parties will not be held liable retrospectively under new defective premises legislation.
answer_text: It is for the courts to determine liability, not for me.  The existing Defective Premises legislation currently has a retrospective period of 6 years, in which responsible parties can be held liable.  This Bill does not change that premise but rather extends that retrospective responsibility.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to fully commence (i) Article (a) 6; (b) 15 to 17; (c) 44; (d) 74 and 75; (e) 86 to 88; (f) 90; (g) 126 to 129;  (h) 132 to 134; (i) 159 and 160; (j) 167; (k) 255; (l) 263; (ii) Schedule 1; and (iii) Schedule 4 of the Water and Sewerage Services (Northern Ireland) Order 2006.
answer_text: I have no plans to commence these Articles or Schedules at present. The Articles and Schedules of the 2006 Order which are not currently enacted are administrative in nature; they relate mainly to my Department's role in overseeing NI Water, as the current undertaker for the provision of water and sewerage services, or any future replacement undertaker. As such, these Articles and Schedules do not need to be enacted at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in percentage terms, to detail (i) how much planning fees have increased since the transfer of planning functions to local councils; and (ii) how much inflation has increased, based on the Consumer Price Index, during that same period.
answer_text: Since the transfer of planning functions to local councils in April 2015, planning fees have increased by approximately 19%.The Consumer Price Index during the same period has increased by 33.6%.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what plans she has to commence sections (i) 55; (ii) 71 to 79; (iii) 80; (iv) 84 to 85; (v) 87 to 89; (vi) 92; and (vii) 95 of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: An update on plans for the commencement of the above provisions of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 is provided in the attached table.Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2011SectionOverview of provisionsFuture commencement plansSection 55:A provision to amend the Sexual Offences Act 2003 to ensure that a district judge (magistrates’ courts) will hear applications relating to closure orders Commencement of these provisions will be re-visited when time and resources permitSections 71 -79:This section relates to powers for PSNI to issue Conditional CautionsThe Out of Courts Disposals workstream is one of the five workstreams included in the Speeding Up Justice Programme. This workstream is currently exploring the potential to expand existing arrangements for dealing with cases outside of the formal court system. This includes powers that have been legislated for but not yet commenced such as Conditional Cautions. Considerable work was undertaken in preparation for these powers shortly after the Justice Act (NI) 2011 was enacted. However, financial restrictions prevented progression to implementation.Whilst the Department could commence this legislation making these provisions available should PSNI ever be in a position to use them, it was felt more appropriate and transparent to only commence at the point when required. Sections 80 & 84These sections relate to Eligibility for criminal legal aid; and Financial eligibility for grant of right to representationThere are no imminent plans to commence this section. The ongoing review of criminal legal aid may lead to its commencement.Section 85Removal of restrictions under Litigation Funding Agreements (LFA)Conditional Fee Agreements and Litigation Funding Agreements are both among the issues being examined through the current foundational review of civil legal services.SectionOverview of provisionsFuture commencement plansSections 88-90Provisions to extend solicitors’ rights of audience in the High Court and Court of Appeal.  The Department is responsible for drafting consequential amendments which reflect the role of an authorised solicitor, but there has been limited capacity to take this forward given other competing priorities. There are also significant policy and operational implications for legal aid. Current resourcing for legal aid reform is currently fully deployed and this issue may be considered in the next Assembly mandate depending on Departmental priorities.Section 92Bail: Repeal applicationsProvisions likely to be revisited as part of potential larger review of bail when time and resources permit.Section 95Provisions to enable court rules to be made on disclosure of information relating to family proceedings concerning children without the need for a court order authorising the disclosureFollowing enactment, the Department issued a discussion document seeking views on the circumstances in which it should be possible to share information about family proceedings concerning child

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action he will take during this term to further progress the recommendations contained in the Review of the Use of Restraint and Seclusion in Educational Settings in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The report on the review of the use of restraint and seclusion in educational settings set out six recommendations for change. Recommendation 1 relates to the repeal of Article 4 1 (c) of the 1998 Education Order. I am considering this matter further before determining the appropriate next steps. Recommendations 2, 3 and 5 relate to the publication of statutory guidance on the use of restrictive and supportive practices in educational settings. A public consultation on draft guidance ran from 5th September to 10th November 2023. My department has taken account of the views expressed in the consultation responses and prepared an analysis report which I am currently considering. Subject to my approval, the consultation report will be published on the departmental website in due course. I will consider the next steps to be taken thereafter.On recommendation 4, officials continue to work with health colleagues to develop regional guidance on pupils’ medication and healthcare needs including the use of equipment in educational settings. It is anticipated that guidance in relation to medication needs will be ready for publication early in the Autumn term.    Recommendation 6, which relates to the department commissioning a periodic review and publishing a report on the use of restrictive and supportive practices in educational settings can only be progressed once the recording and reporting arrangements are finalised.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what plans are in place to prevent duplication between the Strategic Framework Ending Violence Against Women and Girls and other strategies such as the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy.
answer_text: The Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework is designed to co-ordinate efforts across our government and society. Our aim is to support people in working better together to end violence against women and girls.Our officials collaborated closely with officials in other Departments, including those leading on the Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy, as part of the Framework co-design process. This was to ensure actions were aligned and to avoid any potential for duplication.				Cross departmental collaboration is ongoing, and governance structures are being designed to maximise the effectiveness of public resources.Work is ongoing to develop an action plan to support the delivery of the Strategic Framework, considering wider financial implications across departments. We are actively engaging on this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of looked after children currently cared for by kinship foster carers who are in the process of being assessed, but have not yet been approved.
answer_text: Information on number of children in care in different placement settings is collected biannually through the Delegated Statutory Functions Return (DSF) and published annually in the Official Statistics publication “Children’s Social Care Statistics in Northern Ireland”. At 30 September 2023, 485 children and young people were in a kinship care placement that was in the process of being assessed, but not yet approved. These are the latest figure available (source: DSF return 10.3.3 at 30th September 2023, SPPG). Please note that a kinship carer/family may care for more than one child. Hence, the above figure does not reflect the number of households in the process of being assessed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of looked after children currently cared for by independent fostering providers.
answer_text: Information on number of children in different placement settings is collected biannually through the Delegated Statutory Functions (DSF) Return and published annually in the Official Statistics publication “Children’s Social Care Statistics in Northern Ireland”. At 30 September 2023, 345 children in care were in foster care placements provided by independent fostering providers (source: DSF return 10.3.3 at 30th September 2023, SPPG).  This is the latest figure available. Figures for 31 March 2024 are currently being collected and validated and will be published in October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the level of social work vacancies across Health and Social Care Trusts, what mitigations are in place to ensure foster carers and kinship foster carers can access social work support for children in their care when crises arise which could result in a child’s fostering arrangement becoming unsustainable.
answer_text: Foster carers and kinship foster carers receive support from a social worker allocated to them from their local Trust fostering team. Their role is to advise and support foster carers when a looked after child is placed in their care. Foster carers and kinship foster carers also have access to the regional emergency social work service should a crisis arise out of hours. There is also a dedicated helpline and access to 24 hr support through membership of Fostering Network, which the Health and Social Care Trusts provide for all foster and foster kinship carers.In addition, each Looked After Child has an allocated social worker responsible for all aspects of the child’s needs and care planning and who will work closely with all other professionals involved in meeting the best interests of the child in care. During times of crisis, where placement breakdown may occur, the fostering and looked after child team will risk assess each situation in order to put additional supports in place to minimise any placement becoming unsustainable. Supports would include access to respite, additional visiting to the child and foster carers, and referral to specialist services such as the Therapeutic Looked After Child Teams.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the amount spent by (a) her Department; and (b) her Department’s arm’s-length bodies on diversity training in each of the last five years; and (ii) the anticipated spend by (a) her Department; and (b) her Department’s arm’s-length bodies on diversity training in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: As Finance Minister, I have responsibility for the Civil Service - its people, policies and terms and conditions, which includes delivery of learning and development interventions to enable an inclusive Civil Service workforce culture.A breakdown of the spend incurred by my Department on diversity related training in each of the last five years is set out below. This spend includes development costs for learning interventions developed and delivered by my Department for all Civil Service staff:YearExpenditure2020/21Expenditure 2021/22Expenditure 2022/23Expenditure 2023/24Expenditure 2024/25Total£18,370£2,100£33,325£24,491.50£10,246A breakdown of the spend incurred by my Department’s arm’s-length bodies on diversity related training in each of the last five years is set out below.YearExpenditure2020/21Expenditure 2021/22Expenditure 2022/23Expenditure 2023/24Expenditure 2024/25Total£0£0£0£5,191.51£1,113At this stage, anticipated spend by my Department on diversity training for the 2024/25 financial year is £1,050. However, further spend during the 2024/25 year is expected.None of my Department’s arm’s length bodies have anticipated costs related to diversity training for the 2024/25 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the amount spent by (a) her Department; and (b) her Department’s arm’s-length bodies on diversity training in each of the last five years; and (ii) the anticipated spend by (a) her Department; and (b) her Department’s arm’s-length bodies on diversity training in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Staff within the Department of Justice can access e-learning courses on diversity through Learning, Information, Knowledge and Skills (LInKS), the Northern Ireland Civil Service wide system for learning and development. It is not possible to provide a cost specifically for those courses.Diversity training for Northern Ireland Prison Service staff is delivered by tutors at the Prison Service College (PSC) and therefore any associated costs are included within the operational costs of the PSC. It is therefore not possible to provide a cost specifically for diversity training.There has been no further spend by my Department on diversity training in each of the last five years, and there is no anticipated spend on diversity training in the 2024-25 financial year, beyond what is available as outlined above.My Department does not hold information in relation to spend or anticipated spend on diversity training by the following Non-Departmental Public Bodies of my Department: Police Service NI.Northern Ireland Policing Board.Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust.Office of the Police Ombudsman for Northern Ireland.RUC George Cross Foundation.Probation Board for Northern Ireland.Northern Ireland Police Fund.Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland.You may, therefore, wish to contact them directly to request the information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of foster carers that ceased to foster from April 2023 to March 2024.
answer_text: From April 2023 – March 2024 - 398 foster carers ceased to foster in Northern Ireland. Of this number, 92 carers ceased to foster as they secured Adoption or Residence Orders for the child/ren in their care. Additionally, 26 carers ceased their fostering registration in order to become registered as GEM carers (Going the Extra Mile) for the young people in their care who had turned 18 years old. Of the remaining 280, 166 carers were de-registered as they no longer wished to foster, and 85 carers retired from fostering. Finally, 29 foster carers were de-registered following concerns being raised about the care they were providing to Looked After Children. Additionally, the table below details the number of newly approved foster carers for the 2023-24 financial year, per Health and Social Care Trust. TrustFoster Carers ApprovedBelfast Health and Social Care Trust99Northern Health and Social Care Trust75South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust48Southern Health and Social Care Trust92Western Health and Social Care Trust137

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when he intends to announce the annual uplift of allowances for foster carers; (ii) the expected level of uplift; and (iii) when foster carers can expect to receive this.
answer_text: The Department reviews the allowances payable to foster carers on an annual basis to determine an annual uplift to apply to these allowances. The annual review considers rates of inflation, the rates of allowances payable in other UK jurisdictions, and what is affordable within the financial envelope available to the Department. A decision on the allowance rates payable for 2024-25 is currently being finalised and foster carers will be notified of the outcome as soon as possible. Payment of any uplift in 2024/25 will be backdated to April 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has any plans to enhance Northern Ireland’s Ulster-Scots links with the United States of America.
To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the policy relating to prisoners who identify as transgender.
answer_text: The Prison Service has a duty of care to assess the specific and individual needs of all prisoners irrespective of gender to ensure they are safe and secure and treated with dignity and respect. Prison Service guidance on the care and management of transgender prisoners was developed in consultation with the Equality Commission and the South Eastern Health & Social Care Trust.If a person who is transgender is committed to custody by the Courts, NIPS will consider their specific needs on a case-by-case basis.Specific arrangements can be made in respect of where the person is accommodated and how they access services in prison to ensure that they can fully engage in the regime. The Trust is responsible for the delivery of healthcare services for people in prison and will consider any specific healthcare needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what social work support is in place to provide urgent support to foster carers where a foster child has been accused of harmful sexual behaviour towards another child in an educational setting.
answer_text: Foster carers in Northern Ireland are provided with a dedicated supervising social worker who is responsible for providing the foster carer with the support they require to nurture the children and young people in their care. Foster carers also have access to 24 hour social work support via the Regional Emergency Social Work Service and can access peer support via the fostering support groups which are held in many HSC Trust areas.  HSC Trusts can provide foster carers practical support through the use of short breaks when needed and when this is in keeping with the child’s needs. Additionally, each Looked After Child has an allocated social worker responsible for all aspects of the child’s needs and care planning and who will work closely with all other professionals involved in meeting the best interests of the child in care. Each HSC Trust also has social work staff trained specifically in working with  children and young people who have been accused of harmful sexual behaviour. This specialist social work support can be utilised for all children and young people, including those in foster care and can offer individualised advice and guidance to foster carers and all professionals involved in the care of the child, to include those within an educational setting.  This specialist social worker in consultation with the foster carer, the supervising social worker and the child’s social worker will develop a Safety Plan for the young person both within their home and the educational setting to create a safe environment. The specialist social worker will work alongside education professionals and the foster carers to ensure that any additional measures required are proportionate, realistic and regularly reviewed.Where there is concern that the risks associated with the harmful sexual behaviour has increased in severity or complexity, additional support can be accessed via the Education Authority’s Child Protection Support Service (CPSS). The CPSS Designated Officers for Child Protection have been trained in using the AIM 3 Education Guidance which provides guidelines for identifying and managing harmful sexual behaviour, as well as providing guidance on how to effectively respond to carers of the children involved. Some children who engage in harmful sexual behaviour may have significant emotional and behavioural difficulties and may have experienced some form of abuse themselves in the past. This could be of particular relevance for those children who are in foster care. Foster carers who are faced with this challenge may wish to access specific training to assist them in understanding the complexities that can be associated with harmful sexual behaviour in children as an additional support to bolster their ability to meet the needs of the child in their care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the funding his Department has made available to third sector agencies to provide independent support to foster carers who are unable to access timely support from their fostering agencies, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that all foster carers receive appropriate and timely support from fostering services within HSC Trusts and independent fostering agencies. I recognise that the current workforce challenges in Trusts have made this more difficult at this time and we are working to try to address those challenges through ongoing reform programmes. In addition, in each of the last five years, funding was provided to The Fostering Network through my Department’s core grant scheme.  This funding has helped The Fostering Network to provide a range of support to foster parents which enables them to better care for those in foster placements and to promote stability of those placements.  The amounts provided over the past five years are:YearAmount2019/20£43,489.502020/21£43,489.502021/22£43,489.502022/23£43,489.502023/24£21,744.78 My Department intends to bring forward new fostering regulations in the near future. Those regulations will require all fostering service providers to provide foster parents with a written ‘foster care agreement’ which specifies a range of relevant information including the support and training which they will receive.  It is anticipated that the regulations will also require fostering service providers to provide foster parents with learning and development opportunities, advice, information and support (including on independent advocacy services) to allow them to appropriately care for children in a foster placement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his officials have completed their consideration of the Cass Review; and (ii) what actions his Department will take following the recommendations contained in the Cass Review final report.
answer_text: My officials will attend a workshop on Friday 14 June with the Gender Identity Pathway Review Group, which includes clinicians and representatives from the Belfast HSC Trust’s Knowing Our Identity Service for under 18s, to consider and discuss any implications of the Cass Review recommendations. In addition, my officials will attend a further workshop on Monday 17 June with the Gender Identity Service Liaison Panel, which includes clinicians and transgender representative organisations to discuss the Review’s recommendations and any implications for the service provided in Northern Ireland.   My Department will carefully consider the outcomes of the workshops as outlined above and any implications for gender identity services provided in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will be introducing a contaminated land regime, as set out in Part 3 of the Waste Management and Contaminated Land Order (Northern Ireland) 1997.
answer_text: No decision has been taken on the introduction of a contaminated land regime in Northern Ireland.  In the absence of a specific contaminated land regime, many contamination issues are dealt with on an individual site basis through existing planning and environmental protection controls.   Most land remediation occurs as part of planning consent where the cost is met by landowner or developer.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps his Department will take to assist NI Water in addressing sewerage capacity issues.
answer_text: My Department’s role is to set policy and provide funding to NI Water in the form of a subsidy, in lieu of domestic water charges. In terms of funding for 2024-25, I have provided NI Water with a total indicative budget allocation of almost £0.5 billion, representing just under 40% of the total non-ring-fenced budget for my Department. This reflects the central importance of water and sewerage services to our society.		In addition, my Department encourages developers to use Sustainable Drainage Systems (SuDS), to help remove surface water from the sewer network and free up capacity for wastewater.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what plans she has to commence sections (i) 9 to 16; (ii) 17 to 27; (iii) 42 to 43; (iv) 50; (v) 52; (vi) 82; (vii) 83 to 87; and (viii) 91 of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: My Department’s plans in relation to sections (i) 9 to 16; (ii) 17 to 27; (iii) 42 to 43; (iv) 50; (v) 52; (vi) 82; (vii) 83 to 87; and (viii) 91 of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 are outlined below.Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2015SectionsOverview of provisionsFuture commencement plansSections 9 – 16Provisions relating to committal reform and specifically the direct committal of specified cases to the Crown Court.Sections 9-16, 17-27 and 91 of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2015 are presently being taken forward as part of a wider programme of work aimed at Speeding up the Justice system.  The Speeding up Justice programme, which is overseen by the Criminal Justice Board, has five workstreams including Committal Reform, Early Engagement, Out of Courts Disposals, Remit of the Magistrates’ courts and Digital.Sections 9-16 relate to the direct committal of specified cases to the Crown Court which is being taken forward as part of the Committal Reform workstream. The Department is presently working with criminal justice partners regarding the second phase of committal reform (‘direct committal’), which aims to remove the traditional committal process entirely for the most serious offences, instead directly transferring them to the Crown Court at the first court appearance in the magistrates’ court.  Work continues to prepare a business case and progress the technical and operational changes required to support direct committal. Sections 17 – 27:This section relates to powers for PPS to issue Prosecutorial Fines.The powers allow a prosecutor to issue a notice offering an alleged offender over age 18 a prosecutorial fine for one or more summary offence(s) and specifies the information which the notice must contain.The potential role of these provisions is presently being explored as part of the Out of Courts Disposals workstream of the Speeding up Justice work programme, which aims to reduce demand on courts by considering options for dealing with cases more proportionately outside of the traditional court process, where appropriate.  Sections 42 – 43Provisions to streamline the arrangements for the disclosure of criminal record checks, including allowing for on-line updating in certain circumstances (Section 42) ; and provisions to allow those who are self-employed to apply for an enhanced certificate (Section 43)Section 42 has not yet been commenced as the Disclosure and Barring Service (DBS) in England & Wales do not yet have the technology to accept applications from Northern Ireland.  DBS is currently reviewing their portable disclosure solution as they seek to modernise the product to better meet business and customer needs.  AccessNI continues to engage with DBS with a view to joining a new modernised solution.   Section 43 - self-employed people are unable to apply directly to the DBS for a standard or enhanced criminal record check as this service is only available for those positions and types of work included in the Rehab

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his Department has taken to (i) improve recycling infrastructure; and (ii) reduce plastic waste exports.
answer_text: My Department’s £23 million Household Waste Recycling Collaborative Change Programme (HWRCCP) financially supports local councils to transform kerbside recycling and Household Recycling Centre infrastructure and services. Since the Programme’s launch in June 2019, funding, totalling £4.875M, has been allocated to 6 councils for 9 projects. Eligible projects will be supported with grants of up to 50% for eligible costs from DAERA and I would urge all Councils to submit applications to further enhance the local circular economy.		Waste export policy including the export of plastic waste is a matter reserved to the UK Government. However, I am committed to taking actions which will facilitate our transition to a Circular Economy here. I recently launched a consultation, Rethinking Our Resources, which includes measures designed to increase the quality and the quantity of what we currently recycle, and I encourage everyone to respond to these proposals. 	The introduction of packaging Extended Producer Responsibility in 2025 means packaging producers will pay higher fees for packaging which is less recyclable. This will reduce the amount of complex, composite packaging containing plastic being sold. In April 2022, the UK Government introduced a new tax on all packaging where plastic is the main component. Packaging with less than 30% recycled plastic content is subject to a tax of just under £218 per tonne. Together, these interventions will increase the amount of plastic and other waste we can recycle domestically in closed loop processes so we can reduce our dependency on waste exports including the export of plastic waste.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for his assessment of the impact continuing industrial action is having on children, especially those attending special needs schools; and (ii) what action is being taken to minimise the impact on those attending special needs schools.
answer_text: I am pleased that through positive and constructive engagement the strike action planned for this week has been suspended.  This is a very positive development that has allowed our schools to be open as normal for all children and young people including those with special educational needs (SEN).  Attendance at school is vital to ensure continuing education and ongoing support for all our children, young people and their families. I will continue to work with Trade Unions and my Executive colleagues to find a permanent resolution to the ongoing pay and grading dispute.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what action is being taken to ensure vulnerable children in care are given the best start in life.
answer_text: A joint Department of Health and Department of Education Strategy for Looked After Children, A Life Deserved, was published in February 2021. The Strategy contains 62 commitments to action aimed at improving outcomes for care-experienced children and young people, as well as preventing children entering the care system in the first place. To date, good progress has been made against a number of commitments in the Strategy, including: the phased implementation of a framework for integrated therapeutic care for all looked after children in Northern Ireland; the introduction of peripatetic support teams to work alongside children’s homes; the appointment of a looked after children’s champion in the Education Authority; and expansion of supported living arrangements for young people preparing to leave or who have left care. Further work planned includes a review of care planning and the introduction of new fostering Regulations.  In addition, the Department introduced new primary legislation, the Adoption and Children Act (NI) 2022, which will give legislative effect to a number of the commitments to action under the Strategy, including placing care planning, the Going the Extra Mile Scheme and advocacy for looked after children on a statutory basis. Finally, my Department has established a programme of reform aimed at addressing significant pressures and challenges within children’s social care. The aim of the programme is to deliver much needed change to, among other things, ensure that the most vulnerable children, are given the best possible start in life.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) whether her Department has a Stonewall Diversity Champion; and (ii) which of her Department’s arm’s length bodies have a Stonewall Diversity Champion.
answer_text: My Department has a diversity champion who is a member of the wider Civil Service’s Diversity Champions Network – there is no Stonewall Diversity Champion.  The Diversity Champions Network comprises senior leaders from the nine ministerial departments and the Public Prosecution Service, along with four thematic diversity champions for Gender, Race & Ethnicity, LGBTQ+ and Disability. As part of the Civil Service’s commitment to creating an inclusive workforce culture, the Civil Service has been a member of the Stonewall Diversity Champions Programme since July 2018. None of my Department’s arm’s length bodies have a Stonewall Diversity Champion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps his Department is taking to support (i) the digital technology sector; and (ii) creative industries in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: My Department is working collaboratively with Invest NI on the development of a Sub-Regional Economic Action Plan for the North of Ireland. With regards to the Strangford Parliamentary Constituency, this has included engagement with Ards and North Down Borough Council, Newry, Mourne and Down District Council, as well as local Enterprise Agencies.Within the Strangford constituency, Invest NI has made 32 offers of financial assistance to digital technology and creative industries businesses within the 5-year period from 2018-19 to 2022-23 – with total assistance offered of £921,928 leveraging total planned investment of £2,313,188.As part of a series of events throughout the region, Invest NI held a Navigating Growth information session on 12 June in Newtownards to raise awareness of Invest NI’s products and services for business owners and entrepreneurs who are not currently Invest NI clients. Invest NI’s latest Ambition to Grow programme call is seeking innovative and ambitious businesses to apply for up to £45,000 in funding. Targeted at SMEs, the Ambition to Grow programme seeks to create good jobs and increase sales of innovative products or services outside of the North of Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) whether his Department has a Stonewall Diversity Champion; and (ii) which of his Department’s arm’s length bodies have a Stonewall Diversity Champion.
answer_text: My Department does not have a Stonewall Diversity Champion.North West Regional College (NWRC) is the only college working with Stonewall to achieve diversity champions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the steps his Department is taking to assist the establishment of new business ventures in the Strangford constituency.
answer_text: Creating a vibrant ecosystem of new business ventures across the North is one of my top priorities and aligns with my objective of promoting a regionally balanced economy.  My Department provides a suite of interventions to support entrepreneurs across the North who are in the early stages of business development. Support available through Invest NI and InterTradeIreland includes financial assistance, advice and guidance, mentoring, signposting to wider assistance and support for business plan development. Invest NI also provides funding to several partner organisations that deliver a range of debt and equity funds for high-potential start-ups. Following Local Government reform in 2015, responsibility for local business start-ups transferred to the 11 Councils.  In November 2023, the Councils launched Go Succeed, a service available across the North which offers advice and support to entrepreneurs at all stages of business development. 	   Invest NI provides a comprehensive guide to business support on www.nibusinessinfo.co.uk. Two sections of the website are especially useful for start-up businesses - My New Business and Starting a Business.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) whether her Department has a Stonewall Diversity Champion; and (ii) which of her Department’s arm’s length bodies have a Stonewall Diversity Champion.
answer_text: My Department does not have a Stonewall Diversity Champion.  The Northern Ireland Civil Service is a member of the Stonewall Diversity Champions Programme which is co-ordinated by the Department of Finance.  None of my Departments arm’s length bodies have a Stonewall Diversity Champion.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) what estimate she has made of the number of people who are unable to afford private legal fees and are ineligible for legal aid; and (ii) to detail the measures she will take to address this issue.
answer_text: The Department of Justice is currently undertaking a foundational review of civil legal aid provision.  This review will look, among other issues, at the way in which decisions are made about the granting of legal aid, the impact that this has on people with access to justice needs, and the extent to which current provision is meeting the needs of citizens.  In the autumn, this will produce an analysis of available options for reform that have the potential to enhance access to justice, including through reform of financial eligibility rules.A fundamental review of criminal legal aid is expected to produce a report by 30 June 2024 that will make recommendations for reform of criminal legal aid, focusing primarily on remuneration structures, with the primary aim of driving efficiency in the criminal justice system.A call for evidence has been issued to help inform the civil review.  The Department has asked for responses by 18 June 2024.  Further information is available on the Department's website at: Review of Civil Legal Aid – Call for Evidence | Department of Justice (justice-ni.gov.uk).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps he will take to streamline the eviction process for behaviour that adversely impacts neighbouring properties in social housing settings.
answer_text: My Department recently published for consultation proposals to amend existing anti-social behaviour legislation, including a new “absolute” ground for possession of secure tenancies in social housing. This would enable courts to make an order for possession where certain tests are met, which provide clear evidence that anti-social behaviour has already been proven to the satisfaction of another court.This proposal is intended to ensure that possession powers are more effective and timely.Officials are reviewing the consultation responses, and I will consider the recommendations and next steps in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider legislation to give local planning authorities the power to remove estate agent signage which is in breach of planning regulations, similar to the powers granted to DfI Roads.
answer_text: While there is currently no corresponding provision within The Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (the 2011 Act) or The Planning (Control of Advertisements) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 (the Advertisement Regulations) similar to the Roads (NI) Order 1993, there are other statutory regimes providing legislative provisions to enable councils to remove estate agent signage.Section 175 of The Planning Act (Northern Ireland) provides councils with powers to deal with the enforcement of advertisement control.  This allows those landowners, occupiers and businesses contravening advertising regulations to be prosecuted, and if convicted, they may be liable to a fine not exceeding Level 4 on the standard scale (currently £2,500) and potentially a daily fine for ongoing offences.  Councils can also continue to exercise their powers under regulation 7 of the Planning (Control of Advertisement) Regulations (NI) 2015 which enables them to remedy what appears to be a substantial injury to the amenity of the locality or a danger to members of the public, by serving a discontinuance notice.As such, I consider there to be adequate provisions within current planning legislation to address this issue and I have no current plans to introduce additional provisions similar to the powers in The Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he intends to ensure that local enterprise can benefit from additional recycled material being available through his proposed waste strategy.
answer_text: Increasing our recycling, and ensuring as much as possible is kept within Northern Ireland for reprocessing, has an important role to play in both reducing our carbon emissions and reducing our reliance on exporting recyclable material outside of the UK. While increasing recycling can save businesses money through reduced waste management fees, improving the quality of our recycling also supports local re-processors with vital feedstock and the ongoing development of the circular economy here, resulting in both economic and environmental benefits.There are many great examples of the circular economy in action here, however some re-processors are currently having to import feedstock from elsewhere as they are unable to source sufficient volume of the desired quality within Northern Ireland. These reprocessors have expressed that they have capacity to take much more material from NI Councils. I am therefore eager to bring forward proposals which will improve both the quality and quantity of recycling, retaining as much of our resources locally for reprocessing and ensuring that local enterprise can benefit. I recently approved the launch of a consultation with proposals designed to achieve this.  Rethinking Our Resources: Measures for Climate Action and a Circular Economy in NI is currently open for responses until 27 June 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any discussions her Department has had with the Department of Health in relation to the provision of accommodation and facilities in prisons to address the needs of neurodivergent prisoners.
answer_text: Many individuals coming into custody have unmet and undiagnosed healthcare needs, and have often not accessed the services they need in the community.NIPS works in partnership with the South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust to support people in prison who have additional needs.  Softening the prison environment where possible, and introducing therapeutic gardens and mental health hubs is an important part of that work.Recent discussions between my Department and the Department of Health have focussed upon the recommendations put forward in the RQIA Review into Services for Vulnerable Persons Detained in Northern Ireland Prisons (October 2021).One of the recommendations in that Review is in respect of robust screening and specialist provision for specific vulnerabilities such as learning disability, autism, ADHD, acquired brain injury and dementia.  Department of Health, Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) have responsibility for progressing this recommendation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given that animal disease control is no longer under the direction of the Secretary of State, and pursuant to AQW 11306/22-27, to detail the percentage level of (i) TB herd incidences; and (ii) TB animal incidences of the total number of herd and animals tested in the Strangford constituency, in the last three years.
answer_text: As animal disease control returned to my direction on 17 May 2024, my response to the Member’s previous question, AQW 12008/22-27, included this information in the format in which it is held by my department, which is for the area covered by the Newtownards DAERA Direct office.The Department publishes statistics on the bovine Tuberculosis programme on a monthly basis on the DAERA website and these can be found using the link below:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/tuberculosis-statistics-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given that animal disease control is no longer under the direction of the Secretary of State, and pursuant to AQW 11305/22-27, to detail the number of (i) herds which received a TB test; and (ii) animals tested for TB in the Strangford constituency, in the last three years.
answer_text: Information relating to bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) is not held on a constituency basis, rather, it is held on the basis of the geographical areas covered by DAERA Direct Offices. The Strangford constituency that you have requested information on falls within the Newtownards DAERA Direct Office boundary. The number of herds and animals tested in the last three years, in the area covered by the Newtownards DAERA Direct Office are as follows:For the year ending April 2024, 1,776 herds, and 192,619 animals were tested. For the year ending April 2023, 1,806 herds and 200,868 animals were tested. For the year ending April 2022 1,815 herds and 200,326 animals were tested.The Department publishes statistics on the bovine Tuberculosis programme on a monthly basis on the DAERA website and these can be found using the link below:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/tuberculosis-statistics-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: Neither the Department, nor its arm’s-length bodies, have provided funding to Sustainable NI for the delivery of services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the rationale as to why only two sections of the Legal Complaints and Regulation Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 have been commenced.
answer_text: The Legal Complaints and Regulation Act 2016 received Royal Assent on 11 April 2016, with work immediately commencing on the appointment of a Legal Services Oversight Commissioner and the development of the secondary legislation required to give full effect to the Act. The commencement of the remaining provisions is dependent upon necessary preparatory work by the Law Society, the Bar Council and the Legal Services Oversight Commissioner.  The absence of an Executive and Assembly between January 2017 and January 2020, and between February 2022 and February 2024, and the uncertainty around the timing of their return, as well as the prioritisation of departmental resources during the Covid pandemic, had a significant impact on the progress of this work.  With the return of the Executive and Assembly, planning has resumed towards the commencement of the remaining provisions of the legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW11480/22-27, to provide the information in relation to draft legislation that is already in existence but has not yet been moved.
answer_text: My Department has no draft legislation that is already in existence but has not yet been moved.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of tree surveys that have been carried out by his Department in the last five years; and (ii) the cost associated with this.
answer_text: My Department has carried out 20,559 tree surveys in the last 5 years. These have been predominantly in the Greater Belfast area where street trees are inspected and assessed on a cyclic basis. Elsewhere, tree surveys are commissioned as required to assess maintenance requirements, or in advance of major road works, or if a particular issue has been identified.  The overall cost to my Department for tree surveys over the last five years has been c.£99k.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount his Department has spent on planting new trees in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Tree planting is usually carried out by my Department as part of a major works scheme, where it will be incorporated into the overall scheme costs; or as a remediation measure where it has been necessary to remove existing trees for safety reasons. The cost of remedial tree planting would be incorporated into general tree maintenance expenditure.Expenditure specifically incurred on planting trees is not recorded separately in my Department’s finance system, however, I can provide expenditure on Grass Cutting, Weed Control and Tree Maintenance for the past five financial years from 2019-20 to 2023-24 in Table 1 below.Table 1Grass Cutting, Weeds Control and Tree Maintenance2019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24*£m5.96.06.66.77.7*Expenditure figures for 2023-24 are provisional until accounts are fully audited and approved

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the amount of waste exported from Northern Ireland for (i) incineration; (ii) recycling; and (iii) disposal, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Northern Ireland exports waste that falls into two main categories. Notifiable waste and green list waste. Notifiable waste generally consists of refuse derived fuel (RDF), mixed wastes and hazardous wastes. Green list waste generally consists of single stream wastes like metal, paper, plastics, tyres etc. (i) Waste exported for incineration (data consists of notifiable waste incineration exports only).	2019 – 122,799 Tonnes	2020 – 147,936 Tonnes	2021 – 188,194 Tonnes	2022 – 213,925 Tonnes	2023 – 281,660 Tonnes		There may also be some green list waste movements sent for incineration, but NIEA do not record this data on their green list waste database. Only notifiable waste incineration figures provided above.		(ii) Waste exported for recycling (consists of notifiable waste and green list waste exports).2019 – 549,691 Tonnes2020 – 563,170 Tonnes2021 – 783,582 Tonnes2022 – 710,311 Tonnes2023 – 697,636 Tonnes(iii) Waste exported for disposal.2019 – 0 Tonnes2020 – 0 Tonnes2021 – 0 Tonnes2022 – 0 Tonnes2023 – 0 Tonnes
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his estimation of how much is lost to the local economy from waste exported from Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Every year Northern Ireland exports large quantities of waste which could be recycled. 	It is difficult to assess the full economic cost of exporting this as there are several factors to consider, some of which are commercially sensitive. A number of local reprocessors have stated that they could use significantly more high quality local recyclate for their businesses which would add value to the local economy. There are, of course, waste companies that benefit financially from exporting our waste too.  Estimates made by Grant Thorton in 2021 show that export of Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF) from households alone cost our councils at least £16m. In addition, this is exported to Energy from Waste, providing combined heat and power for the countries receiving it, which means Northern Ireland loses this opportunity. What cannot be underestimated is the environmental cost of this export market, including the additional emissions from the transportation of material and the extra risks of our waste moving across the world.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the steps his Department is taking to encourage and support (i) farmers; and (ii) food producers to use sustainable farming methods.
answer_text: My Department is developing a new Farm Support and Development Programme. The overall objectives are to transition to a more sustainable farming sector by seeking to implement policies and strategies that benefit our climate and environment, while very importantly, supporting our economically and socially significant agri-food sector.The schemes and measures of the Farm Support and Development Programme are being introduced in a phased manner over the coming months and years and will provide essential levers to contribute to statutory obligations under the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 with a firm focus on genuine just transition.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail all property under the ownership or control of Invest NI that is currently landbanked, including the start date of each.
answer_text: Invest NI does not landbank any of the property under its ownership or control.  Land and property are held in support of business growth across 49 business parks/industrial estates of which 39 currently have land available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his Department’s assessment of the volume of nappy and incontinence waste products sent to landfill (i) in weight; and (ii) as a proportion of the total amount of waste sent to landfill, in the last five years.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) receives waste return data from authorised waste facilities in Northern Ireland. This data is broken down and provided to the NIEA by way of European Waste Catalogue Codes (EWC code). There is no definitive EWC code for nappies or incontinence waste. The NIEA therefore does not hold this information.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the proportion of funding from the Assets Recovery Community Scheme that has been spent in the Strangford constituency since the introduction of the scheme in 2012.
answer_text: Since it was first launched in 2012 the Asset Recovery Community Scheme (ARCS), has awarded approximately £9 million to projects aimed at tackling crime and the fear of crime.  It funds valuable initiatives, mainly in community settings, that actively help victims, communities or the environment in support of a culture of lawfulness.  While my Department does not hold a breakdown of funding by constituency, there is information available on funding allocated by council area.  It should be noted that many projects would impact on a Northern Ireland-wide basis, rather than within a single geographical area.Further detail on ARCS awards more generally is available athttps://www.justice-ni.gov.uk/publications/asset-recovery-community-schemes

answer_text: I intend to publish the updated Model Licence Conditions by the end of the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 11305/22-27, whether his Department has any role in the gathering and communication of data in relation to TB testing.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the requirements of Animal Health Law that are placed on DAERA, including as they relate to transmissible diseases and disease eradication, no longer sat within my ministerial responsibilities and were subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.However, from 17 May 2024, the Defra Secretary of state has exercised his discretion in relation to disease awareness, disease preparedness and disease control in relation to animal and plant health and these areas are now under my ministerial responsibility.	My Department collates data in relation to TB testing on a monthly basis.  For the 12 months to the end of April 2024, herd incidence in Northern Ireland stood at 10.17% with animal incidence at 1.051%.  These statistics are published on my Department’s website and can be accessed at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/tuberculosis-statistics-northern-ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 10130/22-27, what discussions his Department has had with the Department of Justice to provide accommodation and facilities in prisons to address the needs of neurodivergent prisoners.
answer_text: The recent discussions between my Department and the Department of Justice regarding our joint work within prisons have focused on the implementation of the recommendations put forward in the RQIA Review into Services for Vulnerable Persons Detained in Northern Ireland Prisons (October 2021) and the refreshing of the joint Improving Health within the Criminal Justice Action Plan.No specific conversations have taken place relating to the accommodation and facilities in prison for persons with neurodivergent conditions.   However, work to refresh the joint Action Plan is likely to recommence in the coming months and it may arise during those discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 11214/22-27, to detail (i) the engagement his Department has had with local planning authorities in the last five years to build competence and capacity in the area of Environmental Impact Assessments; and (ii) how his Department measures the effectiveness of that engagement.
answer_text: From April 2019 my Department has engaged with local planning authorities (LPAs) as part of its Environmental Governance Work Programme, intended to enhance competence and capacity on environmental compliance in the planning system, particularly in relation to environmental impact assessment (EIA). The programme included the development and delivery of two levels of bespoke EIA training to over 200 planning staff, the publication of a number of EIA Development Management Practice Notes and the establishment of an Environmental Officers Forum, to facilitate discussions on key and emerging EIA issues. The work programme involved an initial baseline survey of planning staff, ongoing questionnaires for training participants and a post-contract report assessing the effectiveness of the many aspects of the work programme. This report has been shared with LPAs seeking information on their proposals as to how they intend to maintain momentum in this area going forward.  It is important to recognise that it is up to each individual planning authority to satisfy itself that it adequately meets its own statutory obligations in relation to EIA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the results of the social supermarket pilot scheme in Ards and North Down.
answer_text: My Department has been working with local Councils on a co-design basis to roll out Social Supermarket models across Northern Ireland.The pilot project designed through this process and commissioned by Ards & North Down Council fulfilled the Department’s requirements that the model must provide more than food and seek to tackle the underlying reasons why those being referred to the service were facing food insecurity through engagement and provision of wraparound services.  Anonymised user feedback and case studies that have been shared with the Department have demonstrated the positive outcomes of the pilot.  The pilot concluded at the end of March 2024 and I can confirm that Ards & North Down Council have run an open call for a delivery partner, or partners, to continue to provide support through the Social Supermarket  model based on the co-design process and factoring in learning from the pilot scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the impact the removal of fines has had on the return of overdue books to libraries.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Libraries NII have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The removal of library fines has not had a discernible impact on the return of overdue books to libraries. The removal of charges for overdue library books was introduced as a positive measure to encourage participation in libraries, enhance the perception of libraries and remove a barrier to use.The response to the removal of overdue charges has been received as a positive and progressive step.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Libraries NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the criteria used when assessing the need to issue a vesting order for a local government capital scheme.
answer_text: The Department for Communities is not the sole NI Executive Department that may issue a vesting order for a local government capital scheme.If a local council applies to the Department for Communities for an order vesting land in the council, the Department will consider each application on its individual merits. Compulsory acquisition (vesting) raises issues of proportionality, constitutionality and human rights given public interference with private rights. The Department will therefore always seek legal opinion from the Departmental Solicitor before reaching a decision to issue any vesting order.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether an assessment has been made of (i) the Welsh government’s use of recycled nappies in road resurfacing schemes; and (ii) the potential for this in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the work in Wales to trial the use of fibres recovered from used nappies in the production of road resurfacing materials. I am advised that the findings from the trial are not yet know and my Officials will be updated on this through their routine engagements with their counterparts in the Welsh Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 10432/22-27, to detail why those sections and schedules of the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 have not been commenced.
answer_text: The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act 2016 deals with the drink driving regulatory framework and graduated driver licensing. In both areas, significant policy development activity and subordinate legislation is required to implement the new arrangements fully. For drink driving, a key barrier has been the absence of suitable breathalysers to test effectively at lower alcohol levels. I have asked my officials to provide me with an update on the current position both on drink driving and graduated driver licensing, including any remaining barriers to implementation. I will use that information to make a decision on the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 11306/22-27, to clarify whether his Department holds any of the requested information prior to 12 April 2024.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the requirements of Animal Health Law that are placed on DAERA, including as they relate to transmissible diseases and disease eradication, no longer sat within my ministerial responsibilities and were subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.However, from 17 May 2024, the Defra Secretary of state has exercised his discretion in relation to disease awareness, disease preparedness and disease control in relation to animal and plant health and these areas are now under my ministerial responsibility.Information relating to bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) is not held on a constituency basis, rather, it is held on the basis of the geographical regions covered by DAERA Direct Offices. The Strangford constituency that you have requested information on falls within the Newtownards DAERA Direct Office boundary.The herd and animal incidences in the area covered by the Newtownards DAERA Direct office for the last 3 years were as follows: For the year ending March 2024 the herd incidence was 12.45% and the animal incidence was 1.285%. For the year ending March 2023 the herd incidence was 14.92% and the animal incidence was 1.272%. For the year ending March 2022 the herd incidence was 11.23% and the animal incidence was 1.043%.The Department publishes statistics on the bovine Tuberculosis programme on a monthly basis on the DAERA website and these can be found using the link below:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/tuberculosis-statistics-northern-ireland
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 10432/22-27, to provide the rationale for the non-commencement of the various articles and schedules of the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 2007.
answer_text: It is normal practice for provisions in primary legislation to be made operational through Commencement Orders, when required. The 2007 Order contains 86 Articles and 8 Schedules, covering a broad range of policy areas. Many of the Order’s provisions have been commenced, through a number of separate Commencement Orders. However, some provisions are not yet operational, for various reasons, dating from before my time in office. However, my officials would be happy to respond to specific queries on any individual provisions that have not yet been commenced.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) all reviews of policy initiated by their Department which are (a) not yet complete; and (b) yet to be implemented; and (ii) the date on which these reviews commenced.
answer_text: Multiple reviews of policy initiated by the Department are currently underway for our consideration as follows.A review, which commenced in April 2024, is considering the extension of age discrimination legislation to the provision of goods, facilities, and services.A review of the Together: Building a United Community strategy, which commenced in January 2022, is due to be completed and submitted to us ahead of the Summer Assembly Recess.The development of a new Strategy for Victims and Survivors commenced in 2021 through a review of the previous Strategy. This review was completed, including a public consultation, and an updated Strategy will shortly be submitted to us. The Review of the Historical Institutional Abuse Redress Process commenced in December 2021, and the final report was submitted to The Executive Office in June 2022. While a significant number of the Review's recommendations have already been implemented to improve the process for victims and survivors, we will consider the full findings before taking a formal view. A new International Relations Strategy will also be developed. In 2023, Departmental officials completed an independent evaluation of the 2014 International Relations Strategy and a Project Assurance Review, the respective recommendations of which will inform the new Strategy.A review of the implementation of the Racial Equality Strategy has recently been completed and is due to be submitted to us for consideration shortly.
