question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the rationale for Section 9 of the Building Regulations (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2009 not having been commenced.
answer_text: Section 9 of the Building Regulations (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2009 (‘Section 9’) relates to a requirement for a public register of documentation to be kept by district councils. It further notes that any documents to be kept on that register may be prescribed and requires that access arrangements and holding periods must be prescribed, under secondary building regulations.Proposals for amendments to our Building Regulations legislation are normally made based on research and learning from the implementation of equivalent legislation and guidance developed by the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities and Local Government.While the equivalent legislation in England and Wales requiring a public register of documentation to be kept was commenced in 2006, legislation prescribing the required documentation has not been brought forward in those jurisdictions. Such regulations would normally provide a precursor to the introduction of requirements for district councils to maintain a register here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the scope of the Maritime Fisheries Fund (NI) 2022-23 being developed by his Department; and (ii) to detail a timescale for this.
answer_text: My Department is considering how to best utilise the 2024/25 budget allocation to support the needs of the marine and fisheries sectors.  Funding opportunities should be consistent with those available for all industry sectors under previous marine and fisheries schemes of support, with the level of support scaled to the budget that is available.		Preparations are being made, including making the necessary legislation, with the aim of opening the scheme to stakeholders in the summer. 		At this time, HM Treasury has not indicated what level of financial assistance may be available post 2024/25 and this has delayed the progression of a longer-term funding strategy for marine environment and fisheries stakeholders.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following the Speaker's Ruling on Assembly Questions on 12 March 2024, to detail the amount of funding that has been provided to Northern Cricket Union affiliated clubs in the each of last five years.
answer_text: Due to the volume of information to be provided, I have arranged for the information requested to be placed in the Assembly Library.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. This website is publicly accessible and holds records of applications and funding to voluntary and community sector organisations including funding disbursed to the sports sector.  I have included the link for future convenience - https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the rationale for section 16 of the Government Resources and Accounts Act (Northern Ireland) 2001 not having been commenced; and (ii) the impact this has on the scrutiny of government accounts.
answer_text: (i) A full set of Annual Reports and Accounts (ARAs) is prepared for each Department and these are audited and certified by the Comptroller and Auditor General (C&AG) each year. Following implementation of the recommendations of the Review of Financial Process, which were agreed by the Executive in December 2016, these Departmental ARAs have been prepared on a consolidated basis from 2022-23. This means that, as well as Departmental Agencies, they now also include each Department’s sponsored Non-Departmental Public Bodies (NDPBs). This ensures Departmental ARAs provide a valuable annual summary of the financial performance and position of the Departmental group under the remit of each Executive Minister, without the need to commence section 16 of the Government Resources and Accounts Act (Northern Ireland) 2001.(ii) All Departmental ARAs are audited and certified by the NI Audit Office, whilst the accounts of local government bodies are audited and certified by the Local Government Auditor, thereby providing effective scrutiny of government accounts. In addition, data from NI public sector bodies ARAs is included in the UK Whole of Government Accounts, which is examined by the National Audit Office with a view to satisfying themself that they present a true and fair view.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what consideration they have given to extending the age discrimination legislation to include under-16s.
answer_text: Consideration of extending age discrimination legislation to the provision of goods, facilities and services was the subject of consultations in a previous mandate.  A decision was not made in respect of the scope of that legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to list (i) the (a) primary legislation; and (b) secondary legislation drafted by his Department that has not yet been introduced or made; and (ii) the year that each was drafted.
answer_text: The Executive’s Legislative Programme for introduction in 2024 was set out in the First and deputy First Ministers’ Written Ministerial Statement on Thursday 23 May 2024.As of 5 June 2024, one item of subordinate legislation, the Local Government (Remote Meetings) Regulations (Northern Ireland), has been drafted by my Department in 2024 and is awaiting to be laid in the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to list (i) the (a) primary legislation; and (b) secondary legislation drafted by his Department that has not yet been introduced or made; and (ii) the year that each was drafted.
answer_text: My department does not have any primary or secondary legislation drafted that has not yet been introduced or made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to list (i) the (a) primary legislation; and (b) secondary legislation drafted by his Department that has not yet been introduced or made; and (ii) the year that each was drafted.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to list (i) the (a) primary legislation; and (b) secondary legislation drafted by their Department that has not yet been introduced or made; and (ii) the year that each was drafted.
answer_text: Our Written Statement to the Assembly on 23 May regarding the Executive Legislation Programme of bills for introduction in 2024 included reference to an Executive Office bill on Mother and Baby Homes, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses Inquiry and Financial Redress.  Work on drafting the bill is ongoing and our intention remains for it to be introduced into the Assembly this calendar year.Two items of subordinate legislation: the Period Products (the Executive Office Specified Public Service Bodies) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024; and the Fair Employment (Monitoring) (Amendment) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2024, have been drafted by our department in 2024 and have not yet been laid in the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment has been made of (i) the number; and (ii) the cost of hospital admissions as a result of (a) alcohol poisoning; and (b) drug overdoses in each of the last five years.
answer_text: (i) The number of hospital admissions for (a) alcohol poisoning and (b) drug overdoses over the past five years is detailed in the table below:YearAlcohol Poisoning Drug Overdose 2018-2019119538212019-2020110936752020-202190832372021-202256923672022-20234402026(ii) The average cost of each hospital admission has been estimated at £1,500 (source: NI Audit Office Report on Addiction Services in NI, June 2020). While the figures above appear to show admissions to hospital due to substance use have encouragingly been falling in recent years, we cannot be complacent and they still remain higher than we want them to be. Addressing the harm related to alcohol and other drugs is a key priority for my Department, which leads on the Northern Ireland Executive's 10-year Substance Use Strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery. This strategy aims to directly prevent and reduce the harm related to substance use through a range of cross-departmental actions and support. The number of hospital admissions can therefore be used an indicator to measure progress against the outcomes in the strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many people have been awarded money under the Vaccine Damage Payments Scheme in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Vaccine Damage Payment Scheme is a UK wide scheme that is administered by the Business Services Authority, which is part of NHS England, on behalf of the whole UK.  My department does not play any role in the Vaccine Damage Payment Scheme and consequently, it does not hold any information regarding payments made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of asylum cases that have been heard in a (a) First-tier Tribunal; (b) Upper Tribunal; and (c) Court of Appeal, in each of the last five years; and (ii) how many of these cases were successful.
answer_text: Nationality and immigration, including asylum are specified as excepted matters under Schedule 2 of the Northern Ireland Act 1998.Under the terms of a Service Level Agreement (SLA) with HM Courts and Tribunals Service (HMCTS), the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) provides hearing clerks and suitable accommodation in Belfast for several non-devolved Tribunals including the Immigration and Asylum Chambers.However, HMCTS retains administrative and statutory responsibility for these Tribunals, and they should be contacted directly for the information you have requested. You can find details of how to contact HMCTS at: https://www.gov.uk/courts-tribunals/first-tier-tribunal-immigration-and-asylum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has given any consideration to the creation of an Enduring Power of Attorney which covers health and welfare decisions.
answer_text: Enduring powers of attorney (EPAs) are provided for under the Enduring Powers of Attorney (Northern Ireland) Order 1987, which is the responsibility of the Department of Finance. These are limited in scope to property and financial affairs.Part 5 of the Mental Capacity Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 provides for the creation of lasting powers of attorney (LPAs) by which the donor can confer on an attorney authority to make decisions about the donor’s care, treatment and personal welfare, as well as property and financial affairs. Whilst commencement of these provisions is for the Department of Health, the Department of Justice has a significant interest in the operational implications. The Department of Finance also retains an interest in the interaction of LPAs with EPAs.Departments are jointly involved in making implementation plans to allow for commencement of the uncommenced provisions of the 2016 Act. Progress, however, is likely to remain slow given the severe financial pressures facing the Executive at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to list (i) the (a) primary legislation; and (b) secondary legislation drafted by her Department that has not yet been introduced or made; and (ii) the year that each was drafted.
answer_text: In terms of primary legislation, a Justice Bill has been under development since my return as Minister of Justice earlier this year.  Drafting is complete and I intend to submit this Bill to the Executive to seek approval for introduction before the summer recess. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to bring forward legislation to prohibit indirect disability discrimination.
answer_text: Ensuring that disabled people have the same rights and opportunities as the rest of our community is a key priority for me. I am considering the next steps to be taken in the development of an Executive Disability Strategy. This will include consideration of the need for a review and reform of disability legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any measures he will take to ensure that pay secrecy clauses in employment contracts are overridden where there is inequality of pay on gender grounds.
answer_text: I have no immediate plans to legislate in the area of pay secrecy clauses in employment contracts. I would, however, encourage all employers to ensure that there is as much transparency as possible in respect of pay arrangements in workplaces, and remind them of the need to fulfil their workplace equality obligations. If an individual has concerns about practices in a workplace, they should consider contacting the Labour Relations Agency’s Workplace Information Service on 03300 555 300, which can provide information on employment rights matters.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any consideration his Department is giving to improved protection for disabled people against harassment when accessing goods and services.
answer_text: The Executive’s Disability Strategy will aim to address barriers and inequalities and make a real difference to disabled people’s lives. This will include consideration of the need for improved protection against harassment when accessing goods and services. I am currently considering the work that has been taken forward to date and the next steps to be taken to progress the development of the Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will legislate to provide express protection for people, such as carers, friends or family members, who are subjected to direct discrimination or harassment due to (i) their association with a disabled person; or (ii) being wrongly perceived as disabled.
answer_text: I refer the member to the reply for AQW 11390/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will legislate to provide additional protection to disabled tenants who live in rented residential accommodation in relation to the making of reasonable adjustments by landlords to common parts.
answer_text: Adaptations under the Disabled Facilities Grant may be made to the common parts of buildings containing flats, where the disabled person is the occupant of one of the flats. There are currently no plans to legislate for landlords to provide reasonable adjustments, but a landlord can apply for a Disabled Facilities Grant on behalf of a tenant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will legislate to remove the list of capacities from the Disability Discrimination Act 1995 to align with legislation in the rest of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: Ensuring that disabled people have the same rights and opportunities as the rest of our community is a key priority for me. I am considering the next steps to be taken in the development of an Executive Disability Strategy. This will include consideration of the need for a review and reform of disability legislation.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what engagement they or their officials have had with stakeholders in relation to the Strategic Framework Ending Violence against Women and Girls.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans to introduce a specific nappy recycling scheme if proposals to reduce residual waste capacity in bins are introduced.
answer_text: Whilst there are no current plans to introduce a specific nappy recycling scheme here and challenges exist, I would be keen to explore opportunities and infrastructure requirements with District Councils should a prospect to meet and discuss the matter be offered.It is important that we continue to reduce the volume of waste we send to landfill.  The current Rethinking Our Resources consultation on recycling includes a range of proposals to increase the quality and quantity or our recycling, including a proposal to restrict the capacity of residual waste collected at the kerbside. While implementation of any proposals will be informed by consultation responses, my department will assess any unintended consequences of planned proposals and may consider potential exemptions for specific categories or groups where necessary.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of (i) herds which received a TB test; and (ii) animals tested for TB in the Strangford constituency, in the last three years.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the requirements of Animal Health Law that are placed on DAERA, including as they relate to transmissible diseases and disease eradication, no longer sit within my ministerial responsibilities and are now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.

answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the requirements of Animal Health Law that are placed on DAERA, including as they relate to transmissible diseases and disease eradication, no longer sit within my ministerial responsibilities and are now subject to the direction and control of the Secretary of State.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what mechanisms his Department has to conduct monitoring of the Environmental Impact Assessment systems in local planning authorities.
answer_text: The legislative requirements on Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA), insofar as they relate to the planning system, are in place and set out clearly the statutory obligations on councils, in their role as local planning authorities (LPAs).  While my Department has engaged with all LPAs over recent years to build competence and capacity in the area of EIA it has no formal monitoring role in relation to the systems within LPAs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following his statement on 5 March 2024 in relation to XL Bully dogs, what discussions his Department has had with the Housing Executive and other social landlords on the impact the proposed legislation will have on tenants who are XL Bully dog owners.
answer_text: Following my statement to the Assembly on 5 March 2024, my officials made contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) to ascertain the impacts on social housing tenants who own XL Bully type dogs. The NIHE General Conditions of Tenancy allow for the keeping of domestic pets within their dwellings. These Conditions don’t make specific reference to types or breeds of domestic pet. However, in certain circumstances e.g. tenants living in flats, or where the tenant wishes to keep more than one pet, they must seek the consent of the local Area Manager. This approval would not be forthcoming in cases where a dog breed was banned or subject to unmet conditions of ownership.  NIHE added that tenants who keep XL Bully type dogs may be required to provide evidence of their exemption certification and, if found to be non-compliant with the legislation, will be reported to the relevant authorities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following his meeting with the NIO Minister of State, for an update on a financial support package for farmers impacted by the autumn floods.
answer_text: I have made representations to the UK Government about the need for central government intervention and short-term financial support to alleviate the difficulties being faced by farmers and growers as a result of the prolonged adverse weather impacting all four nations of the UK.In addition, further to a recent conversation with NIO Minister of State Steve Baker MP, I intend to write to him detailing the situation in Northern Ireland.  However, as it currently stands, I am not in a position to implement a financial support scheme for farmers impacted by last autumn’s flooding.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of prosecutorial fines issued in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Justice Act (NI) 2015 (Part 3 sections 17-27) provides for powers for public prosecutors to offer low level offenders a ‘Prosecutorial Fine’ which is a financial penalty, up to a maximum of £200 (the equivalent of a level 1 court fine) as an alternative to prosecution of the case at court.  These provisions have, however, not yet been commenced and therefore no prosecutorial fines have been issued in the last five years. If commenced, the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) would have the power to apply a prosecutorial fine for low level offences.  The recipients of a prosecutorial fine will avoid a formal criminal record if the disposal is accepted and paid, although the criminal justice system will retain a record of such disposals to inform decisions on any future offending by the recipients of prosecutorial fines.The Criminal Justice Board has prioritised a programme of work targeted at reducing avoidable delay within the system.  One of the five key workstreams is exploring options in relation to ‘Out of Courts Disposals’.  Such disposals provide an option for certain offences to be dealt with proportionately, and at an early stage, without the need for proceeding through a formal court process and thereby reducing demand on the system.  This would enable resources to be better directed to the prosecution of more serious offences.  As part of this workstream options in relation to Prosecutorial Fines are being explored alongside other out of court disposals.  As with many new initiatives, implementation will require investment and, given the current financial situation, available funding will be a factor in any decisions regarding the introduction of these potentially transformational provisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of Justices of the Peace (i) appointed; and (ii) removed since the commencement of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.
answer_text: There have been no Justices of the Peace appointed or any removed since the commencement of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2015.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of Justices of the Peace in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There are currently 48 Justices of the Peace operational in Northern Ireland.

I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The table below sets out planned maintenance and improvement schemes for NIHE housing stock in Bowtown Estate, Newtownards in 2024/25 and 2025/26.  Delivery of these schemes as indicated is subject to funding, scheme development and approvals being secured in line with anticipated timescales, and delivery contracts being available.2024/25Heating Ards Estates5Low Carbon Pilot Scheme Bowtown Estate, Ards Phase 2382025/26Bathroom Kitchen RewireMovilla Estate 2”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what proportion of current remand prisoners have been on remand for (i) more than two years; (ii) more than one year; and (iii) more than six months.
answer_text: On 26th April 2024, a total of 666 prisoners were held in custody on remand.  The proportions who had been on remand for (i) more than two years; (ii) more than one year but not exceeding two years; and (iii) more than six months but not exceeding one year, are set out in the table below.Time on RemandRemand Prisoners(i) More than two years2.3%(ii) More than one year but not exceeding two years7.7%(iii) More than six months but not exceeding one year14.6%Source: PRISM - Management Information Table Notes:Data is based on snapshot information for those in custody on remand on 26th April 2024.Time on Remand is the sum of all periods of remand during a prisoner’s custodial period. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) her assessment on the availability of information to the Public Prosecution Service on breaches of police bail; and (ii) how such information is accessible by prosecutors to assist with bail applications.
answer_text: My Department’s role in relation to police bail is to set the legislative framework, which is contained in the Police and Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (PACE).  The availability of information to the Public Prosecution Service (PPS) on breaches of police bail is a matter for both the PSNI and the Public Prosecution Service (PPS).  The Chief Constable is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board.  The PPS is independent of my Department.  You may therefore wish to direct your question directly to the PSNI and the PPS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what corporate oversight is in place to monitor the (i) use; and (ii) impacts of street bail.
answer_text: The operation of street bail, including corporate oversight arrangements, is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what proportion of the Magistrates’ Court defendants is made up by road traffic defendants, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Information on the number of defendants dealt with at magistrates’ courts, where the main offence was a motoring offence, has been provided for the period 2018 – 2022, the most recent five year period for which information is available.Motoring offence cases as proportion of all cases dealt with at magistrates' courts, 2018 – 2022 YearProsecutionsMotoringMotoring as % of all cases2018           27,931         12,958 46.4%2019           27,967         12,111 43.3%2020           20,092          8,780 43.7%2021           28,269         12,594 44.6%2022           28,432         13,129 46.2%Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposal at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases dealt with at adult and youth magistrates' courts.3. Figures relate to cases where the main offence at disposal was a motoring offence.4. Figures relate to cases prosecuted by PPS on behalf of PSNI, NCA, Airport constabulary, Harbour Police or PBNI, whether they resulted in a conviction or not.The Criminal Justice Board has prioritised a programme of work targeted at reducing avoidable delay with the system.  One part of the Speeding Up Justice Programme of work is looking at how motoring offences are dealt with in the magistrates’ court, both in terms of exploring the potential for specialised court models and also how some motoring offences and other low level offences can be diverted from the formal court process to reduce demand on the system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether a supervised activity order is now the default penalty for outstanding fine payments below £1000.
answer_text: A supervised activity order is one of a range of powers available to the judiciary when dealing with the non-payment of fines. At a default hearing a judge can deal with the debtor by:making a collection order if not already made; giving more time to pay or permit payment via instalments;making a bank account order; making a vehicle seizure order; issuing a distress warrant;making a supervised activity order or, for 16 or 17 year olds, an attendance centre order;issuing a warrant for committal to prison; remitting all or part of the outstanding amount.The Court may not make a supervised activity order or an attendance centre order without having first considered and dismissed each of the options at (a) - (e), and it may not issue a warrant for committal to prison or remit any or all of the outstanding amount without first considering and dismissing each of the options at (a) to (f).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the Prison and Young Offenders Centre Rules (Northern Ireland) 1995 has been amended to reflect the fine default provisions of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.
answer_text: The Prison and Young Offenders Centre Rules (Northern Ireland) 1995 have not been amended to reflect the fine default provisions of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.  While the pertinent Prison Rule, i.e. Rule 30, provides a general permissive power to grant remission, in line with legal convention this secondary legislation must now be read subject to the express provision in the relevant primary legislation, i.e. the now amended section 13 of the Prison Act (NI) 1953, in which remission has been removed for fine defaulters.  While it is likely that my department will take the opportunity to make a number of minor legislation related changes including this one when it next makes amendments to Prison Rules, there are no current plans to undertake such an exercise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what discussions her Department has had with relevant criminal justice system organisations to develop a joint plan for managing detainees held in police custody awaiting first court appearance, pursuant to the recommendations of the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland.
answer_text: A Working Group of the relevant criminal justice agencies was established in January 2023 and an interim Joint Plan for managing detainees in both PSNI and Court custody suites, their attendance at Court and transfer to prison custody, where appropriate, was implemented from 4 July 2023. Discussions continue to take place on a range of issues, including the possibility of an amendment to Court Rules to allow police to take recognizances for defendants appearing before the Court while in police custody on a charge sheet.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether an action plan has been developed to deliver mutual recognition of penalty points for driving offences with the Republic of Ireland, following the recommendations of the 2016 Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland report on the enforcement of road traffic legislation in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Following the 2016 inspection report, a cross-departmental and interagency group led by the Department for Infrastructure examined and discussed the mutual recognition of penalty points. The conclusion was that the different tariffs for offences and the award of points in the Republic of Ireland for offences that would not attract points in the UK made the two systems incompatible. Criminal Justice Inspection NI accepted this conclusion and, in December 2021, confirmed its agreement to the recommendation being closed off, meaning there was no longer a requirement for an action plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has plans to undertake a public consultation on proposals for a bail act during this mandate.
answer_text: The loss of two years of the mandate has necessitated prioritising my legislative requirements for the remainder of the mandate, which has resulted in a significant reduction in my original planned legislative programme for the 2022 – 2027 period.It is highly unlikely that a Bail Act would have any demonstrable effect in terms of reducing the level of remand in order to counteract the increasing prison population in Northern Ireland.It is also important to note that the draft Law Commission Bail Act did not contain any provisions that would reduce remand numbers.Therefore, the main driver for bringing forward a Bail Bill would be to consolidate, and thereby simplify, existing bail law, although it should be noted that operational partners do not report any difficulties with the existing legislation.It would take several months to develop policy proposals prior to any public consultation, which would divert resources from higher priority work already being progressed by the Department.It is in this context that I am not currently planning to undertake a public consultation on proposals for a Bail Act during this mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether a temporary Legacy Fine Unit was established to deal with legacy debt fine cases; and (ii) what was the result of this.
answer_text: Following a recommendation made by the Criminal Justice Inspectorate Northern Ireland (CJINI) in June 2021, a temporary legacy debt recovery team was established within the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service’s Fine Collection and Enforcement Service in April 2022. To date the team has reviewed 29,500 cases and recovered £2.04m in unpaid legacy fines.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what discussions her Department has had with the PSNI in relation to developing an options paper for police bail, as recommended by the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland in its report The operation of Bail and Remand in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department’s role in relation to police bail is to set the legislative framework, which is contained in the Police and Criminal Evidence (Northern Ireland) Order 1989 (PACE).  My officials are undertaking a wider review of PACE and wrote to the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) to seek their views on a number of proposed updates, including the need for any changes to legislation on police bail.  My officials are considering the PSNI’s response and will engage with them further in this regard.  The practical operation of police bail is however a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on what his Department is doing to support neighbourhood tourism.
answer_text: In November 2023, my Department published a draft 10 Year Plan for Tourism in the North. Neighbourhood tourism, sometimes known as community tourism, supports several key themes in this plan and my priority to promote regional balance across the North. There are a wide range of actions being undertaken by Tourism NI (TNI) to deliver against this theme.  For instance, long-term funding has been provided to the Mourne and Causeway Coast & Glens Heritage Trusts to undertake community tourism activities in their areas.Collaboration with Councils and other tourism partners underpins much of this work. As part of TNI’s Service Level Agreement with Visit Belfast, for example, a series of activities are being delivered to encourage visitors to explore the City’s neighbourhoods more fully, thereby extending stays and spreading the benefits of tourism.   The Strangford Lough tourism cluster is also a notable example of community tourism. Initially funded by Invest NI, the cluster has brought together local community representatives, tourism businesses and other agencies to develop  the visitor product and drive additional visits and spend in the area. TNI  is working with Councils and other stakeholders to explore how the cluster can continue to help the area flourish as a sustainable destination.  I anticipate that neighbourhood tourism will be a key topic for discussion and joint action at the new Tourism Partnership Board which I announced at TNI’s flagship conference earlier this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department's engagement with Belfast City Council in relation to the design and delivery of the York Street Interchange Project.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability, and improve road safety.The York Street Interchange Project has been in development since 2008 and at various stages the Department has engaged with Belfast City Council as a stakeholder in the project. Between 2011 and 2013 this related to consideration of preliminary options and then selection of the preferred option for the project, with further engagement in 2015 on the publication of the statutory orders and public inquiry. Between 2020 and 2024 engagement with council officials and elected members has related to the independent Project Assurance Review (PAR) (2020/21) and a subsequent Placemaking and Active Travel Review (PATR).The Prioritisation of Major Roads Projects in August 2023 listed York Street Interchange in Tier 3, and indicated that work would continue to conclude the PATR. Any future decision I would make as Minister on this scheme’s progression will be informed by the Department’s emerging Transport Plans and the availability of funding. Subject to a positive decision to progress, my officials will engage with Belfast City Council in relation to the design and delivery of the project.My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. The value of our infrastructure cannot be overstated, and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made.Details of the current status of the York Street Interchange project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/york-street-interchange-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what consideration has been given to a feasibility study on the value of Town, City or County Architects for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has no current plans regarding a feasibility study on the value of Town, City or County Architects for Northern Ireland. However, my Department has recently commenced a review of the Architecture and Built Environment Policy for Northern Ireland, and the value of a Town, City or County Architect will be considered as part of this review process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) what plans he has to maximise the Defence Heritage Project; and (ii) what steps he will take to be more innovative in promoting such heritage.
answer_text: (i) My Department commissioned a Defence Heritage survey, which took place between August 2020 and January 2023. A total of 4,592 sites were surveyed across Northern Ireland and detailed site descriptions and photographs were added to the Defence Heritage Record. Staff in Historic Environment Division are completing upload of this data to the Historic Environment Map Viewer, to enable public access to the very significant amount of detailed data which has been recorded, and which is available at:  https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/services/historic-environment-map-viewer This dataset is also currently being used by my staff to identify appropriate sites which meet the criteria for statutory protection as Scheduled Historic Monuments or Listed Buildings and to implement designation. The provision of this data also provides an opportunity for local communities, researchers, and bodies such as Councils or Local landscape Partnerships to engage with this information for the first time and to understand the rich variety of defence heritage assets which surround us in our landscape in Northern Ireland. It is envisaged that the availability of this information will encourage those groups to consider ways in which the value of this resource can be maximised to ensure the ongoing survival of these important heritage assets and use them to tell the story of this period in the history of Northern Ireland.(ii) Now that this significant dataset has been created, my staff in the Historic Environment Record of Northern Ireland (HERoNI) are looking forward to hosting a launch of the new Defence Heritage Record, to promote awareness and use of the Record. The accompanying communications plan will target those bodies and groups who are in key positions to maximise the potential of these sites and deliver community benefits from them, such as local councils, Landscape Partnerships and bodies which provide funding opportunities. When engaging with such bodies my staff will continue to encourage innovative and creative proposals which also ensure the ongoing care of such sites. There are also numerous defence heritage assets within the government estate and my staff will ensure that those are noted in the forthcoming report on the Protocol for the Care of the Government Historic Estate, which provides a benchmark of good conservation and management of such sites for public bodies. Raising awareness of such features within the government estate provides a useful opportunity to ensure ongoing use and flag any potential opportunities which may be available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he will encourage Invest NI to provide free or subsidised access to British Standards Institution information via (i) the NI Business Info website; (ii) its research centre; and (iii) the enterprise centre network, as is the case in other parts of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: Free or subsidised access to the British Standards Institution is not provided for businesses in the North of Ireland as there has been lack of demand for this costly service in the past.Instead, Invest NI offers alternative services. nibusinessinfo.co.uk: free online advice, guidance and signposting to experts on specific business areas. This includes guidance on international technical and management standards, including British standards, with direct links to the British Standards Institution, along with relevant contact information.Technical Advisory Unit offers free advice to North of Ireland manufacturer/engineering businesses or importers to ensure that they buy the correct Technical Standards to implement within their businesses to gain accreditation. Innovate UK Business Growth (incorporates associate membership with the Enterprise Europe Network). The Standards for Growth – BSI Toolkit is delivered in collaboration with Innovate UK Business Growth and supplies access to over 111,000 British, European and International Standards, opportunity for comment on draft standards at public consultation stage, live online training on the benefit use and participation in standards in key areas.   Innovate NI offers free high-level advice and guidance to North of Ireland businesses on the new BSI Innovation Management Standard ISO 56000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the measures being taken to expand testing for Alzheimer's disease to enable people to benefit from new treatments at an early stage.
answer_text: The term dementia can be used to describe a number of symptoms including a decline in memory, reasoning and communication skills and a gradual loss of skills needed to carry out daily activities. Dementia is a progressive condition and can affect people of any age but it is most common in older people. Alzheimer’s Disease is the most common form of dementia.Northern Ireland is projected to have the largest increase in the number of people living with dementia across the UK by 2040, with the current figures of 22,700 set to rise to 43,000. Receiving a dementia diagnosis is key to unlocking access to personalised care and support, as well as accessing treatments that can help to control symptoms. A diagnosis helps enable people to access treatment, plan ahead and identify any potential supports they may require.The Department recognises the urgency with which individuals awaiting assessment will wish to be seen in order to understand their condition and to commence treatment.  The Department has issued a 9 week- target commissioning plan directive which sets out that people should have a first assessment following referral into Health and Social Care Trust memory services within that timescale. All Trusts have advised that they employ rigorous triaging regime on receipt of dementia referrals to ensure that those individuals at greatest risk are seen first.My officials continue to engage with the Regional Trust Dementia Leads in order to scrutinize performance against the 9-week target as well as performance against the Regional Service Delivery plan. My officials advise the planning for a Regional Dementia Project Board is at an advanced stage, with the inaugural session planned for May 2024. It is proposed that the project board will support the delivery of existing dementia services, as well as consider future service delivery models. Key priorities for the Regional Dementia Project Board will be to decrease waiting times for assessment and diagnosis, to consider steps to improve efficiency in systems and processes across the region, to consider regional approaches to training, to improve the experience of diagnosis for people with dementia and their relatives/carers and to formulate a clear regional vision of services into the future.The Regional Dementia Project Board will additionally focus on the Regional Dementia Care Pathway. The roll out of the Regional Dementia Care Pathway will significantly contribute to raising the standards across all trusts, including addressing delays in dementia diagnosis. Although the Pathway was published in 2018 it was recognised that implementation of the pathway would require a significant funding allocation, and consequently, implementation is yet to take place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the legislation, or parts of legislation, that falls under his Department's responsibility that has not yet commenced.
answer_text: The following Bills are currently included in the Department’s draft legislative programme and have yet to be introduced: -Pharmacy Technician BillMental Health and Capacity Bill Alcohol Minimum Unit Pricing BillNorthern Ireland Independent Medical Examiner ServiceQuality, Safety and Regulation of HSC ServicesSafe and Effective Staffing Duty of CandourSignificant work on each of the Bills listed above has however been undertaken within the Department. The Legislation Programme Board, will manage the development of these bills and their introduction into the Assembly.  The Board will also consider departmental plans for legislation in the remainder of this mandate, and the Executive will be kept advised of these as necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the areas of the Strangford constituency that are scheduled to benefit from the LED street lighting replacement scheme in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: My Department has an ongoing capital programme that prioritises the replacement of inefficient street lights with energy efficient LEDs.The programme for 2024-25 is currently being developed and will be finalised and published once budgets for the incoming financial year have been confirmed and agreed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the financial value of fishing opportunity lost to Northern Ireland fishermen as a result of the closure of the Isle of Man fishing grounds.
answer_text: The potential losses from the Isle of Man closure areas for the Northern Ireland nephrops fishery are estimated at £425,870 in landing value per annum; however, Northern Ireland fishing vessels are likely to displace fishing activity to other areas to fish their quota. 		The impact on the pelagic sector is not clear at present. The pelagic landings from the Isle of Man closed areas are estimated at £273,574 per annum.		I have made representations to the DEFA Minister, Isle of Man, in relation to this matter and I have also requested a meeting.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many incidents of unintentional discharge of sewage by NI Water into watercourses has occurred in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The issue you raised is an operational matter for NI Water. You should therefore direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the legislation, or parts of legislation, that falls under his Department's responsibility that has not yet commenced.
answer_text: The following legislation, or parts of legislation, have not yet commenced:The Education (Northern Ireland) Order 2006;The SEND Act (Special Educational Needs and Disability Act (Northern Ireland) 2016);The Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps being taken to address concerns in relation to the under-performance of the new planning portal.
answer_text: The Planning Portal went live in December 2022 and like all large IT Projects with numerous stakeholders it encountered some initial problems.  The Project Team and the Intelligent Client Function (ICF) in Belfast City Council worked closely with the IT supplier to resolve those problems as quickly as possible on a priority basis. The ICF is overseeing the day-to-day contract management and operation of the system.  The number of incidents being reported are very low, with service availability and performance at 100% average availability across all services in March 2024.  They continue to engage with all stakeholders through a number of forums to improve system functionality where possible. Currently 76% of all planning applications and 99% of Property Certificates across local councils are received online.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the legislation, or parts of legislation, that falls under their Department's responsibility that has not yet commenced.
answer_text: The legislation, or parts of legislation, that fall under our Department’s responsibility and have not yet commenced are listed below.The following sections of The Period Products (Free Provision) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 will commence from 12 May 2024:section 1 (excluding section 1(4) and (9);section 2(3) to (6);section 3; andsection 7.The Fair Employment (School Teachers) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 will commence from 12 May 2024.  Section 50 of The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 will commence on 6 June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he would consider proposals to require the attendance of statutory consultees at Pre-Application Discussion meetings as a means to address delays in the planning process.
answer_text: Pre-application discussions (PADs) are a non-statutory collaboration between an applicant and the council or Department during the pre-application stage, as a means to improving the quality of planning applications.  Participation and attendance by statutory consultees is influenced by the nature, scale and complexity of the development proposals involved, as well as the availability of resources, in the context of each Departments’ legislative obligations.  Through the Planning Improvement Programme and the Planning Statutory Consultee Forum (the Forum), my officials have been working with councils and statutory consultees to improve working practices around PADs.  The Forum produced a set of good practice operating principles for planning authorities and statutory consultees which  includes advice on the role and input required from consultees in support of the full end-to-end development management process, including at the PAD stage (https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/consultations-planning-application-process-operating-principles-planning-authorities-and-consultees).  My Department will continue to work closely with councils and all key stakeholders to collectively improve planning processes and increase the efficiency of the overall system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will introduce legislation to remove the parental rights of convicted paedophiles.
answer_text: The courts here currently have a range of powers to effectively remove or totally restrict the parental responsibilities of those who fall within this category, with the best interests of the child principle always at the forefront of decision-making.  As such, there are currently effective mechanisms in place to deal with convicted paedophiles.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount spent by his Department on private investigators, investigating vehicle damage claims due to road defects, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department has not spent any money employing private investigators to investigate vehicle damage claims during the last five financial years.  In addition, I would further advise that my Department does not employ private investigators in relation to vehicle damage claims.  However, a loss assessor may be engaged in relation to a vehicle damage claim to report on the loss incurred and whether the damage is consistent with the details provided by the claimant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will restart funding to the Northern Ireland Law Commission.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Law Commission has been non-operational as a constituted body since April 2015, following the decision of the then Minister of Justice to close it due to financial pressures facing the Department at that time. Those budgetary pressures remain and have, in fact, considerably deepened. My Department faces significant unfunded pressures of over £440 million for 2024–25. While I very much want to see a functional NILC fully operating here, these pressures mean that, in the short term, this is not possible. The Commission does, however, continue to function in a very limited capacity, and I continue to keep this issue under review. Officials will be undertaking a feasibility study in the current mandate which would be of assistance in restarting funding if it becomes available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the value of residential outdoor education for (i) primary; and (ii) post-primary pupils.
answer_text: As with other decisions taken by schools, the specific benefits of outdoor learning for primary or post-primary schools’ pupils is one which individual schools need to assess, taking into account the particular needs of the relevant children and young people.  As part of any outdoor learning activity, schools identify what outcomes they are seeking to achieve and outdoor learning instructors develop programmes to deliver on those outcomes. However, in general, outdoor learning can improve health and wellbeing; personal and social development; and attainment across the curriculum.  Undertaking outdoor learning at Residential Outdoor Education Centres (OECs) also affords specific benefits that are unlikely to be replicated in other education settings. Children and young people benefit by becoming immersed in the outdoor learning experience and can develop resilience, risk awareness and independence whilst undertaking curricular relevant activities.Outdoor learning also offers significant benefits for young people in respect of engaging with key themes in respect of climate change and environmental issues. The immersive, fun and curricular-relevant opportunities that residential outdoor education centres provide also offer particular benefits for children and young people in relation to their physical and mental health and wellbeing, which is important as society continues to recover from the impact of the pandemic.Providing assurance on child protection and safeguarding is a key aspect of any residential outdoor learning activity and is addressed comprehensively in guidance, including for such activities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps her Department is taking to address the underlying causes for the annual rise in the time taken from the report of a crime to its disposal at Crown Court.
answer_text: The Criminal Justice Board has prioritised a programme of work targeted at reducing avoidable delay within the system.  This work is built around five key workstreams and takes forward existing work on committal reform, existing and emerging work in the digital arena and explores other areas for efficiencies such as early engagement, court remits and out of court disposals. This will be an ongoing programme of work to address what is a complex and challenging issue. Some projects will require legislation and it will take time to embed new processes and progress work for long-term change.It should be noted that the system is presently under considerable pressure, dealing with increased caseload and although progress is being made, it will take time and, crucially, resources to resolve this issue.  Any improvements to Crown Court times are likely to be contingent on more sustainable funding arrangements for the criminal justice system as a whole.  		Prior to Covid, the Department’s efforts to tackle delay were having a positive effect, with the overall average time to complete a criminal case falling from 169 days in December 2018 to 149 days in March 2020, when lockdowns were first introduced. This reduction was largely driven by improvements in the magistrates’ courts.  Based on internal management information, the average time taken for the most recent quarter, Quarter 3 2023-24, was 189 days. This is the sixth consecutive reduction, down from the post–Covid highpoint of 232 days which was recorded in Quarter 1 2022-23.  		Tackling delay in the more serious and complex cases heard in the Crown Court is more challenging.  However internal management information also indicates that the situation has improved from the latest annual published figure of 756 days at the end of 2022-23, to 704 days for the most recent Quarter (Quarter 3 2023/24).   	The significant impact that the response to Covid has had on the criminal justice system cannot be underestimated.  The pandemic resulted in significant backlogs in the system, which still persist and will take time to clear.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) how much has been paid in legal aid for immigration (a) advice and (b) cases; and (ii) how much funding was provided to other bodies in respect of immigration matters, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In response to part (i), Table 1 (below) provides a breakdown of authorised legal aid expenditure by category of service (denoting advice, civil or criminal expenditure) where the primary nature and/or primary matter of each case has been used to indicate that it relates to immigration.  This information has been largely sourced from the Legal Service Agency’s case management system (LAMS) which became operational in July 2019.  Prior to this date, information was contained within the previous case management system (Phoenix) that has since been decommissioned.  Accordingly, information pertaining to the first quarter of 2019/20 (April to June 2019) has been derived from management information and may therefore be subject to change.  In addition, figures covering the latest year (2023/24) also constitute management information at this stage and may be subject to revision during preparation for the Agency’s 2023/24 Official Statistics release in summer 2024.Table 1: Legal Aid Authorised Expenditure on Immigration related cases by Financial Year and Category of Service.Category of Service2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24Advice and Assistance£380,193£320,681£468,751£611,468£722,968Representation Higher£294,074£148,293£186,612£305,134£234,462Criminal£14,903£0£2,687£11,016£24,711Total£674,267£468,974£655,364£916,602£957,430Source: Legal Services Agency (Official Statistics and Management Information)Notes: Expenditure figures incorporates a mix of legal fees along with disbursement remuneration that includes interpreter costs as well as expert witness payments e.g. Asylum and Immigration expert, GP Report.Criminal cases relating to immigration include where the primary matter is Conspire assist unlawful immigration Non UK, Assist Unlawful Immigration Member State and Assist unlawful immigration of Non UK.In relation to part (ii), I can confirm that no funding was provided by my core Department and Agencies to other bodies in respect of immigration matters over the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what systems are in place to monitor the demand and supply of probation-approved premises, for those being released from prison.
answer_text: Approved premises act as a transition or half-way house between prison and settlement in the community for high risk offenders leaving custody.  They have two main roles: to help rehabilitate and resettle some of the most serious offenders leaving custody; and to make sure the public are protected in the offenders’ early months in the community.All residents are supervised by the Probation Board for Northern Ireland (PBNI) and managed in accordance with PBNI practice standards.  A proportion may also be subject to monitoring by the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) and under the Public Protection Arrangements Northern Ireland (PPANI).  Allocation of places is through a weekly Hostel Panel, representative of all relevant partners and chaired by PBNI.There are seven approved premises across Northern Ireland, which currently provide 91 places.  Two premises accommodate women in mixed gender establishments.  Four accept only those individuals referred directly by the PBNI; the remaining three also provide accommodation for people who are homeless (referred to as generic beds).  There are other hostels across Northern Ireland which provide accommodation to support the management of offenders, including individuals being supervised by the PBNI, but what distinguishes them from approved premises is that they primarily address a homelessness rather than a risk management need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps her Department is taking to help ensure the rehabilitation of offenders with a history of substance abuse.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service places rehabilitation and resettlement at its core, preparing those in custody for life in the community.  Their aim is to support and challenge those in custody to change and address their offending behaviour whilst in custody, thus reducing their risk of reoffending on release.   Rehabilitation is delivered through the Prisoner Development Model, a person centred approach which challenges individuals to take responsibility for their actions and behaviours and supports them on a journey of change. The model focuses on nine resettlement pathways, based on socio-economic factors which are shown to have an impact on reoffending, one of which is Drugs and Alcohol abuse. Assessments are completed within 30 days of committal, which build a picture of the needs, strengths and risks of individuals.  Any prisoner acknowledging that their use of alcohol/drugs has caused them problems and/or contributed to their offending behaviour will be referred to the Alcohol and Drugs Team whose services are provided through our Healthcare Partner, South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust. In addition to having specialist addictions staff within the healthcare teams at each prison establishment, the Trust has commissioned VCS partner organisations to provide drug counselling and therapeutic interventions on its behalf.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps her Department is taking to help address the smuggling of communication devices into prisons.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) deploy a range of measures to prevent unauthorised articles, including communication devices, enter our prisons. These measures include the searching of prisoners, visitors and staff and the use of a variety of search aids. NIPS is continually looking at ways to enhance its effectiveness in preventing contraband entering our prisons and the recent introduction of X-Ray Body Scanners has already demonstrated that the use of such technology is having a significant impact in this regard.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what enforcement measures are in place in relation to the non-payment of fines.
answer_text: The Fine Collection and Enforcement Service (FCS), which is part of the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS), is responsible for the enforcement and recovery of unpaid fines. Where a court makes a collection order, collection officers use a range of powers to collect and enforce outstanding financial penalties. These powers are set out in the Justice Act (NI) 2016 and include granting instalment orders or additional time to pay, applying for deductions from benefits, making attachment of earnings orders, freezing money in a debtor’s bank account and recommending to the court a bank account order or vehicle seizure order.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she intends to bring forward legislation amending the Ground Rents Act (Northern Ireland) 2001 to create a scheme for the automatic redemption of small ground rents relating to dwelling houses.
answer_text: This question relates to recommendations contained in a 2010 report of the NI Law Commission (NILC) on Land Law. The NILC report was a very detailed piece of work conducted over a number of years, drawing on internal and external expertise in an extremely complex area of law and policy. While aspects of the recommendations may be desirable in policy terms, translating the general recommendations of the report into concrete legislative reform is not a priority for my Department during this mandate, particularly in the context of resources already committed to other important policy initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how many (i) Irish-medium; (ii) integrated; and (iii) special schools are involved in shared classes with pupils from other schools.
answer_text: There are two Irish-medium schools, 21 integrated schools (11 controlled integrated and 10 grant maintained integrated) and eight special schools involved in Shared Education partnerships during the 2023/24 academic year, supported through the Department-funded Mainstreaming Shared Education funding stream.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he is taking to improve GP pharmacists' pay and conditions.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the various issues regarding remuneration for GP practice pharmacists employed by GP Federations which you have raised. I can assure you that such issues are already subject to ongoing discussions between the Department of Health (DoH) and key stakeholders.The GP Pharmacist Oversight Board was established by the Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) within the DoH in December 2023. The group brings together representatives from all key stakeholder groups, including SPPG, DoH, GP Federations and GPC (General Practitioners Committee) to facilitate collaborative working to optimise the working arrangements of the GPP workforce, in line with strategic priorities of the DoH Medicines Optimisation Quality Framework. The group will oversee the delivery of the service and facilitate discussion and resolution to issues relating to the GPP workforce employed in General Practices. The group also aims to develop the General Practice Pharmacist workforce, in line with DoH strategic direction and the Pharmacy Workforce Review, which is being progressed through Service Delivery and Service Development subgroups.DoH will continue to work closely with stakeholders over the following months to address the issues to which this question refers

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps he is taking to ensure the development management service in DFI Roads Southern Division is adequately financed to ensure delivery of the Planning Improvement Programme.
answer_text: I am committed to ensuring that our planning system operates in an efficient and effective way.  I intend to take some time to review the progress that has been made to date through the Planning Improvement Programme and will continue to work closely with key stakeholders to collectively improve processes and increase the efficiency of the system.  Progress is however dependent on the resources available to key areas of the system.I will continue to seek additional funding for the 2024/25 financial year that would enable additional resources to be made available to Roads Southern Division to support their role in responding to Consultations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what powers local planning authorities have to revoke Tree Preservation Orders which were made, but not confirmed by the Planning Service, prior to the transfer of functions to local councils.
answer_text: The power to revoke a Tree Preservation Order (TPO) rests with the Department, after it has consulted with the relevant council, as set out within section 124 of the Planning Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the GeoEnergy NI project; and (ii) whether it is meeting planned timescales.
answer_text: The GeoEnergy NI geothermal demonstrator project commenced in February 2023. The overarching communications plan continues to showcase the project and the potential for geothermal energy to provide heat and cooling to our homes and buildings.  In July 2023, at the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) Greenmount Campus, ground-based geophysical surveys imaged the geological structures and any potential water zones to identify the optimum location for future drilling. The final modelling report, which will include a 3D geological model of the sub-surface and recommendations for future phases of the project is expected in June 2024.In March 2024, we received planning permission to proceed with drilling 5 exploratory borewells on the Stormont Estate.  Here the focus is on shallow geothermal and identifying the most suitable geothermal solution to provide heating and cooling for several buildings on the Estate.  Drilling will commence at the end of May 2024.The project is currently on schedule to be completed in January 2025.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what steps are being taken to ensure that non-state bodies contribute towards compensation being paid to survivors of historical sexual abuse following the Historical Institutional Abuse inquiry.
answer_text: The Inquiry Report recommended that any voluntary institution found by the Inquiry to have been guilty of systemic failings should be asked to make an appropriate financial contribution to the overall cost of the HIA Redress Board and any specialist support services.  The Independent Facilitator, Mr. Paul Sweeney, has drafted a framework for reaching an agreement between the Executive and the six voluntary institutions.  Engagement with institutions is ongoing on the basis of the framework.We are pleased to have secured commitments on interim payments from a number of the institutions and we will continue to work on the overall contributions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many times the Safe Choices working group, as a sub-committee of the Regional Child Protection Committee, has met since its establishment.
answer_text: The Regional Child Protection Committee was stood down with the establishment of the Safeguarding Board for Northern Ireland (SBNI) in 2012. I can confirm that since its establishment, the SBNI has not hosted a sub-committee related to Safe Choices. However, the SBNI hosts a range of other safeguarding and child protection panels and committees, including one specific to child sexual exploitation; further information is available on its website at www.safeguardingni.org/about-us/committees-panels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when Movilla Road, Newtownards, will be resurfaced.
answer_text: The programme for 2024-25 will be developed and published once future budgets have been agreed. In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.
